8.1.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.1.title: Board Approval of Technology Risk Appetite

business.summary: This clause requires the board to formally own and govern the institution's exposure to technology risk, ensuring it is bounded by explicit tolerances that account for operational disruption and customer harm. Failure to comply exposes the institution to uncontrolled technology risk accumulation, regulatory censure from BNM, and potential reputational damage arising from incidents that exceed undeclared or misaligned tolerances. Board-level accountability anchors technology risk within enterprise risk management, preventing technology decisions from being made in isolation from business strategy.

requirements.requirement: Define and formally document a Technology Risk Appetite Statement (T-RAS) that is explicitly aligned with and traceable to the institution's overarching Enterprise Risk Appetite Statement (E-RAS), covering dimensions such as operational availability, data integrity, cybersecurity incidents, and third-party technology dependency.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.1 mandates that the technology risk appetite be 'aligned with the financial institution's risk appetite statement.' Without a documented T-RAS that cross-references the E-RAS, the institution cannot demonstrate regulatory alignment and risks operating technology initiatives outside approved risk boundaries.
requirements.frequency: Annual (or upon material change to E-RAS or business strategy)

requirements.requirement: Establish quantified risk tolerance thresholds for technology-related events, including maximum permissible downtime (RTO/RPO), acceptable data loss windows, incident frequency caps, and customer impact limits (e.g., number of customers affected per incident), documented in a board-approved Risk Tolerance Schedule.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.1(a) requires the board to 'approve the corresponding risk tolerances for technology-related events considering potential impact on business operations as well as its customers.' Qualitative tolerances alone are insufficient; quantified thresholds enable objective monitoring and escalation decisions.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) with input from Chief Operating Officer (COO)
requirements.frequency: Annual review; immediate revision upon material operational change or significant incident

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a Resource Adequacy Plan for technology risk management, demonstrating that budget, skilled personnel, and tooling are sufficient to operate within approved risk tolerances — including provisions for emerging risk scenarios such as cloud migration or AI adoption.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.1(d) requires the board to 'ensure sufficiency and appropriate deployment of resources.' Business units sponsoring technology initiatives must justify that resource commitments are proportionate to the risk exposure introduced, preventing underfunded risk management functions.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) and Chief Financial Officer (CFO)
requirements.frequency: Annual (aligned with budget cycle); reviewed quarterly against actual deployment

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal residual risk acceptance process with documented criteria and designated approving authorities at appropriate seniority levels, ensuring that technology risks retained by the institution after controls are applied are explicitly acknowledged and approved rather than implicitly absorbed.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.1(b) requires the board to 'establish criteria and approving authority for the acceptance of residual risks.' Without a structured acceptance process, residual risks may accumulate invisibly, breaching tolerance thresholds without board awareness.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (each residual risk acceptance decision); process reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Conduct a formal periodic review of the Technology Risk Appetite at board level to assess whether stated tolerances remain appropriate given changes in the external threat landscape, business model evolution, regulatory updates, and lessons learned from technology incidents.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.1(e) mandates review 'at regular intervals with sufficient deliberation to ensure such risk appetite remains relevant with changing risk environment.' Static risk appetite frameworks become misaligned over time; regular board-level deliberation ensures the appetite adapts to evolving conditions.
requirements.owner: Board of Directors (led by Board Risk Committee Chairman)
requirements.frequency: At minimum annually; triggered reviews upon material incidents or significant environmental changes

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement the instrumentation, monitoring infrastructure, and reporting mechanisms that make the board-approved risk appetite operationally enforceable and observable. This includes building a Key Risk Indicator (KRI) framework with real-time or near-real-time dashboards, maintaining a technology risk register, and providing the data necessary for residual risk assessments and board reporting. Without these capabilities, the governance requirements of Clause 8.1 cannot be evidenced to BNM examiners.

requirements.requirement: Design, implement, and maintain a Technology Risk KRI Framework comprising quantified indicators mapped to each approved risk tolerance threshold (e.g., system availability percentage, mean time to detect/respond, patch compliance rate, failed authentication attempts, third-party SLA breach frequency), with automated data collection feeding a risk dashboard accessible to risk and senior management.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.1(c) requires the board to 'ensure key risk indicators are identified to monitor existing and emerging risks against financial institution's risk tolerance.' KRIs must be technically instrumented — not manually compiled — to provide timely and accurate signals of tolerance breaches or trending risks.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Technology Risk
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; KRI library reviewed and recalibrated semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Implement and maintain a centralised Technology Risk Register that captures all identified technology risks, assigned risk owners, current control effectiveness ratings, residual risk scores, and linkage to board-approved tolerance thresholds — integrated with the institution's GRC (Governance, Risk and Compliance) platform.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.1(b) requires identification of risk owners and clear accountability. A technology risk register operationalises ownership by providing a single authoritative record that links each risk to a named owner, control status, and tolerance position, enabling audit trails for BNM examination.
requirements.owner: Head of Technology Risk / Chief Information Officer (CIO)
requirements.frequency: Continuous updates; formal completeness review quarterly

requirements.requirement: Develop automated breach-alert and escalation workflows within the risk monitoring platform that trigger notifications to designated risk owners and senior management when KRI thresholds approach or breach approved tolerance levels, with full audit logging of alert generation, acknowledgement, and remediation actions.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.1(a) requires that risk tolerances account for 'potential impact on business operations as well as its customers.' Automated escalation ensures that tolerance breaches are surfaced to decision-makers in time to prevent customer-impacting events, and provides evidence of control operation for regulatory review.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of IT Operations
requirements.frequency: Real-time detection; escalation logic reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Produce structured, board-ready Technology Risk Reporting packages — including KRI trend analysis, tolerance breach summaries, residual risk acceptance register, and resource deployment status — in a format that enables meaningful board deliberation rather than passive information receipt.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.1(e) requires board review conducted 'with sufficient deliberation.' Management must provide technology teams with data packages that translate technical metrics into business-risk language, enabling the board to make informed risk appetite decisions rather than ratifying pre-determined conclusions.
requirements.owner: Head of Technology Risk / CRO Office
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting to Board Risk Committee; annual comprehensive review package

requirements.requirement: Conduct an annual Technology Risk Resource Adequacy Assessment, quantifying staffing levels, tool coverage, and budget allocation against the risk management workload implied by the approved risk appetite — including gap analysis and a remediation roadmap for identified deficiencies.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.1(d) requires the board to 'ensure sufficiency and appropriate deployment of resources.' Technology leadership must provide an evidence-based assessment — not a subjective assertion — that resources are proportionate to risk management requirements, enabling informed board oversight of resource allocation decisions.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Officer (CIO) / Chief Technology Officer (CTO)
requirements.frequency: Annual; interim assessment upon significant scope or organisational change

governance.summary: This clause establishes the board as the primary governance authority for technology risk appetite, requiring formal board-level structures — including a Board Risk Committee with technology risk competence — to own, approve, and periodically deliberate on risk appetite and tolerance decisions. Governance requirements span the full lifecycle: from framework approval and risk owner designation through to residual risk adjudication and appetite refresh, all with documented minutes and approvals to create a defensible audit trail for BNM supervisory review.

requirements.requirement: Ensure the Board of Directors or its delegated Board Risk Committee has a formal mandate — documented in the committee's terms of reference — to approve the Technology Risk Appetite Statement, associated tolerance schedules, and any material amendments, with board-level sign-off evidenced through signed resolutions and meeting minutes.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.1 places responsibility on 'the board' to 'establish and approve the technology risk appetite.' Regulatory examination will look for formal documentary evidence that approval occurred at board level, not merely at management level. The terms of reference must make this mandate explicit and non-delegable below board level.
requirements.frequency: Terms of reference reviewed annually; approval executed upon each appetite revision

requirements.requirement: Establish a Technology Risk Ownership Framework that assigns named risk owners (by role, not individual) to each category of technology risk in the risk register, with documented accountability including scope of ownership, authority to accept residual risks within defined limits, and escalation obligations when tolerance thresholds are approached.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.1(b) requires the board to 'identify risk owner to ensure clear accountability.' An ownership framework transforms the board's directive into an operational accountability structure, ensuring that every technology risk has a designated steward who is responsible for monitoring, controlling, and reporting on that risk.
requirements.frequency: Framework reviewed annually; ownership assignments updated upon organisational change

requirements.requirement: Formalise a Residual Risk Acceptance Policy that specifies: (i) quantitative criteria distinguishing risks that can be accepted at management level versus those requiring board or Board Risk Committee approval; (ii) mandatory documentation requirements for each acceptance decision; and (iii) time-bound acceptance periods requiring periodic reaffirmation.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.1(b) requires the board to 'establish criteria and approving authority for the acceptance of residual risks.' Without a policy that delineates authority levels and documentation standards, residual risk acceptance decisions may be made inconsistently or at inappropriate levels, exposing the institution to BNM findings of inadequate governance.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) approved by Board Risk Committee
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed annually; individual acceptance decisions per-event with mandatory reaffirmation cycle not exceeding 12 months

requirements.requirement: Schedule and conduct structured Technology Risk Appetite Review sessions at the Board Risk Committee at least annually, with a formal agenda covering: KRI performance against tolerances, material incidents and lessons learned, changes in the external risk environment, management's proposed appetite adjustments, and resource adequacy confirmation — all documented in board minutes with evidence of substantive deliberation.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.1(e) requires review 'at regular intervals with sufficient deliberation.' BNM examiners will assess whether reviews involved genuine board engagement or were purely administrative. Minutes must record questions raised, debate conducted, and the basis for conclusions reached — not merely that a review occurred.
requirements.frequency: Minimum annually; extraordinary session triggered by material incident, regulatory change, or significant business transformation

requirements.requirement: Integrate Technology Risk Appetite governance into the institution's broader Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) governance calendar, ensuring that the Technology Risk Appetite Statement is reviewed in coordination with — and formally reconciled against — updates to the overall Enterprise Risk Appetite Statement to prevent misalignment between technology and enterprise risk boundaries.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.1 explicitly requires the technology risk appetite to be 'aligned with the financial institution's risk appetite statement.' Governance structures must enforce this alignment through coordinated review cycles and a documented reconciliation process, preventing the technology risk framework from drifting out of alignment with enterprise-level risk decisions.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Board Risk Committee
requirements.frequency: Aligned with annual ERM governance calendar; reconciliation documented at each E-RAS review

8.2.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.2.title: Board Oversight Responsibilities

business.summary: Clause 8.2 places ultimate accountability for technology risk governance at the board level, requiring it to actively direct and review the institution's IT and cybersecurity posture rather than delegate it entirely to management. Failure to comply exposes the institution to regulatory censure, enforcement action by BNM, and reputational damage arising from inadequately governed IT risks. Operationally, gaps in board oversight create conditions where strategic misalignment between IT investments and business objectives goes unchecked, and systemic vulnerabilities in critical systems remain unmitigated.

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a board-approved IT and cybersecurity strategic plan covering a minimum rolling horizon of three years, explicitly aligned to the institution's business objectives and updated to reflect changes in the operating and risk environment.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.2(a) mandates board approval and adequacy review of IT and cybersecurity strategic plans spanning at least three years. Without a documented, board-endorsed multi-year plan, the institution cannot demonstrate strategic alignment of technology investments to business goals, increasing the risk of regulatory findings during BNM examinations.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Chief Information Officer (CIO)
requirements.frequency: Reviewed and reaffirmed annually; full refresh every three years or upon material business change

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal board reporting mechanism through which senior management presents evidence that risk assessments on critical IT systems and emerging technology initiatives are comprehensive, robustly supported by control measures, and adequately resourced.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.2(c) requires the board to actively demand senior management demonstrate the quality and depth of risk assessments. Without a structured reporting channel, this obligation defaults to passive receipt of information, leaving the board unable to fulfil its active oversight duty and potentially masking material IT and cyber risks.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Executive Officer (CEO)
requirements.frequency: Quarterly board or Board Risk Committee (BRC) reporting cycle

requirements.requirement: Ensure that all technology-related applications and submissions made to BNM are accompanied by a documented risk assessment that has been reviewed under the institution's approved risk management framework, with board-level visibility prior to submission.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.2(e) requires the risk management framework to support robust risk assessments specifically for technology-related applications submitted to the regulator. Inadequate pre-submission risk assessment creates exposure to regulatory rejection, delayed approvals, and reputational harm.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Compliance Officer (CCO)
requirements.frequency: Per-event (each regulatory submission)

requirements.requirement: Define and enforce a business continuity obligation within the endorsed Technology Risk Management Framework (TRMF) and Cyber Resilience Framework (CRF) that explicitly ties technology resilience outcomes to the continuity of financial services delivery.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.2(b) links the board's endorsement of the TRMF and CRF to continuity of operations and financial services delivery. Without explicit business continuity obligations embedded in these frameworks, technology resilience efforts may not adequately protect the institution's ability to serve customers during incidents.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Chief Executive Officer (CEO)
requirements.frequency: Annual review; triggered on material operational incident or organisational change

technology.summary: To satisfy clause 8.2, technology teams must design, implement, and maintain two foundational frameworks — the Technology Risk Management Framework (TRMF) and the Cyber Resilience Framework (CRF) — capable of producing board-grade evidence of risk governance quality. Risk assessment tooling and processes must be mature enough to comprehensively evaluate critical IT systems and emerging technologies, with outputs structured to enable board scrutiny. All frameworks and technical policies must be kept current within a mandatory triennial review cycle, adapting to changes in the risk environment.

requirements.requirement: Design, implement, and continuously maintain a Technology Risk Management Framework (TRMF) in accordance with paragraph 9.1, capable of identifying, assessing, treating, monitoring, and reporting IT risks across all critical systems and technology initiatives.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.2(b) requires the board to endorse and oversee a sound and robust TRMF developed under paragraph 9.1. The TRMF is the technical backbone for all risk governance; without a mature, implemented framework, the board cannot fulfil its endorsement duty and the institution has no defensible basis for managing technology risk.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Technology Risk
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; formal review at least every three years or upon material change

requirements.requirement: Design, implement, and continuously maintain a Cyber Resilience Framework (CRF) in accordance with paragraph 11.2, covering cyber threat identification, protection, detection, response, and recovery capabilities sufficient to support uninterrupted delivery of financial services.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.2(b) explicitly requires the board to endorse a CRF developed under paragraph 11.2, with an express linkage to operational continuity. A separately governed CRF ensures cyber risk is managed with the dedicated rigour that systemic threats to financial services demand, beyond what a general IT risk framework provides.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; formal review at least every three years, annually for cyber threat landscape alignment

requirements.requirement: Establish and operationalise a structured risk assessment methodology for critical IT systems, including a defined criticality classification process, standardised risk assessment templates, documented control measure inventories, and resource adequacy evaluations that produce board-ready evidence packages.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.2(c) requires the board to demand evidence that risk assessments on critical IT systems are robust, comprehensive, and supported by adequate controls and resources. Technology teams must produce assessment outputs structured for board scrutiny, not just internal operational use.
requirements.owner: Head of Technology Risk / Chief Information Officer (CIO)
requirements.frequency: Per system lifecycle event (procurement, change, decommission); annual reassessment for classified critical systems

requirements.requirement: Implement a formal emerging technology risk assessment process that evaluates novel technologies (e.g., AI/ML, cloud, distributed ledger) prior to adoption, assessing specific IT and cyber risks introduced and the adequacy of proposed mitigating controls.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.2(c) specifically extends the board's oversight obligation to risk assessments on the use of emerging technology. Without a dedicated assessment process distinct from standard IT change risk management, risks unique to emerging technologies — including novel attack surfaces and regulatory uncertainty — may be systematically underweighted.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Officer (CIO) / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Per-event (each emerging technology adoption decision); periodic horizon scanning at least annually

requirements.requirement: Establish a centralised IT policy and framework registry with automated review-cycle tracking, ensuring all IT-related frameworks, policies, and guidelines are flagged for review before the three-year mandatory interval lapses, with review scope calibrated to the complexity of operations and risk environment changes.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.2(d) requires the board to ensure IT-related frameworks and policies are reviewed at least every three years, with review depth commensurate with operational complexity and risk environment changes. A registry with automated tracking prevents unintentional lapse of mandatory reviews, which would constitute a direct breach of this standard.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Governance / Chief Information Officer (CIO)
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; review triggers upon three-year lapse or material environmental change

governance.summary: Clause 8.2 establishes the board as the apex of technology risk governance, requiring formal structures through which the board approves strategic plans, endorses frameworks, and holds senior management accountable for demonstrating risk assessment quality. Governance mechanisms must formalise the board's active oversight role — not passive receipt of information — and create auditable records of board decisions on IT and cybersecurity matters. Policy lifecycle governance must be embedded in standing board and committee calendars to prevent inadvertent non-compliance with mandatory review cycles.

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal board-level approval process for the institution's IT and cybersecurity strategic plans, including defined submission timelines, adequacy review criteria, board resolution requirements, and a documented annual reaffirmation mechanism to confirm the plan remains fit for purpose across its three-year horizon.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.2(a) requires the board to both approve and review the adequacy of IT and cybersecurity strategic plans. A formal process with documented criteria ensures the board exercises genuine, evidenced oversight rather than pro forma approval, and creates the audit trail required to demonstrate compliance to BNM.
requirements.frequency: Annual board review; full approval at each three-year plan cycle or upon material revision

requirements.requirement: Institute a formal board or Board Risk Committee (BRC) endorsement process for the TRMF and CRF, including documented criteria for assessing framework soundness and robustness, an ongoing oversight mechanism to track implementation effectiveness, and an escalation path for material framework deficiencies.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.2(b) requires the board to endorse and oversee — not merely approve — the TRMF and CRF. Endorsement without oversight mechanisms reduces the board's engagement to a one-time administrative act, leaving implementation effectiveness unmonitored and creating regulatory exposure.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee (BRC) Chairman / Company Secretary
requirements.frequency: Endorsement at framework inception and each major revision; oversight reporting quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement a formal senior management accountability structure that requires the CRO, CISO, and CIO to produce and present to the board or BRC structured evidence demonstrating the robustness and comprehensiveness of risk assessments covering critical IT systems and emerging technology, including control adequacy and resource sufficiency attestations.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.2(c) places an affirmative obligation on the board to require senior management to continuously demonstrate risk assessment quality — this demands a governance structure where senior management is formally accountable to produce such evidence on a defined cycle. Without this structure, the board cannot satisfy its duty to 'require' such demonstration.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee (BRC) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Quarterly BRC presentations; ad hoc upon identification of critical system risk events

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a board-level IT policy lifecycle governance process that schedules mandatory triennial reviews of all IT-related frameworks, policies, and guidelines into the board and committee annual agenda, with review depth criteria formally documented and calibrated to operational complexity and risk environment changes.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.2(d) places the obligation to ensure policy reviews directly on the board. Without embedding review milestones into formal board governance calendars with defined depth criteria, review cycles are at risk of lapsing — an outcome that directly contravenes the mandatory minimum interval specified by BNM.
requirements.owner: Board Chairman / Chief Compliance Officer (CCO) / Company Secretary
requirements.frequency: Annual calendar scheduling; triennial mandatory review execution per policy

requirements.requirement: Establish a governance gate for all technology-related regulatory submissions to BNM, requiring documented confirmation that the institution's risk management framework has been applied and that a robust, board-visible risk assessment underpins the submission, before any such application is lodged with the regulator.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.2(e) requires the risk management framework to provide active support to risk assessments for technology applications submitted to BNM. A governance gate formalises this requirement into the submission process, creating an auditable control that prevents regulatory submissions unsupported by adequate risk assessments.
requirements.owner: Chief Compliance Officer (CCO) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Per-event (each BNM regulatory submission involving technology applications)

8.3.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.3.title: Board-Level Technology Committee

business.summary: Without a formally designated board-level committee for technology oversight, the institution risks blind spots in strategic technology decision-making, leaving the board unable to effectively challenge or guide technology investments, cyber risk posture, and digital transformation initiatives. Non-compliance exposes the institution to regulatory sanctions from BNM, reputational damage, and potential financial losses arising from poorly governed technology failures. The requirement anchors technology risk as a board-level concern, ensuring it receives the same strategic attention as financial and credit risk.

requirements.requirement: Formally designate a board-level committee with an explicit and documented mandate to oversee all technology-related matters on behalf of the board, including technology strategy, technology risk, cybersecurity, and digital transformation.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text states the board 'must designate a board-level committee' — this is a prescriptive, non-optional obligation. Without formal designation and a written terms of reference, the institution cannot demonstrate compliance to BNM during supervisory examinations.
requirements.frequency: One-time establishment; reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Define and document the committee's scope of oversight to encompass all material technology-related matters, including IT infrastructure, cybersecurity, technology outsourcing, digital channels, and emerging technology risks.
requirements.rationale: The clause specifies the committee is responsible for 'oversight over technology-related matters' — a broad mandate that requires explicit boundary-setting to ensure no material technology domain falls outside the committee's purview and to prevent governance gaps.
requirements.frequency: Annual review of scope; updated upon material changes to business or technology landscape

requirements.requirement: Recruit, appoint, or retain at least one board member with demonstrable technology experience and competencies to sit on the designated committee, with a succession plan to maintain this capability continuously.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly mandates that 'the composition of the designated committee must include at least one member with technology experience and competencies.' Failure to maintain this qualification at all times — including during board member transitions — constitutes a direct breach of the clause.
requirements.owner: Board Nomination and Remuneration Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Continuous; validated at each board appointment and annually

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal escalation and reporting pathway from the designated technology committee to the full board, ensuring material technology risks and strategic technology decisions are surfaced and minuted at the main board level.
requirements.rationale: The clause positions the committee as 'supporting the board in providing oversight' — meaning the committee is not a substitute for board accountability. A formal reporting cadence ensures the board retains ultimate oversight and cannot claim ignorance of technology matters.
requirements.frequency: At minimum every board meeting cycle; immediately for critical incidents

requirements.requirement: Conduct an annual assessment of the technology committee's effectiveness, including whether the member(s) with technology experience and competencies remain current and adequately skilled given the institution's evolving technology risk profile.
requirements.rationale: The requirement for 'technology experience and competencies' is a living standard — a director who was competent when appointed may become inadequate as the institution adopts cloud, AI, or complex third-party platforms. Annual effectiveness reviews ensure ongoing compliance with the spirit of the clause.
requirements.frequency: Annual

technology.summary: Technology teams must provide the board-level committee with the infrastructure, information, and reporting mechanisms it needs to meaningfully exercise oversight, including dashboards, risk reports, and structured briefings calibrated to board-level comprehension. The CTO and CISO must ensure the committee receives timely, accurate, and forward-looking technology intelligence to support informed decision-making. Technology teams are also responsible for ensuring the committee's technology-competent member has access to systems, environments, and subject-matter experts needed to independently verify management representations.

requirements.requirement: Design and implement a board-level technology reporting package — including a technology risk dashboard, key risk indicators (KRIs), key performance indicators (KPIs), and incident summaries — formatted for non-technical directors while providing sufficient depth for the technology-competent committee member.
requirements.rationale: For the committee to fulfil its mandate of 'oversight over technology-related matters,' it must receive structured, timely, and appropriately calibrated information from management. A reporting package bridges the information gap between operational technology teams and board-level governance.
requirements.frequency: Produced quarterly; updated monthly for KRI monitoring; ad hoc for material incidents

requirements.requirement: Establish a secure, access-controlled board portal or document management system that provides the designated committee with timely pre-read materials, historical meeting records, and technology risk documentation, with appropriate audit trails.
requirements.rationale: Effective oversight requires that committee members — particularly the technology-competent member — have timely and secure access to relevant materials. A governed portal ensures confidentiality of sensitive technology information while enabling the committee to perform its duties.
requirements.frequency: Continuous availability; materials uploaded at least 5 business days before each committee meeting

requirements.requirement: Develop a structured onboarding and ongoing briefing programme for the technology-competent committee member, covering the institution's technology architecture, current risk exposures, regulatory technology obligations under RMiT, and emerging threats.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires that the committee member possess 'technology experience and competencies' relevant to the institution's context. Management must actively enable the application of those competencies by ensuring the member understands the institution's specific technology landscape, not just generic technology concepts.
requirements.frequency: Onboarding upon appointment; quarterly briefing updates; ad hoc for significant technology changes

requirements.requirement: Implement a technology incident and change escalation protocol that automatically notifies the designated committee of material technology events — including significant outages, cyber incidents, and major system changes — within defined timeframes.
requirements.rationale: The committee's oversight responsibility extends beyond scheduled meetings. The verbatim text's mandate for 'oversight over technology-related matters' requires the committee to be informed of material events in near-real-time, not only retrospectively at the next scheduled meeting.
requirements.frequency: Protocol established continuously; triggered per material event

governance.summary: The institution must establish clear governance documentation — including a board resolution, formal terms of reference, and membership criteria — to give the committee its legal standing, authority, and operating parameters. Governance processes must ensure the committee meets with sufficient frequency to exercise meaningful oversight, maintains proper minutes and records for BNM examination, and has a mechanism to verify and periodically revalidate the technology credentials of its qualifying member. As an 'S' (Standard) marker clause, non-compliance is a regulatory breach rather than a recommended practice.

requirements.requirement: Pass a formal board resolution designating the committee and approve documented Terms of Reference (ToR) that define the committee's mandate, authority, quorum requirements, reporting obligations to the full board, and the minimum technology competency requirement for membership.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text's use of 'must designate' requires a formal, documented act of the board. A board resolution and ToR provide the legal and regulatory evidence of designation required for BNM supervisory review and examination.
requirements.frequency: Established at inception; ToR reviewed and ratified annually

requirements.requirement: Define and document the criteria for 'technology experience and competencies' in the committee's Terms of Reference, specifying minimum acceptable qualifications, professional experience, and any continuing professional development obligations for the qualifying member.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates at least one member with 'technology experience and competencies' but does not define these terms. The institution must define these criteria to operationalise the requirement, enable the Nomination Committee to assess candidates, and demonstrate a defensible interpretation to BNM.
requirements.owner: Board Nomination and Remuneration Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Established at inception; reviewed annually and when the role is vacated

requirements.requirement: Ensure the designated committee convenes at a minimum quarterly, with meeting agendas, attendance records, and minutes formally recorded and retained, and with minutes tabled at the next full board meeting to demonstrate the committee's supporting role to the board.
requirements.rationale: The committee's mandate to support the board in 'providing oversight' is an active, ongoing obligation. Infrequent or undocumented meetings would undermine the committee's ability to perform meaningful oversight and would leave the institution without evidence of compliance during BNM examinations.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly minimum; ad hoc as required for material events

requirements.requirement: Establish a vacancy management protocol that requires the Nomination Committee to initiate the recruitment of a replacement technology-competent board member within 30 days of the qualifying member's departure, with interim coverage arrangements documented and approved by the board.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the composition of the committee to 'include at least one member with technology experience and competencies' — this is a continuous obligation. A vacancy protocol prevents inadvertent non-compliance during board member transitions and demonstrates proactive governance to BNM.
requirements.owner: Board Nomination and Remuneration Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Triggered per vacancy event; protocol reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Incorporate technology committee oversight effectiveness into the annual board effectiveness assessment, including a specific evaluation of whether the technology-competent member's qualifications remain adequate for the institution's current and planned technology risk profile.
requirements.rationale: The requirement for 'technology experience and competencies' must be assessed in the context of the institution's evolving technology environment. Embedding this evaluation in the annual board effectiveness review creates an auditable governance cycle that demonstrates ongoing compliance rather than point-in-time compliance.
requirements.frequency: Annual

8.4.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.4.title: Board Discussion on Technology and Cyber Risk

business.summary: This clause places direct accountability on the board for understanding and engaging with technology and cyber risk at a strategic level. Failure to comply exposes the institution to regulatory censure, and more critically, to uninformed strategic decisions during or after a cyber incident — heightening reputational damage, liquidity stress, and stakeholder harm. Board-level cyber literacy is increasingly a baseline expectation from BNM and is foundational to effective enterprise risk governance.

requirements.requirement: Establish a structured board-level cyber risk briefing programme that delivers regular updates on technology risk exposure and the evolving cyber threat landscape specific to the Malaysian financial sector.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.4(a) mandates that the board 'obtain regular updates on technology risk and cyber threats.' Without a structured briefing cadence, boards risk operating on stale or insufficient information, impairing their ability to challenge management and make risk-informed strategic decisions.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly minimum; ad hoc upon material threat emergence or significant incidents

requirements.requirement: Ensure board meeting agendas formally allocate dedicated time for cyber risk discussion covering strategic, reputational, and liquidity risk dimensions, including scenario analysis of stakeholder impact from an extreme cyber incident.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.4(b) requires the board to 'allocate sufficient time to discuss cyber risks, including the strategic, reputational and liquidity risks and impact of operational disruption to stakeholders.' This must be structured into meeting governance — not addressed only when incidents arise — ensuring cyber risk is treated as a standing board-level concern.
requirements.frequency: Every board meeting; deep-dive at minimum annually

requirements.requirement: Commission and review at least one external cyber risk expert assessment per year to supplement internal management reporting at the board level, particularly in areas such as emerging threats, sector-specific attack vectors, and benchmarking against peer institutions.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.4(b) specifies that board discussion 'shall be supported by input from external experts where appropriate.' Relying solely on internal management perspectives may introduce blind spots; external expert input provides independent challenge and sector-wide context the board requires for effective oversight.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Board Risk Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Annual minimum; triggered by significant threat landscape shifts

requirements.requirement: Mandate all board members to complete a BNM-relevant cybersecurity awareness and training programme, with completion tracked and reported to the Board Nomination and Remuneration Committee as part of director competency assessment.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.4(c) requires board members to 'participate in relevant cybersecurity awareness and training programmes.' Embedding training completion in director competency frameworks ensures accountability and demonstrates to BNM that the institution actively invests in board-level cyber literacy.
requirements.frequency: Annual; refresher upon significant changes in threat landscape or regulation

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a board-approved cyber incident impact framework that maps extreme cyber scenarios to business impact dimensions — including customer disruption, liquidity stress, reputational exposure, and counterparty effects — to inform board-level preparedness discussions.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.4(b) specifically calls out board discussion of 'impact of operational disruption to stakeholders which could arise from an extreme cyber incident.' Without a pre-defined impact framework, board discussions lack the structured context needed to evaluate residual risk and strategic response options.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; updated post-incident or upon material business change

technology.summary: Technology teams must build and maintain the reporting infrastructure, threat intelligence pipelines, and training platforms that enable the board to fulfil its obligations under clause 8.4. This means translating complex technical risk signals into board-consumable metrics and scenario narratives, and ensuring the systems that support training and situational awareness are current and fit for purpose. The CISO and CTO are jointly accountable for the quality and timeliness of technology inputs delivered to board-level governance bodies.

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a board-level cybersecurity risk dashboard that aggregates key risk indicators (KRIs), threat intelligence feeds, incident trends, and control effectiveness metrics into a concise, non-technical format suitable for board consumption.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.4(a) requires the board to 'obtain regular updates on technology risk and cyber threats.' A standardised dashboard ensures updates are consistent, timely, and traceable — reducing reliance on ad hoc reporting and enabling trend analysis across board cycles.
requirements.frequency: Updated continuously; board pack version published quarterly

requirements.requirement: Integrate curated threat intelligence sources — including BNM Financial Intelligence Unit advisories, CERT MY, and sector-specific ISACs — into the board reporting pipeline to ensure updates reflect the current Malaysian and regional cyber threat landscape.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.4(a) mandates updates on 'cyber threats,' which requires intelligence that is current, contextualised to the institution's sector and geography, and not limited to internal incident data alone. Threat intelligence integration ensures the board receives forward-looking risk signals.
requirements.frequency: Continuous ingestion; board-ready summary produced quarterly

requirements.requirement: Develop quantified cyber risk scenario models for at least three extreme cyber incident archetypes (e.g., ransomware-driven operational shutdown, critical API/payment system compromise, data exfiltration affecting customer PII) that translate technical impact into financial, liquidity, and reputational metrics for board review.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.4(b) requires the board to discuss 'strategic, reputational and liquidity risks and impact of operational disruption to stakeholders which could arise from an extreme cyber incident.' Technology teams must provide quantified, scenario-grounded input — not qualitative heat maps alone — so the board can engage substantively with risk magnitude and response trade-offs.
requirements.frequency: Annual scenario refresh; updated post-significant incident or major infrastructure change

requirements.requirement: Deploy and manage a board-accessible cybersecurity e-learning platform offering role-appropriate training modules (e.g., cyber threat landscape, social engineering, incident response oversight), with completion tracking and certification tied to director training records.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.4(c) requires board members to 'participate in relevant cybersecurity awareness and training programmes.' A managed platform ensures training is accessible, auditable, and aligned to current threats — and provides the documentary evidence needed to demonstrate compliance with this requirement to BNM.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of IT / CISO
requirements.frequency: Platform maintained continuously; training content reviewed and updated annually

requirements.requirement: Establish a secure, board-appropriate communication channel (e.g., encrypted board portal) for distributing cyber risk reports, threat briefing materials, and external expert inputs, ensuring sensitive threat intelligence is not shared via unsecured email or uncontrolled document repositories.
requirements.rationale: The regular updates and expert inputs required under clause 8.4(a) and 8.4(b) will inevitably contain sensitive threat intelligence and vulnerability information. Distributing this material over unsecured channels would itself constitute a security risk, contradicting the institution's cyber posture and exposing sensitive internal assessments.
requirements.frequency: Operational continuously; security review of platform annually

governance.summary: Clause 8.4 imposes explicit governance obligations on the board itself — not merely on management — making it essential that the institution's governance framework formally encodes these requirements in board charters, committee terms of reference, and meeting procedures. Evidence of compliance must be documentable and auditable, as BNM supervisory reviews will scrutinise board minutes, training records, and agenda logs to assess whether the board is genuinely engaged with technology and cyber risk. The Company Secretary and Board Risk Committee play central roles in operationalising and enforcing these requirements.

requirements.requirement: Amend the Board Charter and Board Risk Committee Terms of Reference to explicitly codify the obligations under clause 8.4 — including the standing agenda requirement for cyber risk discussion, the mandatory training obligation for all directors, and the requirement to seek external expert input — making non-compliance a board governance deficiency.
requirements.rationale: Without formal embedding in governing documents, clause 8.4 obligations remain aspirational and unenforceable. Embedding them in the Board Charter and Committee TOR creates accountability structures that survive board member turnover and provides BNM with visible evidence of institutionalised compliance.
requirements.frequency: One-time embed; reviewed annually during governance document review cycle

requirements.requirement: Implement a formal board meeting agenda management process that reserves a standing agenda item for technology and cyber risk at every board meeting, with minimum time allocations documented in board meeting procedures and enforced by the Company Secretary.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.4(b) requires the board to 'allocate sufficient time to discuss cyber risks.' Without procedural enforcement, cyber risk discussion risks being deferred or compressed under time pressure. A documented minimum allocation — reinforced by the Company Secretary — operationalises this obligation and creates an audit trail.
requirements.frequency: Operationalised at every board meeting; process reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a register of approved external cybersecurity experts and advisors, with a documented process for engaging and briefing the board. The register must include criteria for expert selection (e.g., relevant financial sector experience, independence from management), and engagement must be documented in board minutes.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.4(b) states that board discussion 'shall be supported by input from external experts where appropriate.' Without a governed engagement process, external expert input may be inconsistent, poorly qualified, or undocumented — limiting its value and creating compliance gaps. A register ensures the board can access credible, independent perspectives on demand.
requirements.frequency: Register reviewed annually; external experts engaged at minimum annually or event-triggered

requirements.requirement: Maintain a board training register that records each director's participation in cybersecurity awareness and training programmes, including programme name, provider, date, and content scope. This register must be tabled at the Board Nomination and Remuneration Committee annually and retained for BNM examination.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.4(c) mandates board participation in 'relevant cybersecurity awareness and training programmes.' A formal register ensures the obligation is tracked individually — not just at an institutional level — and provides the evidentiary record BNM supervisors will expect during Technology Risk assessments and SREP reviews.
requirements.owner: Company Secretary / Board Nomination and Remuneration Committee
requirements.frequency: Register updated upon each training completion; tabled annually at BNRC

requirements.requirement: Require board minutes to capture substantive content of cyber risk discussions — including key risks identified, decisions or directions issued to management, and noting where external expert input was received — rather than recording only procedural outcomes. Minutes must be reviewed by the Company Secretary against the clause 8.4 agenda obligations before finalisation.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.4 is a Standard ('S') marker obligation, meaning BNM will assess board-level compliance through documentary evidence. Thin or procedural minutes that fail to reflect substantive cyber risk engagement will not satisfy supervisory scrutiny and may indicate to BNM that discussions are not genuinely occurring. Quality minute-taking is therefore a direct compliance control.
requirements.frequency: Applied at every board meeting; quality reviewed at each cycle

8.5.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.5.title: Board Audit Committee Technology Oversight

business.summary: Failure to maintain an effective BAC-led technology audit function exposes the institution to undetected technology control gaps that could materialise into operational losses, regulatory sanctions, or reputational damage. The BAC's accountability for audit staff competence and corrective action closure creates a direct board-level ownership chain for technology risk, ensuring that technology risk is treated with the same rigour as financial and credit risk. Non-compliance with this clause may trigger MAS-equivalent BNM supervisory action, including directed remediation or increased regulatory scrutiny.

requirements.requirement: The BAC must formally charter an internal technology audit function, with a documented mandate that explicitly assigns BAC responsibility for overseeing the function's overall effectiveness and independence.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text places direct accountability on the BAC ('shall be responsible') for the effectiveness of the internal technology audit function. Without a formal charter, this accountability is ambiguous and unenforceable, leaving the institution exposed to governance gaps that BNM examiners will cite.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; updated upon material changes to the technology landscape or audit structure

requirements.requirement: The BAC must commission and approve a Technology Audit Competency Framework defining the minimum qualifications, certifications (e.g., CISA, CISSP, cloud security credentials), and experience levels required for all technology audit staff, with periodic assessment against that framework.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly requires the BAC to ensure 'adequate competence of the audit staff to perform technology audits.' An undocumented or informal approach to competency assurance cannot demonstrate compliance during a BNM examination and creates operational risk if audits are conducted by staff without appropriate technical depth.
requirements.frequency: Annual competency assessment; framework reviewed every two years or upon emergence of new technology domains

requirements.requirement: The BAC must approve a formal Technology Audit Plan that defines the audit universe, scope, methodology, and annual audit frequency for all material technology domains, including cybersecurity, IT operations, change management, and third-party technology providers.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires the BAC to 'review and ensure appropriate audit scope, procedures and frequency of technology audits.' A BAC-approved annual audit plan operationalises this obligation and provides the evidentiary basis for demonstrating scope adequacy to BNM.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor (CIA), approved by BAC Chairperson
requirements.frequency: Annual approval; interim review triggered by material technology or risk profile changes

requirements.requirement: The BAC must receive and formally review a Technology Audit Corrective Action Report at each BAC meeting, tracking the status of all open technology control gaps identified in audit findings, with management attestation on closure timelines and escalation of overdue items.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires the BAC to 'ensure effective oversight over the prompt closure of corrective actions to address technology control gaps.' A structured reporting mechanism is the business-level mechanism for this oversight; without it, the BAC cannot demonstrate it has discharged this obligation.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor (CIA) reporting to BAC Chairperson
requirements.frequency: Per BAC meeting cycle (minimum quarterly)

requirements.requirement: The BAC must define and enforce a maximum permissible remediation period for technology control gaps, differentiated by risk severity (e.g., Critical: 30 days, High: 90 days, Medium: 180 days), with escalation protocols to the full Board when critical gaps exceed their remediation SLA.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text's emphasis on 'prompt closure' requires the BAC to operationalise 'promptness' through defined timelines. Without explicit SLAs, management discretion over remediation pace is unchecked, undermining the BAC's oversight effectiveness.
requirements.frequency: SLA framework reviewed annually; individual gap tracking continuous

technology.summary: Technology teams must build and maintain an auditable infrastructure of controls, evidence repositories, and tracking systems that enables the internal technology audit function to operate effectively and the BAC to receive accurate, timely oversight data. This includes implementing tooling and processes that support audit staff in performing technically rigorous assessments across the institution's full technology estate. Technology also bears responsibility for executing remediation of identified control gaps within BAC-mandated timelines.

requirements.requirement: Implement a centralised Audit Management System (AMS) capable of maintaining the full technology audit lifecycle — planning, fieldwork, findings, corrective action assignment, status tracking, and closure evidence — with role-based access for auditors, control owners, and BAC reporting.
requirements.rationale: The BAC's obligation to review audit scope, procedures, and corrective action closure ('prompt closure') cannot be discharged without a system of record that provides reliable, aggregated visibility. Manual or fragmented tracking creates risk of oversight failures that BNM will attribute to BAC accountability gaps.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Chief Information Officer (CIO)
requirements.frequency: System maintained continuously; BAC reporting extracts generated per meeting cycle

requirements.requirement: Establish a Technology Audit Evidence Repository with automated log collection, configuration snapshots, and control artefact retention (minimum 3 years) covering all material systems, enabling audit staff to conduct technically substantive reviews without relying solely on management self-attestation.
requirements.rationale: Ensuring 'adequate competence of audit staff' is rendered meaningless if staff lack access to technical evidence. Competent technology auditors require access to raw logs, system configurations, and vulnerability data; the institution must provision this infrastructure to support effective audit execution.
requirements.frequency: Continuous collection; retention reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Implement automated vulnerability and control gap tracking integrated with the AMS, enabling real-time dashboarding of open technology control deficiencies, remediation ownership, and age-of-finding metrics reportable directly to the BAC.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires effective oversight of 'prompt closure of corrective actions.' Automated tracking removes the risk of manual reporting errors or delays, and provides the BAC with objective, independently generated data on remediation velocity rather than management-curated status updates.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; BAC dashboard refreshed at minimum weekly

requirements.requirement: Define and implement a structured Technology Audit Procedures Library covering standard audit methodologies for each technology domain in scope (network security, cloud infrastructure, application security, IT operations, third-party technology), reviewed and updated annually by the technology audit team.
requirements.rationale: The BAC is required to 'ensure appropriate audit scope, procedures and frequency.' Technology teams must provide the procedural foundation — standardised, technically current audit procedures — that enables the BAC to make an informed judgment on procedural appropriateness. Outdated or absent procedures undermine the entire audit function.
requirements.owner: Head of Technology Audit (within Internal Audit function)
requirements.frequency: Annual review; ad-hoc updates triggered by new technology adoptions or material threat landscape changes

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Technology Control Gap Remediation Programme with defined owners, engineering timelines, and closure verification criteria for each open finding, integrated with IT change management processes to ensure technical fixes are implemented, tested, and validated before closure is reported to the BAC.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text's requirement for 'prompt closure of corrective actions' extends beyond management attestation to actual technical remediation. Without an engineering-level programme tied to change management, findings may be administratively closed without underlying control deficiencies being resolved.
requirements.frequency: Continuous programme operation; status reported to BAC per meeting cycle

governance.summary: Clause 8.5 imposes direct, non-delegable governance obligations on the BAC as a board-level committee, requiring the establishment of formal structures for charter accountability, competency oversight, audit plan approval, and corrective action tracking. These governance structures must be embedded in the institution's board committee terms of reference, internal audit charter, and risk governance framework to create a durable, audit-ready compliance posture. BNM examiners will expect documented evidence of BAC deliberation on each of these dimensions, not merely their existence.

requirements.requirement: Embed explicit technology audit oversight responsibilities — including effectiveness review, competency assurance, scope approval, and corrective action oversight — into the BAC's formal Terms of Reference (ToR), with the ToR reviewed and approved by the full Board annually.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text assigns these responsibilities directly to the BAC ('shall be responsible'). Without codification in the BAC ToR, these obligations exist only in regulation and are not institutionally owned. Board-approved ToRs create the governance chain that BNM will inspect to confirm accountability is properly embedded.
requirements.frequency: Annual ToR review; immediate update upon regulatory changes

requirements.requirement: The BAC must formally review and approve an annual Technology Audit Competency Assessment report, prepared by the Chief Internal Auditor, evidencing that each technology auditor meets the minimum competency standards, with a documented remediation plan for any identified competency gaps.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires the BAC to ensure 'adequate competence of the audit staff.' This obligation is prospective and ongoing — the BAC must actively assure competency, not assume it. A formal annual review with documented BAC deliberation creates the audit trail required to demonstrate this obligation has been discharged.
requirements.owner: BAC Chairperson; administered by Chief Internal Auditor (CIA)
requirements.frequency: Annual

requirements.requirement: Establish a BAC Technology Audit Reporting Calendar mandating minimum reporting frequency and content standards for technology audit updates, including: (i) annual Technology Audit Plan approval, (ii) quarterly corrective action status reports, and (iii) immediate escalation for critical technology control gap discoveries.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires the BAC to 'review and ensure appropriate... frequency of technology audits' and maintain 'effective oversight over prompt closure.' A formal reporting calendar operationalises both obligations, preventing ad-hoc or irregular oversight that could allow control gaps to persist undetected.
requirements.owner: BAC Chairperson; administered by Company Secretary
requirements.frequency: Calendar set annually; adhered to throughout the year

requirements.requirement: Maintain formal BAC meeting minutes that specifically record deliberations on technology audit scope, competency assessments, findings, and corrective action status, with individual BAC member sign-off, retained for a minimum of seven years as regulatory evidence.
requirements.rationale: BNM examinations require evidence that the BAC has actively discharged its oversight obligations — not merely that structures exist. Formally minuted deliberations are the primary evidentiary artefact demonstrating that BAC members engaged substantively with technology audit matters as required by the verbatim text.
requirements.frequency: Per BAC meeting; archival maintained continuously

requirements.requirement: Define a formal BAC Escalation and Exception Policy specifying that any technology control gap that breaches its remediation SLA or is assessed as posing systemic technology risk must be escalated to the full Board, with a documented Board response and timeline commitment recorded in Board minutes.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires the BAC to ensure 'effective oversight over the prompt closure of corrective actions.' Effectiveness implies the ability to escalate and compel action when remediation is inadequate. Without a formal escalation pathway to the full Board, the BAC's oversight authority is incomplete and BNM may assess it as ineffective.
requirements.owner: BAC Chairperson; escalation target is Board Chairman
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed annually; invoked on a per-event basis

8.6.title: Senior Management Accountability for Technology Risk

business.summary: This clause establishes senior management as the primary accountable body for day-to-day technology and cyber risk management, creating a direct line of liability if risks materialise without demonstrable management action. Failure to operationalise board-approved frameworks into enforceable policies exposes the institution to regulatory sanctions, reputational damage, and customer harm during service disruptions. The explicit linkage to customer impact metrics (disruption duration, affected customers, transaction value) means unmanaged incidents carry measurable financial and legal consequences.

requirements.requirement: Senior management must operationalise the board-approved Technology Risk Management Framework (TRMF) and Cyber Risk Framework (CRF) into a documented suite of specific policies and procedures, each explicitly mapped to the institution's approved risk appetite statements and risk tolerance thresholds.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.6(a) mandates implementation of board-approved frameworks into 'specific policies and procedures that are consistent with the approved risk appetite and risk tolerance.' Without this translation layer, the TRMF and CRF remain aspirational documents that cannot be enforced operationally, leaving the institution without demonstrable compliance evidence during BNM examinations.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Annual review; immediate update upon board-approved changes to TRMF, CRF, or risk appetite

requirements.requirement: Senior management must commission and maintain a Crisis Management Plan (CMP) for service disruptions that defines quantified escalation thresholds covering: maximum tolerable disruption duration before board-level escalation, defined customer-count thresholds triggering each escalation tier, and minimum financial transaction value or volume thresholds that activate the CMP.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.6(b) explicitly requires the CMP to account for disruption duration, number of customers affected, and number and value of financial transactions impacted. Without quantified thresholds, escalation decisions become subjective and inconsistent, undermining the 'timely escalation' requirement and exposing customers to prolonged harm.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Annual review; triggered review after each material service disruption event

requirements.requirement: Senior management must establish and maintain a structured board reporting programme delivering regular updates on key performance indicators (KPIs) for technology risk, including the status of risk controls, with sufficient granularity to enable the board to conduct informed performance reviews.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.6(c) requires senior management to 'provide regular updates to the board on the status of key performance indicators with pertinent information on the risk controls.' Ad-hoc or informal reporting fails this requirement; a formalised cadence with standardised KPI metrics ensures the board can fulfil its own oversight obligations under RMiT clause 8.1.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Technology Officer (CTO)
requirements.frequency: Quarterly minimum; monthly for institutions with elevated technology risk profiles

requirements.requirement: Senior management must maintain a documented accountability matrix (RACI or equivalent) assigning named senior management individuals as accountable owners for each technology risk category and cyber risk domain, to evidence 'primary responsibility for day-to-day management' as required by the clause preamble.
requirements.rationale: The clause preamble states senior management 'shall bear primary responsibility for the day-to-day management of technology risks including cyber risks.' Without a formal accountability assignment, BNM examiners cannot verify which individual is accountable for specific risk domains, and internal escalation paths remain ambiguous during incidents.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; updated upon senior management structural changes

requirements.requirement: Senior management must establish a formal policy lifecycle management process ensuring all technology risk and cyber risk policies derived from the TRMF and CRF are version-controlled, reviewed against current risk appetite, and attested by the relevant policy owner on a defined schedule.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.6(a) requires policies and procedures to remain 'consistent with the approved risk appetite and risk tolerance,' which is a continuous obligation, not a point-in-time task. Without a lifecycle process, policies drift out of alignment with board-approved risk appetite as the operating environment evolves.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Compliance
requirements.frequency: Annual review cycle; ad-hoc review triggered by material changes to risk appetite or operating environment

technology.summary: Technology teams must build and maintain the instrumentation, automation, and reporting infrastructure that enables senior management to fulfil its clause 8.6 obligations in real time. This includes real-time monitoring systems that capture the three specific customer impact dimensions cited in the clause (disruption duration, customer count, transaction value/volume), automated escalation workflows tied to defined thresholds, and integrated dashboarding that feeds the board KPI reporting cycle. Without this technical substrate, senior management cannot demonstrate timely, evidence-based decision-making during disruptions.

requirements.requirement: Implement a real-time service disruption monitoring system that continuously tracks and records: (i) start timestamp and cumulative duration of each service disruption per channel/system, (ii) count of unique customers impacted in real time, and (iii) aggregate number and cumulative monetary value of financial transactions affected, with automated alerting when predefined thresholds are breached.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.6(b)(i)–(iii) explicitly names disruption duration, customer count, and transaction number and value as the three mandatory customer impact dimensions the CMP must address. Without automated measurement of these three dimensions, senior management cannot make timely, data-driven escalation decisions and cannot produce post-incident evidence for BNM.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of IT Operations
requirements.frequency: Continuous (real-time); system tested quarterly via disruption simulation exercises

requirements.requirement: Implement an automated escalation workflow engine integrated with the Crisis Management Plan that triggers role-specific notifications and escalation actions when monitored disruption metrics breach defined customer impact thresholds, with full audit trail logging of each escalation event, timestamp, notified party, and response action taken.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.6(b) requires the CMP to 'provide for timely escalation of recovery action.' Manual escalation processes are prone to delay and omission; automation ensures escalation is deterministic, timely, and auditable, supporting both operational effectiveness and regulatory examination evidence.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of IT Service Management
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; tested quarterly; reviewed annually for threshold adequacy

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a Technology Risk KPI dashboard accessible to senior management that consolidates key risk indicators and control status metrics from across technology domains (infrastructure, application, cyber, third-party), formatted for direct consumption in board reporting packs with trend analysis, RAG status, and exception commentary.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.6(c) requires senior management to provide the board with 'pertinent information on the risk controls to facilitate informed performance review.' A structured dashboard operationalises this obligation by ensuring KPI data is accurate, current, and consistently formatted, eliminating manual aggregation errors in board packs.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Dashboard updated continuously or daily; board-reporting extracts generated quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement a policy management platform or GRC (Governance, Risk and Compliance) toolset that maintains a digital registry of all technology risk and cyber risk policies and procedures derived from the TRMF and CRF, with explicit linkage to corresponding risk appetite statements, version control, owner attestation workflows, and scheduled review reminders.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.6(a) requires policies and procedures to be 'consistent with the approved risk appetite and risk tolerance.' A centralised, auditable policy register provides BNM examiners with traceable evidence that each policy maps to board-approved frameworks and has been reviewed on schedule, directly supporting examination readiness.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Governance / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Continuous maintenance; annual bulk review cycle; ad-hoc updates as required

requirements.requirement: Implement cyber risk monitoring and threat intelligence tools covering the institution's full technology perimeter, with automated ingestion of threat indicators and event correlation, providing senior management with near-real-time situational awareness of cyber risk posture to support day-to-day management responsibilities.
requirements.rationale: The clause preamble explicitly extends senior management's day-to-day responsibility to 'cyber risks' specifically. Passive or periodic cyber risk assessment alone is insufficient; continuous monitoring tools ensure senior management has the operational visibility required to fulfil its primary accountability role and to escalate cyber incidents under the CMP.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; tooling reviewed and updated annually or upon material threat landscape changes

governance.summary: Clause 8.6 requires a governance architecture that places senior management at the centre of an accountable, documented, and regularly audited technology risk management cycle. The governance structures must provide formal linkages between the board-approved TRMF/CRF, operational policies, crisis escalation authority, and board reporting, ensuring each obligation in the clause is assigned, tracked, and evidenced. Without these structures, senior management cannot demonstrate to BNM that its 'primary responsibility' is exercised systematically rather than reactively.

requirements.requirement: Establish a Senior Management Technology Risk Committee (or equivalent governance body) with a formal charter that assigns day-to-day accountability for technology risk management including cyber risks to named senior management members, with documented terms of reference, meeting frequency, quorum requirements, escalation authority, and minutes retention policy.
requirements.rationale: The clause preamble places 'primary responsibility for the day-to-day management of technology risks including cyber risks' on senior management as a collective body. A formalised committee structure provides the organisational mechanism for discharging this collective responsibility, creates a clear escalation path from operational teams to the board, and produces meeting minutes as BNM examination evidence.
requirements.frequency: Committee meets monthly minimum; charter reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Crisis Management Governance Framework that defines the escalation authority matrix for service disruptions, specifying which senior management role has authority to invoke each escalation tier, the maximum permissible response time between breach of a customer impact threshold and escalation action, and the conditions under which the board must be convened on an emergency basis.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.6(b) requires the CMP to provide for 'timely escalation of recovery action, taking into consideration the impact on customers.'
