Timely escalation requires pre-agreed authority delegations and response time standards; without a governance framework defining these, escalation depends on individual judgment and may be delayed, directly harming customers and breaching the clause requirement.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Operating Officer (COO)
requirements.frequency: Framework reviewed annually; tested via tabletop crisis exercises semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Establish a formalised Board Technology Risk Reporting Protocol that defines: the standard KPI taxonomy and metrics to be reported, the reporting format and level of detail required, the reporting frequency and submission deadline relative to board meeting dates, and the process for senior management to provide contextual commentary on risk control status and emerging risks.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.6(c) requires senior management to 'provide regular updates to the board on the status of key performance indicators with pertinent information on the risk controls to facilitate informed performance review.' A documented protocol ensures consistency, completeness, and timeliness of board reporting, and provides evidence of compliance with the 'regular updates' obligation.
requirements.frequency: Protocol reviewed annually; reporting executed quarterly minimum

requirements.requirement: Implement a formal TRMF and CRF implementation assurance process whereby senior management commissions periodic independent assessments (internal audit or qualified external reviewer) verifying that all technology risk and cyber risk policies and procedures remain consistent with the current board-approved TRMF, CRF, risk appetite, and risk tolerance statements.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.6(a) requires policies and procedures to be 'consistent with the approved risk appetite and risk tolerance,' which is a continuous obligation. An independent assurance process provides senior management with objective evidence that implementation remains aligned, supports the board reporting obligation under clause 8.6(c), and demonstrates to BNM that self-attestation is validated by independent review.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Annual full assurance review; targeted reviews triggered by material changes to frameworks or risk appetite

requirements.requirement: Establish a post-incident governance review process requiring senior management to conduct a structured review following any material service disruption, producing a documented post-incident report that assesses CMP effectiveness against the three customer impact dimensions (disruption duration, customers affected, transaction value), identifies control failures, and tracks remediation actions to closure with board visibility.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.6(b) requires the CMP to address customer impact across duration, customer count, and transaction value. A post-incident governance review process closes the feedback loop, demonstrating to BNM that senior management actively learns from disruptions and adjusts controls accordingly, fulfilling its 'primary responsibility for day-to-day management' obligation on an ongoing basis.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Operating Officer (COO)
requirements.frequency: Per material disruption event; summary of post-incident reviews included in quarterly board report

business.summary: Failure to establish a cross-functional technology committee creates siloed decision-making where technology strategies are set without adequate input from affected business units, increasing the risk of misaligned investments, operational disruptions, and unmitigated technology risks. Without structured board reporting on key technology matters, the board cannot exercise effective oversight, exposing the institution to regulatory censure and reputational damage. Unapproved or inadequately assessed deviations from technology policies can erode control frameworks, leading to systemic vulnerabilities that affect customer trust and business continuity.

requirements.requirement: Formally constitute the Cross-Functional Technology Committee (CFTC) with documented membership drawn from senior management of the cyber function, technology function, and all major business units. The committee composition must be reviewed at least annually or upon material changes in business structure.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly mandates that 'members of the committee must include senior management from both cyber and technology functions, as well as major business units.' Failure to represent all major business units creates gaps in strategic alignment and leaves business-specific technology risks unaddressed at the governance level.
requirements.frequency: One-time establishment; annual membership review

requirements.requirement: Business unit heads must formally contribute technology requirements, risk concerns, and operational feedback to the CFTC on a structured basis, ensuring the committee's oversight of the strategic technology plan reflects real business needs and not solely IT-driven priorities.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.7(a) requires the committee to oversee 'effective implementation of the strategic technology plan.' Effective implementation can only be assessed if business units are actively surfacing adoption barriers, capacity issues, and operational impact — not merely receiving directives from technology functions.
requirements.owner: Head of each Major Business Unit (Retail Banking, Treasury, Operations, etc.)
requirements.frequency: Quarterly (minimum); per-event for material technology changes

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal policy deviation register visible to business stakeholders, capturing all technology policy deviations requested by business units, the associated risk assessments, CFTC approval decisions, and whether the deviation was classified as material and escalated to the board.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.7(c) requires the committee to 'approve any deviation from technology-related policies after having carefully considered a robust assessment of related risks' and mandates that 'material deviations shall be reported to the board.' Business units must have a governed channel to request and track deviations rather than operating outside approved policy boundaries.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (per deviation event); quarterly register review

requirements.requirement: Define and document the criteria for classifying a technology policy deviation as 'material,' including thresholds based on risk exposure, customer impact, regulatory relevance, financial value, and duration of deviation. These criteria must be approved by the CFTC and reviewed annually.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text distinguishes between deviations and 'material deviations' that must be reported to the board, but does not define materiality. Without explicit, pre-agreed criteria, classification will be inconsistent, potentially allowing significant deviations to bypass board-level scrutiny.
requirements.frequency: Annual definition review; applied per-event

requirements.requirement: Embed CFTC oversight of the strategic technology plan into the institution's annual planning cycle, ensuring the committee formally reviews and endorses technology budget allocations, multi-year roadmaps, and technology policy updates before submission to the board.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.7(a) assigns the committee responsibility to 'oversee the formulation and effective implementation of the strategic technology plan and associated technology policies and procedures.' Linking this oversight to the budget and planning cycle ensures that strategic decisions are made with committee-level cross-functional input rather than retrospectively reviewed.
requirements.frequency: Annual (strategic planning cycle); semi-annual progress reviews

technology.summary: Technology teams must build and maintain the informational infrastructure that enables the CFTC to exercise meaningful oversight — including consolidated dashboards, structured reporting packages, and a governed exception management workflow. The CTO and CISO must ensure that committee members receive accurate, timely, and comprehensible technology intelligence to support board-level escalation and policy deviation decisions. Technology governance artefacts such as the strategic technology plan, policy documents, and deviation logs must be version-controlled, accessible, and audit-ready.

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a consolidated Technology Governance Reporting Package (TGRP) delivered to the CFTC at each scheduled meeting. The TGRP must cover: strategic technology plan progress, key technology risk indicators (KTRIs), significant incidents, deviation requests pending approval, and status of previously approved deviations.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.7(b) requires the committee to 'provide timely updates to the board on key technology matters.' The committee can only fulfil this obligation if it receives structured, accurate inputs from the technology function. Ad hoc or verbal-only reporting introduces gaps and is not defensible under BNM examination.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly (minimum); ad hoc for urgent matters

requirements.requirement: Implement a Technology Policy Deviation Management System (or formally governed workflow within existing GRC tooling) to intake, assess, approve, and track all deviations from technology-related policies. The system must capture the requestor, affected policy, risk assessment rationale, CFTC approval decision, compensating controls, deviation duration, and materiality classification.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.7(c) mandates that deviations be approved only 'after having carefully considered a robust assessment of related risks.' A structured system ensures the assessment is documented, auditable, and consistently applied — replacing informal or email-based exception handling that cannot demonstrate 'careful consideration' to BNM examiners.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of IT Governance
requirements.frequency: Continuous (system availability); quarterly deviation log review

requirements.requirement: Maintain a version-controlled Strategic Technology Plan (STP) document and associated technology policies and procedures repository, with change history, ownership metadata, review dates, and approval records. Access must be available to all CFTC members and auditable by BNM upon request.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.7(a) assigns the committee oversight of 'formulation and effective implementation of the strategic technology plan and associated technology policies and procedures.' Without a single, authoritative, version-controlled repository, committee oversight is undermined by document sprawl and version inconsistency.
requirements.frequency: Continuous maintenance; formal annual review cycle

requirements.requirement: Establish a technology risk assessment methodology and templates specifically for evaluating policy deviation requests, incorporating impact dimensions such as cybersecurity risk, operational resilience, regulatory compliance, and customer data protection. Assessments must be completed by qualified risk and technology personnel before CFTC review.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.7(c) explicitly requires that deviation approvals follow 'a robust assessment of related risks.' A standardised methodology prevents superficial assessments and ensures that risk dimensions relevant to BNM's RMiT framework — particularly cyber and operational risks — are consistently evaluated.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (per deviation request); methodology reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Configure automated alerting and escalation triggers within IT operations and GRC systems to notify the CFTC secretariat of events requiring urgent committee attention between scheduled meetings (e.g., critical incidents, emergency policy deviations, significant technology control failures), enabling 'timely updates to the board' as required by clause 8.7(b).
requirements.rationale: The word 'timely' in clause 8.7(b) implies that board updates must not be deferred to scheduled meeting cycles when urgent technology matters arise. Automated triggers ensure that the committee is mobilised promptly and can escalate to the board within required timeframes, avoiding regulatory findings related to delayed disclosure.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (system monitoring); trigger thresholds reviewed semi-annually

governance.summary: The institution must establish a formal committee charter, defined operating cadence, documented escalation pathways to the board, and a robust secretariat function to ensure the CFTC operates with the discipline and accountability expected by BNM. Governance structures must ensure that committee decisions — particularly policy deviation approvals — are properly minuted, traceable, and subject to independent review. The board must receive structured, timely updates and must be notified of all material deviations in a manner that enables meaningful oversight rather than rubber-stamp ratification.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formal CFTC Terms of Reference (ToR) / Charter approved by the board, specifying: committee purpose and mandate, member eligibility criteria (with explicit reference to cyber, technology, and major business unit seniority requirements), quorum requirements, chairperson designation, meeting frequency, decision-making authority, and escalation procedures to the board.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates that senior management 'must establish' the committee with defined membership and responsibilities. A board-approved charter formalises the mandate, prevents ad hoc dilution of membership standards, and provides BNM examiners with documented evidence that the requirement has been institutionalised rather than informally observed.
requirements.frequency: One-time establishment; biennial charter review (or upon material structural changes)

requirements.requirement: Establish a defined cadence for CFTC meetings (minimum quarterly) with a documented agenda framework aligned to the committee's three mandated responsibilities under clause 8.7(a), (b), and (c). All meetings must be formally minuted, with minutes reviewed and approved at the subsequent meeting and retained for a minimum of seven years.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.7 assigns three specific ongoing responsibilities to the committee: oversight of the strategic technology plan, timely board updates, and governance of policy deviations. A structured meeting cadence with formal minutes provides the audit trail required to demonstrate continuous fulfilment of these obligations to BNM.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly meetings (minimum); minutes produced within 14 days of each meeting

requirements.requirement: Define and operationalise a formal Board Reporting Protocol for technology matters, specifying: the format and content of technology updates to the board, the frequency of scheduled reporting (minimum quarterly), the maximum permissible time between a CFTC decision on a material deviation and board notification, and the designated board committee (e.g., Board Risk Committee or Board Audit Committee) to receive these updates.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.7(b) requires the committee to 'provide timely updates to the board on key technology matters' and clause 8.7(c) mandates that 'material deviations shall be reported to the board.' Without a defined protocol, 'timely' is ambiguous and material deviations may be reported inconsistently, creating regulatory exposure.
requirements.frequency: Protocol established once; applied continuously; reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Implement an independent annual effectiveness review of the CFTC, assessing whether the committee's composition continues to meet the cross-functional membership requirements, whether its oversight of the strategic technology plan is substantive, whether board reporting is timely and adequate, and whether the deviation approval process includes genuinely robust risk assessments.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.7 establishes substantive obligations — 'effective implementation,' 'timely updates,' and 'robust assessment' — that go beyond mere formal existence of the committee. An annual effectiveness review, conducted or overseen independently (e.g., by Internal Audit or an external reviewer), provides assurance that the committee is functioning as intended and not as a paper governance construct.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Annual

requirements.requirement: Maintain a CFTC Decisions and Deviations Register as a permanent governance record, capturing all committee resolutions, policy deviation approvals (including the risk assessment rationale), materiality determinations, board escalation dates, and compensating control commitments. This register must be available to Internal Audit, the Board Risk Committee, and BNM upon request.
requirements.rationale: Clause 8.7(c) requires the committee to approve deviations only 'after having carefully considered a robust assessment of related risks' and to report material deviations to the board. A permanent, auditable register is the primary evidence artefact demonstrating that these procedural safeguards were applied consistently and that the board was appropriately informed.
requirements.frequency: Continuous maintenance; quarterly reconciliation with GRC system records

9.1.title: TRMF Integration with Enterprise Risk Management

business.summary: Failure to embed the TRMF within the ERM exposes the institution to siloed risk management, where technology risks are assessed in isolation and not reflected in the institution's aggregate risk profile presented to the Board. This creates blind spots in enterprise-wide risk decision-making, potentially resulting in misallocation of capital, undetected risk concentrations, and regulatory sanctions from BNM for non-compliance with RMiT. Operationally, business units may pursue digital initiatives without adequate technology risk oversight, undermining the institution's overall risk posture.

requirements.requirement: The institution must formally incorporate technology risk as a distinct risk category within its ERM risk taxonomy, ensuring it is reported alongside credit, market, liquidity, and operational risk in all enterprise risk reporting to senior management and the Board.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates TRMF be 'an integral part' of the ERM, which requires technology risk to be visible at the enterprise level — not confined to IT departments. Without formal inclusion in the ERM taxonomy, technology risk remains invisible in aggregate Board risk reports.
requirements.frequency: Annual review of taxonomy; Quarterly reporting cycle

requirements.requirement: The institution's risk appetite statement (RAS) must include explicit technology risk appetite thresholds and tolerances, aligned with and referenced by both the TRMF and the overarching ERM risk appetite framework.
requirements.rationale: Integration as an 'integral part' of ERM requires that technology risk appetite not be set independently. Misalignment between TRMF risk tolerances and ERM-level appetite statements creates governance gaps and may lead to business units accepting technology risks that breach enterprise-wide limits.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; Ad hoc upon material change in risk profile or business strategy

requirements.requirement: Business line heads must participate in technology risk identification and assessment exercises under the TRMF, ensuring that business-driven digital initiatives and third-party technology dependencies are captured within the enterprise risk register.
requirements.rationale: Effective integration requires two-way information flow: business units surface technology risks arising from operations, while the TRMF feeds assessed technology risks back into the ERM. Excluding business lines creates gaps in risk identification coverage.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Business Line Heads
requirements.frequency: Quarterly risk assessment cycles; Per-event for new product or system launches

requirements.requirement: Technology risk loss events, near-misses, and key risk indicator (KRI) breaches must be escalated to and captured within the enterprise-wide risk incident management process, feeding into ERM reporting and lessons-learned cycles.
requirements.rationale: The requirement for TRMF to be 'integral' to ERM implies that technology risk event data must flow into the enterprise risk event database. Parallel, disconnected incident registers prevent the institution from identifying systemic risk patterns across the enterprise.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Operational Risk
requirements.frequency: Continuous (per-event capture); Monthly aggregation for management reporting

requirements.requirement: Internal audit must include TRMF-ERM integration effectiveness as an explicit audit scope item, assessing whether technology risks identified under the TRMF are being reflected accurately and completely in enterprise risk reports and Board risk disclosures.
requirements.rationale: BNM's use of 'must ensure' places accountability on the institution to actively verify integration is functioning. Without dedicated audit coverage, integration may exist on paper but fail in practice, creating regulatory exposure during BNM supervisory examinations.
requirements.frequency: Annual audit; Ad hoc thematic reviews upon regulatory request or material incidents

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement systems and data architectures that eliminate risk management silos, enabling TRMF-generated risk data — including technology risk assessments, control test results, KRI metrics, and incident records — to flow seamlessly into the institution's enterprise-wide risk management platforms. This requires either integration between dedicated IT GRC tools and the enterprise risk system of record, or a unified platform capable of supporting both TRMF and ERM workflows. All integration points must produce auditable, timestamped data trails sufficient to demonstrate TRMF-ERM connectivity to BNM examiners.

requirements.requirement: Implement a unified or integrated GRC (Governance, Risk and Compliance) platform that connects the TRMF risk register, control library, and KRI dashboards to the enterprise risk management system, enabling real-time or near-real-time consolidation of technology risk data into enterprise risk reports.
requirements.rationale: Integration of TRMF as 'an integral part' of ERM cannot rely on manual data transfers between disconnected systems. A technical integration layer ensures data fidelity, eliminates reconciliation errors, and provides a single source of truth for enterprise risk reporting across both frameworks.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of IT Architecture
requirements.frequency: One-time implementation; Continuous operation; Annual architecture review

requirements.requirement: Establish a standardised technology risk taxonomy and data model — including risk categories, control identifiers, KRI definitions, and severity ratings — that maps directly to the institution's ERM risk taxonomy, ensuring consistent classification and aggregation of technology risks at the enterprise level.
requirements.rationale: Without a common taxonomy, technology risks recorded under the TRMF cannot be meaningfully aggregated with other risk categories in ERM reporting. Inconsistent classification prevents the Board from viewing a coherent, consolidated risk picture across the institution.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Risk / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Initial design at implementation; Annual review aligned to ERM taxonomy refresh

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain automated KRI feeds from technology infrastructure monitoring tools (e.g., SIEM, vulnerability scanners, availability monitoring) into the enterprise risk reporting dashboard, providing real-time visibility of technology risk indicators to risk management and senior leadership.
requirements.rationale: TRMF integration with ERM must be operationally meaningful, not merely documentary. Automated KRI feeds ensure that technology risk signals — such as patch compliance rates, system availability breaches, or security event volumes — are continuously reflected in the enterprise risk posture without manual intervention delays.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of IT Operations
requirements.frequency: Continuous automated feeds; Monthly KRI threshold review; Annual feed coverage audit

requirements.requirement: Implement access controls and audit logging within the GRC integration layer to ensure that all TRMF data contributions to the ERM system are traceable to the originating system, the approving risk owner, and the timestamp of submission, maintaining an immutable audit trail for regulatory examination.
requirements.rationale: BNM examinations require evidence that TRMF-ERM integration is genuine and functioning. Audit logs demonstrating the flow of technology risk data from source systems through to enterprise reports provide this evidence and prevent post-hoc manipulation of risk records.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of IT Governance
requirements.frequency: Continuous logging; Quarterly log integrity review; Annual access control audit

requirements.requirement: Conduct annual technology risk management system integration testing to verify that TRMF data flows — risk assessments, control results, incidents, and KRIs — are correctly and completely reflected in ERM consolidated reports, with test results documented and reviewed by the CRO.
requirements.rationale: System integrations degrade over time due to platform upgrades, configuration changes, and data model drift. Regular integration testing ensures that the technical linkage between TRMF and ERM remains accurate and complete, preventing silent failures that would compromise the integrity of enterprise risk reporting.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Risk / Head of Enterprise Architecture
requirements.frequency: Annual; Ad hoc following material system changes or platform upgrades

governance.summary: The institution must establish explicit governance structures that formally bind the TRMF to the ERM at the policy, committee, and reporting levels — making integration a governed obligation rather than a best-effort practice. This includes Board-level mandates, committee terms of reference that create formal linkage between technology risk governance and enterprise risk governance bodies, and documented policy frameworks that define how TRMF outputs are consumed within ERM processes. BNM's 'must ensure' language places non-delegable accountability on the Board and senior management to actively verify that this integration is functioning, not merely existing on paper.

requirements.requirement: The Board must formally approve a written policy mandating TRMF integration with the ERM, specifying the governance linkages, reporting flows, escalation paths, and accountability structures that constitute 'integral' integration. This policy must be reviewed and reaffirmed annually and following material changes to either framework.
requirements.rationale: BNM's use of 'must ensure' in the verbatim text places the obligation at the institutional level, requiring Board ownership. A Board-approved policy provides the governance foundation for all subordinate integration activities and demonstrates to BNM examiners that the institution has made a deliberate, documented commitment to TRMF-ERM integration.
requirements.owner: Board of Directors / Board Risk Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Annual review and reaffirmation; Ad hoc upon material framework changes

requirements.requirement: The Terms of Reference (ToR) of both the Board Risk Committee (BRC) and the Management Risk Committee (MRC) must explicitly include technology risk oversight as a standing agenda item, with defined responsibilities for receiving and challenging TRMF outputs as part of enterprise risk deliberations.
requirements.rationale: Governance integration requires that the bodies responsible for ERM oversight formally own technology risk review. Without explicit ToR provisions, technology risk may be delegated entirely to an IT Steering Committee without enterprise-level challenge, defeating the 'integral part' requirement of the clause.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee Chairman / Company Secretary
requirements.frequency: ToR review: Annual; Standing agenda item: every committee meeting (typically Quarterly)

requirements.requirement: The Chief Risk Officer must produce a consolidated enterprise risk report — submitted to the Board Risk Committee no less than quarterly — that integrates technology risk assessments, KRI status, material incidents, and control effectiveness results from the TRMF alongside all other enterprise risk categories in a single coherent risk view.
requirements.rationale: Integration of TRMF into ERM must manifest in the institution's primary risk communication to the Board. A consolidated report that includes technology risk alongside credit, market, and operational risk demonstrates functional integration and gives the Board a holistic risk picture as intended by the clause.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly consolidated report; Monthly management-level summary

requirements.requirement: A formal TRMF-ERM Integration Charter must be documented and maintained, defining the roles and responsibilities of the CRO, CTO, CISO, and business line heads in maintaining the integration, including explicit RACI assignments for risk identification, assessment, reporting, and escalation across both frameworks.
requirements.rationale: Without clearly assigned responsibilities, the integration between TRMF and ERM will be inconsistently executed, with gaps arising at the boundaries between technology and risk management functions. A RACI-backed charter ensures accountability is unambiguous and auditable.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Technology Officer (CTO)
requirements.frequency: Annual review; Ad hoc upon organisational restructuring or role changes

requirements.requirement: The institution must conduct an annual self-assessment of TRMF-ERM integration effectiveness, using a structured maturity or compliance assessment against the requirements of RMiT 9.1, with findings reported to the Board Risk Committee and remediation actions tracked to closure.
requirements.rationale: BNM's 'must ensure' language requires the institution to actively verify — not merely assert — that integration is functioning. An annual self-assessment provides the feedback mechanism through which the Board discharges its obligation to ensure integration, and creates documented evidence for BNM supervisory review.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Regulatory Compliance
requirements.frequency: Annual; Results submitted to BRC within Q1 of each calendar year

business.summary: Clause 9.2 requires every financial institution to maintain a comprehensive Technology Risk Management Framework (TRMF) that spans risk definition, ownership, classification, controls, and incident response. Failure to comply exposes the institution to regulatory sanctions under the Financial Services Act 2013 and risks uncontrolled technology failures disrupting customers and business operations. The breadth of sub-clauses (a)–(k) signals BNM's expectation that TRMF compliance is institution-wide, not confined to IT departments.

requirements.requirement: Formally adopt and communicate an institution-wide definition of technology risk that is embedded in all relevant policies, training materials, and risk appetite statements, ensuring all business units operate from a common understanding.
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (a) mandates a 'clear definition of technology risk'. Without a shared, operationalised definition, business lines may underreport or mislabel technology risk events, leading to gaps in the institution's risk profile and misstated risk appetite.
requirements.frequency: Reviewed Annually; updated upon material changes to the institution's technology landscape or BNM guidance

requirements.requirement: Classify all information assets and systems by business criticality, assigning a formal risk tier (e.g., Critical, High, Medium, Low) that directly informs recovery time objectives, investment prioritisation, and business continuity planning.
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (d) requires 'risk classification of all information assets/systems based on criticality'. Business units must own this classification for their respective systems to ensure continuity plans and technology investments are commensurate with business impact.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO), in collaboration with business line heads
requirements.frequency: Initial classification upon deployment; reviewed Annually and upon significant system changes

requirements.requirement: Identify, document, and maintain a register of all critical third-party service providers and their connected parties that support delivery of critical technology functions, including an assessment of dependency risk and contractual resilience obligations.
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (i) explicitly requires identification of 'critical third party service providers and their connected parties'. Third-party failures (e.g., cloud outages, outsourced data centre disruptions) can cascade into operational incidents, making vendor dependency mapping a business-critical activity.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Head of Vendor Management
requirements.frequency: Register maintained Continuously; formal review Annually or upon onboarding/offboarding of critical vendors

requirements.requirement: Conduct annual scenario analysis exercises simulating severe conditions (e.g., simultaneous failure of critical systems, major cyber incident) to validate the institution's capacity and readiness to resume critical business functions within defined recovery objectives.
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (j) mandates scenario analysis 'to strengthen capacity and readiness to resume critical systems under severe conditions'. Without validated scenarios, recovery plans remain untested, and the institution cannot demonstrate to BNM or its Board that it can withstand extreme but plausible disruption events.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Chief Operating Officer (COO)
requirements.frequency: At minimum Annually; additionally triggered by major infrastructure changes or following material incidents

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain customer-facing incident management policies and procedures that define escalation paths, customer communication protocols, and service restoration targets to minimise the impact of any service disruption on customers.
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (k) requires 'effective incident management policies and procedures to minimise the impact of any service disruption on the financial institution and its customers'. Business continuity from the customer perspective—communications, SLA management, and regulatory notification—must be explicitly addressed alongside technical recovery.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Head of Customer Operations
requirements.frequency: Policies reviewed Annually; procedures tested Semi-annually via simulation exercises

technology.summary: Technology teams are responsible for implementing the technical systems, controls, and methodologies that operationalise the TRMF across sub-clauses (c) through (k). This includes asset discovery and classification tooling, risk measurement platforms, automated monitoring, a maintained risk information system, and technically sound incident response procedures. The scope is extensive and requires integration across security operations, IT architecture, and vendor management functions.

requirements.requirement: Implement and maintain a comprehensive technology asset inventory system that catalogues all information assets and systems, records their criticality classification tier, maps data flows and interdependencies, and supports automated discovery to prevent shadow IT from remaining unclassified.
requirements.rationale: Sub-clauses (d) and (i) require risk classification of all assets and identification of key resource interdependencies. An accurate, continuously updated asset inventory is the foundational technical prerequisite for all downstream risk measurement, control, and monitoring activities.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of IT Architecture
requirements.frequency: Automated discovery runs Continuously; manual reconciliation Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Develop and document formal risk measurement and assessment methodologies (e.g., qualitative likelihood/impact matrices, quantitative loss exposure models, threat modelling for new system deployments) applicable across different asset classes and risk categories, including emerging technologies as referenced in Appendix 9.
requirements.rationale: Sub-clauses (c) and (e) require identification of technology risks and defined risk measurement methodologies. Without standardised, repeatable measurement approaches—particularly for emerging technologies such as AI/ML, open-source components, and cloud-native services—risk assessments will be inconsistent and incomparable across the portfolio.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Technology Risk
requirements.frequency: Methodology framework reviewed Annually; individual risk assessments conducted per project lifecycle and upon material changes

requirements.requirement: Deploy and operate a Security Operations Centre (SOC) capability with continuous 24x7 monitoring tools (SIEM, anomaly detection, vulnerability scanners) capable of detecting material technology risks in real time, with defined alerting thresholds, escalation workflows, and mean-time-to-detect (MTTD) targets.
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (g) mandates 'continuous monitoring to timely detect and address any material risks'. A reactive, periodic monitoring model is insufficient; automated, always-on detection is required to meet the 'timely' detection expectation, particularly for cyber threats and system degradation events.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Security Operations
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; tooling reviewed and tuned Quarterly; capability assessed Annually

requirements.requirement: Implement and maintain an integrated Technology Risk Information System (TRIS) — a GRC platform or equivalent — that consolidates the institution's risk profile, asset classifications, risk assessments, control statuses, and incident records into a single source of truth, ensuring the technology risk profile remains accurate and up to date at all times.
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (h) explicitly requires an 'effective information system to ensure the technology risk profile remains accurate and up to date'. Manual, spreadsheet-based approaches are operationally fragile and cannot support real-time accuracy at enterprise scale, particularly for BNM examination readiness.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: System maintained Continuously; data quality audits Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Design, implement, and regularly test technical incident response playbooks covering detection, containment, eradication, recovery, and post-incident review phases for all critical system categories, with defined Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs) validated through disaster recovery (DR) exercises.
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (k) requires effective incident management to restore affected services 'to a secure and stable state as quickly as possible'. Technical playbooks operationalise this requirement; without DR testing against defined RTOs/RPOs, recovery timelines under real incident conditions cannot be assured.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of IT Operations
requirements.frequency: Playbooks reviewed Annually; full DR exercise conducted at minimum Annually; tabletop exercises Semi-annually

governance.summary: Governance requirements under Clause 9.2 address the formal structures, oversight mechanisms, and accountability frameworks that ensure the TRMF is not merely a documented artefact but an actively governed, board-endorsed programme. The eleven mandatory elements of the TRMF collectively require Board-level ownership, defined management responsibilities across all functions, and regular reporting cycles that keep governance bodies informed of the institution's evolving technology risk profile.

requirements.requirement: Obtain formal Board of Directors approval for the TRMF document that explicitly addresses all eleven elements under sub-clauses (a)–(k), with documented evidence that the Board has reviewed and endorsed the framework, the technology risk definition, and the institution's overall approach to technology risk management.
requirements.rationale: The clause's 'S' (Standard) marker and its positioning under senior governance obligations imply Board-level accountability for the TRMF's existence and adequacy. Board endorsement establishes institutional authority for the framework and demonstrates to BNM that technology risk governance originates at the highest level.
requirements.owner: Board of Directors / Board Risk Committee (BRC) Chairperson
requirements.frequency: Initial approval upon TRMF establishment; re-approval upon material revisions; minimum Annual review

requirements.requirement: Establish and document a TRMF governance structure that assigns clear technology risk responsibilities at Board, senior management, business line, and technology function levels, with defined reporting lines, escalation paths, and accountability matrices (e.g., RACI) covering all eleven TRMF elements.
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (b) requires 'clear responsibilities assigned for the management of technology risk at different levels and across functions, with appropriate governance and reporting arrangements'. Ambiguity in ownership is the most common cause of governance gaps identified in BNM examinations; a formal RACI eliminates role confusion.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Executive Officer (CEO)
requirements.frequency: RACI reviewed Annually and upon organisational restructuring or material role changes

requirements.requirement: Establish a regular technology risk reporting cycle that provides the Board Risk Committee and Senior Management Committee with a consolidated technology risk dashboard — covering risk profile status, emerging technology risks, critical third-party exposures, open control gaps, and material incidents — on at least a quarterly basis.
requirements.rationale: Sub-clauses (c), (g), (h), and (i) collectively require ongoing identification, monitoring, and accurate profiling of technology risks. Governance bodies cannot fulfil their oversight responsibilities without structured, periodic management information; the reporting cycle operationalises their oversight obligations.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Technology Risk
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting to Board Risk Committee; Monthly reporting to Senior Management

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Emerging Technology Governance Policy that requires all adoption decisions for new or emerging technologies (as referenced in Appendix 9) to pass through a technology risk assessment gate — reviewed by the Technology Risk function and approved by Senior Management — before procurement or deployment.
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (c) specifically calls out 'risks from the adoption of new or emerging technology' and cross-references Appendix 9. Without a governance gate, emerging technology adoption (e.g., generative AI, quantum cryptography, decentralised finance components) may bypass risk controls and introduce unmanaged exposure.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Technology Officer (CTO)
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed Annually; gate applied Per-event (each new technology adoption decision)

requirements.requirement: Mandate and document an Annual TRMF Effectiveness Review conducted by an independent internal audit or second-line assurance function, assessing whether all eleven sub-clause requirements are implemented, operating effectively, and aligned with the institution's current risk profile and BNM's latest regulatory guidance.
requirements.rationale: The 'S' marker signals that BNM regards the completeness of the TRMF as a standard compliance expectation. An independent annual review provides the Board with assurance that the TRMF is not merely documented but genuinely effective, and identifies gaps before they surface in BNM examination findings.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor / Head of Internal Audit
requirements.frequency: Annually; additionally triggered by material regulatory changes or significant incidents that expose TRMF gaps

business.summary: Clause 9.3 requires financial institutions to structurally embed technology risk oversight into the organisation as a standalone, independent function — not subordinated to IT or business lines it is meant to oversee. Failure to establish this independence risks regulatory censure, undetected technology exposures cascading into operational losses, and inadequate escalation of critical risks to decision-makers. The clause directly guards against conflict-of-interest scenarios where technology teams self-assess their own risks without independent challenge.

requirements.requirement: Formally establish a dedicated, enterprise-wide Technology Risk Management function with a mandate, budget, and headcount that is organisationally independent from the IT division and business lines it oversees.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates an 'independent enterprise-wide' function. Independence is structurally compromised if the function sits within IT or is funded/directed by the business unit it reviews. Without structural separation, risk assessments are subject to influence, undermining the function's credibility with BNM and the Board.
requirements.frequency: One-time establishment; annual mandate and resource review

requirements.requirement: Define and document the institution's risk tolerance thresholds for technology risk, and operationalise a process by which the Technology Risk Management function identifies critical technology issues that breach or approach those thresholds and triggers escalation to senior management.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.3(b) requires the function to ensure issues 'that may have an impact on the financial institution's risk tolerance are adequately deliberated or escalated in a timely manner.' Without formalised tolerance metrics, the function cannot objectively determine when escalation is warranted, creating regulatory exposure.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; risk tolerance thresholds reviewed annually or upon material change

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal advisory engagement model between the Technology Risk Management function and critical technology project sponsors, ensuring risk input is provided at project initiation, key milestones, and go-live decision gates.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.3(b) explicitly assigns responsibility for 'advising on critical technology projects.' Without defined engagement points in the project lifecycle, risk advice becomes reactive and ad hoc, increasing the probability of material technology failures reaching production.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Per critical project lifecycle event (initiation, milestone review, go-live)

requirements.requirement: Formalise the function's authority to commission, receive, and independently assess third-party technology risk reports (e.g., external auditors, IT assessment firms, cloud provider audits), and to provide opinion to the Board and senior management without interference from assessed parties.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.3(c) requires 'independent views to the board and senior management on third party assessments.' This independence is meaningless unless the function has direct, unfiltered access to third-party outputs and a protected channel to report its views upward.
requirements.frequency: Per third-party assessment event; at minimum annually for all material vendors

technology.summary: The Technology Risk Management function requires dedicated tooling, data access, and technical capability to independently implement and monitor the TRMF and CRF across the institution's technology estate. Technology teams must provision the function with read-access to risk-relevant telemetry, system inventories, and control monitoring outputs without routing through the IT division being assessed. The function must also maintain a register of critical technology projects and a cyber resilience assessment capability.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and maintain a Governance, Risk and Compliance (GRC) platform configured to operationalise the TRMF and CRF control libraries, mapping controls to assets, owners, and evidence, and generating real-time compliance posture dashboards accessible to the Technology Risk Management function independently of IT operations.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.3(a) requires the function to be 'responsible for implementing the TRMF and CRF.' Manual or spreadsheet-based tracking cannot support enterprise-wide continuous implementation at the scale of a regulated financial institution. A GRC platform ensures the function can monitor control status without dependency on IT-produced reports.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; platform review and reconfiguration annually or upon framework updates

requirements.requirement: Maintain a centralised, authoritative register of all critical technology projects, including project classification criteria, risk ratings, advisory input dates, and escalation history. The register must be owned by the Technology Risk Management function and not the project delivery team.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.3(b) requires the function to advise on 'critical technology projects.' A structured register with defined classification criteria is a prerequisite for identifying which projects require engagement and evidencing that advisory obligations have been fulfilled — both for internal governance and BNM examination.
requirements.frequency: Continuously updated; reviewed at each project milestone and monthly for completeness

requirements.requirement: Implement a technology risk escalation workflow system (integrated with or separate from the GRC platform) that captures risk events, maps them against defined risk tolerance thresholds, triggers escalation notifications to senior management, and maintains an auditable escalation log with timestamps and disposition records.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.3(b) requires issues to be 'adequately deliberated or escalated in a timely manner.' Without a systemised workflow, escalation evidence is anecdotal and cannot be demonstrated to BNM examiners. Timely escalation requires automated alerting, not manual tracking.
requirements.owner: Head of Technology Risk / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; workflow logic reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Provision the Technology Risk Management function with direct, read-only access to security telemetry (SIEM, vulnerability scanners, patch management dashboards, cloud configuration tools) to support independent cyber resilience assessment under the CRF, without reliance on IT operations to produce or filter the data.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.3(a) holds the function responsible for implementing the CRF. Independent implementation requires independent visibility. If the function relies on IT-curated outputs, it cannot provide unbiased assurance to the Board as required by clause 9.3(c).
requirements.frequency: Access provisioned at function establishment; access rights reviewed semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a third-party technology risk assessment repository that consolidates all external audit reports, vendor assessment outputs (e.g., SOC 2, ISAE 3402, cloud shared responsibility matrices), and the Technology Risk Management function's independent opinions, with version control and Board reporting linkage.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.3(c) requires the function to provide 'independent views to the board and senior management on third party assessments, where necessary.' A consolidated repository ensures no third-party assessment is overlooked, enables trend analysis across vendors, and produces a structured artefact for Board reporting.
requirements.owner: Head of Technology Risk / Head of Vendor Risk
requirements.frequency: Updated per assessment receipt; repository reviewed quarterly for completeness

governance.summary: Clause 9.3 imposes structural governance obligations: the Technology Risk Management function must have a formal charter articulating its independence, defined reporting lines to the Board or its Risk Committee, and protected escalation channels that bypass the IT division when assessing it. Governance frameworks must enshrine the function's right to provide unfiltered opinions on third-party assessments and mandate timely deliberation of escalated technology risk issues at Board level. Without these structural governance elements, the 'independence' requirement of the clause is nominal rather than real.

requirements.requirement: Issue and maintain a Board-approved Technology Risk Management Function Charter that explicitly defines the function's independence, scope of authority (enterprise-wide), responsibilities under clauses 9.3(a)-(c), escalation rights, and reporting lines — including a direct reporting line to the Board Risk Committee or equivalent, independent of the CIO/CTO.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates an 'independent' function with enterprise-wide scope. Independence must be codified in a governance instrument approved at Board level; without a charter, the function's independence is not enforceable and will not withstand BNM supervisory scrutiny.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee Chairman / Company Secretary
requirements.frequency: Established at function inception; reviewed and reaffirmed annually by the Board

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Technology Risk Committee (or expand the mandate of an existing Risk Committee) that convenes at minimum quarterly, includes the Head of Technology Risk as a standing member, and has a defined agenda item for deliberation of critical technology risk issues escalated under clause 9.3(b), with minutes evidencing decisions and risk disposition.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.3(b) requires critical issues to be 'adequately deliberated or escalated in a timely manner.' Deliberation requires a governance forum with defined membership, quorum, and decision authority. Without a formal committee structure, escalation lands without accountable recipients and decisions are undocumented.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Board Risk Committee
requirements.frequency: Quarterly meetings minimum; extraordinary sessions upon material risk events

requirements.requirement: Define and embed in the TRMF a formal critical technology project classification policy, specifying criteria (e.g., project value thresholds, systemic impact, new technology adoption, third-party dependency) that trigger mandatory engagement from the Technology Risk Management function as required by clause 9.3(b).
requirements.rationale: The function's advisory obligation under clause 9.3(b) applies specifically to 'critical technology projects.' Without defined classification criteria, the scope of advisory engagement is arbitrary and cannot be consistently evidenced to BNM. Clear criteria also protect the function from scope creep or exclusion.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Information Officer (CIO)
requirements.frequency: Policy established at TRMF implementation; reviewed annually or upon material organisational change

requirements.requirement: Establish a protected, documented channel for the Technology Risk Management function to submit independent opinions on third-party assessments directly to the Board Risk Committee or senior management, with a policy prohibition on pre-review, editing, or suppression of those opinions by assessed parties or their sponsors.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.3(c) requires 'independent views' to the Board and senior management. Independence of views is operationally meaningless unless the reporting channel is structurally protected. Allowing IT or business sponsors to filter the function's opinions before Board submission negates the independence requirement.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee Chairman / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Per third-party assessment requiring independent opinion; channel policy reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Conduct an annual independent assessment (by internal audit or an external party) of the Technology Risk Management function's organisational independence, adequacy of resources, and fulfilment of its mandate under clauses 9.3(a)-(c), with findings reported directly to the Board Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: BNM's 'S' marker designation signals that the requirement is non-negotiable and will be examined during supervisory engagements. An annual independent review provides the Board with assurance that the function operates as mandated and generates documented evidence for regulatory examination. Without periodic review, structural independence may erode over time without detection.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor / Board Audit Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Annual; ad hoc if material changes occur to the function's structure, leadership, or resources

9.4.title: Designation of Chief Information Security Officer

business.summary: Failure to designate a suitably empowered and independent CISO exposes the institution to unmanaged technology and cybersecurity risks that may result in regulatory sanctions, reputational damage, and material financial loss. The CISO role is the institutional anchor for translating technical risk posture into board-level strategic decisions, making it a critical business continuity and risk governance function. Without this role, the board and senior management are effectively operating without visibility into current and emerging technology threats that could materially affect the institution's risk profile.

requirements.requirement: Formally designate a CISO — by whatever name called — through a documented board resolution or equivalent instrument, with an explicit mandate covering the technology risk management function of the financial institution.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates that 'a financial institution must designate a Chief Information Security Officer' with a defined scope. Without formal designation, the institution has no accountable owner for technology risk management, rendering all downstream cybersecurity and risk activities ungoverned and potentially inadmissible as compliance evidence.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (upon establishment or change of role); reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a CISO function with sufficient budgetary resources, headcount, and operational support to fulfil its mandate, including authority to engage external specialists and tools.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires that 'the financial institution must ensure that the CISO has sufficient authority, independence and resources.' Underfunding the CISO function is a direct compliance breach and operationally undermines the institution's ability to detect, assess, and respond to technology risks.
requirements.frequency: Annual (budget cycle); monitored quarterly

requirements.requirement: Structure the CISO role to be operationally independent from day-to-day technology operations, with a reporting line that prevents conflicts of interest (e.g., reporting to CRO or CEO, not CTO).
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.4(a) requires the CISO to 'be independent from day-to-day technology operations.' A CISO embedded within the IT operations function cannot objectively challenge operational decisions or escalate risks without organisational conflict, defeating the purpose of the role.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; formally reviewed at each annual organisational review

requirements.requirement: Establish a structured, recurring mechanism for the CISO to brief the board and senior management on current and emerging technology risks, including their potential impact on the institution's risk profile.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.4(b) mandates that the CISO 'apprise board and senior management of current and emerging technology risks which could potentially affect the financial institution's risk profile.' Without a formalised briefing cadence and format, material risks may not reach decision-makers in time to be acted upon.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly minimum; ad hoc upon material risk events

requirements.requirement: Define and document the CISO's mandate over third-party risk management, ensuring the CISO has visibility and authority over technology risks arising from vendor, outsourcing, and cloud arrangements.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.4(c) explicitly includes 'third party risk management' within the CISO's required expertise. As financial institutions increasingly rely on third parties for critical technology services, excluding third-party risk from the CISO's remit creates a material blind spot in the institution's technology risk posture.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; formally reviewed annually and upon material third-party engagements

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement the systems, platforms, and data pipelines that enable the CISO to exercise meaningful oversight of technology risk across the institution's entire estate, including cloud, on-premises, and third-party environments. The CISO requires real-time risk visibility tooling, structured risk reporting infrastructure, and third-party risk monitoring capabilities to credibly fulfil the obligations in clause 9.4(b) and 9.4(c). Technology implementations must be designed to support CISO independence by ensuring risk data flows directly to the CISO function without operational team intermediation.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and maintain a Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) or equivalent centralised risk monitoring platform that provides the CISO with independent, real-time visibility into technology risk indicators across all critical systems and network segments.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.4(b) requires the CISO to apprise board and senior management of 'current and emerging technology risks.' Without independent monitoring infrastructure, the CISO is dependent on operational teams to surface risk information, which directly contradicts the independence requirement in clause 9.4(a).
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; technology stack reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Implement a structured board and senior management risk reporting platform (e.g., GRC tool or executive dashboard) that translates technical risk data into quantified, business-relevant risk metrics suitable for non-technical audiences.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.4(b) requires the CISO to brief the board and senior management on technology risk impacts. Raw technical telemetry is unsuitable for board consumption; without a reporting layer that contextualises risk in business terms, the institution fails the substantive intent of this clause.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; dashboard content reviewed before each board/EXCO cycle

requirements.requirement: Establish a third-party technology risk management platform or register that provides the CISO with ongoing visibility into vendor risk posture, contractual obligations, SLA performance, and cybersecurity assessments for all material technology service providers.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.4(c) requires the CISO to have expertise and direct involvement in 'third party risk management.' This expertise must be operationalised through tooling that enables active, evidence-based oversight of third-party technology risks, not merely periodic manual reviews.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; formal assessments at least annually per vendor

requirements.requirement: Implement a skills and competency tracking system for the CISO function that maps the incumbent's certifications, training completions, and continuing professional development against the technical and domain requirements specified in clause 9.4(c) and 9.4(d).
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.4(c) specifies requisite competencies across emerging technologies, audit, governance, risk management, and strategic planning, while clause 9.4(d) mandates appropriate certification. Without a structured tracking mechanism, the institution cannot demonstrate ongoing compliance with these skills requirements to BNM examiners.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; updated upon any certification change or renewal

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain an emerging technology risk horizon-scanning capability, including threat intelligence subscriptions and participation in industry information-sharing forums (e.g., FSA-CERT, FinCERT.my), feeding intelligence directly into the CISO's risk assessment process.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.4(b) specifically requires appraisal of 'emerging technology risks,' and clause 9.4(c) demands skills in 'emerging and core technologies.' A horizon-scanning function ensures the CISO can proactively inform the board of threats before they materialise, fulfilling the forward-looking intent of these sub-clauses.
requirements.owner: CISO / Head of Cyber Threat Intelligence
requirements.frequency: Continuous; formal horizon scan reports quarterly

governance.summary: Governance structures must formalise the CISO's authority, independence, and reporting obligations through documented policies, charters, and board-level mandates that cannot be overridden by operational management. The board retains ultimate accountability for ensuring the CISO has the authority and resources required by clause 9.4, while the Board Risk Committee must maintain active oversight of the CISO's risk briefings and recommendations. Governance frameworks must also ensure ongoing compliance with the competency and certification requirements in clause 9.4(c) and 9.4(d), with evidence maintained for regulatory examination.

requirements.requirement: Issue a formal CISO Charter or Terms of Reference, approved by the board, that defines the role's scope, authority, independence parameters, reporting lines, resources entitlement, and accountability to the board and senior management.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the financial institution to 'ensure that the CISO has sufficient authority, independence and resources.' A board-approved Charter is the primary governance instrument that codifies these entitlements, provides the CISO with standing to challenge operational decisions, and serves as the primary evidence document for BNM regulatory examinations.
requirements.frequency: Issued upon designation; reviewed biennially or upon material organisational change

requirements.requirement: Embed the CISO risk briefing as a standing agenda item in Board Risk Committee meetings and EXCO meetings, with minimum attendance requirements and formal minutes recording the board's deliberations and responses to technology risk disclosures.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.4(b) requires the CISO to 'apprise board and senior management of current and emerging technology risks.' Formalising this as a standing agenda item — rather than an ad hoc item — ensures the obligation is met consistently and creates an auditable record demonstrating that the board actively engages with technology risk information.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee Chairman / Company Secretary
requirements.frequency: Quarterly minimum; ad hoc sessions for material risk events

requirements.requirement: Establish a CISO Competency Framework aligned to clause 9.4(c) requirements, covering emerging and core technologies, audit, governance, risk management, strategic planning, IT and cybersecurity program execution, and third-party risk management; use this framework in recruitment, performance appraisal, and succession planning.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.4(c) prescribes specific competency domains that the CISO must possess. Without a documented competency framework, the institution cannot objectively assess fitness for the role, defend its selection to regulators, or ensure continuity of competency through succession planning.
requirements.owner: Board Nomination and Remuneration Committee / CHRO
requirements.frequency: Framework reviewed annually; applied at each CISO appointment and annual performance review

requirements.requirement: Maintain a CISO certification register documenting the incumbent's current professional certifications (e.g., CISSP, CISM, CRISC, or equivalent recognised by BNM), expiry dates, renewal plans, and evidence of continuing professional education, with oversight by the Board Nomination and Remuneration Committee.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.4(d) requires the CISO to 'be appropriately certified.' A maintained certification register provides documentary evidence for BNM examinations and ensures the institution proactively manages certification renewal, preventing lapses that would constitute a direct non-compliance against a Standard ('S') marker obligation.
requirements.owner: Board Nomination and Remuneration Committee / CISO / CHRO
requirements.frequency: Updated continuously upon any certification change; formally reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Conduct an annual independent assessment of the CISO function's effectiveness, authority, independence, and resource adequacy — commissioned by the Board Risk Committee and carried out by Internal Audit or an external reviewer — with findings reported directly to the board.
requirements.rationale: The clause places the obligation on the 'financial institution' to 'ensure' the CISO's authority, independence, and resources, implying active institutional oversight rather than passive assumption. An independent annual assessment is the governance mechanism that allows the board to fulfil this 'ensure' obligation with evidence and to remediate any gaps before they become regulatory findings.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee / Chief Internal Auditor
requirements.frequency: Annual

business.summary: This clause establishes the CISO as the primary accountable officer for protecting the institution's information assets and technologies, directly linking cybersecurity posture to business continuity and regulatory standing. Failure to comply exposes the institution to regulatory sanctions, reputational damage, and material losses arising from unmitigated technology risk. Business units depend on a functioning CISO mandate to ensure that risk appetite, operational policies, and regulatory obligations remain aligned with evolving security threats.

requirements.requirement: Formally define and document the scope of the institution's information assets and technologies for which the CISO is accountable, covering on-premises systems, cloud environments, third-party integrations, and data repositories.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.5 mandates that information assets and technologies be 'adequately protected'; this is only achievable if the full asset perimeter is known. Undefined scope creates blind spots that translate directly into uninsured operational and reputational risk.
requirements.frequency: Annual review, with updates triggered by material changes to the technology estate

requirements.requirement: Establish a structured CISO advisory reporting cadence to the Board Risk Committee and senior management covering developments in the institution's technology security risk profile in relation to its business strategy and operations, as required under clause 9.5(c).
requirements.rationale: Senior management cannot fulfil their own risk oversight duties unless the CISO systematically surfaces technology risk profile changes tied to business context. Absence of this linkage is a direct non-conformance with clause 9.5(c) and undermines Board-level accountability.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly minimum; ad hoc upon material incidents or risk profile changes

requirements.requirement: Allocate sufficient budget, staffing, and authority to the CISO to enable effective formulation and enforcement of TRMF and CRF policies across all business lines, as implied by the enforcement mandate in clause 9.5(b).
requirements.rationale: Policy formulation and enforcement without commensurate organisational authority and resources is ineffective. Underfunded CISO functions are a systemic compliance gap that regulators identify during on-site examinations.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer / Board Audit and Risk Committee
requirements.frequency: Annual budget cycle; reviewed semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Ensure all business unit heads are briefed on and acknowledge CISO-issued policies derived from the TRMF and CRF, with documented accountability for policy adherence within their respective units per clause 9.5(b).
requirements.rationale: Enforcement of compliance across the institution requires business owners to accept responsibility for policy conformance within their domains. Without this, the CISO's enforcement mandate is confined to the IT function and leaves business-owned technology risks unaddressed.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer / Business Unit Heads
requirements.frequency: Upon policy issuance or material revision; annual reaffirmation

technology.summary: To satisfy the CISO's protection mandate under clause 9.5, technology teams must implement systems that enable asset visibility, policy operationalisation, continuous compliance monitoring, and risk profile analytics. These capabilities are the technical foundation upon which the CISO's formulation, enforcement, and advisory duties rest. Without them, the CISO lacks the instrumentation needed to fulfil regulatory obligations or provide credible risk intelligence to senior management.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and maintain a centralised IT Asset Management (ITAM) and Configuration Management Database (CMDB) that inventories all information assets and technologies within the CISO's protection scope, integrated with vulnerability management and risk classification tooling.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.5 requires that information assets and technologies be 'adequately protected'; adequate protection presupposes complete, accurate asset visibility. An ITAM/CMDB system is the technical prerequisite for every downstream security control.
requirements.frequency: Continuous automated discovery; quarterly reconciliation audits

requirements.requirement: Implement a Policy Management System (PMS) to author, version-control, publish, distribute, and track acknowledgement of TRMF and CRF policies formulated under clause 9.5(a), with automated alerts for policy expiry and review triggers.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.5(a) requires the CISO to formulate appropriate policies; a PMS operationalises policy governance, creates an auditable trail of policy lifecycle events, and supports enforcement by ensuring staff receive and acknowledge current policy versions.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; policy reviews at least annually or upon regulatory updates

requirements.requirement: Deploy a Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) platform integrated with automated compliance control monitoring to detect deviations from TRMF/CRF-mandated technical controls, enabling the CISO to enforce compliance per clause 9.5(b).
requirements.rationale: Enforcement of technology policies at scale requires automated monitoring; manual oversight alone cannot provide the coverage or timeliness needed. SIEM-based compliance monitoring generates the evidence base for enforcement actions and regulatory reporting.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; daily exception triage; monthly compliance dashboards

requirements.requirement: Establish a Technology Security Risk Profiling capability — incorporating threat intelligence feeds, vulnerability scoring, and business impact mapping — to generate the risk profile reporting required by clause 9.5(c) for senior management advisory.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.5(c) explicitly requires the CISO to advise on 'developments in the financial institution's technology security risk profile in relation to its business and operations'; this demands a data-driven risk quantification and visualisation capability rather than ad hoc assessment.
requirements.frequency: Continuous data ingestion; monthly risk profile reports; quarterly senior management presentations

requirements.requirement: Implement a Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC) platform aligned to BNM RMiT requirements to track TRMF and CRF control status, map regulatory obligations to technical controls, and produce evidence artefacts for examination by BNM under clause 9.5(b).
requirements.rationale: Enforcement of compliance with 'technology-related regulatory requirements' as stated in clause 9.5(b) requires a system of record that maps regulatory mandates to implemented controls, tracks remediation of gaps, and retains audit evidence to demonstrate continuous compliance.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; control assessments quarterly; regulatory mapping reviewed annually

governance.summary: Clause 9.5 is fundamentally a governance mandate: it codifies the CISO's authority, accountability, and reporting obligations across the three pillars of policy formulation, compliance enforcement, and senior management advisory. Effective governance structures must institutionalise these duties through formal charters, committee structures, and documented escalation pathways so that the CISO function operates independently of conflicting business pressures. Without these structures, the CISO mandate remains aspirational rather than operationally enforceable.
