requirements.requirement: Formalise the CISO's mandate through a Board-approved CISO Charter that explicitly delineates authority to formulate TRMF and CRF policies, enforce compliance, and provide advisory reports to senior management, directly reflecting the three duties enumerated in clause 9.5(a)-(c).
requirements.rationale: A Board-approved Charter gives the CISO institutional legitimacy and independence. Without it, the CISO's authority to enforce policies under clause 9.5(b) can be contested by business units, undermining the regulatory intent of the clause.
requirements.owner: Board of Directors / Board Risk Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Charter approved at CISO appointment; reviewed biennially or upon material organisational change

requirements.requirement: Establish a TRMF and CRF Policy Formulation and Approval process with defined drafting, stakeholder consultation, legal/compliance review, and Board or senior management approval stages, ensuring the CISO's policy formulation duty under clause 9.5(a) is systematic and auditable.
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.5(a) requires the CISO to formulate 'appropriate policies for the effective implementation of TRMF and CRF'; a defined process ensures policies are complete, legally sound, operationally feasible, and traceable to regulatory requirements rather than informally produced.
requirements.owner: CISO / Chief Risk Officer / Company Secretary
requirements.frequency: Process executed upon new policy need or material regulatory change; reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Implement a Technology Compliance Enforcement Framework that grants the CISO authority to issue binding directives, escalate persistent non-compliance to the Board Risk Committee, and impose remediation timelines on business and technology units per clause 9.5(b).
requirements.rationale: Enforcement without escalation authority is ineffective; clause 9.5(b) requires the CISO to 'enforce compliance' which implies a structured mechanism for consequence management. This framework closes the gap between policy issuance and actual adherence.
requirements.frequency: Framework reviewed annually; enforcement actions executed on a continuous and per-incident basis

requirements.requirement: Institutionalise a Technology Risk Advisory reporting structure whereby the CISO presents a Technology Security Risk Profile report to the Board Risk Committee and Executive Management Committee at least quarterly, covering risk profile developments relative to business strategy and operations as required by clause 9.5(c).
requirements.rationale: Clause 9.5(c) explicitly requires the CISO to advise senior management on 'developments in the financial institution's technology security risk profile in relation to its business and operations'; a standing agenda item on committee calendars ensures this advisory duty is fulfilled consistently and is evidenced for regulatory examination.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly standing agenda item; ad hoc upon material incidents or threat landscape changes

requirements.requirement: Maintain a Regulatory Obligations Register mapping all technology-related BNM regulatory requirements (including RMiT, CRF, and related circulars) to CISO-owned controls and policies, reviewed whenever BNM issues new guidance, to support enforcement under clause 9.5(b).
requirements.rationale: The CISO cannot enforce compliance with 'technology-related regulatory requirements' as mandated by clause 9.5(b) without a maintained register of what those requirements are and which internal controls address them. This register also provides the primary evidence artefact during BNM supervisory examinations.
requirements.owner: CISO / Head of Compliance / Chief Risk Officer
requirements.frequency: Register reviewed semi-annually; updated within 30 days of any new BNM issuance

business.summary: Without structured project governance calibrated to project risk and complexity, financial institutions expose themselves to cost overruns, failed deliveries, and unmanaged technology risks that can disrupt core banking operations or breach regulatory obligations. BNM requires that oversight arrangements are proportionate — lightweight governance for low-risk projects, rigorous multi-layer oversight for high-risk or complex initiatives. Failure to comply signals systemic weakness in strategic execution capability and may attract supervisory scrutiny or enforcement.

requirements.requirement: Establish a risk and complexity tiering model for all technology projects that determines the level of governance applied, with at least three tiers (e.g., Standard, Significant, Critical) mapped to predefined risk and complexity thresholds.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates governance 'commensurate with the risk and complexity' of projects. A tiering model operationalises this proportionality requirement, ensuring governance overhead does not impede low-risk delivery while providing rigour where it is needed most.
requirements.frequency: Defined at project initiation; reviewed at each major phase gate

requirements.requirement: Define and publish a Technology Project Governance Policy that mandates the minimum oversight roles and responsibilities required at each project tier, including explicit accountabilities for project sponsors, business owners, and senior user representatives.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires 'project oversight roles and responsibilities' to be established. Without a formal policy, accountability gaps emerge, particularly in cross-functional technology projects where business and IT ownership boundaries are contested.
requirements.frequency: Policy issued at establishment; reviewed annually and upon material project portfolio changes

requirements.requirement: Ensure all technology projects classified as Significant or Critical have a formally appointed senior business sponsor at Head of Department level or above, with documented accountability for benefits realisation and escalation authority.
requirements.rationale: Project oversight roles must include business-side accountability, not merely IT accountability. Absent an empowered business sponsor, risk assessments and governance decisions lack the authority to resolve business-IT conflicts that commonly derail large technology initiatives.
requirements.frequency: Appointed at project initiation; confirmed at each steering committee cycle

requirements.requirement: Integrate technology project risk assessment outputs into the institution's enterprise risk management framework, ensuring material project risks are escalated to and visible at the Board Risk Committee level for Critical-tier projects.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'risk assessments throughout the project life cycle.' Isolating project risk within IT siloes contradicts this requirement. Linking project risks to the ERM framework ensures ongoing Board-level visibility and satisfies BNM's expectation that risk management is embedded across the project lifecycle.
requirements.frequency: Risk assessments conducted at initiation, phase transitions, and upon material scope or risk changes; reported to Board Risk Committee quarterly

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement project management infrastructure that supports risk-proportionate governance, including tooling for structured project delivery, automated risk assessment workflows, and audit-ready reporting across the project lifecycle. Systems must capture and preserve evidence of governance activities — oversight decisions, authority approvals, and risk assessments — to demonstrate compliance to BNM examiners. Integration between project management, risk, and reporting systems is essential to avoid governance gaps arising from manual, disconnected processes.

requirements.requirement: Deploy or configure a centralised project portfolio management (PPM) platform that enforces governance gate controls aligned to project tier, preventing progression to subsequent phases without documented approval from the prescribed authority level.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates 'authority and reporting structures.' A PPM platform with embedded gate controls provides the technical mechanism to enforce these structures systematically rather than relying on manual compliance, and generates an auditable record of authority decisions.
requirements.frequency: Deployed continuously; configuration reviewed semi-annually or when governance policy is updated

requirements.requirement: Implement a standardised risk assessment template and workflow within the PPM platform that triggers mandatory technology risk assessments at project initiation, upon entry to each major phase, upon material scope changes, and at project closure.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly requires 'risk assessments throughout the project life cycle.' Embedding risk assessment as a non-optional workflow step — rather than a discretionary activity — ensures consistent execution and produces the lifecycle-spanning evidence trail BNM expects.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Risk and Security
requirements.frequency: Template reviewed annually; assessments executed per-event as defined in trigger conditions

requirements.requirement: Establish a technology project reporting dashboard accessible to all defined oversight roles, providing real-time visibility into project status, open risks, milestone adherence, and escalation items, with automated alerts for breached thresholds or overdue risk reviews.
requirements.rationale: Effective 'authority and reporting structures' require timely, accurate information flows to those in oversight roles. Without a consolidated reporting capability, oversight bodies receive delayed or inconsistent data, undermining their ability to discharge governance responsibilities.
requirements.frequency: Dashboard maintained continuously; reporting content reviewed monthly by project governance forums

requirements.requirement: Maintain immutable audit logs of all project governance decisions, risk assessment approvals, and authority sign-offs within the PPM platform, with retention aligned to BNM record-keeping requirements (minimum seven years).
requirements.rationale: BNM supervisory reviews require documentary evidence that governance requirements were applied. Immutable audit logs protect the institution from adverse findings by providing a verifiable history of oversight activity across the project lifecycle, directly evidencing compliance with the clause's requirements.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Governance / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Continuous logging; log integrity verified quarterly; retention audited annually

requirements.requirement: Integrate technology project risk data into the institution's enterprise risk management system so that project-level risks above defined thresholds are automatically surfaced in the enterprise risk register without manual re-entry.
requirements.rationale: Risk assessments conducted at project level must feed the institution's broader risk visibility as required under the ERM framework. Manual data transfers introduce latency and transcription errors; automated integration ensures that 'risk assessments throughout the project life cycle' contribute meaningfully to enterprise risk oversight.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Risk / CRO (joint accountability)
requirements.frequency: Integration maintained continuously; data flows validated quarterly

governance.summary: The clause requires formal governance structures that are documented, operationalised, and proportionate to project risk — not ad hoc oversight arrangements. The institution must establish defined committees, authority matrices, and escalation paths for technology projects, with the Board and senior management demonstrably engaged in oversight of material initiatives. Governance structures must be reviewed periodically and tested against actual project execution to confirm they remain effective and aligned with the evolving technology project portfolio.

requirements.requirement: Establish a Technology Project Steering Committee (TPSC) with a mandate covering Critical-tier projects, chaired by the COO or equivalent, with membership including the CTO, CRO, CFO, and relevant business unit heads, with a documented terms of reference defining quorum, decision authority, and escalation obligations.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'project oversight roles and responsibilities' and 'authority and reporting structures.' A formally constituted steering committee with explicit terms of reference gives operational reality to these requirements and provides BNM with evidence that senior management oversight is institutionalised rather than informal.
requirements.frequency: Committee established permanently; meets monthly for active Critical-tier projects; ToR reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a Technology Project Authority Matrix (Delegation of Authority) that specifies, for each project tier, the minimum seniority required to approve project initiation, budget releases, scope changes above defined thresholds, and project termination decisions.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly calls out 'authority and reporting structures.' An authority matrix translates this into enforceable rules, preventing situations where material technology investments are approved below appropriate seniority levels, which would represent a control weakness under BNM examination.
requirements.frequency: Established at policy inception; reviewed annually and upon organisational restructuring

requirements.requirement: Mandate periodic independent project health reviews for all Critical-tier and selected Significant-tier technology projects, conducted by Internal Audit or a designated independent function, assessing governance adherence, risk assessment quality, and reporting accuracy.
requirements.rationale: Governance structures must be effective in practice, not merely documented. Independent reviews provide assurance to the Board and to BNM that 'appropriate governance requirements' are being applied as intended throughout the project life cycle, and surface weaknesses before they materialise as project failures.
requirements.frequency: At least annually per project for multi-year initiatives; at major phase gates for critical projects

requirements.requirement: Institute a Board-level reporting mechanism — through the Board Risk Committee or Board IT Committee — that provides quarterly updates on the technology project portfolio, including the status of Critical-tier projects, aggregated project risk exposures, and any escalated governance exceptions.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires governance 'commensurate with the risk and complexity' of projects. For institutions with material technology programmes, this implies Board visibility of portfolio-level risk, not just operational-level oversight. Board-level reporting closes the loop between project-level risk assessments and institutional governance.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting cycle; ad hoc escalation for material risk events

requirements.requirement: Establish a post-implementation review (PIR) process for all Significant and Critical-tier technology projects, capturing lessons learned on governance effectiveness, risk assessment accuracy, and adherence to authority structures, with findings fed back into the Technology Project Governance Policy.
requirements.rationale: The clause's requirement for governance 'throughout the project life cycle' implicitly includes project closure. PIRs ensure the governance framework is continuously improved based on real project outcomes, and demonstrate to BNM that the institution treats project governance as an active management discipline rather than a compliance checkbox.
requirements.frequency: Executed within 60 days of project closure; findings consolidated into an annual governance improvement report

business.summary: Financial institutions must conduct structured risk assessments for all technology projects to protect operational continuity and safeguard against project failure cascading into broader business disruption. Failure to comply exposes the institution to uncontrolled implementation risks, regulatory censure, reputational damage from system outages, and potential loss of customer trust and revenue. BNM expects business leaders to actively own project risk identification across resource, scope, security, testing, deployment, and recovery dimensions.

requirements.requirement: Establish and enforce a mandatory Technology Project Risk Assessment (TPRA) process for all significant technology projects, covering at minimum the seven risk areas enumerated in Clause 10.2(a) through (g), prior to project approval and at key project milestones.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text states risk assessments 'shall identify and address the key risks arising from the implementation of technology projects.' Without a formalised TPRA process anchored to the seven mandated areas, the institution cannot demonstrate systematic compliance and risks undetected threats materialising into project failure or operational disruption.
requirements.frequency: Per-project (at initiation, key milestone gates, and post-implementation)

requirements.requirement: Implement a Concurrent Project Portfolio Register that tracks the number, size, and duration of all significant technology projects running simultaneously, and use this data to assess resource capacity and vendor load before approving new project commencements.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.2(a) explicitly requires consideration of 'the number, size and duration of significant technology projects already undertaken concurrently.' Without visibility into portfolio-level resource consumption, the institution risks overcommitting internal teams and vendors, degrading delivery quality across all active projects.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (register updated monthly; reviewed at each new project approval gate)

requirements.requirement: Mandate comprehensive User Requirement Specifications (URS) as a formal project deliverable, subject to structured sign-off by business stakeholders, with a documented change control process to manage scope modifications throughout the project lifecycle.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.2(d) requires 'the comprehensiveness of the user requirement specifications to mitigate risks from extensive changes in project scope or deficiencies in meeting business needs.' Incomplete or informal requirements are a leading cause of scope creep, cost overruns, and delivered systems that fail to meet operational needs.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Business Unit Heads
requirements.frequency: Per-project (URS completed before design phase; change control applied continuously)

requirements.requirement: Require formal business sign-off on system deployment plans and tested fallback strategies before any production go-live, ensuring business continuity owners have validated that rollback procedures protect operational capability in the event of prolonged stability issues.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.2(f) mandates 'the appropriateness of system deployment and fallback strategies to mitigate risks from prolonged system stability issues.' Business leaders must confirm fallback plans are operationally viable, not merely technically documented, to avoid extended service disruptions affecting customers and counterparties.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Head of Business Continuity
requirements.frequency: Per-project (prior to each production deployment)

requirements.requirement: Validate Disaster Recovery (DR) operational readiness as a formal post-implementation business acceptance criterion, confirming that DR plans, RTOs, and RPOs are updated and tested to reflect changes introduced by new or enhanced systems before the project is formally closed.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.2(g) requires 'the adequacy of disaster recovery operational readiness following the implementation of new or enhanced systems.' Project closure without confirmed DR readiness leaves the institution exposed to recovery gaps that only surface during an actual disaster, with potentially severe regulatory and operational consequences.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Head of Business Continuity
requirements.frequency: Per-project (post-implementation, before project closure sign-off)

technology.summary: Technology teams must embed risk assessment and mitigation controls across the full project lifecycle, from vendor and resource evaluation at initiation through security hardening, rigorous testing, controlled deployment, and confirmed DR readiness at closure. Each of the seven mandated risk areas in Clause 10.2 requires corresponding technical artifacts, tooling, and procedures owned by IT leadership. Ad hoc or informal technical practices will not satisfy BNM's 'S' marker standard, which demands demonstrable, repeatable engineering rigour.

requirements.requirement: Conduct a formal Technology Complexity and Integration Risk Assessment for each project, documenting risks arising from unproven or unfamiliar technology, multi-vendor proprietary system integration, large-scale data migration or cleansing, and extensive customisation, with defined mitigation plans for each identified risk.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.2(b) enumerates specific complexity risk vectors that must be assessed. Integration failures and data migration errors are among the most costly and disruptive technology project outcomes; systematic assessment and mitigation planning reduces the probability of these materialising in production.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of Enterprise Architecture
requirements.frequency: Per-project (at design phase and reviewed at each major milestone)

requirements.requirement: Embed Security Controls Assessment into the project lifecycle at all phases (design, development, testing, deployment, and post-implementation), covering access controls, data encryption, vulnerability management, and protection of confidential data, with documented security sign-off required before each phase transition.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.2(c) requires 'the adequacy and configuration of security controls throughout the project life cycle to mitigate cybersecurity breaches or exposure of confidential data.' Addressing security only at go-live is inadequate; vulnerabilities introduced during development or testing phases can persist into production if not remediated at source.
requirements.frequency: Per-project (at each SDLC phase gate: design, build, test, deploy, post-implementation review)

requirements.requirement: Develop and execute a comprehensive System and User Acceptance Testing (UAT) Strategy for each project, including test scope, test case coverage metrics, defect severity classification, entry/exit criteria, and documented sign-off by technical and business owners before production deployment.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.2(e) requires 'the robustness of system and user testing strategies to reduce risks of undiscovered system faults and functionality errors.' Inadequate test coverage directly increases the probability of post-implementation defects that impact service availability, data integrity, and customer experience.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Head of Quality Assurance
requirements.frequency: Per-project (strategy defined at project initiation; executed throughout test phases)

requirements.requirement: Produce a documented Deployment and Fallback Plan for every production release, including step-by-step deployment runbooks, defined fallback triggers, tested rollback procedures, and a post-deployment stability monitoring period with defined acceptance thresholds before the deployment is declared stable.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.2(f) mandates assessment of 'the appropriateness of system deployment and fallback strategies to mitigate risks from prolonged system stability issues.' An untested or undocumented fallback exposes the institution to extended outages if a deployment fails, as recovery actions must be improvised under pressure rather than executed from a validated plan.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Operations / Head of IT Infrastructure
requirements.frequency: Per-project (produced and approved before each production deployment)

requirements.requirement: Update and conduct DR tests for all systems affected by a technology project prior to project closure, producing a DR Operational Readiness Report that confirms recovery procedures, RTOs, RPOs, and failover configurations remain valid and effective post-implementation.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.2(g) requires confirming 'disaster recovery operational readiness following the implementation of new or enhanced systems.' System changes can invalidate existing DR configurations and recovery scripts; untested post-implementation DR creates hidden recovery gaps that regulators expect institutions to proactively close.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Operations / Head of IT Infrastructure
requirements.frequency: Per-project (DR test completed and report issued before project formal closure)

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that Technology Project Risk Assessments are policy-mandated, independently reviewed, and escalated to senior management and the Board for significant projects, with audit trails maintained to demonstrate regulatory compliance. The 'S' marker indicates BNM expects this to be a Standard practice embedded in the institution's project governance framework, not a discretionary activity. Effective governance also requires clear accountability assignment, periodic policy review, and integration of TPRA outcomes into enterprise risk reporting.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Board-approved Technology Project Risk Management Policy that mandates the TPRA process, defines 'significant technology project' thresholds, prescribes the minimum seven risk areas per Clause 10.2(a)–(g), and assigns accountability for risk assessment execution, review, and escalation.
requirements.rationale: Without a formally approved policy, the TPRA process lacks institutional authority and enforceability. BNM expects 'S' marker standards to be embedded in policy frameworks rather than ad hoc practice, ensuring consistent application across all project teams and business units.
requirements.owner: Board of Directors / Board Risk Committee
requirements.frequency: Annual review; ad hoc revision upon material regulatory change or significant incident

requirements.requirement: Establish a Technology Project Governance Committee (or equivalent senior management forum) with the mandate to review and approve TPRAs for all significant technology projects, monitor concurrent project portfolio risk, and escalate high-risk projects to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.2 requires risk assessments to address risks that 'could threaten successful project implementation' and risks of 'broader impact on the financial institution's operational capabilities,' both of which have enterprise-level consequences. Senior management oversight ensures that risk acceptance decisions are made at an appropriate authority level, not delegated solely to project teams.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Operating Officer (COO)
requirements.frequency: Monthly (standing agenda item); ad hoc for urgent project approvals

requirements.requirement: Implement an independent Quality Assurance (QA) review function or process to validate that TPRAs are complete, credible, and cover all seven mandated risk areas before project approval, providing an objective check separate from the project team's own assessment.
requirements.rationale: Project teams have inherent incentives to minimise risk ratings to secure project approval. Clause 10.2's 'shall' language requires substantive risk identification, not perfunctory compliance. An independent review layer ensures assessments reflect genuine risk exposure and are not subject to optimism bias or stakeholder pressure.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Operational Risk
requirements.frequency: Per-project (independent review completed before project approval gate)

requirements.requirement: Integrate Technology Project Risk Assessment outcomes and status updates into the institution's Enterprise Risk Reporting framework, providing the Board Risk Committee with quarterly visibility of significant technology projects, their key risk ratings, mitigation status, and any emerging risks escalated from the project governance level.
requirements.rationale: BNM expects risk oversight to flow upward to the Board level for material technology risks. The clause's reference to risks that could lead to 'broader impact on the financial institution's operational capabilities' signals Board-level relevance. Without structured reporting, the Board cannot fulfil its oversight obligations or make informed risk acceptance decisions.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly (Board Risk Committee reporting); per-project milestone for critical projects

requirements.requirement: Ensure that Internal Audit includes Technology Project Risk Assessments within its annual audit plan, conducting periodic assessments of TPRA process adherence, documentation completeness across the seven mandated areas, and the effectiveness of risk mitigations applied across a sample of completed and in-flight projects.
requirements.rationale: The 'S' marker designation means BNM may examine the institution's TPRA practices during supervisory reviews. Internal Audit provides the independent assurance that the institution's self-assessment of compliance is accurate and that governance controls are operating effectively, not merely documented in policy.
requirements.owner: Chief Audit Executive (CAE) / Head of Internal Audit
requirements.frequency: Annual (as part of internal audit plan); targeted reviews following significant project failures or near-misses

10.3.title: Board and Senior Management Project Reporting

business.summary: Failure to keep the board and senior management informed of project risks on an ongoing basis creates blind spots that can result in unchecked risk accumulation, budget overruns, regulatory breaches, or project failures that damage the institution's operations and reputation. BNM expects the board to exercise active oversight — not passive ratification — meaning receipt and review of risk reports is a substantive obligation, not a procedural one. Non-compliance with this clause may constitute a governance control failure, exposing the institution to supervisory action under BNM's enforcement framework.

requirements.requirement: The institution must establish a formal definition and register of 'significant projects' that triggers mandatory board and senior management risk reporting obligations, with criteria documented in the project management policy.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text scopes the obligation to 'significant projects' — without a clear, documented definition, the institution cannot demonstrate consistent application of the reporting requirement. Ambiguity in scope is itself a governance risk and an audit finding.
requirements.frequency: Annual review of criteria; per-project classification at initiation

requirements.requirement: Board and senior management must formally receive and demonstrably review project risk reports throughout the full implementation lifecycle of each significant project, with evidence of review captured (e.g., board minutes, sign-off records).
requirements.rationale: The clause uses the conjunction 'receive and review' — both actions are required. Passive receipt without documented review does not satisfy the regulatory obligation, and BNM examiners will look for evidence of substantive engagement.
requirements.frequency: Per reporting cycle throughout project implementation (minimum quarterly; monthly for high-risk projects)

requirements.requirement: Project risk reports submitted to the board and senior management must specifically cover the management of risks — including risk identification, assessment, mitigation actions taken, and residual risk status — rather than general project status updates.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly states 'management of these risks,' distinguishing this from routine project progress reporting. Reports that omit risk management substance do not fulfill the clause's intent.
requirements.frequency: Per reporting cycle throughout project implementation

requirements.requirement: An escalation framework must be established to ensure that material risk developments in significant projects are reported to the board and senior management on an ad-hoc basis outside of the regular reporting cycle when thresholds are breached.
requirements.rationale: The phrase 'on an ongoing basis' implies that reporting cadence must be responsive to risk events, not only scheduled. A fixed-schedule-only approach may leave the board uninformed during critical periods.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Executive Officer (CEO)
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; ad-hoc escalation as triggered by risk threshold breaches

requirements.requirement: Senior management must ensure that project risk reporting is sustained without gaps from project initiation through to completion and post-implementation review, with no reporting blackouts during any phase of the implementation lifecycle.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates reporting 'throughout the implementation' — any reporting gap during onboarding, testing, migration, or go-live phases would constitute non-compliance and leave the board exposed to unreviewed risk.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Chief Executive Officer (CEO)
requirements.frequency: Continuous throughout project lifecycle

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement systems and tooling that enable timely, structured, and auditable risk reporting to the board and senior management for all significant projects, ensuring reports are generated with sufficient frequency and detail to reflect the real-time risk posture. Reporting infrastructure must produce evidence of report delivery and review, supporting audit and regulatory examination. Integration between project management, risk management, and board reporting platforms is necessary to eliminate manual gaps and ensure data integrity.

requirements.requirement: Implement a centralised project risk reporting system or module (within the PMO toolset or GRC platform) that aggregates risk registers, mitigation status, and residual risk scores per significant project, and generates structured board-ready reports on a defined schedule.
requirements.rationale: Manual, ad-hoc report assembly introduces delays and errors that undermine the 'timely' reporting obligation in the verbatim text. A system-driven process ensures consistent, repeatable, and auditable report generation throughout the project lifecycle.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of IT Governance
requirements.frequency: System operational continuously; reports generated per defined cadence (minimum monthly)

requirements.requirement: Implement automated distribution and acknowledgement tracking for project risk reports, ensuring the system records when reports were sent to board members and senior management and captures digital acknowledgement or read-receipt evidence.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires that the board and senior management 'receive' reports — institutions must be able to demonstrate this to BNM examiners. Automated delivery logs and acknowledgement records constitute the audit trail for the 'receive' obligation.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Head of Project Management Office (PMO)
requirements.frequency: Per reporting distribution event; logs retained per record retention policy

requirements.requirement: Establish a project risk dashboard accessible to senior management (CxO level) providing near-real-time visibility into the risk status of all significant in-flight technology projects, including risk heat maps, open risk items, and overdue mitigation actions.
requirements.rationale: A dashboard supports the 'ongoing basis' requirement by giving senior management continuous access to risk information between formal reporting cycles, enabling more responsive oversight without waiting for scheduled reports.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Officer (CIO) / Head of IT Governance
requirements.frequency: Continuous availability; data refreshed at minimum daily

requirements.requirement: Integrate the project risk reporting system with the institution's board portal or board communication platform to ensure reports are delivered directly into the board's secure document environment, with version control and access logs maintained.
requirements.rationale: Board portals provide secure, auditable delivery and access records that satisfy both the 'receive' obligation and BNM's broader data governance expectations. Emailing reports outside a secure portal creates delivery and confidentiality risks.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Company Secretary (for board portal administration)
requirements.frequency: Per reporting cycle; integration maintained continuously

requirements.requirement: Implement alerting and notification controls within the PMO and risk systems to automatically flag when a significant project's risk reporting is overdue or when a project phase transition occurs without a corresponding risk report having been submitted and reviewed.
requirements.rationale: Automated alerts enforce the 'ongoing basis throughout the implementation' requirement by preventing reporting gaps that might otherwise go undetected until an audit or regulatory examination.
requirements.owner: Head of Project Management Office (PMO) / Head of IT Risk
requirements.frequency: Continuous automated monitoring; alerts triggered in real-time

governance.summary: The institution must establish formal governance structures that embed board and senior management risk oversight as a mandatory, recurring activity within the project management lifecycle, supported by documented policies, terms of reference, and accountability frameworks. Governance mechanisms must ensure that the review obligation is substantive — with board and senior management actively engaging with risk content — and that all reporting activities are evidenced in formal records. BNM will assess the effectiveness of these governance structures during Technology Risk examinations.

requirements.requirement: The board must formally mandate, through a board-approved Technology Project Risk Reporting Policy, the requirement for ongoing risk reporting on significant projects, specifying reporting frequency, report content standards, escalation thresholds, and accountable parties.
requirements.rationale: A board-approved policy provides the governance foundation for the clause's obligations and ensures that reporting requirements are institutionalised rather than dependent on individual discretion. This directly addresses the 'must' language in the verbatim text.
requirements.owner: Board of Directors / Board Risk Committee (BRC)
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed and approved annually; enforced continuously

requirements.requirement: The Board Risk Committee (or equivalent board-level committee) must include a standing agenda item for significant technology project risk reporting at every scheduled meeting during the implementation lifecycle of such projects, with meeting minutes recording the board's review and any directives issued.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires the board to 'receive and review' — standing agenda items and formal minutes constitute the evidentiary record of the review obligation being fulfilled, which BNM examiners will inspect.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee Chairman / Company Secretary
requirements.frequency: Every BRC meeting throughout project implementation (minimum quarterly)

requirements.requirement: The institution must designate a named accountable owner (e.g., CRO or PMO Head) responsible for ensuring that project risk reports are prepared, reviewed for completeness, and submitted to the board and senior management within the defined timelines for each significant project.
requirements.rationale: Clear accountability for the 'timely' reporting obligation prevents reports from being delayed or deprioritised. Without a designated owner, reporting obligations may fall into gaps between organisational units.
requirements.frequency: Accountable owner designated at project initiation; maintained throughout lifecycle

requirements.requirement: The institution's Project Management Framework must require a mandatory risk reporting checkpoint at each major project phase gate (e.g., initiation, design, build, testing, go-live), with board or senior management sign-off on the risk status before the project may proceed to the next phase.
requirements.rationale: Phase-gate risk checkpoints operationalise the 'throughout the implementation' requirement by structurally embedding risk review into the project lifecycle, preventing projects from advancing without board visibility of current risk status.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Head of Project Management Office (PMO)
requirements.frequency: At each project phase gate throughout implementation

requirements.requirement: Internal Audit must include, within its annual technology audit plan, a review of compliance with the board and senior management project risk reporting requirement, assessing report timeliness, content adequacy, evidence of board review, and coverage of all significant projects.
requirements.rationale: Independent assurance over the reporting obligation provides the board with confidence that the 'ongoing basis' requirement is being met consistently, and identifies weaknesses before BNM examination. This also satisfies BNM's expectation for three-lines-of-defence coverage of technology governance.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor / Head of Internal Audit
requirements.frequency: Annual audit; findings reported to Audit Committee

business.summary: Clause 10.4 requires financial institutions to establish a formal Enterprise Technology Architecture (ETA) framework that directly links technology design and acquisition decisions to business goals, organisational units, and data flows. Failure to comply exposes the institution to unmitigated single points of failure, uninformed system acquisition decisions, and an inability to perform credible Business Impact Analysis — creating operational, reputational, and regulatory risk. As an 'S' marker clause, BNM expects this framework to be institutionalised, not aspirational.

requirements.requirement: Formally establish, document, and maintain an Enterprise Technology Architecture (ETA) Framework policy approved by Senior Management, covering the design, planning, implementation, and governance of technology architecture across the entire institution.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text opens with an explicit mandate: 'A financial institution must establish a framework.' Without a formal, approved framework document, all downstream architecture decisions lack authoritative grounding, exposing the institution to fragmented, ungoverned technology growth and regulatory non-compliance.
requirements.frequency: Initial establishment; reviewed and reaffirmed Annually or upon material organisational change

requirements.requirement: Ensure the ETA framework contains an explicit mapping of technology assets — infrastructure, systems, and applications — to supported business functions, organisational units, and data repositories, sufficient to enable credible Business Impact Analysis (BIA).
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.4(c) explicitly requires 'mapping to supported business functions, organisation units, applications, and data to enable business impact analysis.' Without this traceability, BCP/DR teams cannot accurately determine the downstream business consequence of a technology failure, undermining the institution's resilience posture.
requirements.frequency: Maintained Continuously; formally validated Semi-Annually and upon any material system change

requirements.requirement: Use the ETA framework as the primary decision-support tool for all system development and technology acquisition proposals, ensuring alignment between technology investment and documented business goals before procurement or project initiation is approved.
requirements.rationale: The clause states the architecture 'serves as a foundation on which financial institutions plan and structure system development and acquisition strategies to meet business goals.' Without this gate, ad-hoc acquisitions can introduce architectural debt, redundant capabilities, or incompatible systems that inflate operational risk.
requirements.frequency: Applied Per-Event (each system acquisition or major development initiative); framework reviewed Annually

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a business-approved technology roadmap that outlines multi-year priorities for the institution's architecture evolution, directly derived from the ETA framework's long-term priorities component.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.4(e) mandates that the framework 'outline longer-term priorities to guide its evolution.' A technology roadmap translates these priorities into business-consumable milestones, enabling budget planning, resource allocation, and Board-level strategic oversight of technology direction.
requirements.frequency: Reviewed and updated Annually; presented to Board at least once per year

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal process for identifying, documenting, and escalating Single Points of Failure (SPOFs) across business-critical technology components, using the interdependency and fallback facility information maintained in the ETA framework.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.4(b) explicitly states that architecture documentation on 'systems' inter-connectivity and dependencies (e.g. fallback facility)... is critical to support identification of a single point of failure.' Unidentified SPOFs directly threaten service continuity and may cause reportable incidents under BNM's operational risk requirements.
requirements.frequency: SPOF register reviewed Quarterly; updated Per-Event upon any infrastructure change

technology.summary: Technology teams must produce and continuously maintain a multi-layered architecture documentation set that spans baseline inventory, infrastructure topology, system interdependencies, fallback mechanisms, security controls, and a technology-to-business mapping. Beyond documentation, they must operationalise the framework by embedding it into change management, SPOF detection, and network/security policy enforcement workflows. The technical scope spans enterprise architecture, infrastructure, application, and security domains concurrently.

requirements.requirement: Produce and maintain a comprehensive Technology Baseline Register documenting all architecture components (hardware, software, networks, cloud services, third-party platforms) including their versions, configurations, interdependencies, and key rationale for their adoption within the institution.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.4(a) requires the framework to 'provide a comprehensive view of technology throughout the financial institution, baseline architecture components and key rationale for their use.' Without a live, accurate baseline register, architecture governance decisions are made on incomplete information, increasing the risk of incompatible changes or undetected redundancies.
requirements.frequency: Maintained Continuously via Configuration Management Database (CMDB); formally audited Annually

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain detailed infrastructure topology diagrams and system interdependency maps covering all production environments, explicitly documenting inter-system data flows, API dependencies, fallback/failover facilities, and network segmentation boundaries.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.4(b) mandates 'an overall technical design and high-level plan that describes the financial institution's technology infrastructure, systems' inter-connectivity and dependencies (e.g. fallback facility).' Accurate topology and dependency maps are the technical foundation for SPOF identification, DR planning, and security control scoping.
requirements.frequency: Updated Per-Event upon any infrastructure or application change; fully reviewed Semi-Annually

requirements.requirement: Implement a formal Single Point of Failure (SPOF) analysis methodology — using the interdependency maps from 10.4(b) — to systematically identify, risk-rate, and track remediation of SPOFs across all tiers of the technology stack, with findings linked to resilience controls and fallback options.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.4(b) states the architecture information 'is critical to support identification of a single point of failure.' A structured SPOF methodology converts raw topology data into actionable risk findings, directly supporting BNM's expectation for operational resilience in technology operations.
requirements.frequency: Formal SPOF analysis conducted Semi-Annually and Per-Event following major architecture changes

requirements.requirement: Build and maintain a Technology-to-Business Mapping artefact that links each technology component (infrastructure, application, data store) to its corresponding business functions, organisational units, and data classifications, structured to directly feed Business Impact Analysis (BIA) exercises.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.4(c) requires 'mapping to supported business functions, organisation units, applications, and data to enable business impact analysis.' Without this machine-readable or structured mapping, BIA exercises rely on institutional memory rather than verified data, reducing their accuracy and audit defensibility.
requirements.frequency: Updated Continuously upon change; formally reconciled with BIA outputs Annually

requirements.requirement: Define, document, and enforce technology architecture principles and design standards for network infrastructure — including segmentation, access control, encryption standards, and redundancy design — aligned to the institution's IT security policies, and embed these standards in the project initiation and change approval process.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.4(d) requires the framework to 'define principles and guidelines to govern the design and maintenance of the network infrastructure, related technology controls, and IT security policies.' Absent enforceable standards, individual project teams make inconsistent design decisions that collectively erode the institution's security and control posture.
requirements.frequency: Standards reviewed Annually and upon material changes to threat landscape, regulation, or infrastructure platforms

governance.summary: Governance of the ETA framework requires a defined ownership structure, formal review cycles, enforceable architecture principles, and Board-level visibility of long-term technology direction. The framework must not exist as a static document but as a living governance instrument embedded into project approval gates, risk management processes, and strategic planning cycles. Senior Management and the Board carry accountability for ensuring the framework remains current, comprehensive, and operationally effective.

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Enterprise Architecture Governance function or committee — with a documented Terms of Reference, defined membership (including representation from Technology, Risk, Compliance, and Business lines), and clear decision rights — responsible for owning, maintaining, and enforcing the ETA framework.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates a framework covering 'design, planning, implementation, and governance of an enterprise technology architecture.' Governance requires a named accountable body; without it, the framework lacks enforcement authority and becomes an unfulfilled compliance artefact.
requirements.frequency: Committee meets at minimum Quarterly; Terms of Reference reviewed Annually

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formal ETA Framework Policy document — approved by Senior Management — that articulates architecture governance principles, design standards, exception handling procedures, and roles and responsibilities across the institution, covering all sub-requirements in Clauses 10.4(a) through (e).
requirements.rationale: BNM's 'must establish a framework' language in 10.4 is directive and audit-evidenced through documented policy. The policy must demonstrate full coverage of all five sub-clauses; partial or implied compliance is insufficient given the 'S' marker.
requirements.frequency: Reviewed and recertified Annually; updated Per-Event upon material regulatory or organisational change

requirements.requirement: Embed the ETA framework as a mandatory gate in the institution's project governance and change management lifecycle, requiring architecture review and sign-off from the Enterprise Architecture function before approval of any new system development initiative, technology acquisition, or major infrastructure change.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.4 positions the framework as 'a foundation on which financial institutions plan and structure system development and acquisition strategies.' Without a formal project gate, the framework is advisory rather than controlling, undermining its regulatory purpose.
requirements.frequency: Applied Per-Event (each project initiation, acquisition proposal, or major change request)

requirements.requirement: Present an annual Enterprise Technology Architecture Review to the Board Risk Management Committee or equivalent Board-level committee, covering: currency of baseline architecture documentation, SPOF remediation status, compliance of recent technology decisions with architecture principles, and the institution's technology evolution roadmap.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.4(e) requires the framework to 'outline longer-term priorities to guide its evolution,' which implies Board-level awareness and endorsement of technology direction. Board oversight also satisfies BNM's broader expectation that technology risk is governed at the highest level of the institution.
requirements.frequency: Annual Board reporting; interim updates to Board Risk Management Committee upon material architecture changes

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal exception management process within the ETA framework governance structure, requiring documented business justification, risk acceptance sign-off from the CRO, time-bound remediation commitments, and tracking of any approved deviations from architecture principles or standards defined under Clause 10.4(d).
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.4(d) requires 'principles and guidelines to govern the design and maintenance of the network infrastructure, related technology controls, and IT security policies.' Principles without a governed exception process create either blind non-compliance or undocumented deviations — both of which are audit findings under BNM examination.
requirements.frequency: Exceptions reviewed at each Enterprise Architecture Governance Committee meeting (minimum Quarterly); exception register reported to Board annually

business.summary: Clause 10.5 requires financial institutions to embed a formal, end-to-end SDLC methodology that spans all eight lifecycle phases and ties directly to enterprise strategy, risk appetite, and information security obligations. Without a standardised methodology, institutions risk deploying systems that are misaligned with business objectives, expose customer and counterparty data to confidentiality or integrity breaches, and attract regulatory sanctions from BNM. Compliance failure can result in reputational damage, operational disruptions, and enforcement action under the Financial Services Act 2013.

requirements.requirement: The institution must formally adopt, document, and maintain an enterprise-wide SDLC methodology that explicitly covers all eight phases: requirements, design, development, testing, deployment, change management, maintenance, and decommissioning.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text states that 'key phases of SDLC shall include' all eight phases. An incomplete or informal methodology creates gaps where unsecured or non-compliant systems enter production without adequate controls, directly undermining BNM's expectation of effective and secure IT implementation.
requirements.frequency: Policy review Annual; methodology adherence Continuous

requirements.requirement: The SDLC methodology must be integrated with the institution's enterprise architecture function to ensure every IT initiative is traceable to an approved business strategy objective before development commences.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.5(a) explicitly requires integration with enterprise architecture 'to ensure successful execution of business strategy.' Projects initiated without EA alignment risk delivering systems that duplicate capability, create technical debt, or cannot scale to support regulatory or business growth requirements.
requirements.frequency: Per-project initiation and Annual EA roadmap review

requirements.requirement: Business owners must participate in SDLC risk assessments at the requirements and design phases to ensure that each system's risk profile is formally documented, evaluated against the institution's risk appetite, and signed off before development proceeds.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.5(b) mandates integration with 'risk management policies and practices to achieve business objectives.' Business-side risk participation ensures that operational, credit, and compliance risks introduced by new systems are identified early, reducing costly remediation in later phases.
requirements.frequency: Per-project at requirements and design gates; Quarterly portfolio review

requirements.requirement: The institution must establish a formal system decommissioning process, including business sign-off, data retention verification, customer impact assessment, and migration validation, before any production IT system is retired.
requirements.rationale: Decommissioning is explicitly listed as one of the mandatory SDLC phases under clause 10.5. Unmanaged decommissioning risks orphaned customer data, loss of data integrity, and breach of BNM data retention requirements under PD on Data Management and MCS.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (each decommissioning activity)

requirements.requirement: The institution must define and publish measurable SDLC compliance KPIs — such as percentage of projects with completed gate reviews, security sign-off rates, and post-deployment defect rates — and report these to senior management quarterly.
requirements.rationale: Effective adoption of a methodology, as required by clause 10.5, cannot be verified without measurable outcomes. Tracking KPIs enables the board and senior management to detect methodology drift, prioritise remediation, and demonstrate to BNM examiners that the methodology is operationally embedded.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting; Annual target-setting

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement a structured, tooling-supported SDLC framework that enforces phase-gate controls, integrates security testing throughout development, and maintains verifiable artefacts for all eight lifecycle phases. The framework must be interoperable with the institution's enterprise architecture repository and risk management systems so that traceability from business requirement to deployed system is continuously maintained. All controls must demonstrably protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of customer and counterparty information.

requirements.requirement: Implement a documented, tooling-enforced SDLC framework — whether waterfall, agile, or hybrid — that mandates formal entry and exit criteria (gate reviews) for each of the eight phases: requirements, design, development, testing, deployment, change management, maintenance, and decommissioning.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text states all eight phases 'shall include' as mandatory. Gate reviews with defined entry/exit criteria prevent phase skipping, which is the most common cause of security vulnerabilities and non-compliant system deployments in financial institutions.
requirements.frequency: Per-project at each phase gate; Framework review Annual

requirements.requirement: Integrate security-by-design principles into the requirements and design phases, including mandatory threat modelling, secure coding standards, and static/dynamic application security testing (SAST/DAST) in the testing phase, to ensure confidentiality, integrity, and availability of customer and counterparty information.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.5(c) requires integration of 'security principles and requirements' specifically to ensure CIA of customer and counterparty data. Embedding security into early phases reduces remediation cost by orders of magnitude compared to post-deployment fixes and directly satisfies BNM's CIA mandate.
requirements.frequency: Per-project (embedded in each SDLC phase); Tool signature updates Continuous

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain an enterprise architecture repository (e.g., using an EA tool such as ArchiMate-compliant tooling) that is formally linked to the SDLC initiation process, requiring all new system projects to obtain EA impact assessment sign-off before design commences.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.5(a) mandates SDLC integration with enterprise architecture. A governed EA repository ensures technology decisions align with the target-state architecture, prevents proliferation of shadow IT, and provides BNM examiners with a coherent view of the institution's technology landscape.
requirements.frequency: Per-project at initiation; EA repository review Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement a change management process integrated with the SDLC that requires all production deployments to pass through a Change Advisory Board (CAB) review, with mandatory rollback plans, deployment runbooks, and post-implementation reviews for every change.
requirements.rationale: Change management is explicitly named as one of the eight mandatory SDLC phases in clause 10.5. Uncontrolled changes are a leading cause of IT incidents and outages in financial institutions; a formal CAB process ensures risk assessment and authorisation before production impact.
requirements.frequency: Per-change; CAB meeting cadence Weekly or as needed

requirements.requirement: Develop and enforce a system maintenance and decommissioning playbook that includes: patch lifecycle management, end-of-support tracking for all production components, formal data migration and secure data destruction procedures, and evidence archiving sufficient to satisfy BNM examination requirements.
requirements.rationale: Maintenance and decommissioning are mandatory phases under clause 10.5. Failure to manage end-of-life systems introduces vulnerabilities that directly threaten the CIA requirements of clause 10.5(c), and uncontrolled data destruction may violate BNM's data management and customer protection requirements.
requirements.frequency: Maintenance: Continuous; Decommissioning review: Per-event and Annual asset inventory

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure the SDLC methodology is formally approved at board level, embedded in enforceable policy, and subject to independent assurance so that compliance with clause 10.5 is verifiable rather than assumed. Integration with the institution's risk management framework and enterprise architecture governance must be codified in documented policies and overseen through defined committees with clear accountability. BNM expects audit trails demonstrating that the methodology was applied consistently across all in-scope IT projects.

requirements.requirement: The Board of Directors must formally approve the institution's SDLC methodology policy, including its integration requirements with enterprise architecture, risk management, and security principles, and review the policy at least annually or following any material change to the institution's technology strategy.
requirements.rationale: The 'must adopt' language in clause 10.5 reflects a Standard (S) obligation requiring institutional-level commitment. Board approval establishes accountability at the highest level and signals to BNM that the methodology is an enterprise control, not an informal IT convention.
requirements.owner: Board Chairman / Board IT or Risk Committee
requirements.frequency: Annual approval; Ad-hoc upon material change

requirements.requirement: Establish an IT Governance Committee (or equivalent) with cross-functional membership — including representatives from Technology, Risk, Compliance, Business, and Internal Audit — that holds authority to enforce SDLC phase-gate compliance, grant or refuse deployment approvals, and escalate methodology exceptions to the Board.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.5 mandates integration across three domains (enterprise architecture, risk management, and security). A cross-functional committee is the governance mechanism that operationalises this integration, prevents siloed decision-making, and provides the documented authority trail BNM examiners expect.
requirements.frequency: Monthly committee meetings; exception reviews Per-event

requirements.requirement: The institution's risk management framework must formally incorporate SDLC risk assessments as a mandatory control, with defined risk acceptance criteria for each SDLC phase, escalation thresholds, and documented risk owner sign-off recorded in the institution's GRC system.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.5(b) explicitly requires SDLC integration with 'risk management policies and practices.' Embedding SDLC risk checkpoints into the GRC framework ensures assessments are not ad-hoc but are systematically recorded, reportable, and available for BNM examination and internal audit review.
requirements.frequency: Per-project at defined SDLC gates; Framework review Annual

requirements.requirement: Internal Audit must conduct an independent annual review of SDLC methodology adherence across a risk-based sample of completed and in-flight projects, assessing compliance with all eight phases and the three integration requirements, and report findings with management responses to the Board Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: Effective adoption of a methodology requires independent verification. An annual audit provides assurance that the SDLC policy is not merely documented but operationally applied, and enables the Board to discharge its oversight responsibility under BNM's corporate governance expectations for financial institutions.
requirements.frequency: Annual; findings tracked Quarterly until closure

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal SDLC exception management process requiring written justification, risk acceptance by an appropriately authorised approver (commensurate with risk level), time-bound remediation plans, and mandatory reporting of all exceptions to the IT Governance Committee and Board Risk Committee on a quarterly basis.
requirements.rationale: No methodology can be applied uniformly without a controlled exception pathway. Without a formal process, teams bypass controls informally, creating undocumented risk. A governed exception process with escalation and reporting ensures BNM can see that deviations are managed, not ignored, and that risk acceptance is appropriately authorised.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (exception raised); Quarterly aggregate reporting to Board

business.summary: Financial institutions adopting rapid development methodologies (e.g., Agile, DevOps, CI/CD) cannot trade speed for security — BNM explicitly requires enterprise security, governance, and compliance standards to be maintained throughout. The dynamic nature of these environments increases error likelihood, meaning inadequate controls can result in regulatory breaches, unauthorised access incidents, or vulnerable services reaching production. Non-compliance exposes the institution to BNM enforcement action, reputational damage, and material operational risk.

requirements.requirement: Establish and enforce a formal policy mandating that all rapid development initiatives (Agile, DevOps, Scrum, continuous delivery) must demonstrably meet the institution's enterprise security, governance, and compliance standards before any new IT service is released to production.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text states the institution 'must meet enterprise security, governance and compliance requirements when using rapid system development methodology.' Without a formal policy binding rapid development teams to these standards, speed-to-market incentives will systematically erode security controls, creating regulatory exposure under RMiT.
requirements.frequency: Policy review: Annual; Compliance attestation per release: Per-event

requirements.requirement: Conduct and document a formal risk assessment specific to rapid development environments that quantifies the increased likelihood of security errors arising from the dynamic development lifecycle, and use this assessment to justify the security control investments required under this clause.
requirements.rationale: BNM acknowledges that 'the dynamic environment can increase likelihood of errors,' making it a recognized risk driver. A documented risk assessment creates the business case for automation spend, satisfies audit evidence requirements, and ensures the Board understands the residual risk profile of the institution's development practices.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; triggered review upon adoption of a new rapid development methodology or toolchain

requirements.requirement: Define and enforce business-owned release approval gates that require evidence of completed automated security compliance review and vulnerability testing results before any new IT service proceeds to production, ensuring no service is released without a documented security clearance artefact.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires institutions to 'ensure secure release of new IT services.' A business-owned release gate translates this regulatory obligation into an operational control, preventing business units from bypassing security checks under delivery pressure — a common failure mode in rapid development environments.
requirements.frequency: Per-release (every production deployment)

requirements.requirement: Establish clear accountability mapping for rapid development programmes, designating named business owners responsible for ensuring each product team meets enterprise security requirements, and include security compliance performance as a metric in those owners' performance frameworks.
requirements.rationale: The obligation to 'meet enterprise security, governance and compliance requirements' cannot be solely delegated to technology teams. Business accountability ensures that product owners cannot approve releases without owning the associated security posture, creating an incentive structure aligned with the clause's intent.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Business Line Heads
requirements.frequency: Accountability assignment: per programme inception; performance review: Annual

requirements.requirement: Include rapid development security compliance metrics (e.g., percentage of releases with completed automated security reviews, mean-time-to-remediate discovered vulnerabilities, number of security gate failures) in regular management reporting presented to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: Governance of rapid development security requires visibility at Board level. Quantitative metrics allow the Board to assess whether automation of security compliance review is functioning effectively and whether the institution is genuinely preventing unauthorised access and securing new IT service releases as required.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting to Board Risk Committee

technology.summary: Technology teams must embed automated security controls directly into the rapid development toolchain so that security compliance reviews and vulnerability discovery are not manual gates subject to being skipped. This means integrating Static Application Security Testing (SAST), Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST), Software Composition Analysis (SCA), and automated access control validation into CI/CD pipelines as mandatory pipeline stages. The automation must be comprehensive enough that no new IT service can be released to production without passing all security checks, satisfying BNM's requirement to prevent unauthorised access and ensure secure release.

requirements.requirement: Implement automated Static Application Security Testing (SAST) integrated as a mandatory, blocking stage in the CI/CD pipeline for all rapid development workstreams, configured to enforce institution-defined security coding standards and to fail the pipeline build on detection of critical or high-severity findings.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires automating 'IT security compliance review' to prevent errors from the dynamic environment reaching production. SAST directly addresses this by scanning source code at commit or build time, before deployment, ensuring compliance review is not manual and cannot be bypassed under delivery pressure.
requirements.frequency: Automated execution: every code commit/build; ruleset review: Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Deploy and integrate automated Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST) into the CI/CD pipeline to perform runtime vulnerability discovery against deployed application instances in staging environments, configured to block promotion to production upon detection of critical or high-severity vulnerabilities.
requirements.rationale: BNM mandates automation of 'the discovery and testing of security vulnerabilities.' DAST fulfils the 'discovery and testing' obligation for runtime vulnerabilities that SAST cannot detect, such as injection flaws, authentication weaknesses, and insecure API behaviours — directly tracing to the verbatim requirement for secure release of new IT services.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Application Security
requirements.frequency: Automated execution: per deployment to staging; tool signature updates: Continuous; findings review: Per-release

requirements.requirement: Implement automated Software Composition Analysis (SCA) within the CI/CD pipeline to continuously scan all open-source and third-party dependencies for known vulnerabilities (CVEs), licence compliance issues, and outdated components, with pipeline blocking policies applied for critical-severity findings.
requirements.rationale: Rapid development methodologies heavily leverage open-source libraries, which introduce supply chain vulnerabilities. The clause's requirement to automate 'discovery and testing of security vulnerabilities' extends to dependency-introduced vulnerabilities. SCA ensures the institution is not releasing new IT services with known vulnerable components.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Head of Application Security
requirements.frequency: Automated execution: every build; vulnerability database sync: Continuous; policy review: Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement automated Identity and Access Management (IAM) compliance checks within the CI/CD pipeline to validate that all new IT services are deployed with least-privilege access configurations, that service accounts and API keys meet institutional standards, and that no deployment introduces unauthorised access paths or over-privileged roles.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text specifically requires automating 'IT security compliance review to prevent unauthorised access.' Automated IAM validation directly addresses this by ensuring access controls are verified programmatically at deployment time rather than through manual reviews that are error-prone in dynamic development environments.
requirements.frequency: Automated execution: per deployment; access policy review: Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Establish a centralised security findings management platform that aggregates outputs from all automated security tools (SAST, DAST, SCA, IAM checks), provides unified dashboarding, enforces SLA-driven remediation workflows, and generates auditable evidence records for every release demonstrating that security review and vulnerability testing were completed prior to release.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires not only automation but also assurance of 'secure release of new IT services.' A centralised platform creates the audit trail necessary to demonstrate to BNM examiners that automated controls operated as required, and enables management to track remediation of discovered vulnerabilities across the rapid development portfolio.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of IT Operations
requirements.frequency: Platform operation: Continuous; dashboard review: Weekly; audit evidence packaging: Per-release

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that the speed of rapid development does not create accountability gaps — the Board and senior management must maintain oversight of security compliance across all rapid development programmes through defined policies, oversight committees, and assurance functions. A governance framework is needed that bridges the inherently iterative and dynamic nature of rapid development with the structured compliance obligations under RMiT, ensuring that automation of security controls is itself governed, audited, and continuously verified as effective.

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a formal Rapid Development Security Policy that explicitly defines enterprise security, governance, and compliance requirements applicable to all rapid development methodologies used by the institution, including mandatory security control standards, automated tool requirements, release gate criteria, and exemption approval processes.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim clause states the institution 'must meet enterprise security, governance and compliance requirements when using rapid system development methodology.' A formal policy is the foundational governance instrument that operationalises this obligation, makes requirements explicit and enforceable, and provides the standard against which audit and examination will be conducted.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Board Risk Committee
requirements.frequency: Policy issuance: Initial; review: Annual or upon material change to development methodology
