requirements.requirement: Establish a Security Champions Programme embedded within rapid development teams, with designated Security Champions in each product team trained on the institution's security requirements, responsible for ensuring their team's adherence to automated security controls, and empowered to escalate security issues without going through standard management chains.
requirements.rationale: Given that 'the dynamic environment can increase likelihood of errors,' governance requires in-team accountability to catch issues before they reach automated gates. Security Champions create a human governance layer complementing automation, ensuring teams are not solely dependent on tooling to meet enterprise security and compliance requirements.
requirements.frequency: Programme establishment: Initial; champion training: Annual; effectiveness review: Semi-annual

requirements.requirement: Mandate independent internal audit reviews of the automated security toolchain and CI/CD pipeline security controls at least annually, covering: tool configuration correctness, effectiveness of blocking policies, completeness of coverage across all rapid development workstreams, and integrity of audit evidence generated for release compliance artefacts.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires automation of security compliance review to 'ensure secure release of new IT services.' Governance must verify that the automation itself is correctly configured and operating as intended. Without independent audit of the toolchain, misconfigured or disabled controls may go undetected, creating a false assurance of compliance.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Chief Audit Executive
requirements.frequency: Annual; ad-hoc following significant incidents or material toolchain changes

requirements.requirement: Implement a formal security exemption and risk acceptance process for rapid development releases, requiring documented justification, time-bound remediation commitments, CISO approval, and CRO sign-off for any release approved to proceed despite outstanding security findings, with all exceptions reported to the Board Risk Committee quarterly.
requirements.rationale: Even with automation, operational realities may require controlled exceptions. A formal exemption process with senior sign-off ensures the institution's obligation to 'meet enterprise security, governance and compliance requirements' is respected even in exception cases, creates accountability for residual risk acceptance, and maintains an auditable record for BNM examination.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Per-exception approval: as required; exception register review: Monthly; Board reporting: Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Establish a Rapid Development Security Governance Committee (or extend the mandate of an existing IT Risk Committee) to provide ongoing oversight of: automated security tool effectiveness, emerging vulnerabilities in the development toolchain, compliance with the Rapid Development Security Policy, and remediation of audit findings related to rapid development security.
requirements.rationale: The clause's requirement that enterprise 'governance' requirements are met in rapid development contexts implies structured oversight beyond individual team accountability. A dedicated governance forum ensures that security compliance in the rapidly evolving development environment is continuously monitored at a management level, with escalation paths to the Board for systemic issues.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Committee meetings: Monthly; terms of reference review: Annual

business.summary: Failure to segregate production from development and testing environments exposes the institution to operational disruptions, data breaches, and regulatory sanctions arising from unauthorised or accidental changes to live systems. Commingled environments increase the likelihood of unstable code, test data contamination of customer records, and fraud risk. BNM treats this as a Standard (S) requirement, meaning non-compliance may trigger supervisory action, mandatory remediation, or reputational damage.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formal policy mandating physical segregation of the production environment from all development and testing environments across all financial services and products operated by the institution.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim clause imposes an obligation on the institution ('shall physically segregate') with no exceptions for on-premises environments. A board-approved policy formalises this obligation and creates accountability at the business level, reducing exposure to BNM enforcement action.
requirements.frequency: Policy review: Annual; Compliance attestation: Continuous

requirements.requirement: Allocate dedicated budget and resources for separate production, development, and testing infrastructure — including cloud tenancy costs — to eliminate cost-driven sharing of environments.
requirements.rationale: Cost pressure is a common driver for environment co-location. The clause provides no cost-based exemption; therefore the institution must fund segregation explicitly. Failure to budget adequately creates a systemic compliance gap.
requirements.frequency: Annual budget cycle; reviewed quarterly against capacity plans

requirements.requirement: Define and communicate to all business units the acceptable use boundaries between production, development, and testing environments, including a prohibition on using live customer data in non-production environments without explicit, documented approval and pseudonymisation controls.
requirements.rationale: The clause's intent to 'mitigate the risk of unauthorised changes to production systems' extends to data flows. Business units must understand that unauthorised access pathways — including data pipelines from production — constitute a compliance breach.
requirements.frequency: Awareness training: Annual; Policy acknowledgement: Upon onboarding and annually

requirements.requirement: Establish a business continuity impact assessment confirming that development or testing activities cannot affect production system availability, integrity, or confidentiality.
requirements.rationale: Unauthorised changes to production systems, as described in the clause, are not limited to deliberate acts — they include accidental propagation from unsegregated environments. A formal impact assessment documents the business justification for segregation and supports audit evidence.
requirements.frequency: Annual; triggered by material infrastructure changes

requirements.requirement: Include environment segregation compliance status as a standing agenda item in the Technology Risk Committee and report non-conformances to the Board Risk Committee within 30 days of identification.
requirements.rationale: Regulatory obligations under this Standard clause require demonstrable management oversight. Embedding segregation status into risk reporting creates an auditable trail showing the institution actively monitors compliance rather than treating it as a one-time implementation.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly (Committee); Immediate escalation for critical breaches

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement distinct, isolated infrastructure stacks for production, development, and testing — with no shared compute, network segments, storage, or cloud virtual hosts between them. Access controls, network policies, and cloud tenancy configurations must enforce this isolation technically, not merely procedurally. Ongoing monitoring must detect and alert on any configuration drift that reintroduces cross-environment connectivity.

requirements.requirement: Deploy production systems on physically or logically isolated infrastructure (separate servers, network segments, VLANs, or dedicated cloud accounts/subscriptions/projects) from development and testing environments, with no shared network paths that permit lateral movement.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates 'physical segregation' of production from development and testing. On-premises implementations require separate hardware or fully isolated network zones; shared switching or routing infrastructure with insufficient ACLs does not satisfy this requirement.
requirements.frequency: Implementation: Immediate; Review: Quarterly network architecture review

requirements.requirement: For cloud-hosted workloads, ensure production, development, and testing environments run on separate virtual hosts (distinct VMs, container nodes, or cloud accounts) and are deployed in separate cloud tenancies, subscriptions, or organisation units with no shared IAM roles or credentials spanning environments.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly addresses cloud: 'the financial institution shall ensure that these environments are not running on the same virtual host.' This prohibits co-tenancy of prod/dev/test on the same underlying hypervisor node or shared Kubernetes cluster without hard multi-tenancy controls.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement via IaC policies; Architecture review: Per deployment

requirements.requirement: Implement role-based access controls (RBAC) with strict environment-boundary enforcement, ensuring that developer and tester credentials cannot authenticate to production systems and that privileged production access requires separate, individually approved accounts with just-in-time (JIT) provisioning.
requirements.rationale: Physical segregation without corresponding access segregation fails to 'mitigate the risk of unauthorised changes to production systems' as required by the clause. A developer with valid production credentials can bypass infrastructure controls.
requirements.frequency: Access reviews: Quarterly; JIT access logs: Continuous monitoring

requirements.requirement: Deploy Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) policy guardrails (e.g., OPA, Azure Policy, AWS SCPs, or equivalent) that automatically prevent provisioning production workloads into non-production tenancies and vice versa, with policy-as-code stored in a separate, protected repository.
requirements.rationale: Manual enforcement of environment boundaries is error-prone and scales poorly. Automated policy enforcement ensures that cloud misconfigurations — a common cause of accidental co-location — are detected and blocked at provisioning time, supporting continuous compliance with the clause.
requirements.owner: Head of Cloud Engineering / Head of DevSecOps
requirements.frequency: Policy deployment: Continuous CI/CD; Policy review: Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement continuous configuration monitoring and alerting (e.g., CSPM tools, network flow analysis) to detect any reintroduction of cross-environment connectivity, shared credentials, or co-located workloads, with alerts routed to the security operations centre (SOC) within 15 minutes of detection.
requirements.rationale: Environment segregation can degrade over time through infrastructure drift, emergency changes, or misconfiguration. Continuous monitoring provides the detective control needed to sustain the 'physical segregation' required by the clause between periodic audits.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; Incident review: Per alert; Posture reporting: Monthly

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal change promotion pipeline (e.g., CI/CD with mandatory staging gates) that enforces a unidirectional flow from development → testing → production, with no mechanism for direct deployment to production without passing through defined approval and testing stages.
requirements.rationale: The clause aims to 'mitigate the risk of unauthorised changes to production systems.' A governed promotion pipeline is the technical implementation of that mitigation, ensuring that changes reach production only through controlled, auditable pathways.
requirements.owner: Head of DevOps / Head of Application Delivery
requirements.frequency: Continuous pipeline enforcement; Pipeline configuration review: Quarterly

governance.summary: The institution must establish formal governance structures — including policies, standards, audit mechanisms, and ownership — to ensure environment segregation is maintained as an organisational control rather than a one-time technical configuration. Change management, exception handling, and third-party (including cloud provider) oversight must be embedded into governance frameworks. Board-level visibility of segregation compliance is expected given the Standard marker.

requirements.requirement: Maintain a Board-approved Environment Segregation Policy that explicitly references BNM RMiT Clause 10.7, defines the scope of segregation (on-premises and cloud), assigns accountability to named roles, and establishes the consequence framework for violations including mandatory escalation to BNM where required.
requirements.rationale: The clause imposes an obligation on 'a financial institution' — a legal entity — requiring institutional-level policy ownership. Board approval ensures the obligation is acknowledged at the highest governance level and creates the accountability chain required for regulatory examination.
requirements.frequency: Policy approval: Annual; Interim review: Triggered by material changes to cloud or infrastructure strategy

requirements.requirement: Implement a formal exception management process for any temporary or emergency deviation from environment segregation requirements, requiring documented business justification, CTO and CISO co-approval, a defined remediation timeline (not exceeding 30 days), and notification to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: The clause contains no built-in exceptions. Any deviation — however operationally justified — constitutes a non-compliance event that must be managed transparently. A formal exception process demonstrates that the institution is in control even when deviations occur, which is critical for BNM examination.
requirements.frequency: Per-exception; Exception register reviewed: Monthly by Technology Risk Committee

requirements.requirement: Include environment segregation as a mandatory scope item in the annual technology audit programme, requiring independent verification (internal audit or qualified external auditor) that production, development, and testing environments are physically segregated and — for cloud — not co-hosted on the same virtual host.
requirements.rationale: Self-attestation alone is insufficient for a Standard marker clause. Independent audit verification produces the objective evidence BNM examiners require and provides the institution with assurance that technical controls are operating as designed.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Chief Audit Executive
requirements.frequency: Annual audit; Ad hoc review: Following any significant infrastructure change or breach

requirements.requirement: Incorporate environment segregation obligations into all cloud service provider and technology vendor contracts, including the right to audit, requirements for physical or logical host isolation, and contractual warranties that the provider's architecture supports compliance with BNM RMiT Clause 10.7.
requirements.rationale: Where the institution 'is relying on a cloud environment,' the clause places the compliance obligation on the financial institution, not the provider. Contractual protections and audit rights are required to ensure the institution can demonstrate and enforce segregation at the provider layer.
requirements.owner: Chief Procurement Officer / Head of Vendor Management / CTO
requirements.frequency: Contract negotiation: Per engagement; Contractual compliance review: Annual

requirements.requirement: Maintain an up-to-date environment inventory register documenting all production, development, and testing environments (including cloud tenancies, subscriptions, accounts, and virtual host configurations), their owners, segregation mechanisms, and last-verified compliance status — made available to BNM examiners on request.
requirements.rationale: Demonstrating compliance with the clause requires the institution to produce evidence of what environments exist and how they are segregated. An authoritative register enables audit, incident response, and regulatory examination, and supports governance of environment sprawl in complex cloud architectures.
requirements.frequency: Register maintenance: Continuous; Formal reconciliation: Quarterly

business.summary: Inadequate system testing before deployment exposes the institution to operational failures, customer-impacting outages, and regulatory penalties arising from systems that do not meet defined user requirements. The use of real sensitive data in test environments without controls creates material data breach risk, which under PDPA 2010 and BNM's own expectations carries reputational and legal consequences. Compliance with this clause directly underpins operational resilience and customer trust.

requirements.requirement: Establish and formally approve a System Testing Policy that mandates rigorous, multi-phase testing (functional, regression, performance, user acceptance) as a mandatory gate prior to any system deployment, including upgrades and patches to production.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires a 'sound methodology' for 'rigorous system testing prior to deployment.' A formal policy ensures testing is not skipped under delivery pressure and that business stakeholders are accountable for approving system readiness before go-live.
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed annually; applied per-deployment event

requirements.requirement: Define and document measurable user acceptance criteria for each system prior to test commencement, ensuring business owners formally sign off that the system 'meets user requirements' before production deployment is authorised.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires the system to 'meet user requirements.' Without pre-defined, measurable acceptance criteria and formal business sign-off, this condition cannot be objectively demonstrated to regulators or auditors.
requirements.frequency: Per-deployment event (pre-testing phase)

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Sensitive Test Data Authorisation Framework that requires documented business justification, senior management approval, and time-bound access grants whenever real or sensitive customer data must be used in test environments.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly requires 'proper authorisation procedures' where sensitive test data is used. Without a business-level framework, teams may default to using live data without oversight, creating data protection and regulatory exposure.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (each instance of sensitive data use in testing); framework reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Maintain a deployment readiness checklist that captures test completion evidence, UAT sign-off, sensitive data handling declarations, and performance benchmark results as mandatory pre-conditions for change advisory board (CAB) approval.
requirements.rationale: Linking testing outcomes to the deployment approval process ensures the 'prior to deployment' requirement in the clause is operationally enforced and provides an audit trail demonstrating compliance.
requirements.frequency: Per-deployment event

requirements.requirement: Conduct post-deployment reviews within 30 days of major system releases to assess whether testing methodology effectively detected defects, and feed lessons learned back into the testing policy on a continuous improvement basis.
requirements.rationale: A 'sound methodology' implies ongoing refinement. Post-deployment reviews provide empirical evidence that the methodology is achieving its intent and help identify gaps before they manifest in future deployments.
requirements.frequency: Per major release; summary report to Board IT Committee annually

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement a structured, documented testing framework covering all required test types across isolated, controlled environments, with automated tooling to enforce coverage thresholds and demonstrate that systems 'perform robustly.' Sensitive test data must be protected through technical controls including data masking, anonymisation, access controls, and audit logging that prevent unauthorised disclosure at the platform level. These controls must be repeatable, measurable, and auditable to satisfy BNM examination requirements.

requirements.requirement: Implement a formalised, documented testing framework covering at minimum: unit testing, integration testing, system integration testing (SIT), user acceptance testing (UAT), regression testing, performance/load testing, and security testing — with defined entry/exit criteria and minimum pass thresholds for each phase.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires a 'sound methodology for rigorous system testing.' Rigour implies structured, multi-layered testing with measurable outcomes. A single undocumented UAT round does not constitute a sound methodology under BNM's expectations.
requirements.frequency: Framework reviewed annually; applied per-deployment

requirements.requirement: Maintain dedicated, isolated test environments (SIT, UAT, staging) that are logically and technically segregated from production systems, with access controls ensuring only authorised personnel can promote code between environments.
requirements.rationale: Testing in production or in insufficiently isolated environments undermines the ability to detect defects before deployment and creates risk of exposing production data. Environment segregation is a prerequisite for a 'sound testing methodology.'
requirements.frequency: Continuous (environment integrity); access reviews quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement data masking, tokenisation, or synthetic data generation tooling to anonymise sensitive customer or production data before it is loaded into test environments. Real sensitive data must only be permitted in test environments under the approved authorisation framework, and must be purged upon test completion.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'adequate measures to prevent unauthorised disclosure' of sensitive test data. Technical data masking controls are the primary preventive measure, reducing reliance on procedural controls alone and directly satisfying this regulatory obligation.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement via automated pipeline; tooling reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Enforce role-based access control (RBAC) and audit logging on all test environments, capturing who accessed sensitive test data, when, and what actions were performed. Logs must be retained in accordance with BNM's record retention requirements and be tamper-evident.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'proper authorisation procedures' and measures to prevent 'unauthorised disclosure.' Access controls and audit logs provide both the preventive and detective controls needed to demonstrate compliance and investigate incidents.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (automated logging); log reviews monthly; access reviews quarterly

requirements.requirement: Integrate automated test coverage reporting and performance benchmarking into the CI/CD pipeline, producing mandatory test evidence artefacts (test plans, test results, defect logs, performance reports) that are stored in a version-controlled repository and linked to each deployment record.
requirements.rationale: BNM examination requires demonstrable evidence that testing was conducted rigorously. Automated evidence generation ensures artefacts are consistently produced, traceable, and not fabricated retrospectively, directly supporting audit readiness.
requirements.frequency: Per-deployment (automated); pipeline configuration reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Conduct performance and resilience testing under peak-load conditions that simulate realistic production traffic scenarios, with acceptance thresholds defined for response times, throughput, and failure recovery — ensuring the system 'performs robustly' before deployment approval.
requirements.rationale: The clause's requirement that systems 'perform robustly' extends beyond functional correctness to stability under stress. Without load and resilience testing, functional testing alone cannot assure operational performance post-deployment.
requirements.frequency: Per major release; annually for existing systems under significant load change

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that the testing methodology is formally documented, periodically reviewed, and enforced through an independent oversight function that prevents self-certification by development teams. The authorisation and control of sensitive test data must be governed through a defined approval hierarchy with clear accountability, and testing outcomes must be reported to senior management and the Board to demonstrate ongoing compliance. Audit and examination readiness requires that testing artefacts and sensitive data access records are retained and available on demand.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Board-approved System Testing Policy and associated Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) that codify the testing methodology, define roles and responsibilities, and set minimum requirements for all system deployments — reviewed and reaffirmed at least annually or upon material regulatory or organisational change.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to 'establish a sound methodology,' implying a documented, institutionalised standard rather than ad hoc practice. Board approval elevates accountability and ensures the methodology cannot be bypassed at management discretion.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; event-triggered review upon material change

requirements.requirement: Implement an independent testing oversight function (separate from the development team) responsible for reviewing test plans, validating test results, and issuing an independent testing completion certificate before deployment authorisation is granted by the Change Advisory Board (CAB).
requirements.rationale: Independence in testing oversight prevents conflicts of interest where developers self-certify their own work. This directly supports the requirement for a 'rigorous' methodology and aligns with BNM's broader expectation of adequate checks and balances in technology operations.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Audit / Head of IT Quality Assurance
requirements.frequency: Per-deployment event

requirements.requirement: Establish a Sensitive Test Data Governance Committee or designate a named authorising officer (minimum Deputy CRO or equivalent) with documented authority to approve, reject, or revoke access to sensitive data in test environments, with all decisions recorded in a centralised register.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly mandates 'proper authorisation procedures' for sensitive test data use. A named governance body or officer with recorded decision-making ensures accountability, prevents unauthorised approvals, and creates the audit trail BNM would expect to examine.
requirements.frequency: Per-event approvals; register reviewed monthly; governance structure reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Produce and submit quarterly testing governance reports to the Board IT Committee or equivalent, summarising: number of deployments, testing phases completed, critical defects detected and resolved, instances of sensitive test data usage and their authorisation status, and any testing methodology exceptions granted.
requirements.rationale: Board-level visibility ensures that the institution's testing posture is monitored at the highest level and that systemic weaknesses are escalated before they result in compliance failures or operational incidents. This supports BNM's expectation of active Board oversight of technology risk.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting to Board IT/Risk Committee

requirements.requirement: Ensure that all testing artefacts (test plans, test results, defect logs, UAT sign-offs, sensitive data authorisation records, and access logs) are retained for a minimum period consistent with BNM's record retention requirements, indexed by system and deployment, and retrievable within 5 business days for regulatory examination purposes.
requirements.rationale: BNM examination of this clause requires the institution to demonstrate, with evidence, that testing was conducted as required. Inadequate retention or retrieval of artefacts renders compliance undemonstrable, even where testing was correctly performed.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Governance / Company Secretary (for policy)
requirements.frequency: Continuous (automated retention); retrieval readiness tested semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Include system testing methodology compliance as a standing item in the Internal Audit annual plan, with targeted audits of at least two production deployments per year assessing adherence to the testing policy, sensitive data controls, and authorisation procedures, with findings reported to the Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: Independent internal audit provides the third line of assurance that the methodology established under this clause is being operationally followed and that controls over sensitive test data are effective. This creates a governance feedback loop that BNM expects to be in place.
requirements.frequency: Annual audit plan inclusion; minimum two targeted reviews per year

business.summary: Clause 10.9 defines the permissible and expected breadth of system testing activities that financial institutions must apply before deploying or modifying systems. Failure to conduct sufficiently comprehensive testing exposes the institution to operational disruptions, regulatory non-compliance events, reputational harm from customer-facing failures, and potential financial loss from system defects reaching production. As a Guidance (G) marker clause, it sets BNM's expectations for what a well-governed testing programme should encompass, giving institutions flexibility while signalling scrutiny on completeness.

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal System Testing Policy that mandates applicability assessment of all eight test types named in Clause 10.9 — unit, integration, UAT, application security, stress and load, regression, exception, and negative testing — for every new system acquisition or material change, with documented justification where any test type is deemed not applicable.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text uses 'where applicable', requiring institutions to make and record a conscious applicability decision rather than silently omitting test types. A formal policy prevents ad hoc testing scope reductions that increase operational and compliance risk.
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed annually; applicability assessment performed per system development or acquisition event

requirements.requirement: Ensure User Acceptance Testing (UAT) is conducted with active business unit participation for all customer-facing and operationally critical systems, with sign-off from the relevant business process owner before production deployment.
requirements.rationale: UAT is explicitly named in Clause 10.9 and exists to validate that systems meet business requirements in real-world conditions. Without structured business-side participation, defects affecting customer experience or regulatory reporting may only surface post-deployment, creating operational risk and potential MAS/BNM reporting obligations.
requirements.frequency: Per system release or material change

requirements.requirement: Incorporate stress and load testing results as a mandatory gate criterion for systems supporting high-transaction-volume or time-critical business processes (e.g., payments, core banking, trading platforms), with defined pass/fail thresholds aligned to peak business volume projections.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.9 explicitly lists stress and load testing. For financial institutions, system degradation under peak load can constitute a service disruption reportable to BNM under RMiT paragraph 11 and may cause direct financial and reputational harm. Business-defined thresholds ensure testing reflects real operational demands.
requirements.frequency: Per major release; annually for critical systems at minimum

requirements.requirement: Mandate that exception and negative testing outcomes are reviewed by business process owners to confirm that system error-handling behaviour aligns with customer communication standards, regulatory obligations, and operational recovery procedures.
requirements.rationale: Exception and negative testing, named in Clause 10.9, validates how systems behave under invalid inputs and failure conditions. Business review of these outcomes ensures that failure modes do not expose customers to misleading messages, data loss, or uncompliant processing outcomes.
requirements.frequency: Per system release or material change

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement structured, tooled, and evidence-producing testing pipelines that systematically address each of the eight test types referenced in Clause 10.9, calibrated to the risk profile of the system under test. Testing activities must be integrated into the SDLC or acquisition lifecycle and must generate auditable artefacts demonstrating scope, execution, and outcomes. Omission of any test type requires documented technical justification.

requirements.requirement: Implement a CI/CD pipeline that integrates automated unit and integration testing as mandatory pipeline stages, with test coverage thresholds (minimum 80% line coverage for critical modules) enforced as build gates preventing promotion to subsequent environments.
requirements.rationale: Unit and integration testing are the first two types listed in Clause 10.9. Automating them in CI/CD ensures consistent execution across every code change, produces traceable artefacts, and reduces human error in coverage assessment.
requirements.frequency: Every code commit and build; thresholds reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Integrate application security testing (SAST and DAST) tooling into the SDLC pipeline for all internally developed systems, and mandate third-party penetration testing for externally exposed or high-risk systems, with critical and high findings remediated before production release.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.9 explicitly names application security testing. For BNM-regulated institutions, undetected security vulnerabilities in production systems may constitute a technology risk incident requiring reporting. Automated SAST/DAST provides shift-left coverage while penetration testing validates runtime security posture.
requirements.frequency: SAST/DAST per build; penetration testing annually or per major release for high-risk systems

requirements.requirement: Define and execute stress and load test scenarios using representative production traffic profiles, simulating at least 150% of peak projected transaction volumes, for all systems with defined SLAs or RPO/RTO commitments, with results benchmarked against capacity planning models.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.9 names stress and load testing as a test scope component. Financial systems must demonstrate they can sustain peak loads without service degradation — failure to do so risks SLA breach, potential BNM-reportable outages, and customer harm. Results must be retained as audit evidence.
requirements.frequency: Per major release; annually for tier-1 systems; following significant infrastructure changes

requirements.requirement: Maintain a regression test suite for all production systems that covers documented business-critical workflows, executed in full before each production release and upon system changes affecting shared components, with a delta regression suite for minor patches.
requirements.rationale: Regression testing is explicitly listed in Clause 10.9. Without structured regression coverage, changes to one system component may silently break previously validated functionality, leading to production defects that create operational risk and undermine confidence in the change management process.
requirements.frequency: Per release; reviewed and updated quarterly or following major functional changes

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain exception and negative test cases for all system input boundaries, API endpoints, and failure paths, ensuring the system correctly handles malformed inputs, boundary violations, and upstream dependency failures without data corruption or unhandled exceptions.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.9 lists both exception testing and negative testing as distinct scope items. Financial systems processing payments, credit decisions, or regulatory data must be validated against invalid and adversarial inputs to prevent data integrity failures, silent processing errors, or exploitable fault conditions.
requirements.frequency: Per release; updated whenever new input surfaces or system interfaces are modified

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that testing scope decisions are documented, reviewed, and independently verified as part of the SDLC lifecycle governance. The institution requires a Testing Strategy Framework that maps applicable test types to system risk tiers, oversight mechanisms to validate completeness of testing evidence before change approval, and periodic assurance reviews to confirm that testing practices remain aligned with BNM's Clause 10.9 expectations. As a G-marker clause, BNM expects institutions to demonstrate reasoned judgement in scoping — making governance of that judgement essential.

requirements.requirement: Establish a Testing Strategy Framework that classifies all systems by risk tier (e.g., Critical, High, Medium, Low) and maps each tier to mandatory, conditional, and optional test types from the Clause 10.9 list, with applicability rationale templates for each classification.
requirements.rationale: The 'where applicable' qualifier in Clause 10.9 requires structured, defensible applicability decisions. A tiered framework ensures consistent, risk-proportionate scoping rather than ad hoc judgement, and provides auditable evidence to BNM examiners that omitted test types were consciously evaluated.
requirements.frequency: Framework reviewed annually; applied at project inception for each system

requirements.requirement: Require a Test Completion Report for every system deployment or material change, signed by the Head of IT or delegate, documenting the test types executed, coverage achieved, defects identified and resolved, and documented rationale for any test types assessed as not applicable.
requirements.rationale: Regulatory examination of RMiT compliance will focus on documented evidence of testing scope decisions. A signed Test Completion Report creates accountability, establishes an audit trail per Clause 10.9's enumerated test types, and serves as a prerequisite for the Change Advisory Board deployment approval.
requirements.frequency: Per system release or material change event

requirements.requirement: Mandate independent review of Test Completion Reports by the IT Risk function or Internal Audit for Tier 1 (Critical) systems before production deployment approval, assessing completeness of test coverage against the approved Testing Strategy Framework.
requirements.rationale: Self-certification by project teams creates a conflict of interest where delivery pressure may result in compressed or incomplete testing. Independent review — referencing the eight test types in Clause 10.9 — provides the second line of defence required under RMiT's three-lines-of-defence model.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Internal Audit
requirements.frequency: Per Tier 1 system release; sample-based review for Tier 2 systems quarterly

requirements.requirement: Include system testing scope and effectiveness as a standing agenda item in the Technology Risk Committee, with quarterly reporting on: test type coverage rates across releases, defect escape rates (production defects not caught during testing), and open waivers for skipped test types.
requirements.rationale: Governance visibility at the committee level ensures that systemic weaknesses in testing coverage — such as persistent omission of security testing or load testing — are escalated and remediated. Defect escape rates directly measure Clause 10.9 compliance effectiveness in protecting the institution from production failures.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting; ad hoc escalation for material testing failures

requirements.requirement: Include the Testing Strategy Framework and a sample of Test Completion Reports in the annual RMiT self-assessment submission, with evidence demonstrating that all eight test types in Clause 10.9 have been evaluated for applicability across the institution's material system portfolio.
requirements.rationale: BNM's RMiT self-assessment process requires institutions to evidence compliance with guidance clauses. Proactively documenting Clause 10.9 compliance — including applicability decisions and outcomes — reduces regulatory examination risk and demonstrates mature technology governance to BNM.
requirements.frequency: Annually, aligned to BNM RMiT self-assessment cycle

10.10.title: Source Code Review for Critical Systems

business.summary: Failure to enforce adequate source code reviews on critical systems exposes the institution to operational disruptions, data breaches, and regulatory sanctions arising from insecure or substandard code reaching production. This clause places a direct obligation on the institution to treat source code integrity as a business risk control — not merely a technical formality. Non-compliance with this Standard (S) clause may trigger BNM supervisory action, reputational damage, and potential liability from system failures affecting customers.

requirements.requirement: The institution must maintain a formally approved and current inventory of all systems classified as 'critical systems', with each system explicitly subject to mandatory source code review requirements prior to any change being promoted to production.
requirements.rationale: The clause scopes its obligation to 'critical systems' — without a defined and approved inventory, the institution cannot demonstrate compliance. Ambiguity in scope creates regulatory exposure and operational risk from unreviewed changes reaching high-impact systems.
requirements.frequency: Annual review of inventory; updated on-event upon any system classification change

requirements.requirement: The institution must establish a business risk policy that prohibits deployment of any source code changes to critical systems without documented evidence of a completed, adequate source code review, enforced as a mandatory gate in the change approval process.
requirements.rationale: The phrase 'prior to introducing any system changes' mandates a pre-deployment gate. Without a policy-level prohibition backed by the change management process, code changes may bypass review under time or business pressure, directly violating this clause.
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed annually; gate enforced per-event on every change

requirements.requirement: Business units sponsoring changes to critical systems must formally accept residual risk in writing when a source code review identifies findings, and must not approve deployment unless the review findings have been remediated or formally risk-accepted at the appropriate authority level.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires reviews that 'ensure code is secure' — this implies findings must be resolved, not ignored. Embedding risk acceptance accountability at the business owner level ensures that code security is not treated as a technical afterthought and that business sponsors share responsibility for secure releases.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) and respective Business Unit Head
requirements.frequency: Per-event, for each change to a critical system

requirements.requirement: The institution must include source code review effectiveness as a standing agenda item in the Technology Risk Committee, with metrics on review coverage, findings, and remediation rates reported at least quarterly to senior management.
requirements.rationale: Adequate oversight of 'adequate source code reviews' requires senior management visibility into whether the control is functioning effectively. Without management-level reporting, systemic weaknesses in the review programme may go undetected until an incident occurs.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting to Technology Risk Committee

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement tooling, workflows, and technical standards that make source code review a mandatory, evidence-producing, and auditable step in every change pipeline for critical systems. The review programme must cover both security (the code is secure) and quality (developed in line with recognised coding practices), requiring automated and manual review layers. All review outcomes must be traceable, retained, and available for regulatory examination.

requirements.requirement: The institution must deploy and configure Static Application Security Testing (SAST) tooling integrated into the CI/CD pipeline for all critical systems, configured to enforce a policy-defined set of security rules aligned to a recognised standard (e.g., OWASP Top 10, CWE/SANS Top 25), with pipeline promotion blocked on critical or high-severity findings unless formally risk-accepted.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires code to be 'secure' prior to deployment. SAST provides automated, repeatable, and auditable security scanning that ensures every code change is assessed for known vulnerability patterns before reaching production. Manual review alone cannot scale to meet the 'prior to introducing any system changes' requirement across all critical systems.
requirements.frequency: Automated execution on every code commit and pull request; tool configuration reviewed semi-annually

requirements.requirement: The institution must enforce mandatory peer code review via a version-controlled code collaboration platform (e.g., pull request workflow with branch protection rules) for all critical systems, requiring a minimum of two qualified reviewer approvals before merge, with no self-approval permitted. Review records, including reviewer identities, timestamps, comments, and approval status, must be retained.
requirements.rationale: Automated tooling does not replace human judgement for logic flaws, architectural issues, or context-specific security concerns. The 'adequate source code reviews' requirement implies human review as a control layer. Branch protection and audit trails provide the evidentiary record needed to demonstrate compliance to BNM.
requirements.frequency: Enforced per-event on every change; branch protection configuration reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: The institution must adopt and publish a Secure Coding Standard for each primary development language used in critical systems, based on a recognised framework (e.g., OWASP Secure Coding Practices, SEI CERT Coding Standards, NIST SP 800-218 SSDF). Reviewers must verify compliance with this standard as part of every code review for critical systems.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires code to be 'developed in line with recognised coding practices'. Without a defined, adopted, and enforced standard, there is no objective benchmark against which reviewers can assess compliance, making the review programme inadequate by the clause's own terms.
requirements.owner: Head of Application Security / Head of Software Engineering
requirements.frequency: Standard reviewed and updated annually or upon material changes to technology stack

requirements.requirement: The institution must implement Software Composition Analysis (SCA) tooling for all critical systems to detect vulnerabilities in third-party libraries and open-source dependencies included in source code changes, with critical/high CVE findings blocking deployment unless patched or formally risk-accepted.
requirements.rationale: Third-party dependencies are part of the source code base and are a common attack vector (e.g., supply chain vulnerabilities). A review that focuses only on first-party code but ignores dependency vulnerabilities cannot be considered 'adequate' or ensure the code is 'secure' as required by the clause.
requirements.frequency: Automated on every build; dependency vulnerability database refreshed continuously

requirements.requirement: The institution must maintain a centralised, tamper-evident audit log of all source code review activities for critical systems — including reviewer identity, review date, tool scan results, findings, remediation evidence, and final approval — retained for a minimum period aligned with BNM record-keeping requirements and accessible for regulatory examination.
requirements.rationale: BNM examination of compliance with this clause requires documentary evidence that reviews occurred, were adequate, and preceded deployment. Without a centralised and immutable record, the institution cannot demonstrate that the control operated effectively even if it was functionally in place.
requirements.frequency: Continuous log capture; log integrity reviewed quarterly; retention managed per BNM policy

governance.summary: The institution must establish a governance framework that gives the source code review obligation institutional authority, clear accountability, and independent oversight. This includes a policy mandating reviews, defined roles and competency requirements for reviewers, an exception management process, and assurance activities that verify the control is operating as intended. Governance structures must ensure that commercial or operational pressures cannot override the review requirement for critical systems.

requirements.requirement: The institution must maintain a Board-approved Source Code Review Policy (or equivalent Secure SDLC Policy) that explicitly mandates adequate source code reviews for all changes to critical systems prior to deployment, defines 'adequate', specifies minimum review criteria (security and coding practices), and assigns accountability for policy compliance.
requirements.rationale: An 'S' marker clause imposes an institutional obligation — not merely a technical best practice. Policy formalisation at Board or senior management level ensures the obligation has the authority required to override project timelines or delivery pressure, and provides the governance anchor for all downstream controls.
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed and approved annually; updated upon material regulatory or technology changes

requirements.requirement: The institution must define and enforce minimum competency requirements for personnel authorised to conduct source code reviews on critical systems, including relevant technical qualifications, training on the institution's secure coding standard, and familiarity with the system being reviewed. A register of qualified reviewers per critical system must be maintained.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires reviews to be 'adequate' — a review conducted by an unqualified or insufficiently skilled reviewer cannot be adequate regardless of whether a formal process was followed. Competency requirements ensure review quality and provide a defensible basis for the adequacy assertion required by BNM.
requirements.owner: Chief People Officer (CPO) / Head of Human Capital (Technology)
requirements.frequency: Competency assessed at onboarding and annually; register reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: The institution must establish a documented exception management process for source code review, requiring that any exception (e.g., emergency change bypassing full review) is time-bound, approved at a senior level (minimum CISO and CTO), subject to a retrospective compensating review within 48 hours, and reported to the Technology Risk Committee at its next scheduled meeting.
requirements.rationale: Absolute controls fail in practice without a governed exception pathway, resulting in ad hoc bypasses that are invisible to management. A formal exception process maintains the integrity of the control while providing a documented, time-limited, and auditable mechanism for genuine emergencies — preventing silent non-compliance that would be indefensible under BNM examination.
requirements.frequency: Per-event for each exception; aggregate exception reporting quarterly to Technology Risk Committee

requirements.requirement: Internal Audit must include source code review control effectiveness within the annual IT audit programme, verifying that reviews for a sample of critical system changes were conducted by qualified reviewers, preceded deployment, covered both security and coding practice criteria, and produced retained evidence. Findings must be reported to the Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: First- and second-line ownership of the control must be independently verified. Without internal audit coverage, the institution cannot provide the Board and BNM with assurance that the control is operating effectively — particularly important for an 'S' clause where BNM expects demonstrable compliance rather than self-attestation.
requirements.frequency: Annual audit; findings reported to Audit Committee within 30 days of fieldwork completion

requirements.requirement: The institution must ensure that source code review completion and findings closure are mandatory prerequisites in the Change Advisory Board (CAB) approval checklist for all critical system changes, with CAB minutes recording the review status and approving authority sign-off. No critical system change may receive CAB approval without documented evidence of a completed adequate source code review.
requirements.rationale: The clause's 'prior to introducing any system changes' requirement demands that the review gate be embedded in the formal change governance process — not left to individual development teams. CAB-level enforcement ensures cross-functional accountability and creates a governance-layer control that cannot be bypassed without committee-level visibility.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Change Management / COO
requirements.frequency: Enforced per-event on every critical system change request; CAB process reviewed annually

business.summary: Unreviewed or poorly approved system changes represent a direct operational risk — failed deployments can cause service outages, financial loss, and reputational harm to the institution. BNM requires that change review be independent (i.e., not solely the implementing team) and that contingency plans exist and are tested before material changes go live. Failure to comply exposes the institution to regulatory sanction and, more critically, to prolonged business disruption when changes fail in production.

requirements.requirement: Define and document what constitutes a 'material change' to financial systems, including thresholds for risk classification (e.g., core banking upgrades, payment engine modifications, regulatory reporting changes), so that contingency planning obligations are triggered consistently.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text distinguishes 'material changes' as requiring contingency plans. Without a formal definition, institutions cannot consistently identify when the heightened obligations apply, creating compliance gaps and inconsistent risk treatment across business lines.
requirements.frequency: Annual review of thresholds; applied per-event at change classification stage

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Change Advisory Board (CAB) with representation from business operations, risk, and compliance functions to review and approve system changes independently of the implementing technology team.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates 'independent' review and approval. A CAB with multi-disciplinary, non-implementing membership satisfies the independence requirement and ensures business impact is assessed alongside technical feasibility before approval.
requirements.frequency: Per change request; emergency changes reviewed within 24 hours with post-implementation review

requirements.requirement: Define business recovery time objectives (RTOs) and recovery point objectives (RPOs) for each material system change, and require these to be incorporated into the contingency plan for that change prior to implementation approval.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires contingency plans that 'minimise any business disruption.' Without RTO/RPO targets tied to specific changes, contingency plans lack measurable success criteria and may fail to protect critical business services.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (each material change); reviewed annually against updated business impact assessments

requirements.requirement: Maintain a centralised change register accessible to senior management that records all system changes, their classification (material vs. non-material), review and approval status, contingency plan references, and post-implementation outcomes.
requirements.rationale: Demonstrating compliance with independent review and contingency planning obligations requires an auditable record. A centralised register supports both internal governance and BNM examination readiness, linking directly to the clause's requirement to 'establish appropriate procedures.'
requirements.frequency: Continuous (updated per change); summary reporting to Board Risk Committee quarterly

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement structured change management tooling, automated gating controls, and testable rollback mechanisms to operationalise the independent review and contingency planning obligations. The independence requirement means no engineer or team should be able to self-approve and deploy a change to production without a documented, tooling-enforced approval workflow. Contingency plans must not exist only on paper — they must be executable artefacts tested before material changes are released.

requirements.requirement: Implement a change management platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) with mandatory workflow enforcement that prevents deployment to production unless independent reviewer approval is recorded and linked to the change ticket. Approver accounts must be technically segregated from the submitting team.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'appropriate procedures to independently review and approve.' Manual or honour-based processes are insufficient — tooling must enforce segregation so that the implementing team cannot bypass the review gate, providing both a control and an audit trail.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; platform configuration reviewed semi-annually

requirements.requirement: For every material system change, prepare and version-control a rollback or backout plan as a mandatory artefact within the change ticket. The plan must include specific rollback commands, responsible personnel, estimated rollback duration, and success/failure criteria.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires contingency plans 'in the event of an unsuccessful implementation of material changes.' A documented, version-controlled rollback plan is the primary technical contingency artefact. Without specificity, a plan cannot be executed reliably under time pressure.
requirements.frequency: Per material change; artefact quality reviewed by the CAB as part of change approval

requirements.requirement: Mandate pre-production testing of contingency plans for all material changes, including dry-run rollback exercises in a staging environment that mirrors production, with test results documented and attached to the change record before CAB sign-off.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires that contingency plans be 'tested' — not merely documented. Untested rollback plans frequently fail in production incidents. Staging environment testing closes this gap and provides evidence that the plan is executable.
requirements.frequency: Per material change (prior to production deployment window)

requirements.requirement: Implement automated deployment pipeline controls that enforce a post-deployment health check window with predefined pass/fail criteria. If health checks fail within the observation window, the pipeline must halt and trigger the contingency plan automatically or alert the on-call team to initiate rollback.
requirements.rationale: Automating the detection of an unsuccessful implementation and triggering contingency response directly supports the clause objective of minimising business disruption. Manual detection adds latency during which customer impact accumulates.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; pipeline logic reviewed quarterly and after any major incident

requirements.requirement: Implement environment parity controls to ensure staging/UAT environments used for contingency plan testing are maintained at a configuration level within a defined threshold (e.g., ≤7 days infrastructure drift) of the production environment at the time of testing.
requirements.rationale: Contingency plan tests conducted in environments that differ significantly from production produce misleading results. Environment parity is a prerequisite for meaningful testing, ensuring the 'established and tested' standard in the clause is substantively met rather than nominally satisfied.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; parity gap report reviewed monthly

governance.summary: Governance structures must formalise the independence of the change review function, set policy-level requirements for contingency planning, and ensure Board-level visibility into the management of material system changes. The 'S' (Standard) marker indicates this is a non-negotiable baseline — governance frameworks must treat non-compliance with independent review or untested contingency plans as a reportable control failure. Periodic assurance activities must confirm that procedures remain effective and are consistently applied.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Board-approved Change Management Policy that explicitly mandates independent review and approval of system changes, defines materiality thresholds, requires documented and tested contingency plans for material changes, and assigns ownership responsibilities to named roles.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to 'establish appropriate procedures.' A Board-approved policy is the governance instrument that formally establishes these procedures, ensures they carry institutional authority, and demonstrates regulatory compliance intent to BNM examiners.
requirements.frequency: Annual review and reaffirmation; immediate update upon material regulatory or operational change

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal independence framework for the change review function, defining which roles are prohibited from approving their own team's changes, documenting conflict of interest escalation procedures, and requiring that CAB membership is reviewed and approved by the CRO annually.
requirements.rationale: The word 'independently' in the verbatim text is the governing constraint on the review function. Without a formal independence framework, the review may be structurally or practically compromised — for example, if a technology head both implements and approves changes within their own division.
requirements.frequency: Annual membership review; independence breaches reported to Board Risk Committee ad hoc

requirements.requirement: Commission periodic independent assurance (internal audit or qualified external reviewer) of the change management and contingency planning processes to verify that procedures are being followed, contingency plans are being tested as required, and approval records are complete and accurately reflect independent review.
requirements.rationale: BNM's 'S' marker indicates this is a standard that must demonstrably hold in practice, not just in policy. Internal audit provides the Board with objective assurance that the controls are operating effectively and surfaces deviations before they become regulatory findings.
requirements.frequency: Annual audit; targeted reviews following any failed change implementation or material incident

requirements.requirement: Establish a post-implementation review (PIR) process for all material changes, requiring documented assessment of whether the change achieved its objectives, whether any contingency plans were invoked, root cause analysis if implementation was unsuccessful, and lessons learned fed back into the Change Management Policy.
requirements.rationale: The clause's requirement to 'minimise business disruption' is a continuous improvement obligation, not a one-time exercise. PIRs create the feedback loop necessary to refine contingency plans and review procedures over time, and provide governance evidence of institutional learning.
requirements.frequency: Per material change (within 10 business days of implementation); aggregate trends reviewed by Board Risk Committee semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Define and operationalise Key Risk Indicators (KRIs) for the change management control environment — including percentage of material changes with tested contingency plans, change-induced incident rate, and rollback success rate — with breach thresholds triggering escalation to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: Governance requires measurable oversight, not just procedural compliance. KRIs provide the Board and senior management with quantitative signals of whether the independent review and contingency planning processes are performing effectively, enabling proactive intervention before control failures manifest as regulatory or operational incidents.
requirements.frequency: Monthly KRI reporting to senior management; quarterly escalation to Board Risk Committee

10.12.title: Third Party Service Provider System Changes

business.summary: This clause addresses the operational and reputational risk arising from unmanaged third party changes to critical systems. Without contractual controls over TPSP change notifications, secure development practices, and source code access, a financial institution faces unplanned service disruptions, exposure to supply chain cyber attacks, and inability to recover critical systems during a continuity event. Non-compliance could trigger BNM supervisory action and erode customer trust.

requirements.requirement: Maintain a register of all critical systems developed or maintained by third party service providers, clearly documenting which systems fall within scope of Clause 10.12 contractual obligations.
requirements.rationale: The clause is scoped to 'critical systems' — without a formal register, the institution cannot demonstrate that the correct TPSP relationships are governed contractually, leaving regulatory gaps and unknown risk exposure.
requirements.frequency: Reviewed and updated at least annually, and upon onboarding or offboarding any TPSP for critical systems

requirements.requirement: Establish and enforce a minimum advance notice period (to be defined in contracts) that third party service providers must provide before implementing any changes that may impact a critical IT system, including the nature, scope, and rollback plan for the proposed change.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.12(a) explicitly requires 'sufficient notice before any changes are undertaken that may impact the IT system.' Without a defined and contractually mandated notice window, the institution has no operational lead time to assess impact, schedule downtime, or notify downstream stakeholders and customers.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (each TPSP-initiated change to critical systems); contract terms reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Negotiate and maintain source code access or escrow arrangements for all critical systems maintained by third party service providers, ensuring the institution can access, operate, or hand over the source code in a business continuity or insolvency scenario.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.12(c) requires source code to be 'readily accessible for business continuity.' Without escrow or direct repository access rights embedded in contracts, the institution risks losing access to critical system code if the TPSP exits the market, terminates the relationship, or becomes insolvent — rendering business continuity plans unexecutable.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Contractually established at onboarding; access tested at least annually and upon business continuity exercises

requirements.requirement: Conduct pre-contractual due diligence and periodic re-assessment of third party service providers' software development practices to validate alignment with secure by design principles before and during engagement for critical system development or maintenance.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.12(b) requires TPSPs to 'demonstrate' secure by design principles. Demonstration implies an active verification obligation — not mere attestation — meaning the institution must have a structured process to evaluate TPSP development methodology as part of vendor selection and ongoing oversight.
requirements.frequency: Pre-engagement (due diligence) and at least annually thereafter; triggered immediately by any material TPSP security incident

requirements.requirement: Define and document the business impact assessment (BIA) process triggered upon receipt of a TPSP change notification, including stakeholder communication protocols, customer impact evaluation, and escalation thresholds requiring senior management approval before accepting or rejecting a proposed change.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.12(a) requires the institution to receive notice of changes 'that may impact the IT system.' Receiving notice is only half the obligation — the institution must be operationally prepared to evaluate that impact and make informed decisions, otherwise the notice requirement provides no protective value.
requirements.frequency: Process documented and reviewed annually; executed per-event upon receipt of each change notification

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement controls spanning three distinct technical domains: change pipeline governance for TPSP-initiated changes, supply chain security validation covering secure by design principles, and source code custody mechanisms ensuring continuity-ready access. These controls must be operationalised through tooling, configuration baselines, and integration with the institution's broader change management and vulnerability management platforms.

requirements.requirement: Integrate TPSP change notifications into the institution's centralised IT change management platform (e.g., ITSM/CMDB), creating a dedicated change category for third party-initiated changes to critical systems, with mandatory fields capturing notice date, change window, affected systems, rollback plan, and TPSP contact.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.12(a) requires 'sufficient notice' of changes that 'may impact the IT system.' A formal ITSM intake process ensures notice is logged, timestamped, traceable, and subject to impact assessment workflows — enabling the institution to evidence compliance during BNM examinations and audit cycles.
requirements.frequency: Platform capability implemented continuously; each TPSP change record created per-event and reviewed within the defined notice window

requirements.requirement: Develop and apply a Secure by Design Assessment Framework for TPSP evaluation, covering minimum criteria including: threat modelling in the SDLC, static application security testing (SAST) integration, dependency vulnerability scanning (SCA), least-privilege architecture, and security requirements documentation — validated through audit, penetration test reports, or equivalent TPSP-provided evidence.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.12(b) requires TPSPs to 'demonstrate' adoption of secure by design principles 'to mitigate cyber risks from propagating across the supply chain.' The supply chain propagation language requires the institution to technically validate — not merely accept attestation — that upstream vulnerabilities cannot cascade into the institution's environment via TPSP-delivered code or updates.
requirements.frequency: Framework maintained continuously; TPSP assessments conducted at onboarding, annually, and following any major TPSP system release affecting critical systems

requirements.requirement: Establish source code access mechanisms for all critical systems maintained by TPSPs, including at minimum one of: (a) a third-party source code escrow agreement with a licensed escrow agent specifying release conditions, (b) institutional read access to the TPSP's version control repository (e.g., GitLab, GitHub Enterprise), or (c) a contractually mandated periodic source code delivery to the institution's own secured repository.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.12(c) requires source code to be 'readily accessible for business continuity.' 'Readily accessible' implies the institution can retrieve and operationalise the source code without TPSP cooperation — escrow or direct repository access are the only technical mechanisms that satisfy this without dependency on an unresponsive or insolvent provider.
requirements.frequency: Mechanism established at contract inception; escrow deposit or repository access verified at least annually and after each major TPSP release

requirements.requirement: Implement software composition analysis (SCA) and dependency scanning on all code and packages delivered by TPSPs for critical systems, integrated into the institution's security operations tooling, with findings reviewed before acceptance into production environments.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.12(b) specifically references mitigating 'cyber risks from propagating across the supply chain.' Supply chain attacks commonly exploit vulnerable third-party libraries and transitive dependencies — SCA tooling provides a technical control to detect and block such propagation at the point of delivery, complementing the TPSP's own secure development obligations.
requirements.frequency: Automated scanning on each software delivery from TPSP; findings reviewed per-delivery and trended quarterly

requirements.requirement: Conduct at least annual technical testing of source code access and recovery procedures for critical TPSP-maintained systems, including retrieval from escrow or repository, environment build, and functional validation — documenting results as evidence that business continuity access is genuinely 'readily accessible' as required.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.12(c) requires source code to be 'readily accessible for business continuity.' An untested escrow arrangement or repository access credential that has lapsed provides no actual continuity assurance. Periodic tested recovery validates that the access mechanism works end-to-end under realistic recovery conditions.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Operations / Business Continuity Manager
requirements.frequency: At least annually; additionally triggered by material TPSP changes, expiry of escrow agreements, or BCP exercises involving critical systems

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that contractual obligations mandated by Clause 10.12 are consistently embedded in all TPSP agreements covering critical systems, monitored for ongoing compliance, and subject to board-level oversight through the institution's third party risk management framework. Audit rights, escalation protocols, and policy enforcement mechanisms are essential to sustain compliance across the lifecycle of each TPSP relationship.

requirements.requirement: Embed mandatory contractual clauses in all agreements with third party service providers for critical system development or maintenance, explicitly requiring: (a) defined advance notice periods and change notification obligations; (b) adoption and demonstrability of secure by design principles in the TPSP's SDLC; and (c) source code accessibility rights for business continuity — with non-compliance treated as a material breach entitling the institution to remediation or termination.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.12 is explicit that obligations are imposed 'through contractual obligations.' Without standardised, mandatory contract language reviewed by legal and technology risk teams, individual contract negotiations may omit required provisions — creating regulatory exposure that is invisible until an examination or incident reveals the gap.
requirements.frequency: Contract templates reviewed at least annually; applied to all new and renewed TPSP agreements for critical systems on a continuous basis

requirements.requirement: Establish a Third Party Risk Management (TPRM) Committee or extend the remit of an existing risk committee to include oversight of Clause 10.12 compliance, with a formal Terms of Reference requiring at least quarterly reporting on: critical system TPSP inventory, outstanding contractual gaps, TPSP change notification compliance, secure by design assessment outcomes, and source code access test results.
requirements.rationale: BNM expects financial institutions to demonstrate active governance — not merely policy documentation. A formal committee with a reporting cadence ensures management accountability, creates an audit trail of oversight activity, and enables timely escalation of non-compliant TPSPs before risks materialise.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Board Risk Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Committee meets at least quarterly; annual reporting to the Board Risk Committee

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a Third Party IT Risk Policy that specifically addresses critical system TPSP obligations under Clause 10.12, including definitions of 'critical system,' 'sufficient notice,' 'secure by design,' and 'readily accessible source code,' approved by the Board and reviewed at least annually or upon material regulatory or operational changes.
requirements.rationale: Without a policy that defines key terms from the clause, implementation will be inconsistent across procurement teams, legal, and technology. Defined thresholds (e.g., minimum 14-day notice for significant changes, specific escrow release conditions) create a consistent, auditable standard that the institution can demonstrate to BNM supervisors.
requirements.frequency: Policy approved by Board annually; operationally reviewed each quarter by the TPRM function
