requirements.requirement: Establish an audit rights clause and annual compliance monitoring programme requiring critical system TPSPs to provide evidence of: compliance with change notification timelines, current secure by design certifications or assessment results (e.g., ISO 27001, OWASP SAMM, penetration test reports), and status of source code escrow deposits — with non-compliant findings tracked to resolution through a formal remediation register.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.12(b) requires TPSPs to 'demonstrate' secure by design adoption — demonstration implies the institution must actively obtain and evaluate evidence, not passively accept contractual commitments. Without audit rights and a monitoring programme, the institution cannot fulfil its oversight obligation or detect TPSP non-compliance before it translates into institutional risk.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Annual TPSP compliance review; continuous monitoring of change notification adherence; remediation register reviewed at each TPRM Committee meeting

requirements.requirement: Define and operationalise an escalation and incident response protocol for Clause 10.12 breaches, including: failure of a TPSP to provide required notice before a critical system change, discovery that a TPSP's development practices do not meet secure by design standards, or confirmed inaccessibility of source code — with escalation paths to the Board Risk Committee and, where material, notification to BNM as required under applicable reporting obligations.
requirements.rationale: The 'S' (Standard) marker reflects BNM's expectation of routine compliance. However, TPSP failures — particularly unauthorised changes to critical systems or supply chain security incidents — can rapidly escalate into material events requiring regulatory notification. A pre-defined escalation protocol ensures the institution responds promptly and proportionately, reducing regulatory and reputational consequences.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Protocol reviewed annually; activated per-event upon identification of a Clause 10.12 breach or near-miss

business.summary: Unplanned or poorly managed decommissioning of critical systems can disrupt customer-facing services, breach service level commitments, and expose the institution to reputational and regulatory risk. The clause requires the institution to proactively manage customer and operational impact throughout the decommissioning lifecycle. Failure to comply may result in BNM supervisory action, customer complaints, and financial loss from service disruptions.

requirements.requirement: Conduct a formal Customer Impact Assessment (CIA) prior to initiating any critical system decommissioning, documenting all affected customer-facing services, transaction flows, and potential service degradation scenarios.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates 'minimal adverse impact on customers', which cannot be achieved without first identifying and quantifying the scope of customer exposure. A CIA ensures that customer impact is understood, communicated, and mitigated before decommissioning begins.
requirements.owner: Chief Customer Officer / Head of Retail Banking
requirements.frequency: Per-event (prior to each critical system decommissioning exercise)

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a Customer Communication Plan for each critical system decommissioning, specifying advance notice timelines, communication channels, and escalation contacts for customers affected by service transitions or disruptions.
requirements.rationale: Customers must be informed of changes affecting services they depend on. Proactive communication directly mitigates 'adverse impact on customers' as required by the clause and aligns with BNM's broader expectations on fair treatment of financial consumers.
requirements.owner: Chief Customer Officer / Head of Corporate Communications
requirements.frequency: Per-event, with draft plan reviewed at least 30 days before decommissioning date

requirements.requirement: Perform a Business Impact Assessment (BIA) that quantifies the operational consequences of both successful and unsuccessful decommissioning, including revenue-at-risk, SLA breach exposure, and third-party contractual obligations.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires 'minimal adverse impact on business operations', which requires a documented understanding of business dependencies on the critical system. The BIA also underpins the contingency planning requirement by defining acceptable downtime thresholds and recovery objectives.
requirements.frequency: Per-event, updated if scope or timeline changes materially

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Business Sign-Off and Go/No-Go decision gate, requiring written approval from relevant business unit heads and senior management before proceeding with decommissioning and before activating contingency plans if decommissioning fails.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to 'ensure minimal adverse impact', implying deliberate management control. A structured approval gate prevents premature decommissioning and ensures accountability is assigned at the senior level for both the primary plan and the contingency response.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (at each defined gate in the decommissioning project plan)

requirements.requirement: Define and track post-decommissioning business metrics for a minimum of 90 days following completion, monitoring customer complaint volumes, transaction failure rates, and operational incident counts to confirm that adverse impact remains within approved thresholds.
requirements.rationale: Compliance with the 'minimal adverse impact' requirement extends beyond the decommissioning event itself. Post-event monitoring provides evidence of compliance and enables early detection of latent issues that may have been introduced during the decommissioning process.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer / Head of Operations
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring for 90 days post-decommissioning; summary report at 30, 60, and 90 days

technology.summary: Technology teams must deliver a structured, reversible decommissioning process with validated rollback capabilities and formally tested contingency plans. Critical system dependencies, data archival, and failover readiness must all be addressed before decommissioning is executed. The explicit requirement to 'establish and test contingency plans' means that untested plans are non-compliant regardless of their quality on paper.

requirements.requirement: Produce a comprehensive Critical System Decommissioning Technical Plan that maps all upstream and downstream system dependencies, data flows, integration endpoints, batch jobs, and scheduled processes that rely on the system being decommissioned, ensuring no hidden dependencies remain unaddressed.
requirements.rationale: Unidentified dependencies are the primary technical cause of unsuccessful decommissioning. The clause's reference to 'critical systems' implies heightened complexity and interconnectivity; a dependency map is the foundational artefact for achieving 'minimal adverse impact on business operations'.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Chief Technology Officer
requirements.frequency: Per-event; updated iteratively as system analysis progresses during the project

requirements.requirement: Design and implement a Data Archival and Retrieval Strategy prior to decommissioning, ensuring all data retained for regulatory, audit, or operational purposes is migrated to an accessible, format-stable archive that satisfies BNM and internal data retention requirements.
requirements.rationale: Critical systems frequently host transactional, customer, or regulatory data. Loss of access to this data upon decommissioning would constitute an adverse impact on both customers and regulators. The data strategy must be in place and validated before the system is taken offline.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Data Governance Lead
requirements.frequency: Per-event; archive accessibility verified for a minimum of 12 months post-decommissioning

requirements.requirement: Develop a documented Contingency and Rollback Plan that defines the technical steps, responsible parties, tooling, and maximum time-to-restore required to reverse or stabilise a failed decommissioning event, including restoration of the original system from backup if required.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly requires 'establishing contingency plans in the event of unsuccessful system decommissioning'. A rollback plan is the technical manifestation of this requirement; without defined steps and owners, the plan cannot be executed under pressure.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Head of Technology Risk
requirements.frequency: Per-event; reviewed and updated after each test execution

requirements.requirement: Execute at least one full end-to-end rehearsal of the Contingency and Rollback Plan in a non-production environment that mirrors production architecture, with documented test results, defect findings, and evidence of remediation before the production decommissioning date is approved.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly mandates 'testing contingency plans', making untested plans non-compliant. A rehearsal in a production-equivalent environment is the only credible method of validating that the rollback plan will work under real conditions. Test evidence must be retained for BNM examination.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Head of IT Infrastructure
requirements.frequency: Per-event; minimum one full rehearsal, with re-testing if material defects are found

requirements.requirement: Implement an enhanced real-time monitoring and alerting regime during and immediately after the decommissioning window, covering application availability, transaction success rates, error logs, and dependent system health, with defined escalation thresholds that trigger automatic contingency plan activation.
requirements.rationale: The contingency plan can only be activated in time to limit adverse impact if failure signals are detected immediately. Real-time monitoring with pre-defined thresholds operationalises the contingency plan requirement and directly supports the 'minimal adverse impact' obligation during the highest-risk period of the decommissioning event.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Operations / Head of IT Infrastructure
requirements.frequency: Continuous during the decommissioning window; sustained for 30 days post-completion

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that critical system decommissioning is subject to senior management oversight, formal policy, and an auditable approval process from initiation through post-completion review. The 'S' marker indicates a standard BNM requirement with compliance evidence expected to be available on demand during examination. Governance must also ensure that the testing of contingency plans is independently verified rather than self-certified by the implementation team.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Board-approved Critical System Decommissioning Policy that defines the criteria for classifying a system as 'critical', mandates the minimum artefacts required before decommissioning proceeds (CIA, BIA, Technical Plan, Contingency Plan, test evidence), and assigns accountable owners for each stage.
requirements.rationale: Without a formal policy, decommissioning decisions are made inconsistently and accountability is diffuse. The policy provides the governance framework from which all business, technology, and operational requirements flow, and establishes the institution's interpretation of BNM's 'critical systems' threshold, which is subject to supervisory scrutiny.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee / Chief Risk Officer
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed and reaffirmed annually; updated immediately upon material regulatory or organisational change

requirements.requirement: Establish a Technology Change Authority (or equivalent senior governance body) with the mandate to review and approve all critical system decommissioning proposals, including sign-off on the Business Impact Assessment, Contingency Plan, and test results before production decommissioning is authorised.
requirements.rationale: The clause places the obligation on 'a financial institution' as an entity, implying institutional-level accountability rather than individual team discretion. A formalised approval body ensures cross-functional oversight and creates a documented chain of accountability that BNM examiners can trace.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer / Chief Operating Officer
requirements.frequency: Per-event; convened at defined project gates including pre-decommissioning final approval

requirements.requirement: Require Internal Audit or an independent second-line function to review and provide a written opinion on the adequacy of the Contingency Plan and the completeness of test evidence prior to the Technology Change Authority granting final decommissioning approval.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates both 'establishing' and 'testing' contingency plans; independent review prevents self-certification bias and provides the institution with assurance that compliance obligations have been met. This also satisfies BNM's broader three-lines-of-defence expectation under RMiT.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor / Head of Technology Risk (Second Line)
requirements.frequency: Per-event; opinion issued before final go/no-go gate

requirements.requirement: Maintain a Decommissioning Register that records all critical system decommissioning activities, including system name and classification rationale, key dates, approved artefacts, contingency test outcomes, go/no-go decisions, and post-event review findings, retained for a minimum of seven years.
requirements.rationale: BNM supervisory examinations require that institutions demonstrate ongoing compliance, not just point-in-time assertions. A centralised register provides the audit trail necessary to evidence that each decommissioning exercise met the requirements of Clause 10.13 and supports trend analysis across multiple decommissioning events.
requirements.owner: Company Secretary / Head of IT Governance
requirements.frequency: Maintained continuously; reviewed by Board Risk Committee at least annually

requirements.requirement: Conduct a mandatory Post-Implementation Review (PIR) within 30 days of every critical system decommissioning, assessing actual versus anticipated customer and business impact, contingency plan effectiveness, and lessons learned, with findings reported to senior management and incorporated into the Decommissioning Policy and future project plans.
requirements.rationale: Continuous improvement of decommissioning practices is implicit in the obligation to 'ensure minimal adverse impact'. A structured PIR closes the governance loop, enables the institution to refine its contingency planning methodology over time, and demonstrates to BNM a mature risk management culture rather than checkbox compliance.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer / Chief Operating Officer
requirements.frequency: Per-event; PIR report issued within 30 calendar days of decommissioning completion

business.summary: Financial institutions that fail to adopt automated development and security tooling risk slower detection of vulnerabilities in critical systems, exposing the institution to cyber threats that exploit growing IT complexity. Without automation, manual security assessments cannot keep pace with the volume and velocity of modern system changes, increasing operational and reputational risk. BNM's guidance signals regulatory expectation that institutions demonstrate maturity in their SDLC security posture.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formal Automated Tooling Strategy that identifies the suite of automated tools deployed across the software development lifecycle — covering development, testing, deployment, change management, code scanning, and version control — aligned to the institution's critical system inventory.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text enumerates six distinct tooling categories. A documented strategy ensures the institution can demonstrate to BNM that automation has been deliberately applied across all enumerated domains, and that coverage gaps are identified and remediated.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; updated upon material changes to the critical system landscape

requirements.requirement: Define and maintain a formal Critical Systems Register that is used to prioritise timely security assessments facilitated by automated tooling, ensuring highest-risk systems receive the most frequent automated security scanning and testing cycles.
requirements.rationale: The clause specifically references 'timely security assessment of critical systems'. Without a defined critical systems list linked to automated tooling outputs, the institution cannot demonstrate that its automation investment is directed at the highest-risk areas as implied by the regulatory text.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly review of register; continuous automated assessment against listed systems

requirements.requirement: Secure annual budget allocation for procurement, licensing, maintenance, and upskilling associated with automated development and security tools, with a business case referencing emerging cyber threats and growing IT system complexity as justification.
requirements.rationale: The clause frames automation as a response to 'growing complexity in IT systems and emerging cyber threats'. Budget commitment signals institutional intent and ensures tooling remains current against an evolving threat landscape; absence of dedicated funding is a common compliance gap during BNM examinations.
requirements.frequency: Annual (budget cycle); ad-hoc for unplanned critical system additions

requirements.requirement: Establish key performance indicators (KPIs) for automated tooling effectiveness — including mean time to detect vulnerabilities, code scan coverage rate, and percentage of deployments with automated security gates — reported to senior management quarterly.
requirements.rationale: Demonstrating that automated tools 'facilitate timely security assessment' requires measurable outcomes. Management reporting on tooling KPIs provides oversight evidence and supports continuous improvement, both expected under BNM's RMiT governance principles.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting; KPI thresholds reviewed annually

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement an integrated, automated toolchain covering all six categories cited in clause 10.14 — development, testing, deployment, change management, code scanning, and version control — with security embedded throughout rather than bolted on at the end. Automated security gates within CI/CD pipelines are central to achieving the 'timely security assessment' outcome for critical systems. Tooling must be configured to produce audit-ready artefacts that satisfy both internal governance and BNM examination requirements.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and operate a centralised source code version control system (e.g., Git-based platform) for all critical system codebases, enforcing branch protection policies, mandatory peer review, and full audit trails of code changes with attributed authorship.
requirements.rationale: Version control is explicitly listed in the verbatim text. A centralised, policy-enforced version control system provides the foundational traceability required for security assessment and supports change management audit trails expected by BNM.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; configuration reviewed semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Implement automated Static Application Security Testing (SAST) and Software Composition Analysis (SCA) — code scanning tools — integrated into the version control pipeline to scan every code commit and pull request against critical system repositories, with scan results triaged and remediated within defined SLAs.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly names 'code scanning' as an automated tool category and links it to 'timely security assessment of critical systems'. Integrating SAST/SCA at the commit level ensures vulnerabilities are detected early, before propagation into production environments.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (per commit/PR); tool signature/ruleset updates monthly; SLA compliance reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement a CI/CD pipeline for critical systems that automates build, testing (including Dynamic Application Security Testing — DAST), and deployment stages, with mandatory automated security quality gates that block promotion to production if critical or high-severity findings are unresolved.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text enumerates 'testing' and 'software deployment' as distinct automated tool categories. Integrating DAST and security quality gates into CI/CD operationalises the clause's intent of facilitating timely security assessment before changes reach production.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (per pipeline execution); pipeline configuration reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Deploy an automated change management tooling solution that records, approves, and tracks all changes to critical systems — integrating with the version control and CI/CD pipeline — to ensure every production change is traceable, reviewed, and linked to an automated security assessment outcome.
requirements.rationale: Change management is explicitly named in the verbatim text. Automating change records and linking them to security scan results creates a closed-loop audit trail, enabling BNM to verify that no change to a critical system bypassed security assessment.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; change management workflow reviewed semi-annually; audit log retention per BNM records management requirements

requirements.requirement: Maintain an up-to-date Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for all critical systems, generated automatically by SCA tooling during each build, to enable rapid identification of systems affected by newly disclosed vulnerabilities in third-party components as emerging cyber threats are identified.
requirements.rationale: The clause's reference to 'emerging cyber threats' implies the institution must be able to respond swiftly when new vulnerabilities are disclosed. An automated SBOM capability enables targeted, timely security assessment of affected critical systems without manual dependency mapping.
requirements.frequency: Generated per build (continuous); SBOM inventory reviewed monthly; triggered ad-hoc on critical CVE disclosures

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that the deployment and operation of automated development and security tools are subject to formal policy, oversight, and periodic review — preventing automation from introducing new risks (e.g., misconfigured pipelines, unvetted third-party tool integrations) while ensuring tools remain effective against an evolving threat landscape. Board and senior management must have visibility into the institution's automated tooling posture as part of IT risk oversight. Third-party tool vendors must be subject to the institution's technology risk management framework.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formal Automated Development and Security Tooling Policy, approved by the IT Steering Committee or equivalent, that governs the selection, onboarding, configuration, access control, and decommissioning of all automated tools used in the SDLC for critical systems.
requirements.rationale: BNM's RMiT requires that technology risk be managed within a policy framework. Without a dedicated policy, the institution cannot demonstrate that automated tooling deployment is controlled and consistently applied, which is a prerequisite for satisfying the clause's intent across all six tool categories.
requirements.frequency: Policy approved annually; reviewed upon material changes to the tooling landscape or BNM guidance updates

requirements.requirement: Conduct periodic internal audits of automated tooling configurations, access rights, and output quality — at minimum annually — to verify that tools are operating as intended, security gates are not bypassed, and scan coverage of critical systems meets defined thresholds.
requirements.rationale: Automated tools can be misconfigured or circumvented, negating their security value. Independent audit of tooling configurations provides assurance that the 'timely security assessment' facilitated by automation is genuine and not cosmetic, which BNM examiners would expect to verify.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Chief Audit Executive
requirements.frequency: Annual formal audit; continuous monitoring of bypass incidents; ad-hoc triggered by significant incidents

requirements.requirement: Subject all third-party automated tool vendors — including SaaS-based CI/CD, code scanning, and version control platforms — to the institution's Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) framework, including security due diligence, contractual data protection obligations, and periodic performance reviews.
requirements.rationale: Automated tools often process sensitive source code and configuration data. The clause's authorisation to 'deploy automated tools' carries an implicit obligation to manage the risks those tools introduce; unvetted third-party tooling is itself a supply chain risk inconsistent with BNM's broader RMiT requirements.
requirements.owner: CRO / Head of Vendor Risk Management
requirements.frequency: Due diligence prior to onboarding; annual review thereafter; triggered on material vendor incidents or contract renewals

requirements.requirement: Include automated tooling effectiveness metrics — including security scan coverage, critical vulnerability remediation SLA compliance, and pipeline security gate bypass incidents — in the quarterly IT Risk Report presented to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: Board-level visibility into the performance of automated security tools is required to demonstrate that senior oversight of technology risk extends to the mechanisms used for critical system security assessment. This supports BNM's expectation of Board accountability for IT risk under RMiT.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting to Board Risk Committee

requirements.requirement: Establish a training and competency programme ensuring that all personnel involved in operating, configuring, or reviewing automated development and security tools maintain current knowledge of tool capabilities and emerging threat intelligence relevant to their role.
requirements.rationale: The clause references 'emerging cyber threats' as a key driver for automation. Tooling is only effective if operators understand current threats and can configure tools to detect them. A documented competency programme provides evidence of institutional capability and reduces the risk of automation being rendered ineffective by knowledge gaps.
requirements.frequency: Annual training cycle; mandatory on-boarding for new tool administrators; refreshed upon major tool upgrades

10.15.title: Third Party Software Supply Chain Risk

business.summary: Financial institutions that depend on third-party software are exposed to supply chain attacks where a single compromised component can cascade across all dependent systems and services. Failure to assess and manage these risks can result in unplanned service disruptions, regulatory sanctions, reputational damage, and potential data breaches affecting customers. BNM's guidance signals that regulators expect institutions to proactively understand their software supply chain exposure rather than react after an incident.

requirements.requirement: Conduct and document a cyber supply chain risk assessment covering all third-party software used in production systems, identifying potential business impact scenarios (e.g., service outage, data exfiltration, malware propagation) for each material dependency.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly requires institutions to 'consider the potential risks and impacts a cyber supply chain incident may pose to its overall business operations and services.' Without a formal risk assessment, the institution cannot demonstrate it has evaluated these impacts or made informed risk treatment decisions.
requirements.frequency: Annual (full assessment); Triggered on material new third-party software adoption

requirements.requirement: Maintain a business-facing inventory of all material third-party software dependencies, mapped to the business services and critical systems they support, with assigned business owners accountable for each dependency's risk posture.
requirements.rationale: Effective supply chain risk management requires knowing what software underpins which business services. This mapping enables business owners to assess the blast radius of a supply chain incident and prioritise remediation based on business criticality, directly supporting the clause's requirement to consider impact on 'overall business operations and services'.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (updated on change); Quarterly reconciliation

requirements.requirement: Integrate cyber supply chain incident scenarios into the institution's Business Continuity Plan (BCP) and Disaster Recovery (DR) playbooks, including response procedures for compromise of a critical third-party software component or open-source library.
requirements.rationale: The clause frames supply chain incidents in terms of impact to 'business operations and services,' implying that business continuity must account for this threat vector. Without explicit BCP/DR coverage, the institution may be unable to respond effectively to a supply chain compromise affecting production systems.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; Tested during BCP/DR exercises

requirements.requirement: Define and approve a risk appetite statement specifically addressing open-source software usage and third-party software supply chain risk, including thresholds for acceptable vulnerability severity, permitted open-source licences, and conditions under which third-party software may be blocked or quarantined.
requirements.rationale: Without a defined risk appetite, technology and procurement teams lack authoritative guidance on how much supply chain risk is tolerable. The clause's reference to establishing 'open-source software security policy and procedures' implies that deliberate risk appetite decisions must precede policy formation.
requirements.frequency: Annual review and Board endorsement

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement tooling and controls to provide continuous, automated visibility into the composition and vulnerability status of all third-party and open-source software components used across the institution's systems. This spans SBOM generation integrated into CI/CD pipelines, runtime vulnerability scanning, secure access controls over external code repositories, and rigorous testing practices for open-source components. These controls directly operationalise the two specific measures enumerated in clauses 10.15(a) and 10.15(b).

requirements.requirement: Implement Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) generation as a mandatory step in all software build pipelines, producing machine-readable SBOM artefacts (SPDX or CycloneDX format) for every application build. SBOMs must be stored in a centralised repository and linked to the corresponding application and deployment record.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.15(a) explicitly recommends 'adopting Software-Bill-of-Materials (SBOM) to automate the identification and continuous monitoring of potential security vulnerabilities including security issues associated with third party software components.' Without automated SBOM generation at build time, the institution cannot maintain an accurate, real-time picture of its software composition.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Officer (CIO) / Head of Software Engineering
requirements.frequency: Every build (continuous); SBOM store reviewed quarterly for completeness

requirements.requirement: Deploy a continuous Software Composition Analysis (SCA) platform that ingests SBOM artefacts and correlates component versions against up-to-date vulnerability databases (NVD, OSV, vendor advisories). The platform must alert designated security personnel within 24 hours of a newly disclosed Critical or High-severity CVE affecting any production component, and track remediation to closure.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.15(a) requires SBOM to enable 'continuous monitoring of potential security vulnerabilities.' Static point-in-time scans are insufficient; new CVEs are disclosed daily, and production software remains exposed until actively tracked and patched. The 'timely vulnerability assessment' language in 10.15(b) reinforces the need for near-real-time detection.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; Vulnerability triage within 24 hours of critical disclosure

requirements.requirement: Enforce secure access controls for all external source code repositories and package registries used by the institution (e.g., GitHub, GitLab, npm, PyPI). This includes mandatory multi-factor authentication, least-privilege access scoping, automated secret scanning to prevent credential or key leakage into repositories, and DLP monitoring for sensitive data pushed to external platforms.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.15(b) specifically requires 'ensuring secure access to source code repositories in third party platforms' and 'regular monitoring to prevent data leakages.' Compromised repository access is a primary supply chain attack vector, and accidental secret exposure in public repositories is a frequent cause of data breaches in financial institutions.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; Access review quarterly; Secret scanning on every commit

requirements.requirement: Embed open-source software security testing into the Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC), including: static analysis (SAST) for secure coding practice validation, dependency vulnerability scanning pre-merge, licence compliance checks, and dynamic testing of open-source components in staging environments prior to production deployment.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.15(b) mandates 'adoption of secure coding practices, robust testing of open-source software and timely vulnerability assessment.' Treating open-source components as trusted without independent testing is a known supply chain risk; malicious packages and typosquatting attacks specifically target institutions that do not validate open-source intake.
requirements.frequency: Every code commit and pull request (automated); Pre-release gate (manual sign-off)

requirements.requirement: Establish a private, institution-controlled artefact proxy (e.g., Nexus, Artifactory, AWS CodeArtifact) as the sole approved source for all open-source package downloads. The proxy must enforce allowlist-based package policies, scan packages on ingestion, and block packages with known Critical vulnerabilities or unapproved licences from entering the build environment.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.15(b) references 'mitigate security vulnerabilities and propagation of malwares across supply chain.' A controlled artefact proxy prevents developers from pulling malicious or compromised packages directly from public registries, breaking the supply chain attack propagation path before it reaches internal systems.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; Policy review quarterly

governance.summary: Governance structures must formalise the institution's approach to open-source and third-party software risk through documented policies, defined accountability, oversight mechanisms, and regular board-level reporting. The clause's explicit mention of 'establishing open-source software security policy and procedures' signals that informal practices are insufficient and that regulators expect documented, repeatable governance. Effective governance ensures that technology controls in 10.15(a) and 10.15(b) are consistently applied across all teams and business units.

requirements.requirement: Develop, approve, and maintain a formal Open-Source Software (OSS) Security Policy covering: approved and prohibited licence categories, mandatory security review criteria for new open-source adoption, secure coding standards for teams consuming open-source, responsibilities for ongoing monitoring, and escalation procedures for supply chain incidents. The policy must be reviewed and re-approved annually.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.15(b) explicitly requires 'establishing open-source software security policy and procedures.' A formal, Board-endorsed policy provides the authoritative foundation upon which all operational procedures, technology controls, and training programmes are built, and demonstrates regulatory compliance in the event of a BNM examination.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) (owner); Board Risk Committee (approver)
requirements.frequency: Annual review and re-approval; Immediate update upon material regulatory or threat landscape change

requirements.requirement: Establish a Third-Party Software Intake and Approval Process requiring a formal security review — including supply chain risk assessment, SBOM analysis, licence check, and vulnerability scan — before any new third-party or open-source component is approved for use in production systems. Maintain a register of approved components with associated risk ratings.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires institutions to 'consider the potential risks and impacts' of third-party software before use. A formalised intake gate ensures this consideration occurs systematically before risk is introduced into the environment, rather than retrospectively after deployment. The approved component register also supports SBOM accuracy.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (each new component request); Register reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Assign Named Accountable Owners for supply chain security, including a designated Software Supply Chain Risk Lead within the CISO function and explicit accountability mapped to each application's software owner for the currency and security of their SBOM and third-party components. Document these accountabilities in role profiles and technology risk registers.
requirements.rationale: The clause addresses 'security vulnerabilities and propagation of malwares across supply chain' as an institutional responsibility. Without named owners, vulnerability remediation and monitoring activities fall into ownership gaps. Clear accountability is a precondition for the 'timely vulnerability assessment' the clause requires.
requirements.frequency: Defined at role establishment; Reviewed annually and upon organisational change

requirements.requirement: Produce and present a quarterly Supply Chain Security Dashboard to the Technology Risk Committee and Board Risk Committee, covering: total third-party software components in use, open vulnerability counts by severity, SBOM coverage percentage, unresolved critical CVEs past SLA, open-source policy exceptions granted, and any supply chain incidents or near-misses in the period.
requirements.rationale: Board-level visibility is required for effective governance of material technology risks. Clause 10.15's framing of supply chain risk in terms of impact to 'overall business operations and services' elevates this beyond a purely technical concern; senior governance bodies must be able to monitor whether the institution's supply chain risk posture is within appetite.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) (preparer); Board Risk Committee (recipient)
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting; Ad-hoc escalation for Critical supply chain incidents

requirements.requirement: Conduct annual internal audits or independent assessments of supply chain security controls, validating that SBOM processes, OSS security policy adherence, repository access controls, and vulnerability management SLAs are operating effectively. Audit findings must be tracked to remediation with CIO/CISO sign-off and reported to the Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: Governance without independent assurance creates blind spots. Given that the clause is categorised as Guidance (marker 'G'), demonstrating that the institution has evaluated and can evidence the effectiveness of its chosen controls is essential for regulatory examination readiness and for maintaining confidence that 'robust testing' and monitoring obligations under 10.15(b) are being met in practice.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit (executor); Audit Committee (recipient)
requirements.frequency: Annual; Findings tracked to closure on a rolling basis

business.summary: Shadow IT — technology deployed without formal IT oversight — exposes financial institutions to uncontrolled data leakage, regulatory breaches, and operational failures that are invisible to risk management frameworks. Failure to address this exposes the institution to BNM supervisory action, reputational damage, and potential customer data loss. Business units that procure or use unsanctioned systems must be formally accountable within a policy framework that identifies and reduces these risks.

requirements.requirement: Develop and formally approve a Shadow IT Policy that defines what constitutes shadow IT, specifies prohibited and conditionally permitted technology use cases, and establishes escalation procedures for unsanctioned system discoveries.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates 'develop and implement robust policies' — this requires a named, board-approved policy document, not ad-hoc procedures. Without a formal policy, the institution cannot demonstrate to BNM that it has addressed the root requirement.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; immediate revision upon material change in technology landscape or regulatory guidance

requirements.requirement: Require all business units to conduct a self-declaration exercise, inventorying all technology tools, SaaS applications, and data repositories in active use, including those procured outside the formal IT acquisition channel.
requirements.rationale: Identifying shadow IT risks as required by the clause presupposes that the institution has a mechanism to surface unknown systems. Business unit self-declaration creates accountability at the point of usage and enables the institution to quantify its shadow IT exposure.
requirements.frequency: Semi-annual self-declaration; continuous for new technology onboarding

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal technology risk tolerance statement specific to shadow IT, defining acceptable and unacceptable risk thresholds (e.g., data classification levels, customer data exposure, regulatory reporting systems) that guide remediation prioritisation.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates 'reducing' shadow IT risks, implying a risk-based approach rather than blanket prohibition. A risk tolerance statement enables the institution to prioritise critical exposures and demonstrate to BNM a structured, proportionate response.
requirements.frequency: Annual review aligned with enterprise risk appetite statement

requirements.requirement: Embed shadow IT risk awareness and mandatory reporting obligations into staff onboarding, annual compliance training, and business unit performance accountability frameworks.
requirements.rationale: Robust policy implementation, as required by the clause, depends on staff understanding and compliance. Frontline business units are the primary source of shadow IT adoption; without behavioural accountability, policies remain ineffective on paper.
requirements.owner: Chief Human Resources Officer (CHRO) in collaboration with CRO
requirements.frequency: Annual mandatory training; upon policy update; as part of new employee onboarding

requirements.requirement: Define a formal remediation and sanctioning workflow for discovered shadow IT instances, including timelines for assessment, risk-based disposition (approve, migrate, decommission), and escalation to senior management for high-risk findings.
requirements.rationale: The mandate to 'reduce shadow IT risks' requires a structured response mechanism — discovery alone is insufficient. A remediation workflow ensures that identified risks translate into measurable risk reduction, which BNM examiners will expect as evidence of policy implementation.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (triggered on discovery); quarterly aggregate review

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement automated discovery, monitoring, and enforcement controls that operationalise the shadow IT policy, providing continuous visibility into unsanctioned systems, cloud services, and endpoint software. Controls must span network, endpoint, and cloud layers to comprehensively identify shadow IT risks as required by the clause. Evidence of these controls must be retained to support internal audit and BNM examination.

requirements.requirement: Deploy a network-based asset discovery solution (e.g., network access control, passive network scanning) that continuously inventories all devices and systems communicating on the institution's network, including unregistered endpoints, IoT devices, and rogue access points.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to 'identify' shadow IT risks — automated network discovery provides the foundational data layer for this, capturing systems that would never appear in self-declaration exercises. Manual processes cannot achieve the continuous identification required.
requirements.frequency: Continuous automated scanning; monthly reconciliation against authorised asset register

requirements.requirement: Implement a Cloud Access Security Broker (CASB) or equivalent DNS/proxy-based filtering solution to detect, log, and selectively block access to unsanctioned SaaS applications and cloud storage services from institutional networks and managed endpoints.
requirements.rationale: Cloud-based shadow IT (e.g., personal Google Drive, unapproved collaboration tools) represents the highest-volume and most difficult-to-detect category of shadow IT in modern financial institutions. A CASB provides the technical means to both identify cloud shadow IT usage and enforce policy-based controls, directly supporting risk reduction.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; monthly policy rule review; quarterly SaaS risk assessment

requirements.requirement: Deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) or unified endpoint management (UEM) tooling configured to detect and alert on the installation or execution of software not present in the institution's approved application catalogue.
requirements.rationale: Endpoint-installed applications (e.g., unauthorised development tools, screen capture utilities, personal cloud sync agents) constitute a significant shadow IT vector. EDR/UEM controls directly support the identification and risk-reduction requirements of the clause at the device layer.
requirements.frequency: Continuous endpoint telemetry; weekly alert triage; monthly software catalogue reconciliation

requirements.requirement: Maintain a continuously updated authorised technology register (approved hardware, software, and cloud services) integrated with procurement and IT service management workflows, so that any system not in the register triggers an automatic shadow IT review.
requirements.rationale: Identifying shadow IT requires a clear baseline of sanctioned technology. Without an authoritative register, the institution cannot reliably distinguish shadow IT from approved systems. Integration with procurement prevents shadow IT from entering the environment before discovery controls flag it.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Architecture / IT Asset Management
requirements.frequency: Continuous updates; quarterly full reconciliation; annual formal review

requirements.requirement: Implement automated alerting and ticketing integration between shadow IT discovery tools (CASB, EDR, network scanner) and the IT service management platform, ensuring each detection generates a tracked risk item with assigned owner, severity classification, and resolution deadline.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires robust policies to 'reduce' risks — this mandates a closed-loop process where identified shadow IT translates into tracked, time-bound remediation. Automated integration prevents detections from being lost or deprioritised, and generates the audit trail required for regulatory examination.
requirements.frequency: Continuous automated alerting; monthly remediation SLA compliance review

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that shadow IT risks are visible to the Board and senior management, that policies are enforced with clear accountability, and that compliance is independently verified through audit. The clause's requirement for 'robust' policy implementation demands formal oversight mechanisms, not delegated-only controls. BNM will expect evidence that shadow IT risk is treated as a standing board-level and risk committee concern.

requirements.requirement: Establish shadow IT risk as a standing agenda item within the IT Risk Committee or equivalent board-level risk committee, with a quarterly dashboard reporting undiscovered system counts, open remediation items, risk disposition outcomes, and trend analysis.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates that the institution 'develop and implement' policies robustly — robust implementation requires senior oversight that cannot be delegated entirely to operational teams. Board-level visibility ensures that shadow IT receives proportionate governance attention and that risk reduction is measured over time.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) — reporting to Board Risk Committee
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting to Risk Committee; annual Board-level summary

requirements.requirement: Assign named accountability for shadow IT risk management to a senior technology or risk officer (at minimum Head of IT Security or equivalent), with a formally documented mandate, resource allocation, and performance accountability tied to shadow IT risk reduction metrics.
requirements.rationale: Implementing a 'robust policy' requires clear human accountability. Without a named owner with formal mandate, shadow IT programs become diffuse responsibilities that are poorly resourced and deprioritised. A designated owner is the organisational mechanism through which the policy is operationalised.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Officer (CIO) or Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Accountability assigned at policy approval; reviewed annually during performance cycle

requirements.requirement: Integrate shadow IT risk review into the institution's IT and third-party risk management frameworks, ensuring that shadow IT findings feed into the enterprise risk register and that procurement approval gates prevent shadow IT acquisition.
requirements.rationale: Shadow IT risks, once identified, must be managed within the institution's established risk governance structures to ensure proportionate treatment, escalation, and reporting. Isolation of shadow IT management from the broader risk framework creates gaps that BNM examiners will identify during Technology Risk Assessment reviews.
requirements.frequency: Continuous integration; semi-annual framework alignment review

requirements.requirement: Commission an independent internal audit of shadow IT policy design and operational effectiveness at least annually, with the audit scope covering policy completeness, control implementation, remediation closure rates, and staff compliance rates.
requirements.rationale: The requirement to 'implement robust policies' implies the institution must be able to demonstrate effectiveness, not merely existence. Independent internal audit provides objective assurance to the Board and BNM that the shadow IT program functions as designed and that risks are genuinely being reduced.
requirements.frequency: Annual independent audit; follow-up review within 90 days if material gaps identified

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal exception and waiver process for shadow IT items that cannot be immediately decommissioned, requiring documented business justification, interim risk controls, a time-bound remediation plan, and CRO or CIO approval, with all active exceptions reported to the Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: Practical implementation of risk reduction acknowledges that not all shadow IT can be eliminated immediately. A governed exception process ensures that business continuity needs are balanced against risk, and that no shadow IT item persists without active management and senior approval — consistent with the clause's requirement for robust risk reduction.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) with CIO co-approval
requirements.frequency: Per-exception approval; quarterly exception register review by Risk Committee

business.summary: Unpatched systems and end-of-life technology expose the institution to data breaches, service disruptions, and regulatory sanctions that directly threaten customer trust and financial stability. Failure to comply with clause 10.17 can result in BNM supervisory action, including mandatory remediation orders and reputational damage. Proactive vulnerability and EOL management reduces operational risk and demonstrates sound technology stewardship to regulators, auditors, and counterparties.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a board-approved policy mandating that no production system or digital service operates with known security vulnerabilities or on unsupported technology, with defined remediation timelines aligned to vulnerability severity.
requirements.rationale: The clause opens with an absolute obligation — 'must ensure that all systems including digital services are not running with known security vulnerabilities' — which requires a policy framework to operationalise the prohibition across business lines and technology domains.
requirements.frequency: Annual policy review; immediate trigger upon material change in technology estate or threat landscape

requirements.requirement: Maintain a complete, authoritative inventory of all technology assets (hardware, software, operating systems, middleware, and digital service components) with associated support lifecycle dates, enabling proactive EOL identification as required by clause 10.17(c).
requirements.rationale: Identifying systems 'approaching EOL' is impossible without a current and accurate asset register. Business continuity and risk planning depend on knowing when technology transitions must occur, and regulators expect evidence of systematic identification rather than reactive discovery.
requirements.frequency: Continuous maintenance; formal reconciliation quarterly

requirements.requirement: Allocate sufficient budget and resources within annual planning cycles to fund vulnerability remediation, patch management operations, and EOL system migrations, ensuring financial constraints do not defer critical security work.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.17(b) requires patches to be implemented 'in a timely manner' and 10.17(c) requires planned remedial action for EOL systems. Both obligations fail when remediation is perpetually deferred due to underfunded technology budgets, creating systemic risk the business must own.
requirements.frequency: Annual budget cycle; mid-year review for unplanned remediation needs

requirements.requirement: Institute a formal exception management process where business units sponsoring continued use of unsupported or outdated technology must submit a documented justification, risk acceptance, and phase-out commitment for senior management approval, as required by clause 10.17(d).
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.17(d) mandates management approval for any exception, substantiated by a thorough risk assessment and a clear timeline for phasing out the outdated technology. Without a formal process, exceptions accumulate informally and the institution cannot demonstrate regulatory compliance.
requirements.frequency: Per-exception at initiation; annual review of all active exceptions

requirements.requirement: Communicate EOL migration timelines and system vulnerability status to relevant business stakeholders at least quarterly, ensuring that product owners and business line heads are accountable for dependencies on aging technology within their portfolios.
requirements.rationale: EOL remediation under clause 10.17(c) requires planning and implementation that crosses business and technology boundaries. Business line awareness ensures that product roadmaps account for mandatory technology transitions and that stakeholders do not obstruct or delay critical security remediation.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting; ad hoc for critical vulnerabilities

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement a continuous, automated capability to detect vulnerabilities, enforce patch application within defined SLAs, and track system lifecycle status against vendor EOL dates. The security baseline referenced in clause 10.17(a) must be configuration-controlled and version-managed, not maintained as a static document. All patch and EOL activities must produce auditable evidence sufficient to satisfy BNM examination.

requirements.requirement: Develop, maintain, and enforce documented security baselines (hardening standards) for every technology component class — servers, endpoints, network devices, databases, containers, and cloud services — aligned to recognised frameworks such as CIS Benchmarks or DISA STIGs, as required by clause 10.17(a).
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.17(a) explicitly requires a 'current security baseline for the security hardening of technology components' that is 'accurate and up to date'. Without component-specific baselines, patch and configuration management lack a reference state against which compliance can be measured.
requirements.frequency: Baseline review triggered by vendor advisories or major releases; formal review semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Deploy and operate a vulnerability management platform with continuous scanning coverage across all internet-facing and internal systems, with scan results mapped to a severity-tiered remediation SLA (e.g., Critical: 72 hours, High: 14 days, Medium: 30 days, Low: 90 days) to satisfy the timely patching obligation in clause 10.17(b).
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.17(b) requires continuous monitoring and timely implementation of patches. Automated scanning ensures vulnerabilities are detected as soon as signatures are available, and SLA tracking provides the audit trail regulators expect to see when assessing whether remediation is genuinely timely.
requirements.frequency: Continuous scanning; SLA compliance reporting weekly

requirements.requirement: Implement a patch management platform covering all operating systems, middleware, and third-party software with automated patch deployment to non-production environments, mandatory regression testing gates, and production deployment within SLA windows, including rollback capability.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.17(b) requires implementing 'latest patch releases in a timely manner'. A systematic, tool-supported patch pipeline is the only scalable mechanism to achieve this across a financial institution's full technology estate without introducing deployment-related instability.
requirements.frequency: Patch cycles aligned to vendor release cadence (monthly at minimum); emergency patches within 72 hours

requirements.requirement: Maintain an EOL and end-of-support (EOS) tracking register integrated with the asset inventory, with automated alerting at 24-month, 12-month, and 6-month horizons before each component's vendor-declared support end date, enabling the identification and planning required by clause 10.17(c).
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.17(c) requires the institution to 'identify, plan and implement remedial action for technology systems that are approaching EOL'. Automated horizon alerts provide the lead time necessary to procure replacements, fund migrations, and avoid emergency exception requests at the point of EOL.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; formal EOL register review quarterly

requirements.requirement: Apply compensating controls — including network segmentation, enhanced monitoring, restricted access, and intrusion detection — to any system operating under an approved exception for continued use of unsupported technology, with controls documented and reviewed as part of the annual exception review mandated by clause 10.17(d).
requirements.rationale: Where management approval is granted for exception use of outdated technology under clause 10.17(d), the risk assessment must demonstrate that 'associated risks are effectively managed'. Compensating controls are the technical mechanism by which residual risk is contained pending phase-out.
requirements.frequency: Controls validated at exception initiation; reassessed at annual review and upon threat intelligence changes

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that vulnerability and EOL obligations are owned at the appropriate seniority level, tracked through a defined oversight committee, and documented with audit-ready evidence. The exception process in clause 10.17(d) is particularly governance-intensive, requiring formal management approval, documented risk assessments, and mandatory annual reviews that must be captured in board or committee minutes. Governance must also ensure that the annual review obligation is institutionalised rather than left to informal ad hoc practice.

requirements.requirement: Assign formal accountability for clause 10.17 compliance to the Chief Information Security Officer as primary owner, with the Chief Risk Officer as second-line reviewer, and establish reporting lines to the Board Risk Committee for material vulnerabilities, EOL exposures, and active exceptions.
requirements.rationale: BNM's Standard marker places this obligation at the highest level of compliance seriousness. Clear ownership and board-level visibility are prerequisites for the management approval process in clause 10.17(d) and for demonstrating effective governance during BNM examinations.
requirements.frequency: Ownership review annual; Board Risk Committee reporting quarterly

requirements.requirement: Establish a Technology Risk and Vulnerability Steering Committee (or integrate into an existing governance body) meeting at least monthly to review open vulnerability remediation status, patch SLA adherence, EOL migration progress, and exception register, with minutes retained as regulatory evidence.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.17 requires continuous monitoring and timely implementation — governance oversight through a standing committee ensures that operational deviations are escalated and resolved with appropriate authority rather than languishing in implementation teams.
requirements.frequency: Monthly committee meetings; extraordinary session for Critical-severity incidents

requirements.requirement: Implement a formal, documented exception approval process for continued use of unsupported or outdated technology, requiring: (i) a written risk assessment documenting threat exposure and residual risk; (ii) a board-approved or senior management-approved exception with named sponsor; (iii) a specific, dated phase-out milestone; and (iv) annual formal review — all per clause 10.17(d).
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.17(d) provides explicit and detailed requirements for exceptions: management approval, thorough risk assessment, clear phase-out timeline, and annual review. Each element is a distinct regulatory obligation that must be evidenced separately, making a structured process essential for examination readiness.
requirements.frequency: Per-exception initiation; annual review of all active exceptions without exception

requirements.requirement: Define and publish internal patch and EOL management standards incorporating BNM clause 10.17 requirements, including severity-tiered remediation SLAs, security baseline maintenance obligations, and exception criteria, reviewed and approved by senior management at least annually.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.17(a) requires security baselines to be 'accurate and up to date' and clause 10.17(b) requires patches to be applied 'in a timely manner'. Institutionalised standards translate these regulatory obligations into operational definitions that staff, auditors, and regulators can assess against.
requirements.frequency: Annual review and re-approval; triggered review upon material regulatory or threat environment change

requirements.requirement: Conduct an independent internal audit of patch management, vulnerability remediation, security baseline compliance, and the EOL exception register at least annually, with findings reported to the Board Audit Committee and remediation tracked to closure.
requirements.rationale: Third-line assurance over clause 10.17 activities provides the board and BNM with independent evidence that the institution's controls are operating effectively, not merely designed correctly. The annual exception review requirement in clause 10.17(d) is a natural audit scope item requiring independent verification.
requirements.frequency: Annual; findings tracking quarterly until closure

business.summary: Unpatched systems and end-of-life technology represent direct operational and reputational risk to a financial institution — exploitation of known vulnerabilities can result in data breaches, regulatory sanctions, and customer harm. Clause 10.18 mandates a structured, risk-informed patch and EOL framework to ensure the institution can demonstrate proactive management of its technology risk exposure. Failure to comply not only invites BNM enforcement action but also leaves the institution exposed to cyber incidents that could trigger business continuity failures.

requirements.requirement: Establish and formally approve a Patch and EOL Management Policy that defines the scope, objectives, roles, and mandatory processes for all technology assets across the institution, including subsidiaries and outsourced systems where applicable.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires the institution to 'establish a patch and EOL management framework' — a documented, approved policy is the foundational artefact that anchors all downstream technical and governance activities. Without it, no defensible compliance posture can be demonstrated to BNM.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; immediate update upon material technology or regulatory change

requirements.requirement: Maintain a complete, up-to-date inventory of all technology assets — including hardware, operating systems, middleware, applications, and third-party components — with explicit tagging of EOL status and patch currency, to enable risk assessment as required under clause 10.18(a).
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.18(a) mandates 'identification and risk assessment of all technology assets for potential vulnerabilities arising from undeployed patches or EOL systems.' Without a comprehensive asset inventory, identification is impossible and risk assessments will be incomplete, creating blind spots that regulators and auditors will flag.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (automated discovery); formal review Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Define and publish business-approved Service Level Agreements (SLAs) for patch deployment turnaround times mapped to vulnerability severity tiers (e.g., Critical, High, Medium, Low), ensuring business units understand their obligations and escalation paths.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.18(b) requires 'formulation of criteria, priority and turnaround time for patch deployment according to the severity of the vulnerabilities identified.' SLAs translate this regulatory requirement into enforceable operational commitments that business owners, technology teams, and auditors can measure against.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; per-event update when new vulnerability classes emerge

requirements.requirement: Develop and execute an EOL transition roadmap for all systems approaching or past vendor end-of-life, including business impact assessments, compensating controls, and board-approved timelines for system replacement or upgrade.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.18(a) explicitly identifies 'EOL systems' as a vulnerability source requiring identification and risk assessment. Operating EOL systems without a documented, approved transition plan is a material BNM compliance gap and represents unquantified operational risk that must be managed at the business level.
requirements.frequency: Semi-annual review; immediate action upon vendor EOL announcement

requirements.requirement: Fund and resource a structured end-user awareness and training programme to support orderly transitions during patch deployments and EOL system migrations, minimising operational disruption and user-induced incidents.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.18(e) explicitly mandates 'end-user awareness for orderly transition.' Business leadership must allocate budget and mandate participation to ensure that staff are equipped to adapt to system changes, reducing the risk of errors, workarounds, or shadow IT arising from poorly managed transitions.
requirements.frequency: Per patch or EOL transition event; general awareness refreshed Annually

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement automated tooling, structured testing environments, and end-to-end workflow controls to operationalise the patch and EOL management framework mandated by Clause 10.18. This spans vulnerability scanning, patch prioritisation engines, pre-production compatibility testing, and deployment tracking — all integrated into a defensible, auditable process. The technical implementation must be capable of demonstrating compliance evidence to BNM examiners at any point in time.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and maintain an automated vulnerability management platform (e.g., Tenable, Qualys, or equivalent) capable of continuous scanning of all technology assets to identify undeployed patches and EOL components, feeding a centralised risk register as required by clause 10.18(a).
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.18(a) requires 'identification and risk assessment of all technology assets for potential vulnerabilities arising from undeployed patches or EOL systems.' Manual identification at the scale of a financial institution is infeasible and error-prone; automated scanning provides the continuous, comprehensive coverage required to satisfy this obligation.
requirements.frequency: Continuous scanning; full vulnerability assessment at minimum Monthly

requirements.requirement: Implement a patch prioritisation and classification mechanism that ingests vulnerability data (e.g., CVSS scores, threat intelligence feeds, asset criticality ratings) and automatically assigns severity tiers and deployment urgency, aligned to the SLAs defined in the business framework per clause 10.18(b).
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.18(b) mandates 'formulation of criteria, priority and turnaround time for patch deployment according to the severity of the vulnerabilities identified.' A systematic, data-driven prioritisation mechanism ensures consistent application of severity criteria, prevents backlog accumulation of critical patches, and produces auditable decision records.
requirements.frequency: Automated and continuous; rule set reviewed Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a dedicated pre-production testing environment that mirrors production system configurations, to conduct mandatory compatibility and regression testing of all patches prior to deployment in accordance with clause 10.18(c), with documented test results retained as compliance evidence.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.18(c) requires 'conduct of compatibility testing prior to the deployment of patches to minimise disruption to connected systems.' Deploying untested patches in a complex, interconnected financial system can cause cascading failures; a formal test environment is the technical control that satisfies this requirement and protects operational continuity.
requirements.frequency: Per patch deployment cycle; environment configuration validated Monthly

requirements.requirement: Implement an IT Service Management (ITSM) workflow tool (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management, or equivalent) configured to enforce the end-to-end patch deployment process — covering intake, risk assessment, approval gating, testing sign-off, deployment scheduling, post-deployment monitoring, and closure tracking — as mandated by clause 10.18(d).
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.18(d) requires 'adherence to the workflow for end-to-end patch deployment processes including approval, testing, monitoring and tracking of activities.' An ITSM tool enforces process gates, prevents unauthorised or untracked deployments, and generates the audit trail necessary to demonstrate compliance to BNM and internal auditors.
requirements.frequency: Continuous use; workflow configuration reviewed Annually or upon process change

requirements.requirement: Maintain a real-time patch compliance dashboard and reporting capability that provides visibility into patch deployment status, overdue patches by severity tier, EOL asset counts, and SLA breach metrics — enabling both operational management and executive reporting.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.18(d) requires 'monitoring and tracking of activities' as part of the end-to-end workflow. A dashboard operationalises this requirement by providing continuous visibility, enabling early escalation of SLA breaches, and generating the management information needed to demonstrate an active, functioning framework to BNM.
requirements.frequency: Real-time dashboard; formal management report Monthly

governance.summary: Clause 10.18 requires governance structures that provide oversight, accountability, and assurance over the entire patch and EOL management lifecycle, from policy approval through to independent verification of effectiveness. A Change Advisory Board, formal approval authorities, and board-level reporting are essential to ensure that the framework is not merely documented but actively enforced. Governance must also ensure that end-user awareness obligations are institutionalised and that compliance evidence is systematically captured and retained.

requirements.requirement: Establish a Change Advisory Board (CAB) or equivalent governance body with defined membership (technology, risk, business, and security representation) and authority to approve, defer, or reject patch deployments, fulfilling the approval step of the end-to-end workflow mandated by clause 10.18(d).
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.18(d) mandates 'adherence to the workflow for end-to-end patch deployment processes including approval.' A formally constituted CAB provides the institutional authority structure that ensures no patch is deployed without documented risk assessment and sign-off, creating clear accountability and an auditable approval record.
requirements.frequency: CAB convenes Weekly (routine) and on-demand for critical/emergency patches

requirements.requirement: Define and enforce a risk acceptance and exception management process for cases where patches cannot be deployed within mandated SLA turnaround times, requiring documented compensating controls, escalating approval authority based on severity, and mandatory review dates for all open exceptions.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.18(b) sets turnaround time obligations by severity; operational reality means some patches will be delayed. A formal exception process ensures deviations are visible, risk-accepted at the appropriate level, and time-bound — preventing exceptions from becoming permanent unmanaged gaps that attract regulatory scrutiny.
requirements.frequency: Per exception event; exception register reviewed Monthly by risk committee

requirements.requirement: Integrate patch and EOL compliance metrics into the institution's technology risk reporting framework, with regular reporting to the Board Risk Committee and senior management covering patch backlog, SLA adherence, EOL asset status, and critical open exceptions.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.18(a) requires risk assessment of technology assets — this obligation extends to ensuring senior leadership and the board have adequate visibility of the institution's technology vulnerability posture. Board-level reporting demonstrates that governance of technology risk is active and that accountability sits at the highest levels of the institution.
requirements.frequency: Monthly to senior management; Quarterly to Board Risk Committee

requirements.requirement: Mandate independent internal audit reviews of the patch and EOL management framework at least annually, assessing the effectiveness of controls, SLA compliance rates, exception management quality, and the adequacy of end-user awareness activities, with findings reported to the Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: The BNM 'S' marker and the comprehensive scope of Clause 10.18 demand independent assurance that the framework is operating as designed. Internal audit provides the third line of defence verification required to demonstrate to BNM that governance is not self-assessed only, and that material control weaknesses are independently identified and remediated.
requirements.frequency: Annual full review; targeted follow-up reviews as required by findings

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal end-user awareness governance programme with defined content standards, mandatory participation tracking, and completion records for patch and EOL transition communications, ensuring the institution can demonstrate fulfilment of the end-user awareness obligation under clause 10.18(e).
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.18(e) explicitly requires 'end-user awareness for orderly transition.' Without a governed programme that tracks participation and content delivery, this obligation cannot be evidenced to BNM. Governance of awareness activities also ensures communications are timely, relevant, and reach all affected staff before — not after — disruptive system changes.
requirements.owner: Chief Human Resources Officer (CHRO) / CIO (joint ownership)
requirements.frequency: Per patch or EOL transition event; programme effectiveness reviewed Annually

business.summary: This clause requires financial institutions to treat technology evolution as an ongoing strategic and operational risk, not merely an IT concern. Failure to comply exposes the institution to undetected security control failures, operational disruptions from obsolete technology, and potential regulatory sanctions under BNM's RMiT enforcement framework.
