Board-level oversight and long-term strategic planning are mandated to ensure that technology landscape shifts do not catch the institution unprepared.

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal mechanism to brief the Board of Directors — at minimum annually and upon material technology developments — on the potential business operational impact arising from the evolving technology landscape, including emerging threats and disruptive technologies that may render existing controls obsolete.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.19(a) explicitly mandates that 'the board receives advice on the potential impact to business operation arising from evolving technology landscape.' Without a structured briefing mechanism, board members lack the information needed to exercise effective oversight, creating governance gaps and increasing the institution's risk exposure.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Minimum Annual; additionally upon material technology developments or emerging threats

requirements.requirement: Conduct an annual Technology Business Impact Assessment (TBIA) that evaluates how developments in the technology landscape — including cloud adoption trends, AI, quantum computing, and new attack vectors — may disrupt existing security controls and business operations, with findings escalated to senior management and the Board.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to 'continually monitor the effectiveness and security of the technology in use, incorporating developments in technology that may disrupt existing security controls.' A structured TBIA operationalises this monitoring from a business perspective and ensures findings are translated into actionable management decisions.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Business Continuity
requirements.frequency: Annual; supplemented by ad-hoc assessments triggered by significant external technology events

requirements.requirement: Formulate and maintain a Board-approved long-term Technology Strategy (minimum 3-year horizon) that explicitly addresses anticipated changes in the technology landscape, identifies associated business risks including new cyber adversary tactics and techniques, and allocates budget and competent human resources to manage those risks.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.19(b) mandates the institution to 'formulate long-term strategy to address anticipated changes with allocation of competent resources to manage associated risks, including new cyber adversary tactics and techniques.' This requirement must be owned at the business level because resource allocation decisions — headcount, budget, vendor contracts — are business commitments.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Chief Strategy Officer
requirements.frequency: Reviewed and refreshed Annually; formally approved by Board every 3 years or upon material strategic change

requirements.requirement: Maintain a Board-approved System Migration Roadmap that schedules the orderly transition of systems approaching end-of-life or whose technology foundations are assessed as inadequate to preserve ongoing security and reliability, with associated business justification, cost approval, and risk acceptance sign-off for each deferred migration.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.19(c) requires the institution to 'establish roadmap for system migration to preserve security and reliability of the technology infrastructure in an orderly manner.' From a business standpoint, unplanned or deferred migrations carry operational risk and must have explicit business owner accountability and financial resource commitment.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Chief Financial Officer (CFO)
requirements.frequency: Reviewed Annually; updated upon addition or retirement of any system on the roadmap

requirements.requirement: Establish a Technology Talent and Competency Programme to ensure sufficient competent human resources — including internal staff and qualified third-party expertise — are available to manage risks arising from evolving technology and cyber adversary tactics, with competency gaps formally reported to senior management.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.19(b) specifically mandates 'allocation of competent resources to manage associated risks.' Financial institutions that lack skilled personnel to interpret and respond to new cyber adversary tactics will be unable to execute their long-term technology strategy regardless of how well it is documented, creating a direct compliance gap.
requirements.frequency: Competency gap assessment Annually; programme reviewed and updated Annually

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement continuous monitoring capabilities that detect degradation in the effectiveness of existing security controls as the threat and technology landscape evolves. They are further required to maintain actionable intelligence on new cyber adversary tactics and techniques, and to operate a structured system lifecycle management programme with a published migration roadmap. These capabilities must feed directly into the governance and strategy functions mandated by the clause.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and operate a continuous Technology Effectiveness Monitoring (TEM) capability that assesses whether existing security controls remain effective against current threat actors and technology developments, including automated control validation tests, threat emulation exercises (purple teaming), and regular benchmarking against industry frameworks such as NIST CSF and MITRE ATT&CK.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to 'continually monitor the effectiveness and security of the technology in use, incorporating developments in technology that may disrupt existing security controls.' Point-in-time assessments are insufficient; continuous monitoring is explicitly required and must be operationalised through automated tooling and structured exercises.
requirements.frequency: Continuous automated monitoring; structured control validation Quarterly; purple team exercises Annually

requirements.requirement: Establish a Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) capability — whether in-house, via a managed service, or through industry sharing arrangements such as FS-ISAC — that continuously tracks new and evolving cyber adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs), with findings integrated into security control reviews and reported to the CISO and senior management.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.19(b) explicitly references 'new cyber adversary tactics and techniques' as risks that must be managed as part of the institution's long-term strategy. Without a structured CTI function, the institution cannot fulfil its obligation to anticipate and address emerging threats, leaving its long-term technology strategy uninformed.
requirements.frequency: Continuous ingestion of threat feeds; CTI summary reports Monthly; threat landscape briefings to management Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Maintain a comprehensive Technology Asset Lifecycle Register that tracks the support lifecycle status of all hardware, software, and third-party components in use, flags assets approaching end-of-support or end-of-life, and triggers formal risk assessments and migration planning at defined lifecycle thresholds (e.g., 24 months, 12 months, and 6 months before end-of-support).
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.19(c) requires the institution to 'establish roadmap for system migration to preserve security and reliability of the technology infrastructure in an orderly manner.' A roadmap cannot be maintained without accurate, current visibility of asset lifecycle status. This register is the foundational data source for the migration roadmap.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of IT Operations
requirements.frequency: Register maintained continuously; lifecycle status reviewed Quarterly; escalation threshold alerts automated

requirements.requirement: Develop, maintain, and execute a Technology Migration Roadmap that documents the planned migration path for all systems flagged in the Technology Asset Lifecycle Register, including target replacement technologies, migration sequencing, security requirements for migrated environments, rollback plans, and post-migration validation testing to confirm security and reliability are preserved.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.19(c) mandates an 'established roadmap for system migration' specifically to 'preserve security and reliability... in an orderly manner.' The technical team must translate the business-approved roadmap into executable migration plans with sufficient technical detail to ensure that security posture is maintained — not degraded — during and after migration.
requirements.frequency: Roadmap reviewed and updated Annually; individual migration plans updated upon initiation of each migration project

requirements.requirement: Conduct periodic Technology Horizon Scanning exercises — integrating inputs from CTI, industry research, vendor advisories, and regulatory guidance — to identify disruptive technology developments (e.g., post-quantum cryptography requirements, AI-driven attack automation, cloud-native paradigm shifts) that may undermine existing security controls, with findings reported to the CISO and fed into the long-term technology strategy.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to 'incorporat[e] developments in technology that may disrupt existing security controls.' Horizon scanning is the technical mechanism for systematically identifying such developments before they manifest as incidents, enabling proactive rather than reactive strategy adjustment as required by clause 10.19(b).
requirements.frequency: Formal horizon scanning exercise Semi-annually; ad-hoc assessments triggered by significant external technology events

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that board-level oversight of technology effectiveness is institutionalised, not discretionary, with clear accountability chains from operational monitoring teams through to the Board. The institution requires formal policies, committee mandates, and reporting frameworks to operationalise the three sub-obligations of clause 10.19 — board advisory, long-term strategy with competent resources, and an orderly migration roadmap — as ongoing governance obligations rather than one-time exercises.

requirements.requirement: Mandate that the Board Technology or Risk Committee formally receives and deliberates on an annual Technology Landscape Advisory Report, prepared by management, that covers: (i) the current technology effectiveness posture; (ii) disruptive technology developments and their potential impact on business operations; (iii) status of the long-term technology strategy; and (iv) migration roadmap progress. Board minutes must evidence deliberation on these matters.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.19(a) requires the institution to 'ensure its board receives advice on the potential impact to business operation arising from evolving technology landscape.' Governance structures must formalise this as a standing agenda item with documented evidence of board engagement, not merely an ad-hoc briefing, to satisfy BNM's expectation of active board oversight.
requirements.owner: Board Chairman / Chairman of Board Risk or Technology Committee
requirements.frequency: Annual formal report; ad-hoc escalation to Board upon material developments

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Technology Risk and Strategy Policy that assigns clear ownership, accountability, and approval authorities for: (i) technology effectiveness monitoring; (ii) long-term technology strategy formulation and refresh; (iii) cyber threat intelligence integration; and (iv) system migration planning — with designated first, second, and third line responsibilities documented.
requirements.rationale: Without a formal policy framework assigning accountability, the three obligations in clause 10.19 risk being executed inconsistently or not at all. BNM's RMiT framework presupposes a three-lines-of-defence governance model, and the policy must articulate how each line contributes to the monitoring, strategy, and migration requirements of this clause.
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed Annually; updated upon material changes to technology strategy, risk appetite, or regulatory guidance

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal governance process for the development, approval, annual refresh, and Board endorsement of the long-term Technology Strategy, including an explicit requirement for the strategy to address: anticipated changes in the technology landscape, allocation of competent human and financial resources, and identification and management of risks including new cyber adversary tactics and techniques.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.19(b) mandates the institution to 'formulate long-term strategy to address anticipated changes with allocation of competent resources.' A governance process — not merely a strategy document — is required to ensure the strategy is treated as a living obligation with defined owners, review triggers, and board accountability rather than a one-time compliance artefact.
requirements.frequency: Strategy formally approved by Board every 3 years; governance process review Annual; interim updates triggered by material changes

requirements.requirement: Establish a Technology Migration Governance Committee (or extend an existing committee's mandate) with cross-functional representation — including IT, Risk, Compliance, Finance, and Business Operations — to oversee the System Migration Roadmap, approve individual migration projects, review deferred migrations with documented risk acceptance, and report roadmap status to the Board Risk Committee at minimum Semi-annually.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.19(c) requires the institution to 'establish roadmap for system migration to preserve security and reliability of the technology infrastructure in an orderly manner.' Orderly migration requires multi-stakeholder governance to resolve competing priorities, approve resource allocation, and ensure that risk acceptance for deferred migrations is formally recorded and escalated — not managed unilaterally by IT.
requirements.owner: CTO / CRO (co-chairs); members include CISO, CFO representative, Business Unit Heads
requirements.frequency: Committee meets Quarterly; roadmap status report to Board Risk Committee Semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Implement a Technology Competency Governance Framework that defines: (i) the competency profiles required for roles responsible for technology risk management, cyber threat intelligence, and system migration; (ii) a process for identifying and addressing competency gaps through recruitment, training, or qualified third-party engagement; and (iii) annual reporting to senior management on the adequacy of competent resources relative to the institution's technology risk exposure.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.19(b) specifically requires 'allocation of competent resources to manage associated risks, including new cyber adversary tactics and techniques.' Governance must ensure that 'competent' is defined, measured, and reported — not assumed. Without this framework, the institution cannot demonstrate to BNM that its resource allocation decision is evidence-based and risk-proportionate.
requirements.frequency: Competency gap assessment and reporting Annually; framework reviewed Annually

business.summary: Failure to establish a robust cryptography policy exposes the institution to data breaches, regulatory sanctions, and reputational damage arising from the use of outdated or compromised cryptographic controls. Customer-facing and transactional systems are at particular risk if weakened algorithms or unmanaged key lifecycles are exploited by adversaries. BNM requires institutions to treat cryptography as a foundational risk control, not merely a technical setting, demanding board-level ownership and documented recovery readiness.

requirements.requirement: Establish and formally approve a written Cryptography Policy that addresses, at minimum, all five sub-requirements in Clause 10.20(a)–(e), and make it available to all relevant business and technology stakeholders.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly states the institution 'must establish a robust and resilient cryptography policy.' Without a formally documented and approved policy, there is no governance baseline, leaving the institution unable to demonstrate compliance to BNM and exposing it to enforcement action.
requirements.frequency: One-time establishment; reviewed at least annually thereafter

requirements.requirement: Mandate that all customer-facing, transactional, and externally linked applications use only institution-approved cryptographic standards, with non-compliant systems subject to a documented remediation timeline and business risk acceptance process.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.20(c) specifically calls out 'external linked or transactional customer-facing applications' as scope for annual cryptographic review. Exploitation of weakened algorithms in these systems directly impacts customer trust, creates fraud liability, and may constitute a breach of BNM's consumer protection expectations.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; exception review at least annually

requirements.requirement: Develop, fund, and exercise a Cryptographic Key Compromise Recovery Plan that includes escalation chains, business continuity interim measures, and containment strategies, with at least one full test exercise conducted annually.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.20(e) requires both development and testing of compromise-recovery plans. A plan that exists on paper but has never been exercised provides no operational assurance. Testing validates that escalation paths, key regeneration procedures, and interim business-as-usual changes are executable under real incident conditions.
requirements.frequency: Annual testing; plan reviewed after any cryptographic incident or significant technology change

requirements.requirement: Include cryptographic risk exposure — including expired key lifecycle controls, deprecated algorithm usage, and untested recovery plans — as standing agenda items in the institution's enterprise risk management reporting cycle.
requirements.rationale: Cryptographic controls underpin the integrity and confidentiality of the institution's most sensitive data assets. Senior management and the Board must maintain visibility of cryptographic risk posture to discharge their oversight duties and to pre-empt regulatory scrutiny of weaknesses in this domain.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting; annual deep-dive review

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement, maintain, and continuously monitor cryptographic controls across the full stack — from algorithm selection through to key destruction — using current industry standards and dedicated key management infrastructure. The institution's IT asset inventory must be extended to enumerate every cryptographic tool, algorithm, and its associated system mapping with documented rationale. Annual technical reviews must validate that no critical or customer-facing system relies on weakened, deprecated, or unreviewed cryptographic primitives.

requirements.requirement: Adopt and enforce institution-approved industry-standard cryptographic algorithms and protocols, including: symmetric encryption (AES-256 minimum), asymmetric encryption (RSA-2048/ECC P-256 minimum), message authentication (HMAC-SHA-256 minimum), hash functions (SHA-256 minimum), digital signatures (RSA-PSS or ECDSA), and random number generation (CSPRNG from vetted libraries), as defined in standards such as NIST SP 800-series, ISO/IEC 18033, and FIPS 140-2/3.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.20(a) requires adoption of 'industry standards' across these specific cryptographic primitive categories. The explicit enumeration of algorithm types in the clause reflects BNM's expectation that institutions make deliberate, standards-based choices rather than relying on default or legacy configurations.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; standards baseline reviewed at least annually

requirements.requirement: Deploy and operate a dedicated cryptographic Key Management System (KMS) — or equivalent hardware security module (HSM)-backed infrastructure — that enforces and audits the full key lifecycle: generation, distribution, renewal, usage, storage, recovery, revocation, and destruction, as enumerated in Clause 10.20(b).
requirements.rationale: Manual or ad hoc key management processes are operationally fragile and audit-indefensible. A KMS provides automated lifecycle enforcement, access controls, audit trails, and separation of duties across all eight lifecycle stages explicitly named in the clause, directly satisfying the requirement for 'robust and secure processes in managing cryptographic key lifecycles.'
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; configuration reviewed quarterly; access rights reviewed at least annually

requirements.requirement: Extend the institution's IT asset inventory to include a Cryptographic Asset Register that captures: every cryptographic tool and algorithm in use, the rationale for its selection, the cryptographic standard it conforms to, the expiry or renewal date of associated keys, and the mapping to each dependent application system, as required by Clause 10.20(d).
requirements.rationale: Without a comprehensive cryptographic inventory, the institution cannot identify scope for its annual review, cannot demonstrate regulatory compliance, and cannot respond effectively to a compromise event. The clause explicitly requires 'pertinent information on the rationale for each cryptographic method employed and its mapping to supported application systems,' making this a directly auditable artefact.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Head of Cybersecurity
requirements.frequency: Maintained continuously; formally reviewed and reconciled at least annually and upon any new system deployment

requirements.requirement: Conduct at least an annual technical cryptographic standards review for all critical systems, externally linked systems, and transactional customer-facing applications, assessing each against current NIST, IETF, and BNM-aligned standards to identify deprecated, weakened, or end-of-life algorithms and protocols, with remediation plans raised for any findings.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.20(c) mandates 'periodic review, at least annually' of cryptographic standards in use for these specific system categories, with the explicit purpose of preventing 'exploitation of weakened algorithms or protocols.' Annual reviews operationalise this mandate and generate evidence of due diligence for regulatory examination.
requirements.frequency: At least annually; additionally triggered by publication of significant CVEs or NIST algorithm deprecation notices

requirements.requirement: Develop, document, and annually test a Cryptographic Key Compromise Recovery Playbook that specifies: automated and manual detection triggers, escalation contacts and timeframes, key revocation and regeneration procedures for each key type and system, interim cryptographic measures to maintain service continuity, changes to business-as-usual protocols during recovery, and containment strategies to limit the blast radius of a compromise.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.20(e) explicitly requires both 'development and testing' of compromise-recovery plans and mandates that the plan address escalation, key regeneration, interim measures, BAU protocol changes, and containment strategies. Technology teams own the technical execution of these procedures and must validate them through tabletop or live exercises to ensure operational readiness.
requirements.owner: CISO / Head of IT Security Operations
requirements.frequency: Developed once; tested at least annually; updated after any real or simulated compromise event

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure the Cryptography Policy receives appropriate board or senior management approval, is reviewed at defined intervals, and is linked to the institution's wider risk and technology governance frameworks. Oversight bodies must hold accountability for the annual cryptographic review cycle, the integrity of the cryptographic asset register, and the readiness and currency of compromise-recovery plans. Clear ownership, escalation paths, and evidence trails are required to satisfy BNM's examination expectations for this Standard-marker clause.

requirements.requirement: Obtain formal Board or Board Risk Committee approval for the institution's Cryptography Policy and ensure it is reviewed and re-approved at least annually or following any material change to the institution's technology environment or the regulatory cryptographic landscape.
requirements.rationale: BNM's RMiT framework places technology risk governance at the board level. The cryptography policy's scope — covering customer data protection, key compromise recovery, and external system security — makes it a material risk document that requires board-level endorsement to signal institutional commitment and establish clear accountability.
requirements.frequency: At least annually; ad hoc upon material technology or regulatory change

requirements.requirement: Establish a Cryptographic Standards Governance Working Group — comprising representatives from Cybersecurity, IT, Risk, Compliance, and relevant business lines — with a formal mandate to conduct the annual cryptographic standards review required under Clause 10.20(c), approve algorithm changes, and track remediation of identified weaknesses.
requirements.rationale: The annual review of cryptographic standards for critical and customer-facing systems is a specific, recurring regulatory obligation under Clause 10.20(c). Without a designated governance forum with cross-functional membership, review outcomes lack authority, accountability is diffuse, and findings may not be escalated or remediated effectively.
requirements.frequency: Convened at least annually for the mandatory review; ad hoc for algorithm deprecation or incident-driven reviews

requirements.requirement: Integrate the Cryptographic Asset Register, required under Clause 10.20(d), into the institution's enterprise IT asset governance framework, with defined ownership, update triggers (new system deployments, algorithm changes, key renewals), and an annual completeness and accuracy attestation signed off by the Head of IT.
requirements.rationale: A cryptographic asset register that is not governed will rapidly become incomplete or inaccurate, rendering the institution unable to scope its annual review or respond to a compromise event. Governance integration ensures the register is treated as a living artefact with accountable owners, not a one-time exercise.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Head of Cybersecurity
requirements.frequency: Continuous maintenance; annual formal attestation; updated within 30 days of any relevant change

requirements.requirement: Define and enforce a formal cryptographic risk exception and waiver process whereby any system that cannot immediately comply with the institution's approved cryptographic standards must obtain documented risk acceptance from the CRO, a time-bound remediation plan, and compensating controls, with exceptions reported to the Board Risk Committee at least quarterly.
requirements.rationale: Legacy systems, vendor constraints, or third-party integration requirements may prevent immediate compliance with approved cryptographic standards. Without a governed exception process, non-compliant systems persist invisibly, creating unmanaged regulatory and security exposure. A formal process ensures exceptions are visible, time-limited, and escalated appropriately.
requirements.frequency: Exception review at initiation; remediation plan tracked quarterly; exception register reviewed at least annually

requirements.requirement: Embed the Cryptographic Key Compromise Recovery Plan within the institution's broader Incident Management and Business Continuity governance framework, with defined exercise schedules, post-exercise lessons-learned reporting to senior management, and a requirement that the plan be updated and re-approved following any actual compromise event or significant findings from an exercise.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.20(e) requires both development and testing of compromise-recovery plans, including documented escalation processes and containment strategies. Governance integration ensures exercises are scheduled and resourced, that lessons learned drive plan improvement, and that senior management maintains awareness of recovery readiness — all of which are expected evidential artefacts in a BNM examination.
requirements.frequency: Annual testing; plan reviewed and updated within 60 days of any exercise or actual incident

business.summary: Financial institutions that fail to conduct cryptographic due diligence expose themselves to data breaches, regulatory sanctions, and loss of customer trust — particularly where encryption key ownership and control are insufficiently managed. Clause 10.21 imposes affirmative obligations to evaluate and govern cryptographic controls across the full spectrum of information security properties: confidentiality, integrity, authentication, authorisation, and non-repudiation. Non-compliance risks BNM supervisory action, reputational damage, and liability arising from unauthorised access to customer data.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formal cryptographic due diligence programme that evaluates cryptographic controls for all technology systems and applications used by the institution, covering confidentiality, integrity, authentication, authorisation, and non-repudiation properties.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates the institution to 'conduct due diligence and evaluate the cryptographic controls associated with the technology used.' Without a structured programme, ad hoc reviews will miss systemic gaps, leaving the institution exposed to regulatory and operational risk.
requirements.frequency: Annual, and upon onboarding any new technology system or application

requirements.requirement: Classify all systems and applications as either critical (customer data present) or non-critical (no customer data) to determine encryption key ownership obligations, and maintain a live inventory of this classification.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.21(a) creates a tiered obligation: except for non-critical systems without customer information, the institution must retain ownership and control of encryption keys. Without formal classification, the institution cannot demonstrate compliance with this carve-out or defend its key ownership posture to BNM.
requirements.frequency: Continuous — reviewed upon system changes, and validated annually

requirements.requirement: Define and document the institution's risk appetite and tolerance for reliance on third-party cryptographic assessments and third-party key management services, and apply this threshold consistently in vendor selection and ongoing oversight decisions.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.21(c) requires the institution to 'consider whether such reliance is consistent with the financial institution's risk appetite and tolerance.' Without a documented risk appetite position, decisions on outsourced cryptographic functions lack a defensible governance basis and may exceed acceptable risk thresholds.
requirements.frequency: Annual review of risk appetite statement; applied per-event during vendor onboarding

requirements.requirement: Include cryptographic infrastructure costs and system resource requirements as a mandatory line item in technology budgeting and capacity planning processes, ensuring cryptographic controls are sustainably funded.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.21(d) requires the institution to 'give due regard to the system resources required to support the cryptographic controls.' Underinvestment in compute, storage, and network capacity for cryptographic workloads leads to performance degradation and incentivises shortcuts that weaken encryption posture.
requirements.owner: Chief Financial Officer (CFO) in conjunction with CIO
requirements.frequency: Annual budgeting cycle; quarterly capacity review

requirements.requirement: Maintain contractual provisions with all technology vendors and cloud service providers that explicitly address encryption key ownership, key custodian rights, and the institution's ability to independently retrieve or revoke key access — particularly for systems containing customer information.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.21(a) requires the institution to 'retain ownership and control of the encryption keys (themselves or with an independent key custodian).' Absent contractual protections, vendors may assert control over keys, creating unauthorised access risk and complicating incident response or vendor exit.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) and Head of Legal/Compliance
requirements.frequency: Per-event at contract initiation; reviewed at contract renewal and annually

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement cryptographic controls that concretely satisfy all five information security properties mandated by Clause 10.21 — confidentiality, integrity, authentication, authorisation, and non-repudiation — and must operationalise key management in line with industry standards (e.g., NIST SP 800-57, ISO/IEC 27002) regardless of whether keys are self-generated or third-party-sourced. Additionally, technology teams must address the architectural tension between encryption and network visibility, ensuring that encrypted traffic does not become a blind spot for threat detection and monitoring.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and operate a centralised Key Management System (KMS) or Hardware Security Module (HSM) infrastructure that enables the institution to generate, store, rotate, revoke, and audit encryption keys — ensuring the institution retains sovereign ownership and control of all keys protecting customer data.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.21(a) requires the institution to retain ownership and control of encryption keys for all systems containing customer information. A KMS/HSM provides the technical foundation for this control, preventing key material from being accessible to cloud providers or other third parties.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; annual cryptographic configuration review

requirements.requirement: Implement cryptographic algorithms and protocols that satisfy each of the five security properties: symmetric encryption (AES-256) for confidentiality; cryptographic hashing (SHA-256 or above) and HMAC for integrity; PKI-based mutual TLS or digital certificates for authentication; role-bound key access controls for authorisation; and digital signatures (RSA-2048 or ECDSA P-256 minimum) for non-repudiation.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates evaluation and implementation of controls that 'protect the confidentiality, integrity, authentication, authorisation and non-repudiation of information.' Mapping specific cryptographic primitives to each property ensures no security property is addressed only nominally.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Cybersecurity Architecture
requirements.frequency: Continuous; algorithm suite reviewed annually and upon publication of new NIST/BNM guidance

requirements.requirement: Where encryption keys are sourced from or managed by a third party, implement technical compensating controls including: dual-control and split-knowledge procedures, key ceremony audit logging, third-party SoC 2 Type II or equivalent attestation review, and contractual audit rights — in adherence with NIST SP 800-57 or ISO/IEC 11770 key management standards.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.21(b) mandates 'appropriate measures to ensure robust controls and processes are in place to manage encryption keys securely including adhere to relevant industry standard' where the institution does not generate its own keys. These technical controls operationalise the standard.
requirements.frequency: Per-event upon engaging third-party key source; annual technical review

requirements.requirement: Implement encrypted traffic inspection capabilities — such as TLS inspection proxies, SSL/TLS decryption at network boundary controls, or endpoint-based detection agents — to preserve network traffic visibility and threat detection efficacy for encrypted communications.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.21(d) requires the institution to 'give due regard to the risk of reduced network traffic visibility of data that has been encrypted.' Without inspection capabilities, security monitoring tools become blind to malware command-and-control, data exfiltration, and lateral movement occurring within encrypted channels.
requirements.owner: Head of Cybersecurity Operations (SOC Manager) / CISO
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; configuration reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Conduct and document cryptographic performance impact assessments — including CPU utilisation benchmarking, latency measurements, and throughput analysis — for all systems implementing cryptographic controls, and ensure adequate hardware resources (including hardware acceleration where appropriate) are provisioned to prevent performance degradation.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.21(d) requires 'due regard to the system resources required to support the cryptographic controls.' Unmanaged cryptographic overhead on underpowered infrastructure can force administrators to disable or weaken encryption, creating security gaps.
requirements.frequency: At system implementation and upon major architectural changes; annual capacity review

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that cryptographic due diligence is embedded as a repeatable, auditable process — not a one-time exercise — with clear accountability from the Board through to operational teams. This includes formalised cryptographic policy, key management governance frameworks, independent oversight of third-party cryptographic reliance, and periodic assurance reporting to senior management and the Board. Clause 10.21's 'S' marker means BNM expects evidence of systematic compliance, not merely aspirational documentation.

requirements.requirement: Develop, approve, and maintain a Board-endorsed Cryptography Policy that mandates due diligence evaluation of cryptographic controls, defines minimum cryptographic standards, establishes key ownership principles, sets risk tolerance for third-party reliance, and assigns accountabilities across the three lines of defence.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text's opening obligation — to 'conduct due diligence and evaluate the cryptographic controls' — requires a governance instrument that institutionalises this obligation. Without a Board-approved policy, cryptographic decisions default to informal, inconsistent practices that cannot withstand BNM examination.
requirements.owner: Board of Directors (approved); CRO / CISO (owned and maintained)
requirements.frequency: Annual review and Board re-endorsement; ad hoc update upon material regulatory or threat landscape change

requirements.requirement: Establish a Cryptographic Controls Due Diligence framework with documented scope, methodology, and evidence requirements for evaluating cryptographic controls at system onboarding and on a periodic basis — ensuring coverage of all five security properties and all clauses (a) through (d) of Clause 10.21.
requirements.rationale: BNM's use of 'must conduct due diligence' implies a repeatable, structured process. A documented framework converts this obligation into an auditable control with consistent outputs, enabling the institution to demonstrate compliance to BNM examiners and internal audit.
requirements.frequency: Framework reviewed annually; assessments conducted at system onboarding and annually thereafter

requirements.requirement: Establish an independent key custodian arrangement governance process — including custodian selection criteria, legal agreements, dual-control procedures, and periodic custodian performance review — for all customer data systems where the institution uses a third-party key custodian under Clause 10.21(a).
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.21(a) permits use of an 'independent key custodian' as an alternative to direct key ownership. Governance over this arrangement ensures the custodian relationship remains robust, independent, and auditable — and that the institution can demonstrate effective control retention to BNM.
requirements.owner: CRO / Head of Third Party Risk Management
requirements.frequency: Annual custodian performance review; per-event upon material change to custodian relationship

requirements.requirement: Implement a Third-Party Cryptographic Reliance Assessment process that evaluates whether reliance on third-party cryptographic assessments (e.g., vendor SOC reports, penetration test findings, certifications) is consistent with the institution's documented risk appetite and tolerance, with findings escalated to senior management for decisions that approach or breach the stated threshold.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.21(c) explicitly requires the institution to 'consider whether such reliance is consistent with the financial institution's risk appetite and tolerance.' A formal assessment process with escalation triggers prevents implicit over-reliance on vendor-supplied assurance that may not be sufficient for BNM's expectations.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Third Party Risk Management
requirements.frequency: Per-event at vendor engagement; annual review of all active third-party cryptographic reliances

requirements.requirement: Produce and present an annual Cryptographic Controls Assurance Report to the Board Risk Committee or IT Risk Committee, covering: due diligence assessment results, key ownership posture, third-party reliance status versus risk appetite, network visibility risk status, and any identified gaps with remediation timelines.
requirements.rationale: The 'S' (Standard) marker and the breadth of obligations under Clause 10.21 require Board-level visibility to ensure systemic compliance. An annual assurance report fulfils the governance chain from operational controls through to Board oversight, and creates the audit trail expected by BNM during on-site examinations.
requirements.owner: CISO / CRO (prepares); Board Risk Committee or IT Risk Committee (receives and approves)
requirements.frequency: Annual; with interim reporting triggered by material incidents, regulatory changes, or significant gap findings

business.summary: Failure to implement robust cryptographic protocols exposes the institution to data breaches, regulatory sanctions, and reputational damage arising from compromised customer and transactional data. BNM requires that protocol selection and key protection are risk-proportionate, meaning inadequate controls in high-risk environments constitute a direct supervisory breach. Business continuity and customer trust are contingent on the integrity of the cryptographic infrastructure underpinning all digital banking operations.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formal Cryptography Risk Appetite Statement that defines the institution's risk tolerance for cryptographic protocol selection, key exposure scenarios, and acceptable hardware protection tiers (HSM, TEE, or equivalent), aligned to the institution's overall risk profile.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires controls to be 'commensurate with the level of risk', obligating the institution to articulate what risk levels trigger what protective measures before any implementation decision is made.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; triggered on material changes to the institution's risk profile or product portfolio

requirements.requirement: Conduct a comprehensive inventory and classification of all business processes that rely on secret or private cryptographic keys, mapping each to its associated risk level to determine the required protection environment (HSM, TEE, or equivalent).
requirements.rationale: The clause requires protection 'commensurate with the level of risk', which cannot be assessed without a complete business-process-level understanding of where cryptographic keys are generated, stored, and used in transactional, authentication, and data-protection workflows.
requirements.frequency: Annual; updated upon onboarding of new products or services involving cryptographic operations

requirements.requirement: Ensure all third-party vendors and technology partners handling secret or private cryptographic keys on behalf of the institution provide contractual and evidential assurance of compliance with recognised international cryptographic standards and use of HSM, TEE, or equivalent secured devices.
requirements.rationale: Outsourcing cryptographic operations does not transfer regulatory obligation; the clause places the duty on 'a financial institution' without exception, requiring due diligence and binding contractual controls across the supply chain.
requirements.owner: Chief Procurement Officer / Head of Vendor Management
requirements.frequency: At contract inception and annually thereafter; triggered upon material changes to vendor cryptographic architecture

requirements.requirement: Allocate dedicated budget provisions for the acquisition, maintenance, and lifecycle replacement of HSMs, TEEs, or similarly secured devices, treating them as non-negotiable critical infrastructure rather than discretionary spend.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates that key storage and computation 'must be undertaken in a protected environment, supported by a hardware security module, trusted execution environment or similarly secured devices', making hardware-grade protection a regulatory baseline that cannot be deferred on cost grounds.
requirements.frequency: Annual budget cycle; reviewed upon hardware end-of-life or significant changes in cryptographic workload

technology.summary: Technology teams must deploy and operationalise cryptographic protocols that are grounded in recognised international standards for both symmetric (secret key) and asymmetric (public key) schemes, with all secret and private key material generated, stored, and used exclusively within tamper-resistant hardware such as HSMs or TEEs. Protocol implementations must be formally tested before production deployment and continuously monitored to detect algorithm obsolescence or configuration drift. The full key lifecycle — generation, distribution, rotation, and destruction — must be managed within the protected hardware boundary.

requirements.requirement: Implement cryptographic protocols exclusively from recognised international standards bodies (e.g., NIST FIPS 140-2/140-3, ISO/IEC 18033, IETF TLS 1.3, PKCS standards) for both secret key (AES-256, ChaCha20) and public key (RSA-4096, ECDSA P-256/P-384, EdDSA) operations. Prohibit use of deprecated algorithms (MD5, SHA-1, DES, 3DES, RC4, RSA < 2048-bit) across all environments.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires protocol selection to be 'based on recognised international standards', mandating a positive allowlist approach grounded in globally accepted bodies rather than vendor-proprietary or unvalidated schemes.
requirements.owner: Head of Information Security Architecture / CISO
requirements.frequency: Policy enforced continuously via automated configuration management; full algorithm review annually and upon publication of new NIST/BNM guidance

requirements.requirement: Deploy FIPS 140-2 Level 3 (or higher) validated Hardware Security Modules (HSMs) for all secret and private key storage, encryption/decryption computation, and digital signing operations classified as high or critical risk. For lower-risk workloads, document the risk justification for use of TEEs or equivalent secured device alternatives.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly mandates that key storage and computation occur in 'a protected environment, supported by a hardware security module (HSM), trusted execution environment (TEE) or similarly secured devices', and that the level of protection must be commensurate with risk — requiring a graduated but hardware-anchored approach.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Infrastructure / Chief Technology Officer (CTO)
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; HSM firmware and configuration reviewed quarterly; FIPS validation status verified annually

requirements.requirement: Implement a full cryptographic key lifecycle management system integrated with HSMs or TEEs, covering key generation (within the hardware boundary), distribution, storage, rotation, suspension, and destruction. Enforce dual-control and split-knowledge principles for all high-value key management operations.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'a high degree of protection to the applicable secret or private cryptographic keys', which extends beyond storage to encompass the entire lifecycle — any unprotected phase in the lifecycle (e.g., plaintext key export during rotation) negates hardware-based protections.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; key rotation schedules executed per documented policy (minimum annual for long-lived keys, per-session for ephemeral keys)

requirements.requirement: Conduct formal cryptographic protocol testing before production deployment, including: (a) functional correctness testing against standard test vectors, (b) implementation vulnerability testing (padding oracle, timing side-channel, fault injection), and (c) interoperability testing. Maintain test evidence as part of the change management record.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires that protocols be 'tested accordingly' following selection from international standards, establishing a mandatory pre-production assurance gate. Algorithm specification correctness does not guarantee implementation security without empirical validation.
requirements.frequency: Per release/change introducing or modifying cryptographic components; regression suite executed in CI/CD pipeline for continuous assurance

requirements.requirement: Establish continuous cryptographic posture monitoring to detect configuration drift, deprecated protocol enablement (e.g., TLS 1.0/1.1 re-enablement, weak cipher suite activation), and approaching key expiry across all systems in scope. Integrate alerts into the Security Operations Centre (SOC) with defined response SLAs.
requirements.rationale: Effective implementation of suitable protocols requires ongoing assurance that deployed configurations remain compliant with the approved standard baseline; configuration drift or silent protocol downgrade attacks would undermine the 'high degree of protection' mandated by the clause.
requirements.frequency: Continuous automated scanning; configuration compliance reports reviewed monthly

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that cryptographic protocol decisions are formally governed through policies, standards, and oversight committees, with clear accountability assigned to named roles across the institution. Independent assurance — through internal audit, external assessment, or both — must validate that implemented controls meet the regulatory standard and reflect current international best practice. Escalation pathways must exist to inform Board and senior management when cryptographic risk exposures exceed defined tolerance levels.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Board-approved Cryptography Policy and supporting Cryptographic Standards document that defines: approved protocol allowlists, prohibited algorithms, key length minimums, HSM/TEE usage mandates by risk tier, key lifecycle management procedures, and testing requirements. The policy must explicitly reference the international standards on which it is based.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires selection of protocols 'based on recognised international standards', implying a documented, institution-wide standard exists to guide implementation decisions consistently — ad hoc protocol choices by individual teams are incompatible with this requirement.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee (oversight) / CISO (ownership and maintenance)
requirements.frequency: Board approval upon initial adoption; annual review and upon material changes to the cryptographic standards landscape (e.g., NIST PQC standardisation milestones)

requirements.requirement: Establish a Cryptography Governance Committee (or assign cryptographic governance to an existing IT/Cybersecurity Risk Committee) with representation from IT Architecture, Information Security, Risk Management, Compliance, and Internal Audit to review and approve all material changes to cryptographic protocols, key management procedures, and HSM/TEE configurations.
requirements.rationale: Decisions on cryptographic protocol selection directly impact the institution's risk posture and regulatory standing; the clause's 'must be based on recognised international standards' requirement demands a structured review process that cannot be delegated solely to operational IT teams without independent oversight.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly standing meetings; convened ad hoc for urgent cryptographic risk events or mandated algorithm deprecations

requirements.requirement: Commission an annual independent assessment of the institution's cryptographic controls, including protocol implementation review, HSM/TEE configuration audit, key management procedure walkthrough, and testing evidence review, conducted by qualified cryptographic security specialists. Findings must be reported to the Board Risk Committee with a formal management response and remediation plan.
requirements.rationale: Independent assurance validates that the 'effective implementation' of cryptographic controls mandated by the clause is being achieved in practice, not merely on paper. The 'S' marker status reflects BNM's expectation of demonstrable compliance that can withstand supervisory scrutiny.
requirements.frequency: Annual; supplemented by targeted reviews following significant cryptographic incidents, algorithm deprecations, or HSM infrastructure changes

requirements.requirement: Define and maintain a Cryptographic Algorithm Obsolescence and Quantum Readiness Roadmap, tracking the deprecation timelines of currently approved algorithms and planning migration pathways to post-quantum cryptographic standards (NIST FIPS 203/204/205) commensurate with the institution's risk horizon and asset sensitivity.
requirements.rationale: The requirement that protocol selection be 'based on recognised international standards and tested accordingly' is forward-looking; BNM's principle of commensurate risk management obligates institutions to anticipate and plan for algorithm retirement rather than react to it after compromise or mandated deprecation.
requirements.owner: CISO / Head of Information Security Architecture
requirements.frequency: Reviewed semi-annually and upon publication of new NIST or BNM cryptographic guidance; milestone-based Board reporting

requirements.requirement: Maintain a Cryptographic Incident Response Procedure covering key compromise, HSM/TEE failure, and cryptographic protocol vulnerability disclosure, with defined escalation triggers (including mandatory BNM notification thresholds), containment steps, key revocation and re-issuance workflows, and post-incident review requirements.
requirements.rationale: The clause's mandate for a 'high degree of protection' to secret and private keys implies that the institution must be operationally prepared to respond effectively when protections fail; absence of a tested response procedure would constitute a governance gap in the assurance of effective cryptographic controls.
requirements.frequency: Procedure reviewed annually; tabletop exercise conducted annually; activated per-event upon cryptographic incident detection

business.summary: Failure to manage public key certificates appropriately exposes the institution to legal unenforceability of digital transactions, reputational harm from reliance on untrusted Certificate Authorities, and regulatory sanctions under RMiT. Customer-facing digital agreements or transactions whose authentication cannot be proven legally binding and irrefutable constitute a direct financial and legal liability for the institution. Certificate mismanagement — including expired, unrecognised, or improperly issued certificates — can disrupt services, invalidate contracts, and breach BNM requirements.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a risk-tiered framework for selecting Certificate Authorities (CAs), matching the assurance level of the CA to the sensitivity and risk exposure of each business use case (e.g., internal systems vs. customer-facing transactions).
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires certificates to be 'appropriate to the level of risk', mandating a deliberate, documented risk assessment before CA selection. Inadequate CA selection for high-risk operations creates unmitigated exposure to fraud, impersonation, and contractual disputes.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; triggered immediately upon material change in CA trust status or business risk profile

requirements.requirement: Mandate that all digital certificates issued to or on behalf of customers are sourced exclusively from CAs that are 'recognised' — specifically those appearing on approved trust stores (e.g., Microsoft, Mozilla, Apple root programs) or designated by a competent Malaysian authority under the Digital Signature Act 1997.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly states 'certificates associated with customers shall be issued by recognised Certificate Authorities'. Use of an unrecognised CA for customer certificates could render customer authentication legally invalid and expose the institution to liability in dispute resolution.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Chief Operating Officer (COO)
requirements.frequency: Per-event (at certificate issuance); Quarterly validation that approved CAs remain on recognised trust lists

requirements.requirement: Ensure all customer-facing digital signature and authentication workflows are legally admissible and non-repudiable under Malaysian law, specifically the Digital Signature Act 1997, Electronic Commerce Act 2006, and any BNM-issued guidance, by engaging Legal and Compliance to sign off on authentication protocol designs.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires that 'the use of private cryptographic keys corresponding to the user certificates are legally binding and irrefutable'. Without explicit legal validation of the signature workflow, the institution cannot assert non-repudiation in dispute proceedings, undermining the enforceability of digital contracts and transactions.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (at initial workflow design); Annual legal review to track legislative changes

requirements.requirement: Define and enforce a documented certificate lifecycle management policy covering the initial issuance, renewal, and revocation of all institutional and customer certificates, with business owners assigned accountability for certificates within their domain.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires that 'initial issuance and subsequent renewal must be consistent with industry best practices'. Expired or improperly renewed certificates cause service outages, failed authentication, and regulatory findings. Business owners must be accountable for timely renewals in their portfolios.
requirements.frequency: Policy review Annual; Certificate renewal monitored Continuously with automated alerting at 90, 60, and 30 days before expiry

requirements.requirement: Maintain a comprehensive, up-to-date inventory of all public key certificates in use across the institution — including issuing CA, validity period, associated business service, and risk classification — as a single source of truth for operational and audit purposes.
requirements.rationale: Effective risk management of certificates as required by the clause ('appropriate to the level of risk') is impossible without full visibility of the certificate estate. An incomplete inventory creates blind spots that lead to undetected expirations, shadow IT certificate use, and inability to respond to CA compromise events.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (automated discovery); Quarterly manual reconciliation

technology.summary: Technology teams must deploy and operate a PKI infrastructure — including CA integration, certificate lifecycle automation, and Hardware Security Module (HSM)-based private key protection — that satisfies both the strong protection mandate and the industry best practices requirement of the clause. Authentication and digital signature protocols must be implemented using standards (e.g., X.509 v3, TLS 1.2+, PKCS standards) that guarantee cryptographic integrity and legal non-repudiation. Certificate management must be automated sufficiently to eliminate human-error-driven expiry failures while supporting rapid revocation in response to compromise.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and operate a PKI architecture that integrates with trusted, recognised Certificate Authorities for all customer-facing certificate issuance, using X.509 v3 certificates with appropriate key lengths (minimum RSA-2048 or ECC-256) and algorithm suites as per current NIST/IETF best practice.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates that public cryptographic keys be stored in CA-issued certificates and that customer certificates come from recognised CAs. Substandard key sizes or deprecated algorithms (e.g., SHA-1, RSA-1024) would fail the 'industry best practices' requirement and create cryptographic vulnerabilities.
requirements.frequency: Implemented at deployment; Algorithm suite reviewed annually against NIST/IETF publications and upon publication of new cryptographic vulnerabilities

requirements.requirement: Protect all private cryptographic keys corresponding to customer and institutional certificates using FIPS 140-2 Level 3 (or higher) validated Hardware Security Modules (HSMs), ensuring private keys are generated, stored, and used exclusively within the HSM and never exported in plaintext.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'strong protection to ensure that the use of private cryptographic keys corresponding to the user certificates are legally binding and irrefutable'. Plaintext private key storage outside an HSM creates a risk of key theft that would invalidate non-repudiation claims and undermine the legal enforceability of digital signatures.
requirements.frequency: Implemented at deployment; HSM firmware and configuration reviewed Quarterly; penetration testing Annually

requirements.requirement: Implement strong mutual authentication and digital signature protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3 for transport, PKCS#7/CMS or XAdES/PAdES for signatures) for all customer-facing systems that rely on certificate-based authentication, disabling weak cipher suites, legacy protocol versions (SSL, TLS 1.0/1.1), and self-signed certificates in production customer environments.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates that 'implementation of authentication and signature protocols using such certificates are subject to strong protection'. Use of weak or deprecated protocols undermines both the cryptographic assurance and the legal non-repudiation properties that the clause is designed to guarantee.
requirements.frequency: Implemented at deployment; Configuration scanned Continuously via automated tooling; protocol standards reviewed Annually

requirements.requirement: Deploy automated certificate lifecycle management tooling (e.g., ACME protocol integration, CA/B Forum-compliant SCEP/EST, or enterprise CLM platforms) that tracks all certificates across the estate, automates renewal workflows, enforces CA compliance checks, and enables rapid revocation via OCSP/CRL within SLA-defined timeframes.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim requirement that 'initial issuance and subsequent renewal must be consistent with industry best practices' cannot be reliably achieved through manual processes at scale. Automated CLM reduces the risk of expiry-driven outages and ensures revocation propagation is timely after key compromise.
requirements.frequency: Automated continuous monitoring; System capability reviewed Annually; revocation SLA tested Semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Maintain and test a documented CA compromise response procedure, including the capability to mass-revoke affected certificates, re-issue using an alternate recognised CA, and notify affected customers within a defined RTO, triggered whenever a CA in the institution's trust chain is publicly distrusted or breached.
requirements.rationale: The obligation to use certificates 'appropriate to the level of risk' from 'recognised Certificate Authorities' implies an ongoing trust monitoring function. Historical CA distrust events (e.g., DigiNotar, Symantec) demonstrate that CA trust status can change suddenly, requiring the institution to respond rapidly to maintain compliance and protect customers.
requirements.frequency: Procedure tested Annually via tabletop or simulation; CA trust status monitored Continuously

governance.summary: Governance structures must provide Board-level oversight of the institution's PKI risk posture, documented policies that align certificate practices with industry standards and Malaysian legal requirements, and independent audit of compliance across the certificate lifecycle. The clause's dual emphasis on legal non-repudiation and regulatory conformance requires governance to bridge the Legal, Compliance, Technology, and Business functions through formal policy ownership and periodic assurance activities. Third-party CA relationships must be subject to vendor risk management processes consistent with the institution's broader third-party risk framework.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Board-approved Cryptography and Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) Policy that explicitly mandates risk-based CA selection criteria, the exclusive use of recognised CAs for customer certificates, private key protection standards, and certificate lifecycle management requirements aligned with the Digital Signature Act 1997, Electronic Commerce Act 2006, and BNM RMiT requirements.
requirements.rationale: The breadth of the clause — spanning risk classification, legal enforceability, CA recognition, and industry best practices — requires a unifying policy document to operationalise these obligations. Without a Board-approved policy, implementation becomes ad hoc and inconsistent, creating audit exposure and regulatory risk.
requirements.frequency: Policy approved at inception; reviewed and re-approved Annually or upon material changes to technology, law, or BNM guidance

requirements.requirement: Implement a Certificate Authority Governance Committee or assign formal ownership to an existing risk/security committee, responsible for approving the institution's list of permitted CAs, reviewing CA trust status changes, and signing off on risk classifications used to match CA assurance levels to business use cases.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires certificates to be 'appropriate to the level of risk' and mandates 'recognised Certificate Authorities' for customer certificates — decisions that require structured, accountable governance rather than ad hoc technical judgement. A governance body provides the accountability and audit trail required by BNM examiners.
requirements.frequency: Committee meets Quarterly; convened on an emergency basis upon CA distrust events or material risk changes

requirements.requirement: Subject all external Certificate Authority relationships to the institution's Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) framework, including due diligence at onboarding, contractual clauses covering CA obligations (revocation timelines, breach notification, audit rights), and annual performance reviews against CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements and WebTrust criteria.
requirements.rationale: The reliance on recognised CAs for customer certificates creates a material third-party dependency. The clause's requirement for initial issuance and renewal to meet 'applicable legal / regulatory specifications' extends to the CA's own compliance posture, which must be verified through structured vendor governance rather than assumed.
requirements.owner: Head of Vendor/Third-Party Risk Management / CRO
requirements.frequency: Due diligence at onboarding; Annual re-assessment; triggered review upon CA audit failure or public distrust event

requirements.requirement: Mandate that Internal Audit includes PKI and certificate management within its annual Technology Audit Plan, assessing adherence to the Cryptography Policy, completeness of the certificate inventory, effectiveness of lifecycle controls, CA recognition status, and adequacy of private key protection — with findings reported to the Board Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: Independent assurance is necessary to validate that implementation of the clause requirements is effective across the certificate estate. Given that the clause carries an 'S' (Standard) marker, BNM examiners will expect evidence of internal governance and control testing, not solely management attestations.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor / Board Audit Committee
requirements.frequency: Annual audit cycle; findings tracked to closure Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Certificate Incident Response procedure within the institution's Information Security Incident Management framework, covering scenarios including CA compromise, private key exposure, certificate misuse, and mass expiry, with defined escalation paths to Senior Management, BNM notification assessment (per RMiT incident reporting obligations), and customer communication protocols.
requirements.rationale: The clause's requirement for 'strong protection' and legally irrefutable private key use implies that any failure of certificate controls is a material security incident. A pre-defined response procedure ensures that the institution can demonstrate controlled, proportionate, and timely responses to BNM in the event of a certificate-related breach, mitigating regulatory sanction risk.
requirements.frequency: Procedure established at policy inception; tested via tabletop exercise Annually; reviewed after each triggered incident

business.summary: This clause requires financial institutions to formally define how resilient and available their data centres must be, anchored to measurable business recovery outcomes. Without specified objectives, the institution cannot demonstrate alignment between infrastructure capability and business continuity commitments, exposing it to regulatory censure, service disruptions, and reputational damage. Failure to comply may also undermine the institution's ability to recover critical operations within BNM-expected timeframes during a disaster event.

requirements.requirement: Define and document formal business recovery objectives (RTO, RPO, MTPD) for each critical business service, and explicitly link these to corresponding data centre resilience and availability targets.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates that resilience objectives 'effectively support business recovery objectives', requiring a traceable mapping between business continuity parameters and infrastructure specifications. Without this link, data centre investments cannot be validated as fit-for-purpose against regulatory expectations.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; triggered upon any material change to business services or recovery strategy

requirements.requirement: Classify all business services hosted in or dependent on each data centre by criticality tier, and document the minimum acceptable availability percentage (e.g., 99.95%, 99.99%) required per tier to sustain business recovery commitments.
requirements.rationale: Specifying 'availability objectives' as required by the clause demands quantifiable uptime targets tied to service criticality. Without tiered classification, the institution cannot prioritise recovery resources nor demonstrate proportionate protection of systemically important functions.
requirements.frequency: Annual; updated upon business impact assessment (BIA) refresh

requirements.requirement: Conduct an annual Business Impact Assessment (BIA) to validate that current data centre resilience and availability objectives remain aligned to evolving business recovery requirements, and escalate material gaps to senior management for remediation decision.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires objectives to 'effectively support' business recovery, implying ongoing adequacy, not a one-time specification. Periodic BIA ensures that changes in business volume, product complexity, or regulatory thresholds are reflected in updated infrastructure objectives.
requirements.owner: Chief Business Continuity Officer / Head of Business Continuity
requirements.frequency: Annual; ad hoc upon significant organisational or regulatory change

requirements.requirement: Establish and publish an internal Service Level Agreement (SLA) framework documenting committed data centre availability targets for each business unit, providing a contractual basis for accountability and recovery planning.
requirements.rationale: Formalising 'availability objectives' in an SLA framework operationalises the clause's specification requirement and creates enforceable internal accountability between technology and business owners, supporting audit trail and regulatory examination readiness.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Head of IT Service Management
requirements.frequency: Annual review; immediate revision upon SLA breach or material infrastructure change

technology.summary: Technology teams must translate business recovery objectives into concrete, measurable data centre resilience specifications covering redundancy architecture, availability targets, and validated failover capability. This requires designing infrastructure to declared Tier standards, instrumenting continuous availability measurement, and demonstrating through testing that specified objectives are achievable. The technical implementation must produce auditable evidence that each data centre's architecture is aligned to the specified resilience and availability objectives.

requirements.requirement: Design and document a data centre resilience architecture that meets or exceeds the specified availability objectives, including N+1 or higher redundancy for all critical systems (power, cooling, network, compute, storage), and declare each data centre against a recognised Tier classification standard (e.g., Uptime Institute Tier III/IV or TIA-942).
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'resilience objectives' to be specified; translating these into a Tier-rated architecture provides quantifiable, auditable evidence of compliance. Redundancy specifications directly determine the achievable availability percentage and resilience to single points of failure.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of Data Centre Operations
requirements.frequency: Design review at each infrastructure refresh cycle; minimum every three years

requirements.requirement: Implement continuous monitoring tooling to measure and report real-time and historical data centre availability metrics (uptime, Mean Time Between Failures, Mean Time to Recover) against the specified availability objectives, with automated alerting upon breach of defined thresholds.
requirements.rationale: Specifying availability objectives without measurement infrastructure renders compliance unverifiable. Continuous monitoring provides the evidence base to demonstrate objective attainment, identify degradation trends, and trigger corrective action before service impact occurs.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Infrastructure / Head of IT Operations
requirements.frequency: Continuous; monthly reporting to senior management; quarterly trend review

requirements.requirement: Define and execute an annual data centre resilience testing programme, including failover tests, disaster recovery drills, and power/cooling failure simulations, to validate that documented resilience and availability objectives are achievable under realistic failure scenarios.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires objectives to 'effectively support business recovery', implying demonstrated capability, not merely stated intent. Resilience testing provides objective evidence that the specified targets can be met when invoked, and uncovers gaps before an actual incident.
requirements.owner: Head of Technology Risk / Head of IT Disaster Recovery
requirements.frequency: Annual full test; semi-annual partial or component tests; post-incident validation

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a formal Data Centre Resilience Specification document for each data centre facility, detailing availability SLAs, redundancy configurations, recovery time capabilities, geographic separation requirements, and alignment to each supported business service's recovery objective.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly requires the institution to 'specify' objectives — implying formal documented artefacts. A per-facility specification document provides the authoritative reference for design decisions, audit evidence, and vendor/operator accountability.
requirements.owner: CTO / Head of Data Centre Engineering
requirements.frequency: Reviewed and recertified annually; updated upon any significant infrastructure change

requirements.requirement: Establish a Geographic Redundancy and Data Replication Policy requiring that data centres supporting systemically critical functions maintain geographically separated active or active-standby sites with synchronous or near-synchronous replication, consistent with declared RPO objectives.
requirements.rationale: Resilience objectives at the data centre level must account for site-level failures (natural disasters, utility loss). Geographic separation and replication policies operationalise the 'resilience objectives' requirement for the highest criticality workloads, preventing a single facility outage from breaching business recovery commitments.
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed annually; replication lag monitored continuously

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that data centre resilience and availability objectives are formally approved, owned at senior level, periodically reviewed, and audited for ongoing compliance. This includes embedding the specification requirements within the institution's broader Business Continuity Management System (BCMS) and technology risk governance frameworks.
