Clear accountability lines must exist from the Board down to operational owners, with independent assurance that objectives remain current, documented, and demonstrably met.

requirements.requirement: Establish a Board-approved Data Centre Resilience Policy that mandates the specification, ownership, periodic review, and independent audit of resilience and availability objectives for all data centres, with explicit linkage to the institution's Business Continuity Management Policy.
requirements.rationale: The clause imposes a mandatory obligation ('must specify') on the financial institution as an entity, requiring Board-level ownership to ensure the obligation is institutionalised and not delegated without accountability. A formal policy creates the governance structure within which specifications are produced and maintained.
requirements.owner: Board of Directors / Board Risk Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Policy approved at Board level; reviewed annually or upon material regulatory change

requirements.requirement: Designate a named senior executive (minimum Deputy CEO or equivalent) as the accountable owner for data centre resilience objectives, with a documented mandate covering approval of specifications, escalation authority, and accountability to the Board for compliance status.
requirements.rationale: Effective specification of resilience objectives requires organisational authority to direct resources, resolve conflicts between business and technology requirements, and commit the institution to specific recovery standards. Named executive ownership ensures the clause obligation cannot be diffused across departments without accountability.
requirements.frequency: Ownership confirmed annually; reviewed upon executive transitions

requirements.requirement: Integrate data centre resilience and availability objectives into the institution's Technology Risk Management Framework, requiring quarterly risk reporting on objective attainment, exception tracking for SLA breaches, and escalation protocols when resilience metrics fall below specified thresholds.
requirements.rationale: The clause's requirement to 'effectively support business recovery objectives' implies ongoing adequacy, which must be monitored through the risk governance cycle. Integration into technology risk reporting ensures the Board and senior management receive timely, structured information on compliance status and emerging gaps.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Technology Risk
requirements.frequency: Quarterly risk reporting; immediate escalation upon material breach

requirements.requirement: Mandate an annual independent internal audit of data centre resilience and availability objectives, covering completeness of specification documentation, alignment to business recovery requirements, evidence of periodic testing, and remediation of prior-year findings.
requirements.rationale: Independent assurance is required to validate that the institution's self-declared objectives are accurately specified, technically achievable, and actually tested. This provides the Board and BNM examiners with objective evidence of clause compliance beyond management attestation.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor / Head of Internal Audit
requirements.frequency: Annual; findings reported to Board Audit Committee within 30 days of completion

requirements.requirement: Establish a Change Control and Impact Assessment process requiring that any proposed change to data centre infrastructure, hosted services, or business recovery objectives triggers a formal review of whether existing resilience and availability specifications remain valid and adequate.
requirements.rationale: The obligation to 'specify' objectives implies they must remain current and reflective of actual operational reality. Without a change-triggered review gate, infrastructure or business changes can silently invalidate documented specifications, creating a latent compliance gap and undetected recovery risk.
requirements.owner: Head of Technology Governance / IT Change Advisory Board (CAB) Chair
requirements.frequency: Per-change event; aggregate review in quarterly risk reporting

business.summary: Failure to maintain data centre redundancy exposes the institution to prolonged service outages that directly undermine its ability to meet business recovery objectives — including RTOs and RPOs committed to regulators, counterparties, and customers. A single point of failure in any critical infrastructure path (power, cooling, network) can render core banking, payments, and customer-facing systems unavailable, triggering reputational damage, financial loss, and potential enforcement action by BNM. This clause requires the institution to treat redundancy not as an IT concern but as a board-level business continuity obligation.

requirements.requirement: Define and formally document Business Recovery Objectives (RTOs and RPOs) for all critical business services dependent on data centre infrastructure, ensuring these objectives explicitly inform the redundancy specifications required of the data centre.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text ties redundancy requirements to 'effective achievement of the identified business recovery objectives' — redundancy must be sized and designed against specific, documented recovery targets, not implemented generically.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; immediately upon any material change to critical business services

requirements.requirement: Conduct a Business Impact Assessment (BIA) that maps each critical business service to its underlying data centre infrastructure dependencies, identifying all components whose failure would cause a breach of documented recovery objectives.
requirements.rationale: Identifying and eliminating single points of failure ('eliminate any single point of failure') requires a comprehensive dependency map between business services and physical infrastructure. Without this, redundancy investments may miss critical paths.
requirements.frequency: Annual; triggered by significant changes to business services or infrastructure

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a minimum data centre availability SLA (e.g., 99.99% uptime) for all systems supporting critical business functions, with the SLA directly traceable to the institution's business recovery objectives.
requirements.rationale: Business recovery objectives must translate into contractual and operational commitments. Availability SLAs provide a measurable baseline against which redundancy adequacy can be evaluated and reported to senior management.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; reported quarterly to senior management

requirements.requirement: Ensure that procurement and financial planning processes include multi-year budgeting for redundant data centre capacity components (power, cooling, network), treating redundancy as a non-negotiable capital expenditure aligned to regulatory obligation.
requirements.rationale: Redundant capacity components and multiple distribution paths ('redundant capacity components and multiple distribution paths') require sustained capital investment. Failure to budget adequately results in deferred maintenance, degraded redundancy, and increased SPOF risk over time.
requirements.frequency: Annual budgeting cycle; reviewed semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Include data centre redundancy status as a standing agenda item in Business Continuity Management (BCM) governance reviews, with executive sign-off on any identified redundancy gaps and their remediation timelines.
requirements.rationale: Linking data centre resilience to BCM governance ensures that any erosion of redundancy (component failure, deferred replacement, design change) is escalated and resolved before it jeopardises achievement of business recovery objectives.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly BCM governance review

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement physically and logically redundant infrastructure across all critical data centre domains — power, cooling, network, compute, and storage — such that no single component failure can interrupt services beyond the institution's documented recovery objectives. Redundancy must cover both capacity (N+1 or 2N design) and distribution paths (diverse physical routing), with regular failover testing to validate that redundant paths are genuinely capable of carrying full production load. Third-party or co-location data centres used by the institution must be held to equivalent redundancy standards.

requirements.requirement: Design and implement redundant power infrastructure using a minimum N+1 or 2N architecture, including dual Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS), redundant Power Distribution Units (PDUs), automatic transfer switches, and multiple independent diesel generator sets with sufficient fuel capacity to sustain operations through declared recovery timeframes.
requirements.rationale: Power failure is the leading cause of data centre outages. The clause mandates 'redundant capacity components' — power systems are the most critical capacity component. A single UPS or generator failure must not result in loss of computer equipment availability.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Infrastructure / Data Centre Manager
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; monthly generator load testing; annual third-party certification

requirements.requirement: Implement redundant cooling infrastructure with N+1 or better Computer Room Air Conditioning (CRAC/CRAH) units, redundant chiller systems, and independent cooling distribution paths, ensuring no single cooling component failure can cause thermal shutdown of computer equipment.
requirements.rationale: Cooling failures are a primary cause of hardware-level SPOFs in data centres. The clause's requirement to eliminate 'any single point of failure' extends to all capacity components serving computer equipment, explicitly including environmental systems.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Infrastructure / Data Centre Manager
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring via DCIM tooling; quarterly maintenance; annual third-party audit

requirements.requirement: Deploy multiple independent network distribution paths from the data centre to the institution's wide-area network and internet egress points, using diverse physical cable routes, redundant core and aggregation switching layers (dual-homed), and separate upstream ISP or MPLS carriers to eliminate single-path network SPOFs.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly requires 'multiple distribution paths serving the computer equipment.' Network path redundancy ensures that a fibre cut, switch failure, or carrier outage does not isolate the data centre and prevent achievement of recovery objectives.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; quarterly failover path testing; annual architecture review

requirements.requirement: Implement redundant storage paths and compute clustering for all systems hosting critical business applications, including multi-path I/O (MPIO) for storage networks, dual Host Bus Adapters (HBAs), and high-availability clustering for servers, ensuring no single hardware component failure causes service interruption beyond documented RTO.
requirements.rationale: Computer equipment itself must be served by redundant internal distribution paths. Single HBAs, single storage controllers, or non-clustered servers constitute SPOFs at the equipment layer that the clause explicitly requires to be eliminated.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; semi-annual failover testing; annual capacity review

requirements.requirement: Conduct scheduled and documented failover simulation exercises for each redundant infrastructure component (power, cooling, network, compute, storage) to validate that redundant paths can sustain full production load, with results reviewed against business recovery objectives and deficiencies remediated within defined timelines.
requirements.rationale: Passive redundancy that has never been tested may fail when activated under load. The clause requires redundancy to be effective for 'effective achievement of the identified business recovery objectives' — which can only be confirmed through live validation testing.
requirements.frequency: Semi-annual simulation for each redundancy domain; annual full failover exercise

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that data centre redundancy is formally defined in policy, independently verified, and subject to continuous oversight by senior management and the Board. Responsibility for maintaining SPOF-free infrastructure must be assigned to named owners with accountability to the Board Risk Committee, and any identified gaps must follow a structured remediation and escalation process. Third-party and co-location arrangements must be governed by contractual obligations that enforce equivalent redundancy standards.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Data Centre Resilience Policy that mandates minimum redundancy standards (aligned to TIA-942 Tier III or equivalent) for all data centres operated by or on behalf of the institution, explicitly linking redundancy requirements to the institution's documented business recovery objectives and BNM RMiT obligations.
requirements.rationale: The clause imposes a mandatory obligation ('must ensure') that requires formal policy backing to be enforceable across technology, operations, and third-party teams. Without a governing policy, redundancy standards cannot be consistently applied or audited.
requirements.frequency: Annual policy review; Board Risk Committee approval

requirements.requirement: Assign a named Data Centre Resilience Owner at senior management level, responsible for maintaining a live Single Point of Failure (SPOF) Register that documents all identified SPOFs, their risk rating, remediation status, and target closure date, with monthly reporting to the CTO and quarterly reporting to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: The clause's mandate to 'eliminate any single point of failure' requires a structured, trackable process. A SPOF Register ensures that identified gaps do not persist unaddressed and provides the audit trail BNM would expect to see during examination.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Infrastructure (accountable to CTO)
requirements.frequency: SPOF Register updated monthly; reported quarterly to Board Risk Committee

requirements.requirement: Require independent assurance of data centre redundancy adequacy through annual third-party technical audits or Uptime Institute assessments, with findings formally reported to the Board Audit Committee and remediation plans approved by senior management within 30 days of receipt.
requirements.rationale: Independent verification is essential to confirm that self-reported redundancy measures genuinely eliminate SPOFs. Internal teams may normalise degraded redundancy over time; third-party audits provide the objective assurance required by BNM's supervisory expectations for Standard ('S') marker clauses.
requirements.frequency: Annual third-party audit; Board Audit Committee review within 45 days of report issuance

requirements.requirement: Incorporate data centre redundancy compliance requirements into all contracts with third-party co-location providers and outsourced data centre operators, including the right-to-audit, minimum Tier III (or equivalent) infrastructure standards, and mandatory incident notification within defined timeframes when redundancy is degraded.
requirements.rationale: The clause applies to data centres serving the institution regardless of ownership. Third-party arrangements must be contractually governed to ensure equivalent SPOF elimination standards are maintained outside the institution's direct control.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Head of Vendor Management
requirements.frequency: At contract inception and renewal; annual compliance attestation from vendors

requirements.requirement: Define and operationalise an escalation and incident management procedure specifically for data centre redundancy degradation events (e.g., loss of a redundant power path, cooling unit failure), requiring immediate notification to the CTO, activation of enhanced monitoring, and a mandatory remediation timeline with Board Risk Committee notification if full redundancy is not restored within 72 hours.
requirements.rationale: Redundancy is a dynamic state — components fail and are not always immediately replaced, creating temporary SPOFs that can persist. A formal degradation response process ensures that any period of reduced redundancy is actively managed and escalated, directly supporting the institution's ability to 'effectively achieve the identified business recovery objectives.'
requirements.frequency: Per-event; procedure reviewed annually and after each activation

business.summary: Failure to secure and adequately design the production data centre exposes the institution to unplanned outages, regulatory sanctions, and reputational damage resulting from physical breaches or infrastructure failures. Hosting critical systems in an unsecured or disaster-prone location creates concentration risk that could result in simultaneous loss of primary and backup capabilities. BNM expects the institution to treat data centre resilience as a material operational risk, with board-level ownership and sustained investment.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formal policy mandating that all critical systems are hosted exclusively in a designated, purpose-built production data centre space, physically segregated from non-production, shared, or general office environments.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires a 'dedicated space intended for production data centre usage.' Co-location with non-production workloads or general office areas introduces unauthorised access vectors and operational contamination risks that directly violate this requirement.
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed Annually; compliance confirmed at each system deployment

requirements.requirement: Conduct and document a formal disaster risk and geospatial hazard assessment for the production data centre site, covering flood plains, seismic zones, industrial hazard proximity, and extreme weather history, prior to site selection and at every major lease renewal.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly prohibits locating the data centre 'in a disaster-prone area.' Without a structured site risk assessment, the institution cannot demonstrate due diligence to BNM and risks catastrophic, simultaneous loss of critical systems during a natural or industrial disaster.
requirements.frequency: At site selection; reviewed every 3 years or upon material changes to the operating environment

requirements.requirement: Require that capital expenditure budgets and vendor contracts explicitly provision for N+1 or 2N redundancy across all critical data centre infrastructure categories — hardware, electrical, thermal, and connectivity — with no single contract or supplier constituting a single point of failure.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires 'no single point of failure in the design and connectivity for critical components.' Budget and procurement decisions made at the business level are the upstream control that determines whether technical redundancy is achievable; under-investment directly creates regulatory non-compliance.
requirements.owner: Chief Financial Officer (CFO) co-owned with Chief Operating Officer (COO)
requirements.frequency: Annual budget cycle; reviewed at each major vendor contract renewal

requirements.requirement: Define and enforce a formal maintenance programme with contracted SLAs covering all critical data centre components (hardware, UPS, generators, cooling, and cabling infrastructure), ensuring scheduled preventive maintenance is performed and evidenced without creating unplanned outage windows.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates 'adequate maintenance' of critical components. Absence of a structured, documented maintenance programme leaves the institution unable to demonstrate compliance and increases mean-time-to-failure for components that protect against service disruption.
requirements.frequency: Maintenance schedules reviewed Quarterly; maintenance activities performed per manufacturer and vendor SLA cadence

requirements.requirement: Establish a business continuity impact threshold that triggers mandatory escalation and remediation when data centre monitoring systems generate alerts on faults or potential failure indicators, with defined Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs) for critical systems housed in the production data centre.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires 'timely alerts on faults and indicators of potential issues.' Without a business-level response framework tied to those alerts, monitoring becomes a technical exercise with no operational consequence, undermining the intent of the clause.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) co-owned with Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Thresholds reviewed Annually; escalation procedures tested Semi-annually via tabletop or live drill

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement layered physical access controls, eliminate single points of failure across all critical infrastructure categories, and deploy continuous monitoring with automated alerting to satisfy Clause 10.26. Each critical component category — hardware, electrical, thermal, and connectivity — must be independently redundant and individually monitored. Evidence of technical controls must be maintainable and auditable for BNM examination.

requirements.requirement: Implement multi-layered physical access controls for the production data centre space, including perimeter fencing or secured building access, mantrap or airlock entry, biometric or multi-factor authentication at the data centre door, CCTV with minimum 90-day retention, and a visitor access log with mandatory escort policy for all non-authorised personnel.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires the dedicated space to be 'physically secured from unauthorised access.' Physical access controls are the primary technical mechanism; without layered controls, a single control failure enables unauthorised access to critical systems.
requirements.frequency: Controls reviewed and access logs audited Monthly; CCTV and biometric systems tested Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Design and implement fully redundant electrical utility infrastructure for the production data centre, including dual independent utility feeds from separate substations or transformers, N+1 or 2N Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS) systems, and at minimum one diesel generator with automatic transfer switch (ATS) capable of sustaining full data centre load, with sufficient fuel capacity and a tested refuelling contract.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly lists 'electrical utility' as a critical component requiring 'no single point of failure.' Power loss is the most common cause of data centre outages; dual-feed architecture with UPS and generator backup eliminates the electrical single point of failure mandated by the clause.
requirements.owner: Head of Data Centre Operations / Head of Infrastructure
requirements.frequency: UPS battery tests Monthly; generator load tests Quarterly; full failover simulation Annually

requirements.requirement: Deploy redundant thermal management systems for the production data centre using N+1 or 2N precision cooling units (Computer Room Air Conditioning / Computer Room Air Handler), with hot aisle/cold aisle containment, independent cooling circuits, and automatic failover capability, ensuring the thermal environment remains within manufacturer-specified operating ranges under any single cooling unit failure.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly lists 'thermal management' as a critical component requiring 'no single point of failure.' Thermal failure causes accelerated hardware degradation and emergency shutdowns; redundant cooling with automatic failover is the technical control that eliminates this single point of failure.
requirements.owner: Head of Data Centre Operations / Head of Infrastructure
requirements.frequency: Cooling unit maintenance per manufacturer schedule (typically Quarterly); failover simulation Annually

requirements.requirement: Implement hardware-level redundancy for all critical system components within the production data centre, including redundant network interface cards (NICs) with link aggregation or active-active bonding, redundant storage controllers and disk arrays (RAID with hot spares), redundant server power supplies, and dual network uplinks to the core switching layer with no single-switch dependency for any critical system.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires 'no single point of failure in the design and connectivity for critical components' including 'hardware components.' Component-level redundancy ensures that no individual hardware failure results in a critical system outage, which is the baseline expectation for a production-grade data centre.
requirements.owner: Head of Infrastructure / Head of IT Operations
requirements.frequency: Hardware redundancy validated at deployment; reviewed Annually and following any hardware change

requirements.requirement: Deploy a holistic, real-time Data Centre Infrastructure Management (DCIM) or equivalent monitoring platform covering environmental sensors (temperature, humidity, airflow), power draw and UPS health, cooling unit status, physical access events, network link state, and hardware fault indicators — with automated alerting to on-call personnel via multiple channels (SMS, email, ticketing system) and defined escalation tiers for each alert severity.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates 'holistic and continuous monitoring of these critical components with timely alerts on faults and indicators of potential issues.' Point-in-time monitoring or monitoring of only a subset of components does not satisfy 'holistic'; manual check-based monitoring does not satisfy 'continuous' or 'timely.'
requirements.owner: Head of IT Operations / Head of Data Centre Operations
requirements.frequency: Monitoring platform availability reviewed Monthly; alert thresholds and escalation matrix reviewed Semi-annually

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that data centre physical security and resilience obligations are embedded in policy, subject to independent audit, and linked to formal risk management frameworks reported at the board level. Clause 10.26 carries an 'S' marker, meaning BNM expects demonstrable, institutionalised controls rather than ad hoc measures. Governance requirements span policy ownership, risk reporting, audit assurance, and vendor management oversight.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Board-approved Data Centre Physical Security and Resilience Policy that explicitly addresses: dedicated-space requirements for critical systems, physical access control standards, prohibited site characteristics (disaster-prone areas), redundancy mandates for each critical component category, maintenance obligations, and monitoring requirements — with version control, annual review, and Board or Board Risk Committee (BRC) sign-off.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text's 'shall' and 'must' language across multiple requirements necessitates documented, board-approved policy to assign accountability and demonstrate institutional commitment. A policy gap means individual technical controls exist without governance-level ownership, which BNM examiners will treat as a systemic control weakness.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee (BRC) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed Annually and following any material change to data centre footprint, regulatory guidance, or significant incident

requirements.requirement: Integrate data centre physical security and resilience into the institution's Technology Risk Register with defined risk appetite statements, key risk indicators (KRIs) tied to monitoring alert thresholds, and mandatory escalation to the Board Risk Committee when KRIs are breached or when any critical component enters a degraded (non-redundant) state.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text's requirement for 'no single point of failure' and 'continuous monitoring' implies that degraded redundancy states are material risk events. Without formal risk register integration and KRI reporting, the Board cannot exercise oversight of data centre resilience risk, violating the spirit of the clause and BNM's broader risk governance expectations.
requirements.frequency: KRIs reported to Board Risk Committee Quarterly; risk register updated Continuously as new faults or changes occur

requirements.requirement: Commission an independent annual audit of the production data centre physical security and resilience controls, covering access control effectiveness, redundancy configuration validation, maintenance record completeness, and monitoring system coverage — with findings reported directly to the Audit Committee and a tracked remediation plan for all issues rated Medium or above.
requirements.rationale: The 'S' marker and the breadth of the verbatim requirements across physical security, redundancy, maintenance, and monitoring necessitate independent assurance. Internal self-assessment is insufficient for BNM examination purposes; independent audit provides the objective evidence required to demonstrate ongoing compliance.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Audit Committee
requirements.frequency: Annually; additionally triggered by significant incidents, major infrastructure changes, or regulatory findings

requirements.requirement: Establish a Third-Party and Vendor Risk Management sub-process specifically for data centre service providers, co-location providers, and critical infrastructure maintenance vendors, requiring contractual SLAs aligned to the institution's RTO/RPO, right-to-audit clauses, evidence of vendor redundancy and disaster recovery capability, and annual vendor performance reviews.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text's requirements for adequate maintenance and no single point of failure extend to vendor dependencies. A single-vendor or single-contract dependency for UPS maintenance, generator servicing, or network connectivity constitutes a governance-level single point of failure that mirrors the technical SPOF prohibition in the clause.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) co-owned with Chief Operating Officer (COO)
requirements.frequency: Vendor reviews Annually; contract SLA compliance reviewed Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Define and enforce a formal Change Management process for any physical or logical changes to the production data centre environment — including infrastructure additions, decommissions, maintenance windows, and access control changes — requiring impact assessment for SPOF introduction, documented approvals from the Data Centre Operations and Risk functions, and post-change validation of monitoring and redundancy integrity.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text's requirements for 'no single point of failure' and 'holistic and continuous monitoring' can be inadvertently violated by uncontrolled changes. A formal change management process ensures that redundancy and monitoring coverage are assessed before and confirmed after every change, maintaining continuous compliance rather than point-in-time compliance.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Operations / Chief Information Officer (CIO)
requirements.frequency: Per-change; change management process reviewed Annually and following any post-change incident

business.summary: Clause 10.27 mandates that financial institutions maintain structured, documented control procedures across all data centre operations — spanning batch processing, production change management, error handling, and exceptional condition management. Failure to comply exposes the institution to operational disruptions, erroneous financial processing (e.g., failed settlement or salary disbursement batches), and regulatory censure from BNM. Inadequate control procedures also increase the likelihood of undetected processing errors propagating into downstream systems, amplifying financial and reputational harm.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formally approved Data Centre Operations Control Procedures policy that covers all operational domains mandated by Clause 10.27: batch processing management, production change implementation, error handling, and exceptional condition management.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly requires 'adequate control procedures for its data centre operations' as a baseline obligation. Without a formally approved policy, the institution cannot demonstrate regulatory compliance and lacks an authoritative framework for operational staff to follow, increasing the risk of inconsistent or ad hoc practices.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; immediate update upon material change to operations or regulatory guidance

requirements.requirement: Define and publish batch processing Service Level Agreements (SLAs) for all critical financial batch jobs — including interbank settlements, loan interest accruals, card billing cycles, and payroll disbursements — specifying expected completion windows, accuracy thresholds, and escalation triggers for SLA breaches.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires automated tools to 'ensure timely and accurate batch processes.' Business-defined SLAs translate this regulatory obligation into measurable operational targets, enabling monitoring, accountability, and evidence of compliance during BNM examinations.
requirements.frequency: Annual review of SLA thresholds; continuous monitoring against defined targets

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Change Request and Approval process for all production system changes, requiring documented business justification, impact assessment, rollback plans, and sign-off from designated business and technology stakeholders before any change is promoted to production.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly mandates 'procedures for implementing changes in the production system.' Uncontrolled production changes are a primary cause of outages and data corruption in financial institutions; a formal approval chain protects business continuity and provides an audit trail for BNM supervisory review.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (every production change); quarterly review of change process effectiveness

requirements.requirement: Implement a documented Error and Exception Management Framework that classifies batch processing errors by severity, assigns business ownership for resolution, defines maximum tolerable resolution timeframes per severity tier, and mandates root cause analysis for recurring or high-impact exceptions.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'error handling as well as management of other exceptional conditions.' Without a structured framework, errors may go unresolved or be handled inconsistently, leading to financial loss, customer harm, or systemic processing failures that regulators would view as evidence of inadequate operational control.
requirements.frequency: Per-event for error resolution; quarterly trend analysis and framework review

requirements.requirement: Conduct periodic business impact assessments of data centre operational control procedures to validate that existing controls remain adequate relative to changes in business volumes, product complexity, and technology landscape, and to identify gaps requiring remediation.
requirements.rationale: The standard of 'adequate' control procedures in the verbatim text is dynamic — what was adequate two years ago may be insufficient as transaction volumes grow or new products are launched. Periodic business-led assessments ensure controls remain proportionate and effective, demonstrating ongoing regulatory compliance rather than point-in-time adherence.
requirements.frequency: Annual; triggered ad hoc by material business or technology changes

technology.summary: Technology teams must deploy, configure, and maintain automated tooling for batch job scheduling, monitoring, and alerting; implement robust change management tooling with gated promotion pipelines; and build comprehensive error detection, logging, and exception handling capabilities across data centre operations. These implementations must be continuously validated to ensure batch processes complete within defined SLAs and that production systems remain stable and auditable. All tooling must generate sufficient audit trails to support both internal review and BNM examination requirements.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and operationalise an enterprise-grade automated batch job scheduling and management platform (e.g., Control-M, IBM Workload Scheduler, or equivalent) capable of scheduling, sequencing, dependency mapping, and tracking all critical batch jobs across the data centre, with full audit logging of job execution history, outcomes, and any manual interventions.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly mandates 'deployment of relevant automated tools for batch processing management to ensure timely and accurate batch processes.' Manual or semi-automated batch management introduces human error and latency; automated schedulers with dependency management ensure jobs execute in correct sequence and within SLA windows, providing the evidence trail required for regulatory examination.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Infrastructure / Head of Technology Operations
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; tool capability and configuration reviewed annually and after major version upgrades

requirements.requirement: Implement real-time batch monitoring and alerting with automated notification to operations staff and escalation to management when batch jobs fail, are delayed beyond defined SLA thresholds, produce output record-count or checksum discrepancies, or execute outside their defined processing windows.
requirements.rationale: The requirement to 'ensure timely and accurate batch processes' necessitates detection mechanisms that identify deviations from expected outcomes in real time, not retrospectively. Proactive alerting reduces the window between failure occurrence and remediation, minimising downstream financial impact and regulatory exposure.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; alert threshold calibration reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement a gated, tooling-enforced Change Management pipeline for all production system changes, incorporating automated controls for environment segregation (dev/test/staging/production), mandatory approval workflow gates, automated regression and smoke testing prior to promotion, and one-click automated rollback capability. No production change may be deployed outside this pipeline.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates 'procedures for implementing changes in the production system.' Technology must enforce — not merely document — these procedures through tooling that prevents bypass. Ungated production changes are a leading cause of production incidents in financial institutions and represent a direct compliance gap under this clause.
requirements.frequency: Per-event enforcement; pipeline configuration and controls reviewed semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Implement centralised, structured error logging and exception management for all data centre operations, including automated correlation of errors across batch jobs, infrastructure, and application layers, with integration into an ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow) for ticket creation, assignment, tracking, and resolution documentation. Error logs must be retained in accordance with BNM record retention requirements.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'error handling as well as management of other exceptional conditions.' Centralised structured logging enables operations teams to detect patterns, perform root cause analysis, and demonstrate to BNM examiners that errors are systematically identified, tracked, and resolved — not handled ad hoc or silently discarded.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; log integrity and retention compliance audited quarterly

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain automated batch reconciliation controls that validate output record counts, control totals, and checksums against source data at the completion of each critical batch cycle, generating automated reconciliation reports and flagging discrepancies for immediate investigation before downstream distribution or posting.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires batch processes to be 'timely and accurate.' Accuracy cannot be assumed — it must be verified programmatically. Automated reconciliation controls provide a technical safeguard against silent data corruption or processing errors that could propagate into financial records, customer balances, or regulatory reports if undetected.
requirements.frequency: Automated execution per batch cycle; reconciliation control design reviewed annually

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that data centre operations control procedures are formally owned, periodically reviewed, audited for effectiveness, and subject to Board-level oversight. A Change Advisory Board (CAB) process must govern production change approvals, and a defined escalation hierarchy must exist for operational exceptions that exceed technology team authority to resolve independently. BNM's 'S' marker on this clause signals that compliance is non-negotiable, requiring documented evidence of governance activities to be available for supervisory examination at any time.

requirements.requirement: Establish a formally constituted Change Advisory Board (CAB) with defined membership (technology, operations, risk, and affected business line representatives), meeting cadence, decision-making authority, and escalation path to senior management for emergency changes, to review and approve all non-emergency production system changes prior to implementation.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates 'procedures for implementing changes in the production system.' A CAB provides the institutional governance structure that ensures changes are reviewed by appropriately diverse stakeholders before deployment, reducing the risk of operationally or financially disruptive changes and providing BNM with evidence of structured change governance.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Chief Operating Officer (COO)
requirements.frequency: Weekly CAB meetings for normal changes; emergency CAB convened within 2 hours for critical changes; annual charter review

requirements.requirement: Define and document a formal escalation matrix for batch processing failures, production errors, and exceptional conditions that specifies escalation triggers, responsible parties at each tier (operations staff → IT management → C-suite → Board Risk Committee), maximum notification timeframes per severity level, and required documentation at each escalation stage.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'management of other exceptional conditions,' implying governance structures that ensure exceptions are escalated to appropriate decision-making authority — not managed entirely at the operational level. A documented escalation matrix prevents both under-escalation (where significant issues go unnoticed by leadership) and over-escalation (which desensitises senior management to operational events).
requirements.frequency: Escalation matrix reviewed semi-annually; compliance with escalation timeframes audited quarterly

requirements.requirement: Mandate periodic independent review (by Internal Audit or a qualified external party) of data centre operations control procedures, covering adequacy of batch processing controls, production change governance, error handling effectiveness, and exceptional condition management — with findings reported to the Board Risk Committee and tracked to closure.
requirements.rationale: BNM's 'adequate' standard requires the institution to objectively assess whether controls are functioning as designed. Independent review provides assurance that management's self-assessment is not biased, and reporting to the Board Risk Committee ensures governance accountability at the highest institutional level — consistent with BNM's expectation of board-level technology risk oversight.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor / Head of Internal Audit
requirements.frequency: Annual independent review; targeted reviews following significant incidents or material operational changes

requirements.requirement: Establish Key Risk Indicators (KRIs) and Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for data centre operations — including batch SLA breach rates, production incident counts attributable to change failures, mean-time-to-resolution for batch errors, and exception recurrence rates — reported to the Technology Risk Committee and Board Risk Committee on a defined schedule.
requirements.rationale: Governance of data centre operations must extend beyond procedure documentation to ongoing performance measurement. KRIs and KPIs operationalise the clause's requirements into measurable metrics, enabling the Board and senior management to exercise informed oversight and detect degradation in operational control effectiveness before it escalates into a regulatory or business incident.
requirements.frequency: Monthly reporting to Technology Risk Committee; quarterly reporting to Board Risk Committee

requirements.requirement: Implement a mandatory training and competency programme for all data centre operations staff covering the institution's control procedures for batch processing management, production change implementation, error handling, and exceptional condition management — with training records maintained and competency assessed at defined intervals.
requirements.rationale: Control procedures are only effective if the staff responsible for executing them are trained and competent. The clause's requirement for 'adequate control procedures' implicitly encompasses human factors; undocumented or untrained procedures represent a governance gap that BNM examiners routinely identify. Documented training records also serve as evidence of institutional commitment to operational discipline.
requirements.owner: Head of Human Resources / Head of Technology Operations
requirements.frequency: Onboarding training for new staff; annual refresher for existing staff; immediate re-training following material procedure updates or significant operational incidents

business.summary: Failure to segregate incompatible activities in data centre operations exposes the institution to insider threats, accidental data corruption, and regulatory sanctions. Uncontrolled vendor or programmer access to the production environment creates vectors for fraud, data exfiltration, and service disruption. Non-compliance undermines trust in operational integrity and may trigger BNM supervisory action under the Financial Services Act 2013.

requirements.requirement: Establish and enforce a formal Data Centre Activity Segregation Policy that explicitly identifies and lists incompatible activities within the data centre operations environment, including separation between development, testing, staging, and production workloads.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates segregation of 'incompatible activities' to 'prevent any unauthorised activity.' Without a defined and communicated policy, operations staff cannot consistently identify which activity combinations are prohibited, creating compliance gaps and operational risk.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; immediate update upon material change to DC operations or technology architecture

requirements.requirement: Implement a formal, documented approval process requiring explicit business justification for any vendor or programmer access to the production environment, including sign-off by a designated business owner before access is provisioned.
requirements.rationale: The clause specifies that vendor and programmer access 'must be properly authorised,' implying a deliberate, pre-approved process rather than ad hoc or default access. Business ownership of this approval ensures accountability beyond IT teams and creates an audit trail for regulatory inspection.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Head of Operations
requirements.frequency: Per-event (each access request); quarterly review of standing access arrangements

requirements.requirement: Maintain a current register of all vendors and programmers with approved access to the production environment, including access scope, duration, business justification, and authorising officer, accessible for BNM examination on demand.
requirements.rationale: Effective monitoring and authorisation as required by the clause cannot be demonstrated without a maintained register. This register provides the evidentiary basis for BNM supervisory reviews and supports incident investigation.
requirements.owner: Head of Technology / Chief Information Officer (CIO)
requirements.frequency: Continuous maintenance; quarterly reconciliation against active access accounts

requirements.requirement: Conduct periodic risk assessments of the data centre activity segregation model to evaluate whether existing controls remain adequate in light of changes to the vendor landscape, system architecture, or threat environment.
requirements.rationale: The clause's objective of preventing 'any unauthorised activity' is ongoing. A static segregation model degrades in effectiveness as the environment evolves. Regular risk assessment ensures the institution proactively identifies and closes gaps before regulatory or incident exposure.
requirements.frequency: Semi-annual; triggered immediately after significant infrastructure changes or security incidents

requirements.requirement: Define escalation and response procedures for incidents involving actual or suspected unauthorised activity in the data centre operations environment, including mandatory reporting thresholds to senior management and BNM where required.
requirements.rationale: Segregation controls are preventive; the clause's intent to 'prevent any unauthorised activity' implies the institution must also detect and respond when prevention fails. Clear escalation paths protect the institution from compounded harm and support regulatory notification obligations.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Per-event; procedures reviewed annually

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement both logical and physical controls to enforce separation of incompatible data centre activities and enforce strict, monitored access pathways for vendors and programmers entering the production environment. This requires a layered architecture spanning network segmentation, privileged access management, and comprehensive session monitoring. All technical controls must generate tamper-evident audit logs sufficient to demonstrate compliance to BNM examiners.

requirements.requirement: Implement network segmentation and environment isolation in the data centre to enforce strict separation between production, staging, testing, and development zones, preventing lateral movement across incompatible activity domains. Controls must include firewall policy enforcement, VLAN segregation, and micro-segmentation where applicable.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires segregation of 'incompatible activities in the data centre operations environment.' Network-level segmentation is the foundational technical control that makes logical separation enforceable, reducing the attack surface for unauthorised activity spreading across environments.
requirements.owner: Head of Infrastructure / Chief Information Officer (CIO)
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; quarterly firewall rule review; annual architecture review

requirements.requirement: Deploy a Privileged Access Management (PAM) solution to broker and control all vendor and programmer access to the production environment, enforcing just-in-time access provisioning, time-bound sessions, credential vaulting, and automated session termination upon expiry or anomaly detection.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires that vendor and programmer access to production 'must be properly authorised and monitored.' A PAM solution provides the technical mechanism to enforce pre-approved, time-limited access while preventing credential sharing or standing persistent access that could enable unauthorised activity.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; PAM policy rules reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement full session recording and real-time monitoring for all privileged and vendor access sessions in the production environment, capturing keystroke logs, screen recordings, and command execution records in a tamper-evident, centralised log repository with a minimum retention period aligned to BNM audit requirements.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates that access to production by vendors or programmers 'must be properly authorised and monitored.' Session recording provides the technical means to demonstrate monitoring occurred and supports forensic investigation of any unauthorised activity, satisfying both the monitoring requirement and the audit trail needed for regulatory examination.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of IT Security
requirements.frequency: Continuous; log integrity verified monthly; retention policy reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Enforce Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and mandatory Segregation of Duties (SoD) in all data centre management platforms (hypervisors, storage, network management, orchestration tools) to ensure no single user can perform incompatible operations such as developing, deploying, and approving changes within the production environment.
requirements.rationale: Technical SoD directly addresses the clause's requirement to 'segregate incompatible activities.' Without RBAC and SoD controls at the platform layer, environment network segmentation can be bypassed by users with overly broad permissions, defeating the purpose of the segregation mandate.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Operations / Chief Information Officer (CIO)
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; access rights reviewed quarterly via access certification campaigns

requirements.requirement: Integrate data centre access activity logs with a Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system configured with use-case-specific detection rules to alert on anomalous or unauthorised access patterns in the production environment, including after-hours access, access from unapproved IP ranges, and privilege escalation attempts.
requirements.rationale: Passive log collection does not fulfil the clause's monitoring obligation without active detection capability. SIEM integration with tuned alerting enables real-time identification of potential unauthorised activity as required by the clause, reducing mean time to detect and respond to segregation violations.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; detection rules tuned quarterly

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that data centre activity segregation is formally mandated through board-approved policy, that vendor and programmer access to production is subject to a controlled, auditable authorisation framework, and that ongoing compliance is independently verified. The 'S' marker confirms this is a standard BNM requirement, meaning examiners will expect documented evidence of policy, authorisation records, and audit findings.

requirements.requirement: Develop, obtain board or board-level committee approval for, and maintain a Data Centre Operations Segregation Policy that defines incompatible activity classifications, segregation principles, and minimum technical and procedural controls mandated across all data centre environments of the financial institution.
requirements.rationale: The clause imposes an obligation on 'a financial institution' as an entity, implying institutional-level policy ownership. Board-level approval signals that segregation is a governed risk control and not merely an IT operational preference, establishing clear accountability and providing the policy authority from which all operational procedures derive.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Board approval upon initial adoption; annual policy review; ad hoc review after major incidents or regulatory changes

requirements.requirement: Establish a formalised Third-Party and Programmer Production Access Authorisation Framework, including request templates, mandatory fields (business justification, access scope, duration, risk assessment, approving authority), approval authority matrix by risk tier, and defined revocation procedures upon task completion or contract expiry.
requirements.rationale: The clause states access 'must be properly authorised,' implying a structured, evidenced process. A documented framework ensures authorisation decisions are consistent, risk-proportionate, and reviewable by BNM examiners, and prevents informal or undocumented access from persisting in the production environment.
requirements.owner: Head of Technology Risk / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Framework reviewed annually; each access request processed per-event

requirements.requirement: Mandate periodic independent internal audit reviews of data centre activity segregation controls and vendor/programmer access authorisation records, with findings reported to the Board Audit Committee and tracked through to remediation within defined SLAs.
requirements.rationale: The clause's monitoring requirement extends to governance-level oversight. Independent audit provides assurance that declared segregation controls are operating effectively in practice, not merely documented in policy. Board Audit Committee visibility ensures accountability at the highest governance level.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Board Audit Committee
requirements.frequency: Annual full audit; targeted reviews triggered by incidents or significant infrastructure changes

requirements.requirement: Incorporate data centre activity segregation and production access control requirements into all vendor contracts and service-level agreements, including explicit clauses on access scope limitations, monitoring consent, compliance with the institution's access policies, and audit rights.
requirements.rationale: The clause specifically calls out vendor access as a risk scenario requiring authorisation and monitoring. Without contractual obligations, vendors may not be legally bound to comply with the institution's segregation controls, creating both operational and regulatory risk. Contractual embedding supports enforceability.
requirements.owner: Head of Vendor Management / Chief Procurement Officer
requirements.frequency: Embedded at contract initiation and renewal; reviewed annually against current policy standards

requirements.requirement: Implement a quarterly management assurance process whereby the Head of IT Operations or designated delegate certifies compliance with the Data Centre Activity Segregation Policy, including confirmation that all active vendor and programmer production access is current, authorised, and monitored, with exceptions formally escalated to the CRO.
requirements.rationale: Governance requires continuous ownership, not only point-in-time audits. Quarterly management certification creates a regular cadence of accountability between audit cycles, ensures standing access does not silently persist beyond its business need, and produces documented assurance evidence for BNM regulatory examination.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Operations / Chief Information Officer (CIO)
requirements.frequency: Quarterly

business.summary: Failure to plan system capacity against peak periods and growth forecasts exposes the institution to service degradation or outages during critical processing windows, directly impacting customer experience, transaction settlement, and revenue. Unplanned capacity shortfalls during events such as salary crediting cycles, year-end processing, or product launches can trigger regulatory reporting obligations and reputational harm. Effective capacity planning ensures business operations remain uninterrupted and that technology investment decisions are aligned with strategic growth trajectories.

requirements.requirement: Business units must produce and maintain a forward-looking capacity demand forecast, updated at least annually or upon material business changes, that quantifies expected transaction volumes, user growth, and data storage needs over a minimum 3-year horizon aligned with the institution's business growth plans.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly requires planning 'with due regard to business growth plans.' Without structured demand inputs from business owners, technology capacity investments will lag growth, creating avoidable service constraints.
requirements.frequency: Annual minimum; triggered additionally by strategic plan revisions or new product launches

requirements.requirement: All business units must identify and formally document their peak processing periods (e.g., month-end, quarter-end, Hari Raya, salary crediting cycles, tax submission deadlines) and communicate associated transaction volume spikes to the technology function no less than 60 days in advance of anticipated peak events.
requirements.rationale: The clause specifically calls out 'peak processing period' as a planning dimension. Business units are the authoritative source for knowing when peak demand occurs; failure to surface this data prevents technology from provisioning adequate capacity in time.
requirements.frequency: Semi-annual review; ad-hoc notification per upcoming peak event

requirements.requirement: Any proposed new product, service, or channel that is expected to materially alter system load must undergo a capacity impact assessment as part of the business case approval process before launch approval is granted.
requirements.rationale: Business growth plans include new product rollouts, which may introduce sudden load on shared infrastructure. Embedding capacity review into the business case gate ensures growth does not outpace infrastructure without prior planning.
requirements.owner: Chief Business Officer / Head of Product
requirements.frequency: Per new product or service initiative

requirements.requirement: The annual budget cycle must include a dedicated capacity investment line, informed by the capacity demand forecast, to fund hardware, cloud resources, licensing, and managed services required to meet projected load including a defined headroom buffer above peak forecast.
requirements.rationale: Well-planned capacity management requires committed funding. Without explicit budget allocation tied to demand forecasts, capacity upgrades compete against discretionary spend and are frequently deferred, increasing service risk.
requirements.frequency: Annual (budget cycle); mid-year reforecast if material deviations occur

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement continuous capacity monitoring, structured load testing, and a repeatable capacity planning process that integrates inputs from peak-period schedules and architecture change programmes. Systems must be instrumented to detect capacity thresholds before they impact service, and architecture change management must include mandatory capacity impact assessments. The overall objective is to maintain demonstrable evidence that capacity is proactively managed rather than reactively patched.

requirements.requirement: Implement real-time capacity monitoring across all Tier-1 and Tier-2 systems covering CPU utilisation, memory, storage I/O, network bandwidth, and application-layer throughput metrics. Automated alerting must trigger at defined warning thresholds (e.g., 70%) and critical thresholds (e.g., 85%) with escalation to on-call engineers and the IT Operations Centre.
requirements.rationale: The requirement to 'manage' capacity demands continuous visibility. Monitoring provides the operational feedback loop to detect when actual consumption approaches planned limits, enabling intervention before service degradation occurs — particularly relevant to peak processing periods.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (real-time); threshold review quarterly

requirements.requirement: Conduct capacity and load testing on all critical systems at least semi-annually, and additionally before each identified peak processing period. Tests must simulate peak-plus-20% transaction volumes to validate headroom and must produce a formal test report documenting results, identified constraints, and remediation actions.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text references 'peak processing period' as a planning dimension. Load testing is the primary technical method to validate that planned capacity is sufficient before real peak demand materialises, preventing reactive incidents.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Infrastructure / Head of Application Delivery
requirements.frequency: Semi-annual baseline; additionally 4-6 weeks before each major peak event

requirements.requirement: All technology architecture changes — including migrations, platform upgrades, new integrations, and decommissions — must undergo a mandatory capacity impact assessment prior to change approval. The assessment must document expected changes to resource consumption on dependent systems and confirm that post-change capacity headroom meets defined service level targets.
requirements.rationale: The clause specifically identifies 'technology architecture changes' as a dimension requiring capacity planning. Architecture changes frequently introduce unintended load redistribution or resource contention; assessment at the change gate prevents capacity surprises post-implementation.
requirements.frequency: Per architecture change request; reviewed as part of Change Advisory Board (CAB) process

requirements.requirement: Maintain a rolling 12-month capacity trend report for all critical systems, comparing actual consumption against forecasted demand. Trend analysis must identify systems approaching capacity thresholds within the planning horizon and trigger a formal capacity upgrade or optimisation project with documented timelines.
requirements.rationale: Effective capacity management requires trend analysis to distinguish temporary spikes from sustained growth trajectories. This directly supports the requirement to plan capacity 'with due regard to business growth plans' by providing data-driven evidence of growth patterns.
requirements.frequency: Monthly trend data capture; quarterly trend report review

requirements.requirement: Define and maintain documented capacity thresholds and scaling runbooks for all Tier-1 systems, including procedures for both vertical scaling (resource uplift) and horizontal scaling (additional nodes or cloud burst). Runbooks must be tested at least annually and kept current with the production architecture.
requirements.rationale: Proactive capacity management requires not only detection but also pre-defined response procedures. Runbooks ensure that when capacity thresholds are breached — particularly during peak periods — engineers can execute approved, tested remediation steps without ad-hoc improvisation that may introduce additional risk.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Infrastructure / Head of IT Operations
requirements.frequency: Annual runbook review and test; updated upon architecture changes

governance.summary: Governance over system capacity planning must be formalised through policy, ownership, and recurring oversight mechanisms that integrate business demand signals, technology execution, and executive accountability. Given BNM's 'S' (Standard) classification, capacity planning must be evidenced as a structured, repeatable discipline rather than an informal practice. Audit trails, board-level visibility of material capacity risks, and defined escalation paths are essential to demonstrate regulatory compliance.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formal Capacity Management Policy approved by the Board or Board-delegated IT Steering Committee. The policy must define scope, roles and responsibilities, planning horizons, threshold standards, review cadences, and escalation procedures for capacity breaches, with explicit reference to peak period planning and business growth inputs.
requirements.rationale: BNM's use of 'must ensure' in the clause places an institutional obligation on the financial institution as a whole, not merely the IT function. A board-approved policy establishes formal ownership and demonstrates governance-level commitment to compliance.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Information Officer (CIO)
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed and reaffirmed annually; updated upon material regulatory or operational changes

requirements.requirement: Establish a Capacity Review Committee (or integrate capacity management as a standing agenda item in the IT Steering Committee) meeting at least quarterly. The committee must include representation from business, technology, and risk functions, and must review capacity trend reports, upcoming peak periods, planned architecture changes, and any open capacity remediation actions.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires capacity to be 'well-planned and managed' across three dimensions simultaneously — peak periods, growth plans, and architecture changes — which span multiple functions. A cross-functional governance forum ensures integrated oversight rather than siloed management.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly; extraordinary meeting if a critical capacity threshold is breached

requirements.requirement: Define and document a formal escalation and exception management process for capacity shortfalls. Any system projected to breach defined capacity thresholds within 90 days must be formally escalated to senior management with a remediation plan and target resolution date. Material capacity risks that cannot be remediated within 90 days must be reported to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: Capacity shortfalls that remain unescalated become service failures. A structured escalation path ensures that material capacity risks receive senior attention and resource prioritisation commensurate with their potential impact on service availability.
requirements.frequency: Triggered per threshold breach; reviewed at each Capacity Review Committee meeting

requirements.requirement: Maintain a Capacity Management Register — a central inventory of all critical systems, their current utilisation levels, rated capacity, headroom percentage, next scheduled review date, and any open remediation actions. The register must be kept current and made available to internal audit and BNM examiners upon request.
requirements.rationale: BNM examiners assessing compliance with this clause will require documentary evidence of planning and management activities. A maintained register provides auditable proof that capacity is tracked systematically across the institution's technology estate, directly evidencing the 'well-planned and managed' requirement.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Governance / Head of IT Infrastructure
requirements.frequency: Updated monthly; reviewed quarterly by Capacity Review Committee; presented to IT Steering Committee annually

requirements.requirement: Internal Audit must include system capacity management as an annual audit scope item, assessing whether capacity planning processes are operating effectively, whether peak period planning is evidenced, and whether capacity investments are aligned with the institution's approved business growth plans. Audit findings must be reported to the Audit Committee with tracked remediation timelines.
requirements.rationale: Independent assurance is essential to validate that governance and technology controls are functioning as designed. Annual internal audit coverage of capacity management provides the Board with objective evidence of compliance and identifies control gaps before they result in regulatory findings.
requirements.frequency: Annual audit; follow-up on high-risk findings within 90 days

business.summary: Failure to establish real-time monitoring exposes a financial institution to undetected service degradation and prolonged outages, directly threatening customer experience, transactional integrity, and regulatory standing under BNM oversight. Without actionable alerts and defined thresholds, operational teams cannot respond before interruptions escalate into reportable incidents. Periodic review of monitoring scope ensures the institution's risk posture keeps pace with evolving technology and business complexity.

requirements.requirement: Define and formally document the scope of 'key processes and services' subject to real-time monitoring, including customer-facing channels, payment systems, core banking, and critical back-office processes, reviewed at least annually and upon material changes to the technology landscape.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates monitoring of 'key processes and services'; without a formally approved and maintained scope definition, the institution cannot demonstrate regulatory compliance and risks monitoring gaps that lead to undetected outages affecting customers and counterparties.
requirements.frequency: Annual (and upon material change)

requirements.requirement: Establish measurable service availability and performance targets (e.g., uptime SLAs, transaction response times) aligned to customer commitments and BNM expectations, which serve as the business-level basis for setting monitoring thresholds.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires monitoring of 'capacity utilisation and performance'; business-defined SLAs translate regulatory intent into quantifiable thresholds that technology teams can instrument, ensuring alerts are meaningful rather than arbitrary.
requirements.frequency: Annual (aligned to SLA review cycles)

requirements.requirement: Define and operationalise a tiered escalation matrix for service interruption alerts, specifying response time obligations per severity level and naming accountable business owners for each key service, to enable 'timely detection and resolution' as required by the clause.
requirements.rationale: Actionable alerts are only effective when received by personnel with authority and capacity to act; a documented escalation matrix ensures alerts reach the right owners within defined timeframes, reducing mean time to resolution (MTTR) and limiting customer and financial impact.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (with semi-annual review)

requirements.requirement: Incorporate real-time monitoring outcomes and service interruption trends into senior management reporting, providing visibility on capacity utilisation and performance against thresholds on a monthly basis to support informed business decision-making.
requirements.rationale: The periodic update requirement in the clause implies senior management must be aware of monitoring effectiveness; regular reporting creates accountability and enables proactive resource or investment decisions before capacity constraints cause service degradation.
requirements.frequency: Monthly

technology.summary: Technology teams must deploy and maintain an integrated real-time monitoring platform capable of collecting, correlating, and alerting on capacity utilisation and performance metrics across all systems classified as key processes and services. Alerting must be actionable — contextual, routed to the right administrators, and tied to documented thresholds — rather than generating noise. The platform's metric scope and thresholds must support periodic recalibration without service disruption.

requirements.requirement: Deploy a centralised, real-time monitoring and observability platform (e.g., Prometheus/Grafana, Datadog, Dynatrace, or equivalent enterprise tooling) that collects infrastructure, application, and network metrics with polling or streaming intervals of no greater than 60 seconds for all systems within the defined monitoring scope.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly mandates 'real-time monitoring mechanisms'; sub-minute data collection is required to enable genuinely timely detection of service degradation before it escalates to a full interruption, distinguishing real-time compliance from periodic batch monitoring.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (platform reviewed annually)

requirements.requirement: Instrument all key processes and services to expose standardised performance metrics including CPU utilisation, memory consumption, disk I/O, network throughput, transaction latency (p95/p99), error rates, and queue depths, with metric definitions and collection methods documented in a monitoring runbook.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text specifies tracking 'capacity utilisation and performance'; without comprehensive and standardised metric instrumentation across the full service scope, monitoring gaps will exist that could mask emerging capacity constraints or performance degradation.
requirements.owner: Head of Application Development / Head of IT Operations
requirements.frequency: Continuous (instrumentation reviewed upon system change)

requirements.requirement: Configure threshold-based and anomaly-detection alerting rules within the monitoring platform, ensuring each alert is classified by severity, enriched with contextual diagnostic information (affected service, metric value, threshold breached, runbook link), and automatically routed to the responsible administrator team via multiple notification channels (email, SMS, ticketing system integration).
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'actionable alerts to administrators'; alerts without context force administrators to spend time diagnosing before resolving, increasing MTTR; automated routing ensures the right team receives the alert without manual triage delay.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (alert rules reviewed quarterly)

requirements.requirement: Implement alert deduplication, suppression, and escalation logic to prevent alert fatigue; define escalation paths such that unacknowledged Critical alerts auto-escalate to the next tier within 15 minutes, and ensure on-call rosters are maintained and integrated with the alerting platform.
requirements.rationale: Actionable alerts are only effective if administrators respond to them; alert fatigue from excessive noise leads to critical alerts being ignored, undermining the clause's intent of 'timely detection and resolution of service interruptions'.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (escalation paths reviewed semi-annually)
