requirements.requirement: Define and document formal network availability and reliability targets (e.g., uptime SLAs) that are directly tied to the institution's critical business services, including core banking, payment systems, and customer-facing channels.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates a 'reliable' network that supports 'business activities'. Without explicit availability targets, the institution cannot objectively demonstrate compliance or measure failure impact on customers and counterparties.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; targets revised whenever a material business service is onboarded or retired

requirements.requirement: Integrate network capacity and architecture requirements into the institution's strategic business planning cycle, ensuring that approved growth plans (new branches, digital channels, M&A, etc.) trigger a formal network impact assessment before implementation.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires the network to support 'future growth plans'. Without a formal linkage between business strategy and network planning, growth initiatives risk outpacing infrastructure, causing degraded service and compliance gaps.
requirements.frequency: Annual (aligned to strategic planning cycle); ad-hoc for material business changes

requirements.requirement: Establish documented business impact thresholds for network degradation and outage scenarios, clearly defining acceptable recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO) for each business-critical network-dependent service.
requirements.rationale: A 'reliable' enterprise network per clause 10.36 must be underpinned by business-defined tolerance levels. Without defined RTOs and RPOs, technology teams lack the business context to engineer appropriate resilience, and the institution cannot demonstrate proportionate risk management to BNM.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; triggered by material changes to critical business services or risk appetite

requirements.requirement: Maintain a business-owned inventory of all business services and associated network dependencies, updated continuously, to support prioritisation during network incidents and inform future capacity decisions.
requirements.rationale: Supporting 'business activities' as required by the clause demands that the institution knows which services depend on which network segments. This inventory enables impact-aware incident response and rational investment in network resilience.
requirements.frequency: Continuous maintenance; formal validation semi-annually

technology.summary: Technology teams must architect, implement, and maintain an enterprise network that embeds reliability, scalability, and security at every layer — from physical topology to logical segmentation and access controls. The design must be forward-looking, accommodating projected business growth without requiring disruptive re-architecture. Continuous monitoring, capacity management, and security hardening are non-negotiable operational disciplines under this clause.

requirements.requirement: Design and implement a redundant, fault-tolerant network architecture with no single points of failure for critical paths, including redundant core and distribution layer switches, dual ISP uplinks with automatic failover, and geographically diverse data centre interconnects.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates a 'reliable' enterprise network. Redundancy at physical and logical layers is the foundational technical mechanism for achieving reliability, ensuring that no single component failure results in a service outage.
requirements.frequency: Design reviewed annually; failover tests conducted semi-annually; continuous monitoring

requirements.requirement: Implement a scalable network architecture using industry-standard practices such as modular design, software-defined networking (SDN), and dynamic routing protocols (e.g., BGP, OSPF) that allow capacity to be expanded horizontally without service disruption as business volume grows.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires the network to be 'scalable' and to support 'future growth plans'. A static, monolithic network design cannot accommodate growth without costly re-architecture; modular and software-defined approaches enable elastic expansion aligned to business demand.
requirements.frequency: Architecture reviewed annually; capacity headroom assessed quarterly against growth projections

requirements.requirement: Implement defence-in-depth network security controls including network segmentation via VLANs and micro-segmentation, next-generation firewalls at all trust boundaries, intrusion detection and prevention systems (IDS/IPS), and encrypted communications for all sensitive data traversals.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly requires a 'secure' enterprise network. Layered security controls mitigate the risk of lateral movement following a breach, data exfiltration, and external attacks — all of which constitute systemic threats to a financial institution's operational and regulatory standing.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; firewall rule reviews quarterly; penetration testing annually

requirements.requirement: Deploy a comprehensive network performance monitoring and management platform providing real-time visibility into bandwidth utilisation, latency, packet loss, and device health across all network tiers, with automated alerting for threshold breaches and capacity forecasting capabilities.
requirements.rationale: A 'reliable' network per clause 10.36 cannot be managed reactively. Proactive monitoring enables early detection of degradation before business impact occurs, and capacity forecasting data is essential for evidence-based investment decisions aligned to future growth plans.
requirements.frequency: Continuous real-time monitoring; monthly capacity trend reports; quarterly management review

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a formal network capacity plan that models current utilisation baselines, growth scenarios derived from the institution's strategic business plan, and headroom thresholds, triggering procurement and upgrade workflows when utilisation crosses defined thresholds (e.g., 70% sustained utilisation).
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the network to support 'future growth plans', which necessitates a structured capacity management discipline. Without a formal capacity plan tied to business projections, the institution risks reactive, disruptive network upgrades that coincide with peak business demand.
requirements.frequency: Plan updated quarterly; formally reviewed against business strategy annually

governance.summary: Governance frameworks must ensure that enterprise network design decisions are accountable, policy-driven, and subject to independent oversight. Board and senior management must receive adequate visibility into network risk, and formal policies must enforce the reliability, scalability, and security requirements mandated by the clause. Audit and assurance activities must periodically validate that the implemented network continues to meet both regulatory requirements and evolving business needs.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Board-approved Enterprise Network Policy that mandates requirements for reliability, scalability, and security in network design, sets minimum standards for redundancy and capacity headroom, and requires alignment of network architecture with the institution's strategic business plan.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.36 imposes an obligation on the 'financial institution' as a legal entity, not merely its technology teams. A Board-approved policy establishes accountability at the appropriate governance level and creates the normative framework within which all technical implementation decisions are made.
requirements.owner: Board of Directors (endorsed by ITSC/Risk Committee)
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed annually or upon material changes to the business or regulatory environment

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Network Architecture Review Board (or equivalent governance committee) with cross-functional membership (IT, Risk, Business, Compliance) that must approve all material changes to the enterprise network architecture, new network segments, and significant capacity expansions before implementation.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to 'design and implement' a network meeting defined criteria. A governance committee provides the structured oversight to ensure design decisions are evaluated against reliability, scalability, and security criteria — not solely on cost — and that business growth requirements are factored in before changes are made.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) — Chair; CISO, CRO, and COO as standing members
requirements.frequency: Convened as required for material changes; standing agenda item at monthly IT Steering Committee

requirements.requirement: Mandate periodic independent audits and technical assessments of the enterprise network — including vulnerability assessments, configuration compliance reviews, and resilience testing — with findings reported to the IT Steering Committee and material gaps escalated to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: Independent assurance is necessary to verify that the 'reliable, scalable and secure' network required by clause 10.36 is not merely documented but is operationally effective. Self-assessment by technology teams creates a conflict of interest; independent review provides objective evidence for BNM examination purposes.
requirements.owner: Chief Audit Executive (Internal Audit) / Head of IT Risk
requirements.frequency: Full independent audit annually; targeted vulnerability assessments semi-annually; results reported quarterly to Board Risk Committee

requirements.requirement: Implement a formal exception management process requiring documented risk acceptance, compensating controls, and time-bound remediation plans for any deviations from the Enterprise Network Policy or approved architecture standards, with all exceptions approved at an appropriate seniority level and tracked to closure.
requirements.rationale: Policy compliance gaps are inevitable in a dynamic environment; what matters for BNM compliance is that deviations are identified, assessed, and managed in a controlled manner. An exception process ensures that the institution can demonstrate it is managing network risk proactively rather than inadvertently operating outside policy.
requirements.frequency: Exceptions reviewed monthly by IT Risk; aggregate exception status reported quarterly to Board Risk Committee

requirements.requirement: Require that the enterprise network architecture and capacity plan are formally reviewed and signed off by senior management as part of the institution's annual technology risk assessment, with explicit attestation that the network remains capable of supporting current business activities and approved future growth plans.
requirements.rationale: The clause's requirement to support 'future growth plans' creates a continuous, forward-looking obligation. An annual attestation by senior management creates an accountability mechanism that ties network governance to the institution's broader strategic planning cycle, and generates documentary evidence for BNM supervisory review.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) and Chief Technology Officer (CTO) — joint attestation
requirements.frequency: Annual, aligned to the institution's technology risk assessment and strategic planning cycle

business.summary: Failure to ensure reliable, non-single-point-of-failure network services for critical systems exposes the institution to prolonged outages, regulatory sanctions, and reputational damage. Network faults or cyber threats exploiting infrastructure weaknesses can disrupt customer-facing services, payment systems, and core banking operations, triggering BNM reporting obligations and potential licence conditions. This clause mandates that business continuity planning explicitly accounts for network resilience as a foundational dependency of all critical system operations.

requirements.requirement: Formally identify and maintain an up-to-date register of all critical systems and their network service dependencies, distinguishing between primary and secondary network paths, to ensure protection scope is explicitly defined.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text scopes the obligation specifically to 'critical systems' — without a formal, approved register, the institution cannot demonstrate that network reliability controls are applied to the correct systems. Unregistered critical systems represent unmanaged exposure.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; immediate update upon any change to critical system classification

requirements.requirement: Define and formally approve minimum network service availability targets (e.g., 99.99% uptime) for each critical system, and embed these targets into contracts with internal IT and external network service providers.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires that network services 'are reliable' — reliability must be operationally defined and measurable. Without contractual SLAs, the institution has no enforceable standard against which to assess compliance or hold providers accountable during network fault events.
requirements.frequency: Annual SLA review; triggered review upon material service degradation event

requirements.requirement: Incorporate network fault and network-based cyber threat scenarios explicitly into the institution's Business Continuity Plan (BCP) and Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP), with defined Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs) for each critical system.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly references protection against 'potential network faults and cyber threats' — BCPs that do not model network-layer disruptions leave the institution unable to respond effectively. Regulators will examine whether continuity planning addresses identified threat vectors.
requirements.frequency: Annual BCP/DRP review; tested at least annually via simulation exercises

requirements.requirement: Establish a management reporting framework that tracks network resilience KPIs (e.g., failover activation rate, mean time to recover from network faults, SPOF remediation status) and presents these to senior management on a quarterly basis.
requirements.rationale: Demonstrating ongoing reliability requires evidence of measurement and management attention. BNM supervisory examinations will seek evidence that senior leadership monitors network resilience posture, not merely that technical controls exist at a point in time.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting; ad hoc escalation upon breach of defined thresholds

technology.summary: Technology teams must architect and operate network infrastructure for all critical systems with full redundancy across every layer — physical links, routing, devices, and service providers — eliminating any single point of failure. Active threat detection and network segmentation must complement redundancy controls to address the cyber threat dimension of the clause. All resilience controls must be verified through regular testing under realistic fault conditions, not assumed to be functional.

requirements.requirement: Implement fully redundant network paths for all critical systems, including dual ISP connectivity via diverse physical routes, redundant core and distribution layer switches, and redundant WAN links — ensuring no single device, cable, carrier, or exchange point represents a single point of failure.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly mandates 'no single point of failure' in network services for critical systems. Redundancy at each network layer — physical, data link, and routing — is the primary technical means of satisfying this obligation. A single carrier or device failure must not be able to isolate a critical system.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (by design); architecture review annually and upon material change

requirements.requirement: Deploy automated failover mechanisms (e.g., BGP multi-homing, HSRP/VRRP for gateway redundancy, SD-WAN with active-active or active-passive path management) that detect network faults and reroute traffic to redundant paths within defined RTOs, without requiring manual intervention.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires protection against 'potential network faults' — passive redundancy alone is insufficient if failover requires human activation, which introduces delay and human error. Automated failover directly addresses the reliability objective by minimising the window of service disruption caused by network faults.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; failover mechanism testing quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement network segmentation and micro-segmentation for critical systems, enforced by next-generation firewalls (NGFWs) and access control lists (ACLs), to contain the lateral movement of cyber threats and limit blast radius from network-layer attacks.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires protection against 'cyber threats' in addition to network faults. Segmentation ensures that a compromised or attacked network segment cannot propagate to affect other critical systems, directly addressing the cyber threat protection mandate.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; segmentation policy review semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Deploy network-layer threat detection and prevention controls — including Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS), DDoS mitigation (on-premise and upstream scrubbing), and real-time network traffic monitoring via SIEM integration — covering all ingress and egress points of critical systems.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires protection against 'cyber threats' targeting network services. Threat detection at the network layer enables the institution to identify and respond to attacks (e.g., volumetric DDoS, network-level exploitation) before they impact critical system availability, directly supporting the reliability objective.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; threat detection rule tuning quarterly

requirements.requirement: Conduct regular network resilience testing, including scheduled failover drills, controlled single-point-of-failure injection tests (chaos engineering), and penetration testing of network defences — with results documented and remediation tracked to closure.
requirements.rationale: Declared redundancy and reliability cannot be assumed without verification. Testing confirms that failover mechanisms, redundant paths, and threat controls function as designed under realistic fault and attack conditions, providing evidence to satisfy BNM that the reliability obligation is not merely architectural but operationally validated.
requirements.frequency: Failover drills: quarterly; penetration testing: annually; SPOF injection: semi-annually

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that network resilience obligations are owned at the appropriate seniority level, embedded in policy, and subject to independent oversight and audit. The Board and senior management must receive evidence-based assurance that network services for critical systems remain reliable and free from single points of failure on an ongoing basis. Third-party and vendor network services must be governed under contractual and oversight frameworks that extend the institution's resilience requirements to all external parties providing network services to critical systems.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Board-approved Network Resilience Policy that mandates no single point of failure for critical system network services, defines critical system classification criteria, sets minimum reliability standards, and assigns accountable owners for compliance with Clause 10.37.
requirements.rationale: The 'must ensure' language in the verbatim text places a regulatory obligation on the institution as a whole, requiring Board-level ownership. Without a formal, Board-approved policy, resilience controls may be inconsistently applied and lack the authority needed for organisation-wide enforcement.
requirements.frequency: Annual policy review and re-approval; triggered review upon material incident or regulatory guidance update

requirements.requirement: Establish a Technology Risk Committee (or equivalent senior management forum) with a defined mandate to review and approve the institution's critical system register, network resilience posture, SPOF remediation plans, and major network architecture changes affecting critical systems.
requirements.rationale: The obligation to protect critical systems against network faults and cyber threats requires ongoing management oversight, not a one-time design exercise. A formal governance committee ensures accountability, cross-functional visibility, and timely escalation of resilience gaps to decision-makers.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Technology Officer (CTO)
requirements.frequency: Quarterly committee meetings; extraordinary sessions upon material network incidents

requirements.requirement: Implement a third-party network service provider governance framework requiring vendors providing network connectivity or services to critical systems to contractually commit to resilience SLAs, evidence their own redundancy controls, and submit to periodic due diligence assessments by the institution.
requirements.rationale: The clause's reliability obligation applies to network services regardless of whether they are delivered internally or by third parties. Without extending governance to external providers, the institution may satisfy the letter of internal controls while remaining exposed to SPOF risks in outsourced network services.
requirements.owner: Chief Procurement Officer / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Annual third-party due diligence; contract review at each renewal; triggered review upon service degradation

requirements.requirement: Conduct annual independent internal audits of network resilience controls, explicitly assessing SPOF elimination, failover effectiveness, and cyber threat protection for critical systems, with findings reported directly to the Board Audit Committee and remediation tracked to closure.
requirements.rationale: BNM's 'must ensure' standard requires the institution to provide ongoing assurance of compliance — not merely to implement controls once. Independent audit provides the verification mechanism that governance bodies and regulators require to confirm that network reliability obligations are being met in practice.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor (Head of Internal Audit)
requirements.frequency: Annual; supplementary reviews triggered by major network incidents or significant architecture changes

requirements.requirement: Define and operationalise a Network Incident Escalation and Regulatory Notification procedure that specifies escalation thresholds, internal notification timelines, and triggers for BNM reporting obligations arising from network fault or cyber threat events affecting critical systems.
requirements.rationale: Protection against network faults and cyber threats must include a response governance layer — when incidents occur, the institution must be able to demonstrate structured escalation and, where required, timely regulatory notification. Absence of a defined procedure creates regulatory exposure independent of the underlying technical controls.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Compliance Officer (CCO)
requirements.frequency: Annual procedure review; immediate activation upon qualifying network incident

business.summary: Failure to implement network fault prevention exposes the institution to prolonged service outages that directly impair customer-facing operations, payment processing, and regulatory reporting. BNM expects financial institutions to proactively eliminate single points of failure in network infrastructure as a baseline operational resilience posture. Non-compliance risks supervisory action, reputational damage, and potential breach of service availability commitments to customers.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formal Network Resilience Policy that mandates component redundancy, service diversity, and alternate network paths as minimum standards for all production network infrastructure supporting critical business services.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text uses 'expected to include' as a prescriptive baseline, meaning BNM treats these three measures as non-negotiable. A policy document anchors accountability and ensures business ownership of resilience outcomes beyond IT teams.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; immediate update upon material infrastructure change

requirements.requirement: Define and document Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs) for all critical business services, explicitly mapped to the network fault prevention measures in paragraph 10.38 to ensure resilience investments are aligned to business continuity tolerances.
requirements.rationale: Component redundancy and alternate network paths are only meaningful if sized against business-defined tolerance for downtime. Without mapped RTOs/RPOs, technology investments may be misaligned with actual business risk appetite.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; reassessment upon new product launch or material operational change

requirements.requirement: Conduct annual Business Impact Analysis (BIA) exercises that specifically assess the financial, operational, and reputational consequences of network fault scenarios, including single-carrier failure, component failure, and path outage, to validate ongoing investment in resilience measures.
requirements.rationale: The clause references paragraph 10.37 (network fault risks), implying an upstream risk identification process must feed into the controls in 10.38. The BIA provides the business-level evidence that the three prescribed measures are correctly scoped.
requirements.frequency: Annual; ad-hoc following a significant network incident

requirements.requirement: Ensure vendor and third-party contracts for network services explicitly require demonstrated service diversity and alternate path capabilities, with contractual SLAs aligned to the institution's network resilience requirements under RMiT 10.38.
requirements.rationale: Service diversity as mandated in the verbatim text cannot be achieved if all network services are procured from a single vendor on a single physical path. Contractual obligations must mirror regulatory obligations to create enforceability.
requirements.owner: Chief Procurement Officer / Head of Vendor Management
requirements.frequency: At contract initiation and renewal; annual compliance review

technology.summary: Technology teams must engineer redundancy, diversity, and alternate routing into every layer of the production network stack supporting critical financial services. The three specific measures — component redundancy, service diversity, and alternate network paths — each address a distinct failure mode and must be implemented together to satisfy BNM's expectation. Implementation must be validated through regular testing, not merely documented in design artefacts.

requirements.requirement: Implement component redundancy across all critical network infrastructure, including routers, switches, firewalls, load balancers, and network interface cards, using N+1 or active-active configurations. No single network component should constitute a single point of failure for any Critical System or Critical Infrastructure as defined under RMiT.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly lists 'component redundancy' as a required control measure. Active-active or active-passive redundant hardware eliminates the most common cause of network faults — individual device failure — and ensures continuity without manual intervention.
requirements.owner: Head of Network Engineering / Head of Infrastructure
requirements.frequency: Continuous (by design); quarterly audit of redundancy configurations

requirements.requirement: Implement service diversity by procuring connectivity services from a minimum of two independent telecommunications carriers or Internet Service Providers (ISPs), ensuring that the underlying physical infrastructure (cable ducts, exchanges, and Points of Presence) is non-overlapping between providers.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates 'service diversity' as distinct from component redundancy. Dual-carrier connectivity protects against carrier-level outages, which cannot be mitigated by hardware redundancy alone. Physical path independence must be verified, not assumed.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (by design); annual verification of physical path independence with carriers

requirements.requirement: Implement alternate network paths through dynamic routing protocols (e.g., BGP, OSPF) that automatically detect path failures and reroute traffic without manual intervention. Alternate paths must be pre-provisioned, regularly tested, and capable of sustaining production-level traffic loads independently.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text specifies 'alternate network paths' as the third required control. Static or manual failover is insufficient given BNM's expectation for proactive fault prevention. Dynamic routing ensures failover is automatic and within acceptable RTO thresholds.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (operational); failover testing quarterly

requirements.requirement: Establish a network monitoring and alerting capability with real-time visibility into the health of all redundant components, diverse service links, and alternate paths. Alerts must be correlated and escalated automatically to the Network Operations Centre (NOC) with defined response runbooks for each fault scenario.
requirements.rationale: Component redundancy, service diversity, and alternate paths are only effective if faults are detected promptly and failover is confirmed. Monitoring closes the loop between the preventive controls mandated in 10.38 and the incident response processes referenced in paragraph 10.37.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Operations / Head of NOC
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; monthly review of alert thresholds and runbook currency

requirements.requirement: Conduct periodic network resilience tests, including simulated component failures, carrier failover exercises, and alternate path switchover drills, to validate that all three control measures function as designed under realistic load conditions. Test results must be documented and deficiencies remediated within a tracked remediation plan.
requirements.rationale: BNM's use of 'expected to include' implies ongoing operational effectiveness, not merely initial implementation. Untested redundancy configurations frequently fail silently due to configuration drift, software bugs, or capacity changes. Regular testing provides assurance that controls remain effective.
requirements.owner: Head of Infrastructure / Head of IT Operations
requirements.frequency: Quarterly failover tests; full end-to-end resilience drill annually

governance.summary: Governance frameworks must provide structured oversight of network resilience controls to ensure they remain effective, current, and aligned with the institution's risk profile. The three prescribed measures — component redundancy, service diversity, and alternate network paths — require defined ownership, periodic assurance, and Board-level visibility given their direct link to operational continuity. Governance must also bridge technology implementation and regulatory reporting obligations.

requirements.requirement: Assign formal ownership of the Network Resilience framework to a named senior technology executive, with documented accountability for ensuring the three control measures in paragraph 10.38 (component redundancy, service diversity, alternate network paths) are implemented, tested, and reported. This accountability must be reflected in the individual's performance objectives.
requirements.rationale: Regulatory expectations under RMiT are directed at the institution as a whole, but require individual accountability for implementation effectiveness. Without named ownership, resilience gaps may persist unresolved as cross-team responsibilities are diffused.
requirements.frequency: Ownership assignment: continuous; accountability review: annual

requirements.requirement: Establish a Network Resilience register that inventories all critical network segments, documents the component redundancy configuration, service diversity arrangements, and alternate path topology for each segment, and records the last validated test date and outcome. The register must be maintained current and available for BNM examination.
requirements.rationale: BNM examiners expect institutions to demonstrate — not merely assert — compliance with paragraph 10.38. A maintained register provides audit-ready evidence that all three mandated control measures are in place across the full scope of production infrastructure.
requirements.owner: Head of Network Engineering / Head of IT Risk
requirements.frequency: Continuous maintenance; formal review quarterly

requirements.requirement: Include network resilience control effectiveness — covering component redundancy status, service diversity coverage, and alternate path test outcomes — in periodic IT Risk reporting to the Board Risk Committee (BRC) or equivalent senior governance body, with escalation thresholds defined for material gaps.
requirements.rationale: The marker 'G' (Guidance) indicates BNM expects proportionate board-level oversight of network resilience. Boards cannot fulfil their technology risk governance obligations without visibility into whether the specific controls in paragraph 10.38 are functioning effectively.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting to BRC; immediate escalation for critical resilience gaps

requirements.requirement: Integrate network fault prevention control requirements (component redundancy, service diversity, alternate paths) into the institution's third-party and vendor risk management framework, requiring annual assurance from network service providers that their service diversity and path independence commitments are met and independently verified.
requirements.rationale: Service diversity — one of the three mandated controls — inherently depends on external providers. Governance must extend beyond internal controls to vendor assurance, particularly where physical path independence cannot be directly verified by the institution's own staff.
requirements.owner: Head of Vendor Risk / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Annual vendor assurance review; triggered review upon carrier incident or contract change

requirements.requirement: Embed network resilience verification (covering all three control measures in paragraph 10.38) into the institution's Internal Audit plan on a risk-based cycle, with findings reported independently to the Audit Committee and remediation timelines tracked to closure.
requirements.rationale: Independent assurance by Internal Audit validates that management's self-assessment of network resilience control effectiveness is accurate. This is particularly important for alternate path configurations and service diversity, where configuration drift can silently erode control effectiveness between periodic tests.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor / Head of IT Audit
requirements.frequency: Audit cycle: at minimum every two years for critical systems; annual for highest-risk segments

business.summary: Failure to monitor network bandwidth in real time exposes a financial institution to undetected congestion, service degradation, and unplanned outages that directly impair customer-facing channels and critical transaction processing. BNM expects institutions to treat bandwidth resilience as a measurable, managed operational risk — not merely an IT hygiene issue. Institutions without documented metrics and monitoring processes face supervisory findings and potential enforcement action under RMiT.

requirements.requirement: Define and formally approve a Network Bandwidth Monitoring Policy that mandates real-time monitoring of all production network segments, establishes bandwidth utilisation thresholds, and identifies accountable owners for flagged over-utilisation events.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires 'established processes' — this implies formal, documented, and approved procedures rather than ad hoc monitoring. Without a policy, threshold definitions and accountability remain ambiguous, increasing operational risk exposure.
requirements.frequency: Policy review: Annual; Threshold review: Semi-annual

requirements.requirement: Define and publish Network Service Resilience Metrics (NSRMs) that quantify acceptable bandwidth utilisation levels, maximum tolerable congestion durations, and recovery time objectives (RTOs) for bandwidth-related disruptions, aligned with the institution's Business Continuity Plan.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires 'corresponding network service resilience metrics' — these must be defined at a business level to set operational targets and to measure compliance. NSRMs directly link network performance to service availability commitments made to customers and BNM.
requirements.frequency: Defined at policy approval; reviewed Quarterly against actual performance

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal escalation and incident response procedure specifically for bandwidth over-utilisation and network fault events, with defined escalation paths, response time SLAs, and communication obligations to senior management and, where applicable, BNM.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires processes to 'flag any over utilisation of bandwidth and system disruptions due to bandwidth congestion and network faults.' Flagging without a structured response procedure renders detection operationally ineffective and demonstrates inadequate risk management to examiners.
requirements.frequency: Procedure review: Annual; Invocation: Per-event

requirements.requirement: Include network bandwidth resilience performance — including utilisation trends, anomaly counts, and disruption incidents — in regular Management Information (MI) reports presented to the Technology Risk Committee and Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: Senior management oversight of network resilience metrics is necessary to demonstrate that 'established processes' are actively governed and not merely technical artefacts. Trend data reported upward provides the basis for capital expenditure decisions on network capacity.
requirements.frequency: Monthly MI reports; Quarterly Board-level review

technology.summary: Technology teams must deploy continuous, real-time network monitoring tooling capable of measuring bandwidth utilisation across all critical segments, generating automated alerts on threshold breaches, and performing deep traffic analysis to surface trends and anomalies. The solution must produce quantitative resilience metrics that feed both operational dashboards and governance reporting. Coverage must extend to all network layers relevant to financial services delivery, including WAN links, data centre interconnects, internet egress, and cloud connectivity.

requirements.requirement: Deploy real-time network bandwidth monitoring tooling (e.g., NetFlow/IPFIX collectors, SNMP polling, packet brokers, or equivalent) across all production network segments — including WAN, LAN, internet egress, inter-data-centre links, and cloud connectivity — with polling or streaming intervals not exceeding 60 seconds for critical links.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates 'real-time network bandwidth monitoring processes.' Near-real-time telemetry is the technical foundation for all subsequent requirements. Polling intervals exceeding 60 seconds risk missing short-duration congestion bursts that cause transaction timeouts.
requirements.owner: Head of Network Engineering / Head of IT Infrastructure
requirements.frequency: Continuous (24x7x365); quarterly coverage audit

requirements.requirement: Implement automated, threshold-based alerting that triggers notifications when bandwidth utilisation exceeds defined warning (e.g., 70%) and critical (e.g., 85%) thresholds on monitored links, with alerts routed to the Network Operations Centre (NOC) and integrated with the institution's IT Service Management (ITSM) platform for automatic incident ticket creation.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires processes to 'flag any over utilisation of bandwidth.' Automated alerting is the technical mechanism for flagging — manual log review is insufficient to satisfy 'real-time' flagging. ITSM integration ensures traceability and accountability.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Operations / NOC Manager
requirements.frequency: Continuous alerting; threshold calibration: Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement traffic analysis capabilities — including application-layer Deep Packet Inspection (DPI), flow-based analytics, and baseline deviation detection — to identify traffic trends, anomalous consumption patterns, and early indicators of bandwidth congestion or network faults before they cause service disruption.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires 'traffic analysis to detect trends and anomalies.' Raw bandwidth utilisation metrics alone are insufficient; application-level and flow-level visibility is necessary to distinguish legitimate growth trends from anomalous consumption (e.g., caused by misconfigurations, loops, or exfiltration).
requirements.owner: Head of Network Engineering / CISO (for security-relevant anomalies)
requirements.frequency: Continuous analysis; anomaly model recalibration: Monthly

requirements.requirement: Instrument and publish quantitative Network Service Resilience Metrics (NSRMs) from the monitoring platform, including: peak and average utilisation per link, number and duration of threshold breach events, mean time to detect (MTTD) and resolve (MTTR) bandwidth-related incidents, and packet loss and latency percentiles during congestion events.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'corresponding network service resilience metrics.' These must be machine-generated, auditable, and traceable to actual network telemetry — not manually compiled estimates. They form the evidentiary basis for BNM examination and internal governance reviews.
requirements.frequency: Metrics generated continuously; consolidated reports: Monthly

requirements.requirement: Maintain a minimum of 12 months of network bandwidth telemetry and traffic analysis data in a tamper-evident, searchable repository, accessible to both operations teams for troubleshooting and audit/compliance teams for regulatory review.
requirements.rationale: Trend detection — explicitly required by the clause — demands sufficient historical data for baseline establishment and longitudinal analysis. Regulators and internal auditors require access to historical telemetry to verify that monitoring was continuous and that incidents were detected promptly.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Operations / Head of IT Risk
requirements.frequency: Continuous retention; retention policy review: Annual

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that real-time bandwidth monitoring is formally owned, periodically reviewed, and independently validated rather than left as an unmanaged operational activity. The institution must embed network resilience metrics into its Technology Risk Management Framework, assign clear accountability for metric ownership and threshold governance, and subject monitoring processes to regular internal audit. Governance bodies must also oversee third-party network service providers to ensure equivalent monitoring standards apply across the end-to-end network estate.

requirements.requirement: Embed network bandwidth monitoring and resilience metrics within the institution's Technology Risk Management Framework (TRMF) as a named operational risk control, with documented control objectives, control owners, key risk indicators (KRIs), and key performance indicators (KPIs) mapped to Clause 10.39.
requirements.rationale: RMiT requires demonstrated risk management processes, not just technical tools. Formal embedding in the TRMF ensures the control is subject to ongoing risk assessment, ownership accountability, and change management governance — making it examinable by BNM.
requirements.frequency: TRMF update: Upon adoption; KRI/KPI review: Quarterly

requirements.requirement: The Technology Risk Committee (or equivalent governance body) must formally review network service resilience metrics — including utilisation trends, anomaly statistics, and disruption incident summaries — on a quarterly basis, and escalate material findings to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'established processes' to flag disruptions, implying management must actively respond to flagged events. Formal committee review creates a governance record demonstrating that flagged events were deliberated and acted upon by senior management.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly committee review; ad hoc escalation for material incidents

requirements.requirement: Internal Audit must include network bandwidth monitoring controls — covering tooling coverage, alerting effectiveness, traffic analysis capability, and metric accuracy — within the annual IT audit plan, with findings reported to the Audit Committee of the Board.
requirements.rationale: Independent assurance is required to verify that 'established processes' are operating as designed and are not circumvented in practice. Annual audit coverage ensures BNM can rely on the institution's self-attestation of compliance.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor / Head of IT Audit
requirements.frequency: Annual audit; interim reviews if material incidents occur

requirements.requirement: Where network services are provided by third-party vendors (e.g., ISPs, MPLS providers, managed WAN providers), vendor contracts and SLAs must require equivalent real-time bandwidth monitoring, resilience metric reporting, and anomaly notification obligations, and must be subject to annual third-party risk assessments.
requirements.rationale: The clause applies to the institution's overall network resilience posture, which necessarily extends to outsourced network services. BNM's outsourcing requirements under RMiT require that third-party risks are managed and monitored — bandwidth monitoring is no exception.
requirements.owner: Chief Procurement Officer / Head of Vendor Risk Management
requirements.frequency: Contract review: At renewal or annually; risk assessment: Annual

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Network Capacity Management Plan, reviewed semi-annually, that uses traffic trend analysis outputs to project bandwidth demand over a rolling 24-month horizon and drives proactive network upgrade or re-provisioning decisions before utilisation thresholds are breached.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires trend analysis — trend data is only operationally valuable if it informs forward-looking capacity decisions. Without a formal Capacity Management Plan, trend analysis becomes a reporting exercise rather than a control that prevents disruptions, undermining the purpose of the clause.
requirements.frequency: Plan review: Semi-annual; demand forecast update: Quarterly

business.summary: Failure to secure network services supporting critical systems exposes the institution to data breaches, service outages, and regulatory sanctions. The CIA triad—confidentiality, integrity, and availability—directly maps to business risks including reputational damage, financial loss from fraud or disruption, and potential loss of operating licence. BNM's 'S' marker signals that this is a baseline standard for all financial institutions, meaning non-compliance represents an unacceptable floor-level risk.

requirements.requirement: Define and maintain an up-to-date register of all critical systems and their associated network service dependencies, including internal and third-party-hosted components.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text applies obligations specifically to 'network services supporting critical systems.' Without a clear, agreed definition and inventory of critical systems, the institution cannot determine where the obligation applies, creating compliance gaps and unmanaged exposure.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; updated upon any change to system criticality classification or onboarding of new systems

requirements.requirement: Establish and enforce minimum network security requirements as a contractual obligation in all third-party agreements where vendors provide or traverse network services for critical systems.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires network services to be 'designed and implemented' to protect CIA; this obligation does not lapse when delivery is outsourced. Vendor non-compliance directly transfers risk to the institution under BNM's outsourcing accountability principle.
requirements.frequency: At contract inception and at each annual contract review

requirements.requirement: Define quantitative availability targets (e.g., RTO, RPO, uptime SLAs) for network services underpinning each critical system, aligned to the institution's Business Impact Analysis (BIA).
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires 'availability of data.' Business units must translate this into measurable service expectations so that technology teams can engineer to the right availability tier and so that breaches of availability are detectable and reportable.
requirements.frequency: Defined at BIA cycle (minimum annual); reviewed after each significant incident

requirements.requirement: Incorporate network security performance against CIA objectives for critical systems into the institution's quarterly management reporting and board risk dashboard.
requirements.rationale: Senior accountability for network security outcomes ensures that resource allocation, risk acceptance decisions, and remediation prioritisation occur at the appropriate level. BNM expects demonstrable senior oversight of technology risk.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Ensure that any planned or emergency change to network architecture supporting critical systems undergoes a formal business risk assessment, including impact to confidentiality, integrity, and availability, before implementation.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires that network services be 'designed and implemented' to maintain CIA—design-time controls must not be inadvertently removed through poorly governed change. Business sponsorship of risk assessments for network changes anchors accountability at the operational level.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (each change request); assessed before implementation

technology.summary: Technology teams must architect, deploy, and continuously operate network controls that enforce all three pillars of the CIA triad for every network path touching a critical system. This encompasses network segmentation, encryption, access control, redundancy, and continuous monitoring—applied systematically rather than ad hoc. The 'designed and implemented' language in the clause signals that security must be built into architecture from inception, not bolted on after deployment.

requirements.requirement: Implement network segmentation (e.g., VLANs, micro-segmentation, DMZ architecture) to logically and physically isolate network segments hosting critical systems from general corporate and external networks, enforcing least-privilege inter-segment communication via stateful firewalls and ACLs.
requirements.rationale: Segmentation directly protects confidentiality by limiting lateral movement and blast radius of a breach. The clause requires that network services be 'designed' with CIA in mind—segmentation is a foundational design-time control without which the remaining controls are weakened.
requirements.frequency: Implemented at design; reviewed annually and after each material architecture change

requirements.requirement: Enforce end-to-end encryption (minimum TLS 1.2, preferably TLS 1.3) for all data in transit across network services supporting critical systems, including east-west (internal) traffic, and mandate certificate lifecycle management with automated renewal.
requirements.rationale: Encryption of data in transit directly satisfies the confidentiality and integrity requirements of the clause. Unencrypted internal traffic is a common vector for credential theft and data exfiltration; BNM's requirement to 'ensure confidentiality and integrity' cannot be met without cryptographic controls on the wire.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Implemented at design; cipher suite and certificate standards reviewed annually against current cryptographic guidance

requirements.requirement: Deploy network-level Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS) at ingress, egress, and key internal chokepoints for critical system segments, with signatures updated at least weekly and alerts integrated into a centralised SIEM for real-time triage.
requirements.rationale: IDS/IPS protects integrity and confidentiality by detecting and blocking malicious traffic—including exploitation attempts, data exfiltration, and command-and-control communications—before they compromise critical system data. Continuous monitoring is the operational manifestation of the clause's 'implemented' requirement.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; signature updates weekly minimum; tuning review quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement network redundancy for all critical-system network paths, including dual ISP links, redundant core and distribution layer switching (e.g., HSRP/VRRP), and automatic failover mechanisms, to meet the availability SLAs defined in the BIA.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires 'availability of data.' Single points of failure in network paths are a direct threat to this obligation. Redundant, auto-failing network design ensures that hardware or link failure does not constitute a data availability breach for critical systems.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Head of Network Engineering
requirements.frequency: Implemented at design; failover tested semi-annually via planned failover exercises

requirements.requirement: Enforce Network Access Control (NAC) on all ports and interfaces within critical system network segments, requiring device authentication (802.1X or equivalent) and restricting access to authorised, compliant endpoints only—automatically quarantining non-compliant devices.
requirements.rationale: NAC controls which devices may connect to critical network segments, directly enforcing confidentiality and integrity by preventing rogue, unmanaged, or compromised endpoints from accessing critical system data. This satisfies the 'designed and implemented' requirement at the network access layer.
requirements.frequency: Continuously enforced; policy reviewed quarterly; device compliance checks automated on connection

requirements.requirement: Conduct vulnerability assessments and penetration tests targeting network services supporting critical systems at least annually, with findings tracked to remediation in a risk-rated remediation register. Critical and high findings must be remediated within 30 and 90 days respectively.
requirements.rationale: Design-time controls erode over time as threats evolve and configurations drift. Periodic adversarial testing validates that the 'designed and implemented' controls continue to protect CIA in practice. Without testing, the institution cannot demonstrate ongoing compliance to BNM examiners.
requirements.frequency: Annual (minimum); after major architecture changes; findings tracked continuously to closure

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that the technical and business requirements of clause 10.40 are policy-mandated, independently verified, and subject to board-level accountability. This requires a formal network security policy scoped to critical systems, a structured assurance programme, and clear escalation paths when CIA controls fail or degrade. As an 'S' marker clause, BNM expects institutions to demonstrate systematic governance, not merely point-in-time technical compliance.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Board-approved Network Security Policy that explicitly addresses the design, implementation, and ongoing protection of CIA for network services supporting critical systems, with a mandatory review cycle and version-controlled change history.
requirements.rationale: The clause places an institutional obligation ('a financial institution must ensure') requiring senior endorsement. A Board-approved policy elevates network security from a technical preference to a governance commitment, creates an auditable accountability trail, and ensures that resource allocation decisions are aligned to regulatory obligations.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee (BRC) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Policy approved by Board annually; reviewed upon material change to threat landscape, technology architecture, or regulatory guidance

requirements.requirement: Establish an independent assurance function (internal audit or equivalent) that annually reviews the design and operational effectiveness of network security controls for critical systems, benchmarked against the CIA requirements of clause 10.40, with findings reported to the Board Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: Independent assurance is necessary to give the Board credible evidence that the 'designed and implemented' controls are functioning as intended. Management self-attestation alone is insufficient under BNM's governance expectations; the Board Audit Committee requires independent validation to discharge its oversight duty.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Board Audit Committee (BAC)
requirements.frequency: Annual audit cycle; findings tracked quarterly until remediation closure

requirements.requirement: Define and operationalise a Network Security Incident Response Procedure specific to critical systems, specifying escalation triggers (e.g., confirmed CIA breach), notification timelines to BNM per RMIT requirements, internal escalation chains, and post-incident review obligations.
requirements.rationale: The clause's CIA mandate is not satisfied by preventive controls alone—the institution must be able to detect, contain, and recover from CIA failures. A documented, tested response procedure ensures that when controls fail, the institution responds in a manner consistent with its regulatory obligations and minimises harm.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Procedure reviewed annually; tabletop or live drill conducted semi-annually; invoked per-event

requirements.requirement: Implement a formal Change Advisory Board (CAB) process that mandates CIA impact assessment as a mandatory gate for all changes to network services supporting critical systems, with no change approved without a completed assessment signed off by the CISO.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires that network services be 'designed and implemented' to protect CIA—this obligation must persist through the change lifecycle, not just at initial deployment. Governance over change prevents configuration drift and inadvertent weakening of controls by ensuring every modification is assessed against the CIA standard.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Officer (CIO) / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Per-event (each change request); CAB meets at minimum weekly for scheduled changes

requirements.requirement: Maintain a Key Risk Indicator (KRI) framework for network security of critical systems, tracking metrics such as unpatched critical network vulnerabilities, IDS/IPS alert-to-resolution time, encryption compliance rate, and network availability against SLA—with defined thresholds triggering escalation to senior management.
requirements.rationale: Governance requires measurable evidence that CIA controls are effective on a continuous basis, not only at audit time. KRIs convert the clause's qualitative obligation into quantitative signals that allow management and the Board to detect deteriorating compliance before it results in a material incident or regulatory finding.
requirements.frequency: KRIs monitored continuously; reported to senior management monthly; Board Risk Committee quarterly

business.summary: Financial institutions that lack a comprehensive network design blueprint cannot reliably manage operational continuity, vendor relationships, or regulatory obligations tied to network infrastructure. Without visibility into all internal and external interfaces, the institution is exposed to undetected failure points, third-party connectivity risks, and an inability to demonstrate control to BNM examiners. This clause mandates that the blueprint be a living artefact — its absence or staleness constitutes a direct supervisory finding.

requirements.requirement: Formally designate an accountable senior owner responsible for commissioning, maintaining, and certifying the accuracy of the network design blueprint on a continuous basis, covering all internal and external network interfaces.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text uses 'establish and maintain', imposing a perpetual obligation rather than a one-off exercise. Without a named owner, accountability gaps emerge and the blueprint becomes stale, exposing the institution to regulatory censure and unmanaged connectivity risk.
requirements.frequency: Continuous — ownership reviewed Annually

requirements.requirement: Ensure the network design blueprint explicitly maps external network connectivity to all third-party service providers, correspondents, payment networks, and cloud service providers, supporting vendor risk and outsourcing governance obligations.
requirements.rationale: The clause specifically requires identification of 'external network interfaces and connectivity'. External connections represent the institution's largest attack surface and a primary channel for third-party-induced failures; business lines must understand these dependencies for continuity planning and third-party risk management.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (on-boarding/off-boarding of third parties) and Quarterly review

requirements.requirement: Incorporate the network design blueprint as a mandatory reference artefact in Business Continuity Planning (BCP) and Disaster Recovery (DR) exercises, ensuring business units understand network dependencies critical to service restoration.
requirements.rationale: Network segmentation and connectivity topology directly determines recovery sequencing during incidents. If business units are unaware of physical and logical connectivity paths, recovery time objectives (RTOs) will be unachievable in practice, undermining BNM expectations on operational resilience.
requirements.frequency: Annual (aligned to BCP/DR exercise cycle)

requirements.requirement: Allocate dedicated budget and resources for tooling, personnel, and processes required to continuously discover, document, and validate network interfaces, preventing the blueprint from becoming an inaccurate point-in-time snapshot.
requirements.rationale: The 'maintain' obligation in the clause implies ongoing investment. Financial institutions with complex, evolving infrastructure cannot satisfy this requirement through periodic manual documentation alone; failure to fund continuous maintenance renders the blueprint non-compliant by design.
requirements.frequency: Annual (budget cycle) with Quarterly spend review

technology.summary: Technology teams must produce and continuously update a network design blueprint that is accurate, complete, and machine-readable where possible, covering all physical and logical layers of connectivity including segmentation boundaries. This requires tooling for automated network discovery, diagram management, and change-triggered updates so that the blueprint reflects the live environment at all times. Both the physical topology (cables, devices, data centres) and logical topology (VLANs, routing domains, firewall zones, cloud virtual networks) must be represented with sufficient fidelity to support incident response, audit, and change management.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and operate automated network discovery and topology mapping tooling that continuously inventories all active network interfaces, devices, and connectivity paths across on-premises, co-location, and cloud environments, feeding into the network design blueprint.
requirements.rationale: Manual documentation of 'all internal and external network interfaces and connectivity' is inherently incomplete in dynamic environments. Automated discovery ensures the blueprint reflects the actual network state, not an aspirational or outdated design, satisfying the 'maintain' obligation in the clause.
requirements.frequency: Continuous discovery; blueprint artefact reviewed Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Produce and maintain detailed physical network diagrams documenting all physical connectivity between network components: data centre cabling, patch panels, physical switches, routers, firewalls, load balancers, WAN links, and colocation cross-connects.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly mandates 'physical connectivity between network components'. Physical topology documentation is essential for rapid fault isolation during outages; without it, mean-time-to-restore (MTTR) increases and regulatory expectations on resilience cannot be demonstrated.
requirements.frequency: Per-change (updated within 5 business days of any physical infrastructure change)

requirements.requirement: Produce and maintain detailed logical network diagrams covering IP addressing schemes, VLAN assignments, routing protocols, firewall rule-set boundaries, VPN tunnels, DNS zones, and cloud virtual network configurations, with explicit callout of all network segmentation boundaries.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'logical connectivity between network components and network segmentations'. Logical segmentation is the primary control mechanism for containing lateral movement and isolating critical systems; accurate documentation is a prerequisite for both operational management and security assurance.
requirements.frequency: Per-change (updated within 5 business days of any logical network change)

requirements.requirement: Maintain a dedicated register of all external network interfaces and connectivity, including ISP peering links, payment network connections (e.g., RENTAS, IBG, FPX), SWIFT connectivity, API gateways exposed to third parties, and cloud provider interconnects, with documented data flows and security controls at each boundary.
requirements.rationale: The clause calls out 'external network interfaces and connectivity' as a distinct category. External interfaces are the boundary between the institution and the outside world; each represents a potential ingress for threats and must be documented with sufficient detail to support both security review and regulatory examination.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (on each external connection change) and Quarterly validation

requirements.requirement: Integrate the network design blueprint into the institution's Change Management process so that any approved network change automatically triggers a blueprint update workflow, with a mandatory sign-off confirming blueprint accuracy before change closure.
requirements.rationale: The 'maintain' requirement in the clause is operationalised through change management integration. Without this coupling, infrastructure changes accumulate silently and the blueprint drifts from reality, making it unreliable for incident response and non-compliant with BNM's expectation of an accurate, current document.
requirements.frequency: Per-change event

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure the network design blueprint is treated as a controlled, versioned regulatory artefact subject to formal ownership, approval, periodic independent review, and audit validation. Senior management must receive assurance that the blueprint is accurate and complete, while the Board is accountable for the institution's overall network resilience posture. Policies must explicitly mandate the blueprint's scope, update triggers, review cadence, and escalation paths for identified gaps.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formal Technology Operations policy or standard that mandates the existence of a network design blueprint, prescribes its minimum required content (physical topology, logical topology, segmentation boundaries, internal and external interfaces), defines update triggers, and assigns ownership.
requirements.rationale: The clause imposes a 'must establish and maintain' obligation. Without a governing policy, the blueprint has no enforceable standard of completeness or currency; BNM examiners will expect to see a documented policy that translates the regulatory requirement into institutional controls.
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed Annually; updated Per-event on regulatory or material infrastructure change

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal approval and version control process for the network design blueprint, requiring sign-off from the Head of Network & Infrastructure and CISO for each material update, with a maintained change log and version history accessible to auditors.
requirements.rationale: A blueprint without version control and formal approval is not a controlled document and provides no audit trail for regulatory review. BNM expects institutions to demonstrate that their blueprints are accurate and sanctioned by responsible officers, not ad-hoc working documents.
requirements.frequency: Per-change (approval required before each versioned update is finalised)

requirements.requirement: Mandate annual independent validation of the network design blueprint's accuracy by Internal Audit or a qualified independent party, comparing the documented topology against actual network configurations and producing a findings report with remediation timelines.
requirements.rationale: The 'maintain' obligation requires the institution to continuously ensure accuracy, not merely produce a document once. Independent validation provides the Board and senior management with objective assurance that the blueprint reflects reality, and surfaces drift between the documented and actual network states before BNM examination.
requirements.frequency: Annual, with ad-hoc validation following significant infrastructure changes

requirements.requirement: Include network design blueprint accuracy and completeness as a standing agenda item in the Technology Risk Committee (or equivalent), with the CIO and CISO presenting a status update covering any identified gaps, pending updates, and remediation actions at least quarterly.
requirements.rationale: Senior management oversight is required to satisfy BNM's expectation that governance bodies actively monitor compliance with technology risk requirements. Elevating blueprint status to committee level ensures accountability and resource prioritisation for remediation of identified deficiencies.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Incorporate network design blueprint review into the institution's Regulatory Examination Readiness programme, maintaining an evidence pack (current blueprint version, approval records, audit findings, remediation log) that is retrievable within 24 hours of a BNM request.
requirements.rationale: As a Standard (S) marker clause, 10.41 is directly examinable by BNM. Institutions must be able to produce the blueprint and demonstrate its currency on demand. A pre-assembled evidence pack reduces regulatory examination risk and demonstrates proactive compliance management.
requirements.frequency: Semi-annual readiness review; evidence pack updated Per-change

business.summary: Failure to retain sufficient and relevant network device logs exposes the institution to regulatory sanctions, impedes forensic investigations following security incidents, and undermines the institution's ability to demonstrate compliance during BNM examinations. A minimum three-year retention window directly supports post-incident analysis, litigation discovery, and regulatory audit trails, making this a foundational operational risk control.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formally approved Network Log Retention Policy that explicitly defines what constitutes 'sufficient and relevant' network device logs, scoped to all devices within the institution's technology estate, and mandating a minimum three-year retention period.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires logs be 'sufficient and relevant' — without a governing policy defining these terms, the institution cannot demonstrate compliance. Ambiguity in scope creates regulatory exposure during BNM examinations.
requirements.frequency: Annual policy review; immediate update upon material changes to network topology

requirements.requirement: Allocate dedicated budget and resource planning for log storage infrastructure capable of sustaining three years of network device log retention, including projections for log volume growth aligned to business expansion.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates 'at least three years' retention — this is a non-negotiable operational cost. Underfunded storage leads to premature log deletion, directly breaching the minimum retention threshold.
requirements.frequency: Annual budgeting cycle; reviewed quarterly against actual storage utilisation

requirements.requirement: Define and document formal procedures for log retrieval and evidence packaging to support regulatory investigations, forensic engagements, and litigation discovery, ensuring logs can be produced in a forensically sound and timely manner.
requirements.rationale: The clause identifies 'investigations and forensic purposes' as the primary justification for retention. Without retrieval procedures, retained logs are operationally useless when regulators or law enforcement demand evidence.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (triggered by investigation requests); procedures reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Include network device log retention obligations within vendor and managed service contracts where third parties operate network infrastructure on behalf of the institution, ensuring contractual enforcement of the three-year minimum retention requirement.
requirements.rationale: Outsourced network operations do not transfer regulatory responsibility. BNM holds the financial institution accountable for compliance regardless of whether logs are managed internally or by a third party.
requirements.frequency: At contract inception and renewal; annual compliance attestation from vendors

technology.summary: Technology teams must deploy a centralised, tamper-resistant log management infrastructure capable of aggregating logs from all network devices, enforcing a minimum three-year retention lifecycle, and supporting forensic-grade log retrieval. Controls must ensure log completeness, integrity, and availability throughout the full retention period without gaps caused by storage failures or misconfiguration.

requirements.requirement: Deploy a centralised Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) or dedicated log management platform that ingests logs from all network devices — including firewalls, routers, switches, load balancers, VPN concentrators, and IDS/IPS — with automated collection to eliminate manual gaps.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires logs be retained for 'investigations and forensic purposes', implying completeness across the network estate. Decentralised or manual log collection creates blind spots that undermine forensic integrity.
requirements.frequency: Continuous ingestion; platform health reviewed monthly

requirements.requirement: Define and enforce a standardised log retention configuration across all network devices specifying minimum log types to be captured, including authentication events, configuration changes, traffic flows, access control list (ACL) hits, interface state changes, and administrative actions.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires logs be 'sufficient and relevant' — this cannot be satisfied without explicitly specifying which log categories constitute sufficient coverage for forensic reconstruction of network events.
requirements.frequency: Configuration enforced continuously; reviewed annually or upon new device onboarding

requirements.requirement: Implement log integrity controls — including cryptographic hashing (e.g., SHA-256) and write-once/append-only storage — to ensure logs cannot be altered, deleted, or overwritten prior to the expiry of the three-year retention period.
requirements.rationale: Forensic admissibility of logs depends on demonstrable integrity. Without tamper-evidence controls, retained logs may be inadmissible in investigations or disputed during regulatory examinations.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; integrity verification tested quarterly

requirements.requirement: Provision log storage capacity with a minimum three-year retention lifecycle, enforced through automated tiered storage policies (hot/warm/cold) that migrate older logs to cost-effective archival storage while maintaining retrieval capability within defined recovery time objectives (RTOs).
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates 'at least three years' — automated lifecycle management prevents accidental premature deletion due to storage capacity exhaustion, a common cause of unintentional non-compliance.
requirements.frequency: Automated lifecycle enforcement; capacity reviewed quarterly; retention expiry audited monthly

requirements.requirement: Implement log search, filtering, and export capabilities that enable forensic analysts and investigators to reconstruct network events across the full three-year retention window, with query performance benchmarks ensuring timely response to investigation requests.
requirements.rationale: The clause's forensic purpose is only fulfilled if retained logs are retrievable and analysable. Storage without usable search capability renders the retention obligation practically meaningless during time-sensitive investigations.
requirements.frequency: Capability validation tested semi-annually; performance benchmarks reviewed quarterly
