governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure accountability for log retention compliance through formal policy ownership, periodic independent assurance, and integration of log retention requirements into the institution's broader risk management and audit frameworks. Given the three-year mandatory window and BNM's examination scrutiny, oversight mechanisms must detect and remediate retention failures before they crystallise into regulatory breaches.

requirements.requirement: Assign formal ownership of the Network Log Retention Policy to a designated senior accountable officer, with board-level or IT Steering Committee approval of the policy and its minimum three-year retention standard, and documented evidence of approval retained for regulatory examination.
requirements.rationale: BNM's 'S' marker denotes a standard requirement demanding demonstrable institutional accountability. Without a named owner and formal approval, the policy lacks the governance legitimacy required during regulatory scrutiny.
requirements.frequency: Annual policy reapproval; ownership reviewed upon organisational changes

requirements.requirement: Incorporate network device log retention compliance into the annual Internal Audit plan, with audit scope covering retention completeness across all network device classes, storage integrity controls, retrieval capability, and adherence to the three-year minimum retention period.
requirements.rationale: Independent assurance provides the institution and BNM with objective evidence that the retention obligation is being met. Without periodic audit, retention gaps may persist undetected for years, creating compounding compliance failures.
requirements.frequency: Annual audit; findings tracked to closure with quarterly status reporting to Audit Committee

requirements.requirement: Establish a Log Retention Compliance Register that tracks the retention status, storage locations, expiry schedules, and integrity verification results for network device logs across all business units and subsidiaries, updated at minimum quarterly.
requirements.rationale: The three-year retention requirement spans multiple technology refresh cycles and organisational changes. A compliance register provides the audit trail needed to demonstrate continuous adherence and identify gaps proactively.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Risk and Compliance
requirements.frequency: Quarterly updates; reviewed by CRO and CISO semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Define and test an escalation and notification procedure for log retention failures — including storage exhaustion, collection interruptions, and integrity check failures — with mandatory escalation to CISO and CRO within 24 hours and a documented remediation timeline.
requirements.rationale: Any gap in log collection or premature deletion during the three-year window constitutes a potential breach of Clause 10.42. Rapid escalation enables corrective action before the gap becomes material or unrecoverable.
requirements.frequency: Procedure tested semi-annually; escalation events reviewed at each Operational Risk Committee meeting

requirements.requirement: Include network device log retention as a standing agenda item in periodic Technology Risk Committee or Operational Risk Committee reviews, with metrics reported covering storage utilisation, retention coverage rates, integrity verification pass rates, and outstanding audit findings.
requirements.rationale: Management oversight through committee-level reporting ensures sustained senior attention to retention compliance, preventing the clause from becoming a set-and-forget technical configuration that drifts out of compliance over time.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly committee reporting; metrics dashboard reviewed monthly by Head of IT Risk and Compliance

business.summary: A system compromise within any single group entity poses a contagion risk that could cascade across the entire financial group, threatening operational continuity, customer trust, and regulatory standing. Clause 10.43 requires the financial institution to actively contain this blast radius through deliberate architectural safeguards. Failure to comply exposes the institution to group-wide operational disruption, regulatory enforcement action, and reputational damage extending beyond the initially compromised entity.

requirements.requirement: Define and formally document the scope of 'entities within the group' that share technology infrastructure or network connectivity with the financial institution, including subsidiaries, associates, and shared-service entities, and maintain a current group entity register.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text refers explicitly to 'other entities within the group' as the propagation risk surface. Without a formally scoped and maintained group entity register, the institution cannot systematically identify where segmentation safeguards are required, creating blind spots in its risk containment posture.
requirements.frequency: Annual review, updated per-event upon group restructuring or new entity onboarding

requirements.requirement: Conduct a group-level contagion risk assessment to identify and quantify the business impact of a system compromise in one group entity propagating to the financial institution, covering customer data exposure, service unavailability, and financial loss scenarios.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires safeguards calibrated to 'minimise the risk' of cross-entity compromise. A formal risk assessment translates this obligation into measurable risk thresholds and informs proportionate safeguard investment, satisfying BNM's expectation of risk-based decision-making.
requirements.frequency: Annual, and upon material change in group structure or threat landscape

requirements.requirement: Establish intra-group technology risk agreements (e.g., Intra-Group Service Agreements or Technology Risk Schedules) with all group entities that share network infrastructure, clearly stipulating each party's obligations to maintain segmentation controls and report security incidents immediately.
requirements.rationale: The financial institution's safeguard obligations extend to controlling the risk originating from peer group entities. Without contractual or policy instruments binding those entities, the financial institution has no enforceable mechanism to ensure the other side of the network boundary is maintained securely.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Group Chief Risk Officer
requirements.frequency: Reviewed annually, renegotiated upon group restructuring

requirements.requirement: Integrate group-wide network segmentation risk into the institution's Business Continuity Plan (BCP) and Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP), with specific scenarios addressing compromise originating from a group entity and the activation of isolation protocols to protect the financial institution.
requirements.rationale: The clause's objective is to minimise the risk of cross-entity impact. BCP/DRP integration ensures that when a compromise does occur at a group entity, the institution has pre-planned, executable procedures to activate segmentation controls and maintain critical operations, reducing actual impact.
requirements.frequency: Annual plan review; scenario tested at least annually via tabletop or simulation exercise

requirements.requirement: Report group network segmentation risk posture and the status of implemented safeguards to the Board Risk Committee (BRC) on a regular basis, including any identified gaps, incidents involving cross-entity network access, and remediation progress.
requirements.rationale: BNM's 'S' marker denotes a standard mandatory obligation requiring demonstrated governance accountability. Board-level visibility ensures that segmentation safeguards receive appropriate strategic oversight and resource prioritisation commensurate with the group-wide risk they mitigate.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting to Board Risk Committee

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement and continuously maintain controls that enforce strict network boundaries between the financial institution and all other group entities, ensuring that a compromise in one segment cannot freely propagate laterally. The clause's explicit reference to 'logical network segmentation' sets a minimum baseline control expectation, which must be supplemented by monitoring, access control, and regular validation. All segmentation controls must be proportionate to the assessed risk and subject to ongoing verification.

requirements.requirement: Implement logical network segmentation between the financial institution's network environment and all other group entity networks, using controls such as VLANs, firewalls with enforced deny-by-default inter-segment rulesets, Software-Defined Networking (SDN) policies, or equivalent technologies, ensuring no uncontrolled lateral path exists between group entity segments.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly names 'logical network segmentation' as a named safeguard. This is the primary technical control mandated to contain the blast radius of a system compromise. Deny-by-default rulesets ensure that only explicitly authorised and business-justified traffic flows between segments, preventing uncontrolled lateral movement.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Network Security
requirements.frequency: Implemented continuously; rule-base reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Deploy network access controls (NAC) and enforce least-privilege inter-segment communication policies for all connections crossing the boundary between the financial institution and group entities, requiring explicit authorisation, documented business justification, and time-limited access grants where applicable.
requirements.rationale: Segmentation alone is insufficient without strict access governance at the boundary. The clause's requirement to 'minimise the risk' of cross-entity compromise demands that all inter-entity network flows are deliberately authorised and constrained to the minimum necessary, reducing the attack surface exploitable in a compromise scenario.
requirements.owner: CISO / Head of IT Security Operations
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; access grant reviews quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement continuous network traffic monitoring and anomaly detection at all inter-segment boundaries between the financial institution and group entities, with alerting capabilities to detect and respond to unauthorised lateral movement, unusual data flows, or attempts to breach segmentation controls.
requirements.rationale: Preventive segmentation controls must be paired with detective controls to identify failures or breaches of those boundaries. Continuous monitoring enables the institution to detect when a compromise in a group entity is attempting to propagate, triggering containment before the financial institution's own systems are affected.
requirements.owner: CISO / Head of Security Operations Centre (SOC)
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; alert tuning reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Conduct regular penetration testing and network segmentation validation exercises that specifically test the integrity of boundaries between the financial institution and group entities, verifying that logical segmentation controls cannot be bypassed via misconfiguration, unpatched vulnerabilities, or social engineering vectors.
requirements.rationale: Implemented segmentation controls can degrade over time due to configuration drift, software updates, or the introduction of new systems. The clause's obligation to 'implement appropriate safeguards' is not satisfied by a one-time implementation; periodic validation ensures the controls remain effective and commensurate with current threats.
requirements.frequency: At minimum annually; following significant network changes or incidents

requirements.requirement: Maintain a current and accurate inventory of all authorised inter-entity network connections, data flows, and integration points between the financial institution and group entities, including API gateways, shared services, messaging queues, and VPN tunnels, with each connection mapped to a business owner and reviewed for continued necessity.
requirements.rationale: Effective segmentation requires complete visibility of what crosses the boundary. Undocumented connections represent uncontrolled risk vectors that undermine the logical segmentation safeguard. A maintained inventory enables accurate firewall rule governance, faster incident response, and defensible evidence for BNM examination.
requirements.frequency: Maintained continuously; formal review semi-annually

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that network segmentation safeguards are not merely implemented technically but are formally mandated by policy, subject to independent assurance, and embedded in the institution's broader risk management framework. The clause's group-wide scope requires governance instruments that extend beyond the institution's own boundaries, including oversight of group entities' compliance posture. Board and senior management accountability must be clearly assigned to prevent the 'appropriate safeguards' obligation from remaining at a purely operational level.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formal Network Segmentation Policy applicable to the financial institution's connections with all group entities, defining the mandatory safeguard standards, approved segmentation technologies, inter-segment access governance procedures, exception management processes, and roles accountable for implementation and compliance.
requirements.rationale: The clause's 'must implement appropriate safeguards' language requires a durable, policy-level commitment that transcends individual technical decisions. A formal policy creates the governance instrument through which the institution operationalises its compliance, provides a baseline for audit, and ensures consistency of safeguard standards across all group entity interfaces.
requirements.frequency: Annual policy review; updated upon material regulatory or risk changes

requirements.requirement: Assign explicit accountability to a named senior executive (at minimum CISO level) for group-wide network segmentation risk, with a defined mandate to oversee the implementation and ongoing effectiveness of all safeguards required under Clause 10.43, and report directly to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: BNM's 'S' marker implies an expectation that the institution can demonstrate named ownership and accountability for compliance. Without explicit executive ownership, segmentation governance risks becoming diffuse, under-resourced, or inconsistently applied across the institution-to-group-entity boundary.
requirements.frequency: Accountability assignment reviewed annually; reported quarterly

requirements.requirement: Establish an independent assurance programme (Internal Audit or equivalent) that periodically reviews and reports on the effectiveness of network segmentation safeguards between the financial institution and group entities, covering policy compliance, technical control effectiveness, and residual risk posture.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'appropriate safeguards' which implies demonstrated effectiveness, not merely existence. Independent assurance provides the Board and senior management with objective evidence that safeguards are operating as intended and that the institution's obligations under Clause 10.43 are being met in substance, not just in form.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor / Head of Internal Audit
requirements.frequency: Annual independent review; findings tracked to closure

requirements.requirement: Define and embed a cross-entity Security Incident Notification Protocol that mandates immediate notification to the financial institution's CISO and senior management when any group entity experiences a system compromise or security incident with potential to affect the financial institution's network, triggering pre-defined isolation and containment procedures.
requirements.rationale: The clause's objective is to minimise risk of cross-entity compromise propagation. Governance protocols must operationalise the response to a compromise event at a group entity — without a pre-defined notification and escalation protocol, the financial institution may not learn of a threat in time to activate its segmentation controls before impact occurs.
requirements.frequency: Protocol reviewed annually; tested via simulation at least annually

requirements.requirement: Incorporate Clause 10.43 compliance obligations into the financial institution's third-party and intra-group risk management framework, ensuring that technology risk due diligence, onboarding assessments, and ongoing monitoring of group entities include evaluation of their network security posture and its potential to introduce compromise risk to the financial institution.
requirements.rationale: The source of the cross-entity compromise risk under Clause 10.43 may originate from a group entity's own security weaknesses. The financial institution's governance framework must therefore extend its risk oversight to encompass the security posture of entities whose networks interface with its own, consistent with BNM's broader expectations on third-party and intra-group risk management.
requirements.owner: CRO / Head of Third-Party Risk Management
requirements.frequency: Onboarding assessment per new entity; ongoing monitoring reviewed annually

business.summary: Failure to maintain a robust backup strategy directly threatens a financial institution's ability to recover from system failures, cyberattacks, or disasters, potentially causing prolonged service outages, data loss, and regulatory sanctions. Clause 10.44 requires that recovery objectives drive backup design, ensuring the institution can meet its commitments to customers and counterparties even after a major incident. Non-compliance risks reputational damage, BNM enforcement action, and financial loss from unrecoverable transaction data.

requirements.requirement: Define and document Recovery Time Objectives (RTO) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPO) for all critical business functions, and ensure the backup strategy is explicitly designed to meet these objectives.
requirements.rationale: The clause opens with the obligation to 'meet business recovery objectives.' Without formally quantified RTOs and RPOs approved at the business level, the backup strategy has no measurable success criterion and cannot demonstrate regulatory compliance.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; updated upon material changes to critical business processes

requirements.requirement: Maintain a comprehensive and current inventory of all critical data assets — including transaction files, master files, event logs, operating system software, production programs, and system utilities — that must be covered by the backup programme as specified in clause (b).
requirements.rationale: Clause (b) explicitly enumerates the data categories that must be backed up. Without a formal, business-owned asset inventory, the institution cannot demonstrate that backup coverage is adequate, leaving gaps that could result in irrecoverable data loss following an incident.
requirements.frequency: Continuous maintenance; formal review Semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain contracts and SLAs with backup site operators to ensure the offsite backup facility meets environmental security, physical access control, and capacity requirements as referenced in clause (c).
requirements.rationale: Clause (c) mandates that backup media be stored in an 'environmentally secure and access-controlled backup site.' A legally binding SLA ensures the institution has enforceable guarantees over the site's standards, which is critical during a BNM audit or post-incident review.
requirements.frequency: Contract review Annual; site audit at least Annual

requirements.requirement: Ensure business continuity and disaster recovery plans are aligned with and validated against backup restoration capabilities, incorporating results from periodic backup restoration tests as required by clause (e).
requirements.rationale: Clause (e) requires periodic testing to 'validate recovery capabilities.' Test results must feed into business continuity planning so that stated recovery capabilities are realistic, preventing scenarios where a plan on paper cannot be executed in practice.
requirements.frequency: Post each backup restoration test; at minimum Annual

requirements.requirement: Commission and review an independent risk assessment of the end-to-end backup storage and delivery management at least annually, with findings presented to senior management and the Board Risk Committee, as required by clause (f).
requirements.rationale: Clause (f) explicitly requires an 'independent risk assessment' of the entire backup lifecycle. Ownership at the business level ensures that findings translate into funded remediation actions, not merely IT-level reports that lack executive accountability.
requirements.frequency: Annual; additionally triggered by significant infrastructure changes or incidents

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement automated, comprehensive backup systems covering all enumerated data categories, enforce strict physical and logical security controls at backup sites and during media transit, and operate a formal restoration testing programme with documented remediation for failures. The technical implementation must also support independent auditability of the entire backup and delivery lifecycle. Gaps in any of these areas represent direct violations of specific sub-clauses within 10.44.

requirements.requirement: Design and implement automated backup procedures that manage the full backup data lifecycle — including creation, retention, rotation, versioning, and deletion — covering all critical data categories specified in clause (b): critical data, OS software, production programs, system utilities, master files, transaction files, and event logs.
requirements.rationale: Clauses (a) and (b) together require both a lifecycle management process and comprehensive coverage of enumerated data types. Automated procedures reduce human error, ensure no data category is omitted, and provide audit-ready logs of backup execution.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; configuration review Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Provision and maintain an offsite backup site that is physically separated from the primary data centre, with environmental controls (fire suppression, temperature/humidity monitoring, flood protection) and logical/physical access controls (CCTV, biometric or card-based entry, access logs) meeting the requirements of clause (c).
requirements.rationale: Clause (c) mandates an 'environmentally secure and access-controlled backup site.' Colocating backups with production systems negates their value during a site-wide disaster. Environmental and access controls protect media integrity and confidentiality.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; formal site inspection Semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Implement encryption (at-rest and in-transit) and tamper-evident packaging for all sensitive data on removable media, with strict chain-of-custody logging for transportation, aligned to the minimum controls specified in BNM RMiT Appendix 1 as referenced in clause (d).
requirements.rationale: Clause (d) specifically requires secure storage and transportation of sensitive data in removable media per Appendix 1 controls. Unencrypted or untracked media in transit represents a high-severity data breach risk and a direct regulatory violation.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; control review Annual or per Appendix 1 update

requirements.requirement: Establish and execute a formal backup restoration testing programme that simulates real recovery scenarios, validates full data integrity and system operability post-restoration, and covers all critical systems on a defined schedule. Document all test results and track root-cause analysis and remediation for any unsuccessful backup or restoration as required by clause (e).
requirements.rationale: Clause (e) requires periodic testing to 'validate recovery capabilities' and mandates that 'remedial actions shall be taken promptly to fix the root cause of unsuccessful backups.' Untested backups provide false assurance; documented remediation demonstrates regulatory diligence.
requirements.frequency: At minimum Semi-annually for critical systems; Quarterly recommended; remediation per-event

requirements.requirement: Implement centralised backup monitoring and alerting that provides real-time visibility into backup job status, media health, storage capacity, and access events across both primary and offsite backup locations, generating audit-ready logs to support the independent risk assessment required by clause (f).
requirements.rationale: Clause (f) requires an independent risk assessment of the 'end-to-end backup storage and delivery management.' Comprehensive monitoring logs are a prerequisite for an independent assessor to evaluate control effectiveness and identify gaps across the full lifecycle.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; log retention aligned to data retention policy; review Monthly

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that the backup strategy is formalised in Board-approved policy, that accountability for each component of the backup lifecycle is clearly assigned, and that independent assurance over backup controls is embedded in the institution's risk management framework. Clause 10.44's explicit requirement for independent risk assessment and prompt remediation of failures demands governance mechanisms that escalate findings to decision-makers with authority to act.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Board- or senior management-approved Backup and Recovery Policy that explicitly documents the backup strategy, data lifecycle procedures, offsite storage requirements, media security controls, testing schedules, and remediation processes, fulfilling the overarching obligation in clause (a) and the preamble of clause 10.44.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to 'establish' backup and restoration procedures — a term BNM consistently interprets as requiring written, approved, and communicated policy. Without a formal policy, individual practices are uncoordinated and cannot be audited for consistency.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Board Risk Committee
requirements.frequency: Annual review and reapproval; updated upon material regulatory or operational change

requirements.requirement: Define and document clear ownership, roles, and responsibilities for every stage of the backup lifecycle — including backup execution, media management, offsite storage, transportation, testing, and incident remediation — ensuring accountability is traceable to named roles as required across clauses (a) through (f).
requirements.rationale: Diffuse or undefined ownership leads to execution gaps, particularly for obligations like 'prompt remediation' in clause (e) and the independent assessment in clause (f). A RACI matrix or equivalent document ensures no sub-clause falls through organisational cracks.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Head of IT Governance
requirements.frequency: Annual review; updated upon organisational restructuring

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Backup Incident and Remediation Governance process that mandates root-cause analysis for every unsuccessful backup or failed restoration test, tracks remediation actions to closure, and escalates systemic failures to senior management within defined timeframes, as explicitly required by clause (e).
requirements.rationale: Clause (e) states that 'remedial actions shall be taken promptly by the financial institution to fix the root cause of unsuccessful backups.' Without a governed remediation process with escalation paths and closure criteria, prompt action cannot be assured or evidenced to BNM.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of IT Operations
requirements.frequency: Per-event for incidents; governance process review Annual

requirements.requirement: Arrange and govern an independent risk assessment of the end-to-end backup storage and delivery management, ensuring the assessor has appropriate independence from the backup operations team, the scope covers all controls referenced in clauses (b) through (e), and findings with management responses are reported to the Board Risk Committee, as required by clause (f).
requirements.rationale: Clause (f) explicitly requires 'independent' assessment, meaning internal teams responsible for backup operations cannot self-assess. The governance layer must define independence criteria, manage the assessor engagement, and ensure Board-level visibility of findings to drive accountability.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Internal Audit
requirements.frequency: Annual; additionally upon significant infrastructure change, incident, or BNM direction

requirements.requirement: Incorporate backup compliance metrics — including backup success rates, restoration test pass/fail results, open remediation items, and independent assessment findings — into the institution's management information reporting framework for regular review by senior management and the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: The preamble of clause 10.44 and clause (f) together require the institution to demonstrate that 'existing controls are adequate in protecting sensitive data at all times.' Ongoing management information reporting provides the evidence trail needed for Board oversight and BNM examination, beyond point-in-time assessments.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Board Risk Committee
requirements.frequency: Monthly for senior management; Quarterly for Board Risk Committee

business.summary: This clause mandates that financial institutions protect the continuity of critical banking and payment services against destructive cyber-attacks, particularly ransomware. Failure to comply exposes the institution to prolonged service outages beyond its tolerable downtime thresholds, triggering regulatory censure, reputational damage, and customer loss. For Malaysian banks and payment service providers, the inability to restore operations within defined tolerances may constitute a material breach of BNM's business continuity expectations.

requirements.requirement: Define and formally document the tolerable level of service disruption (Recovery Time Objective and Recovery Point Objective) for each critical banking and payment service, calibrated against ransomware and destructive attack scenarios.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates resumption 'within its tolerable level,' which requires the institution to first quantify what 'tolerable' means per service. Without formally defined RTO/RPO, there is no measurable compliance baseline and no means to validate that backup and recovery arrangements are fit for purpose.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; triggered immediately upon material change to critical service inventory or risk appetite

requirements.requirement: Maintain a current and approved inventory of all critical banking and payment services that are subject to the tamper-proof backup and isolated recovery requirement, including their interdependencies and recovery priority sequencing.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly targets 'critical banking and payment services.' Without a defined and approved inventory, the institution cannot demonstrate that its backup arrangements cover the correct scope. Gaps in coverage could render the entire arrangement non-compliant if a ransomware event affects an unlisted critical service.
requirements.frequency: Semi-annual review; updated upon any change to the critical service portfolio

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Business Continuity Plan (BCP) scenario specifically addressing destructive cyber-attacks, including ransomware-driven widespread data loss, with defined escalation paths, stakeholder communication protocols, and customer impact mitigation measures.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text singles out 'destructive cyber-attacks such as widespread data loss caused by ransomware' as the primary threat scenario. Business continuity plans that only address infrastructure failures or natural disasters are insufficient. Ransomware recovery requires unique business-level decisions (e.g., whether to pay, when to invoke isolated recovery, how to communicate to customers and BNM).
requirements.frequency: Annual review and approval; activated upon declared cyber incident

requirements.requirement: Allocate dedicated budget and resources for tamper-proof backup infrastructure and isolated recovery environment, treated as non-negotiable operational expenditure with board-level visibility.
requirements.rationale: Effective implementation of a tamper-proof backup arrangement and an isolated recovery environment requires dedicated funding that cannot be deferred or consolidated with general IT budgets. Underfunding this control directly undermines the institution's ability to meet its regulatory obligation under this clause.
requirements.frequency: Annual budget cycle; reviewed quarterly against spend and capability milestones

requirements.requirement: Conduct annual business impact analysis (BIA) updates to reassess the financial, operational, and reputational consequences of ransomware-induced service unavailability, and ensure recovery tolerances remain aligned with current business risk appetite.
requirements.rationale: Business operations and risk profiles evolve. The 'tolerable level' referenced in the clause must be re-evaluated as transaction volumes, product offerings, and customer dependencies grow. An outdated BIA may set tolerances that are no longer acceptable to the institution or to BNM.
requirements.frequency: Annual; triggered upon significant business change or post-incident

technology.summary: Technology teams must architect and operate a backup ecosystem that is technically isolated from the primary production environment and immune to corruption by ransomware or insider manipulation. This requires immutable storage controls, network-segregated recovery environments, and automated integrity verification mechanisms. The isolated recovery environment must be validated through regular drills that simulate actual ransomware attack scenarios, not just routine backup restoration tests.

requirements.requirement: Implement immutable (write-once, read-many) backup storage for all critical banking and payment service data, enforced at the storage layer using object-lock or equivalent controls that prevent modification or deletion by any account, including privileged administrator accounts, for a defined retention period.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates a 'tamper-proof backup arrangement.' Immutability enforced at the storage layer — not merely at the application or operating system level — is the foundational technical control that prevents ransomware from encrypting or deleting backup data. Without this, backups remain vulnerable to sophisticated ransomware variants that target backup systems.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; configuration audited quarterly

requirements.requirement: Design and operate an isolated recovery environment (IRE) that is network-air-gapped or logically segmented from production and internet-connected networks, with access controlled by out-of-band authentication mechanisms, capable of hosting and running all critical banking and payment services independently.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires an 'isolated recovery environment.' An IRE that shares network segments, credentials, or management planes with the production environment can be compromised by the same ransomware attack, rendering the recovery capability useless. The IRE must be functionally capable of operating critical services, not merely storing data.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operational readiness; validated via quarterly simulations

requirements.requirement: Implement automated cryptographic integrity verification of all backup copies using hash-based validation (e.g., SHA-256 checksums) at the point of creation and at regular intervals, with alerts triggered upon any integrity check failure, and maintain audit logs of all verification results.
requirements.rationale: Tamper-proof backups require active verification — not merely the existence of immutability controls. Ransomware may corrupt data before backup jobs execute. Cryptographic verification provides forensic evidence that backup data is intact and unmodified, supporting both regulatory audit and actual recovery confidence.
requirements.frequency: Automated continuous verification at backup creation; integrity audit reports reviewed monthly

requirements.requirement: Establish and enforce physical and logical separation of backup credentials and encryption keys from production credentials, using dedicated privileged access workstations (PAWs) and a hardware security module (HSM) or equivalent key management system accessible only from the isolated recovery environment.
requirements.rationale: Ransomware attacks frequently target credential stores and key management systems to disable recovery capabilities. If backup decryption keys or administrative credentials are accessible from the compromised production environment, the tamper-proof arrangement is undermined. Separation ensures recovery remains possible even when the entire production environment is compromised.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; access control reviews quarterly

requirements.requirement: Conduct end-to-end isolated recovery exercises at least annually, simulating a ransomware-induced widespread data loss scenario, measuring actual recovery time against defined RTOs, documenting gaps, and remediating identified deficiencies within a tracked action plan.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires that the arrangement enables 'timely resumption within its tolerable level,' which can only be validated through realistic testing — not assumed. Exercises confined to partial restores or non-isolated environments do not prove compliance. Full end-to-end tests in the IRE reveal latent gaps in tooling, skills, documentation, and capacity that tabletop exercises cannot surface.
requirements.frequency: Annual full simulation; semi-annual partial exercises; post-incident triggered review

requirements.requirement: Implement offline or out-of-band backup copies following a 3-2-1-1 or equivalent policy (three copies, two different media types, one offsite, one offline/air-gapped), with the offline copy updated at a frequency that meets the RPO of each critical service.
requirements.rationale: The 'tamper-proof backup arrangement' cannot rely solely on online immutable storage, which, while hardened, remains reachable over networks. An offline copy provides a last-resort recovery option that is physically inaccessible to ransomware, ensuring that even the most sophisticated attacks cannot destroy all backup copies simultaneously.
requirements.frequency: Backup frequency per RPO of each critical service; offline media rotation audited quarterly

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that the tamper-proof backup and isolated recovery capability is subject to Board and senior management oversight, with clear accountability, regular assurance, and integration into the institution's cyber resilience and technology risk frameworks. Policies and procedures must specifically address ransomware scenarios, and third-party dependencies in backup and recovery chains must be governed under vendor risk management arrangements. BNM must be able to rely on documented evidence of ongoing compliance.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Board-approved policy for tamper-proof backup and isolated recovery that explicitly addresses destructive cyber-attack scenarios including ransomware, defines roles and responsibilities, sets minimum standards for immutability, isolation, and testing, and is reviewed at least annually.
requirements.rationale: The clause imposes an obligation on 'a financial institution' as a legal entity, which in Malaysian regulatory practice requires Board-level ownership and approval of the governing policy. Without a dedicated policy, the institution lacks the documented governance framework that BNM expects to find during technology risk examinations.
requirements.owner: Board of Directors (via Board Risk Committee)
requirements.frequency: Annual review and approval; triggered by material incident or regulatory change

requirements.requirement: Designate a named accountable executive (minimum CTO or CISO level) as the owner of the tamper-proof backup and isolated recovery programme, with documented responsibilities for implementation, testing, and reporting to the Board Risk Committee on compliance status and gap remediation.
requirements.rationale: Regulatory obligations require identifiable human accountability. The clause's 'shall establish' language places a direct duty on the institution. Designating a named executive ensures the programme does not fall into organisational ambiguity, and provides BNM with a clear point of accountability during supervisory engagements.
requirements.frequency: Appointment reviewed annually; reporting to Board Risk Committee quarterly

requirements.requirement: Integrate tamper-proof backup and isolated recovery assurance into the institution's internal audit programme, with independent testing of immutability controls, IRE capability, and recovery exercise results conducted at least annually by auditors with relevant cyber resilience expertise.
requirements.rationale: Self-assessment by technology teams is insufficient for regulatory compliance. Independent internal audit provides the governance layer that validates management's claims about the tamper-proof arrangement, tests actual controls rather than policy documentation, and produces findings that the Board and BNM can rely upon as objective assurance.
requirements.owner: Chief Audit Executive (CAE) / Head of Internal Audit
requirements.frequency: Annual audit cycle; findings tracked to closure quarterly

requirements.requirement: Extend vendor risk management and contractual requirements to all third-party providers involved in the backup and recovery chain (e.g., cloud storage vendors, managed backup service providers), requiring them to demonstrate compliance with tamper-proof and isolation standards through contractual SLAs, audit rights, and evidence of certifications.
requirements.rationale: Financial institutions frequently rely on third parties for backup infrastructure. The clause imposes the obligation on the institution regardless of whether controls are insourced or outsourced. Third-party providers that do not meet tamper-proof and isolation standards create compliance gaps that are attributed to the institution under BNM's outsourcing and technology risk frameworks.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Vendor Risk Management
requirements.frequency: Annual vendor assessment; triggered by contract renewal or material change in vendor service

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal post-incident review process for any activation of the isolated recovery environment or any ransomware event, requiring a root cause analysis, recovery timeline assessment against RTOs, and a lessons-learned report submitted to senior management and the Board Risk Committee within 30 days of incident resolution.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires that recovery arrangements enable resumption 'within its tolerable level.' Post-incident governance closes the loop between design intent and actual performance, identifies whether tolerances were met, and drives continuous improvement. BNM expects evidence that institutions learn from incidents and update their controls accordingly.
requirements.frequency: Per incident; minimum annual review of process even if no activations occurred

business.summary: Financial institutions that delegate critical technology functions to third parties without maintaining active board-level oversight expose themselves to unmanaged operational, reputational, and regulatory risk. The clause makes clear that outsourcing does not transfer accountability — the institution is fully liable for failures in security or reliability caused by third party providers. Non-compliance risks regulatory sanction from BNM, service disruptions affecting customers, and reputational damage that erodes market confidence.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formal inventory of all third party service providers engaged for critical technology functions and systems, classified by criticality tier, with clear documentation of the services rendered and associated risk exposure.
requirements.rationale: The clause specifically scopes obligations to 'critical technology functions and systems'. Without a classified inventory, the institution cannot demonstrate effective oversight or identify where its accountability obligations are highest. BNM requires this as the foundational step before any risk management can occur.
requirements.frequency: Continuous — updated upon each new engagement, renewal, or material change; formally reviewed Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Ensure all contracts with critical technology third party service providers contain explicit clauses binding them to comply with all applicable BNM RMiT requirements, including security standards, audit rights, incident notification obligations, and business continuity commitments.
requirements.rationale: The clause states the institution must ensure 'compliance with all relevant regulatory requirements prescribed in this policy document'. Contractual obligations are the primary commercial mechanism through which the institution can hold providers accountable and demonstrate to BNM that regulatory requirements flow down to the supply chain.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) and Chief Legal Officer (CLO)
requirements.frequency: Per-event — at contract formation, material amendment, and renewal; annual review of existing contracts for adequacy

requirements.requirement: Conduct formal pre-engagement due diligence on prospective critical technology service providers, assessing financial viability, operational resilience, security posture, regulatory track record, and geographic risk concentration before any engagement is approved.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to 'address associated risks when engaging' providers — use of 'when engaging' indicates risk must be assessed and addressed prior to, not after, engagement. Failure to conduct pre-engagement due diligence means risks are assumed rather than managed, directly contradicting the institution's retained accountability.
requirements.frequency: Per-event — mandatory prior to each new critical technology engagement; re-assessed upon material changes to provider circumstances

requirements.requirement: Define and enforce minimum service level agreements (SLAs) for security incident response, system availability, and reliability thresholds for all critical technology third party providers, with contractual remedies and escalation rights in the event of breach.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires the institution to ensure 'security and reliability of technology services'. SLAs are the operational mechanism through which these standards are set and enforced externally. Without enforceable SLAs, the institution cannot guarantee the reliability standard the clause demands, nor can it evidence oversight to BNM.
requirements.frequency: Per-event — defined at contract inception; performance reviewed Monthly; formal SLA adequacy review Annually

requirements.requirement: Maintain exit strategy and transition plans for each critical technology third party provider, ensuring the institution can migrate or recover critical functions within defined recovery time objectives without dependency on a single provider.
requirements.rationale: The institution 'remains accountable for managing all risks' including concentration and exit risk. Over-reliance on a single critical provider without an exit plan means the institution cannot fulfil its accountability for service continuity, and exposes customers and the financial system to systemic risk if a provider fails or withdraws.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) in coordination with CRO
requirements.frequency: Annually reviewed and tested; updated upon any material change to critical provider arrangements

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement controls that extend the institution's security and operational visibility into third party environments, ensuring that delegated functions do not become blind spots in the risk posture. This includes continuous monitoring, access control enforcement, and independent verification of provider security and resilience. The clause's retention of full institutional accountability means technology controls must be capable of detecting and responding to third party failures without relying solely on the provider's self-reporting.

requirements.requirement: Implement continuous monitoring of all critical technology third party integrations, including API connections, data pipelines, and hosted services, using automated tooling that generates real-time alerts on availability degradation, anomalous behaviour, or security events attributable to the third party.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to ensure 'security and reliability of technology services' provided by third parties. Continuous monitoring is the technical mechanism that transforms passive contractual obligations into active operational oversight, enabling the institution to detect and respond to provider-side failures independently.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) and Head of IT Operations
requirements.frequency: Continuous — monitoring operates 24/7; alert thresholds and monitoring scope reviewed Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Enforce least-privilege access controls and privileged access management (PAM) for all third party personnel and systems accessing the institution's environments, with session recording, multi-factor authentication, and time-limited access grants for all critical system touchpoints.
requirements.rationale: Effective oversight of third parties requires that the institution controls the boundaries of access to its critical systems. Without strict access controls, third party personnel represent an uncontrolled attack surface. This directly supports the clause's requirement to 'address associated risks' arising from third party engagement.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; access rights reviewed and recertified Quarterly; PAM configurations audited Annually

requirements.requirement: Integrate critical third party service providers into the institution's incident management and response processes, including defined communication protocols, mandatory breach notification timelines in contracts, and playbooks for third party-originated incidents that include provider isolation procedures.
requirements.rationale: The institution 'remains accountable for managing all risks' including those that originate in a third party environment. Without integrated incident response, a provider-side breach or outage cannot be effectively contained or remediated. This ensures the institution can respond to — and be seen by BNM to be responding to — third party incidents as if they were internal events.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Operations and CISO
requirements.frequency: Playbooks reviewed Semi-annually; joint incident response exercises conducted Annually with each critical provider

requirements.requirement: Conduct periodic independent technical assessments of critical third party providers, including vulnerability assessments, penetration testing where contractually permitted, and review of provider-issued security certifications (e.g., ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II), to independently verify security posture beyond self-attestation.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to ensure compliance with security requirements — passive reliance on provider self-attestation is insufficient for an institution that 'remains accountable'. Independent technical verification gives the institution and BNM confidence that security standards are operationally maintained, not just contractually promised.
requirements.frequency: Annually at minimum; upon material change to provider technology or ownership; following any security incident involving the provider

requirements.requirement: Maintain a data flow and system dependency map for all critical technology third party providers, documenting data classifications, cross-boundary data flows, system interconnections, and regulatory data residency obligations, updated whenever a new provider is onboarded or integration scope changes.
requirements.rationale: Effective oversight and risk management, as required by the clause, is impossible without a clear technical picture of what data and systems are exposed to third parties. This map is also essential for BNM examination preparedness, demonstrating the institution's ability to identify and manage 'all risks that arise from engagement of third party service providers'.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Architecture and CISO
requirements.frequency: Continuous — updated per-event upon any change; formally reviewed and validated Annually

governance.summary: The clause directly names the board and senior management as responsible parties, placing governance obligations at the highest levels of the institution. Effective governance requires structured oversight bodies, formal reporting lines, documented policies, and regular assurance mechanisms that give the board independent visibility into third party risk. Governance frameworks must ensure accountability is retained institutionally even as operational functions are delegated to external providers.

requirements.requirement: The board must formally approve a Third Party Risk Management (TPRM) Policy that defines the institution's framework for engaging, monitoring, and exiting critical technology service providers, including risk appetite thresholds, escalation criteria, and minimum due diligence standards aligned to BNM RMiT.
requirements.rationale: The clause states 'the board and senior management must exercise effective oversight' — board-level policy approval is the foundational governance act that demonstrates this oversight is institutionalised rather than ad hoc. Without a board-approved TPRM policy, the institution has no formal basis for consistently managing third party risks across business units.
requirements.owner: Board of Directors (endorsed by Board Risk Committee)
requirements.frequency: Policy approved at inception; reviewed and reaffirmed Annually; updated upon material regulatory change or significant third party risk event

requirements.requirement: Establish a Third Party Risk Management Committee or designate an existing senior management committee with explicit responsibility and terms of reference to oversee critical technology third party engagements, including authority to approve or reject high-risk providers and escalate to the board.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'effective oversight' from both the board and senior management. A dedicated oversight body with clear terms of reference ensures that senior management oversight is structured and documented, not informal. This committee provides the institutional mechanism through which the board's risk appetite is operationalised in third party decisions.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) as Chair; members include CTO, CISO, COO, and CLO
requirements.frequency: Committee meets Quarterly at minimum; convened on an ad hoc basis for critical engagements or incidents

requirements.requirement: Implement a regular board and senior management reporting cycle on critical technology third party risk, including portfolio-level risk exposure, SLA performance, material incidents, concentration risk metrics, and status of remediation actions, presented using a standardised dashboard.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates the board and senior management 'exercise effective oversight' — oversight without information is not oversight. Regular structured reporting ensures that accountability at the top of the institution is substantive and evidence-based, and creates an audit trail demonstrating to BNM that the board is genuinely engaged with third party risk.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) — report prepared by TPRM function, presented to Board Risk Committee
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting to senior management; Semi-annual reporting to the Board; ad hoc escalation for Critical incidents

requirements.requirement: Establish a mandatory notification and escalation protocol requiring that any material third party security incident, service reliability failure, or regulatory breach by a critical provider is escalated to senior management within 24 hours and to the board within 72 hours, with a defined post-incident accountability review.
requirements.rationale: The institution 'remains accountable for managing all risks' — accountability is only meaningful if the board and senior management are informed of material events in time to exercise oversight and direction. Delayed escalation prevents effective governance response and may itself constitute a regulatory breach if BNM notification obligations under this policy document are triggered.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) — escalation obligations enforced by Company Secretary for board notifications
requirements.frequency: Per-event — triggered by defined materiality thresholds; protocol reviewed Annually

requirements.requirement: Commission an independent internal audit review of the Third Party Risk Management framework at least annually, covering adherence to the TPRM Policy, effectiveness of due diligence processes, monitoring controls, and contractual compliance, with findings reported directly to the Board Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: Independent assurance is the mechanism through which the board can verify that senior management's oversight activities are effective and not merely procedural. The clause requires 'effective oversight' — internal audit provides the board with independent evidence that the governance framework is functioning as intended and that the institution's accountability obligations are being discharged.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit — findings reported to Board Audit Committee
requirements.frequency: Annually; targeted reviews triggered by material incidents or significant new critical provider engagements

business.summary: Financial institutions that fail to conduct structured due diligence on third party service providers risk undetected performance degradation, supply chain compromise, and recovery failures that directly impair their ability to meet service obligations and recovery time objectives. The clause mandates that due diligence is not a one-time gate but a continuous control that must adapt to an evolving risk environment. Non-compliance exposes the institution to regulatory censure, reputational damage, and operational disruption cascading from a third party's failure.

requirements.requirement: Establish and enforce a mandatory pre-onboarding due diligence gate for all third party service providers before any contract is executed or service engagement commences, covering at minimum all risk categories in Appendix 8.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly requires due diligence 'prior to its on-boarding or engagement'. Without a formal pre-onboarding gate, the institution assumes unvetted risk from day one of a service relationship, potentially impairing business performance and recovery objectives from the outset.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (each new third party engagement)

requirements.requirement: Define and document measurable business performance and recovery objectives for each third party service provider engagement, and validate that the provider's capabilities meet those objectives as part of the due diligence process.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires that due diligence ensures 'achievement of business performance and recovery objectives remain unimpaired'. Without pre-defined, measurable objectives, there is no baseline against which to assess whether a third party's performance or a change in the risk environment has impaired the institution.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (at onboarding) and Annual review

requirements.requirement: Implement a continuous third party performance monitoring programme throughout the full duration of each service engagement, with defined triggers for re-assessment when performance metrics or the risk environment changes materially.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates due diligence 'throughout the service engagement', not only at onboarding. A point-in-time assessment cannot capture evolving third party risk; continuous monitoring ensures performance and recovery objectives remain unimpaired over time.
requirements.frequency: Continuous, with formal periodic reviews (at minimum Quarterly)

requirements.requirement: Require third party service providers to disclose material changes in their risk profile, ownership, financial stability, sub-contractors, or operating environment, with contractual obligations to notify the institution promptly upon any such change.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates consideration of 'the latest risk environment'. The institution cannot assess the current risk environment of a third party without contractual mechanisms compelling disclosure of material changes, which directly affect the validity of prior due diligence findings.
requirements.owner: Chief Procurement Officer / Head of Vendor Management
requirements.frequency: Continuous (event-driven), with Annual contract review

requirements.requirement: Maintain a comprehensive and current inventory of all third party service providers, categorised by criticality and risk tier, to ensure no provider operates outside the due diligence framework and oversight obligations are proportionate to risk.
requirements.rationale: Due diligence obligations apply to all third party service providers. Without a complete inventory, the institution cannot demonstrate compliance with the pre-onboarding and ongoing due diligence requirements, and critical dependencies may remain unmonitored.
requirements.frequency: Continuous maintenance, Quarterly reconciliation

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement platforms and tooling that operationalise both the pre-onboarding due diligence gate and continuous monitoring obligations, covering the full Appendix 8 risk taxonomy for every third party service provider. Systems must provide auditable records of assessments, automate risk signal ingestion from external and internal sources, and integrate with recovery testing processes to validate that third party-dependent recovery objectives remain achievable. Static spreadsheet-based assessments are insufficient; the risk environment requirement demands near-real-time intelligence feeds and automated alerting.

requirements.requirement: Deploy a Third Party Risk Management (TPRM) platform that enforces a mandatory pre-onboarding assessment workflow covering all Appendix 8 risk categories, preventing contract execution or system access provisioning until the assessment is completed and approved.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim requirement for due diligence 'prior to on-boarding' requires a technical control that makes bypass impossible. A TPRM platform with integration into procurement and identity/access management systems enforces this as a hard gate rather than a procedural checklist.
requirements.frequency: Per-event implementation; platform reviewed Annually

requirements.requirement: Integrate the TPRM platform with external threat intelligence feeds, financial stability data sources, and cyber risk scoring services to continuously ingest signals that reflect the 'latest risk environment' for each active third party, with automated alerting when risk thresholds are breached.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires due diligence to consider 'the latest risk environment' on an ongoing basis. Manual periodic reviews alone cannot satisfy this; automated intelligence ingestion enables the institution to detect material changes in a third party's risk posture between scheduled review cycles.
requirements.frequency: Continuous ingestion; alert thresholds reviewed Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement technical controls to assess and validate the information security posture of each third party, including vulnerability scanning results, penetration test reports, ISO 27001 or equivalent certification status, and security configuration audits, mapped to the Appendix 8 risk categories.
requirements.rationale: Appendix 8 includes technology and cybersecurity risks among the mandatory risk categories. Technical security assessments of third parties provide objective evidence that the institution has actively evaluated risks that could impair business performance or compromise shared systems and data.
requirements.frequency: Pre-onboarding and Annual (or upon material change)

requirements.requirement: Integrate third party service provider dependencies into the institution's Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery testing programme, including periodic failover and recovery drills that validate recovery time and recovery point objectives can be met when a third party is unavailable or degraded.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires that 'recovery objectives remain unimpaired'. Technical validation through actual recovery tests—not only paper-based assessments—is the only reliable method to confirm that third party dependencies do not create hidden recovery bottlenecks.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Operations / Head of Business Continuity
requirements.frequency: Annual minimum; Critical providers tested Semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Maintain a tamper-evident, auditable digital record of all due diligence assessments, risk ratings, approvals, ongoing monitoring results, and remediation actions for each third party, accessible to internal audit and regulators upon request.
requirements.rationale: BNM expects financial institutions to demonstrate compliance with 'S' marker clauses through documented evidence. Auditable records prove that both pre-onboarding and ongoing due diligence obligations have been fulfilled and provide a chronological record showing adaptation to the evolving risk environment.
requirements.frequency: Continuous record maintenance; retention per BNM guidelines

governance.summary: The governance framework must institutionalise third party due diligence as a structured, policy-driven obligation with clear ownership at the senior management and Board level, ensuring that risk appetite, Appendix 8 risk categories, and escalation pathways are formally embedded in policy and enforced through committee oversight. Ongoing due diligence must be a standing agenda item at appropriate governance forums, not an ad hoc activity, so that material changes in the third party risk environment are escalated and acted upon in a timely manner. The Board and senior management bear ultimate accountability for ensuring that third party relationships do not impair the institution's performance and recovery objectives.

requirements.requirement: Develop, approve, and maintain a Board-endorsed Third Party Risk Management Policy that mandates pre-onboarding and ongoing due diligence for all third party service providers, explicitly incorporates all Appendix 8 risk categories as minimum assessment criteria, and defines risk appetite thresholds that determine engagement, conditional engagement, or rejection decisions.
requirements.rationale: The clause uses 'must', establishing a non-discretionary obligation. A Board-approved policy anchors this obligation at the highest governance level, ensures consistent application across all business units, and provides the regulatory evidence of institutional commitment required by BNM.
requirements.owner: Board of Directors / Board Risk Committee
requirements.frequency: Annual review and re-approval; ad hoc upon regulatory or risk environment changes

requirements.requirement: Establish a Third Party Risk Oversight Committee or formally assign third party risk oversight responsibility to an existing senior management committee, with a defined mandate to review due diligence outcomes, approve high-risk engagements, monitor ongoing performance, and escalate material issues to the Board.
requirements.rationale: The ongoing nature of the due diligence obligation ('throughout the service engagement') requires a permanent governance structure with recurring oversight authority, not ad hoc reviews. A named committee ensures accountability and provides a forum for escalation when the risk environment changes materially.
requirements.frequency: Monthly or Quarterly committee meetings; ad hoc for material events

requirements.requirement: Define and document tiered due diligence standards proportionate to third party criticality and risk profile, specifying the minimum scope of assessment, review frequency, and approval authority for each tier, ensuring that all tiers at minimum address the risk categories in Appendix 8.
requirements.rationale: The clause sets a minimum standard ('at a minimum, consider the range of risks outlined in Appendix 8') while implying proportionality through the emphasis on business performance and recovery objectives. Tiered standards enable the institution to apply deeper scrutiny to critical providers without creating disproportionate burden for lower-risk engagements.
requirements.frequency: Tier definitions reviewed Annually; tiering of individual providers reviewed upon material change

requirements.requirement: Require all third party contracts and service agreements to include enforceable clauses obligating the provider to submit to periodic due diligence assessments, provide access rights for audit, disclose material changes in risk profile, and remediate identified deficiencies within agreed timeframes.
requirements.rationale: Ongoing due diligence 'throughout the service engagement' is only meaningful if the institution has contractual rights to obtain the information needed to conduct it. Without contractual access and disclosure obligations, the institution cannot fulfil its regulatory duty to assess the latest risk environment.
requirements.owner: General Counsel / Head of Legal and Compliance
requirements.frequency: Per-event (contract execution and renewal); Annual compliance review of existing contracts

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal escalation and remediation governance process for cases where due diligence findings reveal that a third party poses risks that could impair business performance or recovery objectives, including defined escalation timelines, remediation tracking, and exit or contingency planning obligations.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to ensure that performance and recovery objectives 'remain unimpaired'. Governance must include not only assessment but also the authority and process to act on adverse findings—whether by requiring remediation, imposing enhanced monitoring, or initiating exit procedures—to give effect to the protective intent of the clause.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Operating Officer (COO)
requirements.frequency: Process reviewed Annually; activated per-event upon adverse findings

business.summary: Financial institutions face significant operational, reputational, and regulatory risk when third party service providers fail to meet agreed standards, disrupt services, or mishandle customer data. Clause 10.48 mandates that SLAs contractually bind third parties across nine minimum domains — from regulator access rights to cyber incident disclosure — ensuring that outsourced arrangements do not dilute the institution's obligations to BNM or its customers. Non-compliance exposes the institution to regulatory enforcement, reputational damage, data breaches, and operational failures that cannot be contractually remedied after the fact.

requirements.requirement: Establish a mandatory SLA baseline template covering all nine minimum clauses (10.48(a)–(i)) that must be incorporated into every third party service provider engagement, with no exceptions permitted without documented Board-approved rationale.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text states the institution 'must establish a Service Level Agreement' — the word 'must' renders this a non-negotiable Standard obligation. Failure to include all nine minimum elements in any active engagement constitutes a direct regulatory breach that cannot be cured retrospectively without renegotiation.
requirements.frequency: Per-engagement (new contracts); Annual review of existing contracts

requirements.requirement: Negotiate and enforce regulator access rights in all SLAs, granting BNM and any BNM-appointed examiner unrestricted access to records, files, data, management information, and minutes of all consultative and decision-making processes held by the third party.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.48(a) explicitly requires access rights for 'the regulator and any party appointed by the financial institution.' BNM must be able to supervise the institution's operations even when conducted through third parties; absence of this clause legally impedes BNM examinations and constitutes a material SLA deficiency.
requirements.frequency: Per-engagement; verified at each contract renewal

requirements.requirement: Require third party service providers to provide sufficient prior written notice before engaging in any substantial sub-contracting, with 'substantial' defined quantitatively (e.g., by value threshold or scope of customer data involved) within the SLA.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.48(b) mandates 'sufficient prior notice' for substantial sub-contracting. Without a clear contractual definition of 'substantial' and associated notice periods, the institution cannot monitor its extended supply chain or assess whether sub-contractors introduce unacceptable risk to customer data and operational integrity.
requirements.owner: Chief Procurement Officer / Head of Vendor Management
requirements.frequency: Per-event (triggered by sub-contracting notification); Annual supply chain audit

requirements.requirement: Embed business continuity and exit provisions in all SLAs that guarantee timely recovery and repatriation of all institution-held data from third parties upon contract termination or service failure, with defined Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs) for data retrieval.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.48(f) requires 'arrangements to secure business continuity in the event of an exit or termination' and explicitly mandates that 'data residing in third party service providers are recoverable in a timely manner.' Data lock-in or delayed repatriation during a disorderly exit creates direct customer harm and regulatory breach.
requirements.frequency: Per-engagement; tested Annually via exit simulation exercises

requirements.requirement: Mandate prompt disclosure and notification obligations in all SLAs requiring third party service providers to report any service disruption or cyber incident affecting the institution or its customer data — including incidents within sub-contractors' environments — within a defined notification window aligned with BNM's cyber incident reporting requirements.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.48(g) requires third parties to 'promptly disclose and notify' the institution of disruptions or cyber incidents. Without a contractually enforceable notification window, the institution cannot meet its own reporting obligations to BNM and JPDP under the Personal Data Protection Act 2010, and cannot initiate incident response in a timely manner.
requirements.frequency: Per-event; clause reviewed Annually

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement systems and controls to monitor, enforce, and evidence SLA compliance across the entire third party lifecycle — from onboarding through exit. This includes technical capabilities to grant auditable regulator access, monitor uptime against agreed objectives, ensure disaster recovery and backup systems are operable, facilitate timely data recovery on exit, and integrate third party incident reporting into the institution's security operations. These are not passive contractual provisions but active technical obligations requiring continuous tooling and process support.

requirements.requirement: Implement a Third Party SLA monitoring platform that continuously tracks service level objectives for uptime and availability (clause 10.48(e)) against contractually agreed thresholds for all material third party services, generating automated alerts and audit-ready dashboards for compliance reporting.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.48(e) mandates 'service level objectives for uptime or availability' — these cannot be verified without automated, real-time monitoring. Manual sampling is insufficient for evidencing continuous SLA adherence to BNM examiners and provides no early warning for service degradation.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; Monthly SLA performance reports; Annual platform review

requirements.requirement: Establish and validate disaster recovery and backup capability arrangements with each applicable third party service provider (clause 10.48(d)), including integration testing of the third party's DR environment with the institution's own recovery procedures, and documented evidence of test outcomes.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.48(d) requires 'arrangements for disaster recovery and backup capability, where applicable.' An SLA clause alone is insufficient — DR provisions must be technically validated to confirm they function as contractually specified. Untested DR arrangements discovered during an actual disaster cannot be remedied in real time.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Chief Technology Officer (CTO)
requirements.frequency: Per-engagement setup; Annual DR test with each applicable third party

requirements.requirement: Design and document a data repatriation architecture for each material third party engagement that enables full, verified recovery of all institution and customer data within SLA-defined RTOs and RPOs upon contract exit or termination (clause 10.48(f)), using structured data formats that eliminate proprietary lock-in.
