requirements.rationale: Clause 10.48(f) explicitly requires that 'data residing in third party service providers are recoverable in a timely manner.' Proprietary data formats, opaque storage architectures, and undefined handover procedures are common causes of failed exits. Technical due diligence must confirm data portability before contract signature.
requirements.frequency: Per-engagement design; Annual exit simulation exercise; Pre-exit execution

requirements.requirement: Provision and maintain a secure, auditable access pathway for BNM examiners and institution-appointed auditors to access all records, files, data, management information, and decision-making minutes held by third party service providers (clause 10.48(a)), with full access logging, identity verification, and role-based access controls.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.48(a) grants the regulator access rights that must be technically implementable upon demand. Without a pre-configured, tested access pathway, the institution cannot comply with an on-site or remote BNM examination at short notice — constituting an obstruction of regulatory supervision regardless of contractual intent.
requirements.frequency: Per-engagement provisioning; Annual access pathway test; Per-examination activation

requirements.requirement: Integrate third party service providers into the institution's Security Operations Centre (SOC) incident notification workflow so that cyber incidents and service disruptions reported by third parties (clause 10.48(g)) — including those originating in sub-contractors' environments — are automatically triaged, logged, and escalated within defined SLA notification windows.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.48(g) requires third parties to 'promptly disclose and notify' the institution. A technical integration ensures notifications are received, timestamped, and acted upon — rather than lost in email chains. This directly supports the institution's downstream BNM cyber incident reporting obligations and PDPA breach notification timelines.
requirements.frequency: Per-engagement onboarding; Continuous operational readiness; Annual SOC integration test

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that SLA commitments are not merely contractual boilerplate but are actively owned, reviewed, and enforced across the institution's third party lifecycle. This requires a formal vendor governance framework, Board-level oversight of material outsourcing arrangements, a confidentiality and secrecy undertaking process that survives contract termination, and a structured programme to ensure third parties meet competency and security awareness standards. Oversight must be documented, periodic, and evidenced for BNM examination.

requirements.requirement: Establish a Third Party Governance Framework with a designated Vendor Management Committee responsible for approving, reviewing, and monitoring all material third party SLAs against the nine minimum requirements of clause 10.48, with documented committee minutes and escalation paths to the Board Risk Committee for critical findings.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.48 imposes nine distinct minimum SLA requirements — no single function can oversee all dimensions without a structured governance body. A Vendor Management Committee ensures cross-functional accountability (legal, risk, IT, compliance, operations) and provides BNM with evidence of active institutional oversight rather than passive contract management.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Board Risk Committee
requirements.frequency: Quarterly Committee meetings; Annual Framework review; Per-event escalation for material breaches

requirements.requirement: Implement a standardised written confidentiality and secrecy undertaking process requiring all third party service providers to execute a legally binding undertaking — prior to commencement of services — affirming compliance with secrecy provisions under the Financial Services Act 2013, Islamic Financial Services Act 2013, and PDPA 2010, with explicit provisions surviving contract termination (clause 10.48(c)).
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.48(c) requires 'a written undertaking by the third party service provider on its compliance with secrecy provisions' and mandates that confidentiality obligations apply 'even after the engagement has ended.' Without a standalone, post-termination obligation, customer data and institutional information remain unprotected after a third party relationship ends — exposing the institution to PDPA liability and FSA/IFSA secrecy breaches.
requirements.frequency: Per-engagement (pre-commencement); Reviewed at each contract renewal; Enforceable post-termination

requirements.requirement: Establish a Sub-Contractor Oversight Policy that defines the threshold for 'substantial' sub-contracting (clause 10.48(b)), specifies required notice periods, mandates risk assessment of proposed sub-contractors, and requires written institution approval before substantial sub-contracting commences.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.48(b) requires 'sufficient prior notice' for substantial sub-contracting but does not prescribe the definition of 'substantial' — the institution must define this operationally. Without a policy, the institution cannot consistently apply the requirement across engagements, assess supply chain risk, or demonstrate regulatory compliance when BNM examines sub-contractor management.
requirements.owner: Head of Vendor Management / Chief Procurement Officer
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed Annually; Applied Per-event (upon sub-contracting notification)

requirements.requirement: Implement a Third Party Competency and Certification Register that tracks each third party service provider's compliance with relevant internationally recognised standards (e.g., ISO 27001, PCI DSS, SOC 2) and the currency of key staff certifications (clause 10.48(h)), with annual re-attestation requirements and escalation for non-compliance.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.48(h) requires third parties to 'comply with the relevant internationally recognised standards and ensure their key staff keep their skills up-to-date with the relevant certifications.' Without a governance register and annual re-attestation, the institution cannot evidence ongoing compliance — certifications lapse, and standards evolve. BNM examiners expect documentary proof, not contractual assertions.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of IT Governance
requirements.frequency: Annual re-attestation; Continuous register maintenance; Per-event update on certification changes

requirements.requirement: Develop and administer a formal Security Awareness and Education Programme that includes third party service provider personnel where appropriate (clause 10.48(i)), with documented participation records, a defined curriculum aligned to the institution's security policies, and an annual review of programme effectiveness.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.48(i) requires third parties to 'participate in the financial institution's security awareness and education program, where appropriate.' Third party personnel who handle customer data or access institution systems are a primary vector for social engineering and insider threats. Including them in security awareness training reduces human risk across the extended enterprise and evidences a proactive security culture to BNM.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Human Resources
requirements.frequency: Annual programme delivery; Per-engagement onboarding inclusion; Annual effectiveness review

business.summary: Financial institutions face compounding operational and reputational risk when third party service providers introduce unmonitored cybersecurity exposures — particularly where customer data or critical IT infrastructure is accessible. Clause 10.49 mandates that institutions move beyond periodic reviews to continuous, real-time visibility of third party cybersecurity posture, backed by a formal roadmap and automated monitoring. Failure to comply exposes the institution to regulatory censure, undetected supply-chain breaches, and delayed incident containment that magnifies customer harm.

requirements.requirement: Formulate and maintain a Board-approved roadmap with defined milestones for achieving continuous, real-time monitoring of all material third party service providers' cybersecurity posture, covering the full lifecycle from assessment to incident response.
requirements.rationale: The opening sentence of Clause 10.49 explicitly requires a 'roadmap to achieve continuous monitoring.' Without a documented, time-bound plan, the institution cannot demonstrate regulatory intent or progress toward the standard, and BNM supervisory reviews will identify the absence as a gap.
requirements.frequency: Annual review and refresh; milestone reporting Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Conduct regular measurement and inventory of IT infrastructure components and customer information repositories accessible to each third party, and actively manage the resulting external attack surface exposures to reduce cyber-attack vectors.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.49(a) requires the institution to 'measure the IT infrastructure footprint and the customer information accessible to third parties' and 'regularly manage this external stress state exposures.' Unmanaged third party access paths represent direct vectors for data exfiltration and are a key regulator concern under RMiT's customer data protection obligations.
requirements.frequency: Continuous measurement; formal review Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Classify and maintain a prioritised register of third party service providers by criticality and risk profile, assigning each a required assurance assertion frequency and aligning contractual SLAs to enforce compliance.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.49(d) requires the institution to 'define a priority set of security controls that require more frequent assurance assertion from the third party service providers.' Without a tiered classification and contractual backing, higher-risk third parties may self-report at intervals insufficient to surface emerging risks.
requirements.owner: Chief Procurement Officer / Head of Vendor Management
requirements.frequency: Annual classification review; assurance assertions per assigned tier (Monthly to Quarterly)

requirements.requirement: Integrate third party incident response obligations into all material vendor contracts, including mandatory disclosure timelines for technology and cyber incidents, software vulnerability notifications, and coordinated containment protocols.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.49(c) requires incident response plans to 'incorporate protocols with third party service providers to detect and contain adverse impact of security vulnerabilities resulting from software updates.' Contractual gaps leave the institution unable to enforce disclosure or invoke coordinated response, delaying containment and prolonging customer impact.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Legal & Compliance
requirements.frequency: Embedded at contract inception; reviewed at Annual contract renewal

requirements.requirement: Establish and operationalise an escalation and remediation framework that prescribes business-level response actions when third party security thresholds are breached, including escalation paths to senior management and the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.49(g) requires 'processes to respond to breached thresholds, including investigating failed assertions and remedying control gaps.' A purely technical response without defined business escalation paths risks delayed executive awareness and inadequate resource allocation to remediate material control failures.
requirements.frequency: Framework review Annually; activation Per-event

technology.summary: Technology teams must build and operate a continuous third party risk monitoring stack that spans attack surface discovery, automated security control testing, real-time threat intelligence ingestion, and threshold-triggered alerting. The clause explicitly mandates technology solutions to automate metric testing (10.49(f)), eliminating reliance on manual or periodic point-in-time assessments. Implementations must integrate with the institution's incident response toolchain to enable rapid detection and containment when third party security posture degrades.

requirements.requirement: Deploy a third party attack surface management (ASM) platform capable of continuously enumerating and monitoring the external IT infrastructure footprint of all material third party service providers, including exposed services, certificates, and IP ranges linked to the institution.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.49(a) requires continuous measurement of 'IT infrastructure footprint' accessible to third parties. Manual discovery cannot achieve the real-time visibility mandated by the clause; an automated ASM platform provides continuous enumeration and change detection necessary to manage external attack surface exposures.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Cyber Defence
requirements.frequency: Continuous scanning; findings reviewed Weekly

requirements.requirement: Implement a Data Access Governance (DAG) or Cloud Access Security Broker (CASB) solution to continuously identify and classify customer information repositories accessible by third parties, generating automated exposure reports and triggering alerts on anomalous access patterns.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.49(a) explicitly requires measuring 'customer information accessible to third parties.' Without automated data access visibility, the institution cannot quantify the customer data risk surface or demonstrate to BNM that it manages this exposure in near-real-time.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Security / Data Protection Officer (DPO)
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; exception reports reviewed Daily

requirements.requirement: Implement automated metric testing technology — including security ratings platforms, automated control assurance testing APIs, and scripted assertion frameworks — to continuously validate the security control posture of prioritised third party service providers against defined benchmarks.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.49(f) explicitly requires the institution to 'implement technology solutions to automate metric testing.' Manual questionnaire-based assurance cycles are insufficient to achieve the assurance frequency required under 10.49(d). Automated testing enables objective, frequent, and scalable control validation across the third party portfolio.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Head of Cyber Risk Technology
requirements.frequency: Automated testing Continuous or Weekly per control tier; results reviewed Monthly

requirements.requirement: Integrate threat intelligence feeds — including vendor-specific vulnerability disclosures, CERT advisories, dark web monitoring, and ISAC feeds — into the Security Operations Centre (SOC) SIEM to monitor technology and cyber incident information disclosed by third party service providers at elevated frequency.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.49(e) requires 'monitoring technology and cyber incidents information disclosed by third party service providers at a higher frequency.' Manual monitoring of vendor security bulletins and public disclosures is insufficient; automated ingestion into the SIEM enables correlation with internal telemetry and accelerates detection of supply-chain threats.
requirements.frequency: Continuous ingestion; threat analysis Daily

requirements.requirement: Configure automated threshold alerting and workflow orchestration within the monitoring platform to trigger investigation and remediation tickets when third party security metrics breach defined thresholds, including integration with ITSM tools (e.g., ServiceNow) for failed assertion tracking.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.49(g) requires 'processes to respond to breached thresholds, including investigating failed assertions and remedying control gaps.' Technology automation of threshold breach detection and ticket creation ensures no failed assertion is lost to manual oversight, and creates an auditable remediation trail for BNM examination.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Head of Cyber Defence
requirements.frequency: Configuration reviewed Quarterly; alerting operates Continuously

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain software supply chain security controls, including Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) tracking and automated vulnerability scanning of third party software components, to detect and contain security vulnerabilities introduced through software updates from service providers.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.49(c) requires incident response plans to address 'security vulnerabilities resulting from software updates.' SBOM-based monitoring and automated patch vulnerability scanning provide the technical foundation to detect supply-chain software risks — as demonstrated by incidents such as SolarWinds — before they propagate into production environments.
requirements.frequency: SBOM updated at each release; vulnerability scans Continuous

governance.summary: Governance structures must institutionalise continuous third party cybersecurity monitoring as a standing risk management discipline rather than a project-level initiative. This requires Board and senior management accountability for the monitoring roadmap, formally defined assurance assertion tiers for the third party portfolio, and embedded third party protocols within the institution's incident response governance. BNM's 'S' marker designation makes these governance controls mandatory and subject to direct supervisory examination.

requirements.requirement: Develop, obtain Board Risk Committee approval for, and publish annually a Third Party Cybersecurity Continuous Monitoring Roadmap, with defined milestones, resource commitments, KPIs, and named accountable owners for each phase.
requirements.rationale: The clause opening directly mandates a 'roadmap to achieve continuous monitoring.' Board-level approval and published milestones ensure executive accountability and provide BNM with a structured artefact to assess the institution's progress and commitment. Absence of a Board-approved roadmap is a direct regulatory gap.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Annual approval; Quarterly milestone progress reporting to Board

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Third Party Risk Tiering Policy that formally classifies all service providers by criticality, defines the priority set of security controls applicable to each tier, specifies the required assurance assertion frequency per tier, and mandates escalation actions when assertions fail.
requirements.rationale: Clauses 10.49(d) and (g) require the institution to 'define a priority set of security controls that require more frequent assurance assertion' and to respond to 'failed assertions.' Without a formal tiering policy, the institution cannot consistently determine which controls require elevated frequency or demonstrate to BNM that its prioritisation rationale is risk-based and documented.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Third Party Risk Management
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed Annually; third party tier assignments reviewed Semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Formally embed third party service provider protocols within the institution's Cyber Incident Response Plan (CIRP), including defined roles, communication trees, escalation thresholds, and joint containment procedures specifically addressing software update-related vulnerabilities.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.49(c) requires that 'incident response plans incorporate protocols with third party service providers to detect and contain adverse impact of security vulnerabilities resulting from software updates.' A CIRP that does not explicitly address third party-initiated incidents leaves the institution without governance authority to invoke third party cooperation during a crisis.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Operational Risk
requirements.frequency: CIRP reviewed and tested Annually; updated within 30 days of material third party change

requirements.requirement: Establish a Third Party Cyber Incident Disclosure Governance Framework that defines the institution's process for receiving, logging, escalating, and acting on technology and cyber incident disclosures from third party service providers, including minimum disclosure frequency standards embedded in vendor contracts.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.49(e) requires the institution to 'monitor technology and cyber incidents information disclosed by third party service providers at a higher frequency.' Without a governance framework specifying how disclosures are received and acted upon, the institution cannot ensure consistent treatment across its third party portfolio or demonstrate to BNM that elevated monitoring is operational.
requirements.owner: Head of Third Party Risk Management / CISO
requirements.frequency: Framework reviewed Annually; disclosure logs reviewed Weekly

requirements.requirement: Adopt and maintain a Third Party Security Policy Register that documents leading security policies and product-specific controls applied to mitigate third party risks, including a gap assessment process to benchmark these controls against industry standards (e.g., ISO 27036, NIST SP 800-161) and regulatory guidance.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.49(b) requires the institution to 'adopt leading security policies and controls to mitigate product-specific third party risks.' A documented policy register with benchmarking evidence demonstrates to BNM that the institution's controls are not merely adequate but reflect industry-leading practice, which is the explicit standard set by this sub-clause.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Information Security Policy
requirements.frequency: Policy register reviewed Annually; gap assessment conducted Semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Establish a Threshold Breach Response and Control Remediation Governance Process with defined SLAs for investigation initiation, root cause analysis completion, and control gap remediation, including Board Risk Committee notification thresholds for material or repeated failures.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.49(g) requires 'processes to respond to breached thresholds, including investigating failed assertions and remedying control gaps.' A documented governance process with enforced SLAs and Board notification criteria creates accountability, prevents prolonged exposure to unmitigated third party risks, and provides BNM with an auditable remediation trail.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Third Party Risk Management
requirements.frequency: Process reviewed Annually; breach SLAs enforced Per-event

business.summary: Financial institutions must formally assess and understand the full spectrum of inherent risks before committing to cloud adoption, including commercial, legal, and operational exposure. Failure to conduct this assessment exposes the institution to regulatory censure from BNM, potential data breaches arising from misunderstood shared-infrastructure risks, and inability to exit a cloud arrangement safely. The clause places accountability at the senior leadership level to ensure cloud decisions are risk-informed rather than purely cost-driven.

requirements.requirement: Establish a mandatory pre-cloud-adoption risk assessment process that must be completed and formally approved before any cloud service engagement is authorised. The assessment must evaluate all ten risk dimensions specified in clause 10.50(a) through 10.50(j) and produce a documented risk register with residual risk ratings.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text states the institution 'shall conduct a comprehensive risk assessment prior to cloud adoption' — this is a binding pre-condition, not a post-adoption activity. Without a formalised gating process, cloud projects may proceed without adequate risk visibility, exposing the institution to operational, legal, and regulatory harm.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (prior to each new cloud service adoption or material change to existing cloud services)

requirements.requirement: Conduct a vendor lock-in and application portability assessment for every cloud engagement, including a documented exit strategy that quantifies the cost, timeline, and technical feasibility of migrating data and workloads away from the provider. This assessment must evaluate interoperability with alternative platforms and proprietary dependencies.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.50(e) specifically identifies 'vendor lock-in and application portability or interoperability' as a mandatory risk domain. Commercial dependence on a single cloud provider without a viable exit pathway creates systemic business continuity risk and may impair BNM's ability to effect an orderly resolution of the institution.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (at adoption) and Annual (ongoing review)

requirements.requirement: Assess the geographic location of all cloud infrastructure nodes used for processing or storing the institution's data, explicitly evaluating geo-political instability, cross-border data sovereignty conflicts, and the legal jurisdiction of the cloud provider. Document any scenarios where foreign law could override BNM's supervisory access rights or require disclosure of customer data.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.50(c) mandates assessment of 'geo-political risks and legal risks that may impede compliance with any legal or regulatory requirements.' Malaysia's Financial Services Act 2013 and PDPA impose strict data sovereignty obligations; cloud infrastructure in certain jurisdictions may expose the institution to conflicting legal demands.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) with input from Group Legal Counsel
requirements.frequency: Per-event (at adoption) and upon material changes to provider infrastructure geography

requirements.requirement: Evaluate the commercial and operational implications of cloud service provider termination — whether voluntary, insolvency-driven, or regulatory-forced — and ensure a formal termination risk plan is in place that guarantees the institution's ability to retrieve all data, revoke provider access, and resume operations on alternate infrastructure within defined recovery time objectives.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.50(h) requires assessment of 'termination of a cloud service provider including the ability to secure the financial institution's data following the termination.' The financial consequences of unplanned provider exit without a recovery plan include data loss, service outage, and regulatory breach — each carrying material financial and reputational impact.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (at contract negotiation) and Biennial (simulation/tabletop exercise)

requirements.requirement: Establish a continuous compliance monitoring obligation to verify that cloud services in use continue to meet BNM regulatory requirements and applicable international standards (e.g., ISO/IEC 27017, CSA STAR) on an ongoing basis, not only at the point of adoption. Trigger a re-assessment whenever the regulatory landscape, provider certifications, or service configurations materially change.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.50(j) requires assessment of the institution's 'ability to meet regulatory requirements and international standards on cloud computing on a continuing basis' — the phrase 'continuing basis' makes this an ongoing obligation rather than a one-time checkpoint. Regulatory requirements evolve, and a cloud service compliant today may fall short of future BNM guidelines.
requirements.frequency: Annual (formal review) and Continuous (automated compliance signal monitoring)

technology.summary: Technology teams must operationalise the risk assessment by performing detailed technical due diligence across the cloud deployment stack, covering architecture, migration, security controls, data isolation, and cyber-threat exposure. Each of the ten risk domains in clause 10.50 has a corresponding technical artefact — configuration review, architecture diagram, penetration test result, or data flow map — that must be produced and maintained as evidence. Technology leadership is accountable for ensuring cloud environments are technically configurable to the institution's security standards rather than accepting provider defaults.

requirements.requirement: Assess the sophistication and risk profile of each cloud deployment model under consideration (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) and each service model variant (public, private, hybrid, community cloud), producing a technical risk matrix that maps deployment complexity to the institution's internal security control capability and residual risk tolerance.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.50(a) requires specific assessment of 'sophistication of the deployment model.' More sophisticated or abstract deployment models (e.g., serverless PaaS, multi-cloud SaaS) reduce visibility into underlying infrastructure and may render standard security controls inapplicable, creating hidden residual risk.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of Cloud Architecture
requirements.frequency: Per-event (at adoption) and upon service model changes

requirements.requirement: Conduct a cloud migration risk assessment for any workload proposed for migration from on-premises to cloud infrastructure. The assessment must cover data classification sensitivity, dependency mapping, rollback capability, data integrity validation during transit, and the security control equivalence between the source and target environments.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.50(b) mandates assessment of 'migration of existing systems to cloud infrastructure.' Migration windows represent periods of heightened vulnerability: data in transit may be exposed, access controls may be temporarily relaxed, and rollback failures can result in data loss or extended outage of critical financial systems.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Infrastructure / Cloud Migration Lead
requirements.frequency: Per-event (prior to each migration project)

requirements.requirement: Perform a multi-tenancy isolation and data co-mingling risk assessment for every cloud service, including a technical validation that logical separation controls (e.g., virtualisation boundaries, network segmentation, encryption key segregation) adequately prevent cross-tenant data access. Produce data flow diagrams that explicitly identify where institution data shares physical or logical resources with other tenants.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.50(d) specifically identifies 'multi-tenancy or data co-mingling' as a mandatory risk domain. The verbatim text acknowledges the cloud's inherent 'sharing of resources and services across multiple tenants' — this shared architecture is the primary technical risk vector distinguishing cloud from on-premises deployment.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (at adoption) and Annual (re-validation of isolation controls)

requirements.requirement: Evaluate and document the institution's ability to customise the cloud provider's security configurations to meet internal security baselines and BNM requirements. This includes assessing available controls for encryption (at rest and in transit), key management (including customer-managed keys), identity and access management, network firewall policies, audit logging, and security monitoring integration. Identify any configurations that are provider-managed and cannot be customised.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.50(f) requires assessment of the institution's 'ability to customise security configurations of the cloud infrastructure to ensure a high level of data and technology system protection.' Provider-default configurations are designed for general use and frequently do not satisfy the elevated security baseline required for regulated financial data.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Cybersecurity
requirements.frequency: Per-event (at adoption) and upon provider platform updates that affect available controls

requirements.requirement: Conduct a cyber threat exposure assessment specific to the cloud service provider's infrastructure and supply chain, evaluating the institution's expanded attack surface resulting from shared infrastructure, provider-side vulnerabilities, compromised provider credentials, and API attack vectors. The assessment must include review of the provider's published incident history, third-party penetration test certifications, and the institution's ability to detect and respond to provider-side breaches.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.50(g) mandates assessment of 'exposure to cyber-attacks via cloud service providers.' Financial institutions adopting cloud services inherit a portion of the provider's threat landscape — a breach of the provider's management plane, hypervisor, or shared API gateway can expose all tenants simultaneously, creating a systemic risk not present in dedicated on-premises environments.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (at adoption) and Annual (threat landscape review aligned to provider's audit cycle)

requirements.requirement: Design and technically validate a data recovery and offboarding procedure specific to each cloud engagement, covering bulk data export formats, export API availability, encryption key retrieval, provider-side data deletion certification, and the technical steps required to reconstitute services on alternate infrastructure. Conduct a tabletop or live drill to validate the procedure's feasibility within defined RTO/RPO objectives.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.50(h) requires assessment of 'the ability to secure the financial institution's data following the termination' of a cloud provider. Without a technically validated offboarding procedure, data may be irrecoverable or held hostage to commercial disputes, and the institution may be unable to meet BNM's expectations for operational resilience.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Infrastructure / Business Continuity Manager
requirements.frequency: Per-event (at contract execution) and Biennial (drill/exercise)

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that cloud risk assessments are not solely a technical exercise but are subject to senior management approval, board oversight, and enforceable contractual demarcation with cloud providers. Policies must define who owns each risk domain, how shared responsibility is allocated between the institution and the provider, and how compliance with BNM expectations is monitored and reported through the institution's existing risk governance framework. The ongoing nature of clause 10.50(j) requires embedding cloud risk into regular board and management reporting cycles rather than treating it as a one-time procurement activity.

requirements.requirement: Establish a Board-approved Cloud Risk Management Policy that defines the institution's risk appetite for cloud adoption, mandates the pre-adoption risk assessment process, identifies accountable roles for each of the ten risk domains in clause 10.50, and sets minimum standards for cloud service provider due diligence and ongoing monitoring. The policy must be reviewed whenever BNM issues updated guidance on cloud services.
requirements.rationale: The clause imposes an obligation on the financial institution as a whole ('a financial institution must fully understand…shall conduct') — this institution-level obligation requires a governing policy document that cascades accountability from board to management. Without a board-approved policy, cloud risk management remains ad hoc and cannot be demonstrated to BNM during examination.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Triennial (full review) and event-driven (regulatory updates, material incidents)

requirements.requirement: Ensure every cloud service agreement contains a formally negotiated and documented demarcation of responsibilities, limitations of liability, and audit rights, aligned to a shared responsibility model. The contractual demarcation must explicitly address which security controls are the institution's responsibility versus the provider's, and must preserve the institution's right to conduct or commission independent audits of the provider.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.50(i) requires assessment of 'demarcation of responsibilities, limitations and liability of the cloud service provider.' Ambiguity in shared responsibility is one of the most common root causes of cloud security incidents in regulated industries; without explicit contractual demarcation, the institution may assume controls are in place that are in fact the provider's unmonitored obligation.
requirements.owner: Group Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Group General Counsel
requirements.frequency: Per-event (at contract negotiation and renewal)

requirements.requirement: Integrate cloud service providers into the institution's Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) framework with a cloud-specific risk tier. TPRM reviews for cloud providers must include annual reassessment of the provider's regulatory certifications (e.g., ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, CSA STAR), financial viability, and any material changes to the provider's infrastructure, personnel, or ownership that could affect service continuity or data sovereignty.
requirements.rationale: Clause 10.50(j) requires the ability to 'meet regulatory requirements and international standards on cloud computing on a continuing basis.' A provider's certifications and compliance posture can change — lapsed certifications, failed audits, or ownership changes may render a previously compliant arrangement non-compliant. Embedding cloud providers in TPRM ensures ongoing rather than point-in-time assurance.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Third-Party Risk
requirements.frequency: Annual (scheduled review) and event-driven (material changes to provider)

requirements.requirement: Establish a Cloud Governance Committee (or extend the mandate of an existing Technology Risk Committee) with cross-functional membership from Risk, Technology, Legal, and Compliance. This committee must approve all new cloud adoptions following receipt of the risk assessment, track residual risk remediation actions, and report cloud risk status to the Board Risk Committee at least annually.
requirements.rationale: The breadth of clause 10.50's ten risk domains — spanning commercial, legal, technical, and regulatory dimensions — requires cross-functional oversight that no single function can provide. A committee structure ensures that cloud risk decisions benefit from diverse expert input and that accountability is shared rather than siloed in the IT department.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) (Chair) / Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Chief Compliance Officer (CCO)
requirements.frequency: Quarterly (committee meetings) and Annual (Board Risk Committee reporting)

requirements.requirement: Maintain a live Cloud Services Register that records all cloud services in use, their deployment model, geographic location of data processing and storage, associated risk assessment status, residual risk rating, and scheduled re-assessment dates. The register must be made available to BNM upon request and must be updated within 30 days of any material change to a cloud service engagement.
requirements.rationale: BNM's supervisory framework requires that institutions be able to demonstrate ongoing compliance. A Cloud Services Register provides the evidentiary foundation for all ten risk domains in clause 10.50 and enables the institution to respond promptly to regulatory enquiries. It also surfaces concentration risk across the institution's cloud portfolio (e.g., over-dependence on a single provider across multiple critical systems).
requirements.owner: Chief Compliance Officer (CCO) / Head of Technology Risk
requirements.frequency: Continuous (maintained) and Quarterly (integrity review)

10.51.title: Public Cloud Key Risks and Controls

business.summary: Financial institutions hosting critical systems on public cloud must structurally align their risk management practices with BNM's Appendix 10 benchmarks or bear the burden of demonstrating equivalence. Failure to do so exposes the institution to regulatory censure, potential enforcement action, and reputational damage arising from inadequately controlled cloud-hosted critical systems. The clause creates a direct accountability line from cloud hosting decisions to senior business owners who must justify risk posture to BNM on demand.

requirements.requirement: Maintain a continuously updated register of all systems classified as 'critical' that are hosted on public cloud infrastructure, including the cloud service provider, deployment model, and associated business criticality justification.
requirements.rationale: The clause's obligations are scoped specifically to 'critical systems hosted on a public cloud'. Without a formal critical system inventory tied to cloud hosting, the institution cannot systematically determine which systems fall within scope of Appendix 10 and cannot demonstrate to BNM that the correct controls have been applied.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (updated upon any new cloud deployment or reclassification event)

requirements.requirement: Conduct a formal business impact assessment for each critical system on public cloud that maps identified risks to the Appendix 10 key risk categories, confirming which control measures are adopted and documenting any business rationale for departures.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to 'consider' Appendix 10 risks and controls — this consideration must be documented and defensible. A business impact assessment creates the evidence trail demonstrating that Appendix 10 was actively evaluated, not merely acknowledged.
requirements.frequency: Annual and upon any material change to a critical system's cloud architecture

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal approval process requiring senior business sign-off before any alternative risk management practice that departs from Appendix 10 measures is adopted for a critical system on public cloud, including a pre-approved regulatory justification brief.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly states that institutions relying on alternative practices 'must be prepared to explain and demonstrate to the Bank' their equivalence or superiority. A pre-approved justification brief owned at business level ensures the institution is not caught unprepared during BNM supervisory reviews or on-site examinations.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (prior to adopting any alternative practice) and Annual review thereafter

requirements.requirement: Include public cloud key risk and control compliance (Appendix 10 alignment or documented alternative) as a standing agenda item in the institution's Technology Risk Committee and senior management risk reporting cycle.
requirements.rationale: Embedding Appendix 10 compliance status into regular risk reporting ensures business leadership maintains ongoing visibility of cloud risk posture for critical systems, enabling timely escalation and resource allocation rather than reactive remediation ahead of regulatory inspection.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly

technology.summary: Technology teams are responsible for implementing and evidencing the specific control measures enumerated in Appendix 10 for every critical system hosted on a public cloud, covering domains such as data residency, access control, encryption, resilience, and vendor lock-in. Where the institution opts for alternative controls, the technical team must produce rigorous comparative assessments demonstrating that the alternative controls achieve equivalent or superior risk reduction outcomes. Continuous technical monitoring and audit trails are essential to support on-demand regulatory demonstration.

requirements.requirement: Implement all applicable Appendix 10 technical control measures for each critical system on public cloud, including but not limited to: data encryption at rest and in transit, privileged access management, network segmentation, resilience and recovery controls, and cloud service provider exit/portability controls.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to 'consider' and apply Appendix 10 measures to critical cloud-hosted systems. Technical implementation of these controls is the primary mechanism by which the institution discharges this obligation and reduces actual operational and cyber risk.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (implemented at onboarding; reviewed at minimum annually and upon material change)

requirements.requirement: Conduct a technical gap assessment mapping each critical cloud system's existing controls against every Appendix 10 control measure, producing a scored control coverage matrix that identifies compliant, partially compliant, and non-compliant areas with remediation timelines.
requirements.rationale: A gap assessment operationalises the 'consider' requirement by producing structured evidence that Appendix 10 was systematically evaluated. It also provides the baseline needed to justify any alternative controls, quantifying what gap the alternative is intended to address.
requirements.owner: Head of Cloud Infrastructure / Head of IT Security
requirements.frequency: Annual and upon material changes to cloud architecture or Appendix 10 guidance

requirements.requirement: For any Appendix 10 control measure not adopted, implement and technically document an alternative control with a quantitative or qualitative equivalence assessment demonstrating that the alternative provides risk reduction at least as effective as the Appendix 10 measure it replaces.
requirements.rationale: The clause creates an explicit regulatory test: alternative practices must be demonstrably 'at least as effective as, or superior to' Appendix 10. Technical equivalence documentation is the primary artefact BNM will request when the institution is called to 'explain and demonstrate' its alternative approach.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (upon adoption of each alternative) and Annual review

requirements.requirement: Deploy continuous monitoring and automated alerting for all Appendix 10-aligned controls on critical cloud systems, generating audit logs that are tamper-evident, retained per BNM data retention requirements, and producible on demand for supervisory inspection.
requirements.rationale: The ability to 'demonstrate' control effectiveness to BNM requires real-time and historical evidence of controls in operation. Automated monitoring provides continuous assurance and reduces reliance on point-in-time manual checks that may not reflect actual operational status during a regulatory examination.
requirements.frequency: Continuous

requirements.requirement: Maintain documented and tested cloud exit and portability plans for each critical system hosted on a public cloud, addressing technical dependencies on cloud-native services that could impede migration if the provider relationship is terminated.
requirements.rationale: Vendor lock-in is a key risk category in Appendix 10. Exit planning is a technical control measure that directly addresses the risk of over-dependence on a single cloud service provider for critical systems, and its absence would be a material control gap during BNM assessment.
requirements.frequency: Annual review and upon material change to cloud architecture or provider contracts

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that Appendix 10 compliance for critical cloud-hosted systems is embedded into the institution's risk governance framework with clear ownership, documented policies, and a regulatory-ready posture for BNM examination. The clause's 'explain and demonstrate' obligation requires that the institution not merely implement controls but maintain governance artefacts that can withstand external scrutiny at any time. Board-level oversight of cloud risk strategy is necessary to ensure that decisions to depart from Appendix 10 measures are made at an appropriately senior level.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Board-approved Cloud Risk Management Policy that explicitly references Appendix 10 of BNM RMiT as the baseline control standard for critical systems on public cloud, specifying the governance process for adopting, reviewing, and approving any alternative risk management practices.
requirements.rationale: The clause places an institutional obligation on the financial institution as a whole, not merely its technology function. A Board-approved policy anchors Appendix 10 compliance at the highest governance level, ensuring that departures from the standard are subject to appropriate scrutiny and cannot be made unilaterally at operational level.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Annual review; immediate update upon material change to RMiT guidance or cloud strategy

requirements.requirement: Designate a named senior accountable owner for each critical system on public cloud, responsible for ensuring Appendix 10 controls are in place or formally approved alternatives are documented, and for being the authorised spokesperson in the event BNM requests an explanation of the institution's cloud risk practices.
requirements.rationale: BNM's expectation to 'explain and demonstrate' requires a specific, knowledgeable individual who can respond to regulatory queries with authority. Without named accountability, responsibility diffuses across teams and the institution risks presenting an incoherent or unprepared response during supervisory engagement.
requirements.frequency: Established at system onboarding; reviewed annually and upon ownership changes

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Alternative Control Approval Framework with defined criteria for assessing whether an alternative risk management practice is 'at least as effective as, or superior to' the corresponding Appendix 10 measure, including mandatory sign-off by the CRO and CISO before adoption.
requirements.rationale: The clause creates a specific regulatory test for alternative practices. Without a structured approval framework, the institution has no consistent standard for evaluating alternatives and risks approving inadequate controls that would fail BNM's equivalence test, exposing the institution to regulatory findings.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (upon each alternative practice submission); framework reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Maintain a Regulatory Evidence Pack for each critical system on public cloud, consolidating control implementation evidence, gap assessments, alternative practice approvals, and monitoring reports into a format suitable for immediate production to BNM during on-site examinations or supervisory requests.
requirements.rationale: The clause's requirement to be 'prepared to explain and demonstrate to the Bank' implies regulatory readiness at all times, not just at scheduled review dates. A pre-assembled evidence pack reduces response time, demonstrates maturity of governance, and minimises the risk of incomplete or inconsistent responses under time pressure.
requirements.owner: Chief Compliance Officer (CCO) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Continuously maintained; formal completeness review quarterly

requirements.requirement: Integrate Appendix 10 control compliance status for public cloud critical systems into the institution's internal audit programme, with findings reported to the Board Audit Committee and remediation plans tracked to closure with defined timelines.
requirements.rationale: Independent internal audit verification provides the third line of defence assurance that Appendix 10 controls are operating effectively, not just nominally in place. Board Audit Committee oversight ensures that material control gaps receive executive attention and that the institution's self-assessment of compliance is subject to independent challenge.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Board Audit Committee
requirements.frequency: Annual audit cycle; targeted reviews upon material cloud architecture changes or incident events

business.summary: Financial institutions using cloud services must ensure that customer and counterparty data and proprietary information remain under the institution's full ownership and control, not delegated to cloud service providers. Failure to comply exposes the institution to regulatory sanctions, reputational damage from data breaches, and potential loss of customer trust. The explicit retention of data ownership and key management rights also protects the institution from vendor lock-in and unilateral CSP policy changes that could compromise data confidentiality.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formal Cloud Data Ownership Policy that explicitly asserts the institution's ownership, control, and management rights over all customer and counterparty information and proprietary data hosted on any cloud platform, binding on all business lines and subsidiaries.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates 'retaining ownership, control and management of all data pertaining to customer and counterparty information.' Without a formal policy, individual business units may inadvertently cede data rights to CSPs through permissive contract terms or default cloud configurations, creating regulatory non-compliance and legal exposure.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; immediate update upon new cloud service adoption

requirements.requirement: Conduct pre-contract and annual risk assessments for every cloud service used to process, store, or transmit customer and counterparty information and proprietary data, evaluating the adequacy of safeguards against unauthorised disclosure and access.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'appropriate safeguards' — appropriateness is context-dependent. A structured risk assessment ensures the institution can demonstrate to BNM that safeguard selection was deliberate and proportionate to the sensitivity of data hosted, particularly where multiple CSPs or multi-cloud architectures are used.
requirements.frequency: Pre-deployment; Annual; Ad-hoc upon material service changes

requirements.requirement: Negotiate and maintain cloud service agreements that explicitly prohibit CSPs from accessing, processing, or disclosing customer and counterparty information and proprietary data without the institution's prior written authorisation, and that grant the institution audit rights over data handling practices.
requirements.rationale: Contractual controls are the primary business mechanism for operationalising the clause's mandate to retain 'ownership, control and management.' Without enforceable contractual prohibitions, the institution cannot guarantee protection against 'unauthorised disclosure and access' originating from within the CSP's own operations or subcontractors.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Chief Legal Officer
requirements.frequency: Per contract; Triennial review cycle at minimum

requirements.requirement: Maintain a comprehensive inventory register of all cloud-hosted data assets classified as customer information, counterparty information, or proprietary data, including the cloud platform, geographic region, data sensitivity classification, and assigned business data owner.
requirements.rationale: Effective ownership and control over cloud data, as required by the clause, cannot be exercised without knowing precisely what data exists and where. The register enables accurate scope definition for safeguard implementation and provides evidence of control to BNM examiners.
requirements.frequency: Continuous maintenance; Quarterly reconciliation

requirements.requirement: Define and publish escalation and incident response procedures specific to cloud data breaches involving customer and counterparty information, including BNM notification timelines and customer communication obligations.
requirements.rationale: The requirement to protect against 'unauthorised disclosure and access' implies the institution must be prepared to detect and respond to failures of those safeguards. Business-owned incident response procedures ensure that when a cloud data exposure occurs, the institution can demonstrate the regulatory obligation to protect data was taken seriously at the operational level.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; Tested via simulation exercises semi-annually

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement a layered set of technical controls that enforce data confidentiality, restrict access, and ensure the institution — not the CSP — retains cryptographic authority over cloud-hosted data. The explicit mention of cryptographic key management in the clause elevates key ownership (BYOK or HYOK architectures) to a mandatory control, not an optional enhancement. All controls must apply uniformly across IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS deployments where regulated data is present.

requirements.requirement: Implement customer-managed encryption (Bring Your Own Key — BYOK or Hold Your Own Key — HYOK) for all cloud-stored and in-transit customer and counterparty information and proprietary data, ensuring cryptographic keys are generated, stored, and rotated exclusively under the institution's control, not the CSP's managed key service.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly mandates management of 'relevant cryptographic keys' as part of retaining ownership and control. Using CSP-managed keys (e.g., AWS-managed KMS keys, Google-managed CMEK) means the CSP can theoretically access plaintext data, violating the 'unauthorised access' protection requirement. BYOK/HYOK ensures only the institution can decrypt its own data.
requirements.frequency: Implementation: Immediate for new workloads; Retrospective remediation plan for existing. Key rotation: Annual minimum or per policy

requirements.requirement: Deploy a Hardware Security Module (HSM) or cloud-agnostic key management system (KMS) under the institution's sole administrative control for lifecycle management of all cryptographic keys used to protect cloud-hosted regulated data, with audit logging of all key access and usage events.
requirements.rationale: Effective key management — as required by the clause — requires a dedicated, tamper-resistant infrastructure separate from the CSP control plane. An HSM or independent KMS ensures that key material cannot be extracted or used without the institution's authorisation, and that all cryptographic operations are auditable, supporting both the 'control' mandate and evidence generation for BNM examination.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; HSM firmware and KMS software patching: As-released; Key access log review: Monthly

requirements.requirement: Implement strict identity and access management (IAM) controls for all cloud environments hosting regulated data, enforcing the principle of least privilege, multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all privileged access, just-in-time (JIT) access provisioning, and automated de-provisioning upon role changes or termination.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires protection against 'unauthorised access' to customer and counterparty information. IAM controls are the primary mechanism preventing both external attackers and CSP personnel from accessing data without authorisation. Weak or excessive cloud IAM permissions are one of the most common root causes of cloud data breaches in financial services.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Head of Cloud Infrastructure
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; Access rights review: Quarterly; Privileged access review: Monthly

requirements.requirement: Deploy Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) and Data Loss Prevention (DLP) tooling across all cloud environments, configured to detect and alert on misconfigured storage buckets, overly permissive access policies, unencrypted data at rest, and exfiltration attempts involving customer or proprietary data.
requirements.rationale: The requirement to implement 'appropriate safeguards against unauthorised disclosure' necessitates technical controls that can proactively detect configuration drift and data exfiltration vectors. CSPM continuously validates that cloud infrastructure conforms to security baselines, while DLP prevents regulated data from being transmitted to unauthorised destinations — directly supporting both confidentiality and access control mandates.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; Policy ruleset review: Quarterly; Alert tuning: Monthly

requirements.requirement: Implement data residency controls and technical guardrails to ensure customer and counterparty information and proprietary data is stored only in geographically approved cloud regions, with automated policy enforcement preventing replication or egress to unapproved jurisdictions.
requirements.rationale: Retaining 'ownership, control and management' of cloud-hosted data requires knowing and controlling where data physically resides. Unauthorised cross-border data transfers could expose regulated data to foreign government access or legal frameworks inconsistent with Malaysian law and BNM expectations, constituting a form of 'unauthorised disclosure' under the clause.
requirements.owner: Head of Cloud Infrastructure / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Continuous technical enforcement; Policy review: Annual; Compliance scan: Monthly

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that accountability for cloud data protection is clearly assigned at the Board and senior management level, with documented policies, oversight mechanisms, and assurance activities that can be produced as evidence of compliance to BNM. The clause's 'S' marker indicates this is a standard expectation where BNM will assess not only the existence of controls but the effectiveness of the governance framework overseeing them. Third-party risk governance over CSPs is equally critical given the institution's reliance on cloud infrastructure to host regulated data.

requirements.requirement: Establish a Board-approved Cloud Data Governance Framework that defines the institution's policies for data classification, protection standards, ownership accountability, cryptographic key management governance, and CSP risk management, with explicit coverage of customer and counterparty information and proprietary data.
requirements.rationale: The clause's mandate for the institution to 'retain ownership, control and management' implies that these responsibilities must be formally governed, not ad-hoc. A Board-approved framework establishes the authority, accountability, and standards under which all cloud data protection activities operate, and signals to BNM that senior leadership is actively overseeing compliance.
requirements.owner: Board of Directors / Risk Management Committee
requirements.frequency: Board approval: Initial and upon material revision; Annual review by Risk Management Committee

requirements.requirement: Appoint a designated Data Protection Officer or Cloud Risk Owner at senior management level with explicit responsibility for overseeing compliance with cloud data protection obligations, including cryptographic key management governance, and with a direct reporting line to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: The clause imposes institutional-level obligations that require a named accountable owner to ensure they are met. Without designated ownership, cloud data protection responsibilities risk being fragmented across IT, risk, and legal teams with no single point of accountability, making it impossible to demonstrate 'control and management' to BNM in a credible manner.
requirements.frequency: Appointment: Ongoing (role maintained continuously); Reporting to Board Risk Committee: Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement a Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) process specifically for cloud service providers that includes due diligence on data protection capabilities at onboarding, contractual data protection obligations (including sub-processor controls), annual reassessment, and the right to audit CSP data handling and security controls.
requirements.rationale: The institution cannot fulfil its obligation to 'retain ownership, control and management' of cloud-hosted data without actively governing its CSPs. CSPs are entrusted with infrastructure on which regulated data resides, and the clause does not permit delegation of the institution's data protection obligations to the CSP. TPRM ensures ongoing oversight of the CSP's conduct and capability to support the institution's compliance.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Third-Party Risk
requirements.frequency: Onboarding due diligence: Per-engagement; Annual reassessment; Audit rights exercised: Risk-based, minimum biennial

requirements.requirement: Establish an internal audit programme that annually assesses the operating effectiveness of cloud data protection safeguards, including cryptographic key management controls, IAM configurations, data classification accuracy, and contractual compliance by CSPs, with findings reported to the Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: BNM's 'S' marker indicates the institution must demonstrate sustained compliance, not merely implement controls. Independent internal audit validation provides the Board and senior management with assurance that safeguards against 'unauthorised disclosure and access' are functioning as designed, and surfaces control gaps before they become regulatory findings or data breach events.
requirements.frequency: Annual audit cycle; Findings remediation tracking: Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Maintain a Cloud Data Incident Register and establish a mandatory reporting protocol requiring all incidents involving potential or confirmed unauthorised disclosure of or access to cloud-hosted customer and counterparty information or proprietary data to be escalated to senior management within 24 hours and to BNM in accordance with applicable notification obligations.
requirements.rationale: Effective 'control and management' of cloud data, as mandated by the clause, requires the institution to be able to detect, record, and respond to failures in its safeguards. A formal incident register and escalation protocol ensures that data protection failures are not suppressed at operational levels and that BNM receives timely notification, demonstrating the institution's transparency and accountability in cloud data governance.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Register maintained: Continuously; Incident review by senior management: Monthly; BNM escalation: Per-event as required

business.summary: Financial institutions face significant operational, reputational, and regulatory risk if unauthorised users can access IT assets or sensitive data due to inadequate access controls. Clause 10.53 mandates a formalised policy that addresses the full identity lifecycle — identification, authentication, and authorisation — for every user, ensuring that access privileges reflect the risk profile of each IT asset. Failure to comply exposes the institution to data breaches, regulatory enforcement action by BNM, and erosion of customer trust.

requirements.requirement: The institution must establish and formally approve a written Access Control Policy that explicitly addresses the identification, authentication, and authorisation of all users — including employees, contractors, third-party vendors, and privileged/service accounts — to all IT assets and data.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates 'an access control policy for the identification, authentication, and authorisation of all users to its IT assets and data.' A documented policy is the foundation of enforceable controls; without it, access decisions are ad hoc and unauditable, exposing the institution to BNM supervisory findings and internal control failures.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Chief Operating Officer (COO)
requirements.frequency: Policy established once; reviewed and re-approved Annually or upon material change

requirements.requirement: The institution must conduct and maintain a formal risk assessment of all IT assets and data to determine the level of risk posed by unauthorised access, which must directly inform and justify the level of granularity defined in the access control policy for each asset class.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text states the policy's granularity 'shall be commensurate with the level of risk of unauthorised access.' Without a risk-tiered asset classification, the institution cannot demonstrate to BNM that its access control granularity is proportionate, resulting in either over-permissive controls on high-risk systems or disproportionate overhead on low-risk assets.
requirements.frequency: Initial assessment before policy publication; reassessed Annually and upon any new IT asset onboarding or material change

requirements.requirement: Business units must define and maintain a register of all roles requiring access to IT assets and data, specifying the minimum access privileges necessary for each role to perform its function (principle of least privilege), aligned to the risk classification of those assets.
requirements.rationale: The clause covers 'all users' and requires authorisation controls commensurate with risk. Business-side role definition is the prerequisite for technology teams to implement correct access controls; without business-owned role definitions, IT systems default to over-privileged access, directly increasing the risk of unauthorised access that the clause seeks to mitigate.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Heads of Business Units
requirements.frequency: Initial definition at policy launch; reviewed Semi-Annually and upon organisational restructuring or role changes

requirements.requirement: The institution must establish a formal process for periodic access rights recertification, requiring business unit managers to review and reaffirm or revoke access entitlements for their staff and associated third parties, with results documented and escalated for exceptions.
requirements.rationale: Authorisation of 'all users' implies ongoing validity of access grants, not just provisioning at onboarding. Stale or orphaned accounts represent a primary vector for unauthorised access to IT assets and data. Business managers, as the accountable owners of access decisions, must attest to the continuing appropriateness of granted access.
requirements.owner: Heads of Business Units / Line Managers
requirements.frequency: Quarterly for high-risk systems; Semi-Annually for standard systems

requirements.requirement: The institution must define and enforce procedures for the immediate revocation or modification of user access rights upon triggering events such as employee resignation, role change, contract termination, or security incident, ensuring no residual unauthorised access to IT assets persists.
requirements.rationale: The clause's requirement for authorisation of 'all users' encompasses the full user lifecycle, including offboarding. Delayed revocation of access following employment or engagement changes constitutes a direct failure to maintain authorised access controls and is a recurring finding in BNM technology risk examinations.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (triggered upon role change, resignation, contract end, or security incident); effectiveness reviewed Quarterly

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement controls that operationalise the three pillars of the access control policy — identification (uniquely establishing who a user is), authentication (verifying that identity), and authorisation (enforcing what that verified identity may access) — across all IT assets and data. The depth of technical controls deployed must be calibrated to the risk tier of each asset, meaning high-risk core banking and data systems require stronger, more granular technical enforcement than lower-risk internal tools. Implementation must be auditable, centrally managed, and capable of generating evidence for BNM examination.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and maintain a centralised Identity and Access Management (IAM) platform that enforces unique identification for every user account — human and non-human (service accounts, API credentials) — across all IT assets, prohibiting the use of shared or generic accounts except where technically unavoidable and formally risk-accepted.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates controls for 'identification of all users to its IT assets.' Unique identification is the technical prerequisite for attribution, auditability, and enforcement of all downstream authentication and authorisation controls. Shared accounts break the audit trail and make it impossible to demonstrate compliance with the policy.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of IT Infrastructure
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; platform configuration reviewed Quarterly; audit of orphaned accounts Monthly

requirements.requirement: Implement Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for all user access to IT assets classified as high or critical risk, including remote access, privileged access, access to core banking systems, customer data repositories, and administrative interfaces. Authentication strength must be commensurate with the risk classification of the target asset.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'authentication of all users' with granularity 'commensurate with the level of risk.' Password-only authentication is insufficient for high-risk assets given the prevalence of credential theft; MFA directly reduces the risk of unauthorised access to the institution's most critical IT assets and data as demanded by the risk-proportionate granularity requirement.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; MFA coverage reviewed Monthly; exceptions reviewed and reapproved Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) or Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) across all IT assets, enforcing the principle of least privilege such that authenticated users are authorised only to access resources and perform actions explicitly required for their defined business role, with granularity of permissions proportional to the risk classification of each asset.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates 'authorisation of all users to its IT assets and data' at a granularity 'commensurate with the level of risk.' RBAC/ABAC is the technical mechanism that translates business-defined roles and risk-tiered asset classifications into enforceable access boundaries, preventing lateral movement and data exfiltration in the event of account compromise.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; role-to-permission mappings reviewed Semi-Annually; privilege escalation requests reviewed Per-event

requirements.requirement: Maintain a comprehensive and current IT asset inventory — including applications, databases, infrastructure, network devices, and cloud resources — with each asset assigned a risk classification tier derived from the institution's risk assessment. This inventory must be integrated with IAM and access control systems to ensure policy granularity is consistently applied.
requirements.rationale: The clause's requirement for access controls covering 'all IT assets and data' and granularity 'commensurate with the level of risk' cannot be operationalised without a complete, risk-classified asset register. Unknown or unclassified assets are unprotected assets; incomplete inventories result in uncontrolled access that directly violates the clause.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Head of IT Operations
requirements.frequency: Continuous discovery and reconciliation; full inventory review Quarterly; risk reclassification upon material asset change

requirements.requirement: Implement privileged access management (PAM) controls — including just-in-time (JIT) access provisioning, privileged session recording, and password vaulting — for all administrative and privileged accounts with elevated access to high-risk IT assets, infrastructure, and sensitive data.
requirements.rationale: Privileged accounts represent the highest-risk user category under the clause's requirement for authorisation commensurate with risk. PAM controls directly reduce the blast radius of privileged account compromise, enforce the policy's granularity requirement for high-risk IT assets, and generate the detailed audit trail required to demonstrate compliance during BNM examinations.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; privileged account inventory and session logs reviewed Monthly; PAM configuration audited Quarterly

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure the access control policy is formally approved at the appropriate level of seniority, is subject to regular independent review, and is supported by clear accountability frameworks that span policy ownership, compliance monitoring, and exception management. Given the 'S' (Standard) marker, BNM expects these governance controls to be embedded in the institution's normal operating cycle, not treated as one-off exercises. Governance must also ensure that the risk-proportionate granularity requirement is validated independently of the teams implementing the controls.

requirements.requirement: The Access Control Policy must be approved by the Board of Directors or a duly authorised Board-level committee (e.g., Board Risk Committee or Board Audit Committee), with Senior Management accountability assigned to a named C-suite executive, ensuring the policy carries institutional authority and appropriate seniority of ownership.
requirements.rationale: The clause imposes a mandatory obligation on 'a financial institution' as a legal entity, requiring accountability at the institutional level. Board-level approval ensures the policy has binding authority, cannot be overridden by operational convenience, and demonstrates to BNM that access control is treated as a material governance matter rather than an operational IT issue.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee Chairman / Board of Directors
requirements.frequency: Approved upon initial policy establishment; re-approved Annually or upon material revision

requirements.requirement: An independent function — Internal Audit or a designated second-line risk team — must conduct periodic assessments to verify that the access control policy's level of granularity remains commensurate with the current risk profile of IT assets, and that implemented controls are operating effectively in accordance with the policy.
