requirements.rationale: The exploitation phase depends on the presence of unpatched vulnerabilities in institution systems. A structured vulnerability management programme directly reduces the attack surface available to adversaries who have completed reconnaissance, weaponisation, and delivery phases, interrupting the kill chain before exploitation succeeds.
requirements.frequency: Continuous scanning; critical patch SLA of 72 hours; high severity within 14 days; reviewed monthly by CISO

governance.summary: Governance structures must formalise the institution's cybersecurity operations framework, ensuring that responsibilities for each cyber-attack lifecycle phase are embedded in policy, tested through exercises, and subject to independent oversight. The clause's requirement to 'establish clear responsibilities' creates an obligation that goes beyond technology deployment — it demands documented accountability structures, policy frameworks, and audit trails that BNM examiners can verify. Governance mechanisms must also ensure that the adequacy of mitigating measures is periodically challenged and validated against an evolving threat landscape.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Cybersecurity Operations Policy that explicitly defines mitigating controls, responsible parties, and minimum performance standards for each of the seven cyber-attack lifecycle phases, approved by the Board Risk Committee and reviewed at least annually.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates that financial institutions 'establish clear responsibilities' for cybersecurity operations across all seven phases. A Board-approved policy provides the authoritative governance instrument that assigns accountability, sets minimum control standards, and creates an audit-ready record of the institution's compliance with this requirement.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; interim update triggered by material threat changes or regulatory guidance

requirements.requirement: Establish a Cybersecurity Operations Committee or equivalent governance body with defined terms of reference, comprising representatives from IT, Risk, Compliance, Legal, and relevant business units, meeting at minimum quarterly to review the effectiveness of mitigating measures across all seven lifecycle phases and escalate material gaps to EXCO.
requirements.rationale: Clear responsibilities cannot be effectively maintained without a standing governance forum to monitor, challenge, and escalate cybersecurity operations performance. A multi-disciplinary committee ensures that business, technology, and risk perspectives are integrated in assessing whether mitigating measures across the kill chain remain appropriate.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly committee meetings; extraordinary meetings on material cyber incidents

requirements.requirement: Conduct and document annual red team exercises or threat-led penetration tests that simulate adversary behaviour across all seven cyber-attack lifecycle phases, with findings mapped to specific control gaps and remediation plans tracked through to closure by the Cybersecurity Operations Committee.
requirements.rationale: The requirement for 'appropriate mitigating measures' implies that the adequacy of controls must be validated, not assumed. Threat-led exercises provide empirical evidence of whether existing defences successfully interrupt the kill chain at each phase, enabling governance bodies to make evidence-based decisions on control investments and policy updates.
requirements.frequency: Annual; findings remediation tracked quarterly

requirements.requirement: Maintain an Incident Response Plan (IRP) structured around the seven cyber-attack lifecycle phases, with defined playbooks for each phase, assigned incident commanders, and clear escalation thresholds to senior management, Board, and BNM as required under reporting obligations.
requirements.rationale: Establishing 'clear responsibilities' for cybersecurity operations must include pre-defined response responsibilities for when mitigating measures fail. A kill-chain-aligned IRP ensures that the institution's response to active attacks is structured, swift, and accountable, reducing dwell time and limiting the progression of attacks through later lifecycle phases.
requirements.frequency: Annual IRP review; tested via tabletop exercises bi-annually; updated within 30 days of a material incident

requirements.requirement: Commission an independent audit of cybersecurity operations controls covering all seven lifecycle phases at least annually, with audit findings and management responses reported to the Board Audit Committee and tracked through the institution's audit management system to verified closure.
requirements.rationale: Independent assurance validates that the responsibilities and mitigating measures established under this clause are operating effectively and not merely documented. BNM's examination process for RMiT compliance relies on audit trails and independent verification; without this governance control, the institution cannot demonstrate to the regulator that its cybersecurity operations are genuinely effective across the full kill chain.
requirements.frequency: Annual audit; interim thematic reviews as directed by Board Audit Committee

business.summary: Failure to maintain continuous security monitoring and a functional SOC exposes the institution to undetected breaches, regulatory sanctions under BNM's enforcement powers, and reputational damage that can erode customer trust and market position. The clause mandates proactive defence rather than reactive response, meaning gaps in monitoring coverage or SOC capability directly translate to prolonged dwell time for threat actors and heightened probability of material financial loss. Senior leadership must treat SOC investment as a non-negotiable operational cost rather than an optional IT expenditure.

requirements.requirement: Formally approve and fund the establishment of a Security Operations Centre (SOC) — whether in-house, co-managed, or outsourced — with a defined operating budget covering staffing, tooling, and technology refresh cycles sufficient for 24/7 proactive monitoring operations.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates the SOC be 'supported by competent resources and equipped with the necessary tools and technologies.' Without explicit board-level funding commitment, SOC capability will be under-resourced, rendering the institution non-compliant and operationally exposed to undetected threats.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Chief Financial Officer (CFO)
requirements.frequency: Annual (budget cycle) with quarterly review

requirements.requirement: Define and formally document the business-approved scope of security monitoring to explicitly enumerate all critical systems and supporting infrastructure, ensuring no revenue-generating, customer-facing, or operationally essential system is excluded from SOC coverage.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.9(b) explicitly states monitoring scope 'must cover all critical systems including the supporting infrastructure.' Business owners of each critical system must attest to inclusion in the SOC scope to close coverage gaps that regulators may identify during examination.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Annual review; upon onboarding of new critical systems

requirements.requirement: Integrate SOC-detected anomaly escalation pathways into the institution's incident response and business continuity plans, ensuring that timely detection of anomalous activities, as required by the clause, triggers pre-approved business response actions including customer notification protocols, regulatory reporting to BNM, and service continuity measures.
requirements.rationale: Timely detection is only operationally valuable if it is coupled with pre-defined business escalation and response. Without this linkage, a functioning SOC may detect incidents that then stall at the operational-to-business escalation boundary, negating the protective intent of the clause.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Business Continuity Management
requirements.frequency: Annual plan review; tested semi-annually via tabletop exercises

requirements.requirement: Commission and oversee a recurring Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing (VAPT) programme aligned with BNM RMiT Appendix 5, with business stakeholders formally accepting residual risk findings and approving remediation timelines for identified vulnerabilities.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.9(c) requires 'regular review of its security posture via the conduct of a vulnerability assessment and penetration testing in line with Appendix 5.' Business ownership of VAPT findings ensures that remediation is resourced and prioritised beyond IT, addressing systemic vulnerabilities that could compromise operations.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Per Appendix 5 schedule (at minimum annually; high-risk systems more frequently)

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Security Posture Dashboard accessible to senior management providing aggregate visibility into the institution's real-time threat landscape, SOC operational metrics, open VAPT findings, and remediation progress to enable informed, timely executive decision-making.
requirements.rationale: The clause's emphasis on 'proactive monitoring' and 'regular review of security posture' implies that actionable insight must reach decision-makers. Without executive-level visibility, the SOC operates in isolation and systemic risks may not receive the organisational response proportional to their business impact.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Chief Executive Officer (CEO)
requirements.frequency: Continuous dashboard; monthly executive briefing

technology.summary: Technology teams must architect, deploy, and sustain a SOC capability that achieves genuine 24/7 continuous monitoring across the full estate of critical systems and supporting infrastructure, underpinned by tooling that enables proactive anomaly detection, correlation, and rapid response. The technical implementation must address log ingestion at scale, threat intelligence enrichment, and automated alerting, while a structured VAPT programme independently validates the effectiveness of deployed security controls. All tooling choices and coverage maps must be maintained and tested regularly to ensure they keep pace with the evolving threat landscape.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and operationalise a Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) platform configured to ingest, normalise, correlate, and alert on log and telemetry data from all critical systems and supporting infrastructure, with detection rules tuned to the Malaysian financial sector threat landscape and MITRE ATT&CK framework mappings.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'proactive monitoring of its technology security posture' and 'timely detection of anomalous activities.' A SIEM is the foundational technology enabling log centralisation, pattern correlation, and alert generation across the entire monitored estate; without it, monitoring is fragmented and reactive.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of SOC
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; rule tuning and content updates monthly

requirements.requirement: Maintain a comprehensive and continuously updated asset inventory covering all critical systems and supporting infrastructure (servers, network devices, endpoints, cloud workloads, OT/SCADA where applicable, and third-party-connected systems), and validate that 100% of inventoried assets are onboarded as monitored sources in the SOC.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.9(b) mandates that monitoring scope 'must cover all critical systems including the supporting infrastructure.' An asset inventory gap directly creates monitoring blind spots; attackers routinely exploit unmonitored assets as lateral movement pathways. Coverage validation must be a continuous technical control.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Infrastructure / Head of SOC
requirements.frequency: Continuous inventory sync; quarterly coverage audit

requirements.requirement: Deploy complementary detection technologies beyond the SIEM — including Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR), Network Detection and Response (NDR), and where applicable, User and Entity Behaviour Analytics (UEBA) — to enable multi-layered anomaly detection aligned with the clause's requirement for 'competent resources equipped with the necessary tools and technologies.'
requirements.rationale: A SIEM alone is insufficient for detecting sophisticated threats such as living-off-the-land attacks, insider threats, or encrypted command-and-control traffic. Multi-layered tooling equips SOC analysts with corroborating signals and reduces mean time to detect (MTTD), directly supporting the clause's proactive monitoring mandate.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Security Engineering
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; technology refresh every 2-3 years or upon material threat landscape change

requirements.requirement: Integrate threat intelligence feeds (including sector-specific feeds from BNM's Financial Sector Cyber Threat Intelligence Platform or equivalent) into SOC tooling to enrich alerts, prioritise anomalies, and ensure detection logic reflects current tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) targeting Malaysian financial institutions.
requirements.rationale: The clause's requirement for 'proactive monitoring' implies anticipatory rather than solely reactive detection. Threat intelligence operationalised within the SOC enables detection of known adversary behaviours before they achieve their objectives, directly reducing the risk of 'potential compromise of security controls.'
requirements.owner: Head of SOC / Head of Threat Intelligence
requirements.frequency: Continuous feed ingestion; monthly TTP review and detection rule update

requirements.requirement: Execute a structured Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing (VAPT) programme per BNM RMiT Appendix 5 requirements, encompassing network infrastructure, web and mobile applications, API layers, and critical backend systems, using a combination of internal scans and independent external penetration testers, with findings tracked to closure in a vulnerability management system.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.9(c) explicitly requires 'vulnerability assessment and penetration testing in line with Appendix 5.' VAPT provides independent technical validation that deployed controls are effective, identifies exploitable weaknesses before adversaries do, and feeds directly into the security posture review required by the clause.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Cybersecurity Assurance
requirements.frequency: Per Appendix 5 (minimum annually for full-scope; critical systems and internet-facing assets more frequently — at least semi-annually)

requirements.requirement: Implement Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response (SOAR) capabilities or equivalent automated playbooks to ensure that high-confidence alerts trigger timely, consistent, and documented response actions — supporting the clause's requirement for 'timely detection' and reducing analyst fatigue that degrades continuous monitoring effectiveness.
requirements.rationale: At scale, the volume of security events across all critical systems and infrastructure exceeds manual analyst capacity, threatening the continuity of effective monitoring. Automation of defined response playbooks maintains response timeliness, reduces mean time to respond (MTTR), and creates an auditable record of actions taken against each detected anomaly.
requirements.owner: Head of SOC / Head of Security Engineering
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; playbook review and update quarterly

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that the SOC is formally chartered, that its performance is subject to independent oversight, and that the findings it generates — including VAPT results — are reviewed, risk-accepted, and remediated within a defined accountability framework. The board and senior management must receive regular, structured reporting on the institution's security posture to discharge their oversight responsibilities under BNM RMiT. Competency of SOC resources must be governed through documented qualification standards, training requirements, and succession planning.

requirements.requirement: Establish and formally approve a SOC Charter that defines the SOC's mandate, scope of monitoring authority, organisational structure, staffing competency requirements, escalation pathways, and performance KPIs — with the charter reviewed and reaffirmed by senior management at least annually.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires a SOC 'supported by competent resources' with defined tools and monitoring scope. Without a governance charter, the SOC lacks formal institutional authority, accountability lines are ambiguous, and competency standards are unenforced — conditions that regulators regard as governance failure independent of technical capability.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Annual review; updated upon material organisational or regulatory change

requirements.requirement: Implement a structured SOC competency framework defining minimum qualifications (e.g., relevant certifications such as GCIH, GCFE, CEH, or equivalent), experience requirements, continuous professional development obligations, and succession planning for all SOC roles, ensuring the institution can evidence 'competent resources' to BNM examiners.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.9(a) specifically conditions the SOC on it being 'supported by competent resources.' BNM examiners will assess whether staff qualifications and training records substantiate this assertion. A documented competency framework provides the governance evidence required and ensures operational resilience against staff attrition.
requirements.owner: Head of Human Resources / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Annual competency assessment; training completed per individual development plan

requirements.requirement: Establish a regular Security Posture Reporting cycle delivering structured reports to the Board Risk Committee and senior management covering SOC operational metrics (alert volumes, MTTD, MTTR), open incidents, VAPT findings and remediation status, and emerging threat intelligence — ensuring the institution's leadership maintains awareness of its security posture as required by the clause.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'regular review of its security posture,' which governance frameworks must operationalise through formal reporting to the institution's highest levels. Board-level awareness is a precondition for informed risk decision-making and for the institution to demonstrate to BNM that oversight of security monitoring is genuinely exercised.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Board Risk Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Monthly to senior management; quarterly to Board Risk Committee

requirements.requirement: Establish a VAPT Governance Framework governing scope definition, tester qualification and independence requirements (including that penetration testers are sufficiently independent from the systems being tested), findings classification, risk acceptance authority by tier, and mandatory remediation timelines — aligned with BNM RMiT Appendix 5.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.9(c) mandates VAPT 'in line with Appendix 5,' which prescribes specific requirements for scope, frequency, and independence. Without a governance framework translating Appendix 5 into enforceable institutional policy, VAPT exercises may be inconsistently scoped, under-resourced, or findings may not be formally accepted and remediated — all of which are compliance gaps.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Framework reviewed annually; VAPT findings reviewed within 30 days of report issuance

requirements.requirement: Implement a Continuous Monitoring Policy that formally defines the institution's monitoring scope, minimum alert retention periods, acceptable detection thresholds, mandatory escalation timelines for different severity classes of anomalous activity, and the process for updating monitoring scope when new critical systems are commissioned or retired.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires monitoring to be continuous and to cover 'all critical systems including the supporting infrastructure.' A formal policy creates the governance mechanism that enforces completeness of scope and consistency of response, and provides the documentary evidence required to demonstrate compliance to BNM during Technology Risk examinations.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of IT Governance
requirements.frequency: Annual policy review; scope updates upon change in critical system inventory

requirements.requirement: Conduct an annual independent assessment of the SOC's effectiveness — by internal audit or a qualified external party — evaluating whether monitoring coverage is complete, detection capabilities are functioning as intended, competency requirements are being met, and VAPT findings are being remediated within approved timelines, with results reported to the Board Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: Proactive governance requires assurance that the controls mandated by Clause 11.9 are operating effectively, not merely that they exist on paper. An independent annual assessment provides the Board Audit Committee with objective evidence of control effectiveness and identifies deterioration in SOC capability before it is identified by BNM examiners or exploited by threat actors.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Board Audit Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Annual; findings tracked to closure on a quarterly basis

business.summary: Failure to maintain a structured cyber threat intelligence capability exposes the institution to undetected breaches and reputational damage from unchallenged misinformation circulating online. Without timely threat awareness, incident response is reactive rather than anticipatory, increasing financial and regulatory liability. BNM's Standard marker signals this is a baseline expectation with direct supervisory consequence.

requirements.requirement: Establish and formally charter a Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) programme with defined scope, funding, and accountability, covering all threat vectors relevant to the institution's operating environment including retail banking, payments, and digital channels.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates that a financial institution 'must establish a process' — this is not optional. Without a formally resourced programme, ad hoc threat awareness activities cannot satisfy the systematic collect-analyse-evaluate lifecycle demanded by the clause.
requirements.frequency: One-time establishment; Annual programme review and rechartering

requirements.requirement: Define and maintain an institutional threat profile that maps the institution's digital assets, customer segments, third-party dependencies, and geographic exposure, so that collected threat intelligence is contextualised 'in relation to its environment' as required.
requirements.rationale: The clause specifies threat intelligence must be relevant to the institution's own environment, not generic. Without an up-to-date threat profile, intelligence collection lacks targeting criteria and analysis lacks a baseline for relevance assessment.
requirements.frequency: Continuous maintenance; Formal review Semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Mandate continuous monitoring for data breach incidents involving the institution's customer data, credentials, or proprietary information appearing on dark web markets, breach databases, or threat actor channels, with escalation thresholds and customer notification obligations defined.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly calls out 'data breach incidents' as a named threat category requiring prompt detection. Discovery of compromised customer data triggers regulatory notification obligations under BNM guidelines and the Personal Data Protection Act 2010.
requirements.frequency: Continuous

requirements.requirement: Implement an online brand protection and misinformation monitoring process to detect, assess, and respond to the spread of misleading information about the institution across social media platforms, news aggregators, messaging apps, and websites.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly names 'spread of misleading information in relation to the financial institution over the Internet' as a threat category. Unaddressed misinformation can trigger bank runs, damage customer trust, and require regulatory engagement — all with direct business impact.
requirements.owner: Chief Marketing Officer (CMO) jointly with CISO
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; Escalation response Per-event

requirements.requirement: Define and enforce service level targets for 'prompt detection' of cyber threats, including maximum time-to-detect benchmarks for different threat categories (e.g., active credential stuffing, phishing campaigns, data exfiltration), with breach-of-SLT escalation paths to senior management.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires threats to be 'promptly detected', implying a timeliness standard. Without defined detection SLTs, there is no enforceable standard against which operational performance can be assessed by management or BNM supervisors.
requirements.frequency: SLT definition Annual; Performance review Monthly

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement a layered CTI toolchain capable of automated ingestion from multiple external and internal feeds, correlation against the institution's own environment, and integration with the Security Operations Centre for real-time detection. Coverage must extend beyond perimeter threats to include surface web, dark web, and social media channels. All tooling must produce actionable, contextualised intelligence rather than raw indicator volumes.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and operationalise a Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP) that aggregates feeds from commercial threat intelligence providers, government/sectoral sources (e.g., BNM-CyberSecurity Malaysia advisories, FinancialCSIRT Malaysia, MISP communities), and open-source intelligence (OSINT) sources, with automated indicator normalisation and deduplication.
requirements.rationale: The 'collect' obligation in the verbatim text requires systematic, multi-source ingestion rather than reliance on a single feed. A TIP provides the technical foundation for structured collection 'in relation to its environment' through configurable relevance filtering.
requirements.frequency: Continuous feed ingestion; Platform health review Weekly

requirements.requirement: Integrate the TIP with the institution's SIEM and EDR platforms to enable automated correlation of threat indicators (IPs, domains, hashes, TTPs) against internal telemetry, generating prioritised alerts for SOC analysts with environmental context attached.
requirements.rationale: The 'evaluate' and 'promptly detect' requirements demand that intelligence translates into detection capability, not just passive repositories. SIEM/TIP integration closes the loop between threat knowledge and operational alerting.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; Integration integrity checks Monthly

requirements.requirement: Deploy dark web and deep web monitoring tooling (or contracted managed service) to continuously scan for leaked credentials, stolen payment card data, compromised account information, and threat actor discussions referencing the institution by name, brand, domain, or BIC/SWIFT identifiers.
requirements.rationale: Explicit mention of 'data breach incidents' in the verbatim text requires proactive discovery of exfiltrated data before or immediately after public exposure. Dark web monitoring is the primary technical mechanism for early detection of credential and data compromise.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; Alert triage Per-event; Tool coverage review Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement automated social media and surface web monitoring for brand impersonation, phishing site creation (lookalike domains, rogue mobile apps), and viral spread of content containing misleading information about the institution, with real-time alerting and takedown workflow integration.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires detection of 'spread of misleading information...over the Internet'. Social media monitoring tooling is the direct technical response to this named threat category, enabling prompt detection before reputational or financial harm escalates.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; Takedown initiation Per-event

requirements.requirement: Establish a structured analyst workflow within the CTI process covering the full 'collect, analyse and evaluate' cycle: ingestion pipelines, analyst enrichment queues, threat scoring/prioritisation logic, finished intelligence report templates, and feedback mechanisms to validate indicator relevance against the institutional threat profile.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text specifies three distinct activities — collect, analyse, and evaluate — not merely passive monitoring. Without a documented analytical workflow, collected intelligence degrades into noise and fails the evaluation standard BNM requires.
requirements.owner: Head of Cybersecurity / Threat Intelligence Lead
requirements.frequency: Workflow execution Continuous; Process effectiveness review Quarterly

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure the CTI programme operates with clear policy authority, defined roles, board-level visibility, and integration into the broader risk management framework. Intelligence outputs must flow into incident response, vendor risk, and strategic planning processes rather than remaining siloed within the SOC. BNM supervisors will expect documented evidence of operating processes, not just tooling deployment.

requirements.requirement: Develop and approve a Cyber Threat Intelligence Policy that formally defines the programme's objectives, scope, intelligence collection sources, classification of intelligence outputs, analyst roles and responsibilities, escalation thresholds, and integration points with Incident Response and Risk Management frameworks.
requirements.rationale: The phrase 'establish a process' in the verbatim text implies formalisation. A documented policy provides BNM supervisors with evidence of structured process establishment, and gives internal audit a baseline against which operational compliance can be assessed.
requirements.owner: CISO, approved by Board Risk Committee
requirements.frequency: Annual policy review; Ad hoc review upon material threat environment change

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal threat intelligence sharing arrangement with at least one sectoral or national body (e.g., FinancialCSIRT Malaysia, BNM's cybersecurity coordination channels, regional FS-ISAC participation) to receive and contribute contextualised threat intelligence, with data handling and confidentiality terms documented.
requirements.rationale: BNM RMiT's broader intent is sector-level resilience. Participation in intelligence sharing networks broadens collection coverage 'in relation to its environment' beyond what any single institution can achieve internally, and demonstrates proactive sector engagement to supervisors.
requirements.frequency: Partnership establishment One-time; Participation review Annual; Information exchange Continuous

requirements.requirement: Institute a recurring Cyber Threat Intelligence briefing cadence: monthly operational threat reports to the CISO and senior management, and quarterly strategic threat landscape summaries to the Board Risk Committee, each including status of detected data breach incidents and any active misinformation campaigns.
requirements.rationale: Management and board visibility into threat intelligence outputs is necessary for informed risk decisions and resource allocation. Explicit mention of 'data breach incidents' and 'misleading information' as named threat categories means boards must be made aware of their status, not just technical teams.
requirements.owner: CISO (preparation); Board Risk Committee (receipt and challenge)
requirements.frequency: Operational reports Monthly; Strategic reports Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Define and embed CTI-triggered escalation and activation triggers into the institution's Incident Response Plan, specifying the intelligence confidence thresholds and threat severity levels that mandate escalation from monitoring to active incident response, including mandatory BNM notification timelines for confirmed data breach incidents.
requirements.rationale: The 'promptly detect' obligation in the verbatim text is only meaningful if detection automatically triggers a response chain. Governance integration between CTI and IR ensures intelligence is actioned rather than logged, and that BNM notification obligations are met within mandated timeframes.
requirements.frequency: Trigger definition Annual review; Activation Per-event

requirements.requirement: Commission an annual independent assessment of the CTI programme's effectiveness, covering adequacy of intelligence sources relative to the institutional threat profile, quality and timeliness of analysis outputs, coverage of named threat categories (data breaches, online misinformation), and integration with downstream risk and response processes.
requirements.rationale: The 'evaluate' component of the verbatim text applies not only to individual threat reports but to the programme itself. Independent review prevents operational drift and provides objective evidence of programme maturity for BNM supervisory review and internal audit.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor / External Cybersecurity Assessor
requirements.frequency: Annual; Findings remediation tracked Quarterly

business.summary: Financial institutions must operationalise a structured, complexity-tiered response capability so that flagged anomalous activities are neither ignored nor disproportionately escalated. Failure to do so exposes the institution to uncontained incidents, regulatory censure, and reputational damage. The clause's 'level of complexity' qualifier requires business owners to approve a risk-based triage framework rather than applying a one-size-fits-all reaction.

requirements.requirement: Define and formally approve a tiered anomalous activity response framework that classifies flagged activities by complexity level (e.g., Low / Medium / High / Critical) and maps each tier to predefined investigation and response actions, escalation thresholds, and resolution timelines.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates responses 'based on their level of complexity', requiring the business to codify what complexity means and what differential action it triggers. Without this, operational teams cannot respond consistently or proportionately.
requirements.frequency: Initial establishment, then Annual review or upon material change to threat landscape

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain an on-call incident response roster with defined roles, responsibilities, and authority to act for each complexity tier, ensuring investigation and response actions can be initiated within documented timelines for every tier at any hour.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates the institution must 'investigate and respond' — passive detection alone does not satisfy the obligation. Business operations must ensure human accountabilities are assigned and tested so response occurs, not merely detection.
requirements.frequency: Roster review Quarterly; operational readiness tested Semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Define business-impact criteria for escalating a flagged anomalous activity to senior management and the Board Risk Committee, including thresholds for customer impact, financial loss, data compromise, and regulatory notification obligations under BNM reporting requirements.
requirements.rationale: Higher-complexity anomalous activities carry business and systemic risk that cannot be managed solely at the operational level. Escalation criteria operationalise the 'appropriate response' requirement for high-complexity events.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Per-event (escalation criteria reviewed Annually)

requirements.requirement: Establish a business continuity interface between the anomalous activity response framework and the institution's Crisis Management Plan, ensuring that high-complexity flagged activities can trigger continuity protocols without manual re-triage.
requirements.rationale: The requirement to 'respond' at scale to complex anomalous activity implies potential service disruption. Linking operational response to continuity planning fulfils the 'appropriate' standard in the clause and limits customer and operational impact.
requirements.frequency: Integration reviewed Annually; exercised as part of BCP testing

requirements.requirement: Maintain records of all flagged anomalous activities, their assigned complexity tier, investigation findings, and response actions taken, and make these records available to BNM examiners upon request.
requirements.rationale: Evidence of an 'established appropriate response' requires an audit trail demonstrating the framework was applied, not merely documented. Regulatory examination readiness is implicit in the Standard ('S') marker of this clause.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (records retained per BNM data retention policy)

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement detection, triage, and orchestration capabilities that can automatically flag anomalous activities, enrich them with contextual data to determine complexity, and route them to the correct response workflow. Static alerting without complexity-aware routing does not satisfy the clause. The response infrastructure must be validated continuously to ensure investigation tools remain effective against evolving threat patterns.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and maintain a Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) platform with correlation rules tuned to the institution's asset inventory and risk profile, capable of flagging anomalous activities with sufficient contextual metadata (source, asset criticality, user privilege level, time-of-day baseline deviation) to support complexity-tier assignment at the point of alert generation.
requirements.rationale: Flagged anomalous activities can only be triaged by complexity if the alert carries enough context. The SIEM must produce enriched, actionable alerts — not raw log noise — to enable the 'appropriate response based on complexity' mandated by the clause.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; correlation rule review Quarterly; tuning after each significant incident

requirements.requirement: Implement a Security Orchestration, Automation and Response (SOAR) platform or equivalent playbook automation engine with complexity-tiered playbooks for each category of flagged anomalous activity (e.g., insider threat, malware execution, privilege escalation, data exfiltration), automating triage steps for Low/Medium complexity events and escalating High/Critical events to human analysts with full evidence packages.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'appropriate response' calibrated to 'level of complexity'. Automation of lower-complexity responses reduces analyst fatigue and mean-time-to-respond, while automated escalation ensures complex events receive human investigation. Manual-only processes cannot sustain this at operational volume.
requirements.frequency: Playbook review Quarterly; post-incident playbook updates within 30 days of each significant event

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Security Operations Centre (SOC) function — whether in-house or via a certified Managed Security Services Provider (MSSP) — with documented SLAs for alert acknowledgement and initial investigation completion mapped to each complexity tier (e.g., Critical: 15 minutes acknowledgement / 1 hour initial assessment; Low: 4 hours acknowledgement).
requirements.rationale: The obligation to 'investigate and respond' requires continuous human oversight; automated detection alone does not constitute investigation. SLA-tiering directly implements the 'level of complexity' calibration required by the clause.
requirements.frequency: SLA compliance monitored continuously; reported Monthly to CISO; MSSP SLAs reviewed Annually

requirements.requirement: Deploy digital forensics and incident response (DFIR) tooling — including endpoint detection and response (EDR), network traffic analysis (NTA), and log preservation capabilities — so that investigation of high-complexity flagged activities can be performed with forensic rigour, preserving chain of custody for potential regulatory or legal proceedings.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates 'investigate' as a distinct obligation before 'respond'. High-complexity events may involve insider fraud, APT activity, or regulatory reportable breaches requiring forensically sound evidence. Without DFIR tooling, investigation capability is inadequate for complex scenarios.
requirements.frequency: Tool capability reviewed Semi-annually; forensic readiness tested Annually

requirements.requirement: Integrate anomalous activity flagging with identity and access management (IAM) and privileged access management (PAM) systems so that response actions — such as account suspension, session termination, or access revocation — can be executed automatically or at analyst command within the timeframe mandated by the complexity-tier SLA.
requirements.rationale: The ability to 'respond' to flagged anomalous activities, particularly identity-related ones, requires bidirectional integration between detection systems and access control enforcement points. Without this, response actions require manual coordination across teams and cannot meet complexity-tiered timelines.
requirements.frequency: Integration tested Quarterly; reviewed after each significant identity-related incident

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that the anomalous activity response framework is policy-backed, role-accountable, regularly tested, and subject to Board-level oversight. The 'must establish' language in the clause implies a durable, documented obligation — not ad-hoc practice. The Standard ('S') marker indicates BNM expects this framework to be demonstrably operational at all times and auditable on examination.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a board-approved Anomalous Activity Response Policy that mandates the existence of a complexity-tiered response framework, assigns ownership to the CISO, sets minimum performance standards (e.g., response timelines per tier), and requires annual review and Board Risk Committee attestation.
requirements.rationale: The clause uses 'must establish', which requires a formal, durable governance artefact — not informal practice. Board approval of the policy satisfies the 'appropriate response' standard and creates accountability at the highest level of the institution.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee (BRC) / Company Secretary (for board tabling)
requirements.frequency: Policy approved upon establishment; reviewed Annually; re-approved after material incidents or regulatory changes

requirements.requirement: Conduct annual red team or tabletop exercises that simulate flagged anomalous activities across all defined complexity tiers, with results formally reported to the Board Risk Committee and tracked remediation actions assigned to named owners with completion deadlines.
requirements.rationale: An 'established appropriate response' must be demonstrated to work under realistic conditions, not merely documented. Exercises validate both technological and human response capabilities and produce evidence for BNM examination that the framework is operationally effective.
requirements.frequency: At minimum Annually; high-complexity simulations Semi-annually where practical

requirements.requirement: Institute a Post-Incident Review (PIR) process requiring a formal written review for every flagged anomalous activity at Medium complexity or above, completed within 14 days of resolution, with findings and corrective actions submitted to the CISO and reported to the Board Risk Committee Quarterly in aggregated form.
requirements.rationale: The obligation to 'investigate' does not end at containment. Structured PIRs operationalise continuous improvement of the response framework and demonstrate to BNM that the institution learns from each event and refines its 'appropriate response' capability accordingly.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (PIR); Quarterly aggregate reporting to Board Risk Committee

requirements.requirement: Include anomalous activity response capability — including framework coverage, SLA compliance rates, exercise outcomes, and open remediation items — as a standing agenda item in the institution's Cyber Risk Dashboard reported to the Board Risk Committee and Management Risk Committee at least Quarterly.
requirements.rationale: Board and senior management oversight of the response framework is required for 'appropriate' governance. Visibility of operational metrics ensures that resource allocation, staffing, and tooling remain adequate relative to the complexity and volume of flagged activities.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly dashboard reporting; escalation to full Board upon Critical-tier events

requirements.requirement: Ensure the anomalous activity response framework includes documented criteria and procedures for notifying BNM of significant cybersecurity incidents as required under prevailing BNM reporting guidelines, with the Compliance function verifying that notification obligations are met within mandated timeframes for each applicable complexity tier.
requirements.rationale: High-complexity flagged anomalous activities may constitute reportable incidents under BNM's cybersecurity incident reporting requirements. Governance must embed regulatory notification as a response action — not an afterthought — to satisfy both Clause 11.11 and broader BNM regulatory obligations.
requirements.frequency: Per-event for reportable incidents; notification criteria reviewed Annually or upon BNM guidance updates

business.summary: Failure to establish a comprehensive cyber crisis management framework exposes the institution to uncoordinated, chaotic responses during cyber incidents, amplifying financial losses, reputational damage, and regulatory sanctions. The clause directly links crisis management to business continuity and disaster recovery, meaning a gap here can result in prolonged service outages affecting customers and market confidence. Inadequate stakeholder communication during a cyber event also risks regulatory penalties and loss of depositor trust.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain comprehensive cyber crisis management policies and procedures that are formally documented, approved, and integrated into the institution's overall enterprise crisis management plan, covering cyber-attack scenarios specific to the institution's threat profile.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly mandates 'comprehensive cyber crisis management policies and procedures' integrated into the 'organisation's overall crisis management plan'. Without this, the institution operates with siloed response plans that fail during multi-system cyber events, compounding business impact.
requirements.frequency: Annual review and update; immediate review following any material cyber incident or significant change to the threat landscape

requirements.requirement: Develop and operationalise a cyber-specific stakeholder communication plan that defines roles, pre-approved messaging templates, escalation thresholds, and contact protocols for engaging shareholders, BNM, SC, customers, and employees during a cyber incident.
requirements.rationale: The clause specifically mandates 'a clear communication plan for engaging shareholders, regulatory authorities, customers and employees in the event of a cyber incident'. Absence of this plan during an incident results in inconsistent public statements, delayed regulatory notification, and customer panic — all of which attract additional supervisory scrutiny.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Chief Corporate Affairs Officer
requirements.frequency: Annual review; tested at minimum bi-annually via tabletop exercises; activated per-event

requirements.requirement: Ensure cyber-attack scenarios are explicitly embedded within Business Continuity Plans (BCP) and Disaster Recovery Plans (DRP), covering at minimum: ransomware, DDoS, insider threat, and third-party supply chain compromise scenarios relevant to the institution's operations.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates incorporation of 'cyber-attack scenarios and responses' in 'business continuity and disaster recovery planning'. Generic BCP/DRP that lacks cyber-specific triggers and response paths will fail during a real cyber incident, extending Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs) and causing direct customer impact.
requirements.frequency: Annual update to BCP/DRP; scenario review following each material threat intelligence update

requirements.requirement: Define and document clear escalation processes for cyber incidents, including trigger thresholds, internal escalation chains from operational teams to senior management and Board, and external escalation to BNM under mandatory incident reporting obligations.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text references 'escalation processes' as a distinct mandatory component. Poorly defined escalation paths delay decision-making during high-pressure cyber events, risking breach of BNM's mandatory cyber incident reporting timelines and board-level accountability obligations.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; tested per crisis simulation exercise; updated per-event following post-incident review

requirements.requirement: Conduct regular cyber crisis simulation exercises, including tabletop exercises and full-scale crisis drills, incorporating realistic cyber-attack scenarios to validate the effectiveness of policies, communication plans, escalation processes, and BCP/DRP linkages.
requirements.rationale: Policies and procedures have no operational value unless regularly exercised. The regulatory emphasis on 'comprehensive' planning implies tested readiness. Unexercised plans consistently fail during real incidents, exposing the institution to both operational and regulatory risk.
requirements.frequency: Tabletop exercises at minimum annually; full-scale drills bi-annually; ad hoc following significant threat intelligence changes

technology.summary: Technology teams must build and maintain the technical infrastructure that enables the cyber crisis management framework to function in practice — including detection, containment, communication, and recovery capabilities that are pre-integrated with BCP and DRP systems. The CISO is responsible for ensuring that cyber-attack scenarios are technically plausible, detection-to-response pipelines are automated where possible, and that recovery capabilities meet RTOs defined within BCP/DRP. Technology must also enable secure, resilient communication channels that remain available even when primary systems are compromised.

requirements.requirement: Implement and maintain a Security Incident and Event Management (SIEM) platform and Security Orchestration, Automation and Response (SOAR) capability that detects, correlates, and triggers cyber crisis response workflows automatically based on pre-defined cyber-attack scenario playbooks.
requirements.rationale: Effective cyber crisis response requires rapid detection and automated initial containment to reduce dwell time. Without SIEM/SOAR integration with crisis management workflows, the escalation processes mandated by the clause cannot operate at the speed required during active cyber attacks.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; playbooks reviewed and updated quarterly; full system review annually

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain documented technical playbooks for each material cyber-attack scenario incorporated into the crisis management plan (e.g., ransomware, DDoS, data exfiltration, insider threat), specifying containment steps, system isolation procedures, forensic preservation actions, and recovery sequencing aligned to BCP/DRP RTOs.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'cyber-attack scenarios and responses' to be integrated into BCP and DRP. Technical playbooks are the operational implementation of this requirement, giving security and IT teams unambiguous step-by-step actions under crisis conditions when cognitive load is high.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of IT Operations
requirements.frequency: Annual review and update; immediate update following post-incident review or significant infrastructure changes

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain out-of-band, resilient communication infrastructure (e.g., dedicated crisis bridge lines, encrypted messaging platforms, offline contact directories) for use by crisis response teams when primary communication channels are compromised or unavailable during a cyber incident.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates a 'clear communication plan for engaging shareholders, regulatory authorities, customers and employees'. If primary communication infrastructure is compromised during a cyber attack — a common scenario in ransomware events — the institution cannot execute its communication obligations without pre-established out-of-band channels.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Officer (CIO) / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Infrastructure tested quarterly; contact directories reviewed monthly; full failover test annually

requirements.requirement: Implement immutable, geographically separated backup and recovery infrastructure aligned to cyber-specific RTO and RPO targets defined in the BCP/DRP, with validated restoration procedures for all critical systems and data assets, including offline backups protected from ransomware propagation.
requirements.rationale: The clause integrates cyber crisis management with 'disaster recovery planning'. Cyber-aware DR requires backup architectures that survive the attack vector — standard online backups can be encrypted or deleted during ransomware events, making recovery impossible without immutable offline copies.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Operations / Chief Information Officer (CIO)
requirements.frequency: Backup integrity verified weekly; restoration tests conducted quarterly; full DR test annually

requirements.requirement: Integrate cyber incident tracking and escalation tooling with the institution's crisis management platform to provide real-time situational awareness dashboards for senior management and the Board during a cyber crisis, including incident timelines, affected systems, containment status, and regulatory notification status.
requirements.rationale: Effective escalation processes as required by the clause depend on decision-makers having accurate, real-time information. Without integrated tooling, crisis commanders operate on stale or incomplete data, leading to delayed or incorrect decisions that worsen the impact of the cyber event.
requirements.frequency: System maintained continuously; dashboard configurations reviewed semi-annually; exercised during crisis simulations

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that the cyber crisis management framework is embedded into the institution's formal risk governance architecture — with Board-level ownership, clear policy accountability, and defined oversight mechanisms that validate both readiness and post-incident improvement. The clause's 'S' marker reflects BNM's expectation that these are non-negotiable standard practices, and governance must ensure ongoing compliance rather than point-in-time adherence. Regulatory reporting obligations to BNM during and after cyber incidents must be formally governed with assigned accountability.

requirements.requirement: Obtain Board of Directors approval for the Cyber Crisis Management Policy, with explicit Board-level endorsement of its integration into the overall enterprise crisis management framework, BCP, and DRP. Establish a Board Risk Committee standing agenda item for cyber crisis preparedness reporting.
requirements.rationale: The comprehensive and cross-functional nature of the clause — spanning crisis management, BCP, DRP, and external stakeholder communication — requires Board-level ownership to ensure resources, authority, and accountability are appropriately assigned. BNM supervisory expectations hold the Board ultimately accountable for institution-wide resilience.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee Chairman / Board of Directors
requirements.frequency: Policy approved annually; Board-level cyber crisis readiness report tabled semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Appoint a designated Cyber Crisis Coordinator (typically the CISO or delegated senior officer) with formally documented authority, accountability, and succession arrangements to lead the institution's cyber crisis response, coordinating across technology, operations, legal, communications, and regulatory affairs functions.
requirements.rationale: The clause's requirement for 'escalation processes' and a 'clear communication plan' presupposes a single point of command accountability during a cyber crisis. Without a formally appointed and empowered Cyber Crisis Coordinator, response efforts fragment across departmental lines, slowing containment and communications.
requirements.frequency: Appointment reviewed annually; succession arrangements tested bi-annually during crisis exercises

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal BNM Cyber Incident Regulatory Notification Protocol, aligned to BNM's mandatory reporting timelines, that defines trigger criteria, approval authority for notifications, designated regulatory liaison, and documentation standards for all regulatory communications during and following a cyber incident.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly mandates engaging 'regulatory authorities' in the communication plan. BNM's RMiT and associated circulars impose specific notification timelines for material cyber incidents. A governance protocol with assigned accountability prevents ad hoc, delayed, or inconsistent regulatory disclosures that attract further supervisory action.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Compliance Officer (CCO)
requirements.frequency: Protocol reviewed annually; regulatory liaison contacts verified quarterly; activated per-event

requirements.requirement: Implement a mandatory Post-Incident Review (PIR) governance process for all material cyber incidents, producing a formal PIR report with root cause analysis, control failure identification, and time-bound remediation action plans, tabled to Board Risk Committee within 30 days of incident closure.
requirements.rationale: Comprehensive crisis management as required by the clause must be a continuous improvement cycle, not a static document. A governed PIR process ensures that each real or simulated cyber crisis translates into measurable improvements to policies, procedures, technical controls, and communication plans.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (mandatory for all material cyber incidents); summarised in annual cyber risk report to Board

requirements.requirement: Conduct an annual independent assessment of the Cyber Crisis Management framework — encompassing policy adequacy, BCP/DRP integration, communication plan operability, escalation process effectiveness, and technology readiness — by the Internal Audit function or a qualified independent third party, with findings reported to the Board Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: BNM's 'S' marker reflects a standard that must be independently validated, not self-certified. Annual independent assessment provides assurance to the Board, regulators, and stakeholders that the cyber crisis management framework remains fit-for-purpose against an evolving threat landscape, and identifies gaps before they are exposed during an actual incident.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor / Board Audit Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Annual independent assessment; interim management self-assessment semi-annually

business.summary: Failure to maintain a comprehensive and tested CIRP exposes the institution to prolonged operational disruption, regulatory censure under BNM's enforcement framework, and reputational damage that erodes depositor and investor confidence. The five-phase structure mandated by clause 11.13 directly ties cybersecurity posture to business continuity, meaning gaps in any phase can cascade into systemic financial impact. Non-compliance with an 'S' marker clause can trigger supervisory intervention, mandatory remediation timelines, or public enforcement action under FSMA 2013.

requirements.requirement: Formally establish a Cyber Emergency Response Team (CERT) with documented governance charter defining membership, roles, responsibilities, invocation criteria, and escalation paths for all severity levels of cyber incidents.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.13(a) explicitly mandates a 'clear governance process, reporting structure and roles and responsibilities' for the CERT. Without a formalised charter, incident response devolves into ad hoc action, prolonging mean-time-to-contain and increasing business disruption and regulatory exposure.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (invocation); Annual (charter review and role confirmation)

requirements.requirement: Define and publish institution-wide cyber incident classification tiers (e.g., P1–P4) with corresponding business impact thresholds, escalation timelines, and mandatory notification triggers for senior management, Board, and BNM.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.13(a) requires 'invocation and escalation procedures' — a prerequisite for these is a shared classification taxonomy so business units, IT, and governance stakeholders operate from the same severity baseline. Misclassification of a significant incident risks delayed Board and regulatory notification.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (operational); Annual (tier threshold review)

requirements.requirement: Ensure CIRP-aligned Business Continuity Plans (BCPs) exist for all critical business functions, incorporating cyber-specific contingency scenarios as required under clause 11.13(d), and that these plans are tested jointly with technology and operations teams.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.13(d) mandates 'multiple strategies including contingency plans as part of incident recovery to swiftly resume business operations.' BCPs that are not cyber-aware will fail under actual incident conditions, extending recovery time and heightening regulatory and reputational risk.
requirements.frequency: Annual (full review and test); Bi-annual (tabletop exercise)

requirements.requirement: Mandate formal post-incident review meetings within a defined timeframe (e.g., 14 days) following any material cyber incident, with outputs including a Board-level incident report, lessons-learned register, and long-term risk mitigation action plan with assigned ownership and deadlines.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.13(e) explicitly requires 'post-incident review incorporating lessons learned and develop long-term risk mitigations.' Without a structured process, institutional knowledge from incidents is lost and the same vulnerabilities are re-exploited, creating recurring business and reputational risk.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (post material incident); Annual (aggregate lessons-learned review)

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a cyber insurance policy and external incident response retainer agreements (legal, PR, forensics) to support rapid mobilisation of specialist resources during a significant cyber incident, aligned with recovery objectives in clause 11.13(d).
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.13(d) requires strategies to 'swiftly resume business operations' and 'significantly enhance redundancy and resilience.' Delayed access to legal counsel or forensics providers during an active breach materially extends recovery timelines and amplifies financial and regulatory impact.
requirements.frequency: Annual (retainer and policy review)

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement and maintain the detection, containment, forensics, and recovery tooling that underpins all five CIRP phases mandated by clause 11.13. This includes a continuously monitored security operations capability, forensic evidence preservation infrastructure, and tested backup and recovery systems that meet defined recovery time and point objectives. Tooling alone is insufficient — automated capabilities must be integrated with documented runbooks so CERT members can execute consistently under incident pressure.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and operate a 24x7 Security Operations Centre (SOC) or equivalent managed detection and response capability with SIEM, EDR, and network traffic analysis tools capable of identifying points of compromise and generating real-time alerts correlated to threat intelligence feeds.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.13(b) requires 'effective and expedient processes for identifying points of compromise and assessing the extent of damage.' Manual or reactive detection capabilities cannot meet the 'expedient' standard; automated, always-on detection is the technical baseline for this phase.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (operational); Quarterly (detection rule tuning and coverage review)

requirements.requirement: Establish a forensic evidence preservation capability including tamper-evident log archiving (minimum 12 months online, 3 years offline), disk imaging tools, and a documented chain-of-custody process for all evidence collected during an incident.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.13(b) mandates 'preserving sufficient evidence for forensics purposes.' Without immutable, well-documented evidence preservation, post-incident forensic analysis is unreliable, regulatory investigations are impeded, and legal proceedings are compromised.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (log retention); Annual (capability and process validation)

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain technology-specific incident response runbooks for all critical system categories (e.g., core banking, payment systems, cloud infrastructure, endpoints) covering isolation procedures, threat eradication steps, and safe resumption checklists aligned to clause 11.13(c).
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.13(c) requires the institution to 'identify and implement remedial actions to prevent or minimise damage' and 'contain and remove the known threats.' Runbooks operationalise this requirement, ensuring CERT members execute correct containment actions under time pressure without improvisation.
requirements.frequency: Annual (review and update); Per-event (post-incident update if gaps identified)

requirements.requirement: Implement and regularly test immutable, segmented backup systems for all critical systems with defined Recovery Time Objectives (RTO) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPO), validated through scheduled disaster recovery exercises that simulate cyber-specific failure scenarios (e.g., ransomware, data destruction).
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.13(d) mandates 'multiple strategies including contingency plans' and to 'significantly enhance redundancy and resilience.' Backups that are not segmented from production environments are themselves vulnerable to ransomware propagation, rendering recovery impossible and violating the redundancy requirement.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (backup execution); Semi-annual (full DR test); Annual (RTO/RPO validation)

requirements.requirement: Integrate threat intelligence platforms (TIPs) with detection tooling to enable automated ingestion of indicators of compromise (IoCs) and threat actor TTPs, supporting rapid identification and attribution during the detection, containment, and post-incident phases.
requirements.rationale: Clauses 11.13(b) and (e) together require expedient identification of threats and development of 'long-term risk mitigations.' Threat intelligence integration accelerates both the detection phase and the post-incident analysis phase by providing contextual understanding of attack vectors and actor motivations.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (feed ingestion); Quarterly (TIP platform and feed relevance review)

governance.summary: Clause 11.13 imposes governance obligations that span Board accountability, policy lifecycle management, and regulatory reporting — all of which must be formally embedded in the institution's governance framework to satisfy BNM's 'comprehensive' CIRP standard. The Board and senior management are accountable for ensuring the CIRP is not merely documented but operationally embedded, tested, and continuously improved. Governance structures must also ensure that post-incident learnings flow upward to policy and risk appetite updates, closing the feedback loop mandated by clause 11.13(e).

requirements.requirement: Board of Directors must formally approve the CIRP as a governed policy document, review its adequacy at least annually, and receive summary reports on all material cyber incidents including CERT activation outcomes, containment timelines, and post-incident remediation status.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.13(a) establishes a 'clear governance process' as the foundation of preparedness. Under BNM's governance expectations and FSMA 2013, ultimate accountability for risk management rests with the Board — delegating CIRP oversight below Board level without formal reporting lines violates this governance standard.
requirements.owner: Board Chairman / Board Risk Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Annual (CIRP policy approval); Per-event (Board notification for material incidents); Quarterly (Board Risk Committee cyber incident dashboard)

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal BNM incident notification procedure embedded within the CIRP, defining trigger thresholds, responsible notifying officers, mandatory timelines, and required notification content in line with BNM's supervisory reporting expectations.
requirements.rationale: While clause 11.13 does not specify notification timelines explicitly, BNM's broader supervisory framework and the 'escalation procedures' mandated in 11.13(a) inherently include regulatory notification. Failure to notify BNM in a timely manner during a significant cyber incident constitutes a supervisory breach independent of the technical impact.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Compliance Officer (CCO)
requirements.frequency: Per-event (notification execution); Annual (procedure review and regulatory update check)

requirements.requirement: Conduct at least one full-scale CIRP simulation exercise annually, including tabletop or live-fire scenarios that test all five phases of the plan (preparedness through post-incident), with participation from CERT members, senior management, and where appropriate, the Board, followed by a formal after-action report.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.13(a) requires an established governance process for invocation and (e) requires post-incident review incorporating lessons learned. A CIRP that is untested is governance theatre — BNM's 'comprehensive' standard requires demonstrated operational readiness, not just documentation.
requirements.frequency: Annual (full simulation); Bi-annual (tabletop for specific scenarios such as ransomware or supply chain attack)

requirements.requirement: Maintain a centralised CIRP lessons-learned register that tracks findings from every CERT activation and simulation exercise, with mandatory review by the Risk Committee and evidence that findings are converted to measurable, time-bound remediation actions with accountable owners.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.13(e) explicitly mandates 'post-incident review incorporating lessons learned and develop long-term risk mitigations.' A register without a structured review-and-action workflow means findings are documented but not acted upon — failing the 'long-term risk mitigations' requirement and perpetuating institutional vulnerabilities.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (entry creation); Quarterly (Risk Committee review); Annual (strategic risk mitigation prioritisation)

requirements.requirement: Define and enforce third-party cyber incident notification and cooperation requirements in all material vendor and outsourcing contracts, ensuring that incidents affecting outsourced systems or data trigger timely notification to the institution's CERT and do not impede the containment and recovery phases.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.13(c) requires containment and eradication of threats across the institution's environment, which necessarily includes third-party managed systems. Without contractual notification obligations, the institution loses visibility and control over a significant portion of its attack surface during an active incident.
requirements.owner: Chief Procurement Officer (CPO) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Per-contract (inclusion at onboarding); Annual (vendor compliance attestation review)

business.summary: This clause ensures the financial institution can mount a coordinated, competent response to cyber incidents at any hour without delay caused by unfamiliarity or unreachable personnel. Failure to comply risks extended incident dwell times, regulatory censure under RMiT, reputational damage, and potential financial losses from prolonged service disruption. Contactability gaps and knowledge deficiencies in CERT members are among the most common root causes of poor incident outcomes during BNM post-incident reviews.

requirements.requirement: Define and formally document the CERT membership roster, including primary and backup members for each functional role (e.g., Incident Commander, Forensic Analyst, Communications Lead), ensuring 24/7 coverage without single points of failure.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires CERT members to 'remain contactable at all times', which mandates structured rostering with redundancy. Without defined roles and backups, a single unavailable member can paralyse the institution's incident response capability.
requirements.frequency: Reviewed Quarterly and updated upon any personnel change

requirements.requirement: Establish and enforce a mandatory CERT onboarding programme requiring all new CERT members to demonstrate competency in the incident response plan and handling procedures before being assigned to active on-call duties.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires members to be 'conversant with' both the plan and procedures — passive distribution of documents does not satisfy this. Formal competency verification ensures readiness before a real incident, reducing business impact from an unprepared responder.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (upon joining CERT) and annually thereafter

requirements.requirement: Conduct scheduled tabletop exercises and live-fire incident simulations to validate that CERT members remain operationally familiar with the incident response plan and handling procedures, including scenarios covering ransomware, data breaches, and DDoS attacks.
requirements.rationale: Regulatory use of 'conversant' implies active, current knowledge — not merely historical training. Regular exercises maintain proficiency, expose procedural gaps, and demonstrate ongoing compliance to BNM during examinations.
requirements.frequency: At minimum Semi-annually; additionally after any major incident or significant plan revision

requirements.requirement: Define and publish a formal escalation and notification matrix specifying how and when CERT members are contacted during an incident, including escalation thresholds to senior management and BNM reporting obligations under RMiT.
requirements.rationale: Contactability is only operationally meaningful if there is a clear, tested protocol for initiating contact. This directly supports the 'remain contactable at all times' requirement and ensures business-critical decisions are not blocked by communication breakdowns.
requirements.frequency: Reviewed Annually and after each incident debrief

requirements.requirement: Maintain documented evidence of CERT member acknowledgement — signed confirmation that each member has read, understood, and accepted their obligations under the incident response plan and handling procedures.
requirements.rationale: Regulatory compliance requires demonstrable assurance. Acknowledgement records provide audit-ready evidence that the institution has taken affirmative steps to make members 'conversant', directly satisfying BNM's examination expectations for S-marker clauses.
requirements.frequency: Annually and upon any material revision to the incident response plan

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement systems that ensure CERT members can be reached reliably at any time and that authoritative, current versions of the incident response plan and handling procedures are immediately accessible during an incident. The infrastructure must support 24/7 contact capability with redundancy, and training/knowledge management platforms must be in place to verify and sustain member proficiency. All systems supporting contactability and plan access must themselves be resilient and available independent of primary corporate IT infrastructure.

requirements.requirement: Deploy a dedicated out-of-band communication platform (e.g., secure paging system, encrypted mobile application, or dedicated incident bridge line) for CERT member notification and coordination that operates independently of the primary corporate network and email infrastructure.
requirements.rationale: The 'contactable at all times' requirement implicitly recognises that cyber incidents may degrade or destroy primary communication channels. An out-of-band system ensures contactability persists precisely when it is most needed — during active attacks on corporate infrastructure.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; system availability tested Monthly
