requirements.requirement: Implement an on-call scheduling and alerting system (e.g., PagerDuty, OpsGenie, or equivalent) that maintains current CERT contact details, enforces rotation schedules, tracks acknowledgement of alerts, and automatically escalates to backup members if primary contacts do not respond within a defined SLA.
requirements.rationale: Manual roster management is error-prone and cannot guarantee the 'at all times' contactability standard. Automated on-call management with escalation logic directly addresses the risk of unreachable CERT members and creates an auditable contact trail.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; roster accuracy reviewed Weekly; full failover test Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Maintain a secure, offline-accessible (or out-of-band hosted) incident response runbook repository containing the current incident response plan and all handling procedures, ensuring CERT members can retrieve authoritative documents even when corporate systems are unavailable.
requirements.rationale: Being 'conversant with' procedures is of limited value if documents cannot be accessed during an incident. Offline or out-of-band availability ensures the plan remains a working reference tool rather than a document stored only on potentially compromised systems.
requirements.frequency: Repository updated within 5 business days of any plan revision; access tested Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Integrate CERT contact records into a centralised identity and access management (IAM) directory with automated alerts triggered when contact details (mobile numbers, personal email) have not been verified within 90 days, ensuring data currency.
requirements.rationale: Stale contact information directly undermines the 'contactable at all times' requirement. Automated staleness detection prevents silent compliance failure where contact records exist but are out of date and untested.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Head of Identity and Access Management
requirements.frequency: Automated verification every 90 days; full audit Annually

requirements.requirement: Deploy a learning management system (LMS) module or equivalent tracking mechanism to record, monitor, and report completion of CERT training, tabletop exercises, and plan-acknowledgement activities, generating compliance dashboards visible to the CISO.
requirements.rationale: Demonstrating that members are 'conversant with' the plan requires auditable training records. An LMS provides the evidentiary trail necessary for both internal governance and BNM supervisory examinations, and flags members whose certifications have lapsed.
requirements.frequency: Continuous tracking; compliance status reported Monthly to CISO

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that CERT readiness is a formally owned, periodically audited, and board-visible obligation — not an informal operational arrangement. Policies must define minimum standards for member competency and contactability, and management must receive regular assurance that those standards are met. Board-level visibility is warranted given BNM's S-marker designation, which signals that non-compliance is directly assessable during regulatory examinations.

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal CERT Charter or Terms of Reference document that defines membership criteria, minimum competency standards, on-call obligations, contactability requirements, and consequences for non-compliance, formally approved by the Board Risk Committee or equivalent governing body.
requirements.rationale: Without a formal charter, CERT obligations exist only informally and cannot be enforced or audited. BNM's S-marker requirement demands institutional-level commitment, which requires board-level governance documentation that binds the institution and its personnel.
requirements.frequency: Approved at inception; reviewed Annually; updated within 30 days of material change

requirements.requirement: Mandate an annual independent assessment (internal audit or qualified third party) of CERT member readiness, evaluating knowledge of the incident response plan, handling procedures, and contactability controls, with findings reported to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: Independent assurance is the governance mechanism for confirming that 'must ensure' obligations have been met. Self-attestation by the CISO alone is insufficient for an S-marker clause; board-level reporting closes the accountability loop and demonstrates institutional oversight to BNM.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Chief Audit Executive
requirements.frequency: Annually; additional review triggered by any significant incident or material plan revision

requirements.requirement: Incorporate CERT readiness metrics — including percentage of members with current competency certification, on-call coverage gaps, and failed contact attempts from drills — into the institution's quarterly operational risk reporting to senior management and the Board.
requirements.rationale: Governance accountability requires quantified, recurring visibility into compliance status. Embedding CERT readiness in risk reporting ensures management is aware of degradation before an incident exposes it, and provides a documented management response trail for BNM.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting; metrics collected Continuously

requirements.requirement: Define and enforce a Human Resources (HR) policy requiring CERT member contact details to be updated within 24 hours of any change (e.g., new mobile number, change of personal email) and requiring members to acknowledge their on-call obligations annually as a condition of continued appointment.
requirements.rationale: The 'contactable at all times' requirement cannot be delegated solely to technology — it requires a people-governance obligation. An HR-enforced policy creates a contractual accountability mechanism that reinforces the technical controls and provides a remediation pathway for non-compliant members.
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed Annually; individual acknowledgements renewed Annually

requirements.requirement: Establish a post-incident review process that explicitly evaluates CERT member readiness as a root cause dimension — assessing whether any response delay or failure was attributable to insufficient familiarity with the plan or a contactability failure — with corrective action tracked to closure.
requirements.rationale: Governance is only effective if it learns from real events. Embedding CERT readiness as a structured post-incident dimension ensures that clause 11.14 compliance is continuously improved through operational feedback, and that systemic weaknesses are formally closed rather than informally noted.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (after every declared incident); summary trend report Annually to Board

business.summary: Without a dedicated out-of-band communication channel, a financial institution risks complete operational paralysis during a cyber incident that disables primary infrastructure — precisely when coordinated crisis response is most critical. Failure to comply exposes the institution to prolonged incident duration, regulatory censure under RMiT, reputational damage, and potential systemic risk if the institution cannot coordinate with BNM, correspondent banks, or payment infrastructure operators. This clause directly underpins the institution's ability to meet crisis communication obligations to both internal decision-makers and external stakeholders including regulators and customers.

requirements.requirement: Define and maintain a Crisis Communication Strategy that explicitly designates the out-of-band channel as the authoritative communication mechanism when primary infrastructure is compromised or unavailable, covering both internal stakeholders (Board, EXCO, Crisis Management Team) and external stakeholders (BNM, correspondent banks, payment operators, key vendors, major corporate clients).
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates coverage of 'both internal and external stakeholders' — a strategy document that maps stakeholder categories, contact hierarchies, and communication triggers is the minimum business instrument needed to operationalise this obligation.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; immediate update upon material changes to stakeholder landscape or infrastructure

requirements.requirement: Maintain a continuously up-to-date Out-of-Band Contact Directory covering all designated internal crisis roles and external counterparties, including pre-agreed alternate contact numbers, secure email addresses, and physical fallback locations, validated at least semi-annually and immediately following personnel changes.
requirements.rationale: Effective 'coordination and communication' during a crisis is impossible without current, accurate contact information for all relevant parties. Stale contact data renders even a technically sound out-of-band system operationally useless when it is needed most.
requirements.frequency: Semi-annual validation; ad-hoc updates upon personnel or counterparty changes

requirements.requirement: Integrate out-of-band communication procedures into the institution's Business Continuity Plan (BCP) and Cyber Incident Response Plan (CIRP), defining explicit trigger conditions (e.g., confirmed compromise of email, VoIP, or corporate network) that mandate activation of the out-of-band channel.
requirements.rationale: The clause specifies the infrastructure must be available 'if the primary communication infrastructure is compromised or rendered unavailable during a crisis' — embedding activation triggers in existing BCP and CIRP documents ensures the channel is invoked systematically rather than ad hoc.
requirements.frequency: Annual review aligned with BCP/CIRP review cycle; update following each crisis simulation or actual incident

requirements.requirement: Conduct annual tabletop exercises and at least one full live simulation per year that specifically tests out-of-band communication activation, stakeholder reachability, and message integrity across both internal and external channels, with documented results and remediation tracking.
requirements.rationale: A communication infrastructure that has not been exercised under simulated crisis conditions cannot be relied upon as 'reliable' as required by the clause. Exercises validate that the channel functions end-to-end and that staff know how to use it under stress.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Crisis Management Team Lead
requirements.frequency: Annual (tabletop minimum); at least one live simulation annually

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal communication protocol with BNM and relevant industry bodies (e.g., PayNet, PIDM) that pre-registers the institution's out-of-band contact points, ensuring regulatory notification obligations can be fulfilled even when primary systems are unavailable.
requirements.rationale: External stakeholders explicitly include regulators; the institution's ability to notify BNM within mandated timeframes during a cyber incident depends on having a pre-established, mutually known out-of-band channel to the regulator.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Chief Compliance Officer (CCO)
requirements.frequency: Annual registration confirmation; immediate update upon changes

technology.summary: Technology teams must deploy, harden, and maintain a communication infrastructure that is physically and logically independent from the institution's primary network, email, and collaboration systems, ensuring it remains operational even during a full compromise of primary systems. The out-of-band channel must satisfy both the 'secure' and 'reliable' conditions in the clause — meaning end-to-end encryption, strong authentication, high availability, and resilience against the same threat vectors that might disable primary infrastructure. All technical components must be subject to regular testing, patching, and capacity validation independent of the primary IT operations lifecycle.

requirements.requirement: Deploy an out-of-band communication system that is physically and logically segregated from the institution's primary corporate network, hosted on independent infrastructure (separate ISP circuit, dedicated hardware, and optionally a geographically separate data centre or sovereign cloud tenancy), ensuring it remains reachable when primary networks are down or compromised.
requirements.rationale: The clause's requirement for infrastructure that remains available when the 'primary communication infrastructure is compromised or rendered unavailable' makes logical and physical segregation a non-negotiable technical control — a system sharing the same network fabric as primary systems cannot satisfy this requirement.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of IT Infrastructure
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; architecture review annually

requirements.requirement: Implement end-to-end encryption for all out-of-band communications (voice, messaging, and file transfer) using industry-standard cryptographic protocols (minimum TLS 1.2/SRTP for voice, end-to-end encrypted messaging platforms), with cryptographic keys managed independently of the primary PKI infrastructure that may be compromised during a crisis.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates the infrastructure be 'secure' — a communication channel used during a cyber crisis is a high-value interception target; encryption with independently managed keys ensures confidentiality of crisis communications even if the institution's primary PKI or certificate infrastructure is under adversary control.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; cryptographic algorithm review annually or upon NIST/NACSA advisories

requirements.requirement: Implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) using authentication methods independent of the primary identity provider (e.g., hardware tokens, TOTP apps, or PKI smartcards with a separate CA) for all access to the out-of-band communication platform, ensuring access is not gated on systems that may themselves be compromised.
requirements.rationale: If the out-of-band system relies on the same Active Directory, SSO, or identity provider as primary systems, a compromise of those systems also locks out the out-of-band channel. Independent authentication satisfies both the 'secure' requirement and ensures availability of access when primary identity systems fail.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Identity and Access Management
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; access rights review quarterly

requirements.requirement: Ensure the out-of-band communication infrastructure meets a minimum availability SLA of 99.9% (excluding planned maintenance), achieved through redundant components (dual power feeds, multiple ISP uplinks, geographic redundancy), with a documented and tested failover process, and an independent monitoring system that does not rely on primary network management tools.
requirements.rationale: The clause's requirement that the infrastructure be 'reliable' sets a high bar — a system that fails under crisis conditions (e.g., due to power outage co-located with the primary data centre) provides no benefit. Redundancy and independent monitoring are the technical mechanisms for reliability.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Infrastructure / Head of IT Operations
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; failover testing semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Maintain a hardened, pre-provisioned set of out-of-band communication devices (encrypted smartphones, satellite phones, or ruggedised endpoints) in secure physical storage at designated crisis locations (primary and alternate sites), pre-configured with all necessary credentials, contact lists, and applications, with device readiness verified quarterly.
requirements.rationale: Software-only out-of-band channels rely on end-user devices that may themselves be compromised or unavailable during a cyber incident; pre-staged physical devices with independent connectivity (e.g., mobile network, satellite) provide a last-resort communication layer fully independent of primary infrastructure.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly readiness check; immediate replacement upon device failure or compromise

requirements.requirement: Conduct semi-annual technical penetration testing and vulnerability assessments of the out-of-band communication platform, applying patches and security updates on an independent patching schedule to ensure the system does not inherit vulnerabilities from the primary IT environment and remains hardened against compromise.
requirements.rationale: An out-of-band system that is neglected in terms of security hygiene may itself be compromised, negating its value as a resilient alternative. Independent assessment and patching ensures the 'secure' and 'reliable' conditions are maintained over time, not just at deployment.
requirements.frequency: Semi-annual assessment; emergency patching within 72 hours of critical CVE disclosure

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that the out-of-band communication obligation is owned at the executive level, embedded in formal policy, and subject to independent oversight — preventing the common failure mode where technical infrastructure is deployed but never exercised, updated, or audited. Board and senior management must demonstrate active accountability for crisis communication readiness as part of their broader technology risk oversight responsibilities under RMiT. Regular reporting, independent audit, and post-incident review processes are required to provide assurance that the infrastructure will function as designed when a real crisis occurs.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a board-approved Out-of-Band Communication Policy that defines the scope, objectives, minimum security requirements, ownership, testing cadence, and activation criteria for the institution's out-of-band communication infrastructure, reviewed and approved at least annually by the Board or delegated Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: Board-level policy formalises the institution's commitment to the 'establish' obligation in the clause and provides the governance anchor for all subordinate procedures and technical controls. Without a formal policy, implementation and maintenance risk being deprioritised under operational pressures.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Annual policy review and Board approval; ad-hoc review following material incidents or regulatory guidance updates

requirements.requirement: Assign explicit executive ownership of the out-of-band communication infrastructure to a named C-suite officer (typically the CRO or COO), with documented accountability in the institution's Technology Risk Management Framework, and include out-of-band communication readiness as a standing agenda item in the Crisis Management Committee.
requirements.rationale: Regulatory obligations without a named accountable executive tend to fall between functional silos; the clause's mandatory 'shall establish' language requires ongoing institutional commitment that must be anchored to executive accountability to ensure sustained investment and prioritisation.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; accountability reviewed annually as part of performance and risk governance cycles

requirements.requirement: Include out-of-band communication infrastructure status, test results, and identified gaps in the quarterly Technology Risk Report presented to senior management and the Board Risk Committee, with a defined escalation path for critical findings and a remediation timeline tracked to closure.
requirements.rationale: Board and senior management oversight of crisis communication readiness requires structured, periodic reporting — without visibility into test outcomes and infrastructure status, governance bodies cannot discharge their RMiT oversight obligations or make informed risk acceptance decisions.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting; immediate escalation for critical gaps or failed tests

requirements.requirement: Mandate independent internal audit coverage of the out-of-band communication infrastructure at least once every two years, assessing policy compliance, technical control effectiveness, test adequacy, and contact directory currency, with findings reported to the Audit Committee and tracked for remediation by management.
requirements.rationale: Independent assurance is the governance mechanism that validates whether the institution has genuinely 'established' a reliable and secure infrastructure versus merely having documented one — audit findings provide the Board with evidence-based confidence in crisis communication readiness.
requirements.frequency: Biennial independent audit; follow-up review if critical findings are identified

requirements.requirement: Embed a mandatory Post-Incident Review process for any cyber incident where primary communication infrastructure was impacted or where the out-of-band channel was activated, producing a documented lessons-learned report within 30 days that feeds directly into policy, procedure, and technology updates with Board Risk Committee visibility.
requirements.rationale: The clause's crisis-specific scope means real incidents are the most important data points for evaluating whether the infrastructure met the 'secure and reliable' standard — a formal post-incident review loop ensures continuous improvement and demonstrates to BNM examiners that the institution learns from experience.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Crisis Management Committee
requirements.frequency: Per-event (within 30 days of qualifying incident); findings tracked quarterly

requirements.requirement: Establish a third-party and vendor governance requirement ensuring that material external counterparties (correspondent banks, core technology vendors, payment infrastructure operators) have compatible and pre-tested out-of-band communication capabilities, documented in contractual arrangements or bilateral agreements reviewed annually.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly covers 'external stakeholders' — the institution's out-of-band infrastructure only provides value if external parties it must communicate with during a crisis also have functioning, compatible channels. Vendor and counterparty governance closes this dependency gap.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Head of Vendor Management
requirements.frequency: Annual counterparty review; capability testing at least annually

business.summary: Clause 11.16 requires financial institutions to validate that their Cyber Incident Response Plan (CIRP) is operationally effective under realistic threat conditions, not merely documented. Failure to comply exposes the institution to undetected gaps in crisis communication, decision-making, and recovery coordination, which could amplify the impact of a real cyber incident and attract regulatory censure. Board-level participation mandates ensure accountability is embedded at the highest governance tier rather than delegated entirely to technical teams.

requirements.requirement: Conduct a formally scoped annual cyber drill exercise that tests the end-to-end CIRP, incorporating at least two threat scenarios reflective of current and emerging threats (e.g., social engineering, ransomware), with documented objectives, pass/fail criteria, and a post-exercise after-action report.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates an 'annual cyber drill exercise to test the effectiveness of its CIRP based on various current and emerging threat scenarios.' A single generic tabletop with no defined scope does not satisfy this requirement; scenarios must be threat-informed and CIRP-linked.
requirements.frequency: Annual (minimum); scenario refresh recommended biannually to reflect emerging threats

requirements.requirement: Mandate and document the active participation of board members and senior management (C-suite) in the annual cyber drill, with attendance records, individual role assignments, and a post-drill debrief capturing their decision-making performance against predefined impact-level scenarios.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly states involvement of 'members of the board, senior management.' Passive observation does not constitute 'involvement'; board members must be exercised on escalation decisions and communication approvals commensurate with their crisis-response authority.
requirements.frequency: Annual

requirements.requirement: Contractually obligate and operationally include all critical third-party service providers in the annual cyber drill, validating their response SLAs, communication protocols, and recovery support commitments under simulated incident conditions.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires 'involvement of relevant third party service providers' and explicitly tests 'readiness and effectiveness of CERT and relevant third party service providers in supporting the recovery process' under sub-clause (b). Third-party non-participation must be treated as a CIRP gap requiring remediation.
requirements.frequency: Annual; third-party contract reviews at each renewal cycle

requirements.requirement: Establish and operationalise a scenario library covering multiple cyber incident impact levels (low, medium, high, critical) that exercises escalation paths, internal communications, and external notifications (e.g., BNM, customers, media) aligned to the institution's incident classification matrix.
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (a) requires testing 'effectiveness of escalation, internal and external communication and decision-making processes that correspond to different impact levels of a cyber incident.' A single severity scenario leaves untested the escalation triggers and communication cascades specific to other impact bands.
requirements.frequency: Scenario library reviewed and updated annually pre-drill

requirements.requirement: Produce a formal post-drill report within 30 days of each exercise, submitted to the board for deliberation, that records identified CIRP deficiencies, stakeholder performance observations, remediation commitments, and timeline for closure of gaps.
requirements.rationale: The clause states 'the result of the annual cyber drill exercise must be reported to the board in a timely manner.' 'Timely' must be operationally defined; unresolved findings without board visibility represent both a compliance gap and an unmitigated operational risk.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Board Risk Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Annual; ad hoc if significant deficiencies are identified

technology.summary: Technology teams must build and maintain the technical infrastructure that makes cyber drill exercises realistic and measurable, including out-of-band communication systems, CIRP automation workflows, and CERT tooling. The clause's requirement to test 'readiness and effectiveness of CERT' implies that detection, containment, and recovery tools must be exercised under simulated incident conditions, not merely documented. Out-of-band communication channels must be independently operable when primary networks are assumed compromised.

requirements.requirement: Design, deploy, and maintain a dedicated out-of-band communication infrastructure (e.g., encrypted satellite phone network, out-of-band messaging platform, or secure secondary email system) that operates independently of the institution's primary IT environment and can be activated within a defined RTO during a simulated or real cyber incident.
requirements.rationale: The clause states 'the out-of-band communication methods must also be tested regularly as part of the institutions cyber drill exercises.' Out-of-band systems are only effective if they are operationally proven; a system that has never been tested in isolation from the primary network provides false assurance.
requirements.frequency: Tested at minimum annually in the main drill; additionally tested quarterly as a standalone communications check

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain an integrated drill management platform or structured exercise toolkit (injects, scenario scripts, role-player guides, evidence capture templates) that enables realistic simulation of social engineering, ransomware, and data exfiltration scenarios calibrated to the institution's current threat landscape.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires drills based on 'various current and emerging threat scenarios (e.g. social engineering).' Ad hoc or improvised drills cannot reliably test CIRP effectiveness against threat-informed scenarios; a structured exercise toolkit ensures consistency, repeatability, and auditability.
requirements.frequency: Toolkit reviewed and updated semi-annually; deployed annually for the main exercise

requirements.requirement: Conduct a CERT operational readiness assessment as part of each annual drill, validating that CERT tooling (SIEM, EDR, forensics platforms, threat intelligence feeds), runbooks, and personnel response times meet defined SLOs under simulated incident pressure, including handoff procedures to third-party incident response retainers.
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (b) explicitly requires testing 'readiness and effectiveness of CERT and relevant third party service providers in supporting the recovery process.' Technical readiness cannot be assumed from documentation alone; tooling performance under load and personnel competency must be validated empirically.
requirements.frequency: Annual (integrated into drill); quarterly CERT readiness self-assessments

requirements.requirement: Instrument the CIRP workflow with measurable technical metrics (mean time to detect, mean time to contain, escalation trigger latency, recovery time vs. RTO/RPO targets) that are automatically captured during drills, enabling objective post-drill performance analysis and year-on-year benchmarking.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires testing 'effectiveness' of the CIRP; effectiveness is only demonstrable if quantitative baseline metrics exist. Subjective post-drill debriefs without data capture cannot satisfy a regulatory audit request for evidence that the CIRP was meaningfully tested.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of IT Security
requirements.frequency: Metrics framework reviewed annually; data captured per drill and per real incident

requirements.requirement: Integrate third-party service provider technical interfaces (APIs, secure communication channels, shared incident ticketing) into drill scenarios to validate interoperability, failover, and data-sharing protocols under simulated incident conditions where primary channels may be unavailable.
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (b) requires validating 'readiness and effectiveness of relevant third party service providers in supporting the recovery process.' Technical integration points (e.g., managed SOC handoffs, cloud provider failover APIs, telco continuity links) can only be validated through live test execution, not SLA documentation review.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Head of Vendor Management
requirements.frequency: Annual (integrated into drill); interface testing at each major third-party contract renewal

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that the annual cyber drill is a formally governed, board-visible process with clear ownership, defined accountability for remediation, and enforceable third-party obligations. The clause's requirement for board reporting and board member participation elevates this beyond an IT operational activity into a board-governed risk management obligation. Governance must also ensure that out-of-band communication testing and CIRP updates are embedded in a continuous improvement cycle, not treated as one-time exercises.

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Annual Cyber Drill Governance Charter, approved by the Board Risk Committee, that defines the drill's scope, mandatory participants (including board members and named third parties), scenario requirements, success criteria, reporting obligations, and the remediation tracking process for post-drill findings.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates involvement of board members and senior management, timely reporting to the board, and multi-stakeholder coordination. A governance charter operationalises these obligations and creates a binding internal framework that can be produced as evidence during BNM supervisory reviews.
requirements.frequency: Charter reviewed and reapproved annually before the drill cycle commences

requirements.requirement: Mandate that the Board of Directors formally reviews and minutes the annual cyber drill after-action report at a scheduled Board Risk Committee or full Board meeting, with documented deliberation on identified gaps, agreed remediation timelines, and resource allocation decisions.
requirements.rationale: The clause states results 'must be reported to the board in a timely manner.' Board receipt alone is insufficient; formal minuting of deliberations demonstrates that the board exercised its oversight duty and is aware of the institution's CIRP maturity level — a standard BNM supervisory expectation.
requirements.owner: Company Secretary / Board Risk Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Annual; extraordinary session if the drill reveals Critical deficiencies

requirements.requirement: Embed contractual clauses in all critical third-party service provider agreements requiring annual participation in the institution's cyber drill exercises, with non-participation treated as a material contract breach, and maintain a participation tracker reviewed by the Vendor Risk Management Committee.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'involvement of relevant third party service providers' and tests their 'readiness and effectiveness.' Without contractual obligations, third parties can decline participation, leaving a material CIRP gap that BNM would identify as a compliance deficiency during examination.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Vendor Management
requirements.frequency: Contractual requirements reviewed at each vendor renewal; participation tracked annually

requirements.requirement: Establish a CIRP Continuous Improvement Register that formally captures all deficiencies identified in annual drills, assigns named owners and remediation deadlines, tracks closure evidence, and is reviewed quarterly by the Cybersecurity Steering Committee and annually by the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: The drill's regulatory value is realised only when identified weaknesses are remediated before the next exercise. Without a governed improvement register, the institution risks repeating the same deficiencies year-over-year, which is a pattern that would attract heightened BNM supervisory scrutiny.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly progress reviews; annual comprehensive review aligned to next drill cycle

requirements.requirement: Define and enforce a formal Out-of-Band Communication Testing Policy that schedules stand-alone testing of out-of-band channels at minimum quarterly (in addition to the annual drill), documents test results, assigns accountability for channel maintenance, and escalates failures to the CISO and CRO within 24 hours.
requirements.rationale: The clause states out-of-band methods 'must also be tested regularly as part of the institutions cyber drill exercises,' with 'regularly' implying a cadence beyond annual. A standalone policy formalises this obligation, defines 'regularly' operationally, and prevents out-of-band channels from falling into disrepair between annual exercises.
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed annually; testing executed quarterly at minimum

business.summary: Financial institutions face significant financial exposure from cyber incidents and must ensure both internal loss provisions and any cyber insurance arrangements are adequate and operationally fit-for-purpose. Failure to align insurance coverage with actual risk exposure, or allowing IT changes to inadvertently void coverage, can leave the institution unprotected at the moment of greatest need. Additionally, insurer-imposed obligations during a crisis can conflict with the institution's fiduciary duty to customers, creating legal and reputational risk.

requirements.requirement: Conduct and document scenario analysis of extreme adverse cyber events to establish quantified loss estimates, and ensure internal loss provision arrangements are calibrated to those estimates on a defined review cycle.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires loss provision adequacy to be assessed 'based on its scenario analysis of extreme adverse events.' Without quantified scenario outputs, loss provisions cannot be validated against regulatory expectations, leaving the institution exposed to capital inadequacy findings during BNM examinations.
requirements.frequency: Annual minimum; triggered additionally by material changes in the institution's threat landscape or operational profile

requirements.requirement: Where cyber insurance is maintained, formally validate that the policy scope covers all information security event categories and liability types relevant to the institution's business activities, including third-party and regulatory liability exposure.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.17(a) requires the scope of the insurance policy to 'adequately cover the information security events and types of liability that the financial institution is exposed to.' A mismatch between policy scope and actual liability exposure constitutes a material gap that may only be discovered post-incident when claims are denied.
requirements.owner: Chief Financial Officer (CFO) in coordination with Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Annual policy renewal review; ad hoc upon material expansion of digital services or business lines

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal review mechanism to assess whether proposed changes to IT services, systems, or security controls could trigger policy exclusions or breach policy warranties, and require sign-off before such changes are implemented.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.17(b) explicitly requires the institution to 'ensure that any changes to IT services and control measures do not result in unintended exclusions of cover.' Unreviewed IT changes — such as decommissioning a mandated security tool or outsourcing a covered function — can silently void coverage without management awareness.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) in coordination with CISO
requirements.frequency: Continuous; embedded into the change management approval workflow

requirements.requirement: Before and during a cyber incident, actively identify and manage conflicts of interest arising from insurer obligations (e.g., mandatory use of insurer-appointed forensic firms or legal counsel) to ensure those obligations do not override the institution's duty to act in the best interests of its customers and stakeholders.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.17(c) directly addresses the conflict between the insurer's objective to 'minimise the cost of its liability' and the institution's obligation to act in its best interest and that of its customers. Failure to pre-empt these conflicts through contractual negotiation and crisis protocols can result in regulatory censure for customer harm.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) with Legal Counsel
requirements.frequency: Pre-negotiated at policy inception; reviewed at each renewal; activated per-incident

requirements.requirement: Ensure that all insurance policy terms, conditions, warranties, and attestation obligations are formally acknowledged by relevant senior management and reflected in crisis response policies and procedures, including specific protocols for insurer notification and engagement during an incident.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.17(b) requires that terms and conditions 'have them reflected in the crisis response policies and procedures as appropriate.' Insurance policies frequently impose notification timeframes, documentation obligations, and approval requirements that, if missed during a crisis, invalidate claims. Embedding these into crisis runbooks ensures compliance under pressure.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) with Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Reviewed at each policy renewal and after each material crisis response exercise or actual incident

technology.summary: Technology teams must maintain structured capabilities for cyber scenario modelling, asset and control inventory management, and integration of insurance policy obligations into incident response tooling and runbooks. A critical technical responsibility is ensuring that the IT change management process includes an insurance coverage impact gate to prevent inadvertent policy exclusions. Additionally, incident response platforms must encode insurer-specific obligations to surface them at the right moment without subordinating the institution's decision-making autonomy.

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a cyber scenario analysis model capable of quantifying financial impact from extreme adverse events, including ransomware-driven operational shutdown, large-scale data breach with regulatory fines, and third-party supply chain compromise, with outputs that feed directly into loss provision calibration.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires loss provisions to be assessed 'based on its scenario analysis of extreme adverse events.' Technology teams must provide the analytical infrastructure — threat modelling, loss quantification tooling, and historical incident data — to make this assessment defensible. Qualitative estimates will not withstand BNM scrutiny.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) in coordination with Head of Enterprise Risk Technology
requirements.frequency: Model maintained continuously; scenarios refreshed annually or following material threat intelligence updates

requirements.requirement: Maintain a current and comprehensive inventory of IT services, security controls, and data assets, mapped against the cyber insurance policy's coverage conditions and warranties, to enable rapid impact assessment when changes are proposed.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.17(b) requires the institution to ensure IT changes do not create 'unintended exclusions of cover.' Without a maintained mapping of IT assets and controls to policy conditions, it is operationally impossible to assess change impact on coverage. This inventory must be granular enough to surface dependencies on specific tools or configurations that are conditions of coverage.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Chief Technology Officer (CTO)
requirements.frequency: Maintained continuously; formally reconciled against insurance policy conditions at each renewal

requirements.requirement: Integrate an insurance coverage impact assessment step into the IT change management workflow (ITSM/CMDB tooling), requiring the CISO or designated Insurance Liaison to review and approve changes that could affect policy conditions, warranties, or security control attestations before change approval is granted.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.17(b) specifically calls out the risk of IT changes producing 'unintended exclusions of cover.' Embedding this check in the change management system makes it a mandatory control gate rather than a discretionary review, ensuring no change bypasses assessment regardless of urgency or organizational pressure.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Operations / Head of IT Service Management
requirements.frequency: Enforced on every standard and emergency change; reviewed for process effectiveness quarterly

requirements.requirement: Encode cyber insurance policy obligations — including insurer notification timelines, mandatory use of approved vendors, evidence preservation requirements, and claim documentation standards — into the institution's incident response platform (SOAR/ticketing system) as automated workflow prompts that surface at the appropriate incident phase.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.17(b) and (c) require insurance obligations to be reflected in crisis response procedures and actively managed for conflicts of interest. Encoding these into the SOAR or equivalent platform ensures obligations are surfaced to responders in real time without relying on responders recalling policy details under crisis pressure, while the conflict-of-interest flag ensures management is alerted before ceding decision authority to insurer-appointed parties.
requirements.frequency: Configured at policy inception; updated at each policy renewal; tested during tabletop exercises

requirements.requirement: Maintain a cyber incident evidence preservation and chain-of-custody capability (forensic imaging, log retention, access audit trails) that satisfies both internal investigation needs and insurance policy evidentiary requirements, ensuring logs are retained for the minimum period required by the policy.
requirements.rationale: Cyber insurance claims are routinely contested or denied due to insufficient forensic evidence. The institution's obligation under clause 11.17(b) to comply with policy terms includes meeting evidentiary standards. Technology must ensure that log and artefact retention periods, integrity controls, and access restrictions align with what the insurance policy requires to substantiate a claim.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) in coordination with Head of IT Infrastructure
requirements.frequency: Capability maintained continuously; retention periods reviewed at each policy renewal

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that cyber insurance and loss provision arrangements are subject to formal Board-level oversight, with clear accountability for policy adequacy, terms compliance, and conflict-of-interest management. The institution requires documented policies that operationalise the insurance obligations, a defined review cycle aligned to the annual insurance renewal, and a conflict-of-interest escalation protocol that preserves management autonomy during an incident. BNM will expect these arrangements to be evidenced through Board minutes, policy documents, and crisis response procedure records.

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Board or Board Risk Committee mandate requiring annual review and approval of the institution's cyber loss provision adequacy, supported by documented scenario analysis outputs and a comparison against the prior year's provision level with variance commentary.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires review of 'loss provision arrangements to ensure its adequacy' based on scenario analysis. Board-level ownership of this review ensures the decision is made at the appropriate level of authority, creates an audit trail for BNM examination, and drives accountability for capital adequacy relative to cyber risk.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee Chairman / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Annual; extraordinary review triggered by material cyber incidents or significant changes in risk profile

requirements.requirement: Establish a Cyber Insurance Governance Policy that defines roles and responsibilities for policy procurement, scope validation, terms and conditions review, warranty compliance monitoring, change impact assessment, and claims management, with named owners for each function.
requirements.rationale: Clauses 11.17(a), (b), and (c) impose multiple distinct obligations requiring coordinated action across Risk, Technology, Finance, Legal, and Operations. Without a governing policy that assigns explicit ownership, these obligations risk being treated as shared responsibility — effectively meaning no one owns them — creating gaps that BNM will identify as control failures.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) with Board Risk Committee endorsement
requirements.frequency: Reviewed annually at policy renewal; updated following material incidents or regulatory guidance changes

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a documented Conflict of Interest Management Framework specific to cyber insurance, which pre-identifies foreseeable conflicts (e.g., insurer-mandated forensic firms, notification timing, remediation scope decisions), establishes escalation triggers, and designates the authority level required to override or negotiate insurer instructions during an active incident.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.17(c) explicitly requires the institution to 'anticipate and adequately manage any conflict of interest that may arise from the objective of the insurer to minimise the cost of its liability.' Pre-incident governance design is essential: during a live crisis, ad hoc conflict resolution will be too slow and may result in decisions that harm customers or expose the institution to regulatory censure.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) with Legal Counsel and Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Established at policy inception; reviewed annually and after each incident where insurer engagement was required

requirements.requirement: Integrate cyber insurance terms, conditions, warranties, and attestation obligations into the institution's crisis response and business continuity policies and procedures through a formal document management process, with version control and traceability back to the specific insurance policy version from which obligations derive.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.17(b) requires that terms and conditions 'have them reflected in the crisis response policies and procedures as appropriate.' Traceability between policy obligations and procedural controls is necessary both for internal compliance assurance and for demonstrating to BNM that the institution has operationalised its insurance commitments rather than merely acknowledged them.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Business Continuity Management
requirements.frequency: Updated at each policy renewal; validated through tabletop exercises at least annually

requirements.requirement: Establish an annual cross-functional Insurance Coverage Review Committee (or equivalent standing agenda item) comprising Risk, Technology, Finance, Legal, and Operations, tasked with validating that the cyber insurance scope remains aligned to the institution's evolving risk profile, IT environment, and regulatory obligations, with findings reported to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.17(a) requires ongoing assurance that the policy 'adequately covers the information security events and types of liability that the financial institution is exposed to.' As the institution's digital footprint, product set, and threat landscape evolve, coverage adequacy requires active governance rather than point-in-time review at policy renewal, ensuring that gaps are identified and remediated before an incident reveals them.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) as Committee Chair; members include CISO, CFO, General Counsel, and COO
requirements.frequency: Formal committee review annually; interim review triggered by material changes to IT environment, business model, or threat intelligence

11.18.subsection: Cyber Reporting and Threat Information Sharing
11.18.title: Cyber Incident Notification to BNM

business.summary: Failure to notify BNM of cyber incidents in a timely and compliant manner exposes the institution to regulatory sanctions, licence revocation risk, and reputational damage. This clause requires the institution to maintain operationally ready notification processes that align simultaneously with multiple BNM policy instruments, demanding cross-functional coordination between operations, compliance, and senior management. Non-compliance undermines BNM's systemic oversight of cyber risk across the Malaysian financial sector and can result in enforcement action under the Financial Services Act 2013 or Islamic Financial Services Act 2013.

requirements.requirement: Define and document an institution-wide cyber incident classification framework that maps incident types and severity thresholds to mandatory BNM notification obligations under Operational Risk Reporting Part C, Business Continuity Management Part C, and Merchant Acquiring Services paragraphs 19.25–19.26.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text references three distinct BNM policy documents, each with potentially different scope, thresholds, and timelines. Without a unified classification framework, the institution risks inconsistent application — notifying under one policy but not another — leading to regulatory gaps and potential enforcement.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; immediate update upon BNM issuance of new or amended policy documents

requirements.requirement: Establish documented notification timelines and escalation triggers for each referenced BNM policy document, including explicit breach-of-threshold criteria that activate the notification obligation under Operational Risk Reporting Part C, BCM Part C, and Merchant Acquiring Services paragraphs 19.25–19.26.
requirements.rationale: Each BNM policy document may impose distinct notification windows (e.g., within hours for operational risk events). Ambiguity in timelines is a common cause of late notifications, which BNM treats as a separate compliance breach independent of the underlying incident.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (triggered on incident classification); procedural review annually

requirements.requirement: Designate and formally appoint a named Senior Management owner (with documented alternates) who holds ultimate accountability for authorising and submitting cyber incident notifications to BNM, ensuring the obligation is not delegated below an appropriate seniority level.
requirements.rationale: The clause places the obligation on the financial institution as a legal entity. Without clear ownership at senior management level, notifications may be delayed, incomplete, or submitted without appropriate authorisation, exposing senior officers to personal regulatory liability.
requirements.frequency: Role review annually or upon personnel change; execution per-event

requirements.requirement: Maintain a regulatory change monitoring process to identify and incorporate any additional BNM policy documents that specify cyber incident notification requirements, as referenced by the phrase 'any other relevant policy documents specified by the Bank' in the clause.
requirements.rationale: The clause contains a forward-looking obligation — the notification scope is not static and expands whenever BNM issues new policy instruments. Without active monitoring, the institution may be unaware of new notification requirements and be found non-compliant upon examination.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; formal gap assessment quarterly

requirements.requirement: Conduct at least annual tabletop exercises simulating a material cyber incident that triggers notification obligations under all three referenced policy documents simultaneously, with after-action reports submitted to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: In a real incident, the institution must execute multi-policy notification in parallel under time pressure. Untested procedures fail under stress. Simulation validates timelines, ownership, and completeness of notification, and provides documented evidence of due diligence for BNM supervisory review.
requirements.frequency: Annual minimum; additionally after any material cyber incident

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement systems capable of detecting, classifying, and escalating cyber incidents rapidly enough to meet notification timelines prescribed across multiple BNM policy documents. This requires integration between security monitoring platforms, incident management tooling, and regulatory reporting workflows, with full audit trails to demonstrate compliance. Given that notification obligations span Operational Risk Reporting, BCM, and Merchant Acquiring Services policy instruments, the technical architecture must support simultaneous, structured reporting to BNM through its prescribed channels.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and operationalise a Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) platform with correlation rules and automated alerting capable of detecting cyber incidents across all in-scope environments — including merchant acquiring infrastructure — and generating classified incident tickets with severity ratings mapped to BNM notification thresholds.
requirements.rationale: Notification to BNM cannot occur until an incident is detected and classified. The verbatim clause implies comprehensive cyber incident coverage, including merchant acquiring environments (paragraphs 19.25–19.26). Manual detection processes cannot meet the notification timelines prescribed in Operational Risk Reporting Part C.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; correlation rule review quarterly; platform health review monthly

requirements.requirement: Implement a Security Orchestration, Automation and Response (SOAR) or integrated incident management platform that automates escalation workflows, enforces notification deadline countdowns, and routes incident records to designated BNM notification owners with pre-populated report templates aligned to each referenced policy document.
requirements.rationale: Multi-policy notification under concurrent timelines (Operational Risk Reporting Part C, BCM Part C, Merchant Acquiring Services 19.25–19.26) creates coordination complexity that manual processes cannot reliably handle during an active incident. Automated workflows reduce human error and ensure no notification obligation is missed.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; workflow review semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Establish immutable, tamper-evident logging of all cyber incident detection, classification, escalation decisions, and BNM notification submissions — including timestamps, authorising personnel, and content of notifications — stored for a minimum retention period consistent with BNM record-keeping requirements.
requirements.rationale: BNM supervisory examinations will assess both whether notifications were made and whether they were made on time and with complete information. Immutable audit logs provide the evidentiary record required to demonstrate compliance with the notification obligation and defend against regulatory findings.
requirements.frequency: Continuous log capture; log integrity audit quarterly; retention compliance review annually

requirements.requirement: Integrate merchant acquiring transaction monitoring and fraud detection systems with the central incident management platform to ensure cyber incidents affecting merchant acquiring services are automatically detected and flagged for notification assessment under Merchant Acquiring Services paragraphs 19.25–19.26.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly references Merchant Acquiring Services paragraphs 19.25–19.26 as a separate notification instrument. Without dedicated integration, cyber incidents originating in or impacting acquiring infrastructure may be handled as generic IT incidents, bypassing the specific notification requirements applicable to that context.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; integration health review monthly

requirements.requirement: Maintain and test a technology-supported Business Continuity fallback for cyber incident notification — including offline notification templates, out-of-band communication channels, and documented manual escalation procedures — to ensure BNM can be notified even when primary IT systems are compromised or unavailable as part of the incident itself.
requirements.rationale: A material cyber incident may impair the very systems used to manage and report the incident. BCM Part C notification obligations persist regardless of system availability. Without an out-of-band fallback, the institution may be unable to fulfil its notification duty precisely when it matters most.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Resilience / Business Continuity Manager
requirements.frequency: Fallback testing semi-annually; procedure review annually

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that cyber incident notification to BNM is embedded as a formal, board-level obligation with clear accountability, documented policies, and independent oversight rather than treated as an ad hoc operational task. The multi-policy nature of the clause — referencing Operational Risk Reporting, BCM, and Merchant Acquiring Services simultaneously — requires cross-functional governance coordination and regular assurance activities to confirm that notification readiness is maintained. Board and senior management must receive periodic reporting on notification performance as part of their fiduciary oversight of regulatory compliance.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Board-approved Cyber Incident Notification Policy that explicitly incorporates the notification obligations under Operational Risk Reporting Part C, Business Continuity Management Part C, and Merchant Acquiring Services paragraphs 19.25–19.26, with a version control mechanism to incorporate future BNM policy additions referenced by the clause.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim clause imposes a legal obligation on the institution as a whole. Board-level policy approval establishes institutional ownership, creates accountability, and provides the normative framework from which operational procedures, roles, and technology requirements flow. Without a governing policy, implementation is fragmented and unenforceable internally.
requirements.frequency: Board approval upon adoption; annual review; ad hoc review upon BNM policy amendment

requirements.requirement: Define and maintain a Cyber Incident Notification RACI matrix covering all roles involved in incident detection, classification, authorisation, submission, and post-submission follow-up, with explicit designation of the individual accountable for each BNM notification channel referenced in the clause.
requirements.rationale: The clause addresses the institution collectively, but execution requires individual accountability. Ambiguity in roles during a live incident — particularly across multiple simultaneously triggered policy notification streams — is the primary cause of delayed or incomplete BNM notifications, which constitute separate regulatory breaches.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; immediate update upon organisational restructuring or personnel changes

requirements.requirement: Mandate quarterly reporting to the Board Risk Committee on the status of cyber incident notification capability, including: number and nature of incidents assessed against notification thresholds, notifications submitted to BNM with timeliness metrics, gaps identified in notification procedures, and remediation status.
requirements.rationale: Board oversight of notification compliance requires structured, recurring information. Without periodic reporting, the Board cannot discharge its fiduciary duty to ensure the institution meets its regulatory obligations under this clause. BNM examiners routinely assess Board-level awareness of notification performance.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting to Board; annual summary in Board Risk Report

requirements.requirement: Establish an Internal Audit programme that includes annual independent testing of end-to-end cyber incident notification readiness, covering detection-to-notification timelines, accuracy of policy document mapping, RACI adherence, and completeness of notification content against each referenced BNM policy document.
requirements.rationale: Self-assessment by first and second lines of defence is insufficient to provide the independent assurance required for a Critical regulatory obligation. Internal Audit provides the third-line assurance that governance controls are operating effectively and that the institution can demonstrate compliance readiness to BNM upon examination.
requirements.frequency: Annual audit; targeted follow-up review 6 months after any material notification event or audit finding

requirements.requirement: Implement a post-notification review process requiring a formal Root Cause Analysis (RCA) and lessons-learned report to be completed within 30 days of any BNM cyber incident notification, with findings reviewed by the Management Risk Committee and material findings escalated to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: The notification obligation does not end at submission. Continuous improvement of detection, classification, and notification processes requires structured post-event analysis. BNM expects institutions to demonstrate learning from incidents, and the Management and Board review creates accountability for remediation of identified gaps.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (within 30 days of each notification); aggregate trend review quarterly

11.19.subsection: Cyber Reporting and Threat Information Sharing
11.19.title: Cyber Threat Intelligence Sharing

business.summary: Financial institutions are obligated to actively contribute to the collective cyber defence of the Malaysian financial sector by sharing relevant threat intelligence through authorised platforms in a timely manner. Failure to comply exposes the institution to regulatory sanctions, reputational damage, and broader systemic risk to the financial sector. Non-participation in industry initiatives signals a breakdown in the institution's commitment to sector-wide resilience, which BNM scrutinises during supervisory assessments.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formal Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) sharing policy that mandates participation in BNM-designated, industry-operated, and law enforcement-affiliated threat sharing platforms, including FinSAC and FS-ISAC, with explicitly defined timeliness thresholds for different threat severity levels.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires sharing 'on a timely basis via the relevant sharing platforms developed by the Bank, the industry, or law enforcement agencies.' Without a formal policy defining timeliness and approved platforms, the institution cannot demonstrate compliance during BNM supervisory reviews.
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed annually; sharing obligations are continuous and event-driven

requirements.requirement: Formally allocate dedicated budget and human resources — including named personnel with defined responsibilities — to participate in industry-wide cyber threat intelligence initiatives such as BNM's FinSAC working groups, CyberSecurity Malaysia exercises, and Financial Services Information Sharing and Analysis Center (FS-ISAC) programmes.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly states the institution 'must allocate resources to participate in any industry-wide initiatives.' Resource allocation must be deliberate, documented, and approved at senior management level to withstand regulatory scrutiny and ensure continuity of participation.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Chief Operating Officer (COO)
requirements.frequency: Annual budget cycle; participation tracked quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement a pre-sharing review process that assesses all cyber threat intelligence against applicable data protection laws — including the Personal Data Protection Act 2010 (PDPA) and any customer confidentiality obligations — to ensure shared intelligence is appropriately anonymised or redacted before dissemination.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim clause opens with 'Subject to the applicable data protection laws,' making legal compliance a prerequisite condition. Sharing unredacted intelligence that contains personal data or proprietary customer information exposes the institution to PDPA enforcement action and civil liability, independent of RMiT obligations.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Group Legal Counsel
requirements.frequency: Per-event (applied to every CTI submission before sharing)

requirements.requirement: Define and document the institution's participation commitments in a CTI Sharing Register, tracking all platforms subscribed to, types of intelligence shared, frequency of contribution, and industry working groups attended, to serve as evidence of compliance for BNM examinations.
requirements.rationale: BNM's supervisory assessments under RMiT require demonstrable evidence of compliance. A participation register provides an audit trail directly traceable to the clause's requirements for timely sharing and resource allocation, enabling management to identify gaps and demonstrate sustained engagement.
requirements.frequency: Updated per-event; reviewed and certified quarterly

requirements.requirement: Establish mutual benefit agreements or memoranda of understanding (MoUs) with peer financial institutions and relevant industry bodies to formalise the terms of bilateral and multilateral threat intelligence exchange, including scope, classification, handling obligations, and liability limitations.
requirements.rationale: While the clause mandates sharing through platforms, formalised agreements protect the institution's legal position, govern information handling by recipients, and ensure reciprocal intelligence flows that improve the institution's own threat posture — directly supporting the 'collective threat intelligence capabilities' objective stated in the verbatim text.
requirements.owner: Group Legal Counsel / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Established at onboarding; reviewed biennially or upon material change

technology.summary: Technology teams must build and maintain the integrations, tooling, and data pipelines required to receive, process, enrich, and disseminate cyber threat intelligence through BNM-approved and industry-recognised platforms in a timely, automated, and legally compliant manner. This includes both inbound consumption of threat feeds and outbound sharing of indicators of compromise (IOCs) and threat reports. Systems must enforce data classification and anonymisation controls before any intelligence leaves the institution's perimeter.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and operationalise integration connectors to the mandated CTI sharing platforms — including BNM's FinSAC portal, CyberSecurity Malaysia's MyCERT, and FS-ISAC's TAXII/STIX feeds — enabling both automated ingestion of incoming threat intelligence and structured submission of the institution's own IOCs, threat actor TTPs, and incident indicators.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates sharing 'via the relevant sharing platforms developed by the Bank, the industry, or law enforcement agencies.' Without technical integration, the institution must rely on manual processes that cannot meet the 'timely basis' requirement at operational scale, particularly during active incident response.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; integration health reviewed monthly

requirements.requirement: Implement a Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP) — such as OpenCTI, MISP, or an equivalent commercial solution — capable of aggregating, correlating, and normalising CTI from multiple sources using STIX 2.1 and TAXII 2.1 standards, and automating the preparation and transmission of outbound threat intelligence packages to sharing platforms.
requirements.rationale: Timely sharing as required by the clause demands automation; manual compilation and submission of threat intelligence is error-prone and too slow for operational threat environments. A TIP enables structured, standardised, and auditable intelligence sharing that meets both the timeliness and data quality expectations of industry platforms.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; platform and feed review quarterly

requirements.requirement: Develop and enforce automated data sanitisation and anonymisation pipelines that strip, mask, or pseudonymise any personal data, customer-identifiable information, or proprietary institution data from threat intelligence artefacts prior to outbound sharing — with mandatory pipeline traversal enforced as a technical control, not relying solely on manual review.
requirements.rationale: The clause's predicate condition 'subject to applicable data protection laws' mandates that no PDPA-regulated personal data is included in shared intelligence. Technical enforcement — rather than process-only controls — provides the strongest assurance and removes human error from the pre-sharing compliance gate.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Data Protection Officer (DPO)
requirements.frequency: Applied to every outbound intelligence package; control reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Instrument comprehensive audit logging of all CTI sharing activities — capturing what intelligence was shared, when, to which platform, by which system or analyst, and what data sanitisation checks were applied — with logs stored in a tamper-evident, SIEM-integrated repository retained for a minimum of five years.
requirements.rationale: Demonstrable compliance with the clause's timeliness and platform requirements during BNM examinations requires verifiable audit trails. Logging also supports post-incident forensic review and enables management reporting on the institution's contribution to collective threat intelligence as required under the resource allocation obligation.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Security Operations / CISO
requirements.frequency: Continuous logging; log integrity and retention reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Integrate received threat intelligence from sharing platforms into the institution's Security Operations Centre (SOC) detection and response workflows — including automated IOC ingestion into SIEM, firewall, and endpoint detection tools — ensuring that shared intelligence translates into improved defensive capability rather than passive accumulation.
requirements.rationale: The clause's objective of 'improving collective threat intelligence capabilities' implies the institution must both contribute to and benefit from shared intelligence. Technical operationalisation of received CTI demonstrates good-faith participation and directly improves the institution's own security posture, which BNM may assess as part of holistic RMiT evaluations.
requirements.owner: Head of Security Operations (SOC Manager) / CISO
requirements.frequency: Continuous; effectiveness reviewed quarterly via threat hunting metrics

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that the institution's CTI sharing obligations are owned at senior management level, resourced appropriately through formal budget processes, and subject to independent oversight that verifies both timeliness of sharing and active participation in industry initiatives. Board and senior management accountability must be clearly established so that compliance is not treated as a discretionary operational activity. Regular reporting to the Board Risk Committee (BRC) on sharing activity, gaps, and industry engagement outcomes is required to demonstrate sustained institutional commitment.

requirements.requirement: Approve and maintain a Board-endorsed CTI Sharing and Industry Participation Policy that defines the institution's obligations under Clause 11.19, assigns executive ownership to the CISO, establishes timeliness standards for sharing at each threat classification level, identifies approved platforms, and outlines the PDPA compliance pre-conditions — reviewed and re-approved annually by the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: A Board-approved policy establishes the legal and regulatory basis for the institution's CTI activities, allocates accountability to senior management, and signals to BNM that compliance is embedded at the governance level rather than treated as a discretionary technical function. The clause's use of 'must' establishes a mandatory obligation that requires formal governance ownership.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee (BRC) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Annual policy review and re-approval

requirements.requirement: Establish a quarterly senior management reporting mechanism — reported to the Risk Management Committee (RMC) and summarised to the Board Risk Committee — covering the institution's CTI sharing activity metrics, timeliness performance against defined thresholds, list of industry initiatives participated in, resources allocated, and any identified compliance gaps.
requirements.rationale: Governance oversight requires visibility. Without structured reporting, senior management cannot confirm that the 'timely basis' sharing requirement and 'resource allocation' obligation are being met. The reporting cadence ensures accountability and provides an early-warning mechanism for BNM examination readiness.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting; annual attestation to the Board

requirements.requirement: Include CTI sharing capability and industry participation as a standing agenda item in the institution's annual technology and cybersecurity budget planning process, with the CISO formally submitting resource requirements — headcount, tooling, platform subscription fees, and training — for RMC and Board approval, ensuring multi-year funding continuity.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly states the institution 'must allocate resources.' Governance must translate this obligation into a funded mandate through the formal budget process. Ad-hoc or discretionary resourcing creates compliance risk if funding is withdrawn during cost-cutting cycles without Board-level awareness of the regulatory implications.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Chief Financial Officer (CFO) / CISO
requirements.frequency: Annual budget cycle; mid-year variance review

requirements.requirement: Commission an annual independent internal audit of the institution's CTI sharing practices, covering: completeness and timeliness of intelligence shared, adherence to the pre-sharing PDPA compliance process, verification of active participation in industry initiatives, adequacy of allocated resources, and accuracy of the CTI Sharing Register — with findings reported to the Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: Independent assurance is required to validate that management's self-reported compliance position is accurate. An annual audit creates a feedback loop between operational practice and governance oversight, identifies systemic gaps before BNM examinations, and demonstrates to the regulator that the institution's compliance function actively monitors adherence to Clause 11.19.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Audit Committee
requirements.frequency: Annual; ad-hoc review triggered by material incidents or regulatory enquiries
