requirements.requirement: Establish a Legal and Compliance review gate — led by the Group Legal Counsel and Data Protection Officer — within the CTI sharing workflow to provide standing legal guidance on the PDPA and Banking Secrecy Act boundaries applicable to each category of threat intelligence, updated whenever relevant laws or BNM guidance change, and documented in a Sharing Eligibility Framework.
requirements.rationale: The clause's predicate condition 'subject to applicable data protection laws' creates a legal compliance obligation that must be institutionalised through a governance process, not left to ad-hoc legal advice. A Sharing Eligibility Framework provides CISO and technical teams with pre-cleared guidance, reducing delay in timely sharing while managing legal risk — directly reconciling the tension between the timeliness mandate and the data protection constraint.
requirements.owner: Group Legal Counsel / Data Protection Officer (DPO)
requirements.frequency: Framework reviewed semi-annually or upon legislative change

11.20.subsection: Cyber Reporting and Threat Information Sharing
11.20.title: Stakeholder Collaboration on Cyber Threats

business.summary: Failure to actively collaborate with stakeholders and authorities in combating cyber threats exposes the institution to prolonged incident response times, regulatory censure, and reputational damage arising from isolated threat management. Active collaboration enables early warning of sector-wide attacks, reduces breach impact, and demonstrates to BNM that the institution is a responsible participant in Malaysia's national cyber resilience ecosystem. Non-compliance with this Standard clause risks supervisory action and exclusion from critical threat intelligence networks that could materially affect operational continuity.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain formal collaboration relationships with all relevant Malaysian authorities including Bank Negara Malaysia (BNM), the National Cyber Security Agency (NACSA), Royal Malaysia Police (PDRM) Cyber Crime Division, and CyberSecurity Malaysia, documented via signed Memoranda of Understanding or equivalent engagement agreements.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates cooperation with 'relevant stakeholders and authorities' — undefined generality must be operationalised through concrete, documented relationships. Without formalised agreements, collaboration is ad-hoc and non-demonstrable to regulators during examination.
requirements.frequency: Per-event establishment; Annual review of active agreements

requirements.requirement: Enroll and maintain active membership in at least one Financial Services sector cyber threat intelligence sharing body, such as the Financial Institutions Information Sharing and Analysis Center (FS-ISAC) or equivalent BNM-recognised Malaysian financial sector forum, with designated staff who actively participate in information exchange sessions.
requirements.rationale: 'Collaborate and cooperate closely' implies ongoing, structured engagement rather than passive membership. Sector-level ISACs provide the operational mechanism for sharing actionable threat intelligence among peers, directly enabling the 'combating cyber threats' objective of the clause.
requirements.frequency: Continuous membership; Minimum quarterly active participation

requirements.requirement: Designate a named senior business owner (minimum Head of Department level) as the institution's primary point of contact for each relevant authority and stakeholder body, with documented escalation paths, contact directories, and availability commitments for cyber crisis scenarios.
requirements.rationale: Effective collaboration requires identifiable, accountable representatives. 'Closely' in the clause implies proximity and responsiveness — an anonymous or rotating contact undermines the quality of cooperation expected by BNM and peer institutions during active threat events.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; Contact directory reviewed semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Allocate dedicated budget for participation in national and sector cybersecurity exercises (e.g., BNM-led FINEX, NACSA CyberSAFE exercises), joint tabletop simulations with peer institutions, and threat intelligence platform subscriptions, with Board visibility on expenditure.
requirements.rationale: Combating cyber threats through collaboration requires operational investment. Budget inadequacy is a common barrier that converts a regulatory obligation into a paper exercise. Explicit budget allocation ensures 'collaboration' is substantive and evidenceable.
requirements.frequency: Annual budget planning; Expenditure reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a Stakeholder Collaboration Register listing all authorities and stakeholder bodies, the nature of the relationship, collaboration frequency, information classification agreements, and last engagement date, made available to BNM upon supervisory request.
requirements.rationale: BNM examiners assessing compliance with clause 11.20 will require evidence of 'close collaboration.' A maintained register transforms intent into demonstrable, auditable proof of ongoing cooperation with relevant parties.
requirements.frequency: Continuous updates; Formal review quarterly

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement the infrastructure and tooling necessary to operationalise collaboration at machine speed, ensuring threat intelligence can be ingested from, and shared with, authorities and peers in a secure, timely, and standardised manner. Platforms supporting STIX/TAXII protocols, secure communication channels, and automated IOC enrichment are the foundation of technically meaningful collaboration under this clause. Integration with national platforms such as MySERT and BNM's reporting infrastructure is specifically required to satisfy the 'authorities' dimension of the clause.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and operate a Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP) — such as MISP, OpenCTI, or a commercial equivalent — capable of ingesting, normalising, and sharing cyber threat intelligence in STIX 2.x / TAXII 2.x formats with external stakeholders and authority feeds, integrated with the institution's SIEM and SOAR toolchain.
requirements.rationale: Standardised threat intelligence exchange is the technical enabler of 'close collaboration.' Without a TIP, intelligence sharing is manual, slow, and error-prone. STIX/TAXII interoperability is the international standard expected by FS-ISAC and peer institutions, and increasingly by BNM for structured threat data submission.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; Platform capability review annually

requirements.requirement: Establish secure, encrypted, and authority-compliant communication channels (e.g., encrypted email via S/MIME, secure portal access, dedicated VPN links) for real-time and emergency information exchange with BNM, NACSA, CyberSecurity Malaysia MySERT, and PDRM, tested and verified to be operational at all times.
requirements.rationale: Clause 11.20 requires cooperation with 'authorities' — this is only achievable if technical communication paths to those authorities are pre-established, verified, and resilient. Discovering a broken communication link during an active cyber incident directly contradicts the obligation to 'cooperate closely.'
requirements.frequency: Continuous availability; Connectivity testing quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement automated Indicator of Compromise (IOC) ingestion pipelines that consume threat feeds from BNM-authorised sources, FS-ISAC, CyberSecurity Malaysia, and NACSA, propagating validated IOCs into network security controls (firewall, IDS/IPS, EDR, email gateway) within defined SLAs — target under 1 hour from feed publication to control enforcement.
requirements.rationale: Collaboration on cyber threats only produces security value if shared intelligence is operationalised. Automated ingestion ensures that information received from stakeholders and authorities is acted upon at machine speed, directly fulfilling the 'combating cyber threats' purpose of the clause.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; SLA compliance reviewed monthly

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a secure API or portal integration with BNM's cyber incident reporting system and MySERT's national threat reporting interface, enabling the institution to submit structured cyber incident notifications and threat intelligence contributions in the formats and timelines prescribed by those authorities.
requirements.rationale: Cooperation with 'authorities' includes proactive outbound sharing, not only inbound consumption. Technical integration with authority reporting systems transforms the legal obligation into an operational capability that can be exercised under time pressure during an active event.
requirements.frequency: Integration maintained continuously; Tested semi-annually via dry-run submissions

requirements.requirement: Implement data classification and anonymisation controls within the TIP and sharing pipelines to enable the institution to share threat intelligence externally without inadvertently exposing customer PII, proprietary business information, or details that could reveal internal security architecture weaknesses, in compliance with PDPA 2010.
requirements.rationale: Effective collaboration requires willingness to share — but sharing must be governed by privacy and confidentiality boundaries. Without automated redaction and classification tagging, legal risk will inhibit sharing, undermining the clause's intent. This control makes 'close collaboration' legally safe to execute.
requirements.frequency: Controls reviewed annually; Classification tags validated per-sharing event

governance.summary: Governance structures must institutionalise collaboration as a standing obligation with Board visibility, defined policies, and accountable roles — rather than leaving it as an informal, personality-dependent activity. A Board-approved Cyber Threat Collaboration Policy, a formal stakeholder engagement programme, and periodic Board reporting on the effectiveness of collaboration are the minimum governance foundations required for sustained compliance with clause 11.20. These structures ensure that 'close collaboration' survives leadership changes and is embedded in the institution's operating model.

requirements.requirement: The Board of Directors must approve a Cyber Threat Information Sharing and Stakeholder Collaboration Policy that mandates the institution's participation obligations, defines the categories of relevant stakeholders and authorities, prescribes information classification requirements for shared intelligence, and assigns accountability to named senior management roles.
requirements.rationale: The clause imposes an obligation on 'a financial institution' as a legal entity — which requires Board-level ownership, not just operational execution. A Board-approved policy provides the mandate, resource authority, and accountability framework within which all collaboration activities operate.
requirements.frequency: Initial approval; Annual review and reaffirmation

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Cyber Threat Collaboration Programme governed by the CISO, with documented engagement plans for each authority and stakeholder body, defined contribution commitments (e.g., minimum threat intelligence submissions per quarter), escalation procedures for joint incident response, and a programme review cycle.
requirements.rationale: 'Collaborate and cooperate closely' sets a qualitative standard of engagement intensity. A structured programme with defined contribution targets prevents the institution from claiming compliance through passive membership alone, and provides auditable evidence of active, sustained cooperation.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; Programme effectiveness reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Include a standing agenda item on Cyber Threat Collaboration effectiveness in Board Risk Committee meetings, presenting metrics on intelligence sharing activity, participation in joint exercises, engagement with BNM and NACSA, and outcomes of collaborative threat mitigation efforts, with minutes evidencing Board oversight.
requirements.rationale: Board-level oversight of collaboration effectiveness is required for the institution to demonstrate that the obligation is embedded at the highest level of governance. BNM supervisors will assess whether the Board is genuinely engaged with this obligation or whether it is managed entirely below senior management visibility.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly Board Risk Committee reporting

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a Joint Incident Response and Collaboration Protocol agreed with BNM and, where applicable, NACSA and PDRM, that specifies the institution's obligations for information sharing during active cyber incidents, communication timelines, escalation triggers, and mutual support expectations.
requirements.rationale: Effective cooperation during a live cyber threat event cannot be improvised. A pre-agreed protocol with authorities operationalises the 'closely' qualifier in the clause — ensuring that when threats materialise, collaboration is governed, not chaotic. This also satisfies BNM's expectation of preparedness demonstrated through documented arrangements.
requirements.frequency: Agreement established and maintained continuously; Protocol reviewed annually and after any invocation

requirements.requirement: Conduct an annual Collaboration Effectiveness Assessment — independently reviewed by Internal Audit or a qualified third party — evaluating whether the institution's stakeholder and authority collaboration activities are substantive, current, and commensurate with the institution's risk profile, with findings reported to the Board Risk Committee and action plans tracked to closure.
requirements.rationale: Independent assurance of collaboration quality prevents the governance structure from becoming ceremonial. An annual assessment provides the Board and BNM with objective evidence that the institution's compliance with clause 11.20 is genuine and improving, not merely documented on paper.
requirements.frequency: Annual; Findings tracked quarterly until remediated

business.summary: Failure to extend the Cyber Risk Framework (CRF) to digital services exposes the institution to customer data breaches, fraudulent transactions, and regulatory sanctions. Each digital delivery channel carries distinct risk profiles; unaddressed gaps in channel-specific security controls create material liability in impersonation, unauthorised transactions, and loss of customer trust. BNM's 'S' marker signals that these controls are non-negotiable baseline obligations for continued licensing and operation.

requirements.requirement: Formally expand the institution's Cyber Risk Framework (CRF) to explicitly include all digital service delivery channels, with channel-specific risk appetite statements, control objectives, and accountable owners approved by the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text states the institution 'must expand its CRF' — this is a mandatory structural change to existing risk governance, not merely an operational adjustment. Failure to document this expansion leaves the institution unable to demonstrate compliance to BNM examiners and creates gaps in risk ownership across channels.
requirements.frequency: One-time implementation; annual review and reaffirmation thereafter

requirements.requirement: Define and maintain a comprehensive inventory of all digital service delivery channels (e.g., mobile banking, internet banking, API-based services, ATM, kiosk) and map each channel to its applicable minimum-security controls as prescribed in Appendices 2, 3, 4 and 11 of BNM RMiT.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.1(a) requires adopting minimum-security controls 'for respective delivery channels' — implying channel-specific, not generic, controls. Without a channel inventory, the institution cannot demonstrate that each channel has been assessed and adequately protected, exposing it to enforcement action.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Head of Digital Banking
requirements.frequency: Maintained continuously; reviewed upon launch of any new digital channel

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal programme to protect the confidentiality and integrity of all customer and counterparty information and transactions processed across digital channels, with documented data classification, handling standards, and breach response procedures.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.1(a) explicitly mandates 'confidentiality and integrity of customer and counterparty information and transactions'. Breaches of customer data on digital channels carry dual liability: regulatory penalties under RMiT and PDPA, and reputational damage that directly impacts customer retention and licence standing.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; breach response exercised at minimum annually

requirements.requirement: Implement and periodically review fraud risk controls aligned to authentication and transaction authorisation requirements across digital channels, with quantified fraud loss thresholds and escalation procedures to senior management.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.1(b) specifically links authentication and authorisation controls to mitigating 'impersonation and fraud risk'. Business leadership must own fraud outcomes, not merely delegate them to technology teams — fraud losses on digital channels are a direct business risk requiring management-level accountability and monitoring.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Fraud Risk Management
requirements.frequency: Quarterly fraud risk review; continuous transaction monitoring

requirements.requirement: Ensure business continuity and operational resilience plans account for the physical and logical security controls referenced in clause 12.1(c), including scenarios where physical or logical access controls are compromised across digital service infrastructure.
requirements.rationale: Strong physical and logical controls, as required by clause 12.1(c), underpin the availability and integrity of digital services. Business owners must understand the operational consequences of control failure (e.g., data centre physical breach, privileged access compromise) and ensure recovery objectives are reflected in BCP/DRP documentation.
requirements.frequency: Annual BCP review; tested semi-annually

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement and continuously validate a layered security control stack covering each digital delivery channel — encompassing encryption, access controls, authentication mechanisms, and physical infrastructure security — as detailed in RMiT Appendices 2, 3, 4 and 11. Authentication and transaction authorisation controls must be sufficiently strong to prevent impersonation and fraudulent transactions at scale. All controls must be subject to regular technical assessment, not merely deployed once and forgotten.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and continuously maintain channel-specific minimum-security controls as defined in RMiT Appendices 2, 3, 4 and 11, including but not limited to: encryption-in-transit and at-rest for all digital channel data flows, API security gateways, session management controls, and network segmentation between digital service tiers.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.1(a) mandates 'adopt and regularly assess the minimum-security controls for respective delivery channels' with explicit reference to Appendices 2, 3, 4 and 11. Generic security controls are insufficient — controls must be tailored and demonstrably mapped to each channel's technical architecture and threat model.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of IT Security
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; formal assessment at least annually and upon material change

requirements.requirement: Implement strong multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all customer-facing digital channels, with authentication strength calibrated to transaction risk level (e.g., step-up authentication for high-value or anomalous transactions), in line with Appendix 3 minimum-security controls.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.1(b) explicitly requires 'proper authentication of users or devices' to mitigate impersonation risk. Weak or single-factor authentication on digital channels is a primary vector for account takeover fraud. Appendix 3 provides the minimum baseline — implementations must meet or exceed these requirements.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; authentication mechanisms reviewed annually and after fraud incidents

requirements.requirement: Implement transaction authorisation controls for all digital channel transactions, including real-time transaction risk scoring, out-of-band authorisation for high-risk transactions, and automated blocking or step-up challenges for transactions exceeding defined risk thresholds, as per Appendix 3.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.1(b) separately mandates 'authorization of transactions' as distinct from user authentication — recognising that a legitimately authenticated session can still be used to execute fraudulent transactions. Risk-based transaction authorisation is essential to detect and block authorised-push-payment fraud and session hijacking.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Security / Head of Digital Banking Technology
requirements.frequency: Continuous real-time operation; rule-set reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement and enforce logical access controls across all digital service infrastructure, including role-based access control (RBAC), privileged access management (PAM), just-in-time access provisioning, and comprehensive access audit logging with tamper-evident storage.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.1(c) mandates 'strong logical control measures'. Uncontrolled privileged access to digital service infrastructure represents a critical insider threat and supply-chain risk vector. Logical controls must prevent unauthorised access to production systems, customer data stores, and cryptographic key material.
requirements.frequency: Continuous enforcement; access reviews quarterly; PAM logs reviewed monthly

requirements.requirement: Implement and regularly assess physical security controls for all infrastructure hosting digital services, including data centre access controls, CCTV monitoring, environmental controls, equipment asset management, and clean-desk/clear-screen policies at operational sites.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.1(c) equally mandates 'strong physical control measures' alongside logical controls. Physical breaches of infrastructure hosting digital services can bypass all logical controls — enabling direct data extraction, hardware implants, or service disruption. Physical controls must be assessed as rigorously as cyber controls.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Infrastructure / Head of Facilities Security
requirements.frequency: Physical access reviews monthly; formal physical security assessment annually

requirements.requirement: Conduct regular technical security assessments of digital service delivery channels, including vulnerability assessments, penetration testing, and configuration reviews, with remediation tracked to closure within defined SLAs based on vulnerability criticality.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.1(a) requires institutions to 'regularly assess' minimum-security controls — assessment is a continuous obligation, not a one-time exercise. Unassessed controls degrade over time as threats evolve; regular technical testing provides evidence of ongoing compliance and identifies control gaps before they are exploited.
requirements.frequency: Vulnerability assessments quarterly; penetration testing at least annually and after major changes

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure the CRF expansion mandated by clause 12.1 is formally approved, resourced, and subject to independent oversight at Board and senior management levels. Policy frameworks must translate the minimum-security controls from RMiT Appendices 2, 3, 4 and 11 into enforceable internal standards, with regular assurance reporting to demonstrate ongoing compliance. Accountability for authentication, fraud risk, and physical/logical control failures must be clearly assigned and escalation paths defined.

requirements.requirement: The Board Risk Committee must formally approve the expanded Cyber Risk Framework (CRF) that incorporates digital services security controls, and receive at minimum annual assurance reporting on the effectiveness of those controls, with material control failures escalated immediately.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text places the CRF expansion obligation on 'a financial institution' as an entity — implying Board-level ownership. BNM expects the Board to be informed and accountable for the institution's overall security posture on digital services, not merely to delegate it to management without oversight.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee Chairman / Board of Directors
requirements.frequency: CRF approval: upon expansion and annually thereafter; reporting: at minimum annually, with material issues ad-hoc

requirements.requirement: Develop, approve, and maintain a Digital Services Security Policy that translates RMiT Appendices 2, 3, 4 and 11 minimum-security controls into enforceable internal standards for each delivery channel, with version-controlled documentation, clear ownership, and mandatory staff attestation.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.1(a)'s requirement to 'adopt' minimum-security controls necessitates formal policy instruments — controls adopted informally or only in technical configuration cannot be consistently governed, audited, or enforced. Policy documentation is also the primary artefact BNM examiners review during Technology Risk Examinations.
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed annually; updated within 90 days of any material RMiT amendment

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Authentication and Fraud Risk Governance Framework, including a fraud risk appetite statement, defined escalation thresholds, a cross-functional fraud management committee (Technology, Risk, Operations, Compliance), and mandatory incident reporting procedures for impersonation and fraud events on digital channels.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.1(b) explicitly links authentication and authorisation controls to mitigating 'impersonation and fraud risk' — this requires structured governance, not just technical controls. Without a governance framework, fraud risk decisions are ad-hoc, risk appetite is undefined, and the institution cannot demonstrate a risk-managed approach to BNM.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Compliance
requirements.frequency: Fraud committee meets quarterly; risk appetite reviewed annually; incidents reported per BNM notification timelines

requirements.requirement: Establish an independent assurance programme (internal audit or qualified third party) to annually assess compliance with clause 12.1 controls across all digital delivery channels, with findings reported to the Audit Committee and remediation plans tracked to closure with defined accountable owners.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.1(a) mandates 'regularly assess' — independent assurance is distinct from management self-assessment and is required to provide the Board and BNM with objective evidence of control effectiveness. Management-only assessment creates a self-review threat that undermines the reliability of compliance reporting.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Audit Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Annual independent assessment; findings tracked quarterly until remediated

requirements.requirement: Maintain a Digital Services Security Controls Register that documents all minimum-security controls by delivery channel, maps each control to the relevant RMiT Appendix, records the last assessment date and outcome, and tracks open remediation items — with this register reviewed by senior management quarterly.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.1(a) references controls across Appendices 2, 3, 4 and 11 for 'respective delivery channels', creating a complex multi-dimensional compliance obligation. A structured controls register is the governance mechanism required to ensure no channel or control is overlooked, and to provide an auditable trail of ongoing compliance activity for BNM examination purposes.
requirements.frequency: Register updated continuously; formal senior management review quarterly

business.summary: This clause creates direct financial exposure for institutions that have not fully implemented the digital services security controls mandated under paragraphs 12.1 to 12.9, requiring them to absorb the full cost of any resulting fraud without passing liability to customers. Beyond fraud loss absorption, institutions must proactively maintain documented justifications for their alternative measures, creating an ongoing compliance obligation that sits squarely at the intersection of risk management and legal accountability. Failure to maintain adequate documentation or to assume liability upon a fraud event constitutes a compounded regulatory breach — both of the underlying security control and of this clause's explicit liability and documentation requirements.

requirements.requirement: Maintain a Board-endorsed register of all digital services security controls under paragraphs 12.1 to 12.9 that have not yet been fully implemented, including each gap's business justification, risk rating, alternative measure in place, and a time-bound remediation roadmap.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text conditions the alternative controls regime on the institution being 'ready to provide documented explanation' at any time. Without a live, pre-prepared register, the institution cannot demonstrate readiness during a BNM examination or following a fraud incident, constituting a breach independent of any underlying control gap.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly review; updated on a per-event basis when a new control gap is identified or closed

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal fraud liability provisioning process whereby the institution quantifies and financially provisions for its full exposure to fraud losses attributable to each active control gap from paragraphs 12.1 to 12.9, consistent with the liability assumption mandated under Clause 12.2(b).
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text states the institution 'must assume the liability of any fraud that occurs due to the gaps.' Without dedicated financial provisioning, fraud restitution obligations may be unbudgeted, creating liquidity risk, reputational harm, and potential regulatory criticism of capital adequacy management.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reassessment, aligned with risk register updates

requirements.requirement: Define and operationalise a customer restitution procedure that ensures full and prompt reimbursement of customers who suffer fraud attributable to an active control gap, without imposing liability on the customer, and document this procedure as part of the institution's digital services liability framework.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.2(b) places liability unambiguously on the institution, not the customer. A pre-defined restitution procedure ensures the institution meets this obligation consistently and avoids regulatory censure for delayed or contested reimbursements following fraud events linked to documented control gaps.
requirements.owner: Chief Customer Officer / Head of Retail Banking
requirements.frequency: Per-event (triggered by fraud incidents); procedure reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Conduct a business impact assessment (BIA) for each unimplemented control from paragraphs 12.1 to 12.9, quantifying potential fraud exposure, revenue at risk, and reputational impact, to inform the adequacy decision for alternative measures and to support the documented equivalency justification required under Clause 12.2(a).
requirements.rationale: The clause requires that alternative measures achieve 'equivalent or superior effectiveness.' A BIA provides the quantitative basis for making and documenting that equivalency claim, grounding the justification in measurable risk rather than subjective assertion, and strengthening the institution's position before BNM.
requirements.frequency: Upon identification of each control gap; reviewed annually thereafter

requirements.requirement: Ensure that cyber insurance arrangements or internal loss reserves explicitly cover fraud liabilities arising from the absence of RMiT-specified digital services security controls, and that coverage adequacy is validated against the quantified exposure in the BIA on a periodic basis.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.2(b)'s unconditional liability assumption creates a contingent financial obligation. Without insurance or reserve coverage sized to actual gap exposure, a high-volume fraud event could materially impair the institution's capital base, triggering prudential concerns alongside the compliance breach.
requirements.frequency: Annual policy renewal review; coverage adequacy validated quarterly against BIA outputs

technology.summary: Technology teams must build and sustain a rigorous control gap management capability that maps every active deviation from the security controls specified in paragraphs 12.1 to 12.9, technically validates that each alternative measure achieves equivalent or superior protective effect, and deploys compensating fraud detection mechanisms calibrated specifically to the attack vectors left exposed by absent controls. The equivalency claim under Clause 12.2(a) is a technical assertion that must be supported by measurable evidence — test results, detection rates, and security metric comparisons — not merely narrative policy statements. Continuous monitoring and automated attribution of fraud events to specific control gaps are essential to meet the liability and documentation obligations imposed by Clause 12.2(b).

requirements.requirement: Build and maintain a machine-readable security control gap register, mapped at the requirement level to each sub-clause of paragraphs 12.1 to 12.9, that records implementation status (fully implemented, partially implemented, not implemented), the specific alternative control deployed, and evidence artefacts supporting equivalency — updated in real time as control states change.
requirements.rationale: The institution must be 'ready to provide documented explanation' under Clause 12.2(a) at any point. A static, manually maintained document is insufficient for a continuously evolving digital services environment; an integrated register provides audit-ready, version-controlled evidence that survives personnel changes and is producible on demand to BNM.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (automated where possible); formal reconciliation quarterly

requirements.requirement: For each unimplemented control from paragraphs 12.1 to 12.9, design, implement, and technically validate a compensating control using a documented equivalency testing methodology, producing test reports that demonstrate — through measurable security metrics (e.g., detection rates, mean time to detect, false negative rates) — that the alternative achieves equivalent or superior effectiveness against the same threat vectors.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.2(a) requires that the documented explanation show alternatives achieve 'equivalent or superior effectiveness.' Equivalency is a technical determination that requires empirical evidence, not assertion. Without a structured testing methodology and test artefacts, any equivalency claim is vulnerable to BNM challenge, particularly following a fraud event.
requirements.frequency: Upon deployment of each alternative control; re-validated annually and after material changes to the threat landscape or system architecture

requirements.requirement: Deploy enhanced, gap-specific fraud detection and transaction monitoring rules calibrated to detect the attack vectors and fraud typologies that the absent RMiT-specified controls were designed to mitigate, with tuning parameters documented and linked to each specific control gap in the register.
requirements.rationale: Where a specified control is absent, the institution has expressly assumed liability for resulting fraud under Clause 12.2(b). Enhanced detection targeted at the exposed attack surface is both a compensating control that supports the equivalency argument under 12.2(a) and a means of minimising the fraud losses for which the institution will ultimately bear full liability.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; rule effectiveness reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement automated logging and forensic evidence capture that attributes detected fraud events to specific control gaps, tagging incidents with the relevant unimplemented paragraph reference from 12.1 to 12.9, to enable accurate liability determination, regulatory incident reporting, and post-incident evidence preservation.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.2(b) creates a liability obligation contingent on fraud occurring 'due to the gaps.' Accurate causal attribution between a fraud event and a specific control gap requires forensic-quality, tamper-evident logs captured at the time of the incident, not reconstructed retrospectively. This evidence chain also protects the institution in disputed fraud cases.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Security Operations / CISO
requirements.frequency: Continuous (real-time logging); attribution reports reviewed monthly

requirements.requirement: Establish a control gap closure tracking pipeline integrated with the institution's project delivery framework, providing automated progress reporting against the remediation roadmap for each unimplemented control from 12.1 to 12.9, with escalation triggers when milestones are missed or when fraud event rates in gap-exposed channels breach defined thresholds.
requirements.rationale: The alternative controls regime under Clause 12.2 is intended to be transitional, not permanent. Without an automated closure tracking mechanism, control gap remediation is deprioritised in favour of other delivery work, extending the institution's liability exposure indefinitely and undermining the good-faith posture required for a defensible alternative controls position before BNM.
requirements.frequency: Continuous tracking; executive escalation dashboard reviewed monthly

governance.summary: Clause 12.2 imposes governance obligations that are as demanding as its technical ones: the institution must have a structured, Board-sanctioned framework for approving exceptions to the specified controls, producing and safeguarding equivalency documentation, and accepting institutional liability in a manner that is deliberate and auditable rather than implicit. The explicit liability assumption in Clause 12.2(b) elevates this from a technology compliance matter to a Board-level risk acceptance decision, requiring governance structures that ensure senior accountability, regular oversight, and clear escalation paths when gap exposure increases. BNM's examination approach will scrutinise both the adequacy of documentation and the rigour of the governance process that produced and approved it.

requirements.requirement: Develop and obtain Board Risk Committee approval for a formal Alternative Controls and Liability Policy that defines: (i) the criteria for what constitutes an acceptable alternative measure achieving equivalent or superior effectiveness; (ii) the delegated authority levels for approving alternative controls at each tier of risk; (iii) the institution's explicit acceptance of the liability mandated under Clause 12.2(b); and (iv) the documentation standards required for BNM examination readiness.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text makes both the documentation obligation (12.2(a)) and the liability assumption (12.2(b)) unconditional requirements. A Board-approved policy ensures these obligations are institutionally embedded, not dependent on individual judgement, and provides a defensible governance anchor when BNM reviews the institution's approach to alternative controls.
requirements.frequency: Adopted upon first activation of the alternative controls regime; reviewed annually thereafter

requirements.requirement: Implement a formal exception approval workflow requiring, at minimum, CRO-level sign-off for each unimplemented control from paragraphs 12.1 to 12.9, with Board Risk Committee notification for gaps assessed as High or Critical risk, and documented evidence that the approving authority has reviewed and accepted the equivalency justification and the associated liability exposure before any alternative measure is relied upon.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.2(a) requires that the institution be 'ready to provide documented explanation.' The approval workflow creates the governance paper trail demonstrating that exceptions were deliberate, reviewed by appropriately senior officers, and not the result of oversight or inaction. This is essential for demonstrating good faith to BNM and for internal accountability.
requirements.frequency: Per-exception (at point of identifying each control gap); documented approval renewed annually

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a centralised, version-controlled document repository containing all Clause 12.2 compliance artefacts — including the control gap register, equivalency justification documents, BIA reports, approval records, and test evidence — with defined retention periods, access controls, and a production-readiness standard enabling complete document packages to be presented to BNM within 24 hours of a regulatory request.
requirements.rationale: The institution must be 'ready to provide documented explanation' under Clause 12.2(a). Readiness is a state, not an activity — it requires that documentation be maintained in a current, complete, and immediately accessible condition at all times, not assembled reactively following a regulatory enquiry or fraud event. A 24-hour production standard reflects realistic BNM examination timelines.
requirements.frequency: Continuous maintenance; repository completeness audited quarterly

requirements.requirement: Establish a quarterly Board Risk Committee reporting cycle that presents: (i) the current status of all active control gaps from 12.1 to 12.9; (ii) the quantified fraud liability exposure attributable to each gap; (iii) the effectiveness metrics of alternative controls in place; and (iv) progress against the remediation roadmap — enabling Board-level oversight of the institution's ongoing liability position under Clause 12.2(b).
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.2(b) places institutional — and by extension, Board — accountability for fraud losses arising from control gaps. Without structured Board reporting, Directors cannot fulfil their duty of oversight over a material, contingent financial liability. Quarterly reporting also creates a documented record of Board engagement, which is critical to demonstrating governance rigour during BNM examinations.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Board Risk Committee
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting cycle; ad-hoc escalation on material fraud events

requirements.requirement: Define and embed a fraud incident response protocol specific to Clause 12.2 liability events, encompassing: (i) causal attribution to a specific control gap; (ii) immediate customer restitution authorisation; (iii) BNM incident reporting in accordance with applicable notification timelines; (iv) internal post-incident review; and (v) a mandatory assessment of whether the fraud event requires the alternative control to be re-evaluated or the remediation timeline accelerated.
requirements.rationale: The liability assumption under Clause 12.2(b) is activated at the point a fraud event occurs. Without a pre-defined protocol, institutional responses risk being delayed, inconsistent, or insufficiently documented — each of which compounds the regulatory exposure. The mandatory re-evaluation step ensures that fraud events translate into control improvement rather than merely triggering restitution without systemic learning.
requirements.frequency: Protocol activated per fraud event; full protocol reviewed annually or after each activation

12.3.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.3.title: CRF Enhancement for Digital Fraud

business.summary: Clause 12.3 requires financial institutions to materially strengthen their Cyber Risk Framework (CRF) to address the growing attack surface introduced by mobile digital services. Failure to comply exposes the institution to direct financial liability from customer fraud losses, regulatory enforcement action, and severe reputational damage that undermines public confidence in digital banking. The clause is particularly significant as it imposes explicit board-level accountability, meaning non-compliance cannot be absorbed at the operational level.

requirements.requirement: Formally expand the institution's CRF scope documentation to designate customers' mobile devices and access points (e.g., public Wi-Fi, compromised networks) as recognised threat vectors, with corresponding countermeasures catalogued and owned within the risk register.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.3(a) explicitly mandates expansion of threat identification scope beyond institution-controlled infrastructure to encompass customer-side endpoints. Without formalising this in the CRF, the institution's risk register understates its exposure and countermeasures remain unallocated, creating a compliance gap and a blind spot in fraud loss modelling.
requirements.frequency: Annual review of scope; immediate update upon identification of new mobile threat vectors

requirements.requirement: Develop and operationalise clearly defined incident handling procedures specifically for cybersecurity breaches involving digital services, including a customer-facing response playbook that specifies actions to help affected customers contain financial damage (e.g., account freeze initiation, card blocking, guided fraud reporting).
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.3(d) requires 'clearly defined and effective incident handling procedures to assist customers to contain the potential damage'. A generic IT incident response plan does not satisfy this requirement — the procedures must be customer-centric and focused on limiting harm to victims, not merely restoring system availability.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Head of Digital Banking
requirements.frequency: Procedures reviewed semi-annually; tested via simulation exercises annually

requirements.requirement: Formalise an operational arrangement (documented, role-assigned, and rehearsed) that enables swift coordinated response across all relevant business functions (fraud, IT, operations, legal, communications, customer service) when advanced fraud tactics are detected, with defined escalation paths and response SLAs.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.3(e) mandates formalised cross-functional operational arrangements specifically for institutions relying on multiple business functions, which applies to virtually all licensed financial institutions. The absence of such arrangements creates coordination failures during active fraud campaigns, amplifying customer losses and regulatory exposure.
requirements.frequency: Arrangement reviewed quarterly; cross-functional simulation drills conducted annually

requirements.requirement: Define and document quantitative and qualitative escalation thresholds for digital fraud countermeasures, incorporating both actual victim impact metrics (e.g., number of affected customers, total fraud losses) and emerging fraud typology intelligence, to trigger pre-approved escalation actions without requiring ad-hoc senior approval during a live incident.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.3(f) requires senior management to 'define threshold for escalation of countermeasures considering the actual impact to victims of digital fraud and emerging fraud environment'. Pre-defined thresholds prevent response paralysis during active fraud surges and ensure proportionate, timely intervention that limits customer harm.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Fraud Management
requirements.frequency: Thresholds reviewed quarterly and recalibrated based on fraud trend analysis

requirements.requirement: Establish a structured board reporting cycle for digital fraud management outcomes, including a standardised reporting pack that summarises management review findings, CRF effectiveness metrics, countermeasure upgrade activities, and reputational risk indicators linked to digital fraud incidents.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.3(g) requires the board to be apprised of management review outcomes to preserve public confidence and mitigate reputational risk. This imposes a direct board-level obligation that requires formal reporting infrastructure — ad-hoc verbal updates are insufficient to demonstrate compliance.
requirements.frequency: At minimum semi-annual; quarterly reporting recommended for institutions with elevated fraud exposure

technology.summary: Clause 12.3 imposes a comprehensive set of technical obligations on the institution's mobile banking infrastructure, requiring defense-in-depth controls at the application layer, continuous runtime monitoring of deployed mobile applications, and the technical capability to rapidly patch or upgrade security controls in response to emerging vulnerabilities. Technology teams must treat the customer's mobile device as an untrusted, partially adversarial endpoint and engineer controls accordingly.

requirements.requirement: Implement layered (defense-in-depth) security controls for all customer-facing mobile banking applications, including at minimum: binary code obfuscation, root/jailbreak detection, runtime application self-protection (RASP), certificate pinning, anti-tampering controls, and secure enclave storage for sensitive banking data (tokens, credentials, session keys).
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.3(b) mandates adoption of 'layered (defense-in-depth) security controls to protect the digital service application deployed to customers' mobile devices and the relevant banking data contained in it'. A single control (e.g., certificate pinning alone) does not constitute a layered approach; each control layer must address a distinct attack vector to satisfy the depth-in-defence requirement.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Mobile Engineering
requirements.frequency: Implemented at deployment; reviewed and tested with each major application release and at minimum annually via penetration testing

requirements.requirement: Deploy a continuous mobile application threat monitoring capability — such as a Mobile Threat Defence (MTD) solution or application telemetry pipeline — to detect in-production exploitation attempts, anomalous usage patterns, device compromise indicators, and application integrity violations across the active customer device estate.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.3(c) mandates 'continuous surveillance and monitoring to detect any exploitation of the digital service application deployed to customers' mobile devices'. Periodic penetration tests alone do not satisfy this requirement; the clause requires runtime, in-production detection capability that operates continuously rather than point-in-time.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; monitoring rules and detection logic reviewed monthly and after each identified exploitation incident

requirements.requirement: Establish a rapid mobile application security patching capability, including an expedited release pipeline for out-of-cycle security patches, server-side kill-switch or feature-flag controls to disable vulnerable features without requiring a full app store release, and a defined SLA for security fix deployment (e.g., critical vulnerabilities remediated within 24-72 hours).
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.3(c) explicitly requires institutions to 'ensure the swift upgrade of security controls to mitigate new vulnerabilities'. Without a fast-track release mechanism, the institution cannot demonstrate swift response — standard app store review cycles (typically 24-72 hours with risk of rejection) are insufficient without server-side fallback controls.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of DevSecOps
requirements.frequency: Capability maintained continuously; SLA compliance reviewed monthly; pipeline tested quarterly via tabletop exercises

requirements.requirement: Expand threat intelligence ingestion and correlation capabilities to include mobile-specific threat feeds (e.g., malware targeting Malaysian banking apps, rogue application campaigns, SIM-swap fraud indicators), and integrate these feeds into the institution's Security Operations Centre (SOC) monitoring workflows to enable proactive countermeasure deployment.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.3(a) requires expansion of cybersecurity threat identification to include customers' mobile devices and access points. Without mobile-specific threat intelligence, the SOC operates with incomplete situational awareness and cannot identify emerging fraud techniques (e.g., overlay malware, screen readers) before they achieve scale against the institution's customer base.
requirements.frequency: Threat feeds updated continuously; ingestion pipeline health reviewed monthly; intelligence applicability reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement technical controls to protect banking data at rest and in transit within mobile applications, including encryption of locally cached data using device-bound keys, prevention of data leakage via screenshot blocking, clipboard interception controls, and screen overlay detection, with documented data inventory for all banking data persisted on customer devices.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.3(b) specifically references protection of 'the relevant banking data contained in' the mobile application. A data-centric protection model requires the institution to know what banking data resides on devices, how it is protected at rest, and what controls prevent exfiltration — this cannot be addressed by network controls alone.
requirements.frequency: Implemented at deployment; data inventory reviewed semi-annually; controls tested with each major release

governance.summary: Clause 12.3 establishes an explicit governance chain running from operational fraud management through senior management review up to board-level appraisal, with particular emphasis on preserving public confidence and managing reputational risk. Governance structures must be formalised — not ad-hoc — and must demonstrably connect the institution's fraud management effectiveness to board oversight, ensuring accountability cannot be diffused across functions without clear ownership.

requirements.requirement: Institute a formal Senior Management Digital Fraud Review Committee (or embed this mandate within an existing risk committee with documented terms of reference) with a defined charter, quorum requirements, and prescribed agenda items covering: CRF effectiveness assessment, fraud loss and victim impact metrics, emerging fraud typology analysis, countermeasure adequacy, and threshold breach review.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.3(f) mandates 'regular review by senior management to ensure the effectiveness of digital fraud management'. The use of 'regular' and the requirement to 'define threshold for escalation' implies a structured, recurring forum — not informal reviews. Without documented terms of reference, the institution cannot demonstrate that reviews are systematic and complete.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Executive Officer (CEO)
requirements.frequency: Quarterly as minimum cadence; ad-hoc convening required when escalation thresholds defined under 12.3(f) are breached

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Board-level Digital Fraud Reporting protocol, including a defined reporting template, submission timeline relative to management review completion, and Board Risk Committee (or equivalent) terms of reference that explicitly assign responsibility for receiving and challenging management's assessment of digital fraud management effectiveness and reputational risk.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.3(g) requires the institution to 'apprise the board on the outcome of management reviews to preserve public confidence in the security of digital services and mitigate reputation risk to the industry'. This is a direct board obligation — the board must be informed and must be seen to engage with the findings, not merely receive a report without documented consideration.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee Chairman / Company Secretary
requirements.frequency: At minimum semi-annual board reporting; quarterly if management reviews surface material concerns or threshold breaches

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a cross-functional Digital Fraud Incident Response Charter that formally assigns roles, responsibilities, decision rights, and escalation authorities across fraud operations, IT security, legal and compliance, customer service, corporate communications, and senior management for responding to advanced digital fraud campaigns.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.3(e) requires the institution to 'formalise operational arrangement to enable swift coordinated response and rapid upgrade of countermeasures to defend against advanced fraud tactics'. The word 'formalise' implies a documented, approved, and communicated arrangement — not informal working relationships. This charter provides the governance foundation for the operational arrangement required.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Charter reviewed and reaffirmed annually; updated within 30 days following any major fraud incident that exposed coordination gaps

requirements.requirement: Integrate the expanded CRF scope (customers' mobile devices and access points as per clause 12.3(a)) into the institution's enterprise-wide risk taxonomy, risk appetite statement, and Key Risk Indicators (KRIs), ensuring that mobile fraud risk is explicitly measured, monitored, and reported as a distinct risk category rather than being subsumed within generic cyber risk.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.3(a), read in conjunction with paragraphs 11.2 and 11.3, requires systematic identification and management of threats at customer endpoints. Without explicit representation in the risk taxonomy and appetite framework, mobile fraud risk lacks ownership, measurement, and escalation triggers — rendering CRF enhancement procedurally compliant but substantively ineffective.
requirements.frequency: Risk taxonomy and KRIs reviewed annually; KRI thresholds recalibrated semi-annually based on fraud trend data

requirements.requirement: Conduct an annual independent assessment (internal audit or qualified external party) of the CRF enhancements mandated under clause 12.3, covering the completeness and effectiveness of mobile threat identification, layered controls, surveillance capabilities, incident handling procedures, and cross-functional response arrangements, with findings reported to the Board Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.3(f) and (g) collectively require senior management and the board to form a view on CRF effectiveness — an obligation that cannot be credibly discharged without independent verification. Without periodic independent assessment, management reviews risk becoming self-validating, and the board cannot fulfil its oversight duty with the objectivity required to 'preserve public confidence in the security of digital services'.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Board Audit Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Annual; scope expanded or interim review triggered if a material digital fraud incident occurs or a regulatory inspection raises findings

12.4.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.4.title: Fraud Detection Capabilities

business.summary: Financial institutions face direct financial loss, reputational damage, and regulatory sanction if fraud detection rules are not updated promptly when new modus operandi emerge. Failure to act on emerging fraud patterns—such as account takeover using stolen credentials—exposes customers and the institution to preventable fraud losses. The obligation to apply risk analytics and continuously upgrade capabilities further means that a static or reactive fraud programme is non-compliant.

requirements.requirement: Establish a documented, time-bound escalation and response process that mandates updating fraud detection rules within a defined SLA (e.g., within 24–72 hours) upon internal or external identification of a new fraud modus operandi.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text specifies rules must be updated 'in a timely manner upon detection'—without a defined SLA the institution cannot demonstrate timely compliance and remains exposed during the window between detection and rule deployment.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (triggered by each newly identified modus operandi)

requirements.requirement: Maintain a fraud intelligence function that actively monitors internal fraud data, industry threat-sharing networks (e.g., PayNet, AIBIM fraud working groups, BNM advisories), and dark-web credential exposure sources to ensure new modus operandi are detected without delay.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires updates 'upon detection'—the institution must have active detection channels for emerging threats, not merely reactive ones. Account-takeover via stolen credentials is explicitly cited, making credential-leak monitoring a direct regulatory obligation.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Fraud Risk
requirements.frequency: Continuous

requirements.requirement: Define and publish internal performance metrics for fraud detection accuracy (false-positive rate, false-negative rate, fraud detection rate) that are reviewed at least quarterly, with formal improvement targets tied to the risk analytics obligation.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires 'appropriate risk analytics to improve the accuracy of fraud detection'—accuracy improvement must be demonstrably measured and tracked, not merely asserted.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly review; continuous metric collection

requirements.requirement: Conduct an annual business impact assessment of the institution's fraud detection programme against Appendix 11 Fraud Detection Standards, identifying capability gaps and budgeting remediation activities within the annual planning cycle.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates 'continuous upgrade of fraud detection capabilities as specified in Appendix 11'—business leadership must allocate resources and track progress against the BNM-prescribed standards, not treat them as a one-time implementation exercise.
requirements.frequency: Annual (and upon material changes to Appendix 11 standards)

requirements.requirement: Establish a Fraud Operations Centre (or equivalent function) with clearly defined responsibilities, staffing levels, and 24/7 coverage to ensure that fraud pattern analysis, rule updates, and customer impact triage can be executed at any time, including outside business hours.
requirements.rationale: Fraud modus operandi do not respect business hours; 'timely manner' implies operational readiness around the clock. Account takeover attacks are frequently executed overnight or during weekends to exploit reduced monitoring.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Head of Fraud Operations
requirements.frequency: Continuous (operational function); headcount review annually

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement and maintain a fraud detection platform capable of near-real-time rule deployment, machine learning-driven risk analytics, and credential compromise detection—all aligned to the specific technical standards prescribed in Appendix 11. Systems must be architected to allow rapid rule changes without service downtime and must produce audit-ready logs of rule versioning and update timestamps. Continuous capability upgrades require a formal technology roadmap with regular benchmarking against emerging fraud techniques.

requirements.requirement: Implement a real-time fraud detection engine capable of evaluating transactions and authentication events against a dynamic, versioned rule set, with a deployment pipeline that allows new or modified rules to be pushed to production within the institution's defined SLA (e.g., 24–72 hours) without requiring full system downtime.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires rules to be updated 'in a timely manner'—a rule engine that requires extended change-freeze periods or manual deployments cannot meet this obligation, particularly for preventing fraudulent transactions or account takeover in near-real time.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of Digital Banking Technology
requirements.frequency: Per-event rule deployment; engine review semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Integrate risk analytics capabilities—including supervised and unsupervised machine learning models—into the fraud detection pipeline to score transactions and authentication requests, with model performance (precision, recall, AUC) tracked continuously and models retrained or recalibrated at a minimum quarterly cadence.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly requires 'appropriate risk analytics to improve the accuracy of fraud detection'—static rule-only systems do not fulfil this obligation. ML-based scoring enables detection of novel modus operandi that evade known rule patterns.
requirements.owner: CTO / Head of Data Science / CISO
requirements.frequency: Continuous scoring; quarterly model retraining minimum

requirements.requirement: Deploy credential compromise detection controls, including: (a) detection of login attempts using credentials found in breach databases (e.g., integration with HIBP or equivalent feeds), (b) device fingerprinting and behavioural biometrics for account takeover detection, and (c) step-up authentication triggers when anomalous credential use is detected.
requirements.rationale: The clause specifically calls out 'account takeover using stolen customer credentials' as a threat to be prevented—these technical controls directly address that risk and are consistent with the account takeover detection standards expected under Appendix 11.
requirements.owner: CISO / Head of Cybersecurity / Head of Identity & Access Management
requirements.frequency: Continuous; credential feed refresh daily or in real-time

requirements.requirement: Maintain an immutable, timestamped audit log of all fraud detection rule versions, including: rule content, creator, approver, deployment timestamp, and decommission timestamp. Logs must be queryable for regulatory examination and retained for a minimum of 7 years in line with BNM record-keeping requirements.
requirements.rationale: Demonstrating 'timely manner' compliance requires irrefutable evidence of when a modus operandi was identified and when the corresponding rule was deployed. Without versioned audit logs, the institution cannot prove compliance during a BNM examination.
requirements.owner: CTO / Head of IT Audit & Compliance
requirements.frequency: Continuous (automated logging); retention reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Conduct a technology capability assessment against Appendix 11 Fraud Detection Standards at least annually, producing a gap analysis with a time-bound remediation roadmap. Emerging capabilities (e.g., graph analytics for mule account networks, federated fraud intelligence) must be evaluated and incorporated where they materially improve detection accuracy.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates 'continuous upgrade of fraud detection capabilities as specified in Appendix 11'—continuous upgrade requires a structured technology roadmap, not ad-hoc patching. Annual benchmarking ensures the institution's capability keeps pace with evolving fraud techniques.
requirements.frequency: Annual assessment; roadmap updated semi-annually

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that fraud detection obligations under Clause 12.4 are embedded in board-approved policy, with clear ownership, escalation paths, and assurance mechanisms to verify that rules are updated within SLAs and that risk analytics investments deliver measurable accuracy improvements. Compliance with Appendix 11 must be formally assessed and evidenced at least annually, with findings reported to senior management and the Board Risk Committee. Third-party fraud intelligence sharing arrangements must also be governed by formal agreements to maintain integrity and timeliness of modus operandi data.

requirements.requirement: Maintain a Board-approved Digital Fraud Management Policy that explicitly defines: (a) the institution's obligation to update fraud detection rules in a timely manner upon detection of new modus operandi, (b) the maximum permissible rule-update SLA for different fraud severity levels, and (c) accountability for compliance with Appendix 11 Fraud Detection Standards.
requirements.rationale: The 'S' (Standard) marker under RMiT requires a formalised, documented approach. Without a Board-approved policy, the institution lacks the foundational governance instrument needed to assign accountability and demonstrate regulatory compliance.
requirements.frequency: Annual policy review; immediate update upon material regulatory change

requirements.requirement: Establish a Fraud Management Committee (or equivalent senior management forum) that meets at least monthly to review: new modus operandi detected during the period, rule updates deployed, SLA adherence, fraud detection accuracy metrics, and any Appendix 11 compliance gaps.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires timely action upon detection—governance oversight at senior management level ensures that operational teams are held accountable to SLAs and that resource constraints or escalation failures are surfaced and resolved promptly.
requirements.frequency: Monthly; emergency session within 48 hours of a significant fraud event

requirements.requirement: Implement a formal Fraud Intelligence Sharing governance framework that regulates the institution's participation in industry fraud information sharing (e.g., PayNet, AIBIM, Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department referrals), including data classification, permissible use, and timeliness standards for acting on received intelligence.
requirements.rationale: 'Detection of new fraud modus operandi' inherently depends on intelligence sources that extend beyond the institution's own transaction data. Without a governance framework, intelligence may be received but not acted upon within the required timeframe.
requirements.owner: CRO / Head of Fraud Risk / Chief Compliance Officer (CCO)
requirements.frequency: Framework reviewed annually; intelligence acted upon per-event

requirements.requirement: Commission an independent internal audit review of Clause 12.4 and Appendix 11 compliance at least annually, assessing: rule-update SLA adherence, effectiveness of risk analytics, credential compromise controls, and alignment with Appendix 11 standards. Audit findings and management responses must be reported to the Board Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: The 'continuous upgrade' and 'timely manner' obligations require independent assurance that operational practices match policy commitments. Self-assessment by the fraud operations team is insufficient for BNM examination purposes.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Board Audit Committee
requirements.frequency: Annual; findings tracked to closure quarterly

requirements.requirement: Define and enforce a Fraud Incident Governance process that requires: (a) logging of every newly detected modus operandi in a centralised fraud incident register, (b) documented root-cause analysis for each fraud event, (c) evidence of the rule or control change deployed in response, and (d) post-implementation review within 30 days to validate the control's effectiveness.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires updates 'upon detection'—the institution must be able to produce a traceable record linking detection of a modus operandi to the specific rule change deployed, demonstrating 'timely manner' compliance during BNM supervisory review.
requirements.owner: CRO / Head of Fraud Risk / Chief Compliance Officer (CCO)
requirements.frequency: Per-event registration; 30-day post-implementation review; register audited quarterly

12.5.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.5.title: Digital Service Delivery Risk Mitigation

business.summary: Financial institutions face direct reputational, financial, and regulatory liability if digital service delivery channels are exploited for phishing or social engineering. Failure to adopt secure channels or to provide customers with verifiable authentication mechanisms exposes customers to fraud losses and the institution to enforcement action, compensation claims, and loss of trust. Proactive advance notification of new controls is equally critical, as abrupt changes without customer awareness can undermine adoption and drive customers toward insecure workarounds.

requirements.requirement: Establish and enforce a Digital Communication Channel Policy that classifies each channel used (email, SMS, push notification, in-app messaging, voice call) by phishing risk level, mandates secure-by-default channel selection, and prohibits use of high-risk channels without documented compensating controls.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.5(a) explicitly requires adoption of secure communication channels and mandates compensating controls when phishing-prone channels are used. Without a formal classification and approval process, business units may default to convenient but insecure channels, creating unmitigated phishing attack surfaces.
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed Annually; channel risk classification reviewed Quarterly or upon introduction of any new channel

requirements.requirement: Implement a structured Customer Advance Notification Programme that mandates proactive, multi-channel communication to all affected customers at least 30 days before any new security control is activated, including plain-language explanation of what is changing, why, and what customers must do.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.5(b) expressly requires that all customers be properly informed in advance of new controls. A structured programme with defined lead-time prevents adoption friction, reduces support escalations, and ensures the institution can demonstrate regulatory compliance with a documented communication trail.
requirements.owner: Chief Customer Officer (CCO) / Head of Retail Banking
requirements.frequency: Per-event (triggered by every new or materially changed security control deployment); effectiveness review Annually

requirements.requirement: Deploy and publicise practical, customer-accessible mechanisms that allow customers to independently verify the authenticity of inbound calls made by the institution or any of its appointed outsourced service providers, such as a unique call reference code verifiable via the institution's official app or website.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.5(c) requires practical verification mechanisms for institution-initiated calls. Without these, customers cannot distinguish legitimate calls from vishing attacks impersonating the bank or its vendors, resulting in social engineering losses and reputational damage attributable to the institution.
requirements.owner: Chief Customer Officer (CCO) / Head of Digital Banking
requirements.frequency: Mechanism availability is Continuous; customer awareness campaign reviewed Bi-annually

requirements.requirement: Extend the communication security and caller-verification obligations contractually to all appointed outsourced service providers who contact customers on behalf of the institution, and conduct periodic compliance checks to confirm adherence.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.5(c) explicitly names 'appointed outsourced service providers' as parties whose calls customers must be able to authenticate. Regulatory obligation extends beyond the institution's own operations; failure to bind and audit third parties creates a compliance gap and a fraud vector exploitable by impersonation of vendors.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Vendor Management
requirements.frequency: Contractual obligations set at On-boarding; compliance verification Annually or upon material change in vendor engagement

requirements.requirement: Establish a Digital Fraud Risk Register specific to digital service delivery channels, tracking identified phishing threats, compensating controls in use, customer complaint data linked to channel fraud, and remediation timelines, with escalation to senior management.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.5(a) requires that attendant risks be actively mitigated, implying ongoing identification and treatment. A dedicated risk register operationalises this obligation, ensures risks do not lapse unaddressed, and provides an auditable record demonstrating continuous compliance to BNM examiners.
requirements.frequency: Risk Register updated Continuously; formally reviewed Quarterly by senior management

technology.summary: Technology teams must harden every digital communication channel against phishing exploitation and implement compensating controls where secure channels are not technically feasible. Systems must be built or integrated to enable real-time, customer-accessible call-authentication verification and to deliver advance customer notifications reliably at scale. All controls must be auditable, with logging sufficient to demonstrate operational effectiveness to BNM examiners.

requirements.requirement: Enforce anti-phishing email authentication standards — SPF, DKIM, and DMARC at enforcement policy (p=reject) — for all institution-originated email domains used in customer communications, and configure inbound email gateways to reject or quarantine unauthenticated messages purporting to be from the institution.
requirements.rationale: Email is the primary phishing-prone channel referenced implicitly in Clause 12.5(a). SPF/DKIM/DMARC collectively prevent domain spoofing; without enforcement-mode DMARC, attackers can send institution-branded phishing emails that are indistinguishable to recipients and mail servers, directly exploiting the channel risk the clause targets.
requirements.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of Infrastructure
requirements.frequency: Implementation is a one-time baseline; DMARC aggregate report review Weekly; full configuration audit Quarterly
