requirements.requirement: Where SMS is used as a communication or OTP delivery channel (a phishing-prone medium), implement compensating controls including: transaction-context binding in OTP messages (amount, recipient, timestamp), SIM-swap detection via telco API integration, and suppression of clickable URLs within SMS messages sent to customers.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.5(a) requires compensating controls when the channel is prone to phishing exploits. SMS is explicitly high-risk due to SIM-swapping and smishing; these technical compensations directly address the residual risk without replacing the channel, satisfying the compensating control requirement.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; SIM-swap API integration health checked Daily; control effectiveness reviewed Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Build and operate a Caller Verification System (CVS) that generates a unique, time-limited, human-readable reference code for every institution-initiated or outsourced-provider-initiated outbound call, making this code visible to the customer in real-time via the official mobile banking app and internet banking portal for independent verification.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.5(c) mandates practical means for customers to verify call authenticity. A CVS directly satisfies this by providing an out-of-band, app-based verification signal that a fraudster impersonating the institution via voice cannot replicate, closing the vishing gap inherent in unauthenticated call centres.
requirements.frequency: Continuous availability (99.9% uptime SLA); reference code TTL must not exceed the call duration plus a 5-minute grace period; system audit Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement a Customer Notification Delivery Platform capable of targeting all affected customer segments across multiple channels (in-app push, email, SMS, internet banking inbox) with configurable advance scheduling, delivery confirmation tracking, and audit logging of notification send, delivery, and open events per customer record.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.5(b) requires that all customers be properly informed in advance. A platform with confirmed-delivery tracking enables the institution to demonstrate compliance — showing not only that notifications were sent but that they reached customers — and to trigger escalation (e.g., physical mail) for customers with no digital delivery confirmation.
requirements.frequency: Platform availability Continuous; notification campaign logs retained for a minimum of 7 years per BNM record-keeping requirements

requirements.requirement: Integrate phishing site take-down automation via a contracted cyber-threat intelligence feed, enabling detection and reporting of fraudulent domains impersonating the institution within 1 hour of identification and initiating take-down requests within 4 hours, with SIEM alerting for each incident.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.5(a) requires active mitigation of phishing risk arising from digital service delivery. Phishing sites directly exploit the institution's digital presence; automated detection and take-down is the technical complement to secure channel controls, reducing the window of customer exposure from days to hours.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; incident response SLA reviewed Quarterly; annual simulation exercise to validate take-down process

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that digital fraud risks arising from service delivery channels are formally owned, periodically assessed, and reported to Board-level oversight committees. Policies must bind both internal teams and outsourced service providers to consistent security and customer communication standards, with clear accountability for non-conformance. Ongoing audit and assurance activities must validate that technical and business controls remain effective as the threat landscape and digital service portfolio evolve.

requirements.requirement: Formalise a Digital Fraud Management Policy approved by the Board Risk Committee that explicitly addresses Clause 12.5 obligations: secure channel selection criteria, compensating control approval workflow, customer advance notification standards, and caller-verification requirements applicable to the institution and all appointed outsourced service providers.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.5 imposes obligations on the institution as a whole, not individual teams. Board-approved policy creates an enforceable framework, establishes accountability, and provides the governance instrument BNM examiners will request as primary evidence of institutional commitment to compliance.
requirements.frequency: Policy approved at inception; reviewed and re-approved Annually or upon material regulatory update

requirements.requirement: Establish a Digital Channel Security Review Board — comprising representatives from Risk, Technology, Compliance, and Customer Experience — with a quarterly mandate to review the channel risk classification register, approve or reject compensating controls for phishing-prone channels, and sign off on any new digital service delivery channel prior to production deployment.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.5(a) requires compensating controls be adopted when a phishing-prone channel is used; this requires a cross-functional approval body with the authority to halt insecure channel deployments. The Review Board operationalises this gate and ensures no new risk is introduced without documented risk acceptance.
requirements.owner: CRO (Chair) / CISO, CTO, Head of Compliance (Members)
requirements.frequency: Scheduled Quarterly; convened ad hoc for any new channel or material control change

requirements.requirement: Incorporate Clause 12.5 compliance obligations into all outsourced service provider contracts and third-party risk assessments, requiring providers who contact customers on the institution's behalf to adhere to the institution's caller-verification protocol, to undergo annual compliance attestation, and to be subject to right-to-audit provisions.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.5(c) explicitly names outsourced service providers as entities whose call authenticity customers must be able to verify. Governance over third parties through contractual obligation and audit rights is the only mechanism the institution can use to extend and enforce this regulatory requirement beyond its own operations.
requirements.owner: Head of Vendor Management / CRO / Legal Counsel
requirements.frequency: Obligations embedded at contract On-boarding; annual compliance attestation from each relevant vendor; full audit exercise Bi-annually or upon incident

requirements.requirement: Institute a Customer Awareness Governance Framework that mandates pre-deployment approval of all customer communication content related to new security controls, defines the minimum 30-day advance notification lead-time, and requires a post-deployment effectiveness review measuring customer awareness uplift, complaint volumes, and adoption rates.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.5(b) requires customers be 'properly informed in advance.' Governance over the content, timing, and effectiveness of communications ensures the institution can substantiate that the notification obligation was met substantively — not merely that messages were sent — and supports continuous improvement of communication strategy.
requirements.owner: Chief Compliance Officer (CCO) / Head of Customer Experience
requirements.frequency: Approval process Per-event (before each new control rollout); effectiveness review within 60 days of deployment; framework reviewed Annually

requirements.requirement: Mandate semi-annual internal audit assessments of Clause 12.5 controls covering: channel security configurations, compensating control adequacy, caller-verification system availability and usage metrics, customer notification delivery records, and outsourced provider compliance attestations, with findings reported to the Board Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.5 is an 'S' (Standard) marker obligation, meaning BNM expects demonstrable and sustained compliance. Internal audit provides independent assurance that controls designed to satisfy the clause are operating effectively, identifies control gaps before external examination, and ensures Board-level visibility into the institution's digital fraud risk posture.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor / Board Audit Committee
requirements.frequency: Formal audit Semi-annually; findings tracked to closure Continuously; summary reported to Board Audit Committee Quarterly

12.6.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.6.title: Customer Fraud Risk Empowerment

business.summary: This clause requires financial institutions to offer customers meaningful, self-service controls over their payment cards — specifically for card-not-present (CNP) and overseas transactions — as a frontline fraud mitigation measure. Failure to provide such controls exposes the institution to regulatory censure, reputational damage, and increased fraud liability, since the clause explicitly states that customer empowerment does not transfer or diminish the institution's own duty of care. Institutions must therefore treat customer controls as a complement to — not a substitute for — their own security obligations.

requirements.requirement: Offer customers a self-service mechanism to activate and deactivate their payment cards specifically for card-not-present (CNP) transactions, accessible through at least one digital channel (e.g., mobile banking application or internet banking portal).
requirements.rationale: Directly mandated by the verbatim text: 'offering customer mechanisms to manage activation and de-activation of payment card for card-not-present transactions.' CNP fraud is a leading vector for unauthorised digital payments; enabling customers to disable CNP capability when not needed materially reduces exposure.
requirements.owner: Chief Digital Officer / Head of Retail Banking
requirements.frequency: Continuous availability; feature review Annually or upon material change to fraud landscape

requirements.requirement: Offer customers a self-service mechanism to activate and deactivate their payment cards specifically for overseas transactions, accessible through at least one digital channel, with near-real-time effect.
requirements.rationale: Directly mandated by the verbatim text: 'activation and de-activation of payment card for... overseas transactions.' Fraudulent overseas card usage is a high-impact, high-frequency event type; enabling granular geographic controls empowers customers to limit exposure when travelling or at rest.
requirements.owner: Chief Digital Officer / Head of Card Business
requirements.frequency: Continuous availability; feature review Annually

requirements.requirement: Conduct and document a 'reasonably practical' assessment to identify all additional fraud-risk empowerment mechanisms that could be offered to customers beyond the minimum CNP and overseas card controls (e.g., transaction velocity limits, spending category restrictions, temporary card freezes), and implement those determined to be feasible.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text uses the qualifier 'where it is reasonably practical,' implying a positive obligation to assess what is feasible, not merely deliver the minimum. Documenting this assessment demonstrates regulatory good faith and supports a proportionate compliance posture.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer / Head of Fraud Management
requirements.frequency: Biannual review or upon significant product, technology, or regulatory change

requirements.requirement: Establish and communicate to customers — through product disclosures, onboarding materials, and digital channel interfaces — that customer-activated controls are supplementary fraud tools and do not transfer the institution's liability, responsibilities, or duty of care for the security of its digital services.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly states: 'This shall not absolve the financial institution from its liabilities, responsibilities and duty of care.' Commercial terms, disclosures, or marketing materials must never be worded in a manner that implies customers bear fraud liability by virtue of having access to these controls.
requirements.owner: Chief Compliance Officer / Head of Legal
requirements.frequency: Review of disclosures Annually and upon any product change

requirements.requirement: Integrate customer fraud-empowerment controls into the institution's broader digital fraud management strategy, ensuring that customer control adoption rates and associated fraud outcomes are tracked as Key Risk Indicators (KRIs) reported to senior management.
requirements.rationale: The clause sits within the 'Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness' subsection, indicating that customer controls are a component of a coherent fraud management programme, not a standalone feature. KRI tracking allows the institution to measure effectiveness and demonstrate proactive regulatory compliance.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer / Head of Fraud Management
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting to Senior Management Committee; Annual review of KRI thresholds

technology.summary: Technology teams must build, integrate, and maintain real-time self-service card control capabilities within the institution's digital channels, backed by robust core banking and card management system integrations that enforce control changes with minimal latency. All control actions must be fully auditable, and the institution's own fraud detection and monitoring systems must continue to operate independently of any customer-set controls, preserving the institution's duty of care. The platform must be resilient, secure against unauthorised control changes, and accessible across supported digital channels.

requirements.requirement: Implement a real-time card control feature within the mobile banking application and internet banking portal that enables customers to toggle CNP transaction capability on or off for each of their payment cards, with control changes taking effect within a maximum of 60 seconds of customer action.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates 'mechanisms to manage activation and de-activation of payment card for card-not-present transactions.' Near-real-time enforcement is essential; a delayed control change during an active fraud event renders the control ineffective and may expose the institution to liability claims.
requirements.owner: CTO / Head of Digital Banking Technology
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; performance SLA reviewed Quarterly; full feature review Annually

requirements.requirement: Implement a real-time card control feature that enables customers to toggle overseas transaction capability on or off for each of their payment cards, with control changes taking effect within a maximum of 60 seconds of customer action and with clear on-screen confirmation of the applied state.
requirements.rationale: Directly mirrors the verbatim mandate for 'activation and de-activation of payment card for... overseas transactions.' The confirmation requirement ensures the customer has accurate situational awareness of their card's current state, reducing friction and support escalations.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; performance SLA reviewed Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Integrate the customer-facing card control interface with the card management system (CMS) and/or card scheme authorisation rules via secure, authenticated APIs, ensuring that control flags are enforced at the authorisation decisioning layer and cannot be bypassed by downstream processing.
requirements.rationale: Customer-set controls are only meaningful if they are enforced at the point of transaction authorisation. Loose coupling between the UI and the authorisation engine creates a gap where a customer may believe a card is deactivated for CNP or overseas use, yet transactions are still approved, directly violating the institution's duty of care.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Architecture / Head of Card Systems
requirements.frequency: Integration validated at each CMS release; penetration tested Annually

requirements.requirement: Implement end-to-end audit logging for all customer card control actions — including control type, previous state, new state, timestamp, channel, device fingerprint, and session authentication method — stored in a tamper-evident log retained for a minimum period consistent with BNM record-keeping requirements.
requirements.rationale: The institution retains liability even after customers set their own controls. Comprehensive audit logs are essential to investigate fraud disputes, demonstrate regulatory compliance, and provide evidence in the event of litigation. They also support the institution's ongoing duty of care by enabling forensic analysis of control-change patterns.
requirements.frequency: Continuous logging; log integrity reviewed Quarterly; retention policy reviewed Annually

requirements.requirement: Ensure that the institution's independent fraud monitoring and detection systems (e.g., transaction monitoring engine, behavioural analytics) continue to evaluate all transactions regardless of whether a customer has set card controls, so that the institution's own fraud detection duty of care operates in parallel with customer-initiated controls.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly states that customer empowerment 'shall not absolve the financial institution from its liabilities, responsibilities and duty of care.' This requires that fraud monitoring systems never rely solely on customer-set flags as a substitute for the institution's own detection logic — the two layers must be architecturally independent.
requirements.owner: CTO / CISO / Head of Fraud Technology
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; architecture independence verified at each major release; reviewed Annually

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that customer fraud empowerment controls are embedded in formal policy, subject to periodic board-level oversight, and continuously monitored for effectiveness — while preserving clear accountability for the institution's residual fraud liability and duty of care. Policies must explicitly delineate the boundary between customer responsibility (use of controls) and institutional responsibility (security of digital services), and this boundary must be reflected in customer agreements, staff training, and internal escalation procedures. Regulatory evidence trails — including the 'reasonably practical' assessment and fraud outcome metrics — must be maintained to support BNM examination readiness.

requirements.requirement: Establish or update a Digital Fraud Management Policy that formally mandates the provision of customer card control mechanisms for CNP and overseas transactions, defines the 'reasonably practical' assessment process for additional controls, and explicitly affirms the institution's non-transferable liability and duty of care for digital service security.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text imposes a positive obligation ('shall empower') alongside a liability preservation clause ('shall not absolve'). A formal policy anchors both obligations in the institution's governance framework, ensures consistent implementation across business units, and provides the primary reference document for BNM examination.
requirements.frequency: Policy approved by Board Risk Committee upon adoption; reviewed Annually or upon material regulatory change

requirements.requirement: Establish Board-level oversight of the Digital Fraud Management programme, including periodic reporting on customer card control adoption rates, fraud incidents occurring despite customer controls being enabled, and the institution's residual fraud liability exposure — ensuring the Board is informed of the institution's ongoing duty of care obligations under Clause 12.6.
requirements.rationale: The clause's explicit preservation of institutional liability ('liabilities, responsibilities and duty of care') elevates this to a Board governance matter. Board visibility ensures strategic decisions on fraud investment are made with awareness of regulatory obligations, and demonstrates to BNM that governance is operating at the appropriate level.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting to Board Risk Committee; Annual deep-dive review

requirements.requirement: Conduct and formally document a biannual 'Reasonably Practical Assessment' that evaluates the feasibility of new or enhanced customer fraud empowerment mechanisms, records the rationale for decisions to implement or defer each mechanism, and obtains sign-off from the CRO and CTO. Findings must be presented to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim qualifier 'where it is reasonably practical' is not self-executing — it requires a structured, documented evaluation process to demonstrate that the institution has genuinely considered what is feasible. Without this, the institution cannot defend a decision not to implement a particular control against BNM scrutiny.
requirements.frequency: Biannual; additionally triggered by material fraud events, technology changes, or BNM guidance updates

requirements.requirement: Define and maintain a clear Fraud Dispute Resolution Framework that governs how the institution handles customer fraud claims in scenarios where the customer had available card controls (e.g., CNP toggle) but did not activate them, ensuring that the framework explicitly prohibits the institution from using customer non-use of controls as the sole basis for denying a fraud claim or transferring liability.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text states that customer empowerment 'shall not absolve' the institution of liability. A dispute resolution framework that improperly shifts liability to customers on the grounds that they failed to use available controls would directly contravene this clause and expose the institution to regulatory and legal risk.
requirements.owner: Chief Compliance Officer / Head of Legal / Head of Retail Banking
requirements.frequency: Framework reviewed Annually; applied Per-event for individual fraud disputes

requirements.requirement: Implement a staff training and awareness programme for all customer-facing, compliance, and technology staff covering the institution's obligations under Clause 12.6 — including the purpose of customer card controls, the institution's non-delegable duty of care, correct handling of customer disputes involving these controls, and escalation procedures for fraud events.
requirements.rationale: Effective governance of customer empowerment controls requires that staff across business, operations, and technology functions understand both what controls exist and the limits of what those controls mean for institutional liability. Misrepresentation to customers by staff — even inadvertently — could create liability exposure inconsistent with the clause's requirements.
requirements.owner: Chief People Officer / Chief Compliance Officer
requirements.frequency: Mandatory onboarding training; Annual refresher; ad hoc upon policy update or significant fraud event

12.7.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.7.title: Customer Fraud Awareness Programmes

business.summary: Financial institutions face direct reputational, financial, and regulatory exposure when customers fall victim to digital fraud due to insufficient awareness. Clause 12.7 requires that customer education not be treated as a one-time campaign but as a continuously monitored and improved programme, directly linking customer competency to the institution's fraud risk posture. Failure to comply may result in BNM supervisory action, increased fraud losses, and erosion of customer trust in digital channels.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formalised Customer Fraud Awareness Programme with a documented continuous review cycle, measurable effectiveness metrics, and improvement targets, covering all active digital service channels (e-banking, mobile banking, etc.).
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly mandates 'continuous efforts to review and enhance the effectiveness' of awareness programmes — a static or ad-hoc campaign does not satisfy this obligation. Without a formalised programme structure, the institution cannot demonstrate regulatory compliance or systematically reduce customer fraud exposure.
requirements.owner: Chief Retail Banking Officer / Chief Digital Officer
requirements.frequency: Continuous; formal effectiveness review at minimum Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Develop and publish timely, practical customer-facing content that identifies current and emerging fraud modus operandi, delivered across all customer touchpoints including mobile apps, internet banking portals, branch materials, and official social media channels.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.7(a) requires 'continuous and timely updates of practical information on how to identify potential fraud, including specific information about new or common modus operandi.' Generic or infrequent content updates leave customers uninformed about evolving threats such as phishing, SIM-swap, and social engineering attacks prevalent in the Malaysian digital banking landscape.
requirements.owner: Chief Marketing Officer / Head of Customer Communications
requirements.frequency: Continuous; content refresh within 72 hours of a confirmed new modus operandi being identified

requirements.requirement: Communicate clearly and proactively to customers about both new and existing security measures, including how to verify the authenticity of the institution's e-banking websites and official mobile applications, in plain, accessible language suited to diverse customer literacy levels.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.7(b) mandates 'clear explanation about new and existing security measures, such as how to verify genuine e-banking websites and mobile applications.' Customers who cannot distinguish genuine from fraudulent interfaces are the primary target of phishing and spoofing attacks, representing a direct fraud liability for the institution.
requirements.owner: Head of Digital Banking / Chief Customer Experience Officer
requirements.frequency: Upon each new security measure deployment; annual refresh for existing measures

requirements.requirement: Implement interactive and experiential fraud awareness initiatives — such as in-app simulations, gamified security modules, or guided onboarding walkthroughs — to move customers beyond passive information consumption toward demonstrated understanding of fraud risks and controls.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.7(d) specifically calls for 'measures to further improve customer understanding and familiarisation with fraud risks and controls, such as through interactive simulations of security features.' Research consistently shows that experiential learning significantly outperforms static content in improving recall and behavioural change, reducing the likelihood of customers falling for fraud.
requirements.owner: Chief Digital Officer / Head of Digital Product
requirements.frequency: Programme availability: Continuous; content refresh: Bi-annual minimum

requirements.requirement: Define and track key performance indicators (KPIs) for programme effectiveness, including customer engagement rates, fraud incident rates attributable to customer error, complaint volumes, and simulation completion rates, with results used to drive programme enhancements.
requirements.rationale: The obligation to 'review and enhance the effectiveness' of the awareness programme requires objective measurement. Without defined KPIs and outcome tracking, the institution cannot demonstrate to BNM that its enhancement efforts are evidence-based, nor can it discharge its duty to continuously improve customer competency.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer / Head of Fraud Risk Management
requirements.frequency: Monthly tracking; Quarterly review with Senior Management

technology.summary: Technology teams must build and maintain the digital infrastructure that enables real-time customer alerting, timely multi-channel content delivery, and interactive fraud awareness experiences across all digital banking platforms. The most technically demanding obligation is the real-time alert capability under Clause 12.7(c), which requires automated detection and customer notification when security controls are absent or not yet deployed. All systems must be capable of rapid content updates to meet the timeliness expectations of Clause 12.7(a).

requirements.requirement: Design and deploy a real-time customer alert and notification engine capable of triggering in-session warnings or push notifications when the system detects that a security measure is absent, degraded, or not yet available for a customer's device, browser, or transaction context.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.7(c) mandates 'real-time alerts of possible risks when security measures are absent or have not yet been implemented.' This is a technical control gap notification capability — for example, alerting a customer transacting on a browser without TLS validation, or on a device where the bank's mobile app certificate pinning has not yet propagated. Without this capability, customers are silently exposed to elevated fraud risk.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; alert latency target under 3 seconds from detection to customer notification

requirements.requirement: Implement a centralised content management system (CMS) integrated with all digital banking channels (web, mobile, ATM, push notification) capable of publishing fraud awareness content updates across all channels simultaneously, with versioning, approval workflows, and audit trails.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.7(a) requires 'continuous and timely updates of practical information.' Without a unified CMS, content updates are manual and error-prone, introducing delays between threat identification and customer notification. A centralised system ensures consistency across channels and supports audit demonstration to BNM that updates are timely and coordinated.
requirements.frequency: Platform availability: 99.9% uptime; content deployment pipeline: capable of same-day publication

requirements.requirement: Develop or integrate interactive fraud simulation modules within the institution's mobile banking application and internet banking portal, enabling customers to practise identifying phishing attempts, verifying genuine app/website indicators, and recognising social engineering scripts in a sandboxed environment.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.7(d) explicitly references 'interactive simulations of security features' as a required measure for improving customer familiarisation. Embedding simulations within the institution's own digital channels maximises reach and relevance while linking the learning experience directly to the customer's actual banking environment.
requirements.frequency: Initial deployment within programme roadmap; content scenarios refreshed Bi-annually; platform maintained continuously

requirements.requirement: Implement in-app and web portal security verification UI components — such as personalised security images, extended validation (EV) certificate indicators, and official app store verification prompts — accompanied by contextual tooltips or micro-tutorials explaining how customers can use these features to confirm authenticity.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.7(b) requires 'clear explanation about new and existing security measures, such as how to verify genuine e-banking websites and mobile applications.' Embedding the explanation directly at the point of the security feature (rather than in a separate FAQ) maximises the likelihood that customers encounter and absorb the information during normal usage.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Security / Head of UX Engineering
requirements.frequency: Deployed at feature launch; reviewed for accuracy and visibility Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Build a fraud awareness analytics dashboard tracking customer engagement with awareness content, simulation completion and pass rates, alert delivery and acknowledgement rates, and correlation with downstream fraud incident data, surfaced to Fraud Risk and Digital Banking management in near real-time.
requirements.rationale: The requirement to 'review and enhance the effectiveness' of the programme is only actionable if technology provides timely, granular data on programme performance. The analytics capability enables data-driven decisions on content gaps, under-served customer segments, and channels with low engagement, forming the evidence base for continuous programme improvement.
requirements.owner: Head of Data & Analytics / Head of Fraud Risk Technology
requirements.frequency: Dashboard available continuously; automated Weekly summary reports to programme owners

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that the Customer Fraud Awareness Programme operates with clear ownership, Board-level oversight, and a documented policy framework that mandates continuous improvement rather than periodic campaigns. The 'S' marker reinforces that BNM considers these obligations standard minimum expectations, and the institution must be able to produce evidence of structured governance, regular management reviews, and documented responses to new modus operandi during supervisory examinations. Governance must also define the escalation pathway when real-time alerts under Clause 12.7(c) identify systemic security gaps.

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Customer Fraud Awareness Programme Policy endorsed by the Board or Board Risk Committee, designating an accountable programme owner, defining review cycles, setting minimum content standards, and mandating measurable effectiveness targets aligned to Clause 12.7 sub-clauses (a) through (d).
requirements.rationale: BNM's use of 'must maintain continuous efforts' establishes a standing regulatory obligation, not a project. A Board-endorsed policy creates the governance mandate, accountability structure, and minimum standards necessary to institutionalise the programme and demonstrate to BNM that compliance is embedded in the institution's operating framework.
requirements.frequency: Policy approved at inception; reviewed and reaffirmed Annually or upon material regulatory change

requirements.requirement: Maintain a live Fraud Modus Operandi Register managed by the Fraud Intelligence function, with defined SLAs for escalating newly identified modus operandi to the awareness programme content team, and a documented approval process for emergency content publication triggered by emerging threats.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.7(a) requires 'continuous and timely updates' including 'specific information about new or common modus operandi.' Without a structured register and escalation SLA, awareness content will lag behind the threat landscape, leaving customers exposed and the institution unable to demonstrate timeliness of response to BNM.
requirements.owner: Head of Fraud Risk Management / CISO
requirements.frequency: Register maintained continuously; SLA for content escalation: within 24 hours of confirmed new modus operandi

requirements.requirement: Establish a Senior Management-level Fraud Awareness Programme Review Committee meeting at minimum Quarterly to assess programme effectiveness KPIs, review new and emerging fraud threats, approve content updates and simulation scenarios, and direct programme enhancements, with minutes tabled to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: The regulatory obligation to 'review and enhance effectiveness' is a continuous Senior Management responsibility under Clause 12.7. A standing review committee with Board-level reporting ensures that programme improvement decisions are made with appropriate authority, cross-functional input (Risk, Digital, Compliance, Marketing), and audit trail.
requirements.owner: CRO (Chair) / Chief Digital Officer / Head of Compliance / CISO
requirements.frequency: Quarterly; extraordinary session within 5 business days of a significant fraud event or regulatory directive

requirements.requirement: Define and operationalise a governance process for security measure gap notifications under Clause 12.7(c), including documented escalation paths when a real-time alert system identifies that a security measure is absent or unimplemented, with defined response timelines and Board Risk Committee notification thresholds.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.7(c) mandates real-time alerts 'when security measures are absent or have not yet been implemented' — this implies a governance obligation, not just a technical one. The institution must have documented procedures for how such alerts are triaged, who is accountable for remediation, and when Board escalation is required, particularly where the gap affects a material customer population.
requirements.owner: CRO / CISO / Head of IT Governance
requirements.frequency: Process defined at inception; tested Semi-annually via tabletop or simulation exercises

requirements.requirement: Produce an Annual Customer Fraud Awareness Programme Effectiveness Report for Board Risk Committee review, incorporating quantitative KPI performance, qualitative assessment of content relevance, outcomes from interactive simulation programmes, audit findings, and a forward-looking enhancement plan for the next programme cycle.
requirements.rationale: BNM may request evidence of programme effectiveness during Technology Risk examinations. An Annual Board-level report creates a formal record of the institution's discharge of its Clause 12.7 obligations, demonstrates that enhancement decisions are evidence-based, and ensures that the highest governance body is informed of the institution's customer fraud risk exposure and mitigation posture.
requirements.owner: CRO / Head of Fraud Risk Management
requirements.frequency: Annual; tabled at Q1 Board Risk Committee meeting following year-end

12.8.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.8.title: Customer Account Suspension and Kill Switch

business.summary: Clause 12.8 imposes a customer-centric obligation on financial institutions to provide immediate, self-service mechanisms for fraud containment, directly reducing the institution's exposure to fraud liability and reputational damage. Failure to implement a functional kill switch or maintain an effective contact centre may constitute a regulatory breach and expose the institution to customer compensation claims, BNM enforcement action, and erosion of public trust. The restoration requirement further ensures that compliance does not create disproportionate customer harm through prolonged service denial.

requirements.requirement: Provide customers with a convenient, multi-channel mechanism (digital and assisted) to report suspected fraud and trigger immediate account suspension without requiring branch or full contact centre escalation as a first step.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates 'convenient means' and 'swiftly' suspend — implying friction-free access. Delayed suspension increases the fraud loss window and shifts liability risk to the institution. BNM expects customers in distress to self-serve without operational bottlenecks.
requirements.owner: Chief Retail Banking Officer / Head of Consumer Banking
requirements.frequency: Continuous (24/7 availability); quarterly review of channel accessibility

requirements.requirement: Maintain a contact centre that is staffed at sufficient capacity to handle elevated fraud-reporting call volumes, including peak and after-hours periods, with defined service level targets for abandoned call rate and average handle time for fraud-related calls.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.8(b) explicitly states the contact centre must be 'adequately resourced and operating effectively to provide prompt and adequate assistance to customers in distress.' Understaffing during fraud waves directly contradicts this obligation and creates regulatory and reputational risk.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer / Head of Contact Centre Operations
requirements.frequency: Continuous staffing; monthly SLA review; annual capacity planning

requirements.requirement: Define and publish (internally and to customers) a maximum account restoration timeframe following successful identity validation after a kill-switch suspension, expressed as a concrete SLA (e.g., within 4 business hours or same-day).
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.8(c) requires restoration 'within a reasonable timeframe upon validation.' Without a defined SLA, 'reasonable' is subjective and unenforceable internally. A documented SLA enables staff accountability, customer expectation management, and BNM audit evidence.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer / Head of Digital Banking
requirements.frequency: SLA definition: Annual review; SLA performance: Monthly monitoring

requirements.requirement: Establish a customer communication protocol that proactively notifies customers of their account suspension status, next steps for restoration, and estimated resolution timeframe immediately upon activation of the kill switch.
requirements.rationale: The obligation to restore access within a 'reasonable timeframe' and to provide 'adequate assistance' implies the institution must keep customers informed during suspension. Silent suspension without guidance increases customer distress and contact centre volume, undermining both compliance and customer experience.
requirements.owner: Head of Customer Experience / Chief Marketing Officer
requirements.frequency: Per-event (triggered on every kill-switch activation); template review: Semi-annual

requirements.requirement: Conduct periodic customer awareness campaigns educating account holders on the existence, purpose, and usage of the kill switch feature, including how to access it via all available channels.
requirements.rationale: The requirement for 'convenient means' presupposes customers are aware such means exist. A kill switch that customers cannot find or do not know about fails the spirit of Clause 12.8. Proactive awareness also reduces the contact centre burden during fraud incidents.
requirements.owner: Chief Retail Banking Officer / Head of Marketing
requirements.frequency: At least semi-annual campaigns; on-boarding communication for new customers

technology.summary: Technology teams must design and deploy a secure, highly available self-service kill switch capability that can be accessed independently of the primary banking platform — ensuring it remains operational even if the main application is compromised or unavailable. The solution must integrate with core banking, fraud management, identity verification, and contact centre systems to provide end-to-end suspension and validated re-activation workflows with full auditability. Security of the kill switch itself is paramount, as a compromised mechanism could be weaponised by attackers to lock out legitimate customers.

requirements.requirement: Implement a secure, self-service kill switch accessible via at least two independent channels (e.g., mobile banking app, dedicated web portal, USSD/IVR fallback) that allows customers to suspend all digital access to their account within a defined response time (target: ≤30 seconds end-to-end) without requiring agent intervention.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.8(a) mandates a 'secure self-service kill switch solution.' Multi-channel availability ensures access even if the primary channel is compromised or unavailable. The speed requirement aligns with 'swiftly' in the verbatim text — every second of delay after a fraud suspicion represents continued exposure.
requirements.frequency: Implementation: One-time build; availability monitoring: Continuous; penetration testing: Annual

requirements.requirement: Architect the kill switch as a logically and, where feasible, physically isolated capability with its own authentication pathway (e.g., step-up authentication using OTP or biometric) that does not depend on the standard session authentication flow, ensuring it remains operational if core banking or the primary authentication service is degraded.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim requirement for a 'secure' kill switch means the mechanism must not be trivially abused to lock out legitimate users, nor fail during the very incidents (e.g., platform attacks) when it is most needed. Isolated architecture prevents the kill switch from being disabled by the same attack vector targeting main banking services.
requirements.frequency: Architecture review: Annual; DR failover testing: Semi-annual

requirements.requirement: Implement an automated, validated re-activation workflow that requires multi-factor identity verification (e.g., biometric liveness check, OTP, knowledge-based authentication) before restoring digital access, with the restoration action reflected across all channels within a defined propagation SLA (target: ≤5 minutes after validation approval).
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.8(c) requires restoration 'upon validation' — the technology must enforce validation as a gate, not a courtesy step. Propagation delay across channels after validation approval would constitute a failure to restore 'within a reasonable timeframe' even if the validation itself is prompt.
requirements.owner: CTO / Head of Identity and Access Management
requirements.frequency: Implementation: One-time build; validation logic review: Annual; propagation SLA monitoring: Continuous

requirements.requirement: Integrate the kill switch system with the fraud management platform and core banking system to ensure that a suspension event triggers real-time blocking of all transaction channels (cards, internet banking, mobile banking, API-based third-party access), generates a fraud case record, and flags the account for enhanced monitoring upon re-activation.
requirements.rationale: A kill switch that suspends the mobile app but leaves card transactions or third-party open banking access active fails to deliver the fraud containment intended by Clause 12.8. Full-channel blocking is implicit in the requirement to provide customers a means to 'suspend their account' — not merely one channel.
requirements.owner: Head of Fraud Technology / Head of Core Banking Systems
requirements.frequency: Integration testing: Quarterly; full end-to-end regression: Semi-annual

requirements.requirement: Maintain immutable, timestamped audit logs of all kill-switch activation and re-activation events, including the channel used, authentication method applied, elapsed time from suspension to restoration, and agent IDs for any assisted interactions, retained for a minimum period consistent with BNM record-keeping requirements.
requirements.rationale: Auditability is foundational to demonstrating compliance with all three sub-clauses of 12.8. Logs provide evidence for BNM examinations, support internal fraud investigations, and enable post-incident analysis to improve restoration timeframes. Immutability prevents tampering by both external actors and insiders.
requirements.owner: CISO / Head of IT Audit and Compliance
requirements.frequency: Log review: Monthly exception-based; full audit trail integrity check: Quarterly

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that the kill switch capability, contact centre resourcing, and restoration processes are subject to defined policies, performance standards, and regular independent assurance. Ownership must be clearly assigned across business, technology, and risk functions with escalation paths for systemic failures or SLA breaches. BNM expects evidence of ongoing management oversight — not merely initial implementation — meaning periodic testing, reporting to senior governance forums, and documented remediation of gaps are all required.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Board-approved Digital Fraud Customer Protection Policy that explicitly covers the kill switch capability, contact centre resourcing standards, and account restoration SLAs, with mandatory annual review and sign-off by the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.8 is a Standard (S) marker requirement, indicating BNM expects institutionalised compliance, not ad hoc implementation. A Board-approved policy formalises the institution's commitment, assigns accountability at the highest level, and provides the governance framework against which operating teams are held accountable.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee / Chief Risk Officer
requirements.frequency: Policy review: Annual; Board endorsement: Annual

requirements.requirement: Implement quarterly kill-switch operational testing (including simulated customer-initiated suspension and re-activation across all channels) and table results, including any SLA breaches or channel failures, to the Management Risk Committee with documented remediation timelines.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.8(a) requires the solution to be 'secure' and Clause 12.8(c) requires restoration 'within a reasonable timeframe' — both imply the institution must verify these properties regularly, not merely at launch. Untested systems frequently degrade silently, and BNM expects evidence of ongoing operational assurance.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer / Head of Operational Risk
requirements.frequency: Testing: Quarterly; reporting to Management Risk Committee: Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Define and enforce contact centre resourcing standards for fraud-related interactions, including minimum staffing ratios, escalation protocols for high-volume fraud events, and measurable KPIs (e.g., abandoned call rate <5%, average speed of answer <60 seconds for fraud lines), subject to monthly management review.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.8(b) explicitly requires the contact centre to be 'adequately resourced and operating effectively.' Without defined, measurable standards, 'adequately' has no operational meaning and cannot be assured or demonstrated to BNM. KPI-based governance converts a qualitative obligation into a verifiable compliance position.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer / Head of Contact Centre
requirements.frequency: KPI monitoring: Monthly; resourcing adequacy review: Quarterly; annual capacity plan sign-off

requirements.requirement: Establish a kill-switch incident escalation and reporting procedure that requires all cases where the restoration SLA is breached, or where the kill switch failed to function as expected, to be reported to the CRO within 24 hours and included in the quarterly Operational Risk Report submitted to the Board Audit and Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: Clause 12.8(c) creates an obligation to restore access within a 'reasonable timeframe.' Governance must define what constitutes a failure of that obligation and ensure such failures surface to accountable senior leaders. Without a mandatory escalation path, SLA breaches may be resolved at an operational level without appropriate oversight or systemic remediation.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer / Head of Operational Risk
requirements.frequency: Per-event escalation (within 24 hours of SLA breach); aggregate reporting: Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Commission an independent internal audit of the kill switch end-to-end process (covering technical controls, contact centre resourcing, identity validation, and restoration SLA adherence) at least annually, with findings reported to the Board Audit Committee and a management response tracking register maintained until closure.
requirements.rationale: BNM's supervisory expectations for Standard (S) marker clauses require evidence of independent assurance, not merely management self-certification. An annual internal audit provides objective validation that all three sub-clauses of 12.8 are operationally effective, identifies control gaps, and demonstrates to BNM a culture of continuous compliance improvement.
requirements.owner: Chief Internal Auditor / Head of Internal Audit
requirements.frequency: Audit execution: Annual; findings tracking: Continuous until closure

12.9.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.9.title: Post-Incident Customer Protection

business.summary: Clause 12.9 requires financial institutions to move beyond internal incident containment and actively protect customers whose accounts or credentials may have been compromised. Failure to comply exposes the institution to regulatory sanctions, reputational damage, and civil liability arising from preventable customer losses that occur after an incident is identified. Timely notification and credential action are not merely operational courtesies — they are mandatory safeguards that define the institution's duty of care to its customers.

requirements.requirement: Define and operationalise a Post-Incident Customer Protection (PICP) procedure that activates automatically upon detection of any incident involving potential fraud or compromise of customer data, covering heightened account monitoring, customer notification, and credential revocation as mandated by sub-clauses (a), (b), and (c).
requirements.rationale: The clause states the institution 'must adopt additional measures' beyond existing incident handling, signalling that a standalone PICP procedure is required. Without a formally activated procedure, individual response actions may be inconsistent, delayed, or omitted entirely, exposing customers to continued fraud risk.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (triggered upon incident classification); procedure reviewed Annually

requirements.requirement: Establish defined customer notification standards specifying maximum time-to-notify thresholds, mandatory content (nature of incident, self-mitigation steps, contact channels), and approved communication channels (SMS, email, in-app push, branch), aligned to sub-clause (b).
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (b) explicitly requires the institution to 'notify affected customers and provide them with the necessary information to apply mitigating measures.' Without prescribed content standards and timelines, notifications may omit actionable guidance, rendering them non-compliant and leaving customers unable to protect themselves.
requirements.frequency: Per-event; standards reviewed Annually or after every major incident

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a Customer Impact Assessment (CIA) methodology to rapidly identify and segment affected customers by risk tier (e.g., confirmed compromise vs. potentially exposed), ensuring proportionate application of heightened monitoring and revocation measures under sub-clauses (a) and (c).
requirements.rationale: Effective targeting of protective measures requires knowing which customers are affected and to what degree. Blanket or delayed identification wastes response capacity and may leave high-risk customers under-protected while over-burdening low-risk ones, undermining the efficiency and proportionality expected under a Standard ('S') clause.
requirements.frequency: Per-event; methodology reviewed Semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Ensure adequate customer-facing support capacity (contact centre staffing, IVR queuing, branch readiness) is scalable and pre-planned for post-incident surges arising from customer inquiries following notifications issued under sub-clause (b).
requirements.rationale: Customer notifications mandated by sub-clause (b) will predictably generate inbound inquiry volumes. Without pre-planned surge capacity, customers will be unable to access support to apply mitigating measures, directly undermining the protective intent of the notification requirement and creating secondary reputational and complaint risk.
requirements.owner: Chief Customer Officer / Head of Retail Banking
requirements.frequency: Per-event activation; capacity planning reviewed Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Maintain complete, timestamped records of all post-incident customer protection actions taken — including accounts placed under heightened monitoring, notifications dispatched, and credentials or payment instruments revoked and re-issued — to support regulatory reporting, audit, and potential litigation.
requirements.rationale: BNM may require the institution to demonstrate compliance with each sub-clause of 12.9. Incomplete records will impede supervisory review and leave the institution unable to evidence that customers were protected. Records also support internal lessons-learned and potential civil liability defence.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (per-event capture); records retained per BNM data retention requirements

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement integrated systems capable of dynamically elevating monitoring controls on affected accounts, executing multi-channel customer notifications with regulatory-grade content, and automating credential and payment instrument revocation and re-issuance workflows at speed and scale. These capabilities must be interoperable across fraud detection, core banking, identity management, and communications platforms, with full audit logging to support compliance evidence. Given the 'S' marker, these systems must be production-grade, tested, and ready to activate under real incident conditions.

requirements.requirement: Implement an automated heightened account monitoring module that — upon incident trigger — applies elevated transaction velocity rules, step-up authentication thresholds, and real-time anomaly scoring to all identified affected accounts, as required by sub-clause (a).
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (a) requires heightened monitoring to activate in response to an incident, not merely as a static control. Manual rule changes are too slow and error-prone at scale. Automated, parameter-driven escalation ensures all affected accounts receive enhanced surveillance immediately upon identification, minimising the fraud exposure window.
requirements.frequency: Per-event activation; rule parameters reviewed Quarterly; penetration tested Annually

requirements.requirement: Build and operate a multi-channel customer notification engine capable of delivering templated, personalised incident notifications via SMS, email, and in-app messaging simultaneously, with delivery confirmation logging, supporting the notification mandate in sub-clause (b).
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (b) requires that affected customers are notified and equipped with information to apply mitigating measures. A multi-channel approach maximises notification reach across diverse customer segments. Delivery confirmation logging creates the audit trail required to demonstrate compliance and identify customers who did not receive notification for follow-up action.
requirements.frequency: Per-event activation; channel delivery capacity tested Semi-annually

requirements.requirement: Implement an automated credential revocation and re-issuance workflow covering usernames, passwords, OTP seeds, security tokens, and designated payment instruments (cards, virtual accounts), capable of bulk revocation scoped to affected customer sets, as required by sub-clause (c).
requirements.rationale: Sub-clause (c) mandates revocation and re-issuance 'where there is a potential risk of exploitation due to compromised data or other fraudulent activity.' Manual credential management at incident scale is operationally infeasible and risks delay. Automated, scoped bulk revocation with immediate re-issuance pathways is the only viable mechanism to meet the clause's protective intent.
requirements.owner: Head of Identity and Access Management (IAM) / CISO
requirements.frequency: Per-event activation; workflow tested Semi-annually in tabletop and simulation exercises

requirements.requirement: Integrate the fraud detection platform, core banking system, identity management platform, and customer notification engine via APIs to enable end-to-end automated PICP workflow orchestration with a defined maximum latency target from incident classification to first protective action.
requirements.rationale: The three sub-clauses of 12.9 require coordinated, near-simultaneous actions across multiple technology domains. Siloed systems operating without integration will introduce dangerous latency between incident identification and customer protection. A defined latency target operationalises the requirement and enables SLA measurement.
requirements.frequency: Integration health monitored Continuously; end-to-end tested Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement comprehensive, immutable audit logging for all PICP system actions — account monitoring escalations, notification dispatches with delivery status, and credential/instrument revocations and re-issuances — with tamper-evident storage and retention aligned to BNM requirements.
requirements.rationale: Compliance with all three sub-clauses must be demonstrable to BNM supervisors. Immutable logs with full timestamps and actor attribution provide the evidence base for regulatory examination. Tamper-evident storage ensures log integrity is preserved for potential enforcement or litigation proceedings.
requirements.frequency: Continuous capture; log integrity reviewed Monthly; retention policy reviewed Annually

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure that post-incident customer protection obligations are embedded in the institution's risk management framework, with clear ownership, escalation paths, and Board-level oversight. Policies must define the thresholds and decision criteria for triggering each sub-clause action, and the effectiveness of the PICP framework must be independently tested and reported on a regular cadence. Given the externally visible and customer-facing nature of these obligations, governance failures carry both regulatory and reputational consequences.

requirements.requirement: Establish a Board-approved Post-Incident Customer Protection Policy that formally incorporates all three sub-clause obligations — heightened monitoring, customer notification, and credential/instrument revocation — and assigns accountable owners, escalation triggers, and decision authority at each stage of the PICP workflow.
requirements.rationale: A Standard ('S') marker clause imposes a mandatory obligation on the institution as a whole, not merely its operational teams. Board approval of the PICP Policy establishes institutional accountability and ensures senior management is aware of and committed to the regulatory requirement, reducing the risk of the obligation being treated as a technical afterthought.
requirements.frequency: Policy approved by Board Annually; reviewed after every material incident

requirements.requirement: Define and document quantitative and qualitative criteria for classifying an incident as involving 'potential fraud or compromise of customer data,' establishing clear thresholds that trigger PICP activation and ensuring consistent, defensible application of sub-clauses (a), (b), and (c).
requirements.rationale: The clause applies to incidents involving 'potential' fraud or compromise — a deliberately broad standard. Without defined classification criteria, response teams may under-trigger or over-trigger PICP actions, leading either to customer harm from delayed protection or to operational overload from disproportionate responses. Clear thresholds also provide a basis for regulatory defence.
requirements.frequency: Criteria reviewed Annually and after each PICP activation; approved by Risk Management Committee

requirements.requirement: Conduct at minimum annual simulation exercises (tabletop and live-fire) testing the end-to-end PICP workflow across all three sub-clauses, with results documented, gaps remediated, and a summary report tabled to the Board Risk Committee.
requirements.rationale: Regulatory compliance cannot be assumed — it must be demonstrated through tested capability. Sub-clauses (a), (b), and (c) require coordinated actions across multiple teams and systems; untested workflows will fail under real incident pressure. Annual simulation ensures the institution can evidence to BNM that its PICP procedures are operationally effective, not merely documented.
requirements.owner: CRO / Head of Business Continuity and Resilience
requirements.frequency: Annually (minimum); after any material incident or significant system change

requirements.requirement: Establish a post-incident regulatory notification and reporting protocol that determines when and how BNM must be informed of incidents triggering PICP activation, including the content of regulatory reports covering customer impact scope, protective actions taken, and outcomes.
requirements.rationale: BNM's supervisory oversight of financial institutions requires timely and accurate reporting of material incidents. Failure to report, or reporting that omits the customer protection actions taken under 12.9, undermines supervisory effectiveness and may constitute a secondary regulatory breach. A defined protocol eliminates ambiguity and ensures consistent, complete disclosures.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (regulatory notification); protocol reviewed Annually

requirements.requirement: Implement a post-incident lessons-learned governance cycle requiring a formal After-Action Review (AAR) following each PICP activation, with documented findings on the effectiveness of monitoring, notification, and revocation actions, and a tracked remediation plan tabled to the Risk Management Committee.
requirements.rationale: Continuous improvement of PICP effectiveness requires systematic capture of what worked and what failed in each real-world activation. An institutionalised AAR cycle ensures that gaps identified in clause 12.9 compliance are escalated to governance bodies rather than remaining at operational level, and that remediation is tracked to closure rather than deferred indefinitely.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (within 30 days of incident closure); findings tracked to remediation

business.summary: This clause requires financial institutions to calibrate their technology audit programme — its breadth, cadence, and depth — to the actual risk profile of each system and application. Failure to do so creates blind spots where critical or complex systems go under-scrutinised, exposing the institution to undetected operational failures, data breaches, and regulatory sanctions. BNM's proportionality principle means a one-size-fits-all audit schedule is non-compliant and operationally insufficient.

requirements.requirement: Define and maintain a technology asset inventory that classifies each system and application by business criticality, operational complexity, and technological sophistication, forming the foundational input for determining audit scope and frequency.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text anchors audit parameters to 'complexity, sophistication and criticality of technology systems and applications.' Without a formalised classification, the institution cannot demonstrate that its audit programme is commensurate with these dimensions, exposing it to BNM scrutiny.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; updated within 30 days of any material system change or new system onboarding

requirements.requirement: Establish a risk-tiered audit frequency model that mandates higher audit cadence (e.g., semi-annual or quarterly) for Tier-1 critical systems (core banking, payment switches, customer-facing platforms) and proportionally lower frequency for low-criticality internal tools.
requirements.rationale: The clause's phrase 'commensurate with … criticality' directly requires that critical systems receive more frequent audit scrutiny. A flat annual audit cycle for all systems fails this proportionality test and leaves high-impact systems inadequately covered.
requirements.frequency: Tiered: Tier-1 systems — semi-annual minimum; Tier-2 — annual; Tier-3 — biennial; schedule reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Allocate dedicated audit budget and specialist resources proportionate to the complexity and sophistication of technology systems under audit scope, ensuring that resource constraints do not reduce audit intensity below the level warranted by system risk.
requirements.rationale: The clause's reference to 'intensity' implies depth of scrutiny, not merely frequency. Insufficient audit resourcing — budget, skilled auditors, tooling — directly undermines intensity, particularly for sophisticated systems (e.g., AI/ML platforms, real-time settlement infrastructure) requiring specialist expertise.
requirements.frequency: Annual budget cycle; mid-year review if material technology changes occur

requirements.requirement: Ensure business units provide complete and accurate system metadata (including interdependencies, data sensitivity, transaction volumes, and regulatory obligations) to the internal audit function as input to audit scoping decisions.
requirements.rationale: Audit scope cannot be 'commensurate with … complexity' unless business owners disclose the full operating context of each system. Incomplete disclosures lead to under-scoped audits that miss critical risk vectors, creating liability for both the business unit owner and the institution.
requirements.owner: Chief Operating Officer (COO) / Business Unit Heads
requirements.frequency: Annual submission aligned to audit planning cycle; ad hoc for new or materially changed systems

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal escalation and exception process whereby any proposed reduction in audit scope, frequency, or intensity for a critical or complex system must receive written approval from the CRO and be reported to the Board Audit Committee with a documented risk acceptance rationale.
requirements.rationale: The proportionality requirement in the clause creates a de facto minimum floor of audit coverage for critical systems. Any deviation from this floor without documented risk acceptance constitutes non-compliance and must be governed through a formal exception management process to preserve regulatory defensibility.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (triggered by any proposed scope reduction); reviewed at each Board Audit Committee meeting

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement a systematic, data-driven capability to classify systems by complexity, sophistication, and criticality, and expose that classification to the audit function in a structured and auditable manner. This includes maintaining tooling that enables variable-intensity technical audits — from automated continuous scans for high-criticality systems to periodic manual reviews for simpler applications. The evidence trail from these technical audit activities must be retained in a tamper-evident, regulator-accessible repository.

requirements.requirement: Implement and maintain a Configuration Management Database (CMDB) or equivalent technology asset registry that records system complexity indicators (architecture layers, integration points, third-party dependencies), sophistication indicators (AI/ML components, cryptographic implementations, real-time processing), and criticality ratings (RTO/RPO thresholds, regulatory designation, revenue impact) for every technology asset in scope.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires audits to be calibrated to 'complexity, sophistication and criticality.' The CMDB provides the objective, versioned data source that both the IT function and the internal audit function use to determine appropriate audit parameters. Without it, audit scope decisions are subjective and indefensible under BNM examination.
requirements.owner: Head of IT / Chief Technology Officer (CTO)
requirements.frequency: Continuously maintained; full reconciliation against production environment quarterly

requirements.requirement: Deploy a tiered technical audit toolset calibrated to system complexity and sophistication: automated vulnerability scanning and continuous configuration compliance monitoring for Tier-1 critical systems; scheduled automated scans with manual penetration testing for Tier-2 systems; and periodic automated scans for Tier-3 systems. Tool coverage and scan depth must be documented per tier.
requirements.rationale: The clause's 'intensity' dimension requires that complex and sophisticated systems receive deeper technical scrutiny than simpler ones. A uniform toolset applied at the same depth across all systems fails to satisfy proportionality. Tiered tooling ensures audit intensity is technically operationalised, not just policy-stated.
requirements.frequency: Tier-1: continuous monitoring with monthly deep scans; Tier-2: quarterly scans with annual penetration testing; Tier-3: semi-annual scans; toolset reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Implement a centralised, tamper-evident audit evidence repository (e.g., SIEM-integrated log archive or dedicated GRC platform) that captures and retains technical audit artefacts — scan reports, configuration snapshots, access logs, change records — for a minimum of seven years, with access controls that allow internal audit and regulatory examiners to retrieve evidence independently of IT operations.
requirements.rationale: Audit intensity is only demonstrable if evidence of the audit activity is preserved and accessible. The clause implicitly requires that the institution can demonstrate to BNM that its audit programme was commensurate with system risk; this requires a reliable, tamper-evident evidence trail that is not controllable by the audited party.
requirements.frequency: Continuous ingestion; integrity checks monthly; retention policy reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Integrate the CMDB criticality and complexity classification into the IT change management and risk assessment workflow, such that any change that materially increases a system's complexity, sophistication, or criticality automatically triggers a re-evaluation of its audit tier and associated audit parameters within 30 days.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires audit parameters to remain commensurate with current system characteristics, not historical ones. Systems evolve — a simple internal tool may become a critical customer-facing platform after a release. Without automated integration between change management and audit classification, audit scope drifts out of alignment with actual risk.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (triggered by material system changes); full review at each annual audit planning cycle

requirements.requirement: Provide the internal audit function with read-only, privileged access to production system logs, configuration management tooling, network topology diagrams, and API documentation for all in-scope systems, ensuring technical auditors have the access required to independently verify the scope and intensity of controls without relying on IT-mediated evidence.
requirements.rationale: Audit intensity requires that auditors can probe systems at the depth warranted by their complexity. If technical access is gated or filtered by the IT function being audited, the independence and completeness of the audit is compromised, directly undermining the clause's requirement for an intensity commensurate with system sophistication.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of IT Security
requirements.frequency: Access provisioned on an ongoing basis; access rights reviewed semi-annually and upon auditor role changes

governance.summary: The governance framework must ensure that the Board Audit Committee and senior management have formal oversight of the technology audit programme's design, execution, and results, with documented policies that encode the proportionality principle into institutional standards. Governance structures must prevent the audit function from being constrained in scope or frequency by cost pressures or operational convenience, and must ensure independent validation that the audit programme is genuinely commensurate with technology risk. Periodic self-assessments and external quality assurance reviews are necessary to validate the programme's adequacy against BNM's expectations.

requirements.requirement: Establish and Board-approve a Technology Audit Policy that explicitly encodes the proportionality principle — mandating that audit scope, frequency, and intensity are determined by system complexity, sophistication, and criticality ratings — and defines the methodology, roles, responsibilities, escalation paths, and minimum standards for each audit tier.
requirements.rationale: The clause imposes an institutional obligation ('A financial institution must ensure') that requires a formal policy instrument to operationalise and sustain compliance across audit cycles. Without a Board-approved policy, individual audit decisions are ad hoc and not institutionally accountable, leaving the institution exposed in a regulatory examination.
requirements.frequency: Board approval upon initial adoption; reviewed and re-approved annually or following material regulatory changes

requirements.requirement: Mandate that the annual technology audit plan — including the scope, frequency, and planned intensity for each system and application tier — is reviewed and approved by the Board Audit Committee before the commencement of each audit cycle, with explicit documentation that the plan is commensurate with the current complexity, sophistication, and criticality profile of the technology estate.
requirements.rationale: Board-level approval of the audit plan is the primary governance control ensuring that resource and scope decisions are made at an appropriate level of authority. This directly fulfils the institution-level obligation in the clause and creates a clear accountability record for BNM examination.
requirements.owner: Board Audit Committee Chairperson / Head of Internal Audit
requirements.frequency: Annual (pre-cycle approval); ad hoc review if material changes to technology estate occur mid-cycle

requirements.requirement: Require the Head of Internal Audit to present a semi-annual Technology Audit Programme status report to the Board Audit Committee, including: percentage of planned audits completed on schedule, findings by severity tier, remediation status of prior findings, and a formal attestation that the programme's scope and intensity remain commensurate with current technology risk.
requirements.rationale: The clause's ongoing obligation ('must ensure') cannot be satisfied by a once-a-year planning exercise alone. Continuous Board oversight through structured reporting ensures that deviations from planned audit coverage — due to resourcing gaps, scope creep, or emerging technology risk — are surfaced and addressed at governance level in a timely manner.
requirements.frequency: Semi-annual reporting to Board Audit Committee; summary included in Annual Report internal audit section
