requirements.requirement: Commission an independent external quality assurance (QA) review of the technology audit function at least every three years, specifically assessing whether the audit programme's scope, frequency, and intensity are commensurate with the institution's technology risk profile in accordance with BNM RMiT requirements and international standards (e.g., IIA Standards, ISACA frameworks).
requirements.rationale: Internal audit cannot objectively assess its own adequacy. An external QA review provides independent validation that the programme genuinely satisfies the proportionality requirement of the clause, and demonstrates to BNM that the institution has sought external assurance — a key indicator of governance maturity expected of Standard-marker obligations.
requirements.frequency: Every three years minimum; additionally triggered following material technology transformation programmes or adverse BNM examination findings

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Technology Audit Scope Exemption Register where any system or application that is downgraded in audit tier — resulting in reduced scope, frequency, or intensity relative to its criticality rating — is recorded with: the justification, the approving authority (minimum CRO), the compensating controls in place, and the scheduled date for re-assessment. The register must be tabled at each Board Audit Committee meeting.
requirements.rationale: Proportionality obligations require that any departure from the minimum audit standards warranted by a system's risk profile is explicitly governed and time-limited. Without a formal exemption register, reductions in audit intensity occur informally and are undetectable during regulatory examination, creating compliance exposure under the 'must ensure' language of the clause.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Internal Audit
requirements.frequency: Register maintained continuously; tabled at every Board Audit Committee meeting (minimum quarterly)

business.summary: Failure to maintain a structured annual technology audit plan exposes the institution to undetected operational failures, third-party risks, and regulatory censure. Without defined coverage of critical technology services and post-implementation reviews, material system deficiencies may go unaudited, undermining service continuity and customer trust. BNM expects the audit plan to be a living document aligned to the institution's evolving technology risk profile.

requirements.requirement: Establish and formally approve an annual technology audit plan that explicitly lists all critical technology services, third-party service providers, material external system interfaces, and identifies any delayed or prematurely terminated critical technology projects for audit coverage.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates 'appropriate frequency and coverage' of specific categories. Without a formally documented plan identifying each category, the institution cannot demonstrate compliance and risks regulatory findings during BNM examinations.
requirements.frequency: Annual (plan produced before start of each financial year)

requirements.requirement: Define and document the business risk criteria used to classify technology services as 'critical', and maintain a live register of critical technology services, material external system interfaces, and third-party service providers that feeds directly into the audit plan.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'appropriate frequency and coverage of critical technology services' — this presupposes a defined and agreed classification framework. Without it, audit coverage cannot be objectively justified to BNM.
requirements.frequency: Annual review of classification criteria; continuous update of the register

requirements.requirement: Mandate post-implementation reviews (PIR) for all new or materially enhanced technology services as a standard business process, with PIR completion dates included within the annual technology audit plan.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly requires 'post-implementation review of new or material enhancements of technology services'. Embedding PIRs as a scheduled audit deliverable ensures business units cannot bypass review obligations following go-live.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (triggered by each new or material technology change); tracked annually

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal escalation and reporting mechanism to include delayed or prematurely terminated critical technology projects in the audit plan scope, with documented rationale for any change in project status.
requirements.rationale: The clause specifically calls out 'delayed or prematurely terminated critical technology projects' as an audit category. Without a trigger-based escalation mechanism, such projects may fall outside audit coverage, leaving unresolved technology risks unexamined.
requirements.owner: Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Chief Audit Executive (CAE)
requirements.frequency: Per-event (triggered by project delay or termination); reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Conduct a structured annual review of the preceding year's technology audit plan to assess whether coverage and frequency were adequate, document gaps, and use findings to improve the subsequent year's plan.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires the institution to 'establish annual review' of the plan — this implies retrospective evaluation, not merely plan creation. Continuous improvement of the audit plan demonstrates adaptive governance to BNM.
requirements.owner: Chief Audit Executive (CAE) / Audit Committee Chairman
requirements.frequency: Annual

technology.summary: Technology teams must provide structured, timely data inputs to the audit function covering the full inventory of critical systems, third-party integrations, and material interfaces. Systems and tooling must support auditability by generating the artefacts needed for post-implementation reviews and project status tracking. The technology function is also responsible for ensuring audit access and evidence are available for all mandated coverage categories.

requirements.requirement: Maintain a centralised, continuously updated Technology Asset and Service Inventory that catalogs all critical technology services, material external system interfaces, and third-party service provider connections, tagged with criticality classification, and made accessible to the internal audit function.
requirements.rationale: The audit plan's 'appropriate coverage' of critical technology services and material external interfaces depends on a reliable, current inventory. Without it, the audit function cannot scope engagements accurately, and coverage gaps become inevitable.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of IT Architecture
requirements.frequency: Continuous update; formal annual reconciliation against the audit plan

requirements.requirement: Implement a Technology Project Portfolio Management (PPM) system that tracks the status of all critical technology projects, automatically flags projects that are delayed beyond defined thresholds or are prematurely terminated, and generates notifications to the audit function.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates audit coverage of 'delayed or prematurely terminated critical technology projects'. Automated status tracking and alerting ensures the audit plan is dynamically updated when project status changes, preventing coverage gaps.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Programme Management Office (PMO)
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; status reports generated monthly and on-event

requirements.requirement: Establish a standardised Post-Implementation Review (PIR) framework for technology deployments, including mandatory evidence collection (test results, configuration baselines, incident logs, performance benchmarks) that is preserved and accessible for audit within 90 days of go-live for new or materially enhanced technology services.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires 'post-implementation review of new or material enhancements of technology services'. A technical framework with defined evidence artefacts ensures PIR audits are substantive and not merely procedural tick-box exercises.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (each technology deployment or material enhancement)

requirements.requirement: Provide the internal audit function with read-only, privileged access to system logs, configuration management databases (CMDB), and third-party service provider performance dashboards to support independent audit fieldwork across all mandated coverage categories.
requirements.rationale: Effective audit of critical technology services and third-party providers requires direct evidence access. Without technical access controls enabling audit independence, the audit function's coverage and objectivity are compromised.
requirements.frequency: Continuous access provisioned; access rights reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Implement audit management tooling or GRC (Governance, Risk and Compliance) platform integration that maps audit engagements to the technology asset inventory, tracks coverage against planned frequency, and produces management reports showing year-to-date audit completion status versus the approved annual technology audit plan.
requirements.rationale: Demonstrating 'appropriate frequency and coverage' to BNM requires quantitative reporting against the plan. GRC tooling provides the audit trail and coverage metrics necessary for regulatory examination and Board reporting.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Risk and Compliance / Chief Audit Executive (CAE)
requirements.frequency: Continuous tracking; quarterly management reporting; annual plan reconciliation

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure the annual technology audit plan is independently developed, formally approved by the Audit Committee, and subject to rigorous oversight throughout the year. Board-level accountability for technology audit coverage is essential given the 'S' (Standard) marker, and the plan must be explicitly linked to the institution's broader technology risk management framework. Gaps or deviations from the plan must be formally escalated and remediated.

requirements.requirement: Require the Board Audit Committee to formally review and approve the annual technology audit plan before commencement of each audit cycle, with documented evidence that the plan addresses all mandated coverage categories: critical technology services, third-party service providers, material external system interfaces, delayed or prematurely terminated projects, and post-implementation reviews.
requirements.rationale: BNM expects Board-level oversight of the technology audit function. Formal Audit Committee approval of the plan with documented coverage mapping ensures accountability at the highest governance level and provides a clear regulatory examination artefact.
requirements.frequency: Annual (prior to start of audit cycle)

requirements.requirement: Establish a Technology Audit Charter that defines the mandate, independence, authority, and coverage obligations of the technology audit function, explicitly referencing RMiT Clause 13.2 coverage categories, and have this charter approved by the Board Audit Committee.
requirements.rationale: A charter formalises the audit function's authority to access all systems, data, and third-party arrangements necessary to discharge its obligations. Without it, the audit function may lack the institutional mandate to audit sensitive areas such as material external interfaces or third-party providers.
requirements.owner: Chief Audit Executive (CAE) / Board Audit Committee
requirements.frequency: Reviewed and reaffirmed annually; updated on material organisational change

requirements.requirement: Implement a quarterly Audit Committee reporting cycle covering year-to-date technology audit plan execution status, coverage achieved versus planned, any deviations or deferrals from the approved plan, and actions taken to address delayed or prematurely terminated technology projects identified during the period.
requirements.rationale: The requirement to establish an annual review implies ongoing governance, not a once-a-year exercise. Quarterly Board Audit Committee reporting ensures deviations are caught and remediated within the audit cycle rather than discovered retrospectively.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly reporting; ad hoc escalation on material deviations

requirements.requirement: Define a formal policy for managing deviations from the approved annual technology audit plan, including documented approval authority for deferring or cancelling planned audit engagements, mandatory compensating controls during deferral periods, and Board Audit Committee notification for any deferral exceeding 90 days.
requirements.rationale: The clause's requirement for 'appropriate frequency' implies that deviations must be governed and justified. An unmanaged deviation from the plan — e.g., failure to audit a critical third-party provider — could constitute a regulatory breach if not formally tracked and escalated.
requirements.owner: Chief Audit Executive (CAE) / Board Audit Committee
requirements.frequency: Per-event (triggered by any plan deviation); policy reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Ensure the annual technology audit plan is aligned with and cross-referenced to the institution's Technology Risk Appetite Statement and Risk Register, so that audit coverage frequency is proportionate to the risk rating of each critical technology service, third-party provider, and material external interface.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires 'appropriate frequency' — appropriateness must be calibrated against documented risk. Linkage between the audit plan and the risk register provides the objective basis for justifying coverage decisions to BNM examiners.
requirements.frequency: Annual alignment exercise; updated when risk register has material changes

business.summary: Failure to maintain professionally certified, specialised technology audit resources exposes the institution to undetected systemic risks across its technology stack and delivery channels. Without auditors who are adequately conversant with the institution's evolving technology landscape, audit findings may be superficial, incomplete, or untimely — undermining the Board's assurance over technology risk. This directly increases the likelihood of regulatory censure, reputational damage, and unmitigated operational or cyber incidents.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a dedicated internal technology audit function with headcount ring-fenced exclusively for technology audit activities, separate from general internal audit resources.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly mandates 'dedicated technology audit resources', precluding the practice of rotating general auditors into technology assignments on an ad-hoc basis. Commingling resources dilutes accountability and technical depth, creating coverage gaps across critical systems.
requirements.frequency: Continuous (staffing maintained at all times; reviewed annually against technology complexity growth)

requirements.requirement: Define and maintain a Technology Audit Competency Framework that maps required specialisations (e.g., cybersecurity, cloud infrastructure, core banking, digital channels, data governance) to business-critical technology domains and delivery channels operated by the institution.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires 'sound knowledge in the areas audited' and conversance with 'technology systems and delivery channels'. A formal competency framework operationalises this by identifying coverage gaps before an audit cycle begins, ensuring no material technology domain is audited by unqualified staff.
requirements.frequency: Annual review; updated upon introduction of new technology systems or delivery channels

requirements.requirement: Establish a mandatory continuing professional development (CPD) programme for technology auditors, with minimum annual CPD hours specified per role tier, aligned to the institution's technology roadmap and emerging threat landscape.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires auditors to be 'adequately conversant with the developing sophisticate' of technology systems — implying an ongoing obligation, not a one-time credential. A CPD programme institutionalises this requirement and provides evidence of continuous upskilling for regulatory examination.
requirements.owner: Chief Audit Executive (CAE) / Chief People Officer (CPO)
requirements.frequency: Annual CPD completion; programme reviewed bi-annually

requirements.requirement: Include technology audit resource competency status — comprising certification currency, CPD completion rates, and coverage-to-domain alignment — as a standing agenda item in the Audit Committee's quarterly reporting pack.
requirements.rationale: Board-level visibility over audit function capability is essential for the Audit Committee to discharge its oversight responsibility. The clause's 'S' marker reflects BNM's expectation that competency is an institutional commitment, not merely an HR matter, requiring governance-layer monitoring.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Define and document an approved roster of external technology audit co-sourcing or guest auditor arrangements to supplement internal capability for highly specialised domains (e.g., OT/ICS, cryptographic systems, AI/ML model risk) where in-house expertise is impractical to maintain.
requirements.rationale: For institutions with diverse or rapidly evolving technology portfolios, the clause's requirement for 'sound knowledge in the areas audited' may exceed internally sustainable capacity. A pre-approved co-sourcing roster ensures specialist coverage without compromising audit independence or timeliness.
requirements.owner: Chief Audit Executive (CAE) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Annual review of roster; engaged per audit plan as needed

technology.summary: Technology teams must actively support the audit function's conversance with evolving systems by providing structured knowledge transfer, maintaining up-to-date technology architecture documentation, and ensuring auditors have appropriate read-only access to relevant systems and tooling. The clause's reference to 'delivery channels' and 'developing sophisticate of technology systems' imposes a technical obligation to keep audit capabilities aligned with the pace of technology change. Failure to do so results in auditors assessing systems they do not understand, producing unreliable assurance.

requirements.requirement: Maintain a living Technology Architecture Register accessible to the internal audit function, documenting all production systems, delivery channels, integration points, and material technology changes — updated within 30 days of any significant architectural change.
requirements.rationale: Auditors cannot be 'adequately conversant' with the institution's technology systems without authoritative, current documentation. An accessible register enables technology auditors to scope engagements accurately and identify which specialisations are required for each audit, directly satisfying the 'developing sophisticate' requirement.
requirements.owner: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) / Head of Enterprise Architecture
requirements.frequency: Continuous maintenance; formal review quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement a structured Technology Induction and Briefing Programme for technology auditors, delivered by technology subject-matter experts prior to each major audit cycle, covering material system changes, new delivery channels, and identified technology risk areas since the previous cycle.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires auditors to be conversant with 'developing sophisticate' — a dynamic standard that escalates as the institution adopts new technologies. Periodic SME-led briefings operationalise this by transferring tacit system knowledge that documentation alone cannot convey, particularly for complex core banking, cloud, and cybersecurity environments.
requirements.frequency: Pre-audit cycle (minimum semi-annual); ad-hoc upon material technology change

requirements.requirement: Provision technology audit staff with read-only, auditor-class access to relevant SIEM, log management, vulnerability management, configuration management, and GRC platforms, governed by a formal access management policy and access review.
requirements.rationale: Professionally certified technology auditors require tool access commensurate with their specialisation to conduct substantive testing. Restricting auditors to manual sampling or relying solely on management-provided outputs undermines audit independence and limits the auditors' ability to demonstrate 'sound knowledge in the areas audited' through direct evidence inspection.
requirements.frequency: Access provisioned per engagement; access rights reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Establish a Technology Audit Toolset — including data analytics, network scanning, vulnerability assessment, and configuration review tools — maintained, licensed, and kept current by IT, available exclusively for internal audit use on isolated audit infrastructure.
requirements.rationale: Specialised technology audit competency (as mandated) cannot be exercised without commensurate tooling. Audit-grade tools on isolated infrastructure ensure auditors can independently verify technical controls without dependence on operational teams, preserving the integrity of audit findings particularly for cybersecurity and infrastructure audits.
requirements.frequency: Tool currency reviewed quarterly; infrastructure reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Produce and maintain a Delivery Channel Technology Map — documenting online banking, mobile banking, ATM/CDM networks, API banking, and emerging channels — including third-party dependencies, technology stacks, and relevant regulatory obligations, made available to the audit function.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly names 'delivery channels' as a domain in which technology audit resources must be conversant. A formal channel map enables auditors to assess end-to-end technology controls across customer-facing channels, which are high-risk areas for fraud, data breaches, and regulatory non-compliance in a Malaysian banking context.
requirements.frequency: Updated per channel change; formal review semi-annual

governance.summary: Governance structures must formalise the institution's commitment to technology audit competency through Board-level policy, independent oversight by the Audit Committee, and rigorous tracking of professional certification status against a defined minimum standard. The clause's 'S' marker establishes this as a non-negotiable supervisory expectation, meaning BNM will assess not only whether qualified auditors exist but whether governance mechanisms ensure their qualifications remain current and appropriate. Governance frameworks must therefore create binding obligations around certification, competency assessment, and escalation when gaps arise.

requirements.requirement: The Board, through the Audit Committee, must approve and annually review a Technology Audit Competency Policy that specifies: minimum mandatory professional certifications by role (e.g., CISA, CISSP, CCSP, CEH, CIA), minimum team composition by specialisation, CPD requirements, and escalation procedures when competency gaps are identified.
requirements.rationale: Board-level policy ownership anchors the clause's requirements — 'must ensure the internal audit function must have dedicated technology audit resources' — at the highest governance level. Without a Board-approved policy, competency standards are subject to management discretion and may erode over time, particularly under cost or headcount pressures.
requirements.owner: Audit Committee Chairman / Board of Directors
requirements.frequency: Annual approval; out-of-cycle review upon material regulatory change or audit function restructuring

requirements.requirement: Maintain a centralised Professional Certification Register for all technology audit staff, recording: certification name, certifying body, date of attainment, expiry date, and renewal status. The register must be reviewed by the CAE quarterly and reported to the Audit Committee semi-annually.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates that resources be 'professionally certified' — a status that lapses if renewal requirements are unmet. A register with active tracking prevents certification expiry going undetected and provides BNM with auditable evidence of ongoing compliance during regulatory examinations or supervisory reviews.
requirements.frequency: Quarterly internal review; semi-annual Audit Committee reporting

requirements.requirement: Conduct an annual Technology Audit Capability Assessment, independently validated by the Audit Committee, benchmarking the function's specialisations and certifications against the institution's current and planned technology risk profile, with a documented remediation plan for identified gaps.
requirements.rationale: The clause's requirement to be 'adequately conversant with the developing sophisticate' of technology systems creates a moving target. An annual capability assessment operationalises this by formally comparing current audit competency against the institution's evolving risk profile, ensuring gaps are surfaced and remediated before they result in audit coverage failures.
requirements.frequency: Annual; triggered ad-hoc by material technology transformation events

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal escalation and remediation protocol for technology audit competency gaps: defining trigger thresholds (e.g., uncertified auditor assigned to a specialised domain), mandatory escalation to the CAE and Audit Committee, interim controls (e.g., co-sourcing), and target resolution timelines.
requirements.rationale: Competency gaps will inevitably arise due to attrition, certification lapses, or the emergence of new technology domains. A documented escalation protocol ensures gaps are managed transparently rather than silently, preserving audit quality and demonstrating to BNM that the institution has controls to maintain compliance with the clause even during transition periods.
requirements.frequency: Protocol reviewed annually; activated per event

requirements.requirement: Include technology audit function competency as a standing scope item in any External Quality Assessment (EQA) or independent peer review of the internal audit function, with EQA findings on competency reported directly to the Board.
requirements.rationale: The IIA Standards require periodic external quality assessments of the internal audit function. Scoping technology audit competency into this assessment provides the Board with independent assurance — beyond management self-assessment — that the institution satisfies the clause's requirements, which is particularly important given BNM's supervisory scrutiny of technology audit effectiveness in Malaysian financial institutions.
requirements.frequency: Every 5 years (mandatory EQA per IIA Standards); self-assessment annually in intervening years

business.summary: This clause permits technology auditors to serve in an advisory capacity during the planning and development of major products, systems, third-party adoptions, and technology operations, but introduces a significant operational risk: if the same auditors later conduct post-implementation reviews of the same initiatives, their prior advisory involvement may compromise audit objectivity. Financial institutions that fail to manage this dual-role tension risk producing biased audit findings, weakening internal control assurance, and exposing themselves to regulatory censure by BNM for inadequate audit independence governance.

requirements.requirement: Define and document the specific triggers and scope under which technology audit resources may be engaged in an advisory or consulting capacity, explicitly covering new major products, systems, adoption of third-party service providers, and technology operations during planning and development phases.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text permits advisory engagement only for enumerated categories during specific lifecycle phases. Without clear business-level criteria, advisory engagements may occur ad hoc and without adequate independence safeguards, creating uncontrolled audit risk and potential regulatory non-compliance.
requirements.frequency: Policy defined at inception; reviewed Annually or upon any major organisational or regulatory change

requirements.requirement: Establish a mandatory pre-engagement independence declaration process for technology auditors before they assume any advisory or consulting role, requiring each auditor to formally assess and declare whether the proposed engagement could materially impair their independence or objectivity in any subsequent post-implementation review of the same product, system, or operation.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly requires that auditors 'carefully consider whether such an advisory or consulting role can materially impair their independence or objectivity in performing post-implementation reviews.' A formal declaration operationalises this obligation and creates an auditable record demonstrating due diligence.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (prior to commencement of each advisory engagement)

requirements.requirement: Implement a restriction mechanism whereby a technology auditor who has served in an advisory or consulting capacity on a major product, system, or technology operation is presumptively excluded from leading or significantly participating in the subsequent post-implementation review of that same initiative, unless a documented independence assessment confirms no material impairment.
requirements.rationale: The clause's concern about independence impairment in 'post-implementation reviews of the products, systems and operations concerned' directly implies that institutions must operationalise a safeguard—not merely document a consideration. Failure to restrict dual-role auditors creates the very independence risk the clause seeks to prevent.
requirements.owner: Chief Audit Executive (CAE) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Per-event (at planning stage of each post-implementation review)

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal request and approval process for business units seeking technology audit advisory support, including documented justification of the advisory need, defined scope boundaries, and sign-off by the CAE, to prevent advisory engagements from being used by project teams to informally co-opt audit resources in ways that undermine audit independence.
requirements.rationale: Without a structured request process, business or technology units may engage audit resources informally during project development, creating undocumented advisory relationships that later invisibly compromise post-implementation audit integrity. Formalising requests protects both the audit function and the institution.
requirements.frequency: Per-event; register reviewed Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Maintain a centralised Advisory Engagement Register that records all instances where technology audit resources have been engaged in an advisory or consulting capacity, capturing the auditor(s) involved, the product/system/operation concerned, the phase and duration of advisory involvement, and the independence assessment outcome.
requirements.rationale: A register provides the evidentiary foundation for demonstrating regulatory compliance to BNM and enables the CAE and Audit Committee to identify cumulative independence risks across multiple concurrent projects. It also facilitates audit planning decisions for future post-implementation reviews.
requirements.frequency: Maintained continuously; reported to Audit Committee Quarterly

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement systems and tools that enable structured tracking of audit advisory engagements, enforce independence conflict checks during audit assignment workflows, and generate the evidence trails needed to demonstrate compliance with this clause. The primary implementation challenge is ensuring that audit management platforms can distinguish between advisory-role involvement and assurance-role involvement for the same system or project, and surface conflicts automatically before audit assignments are finalised.

requirements.requirement: Configure the institution's audit management system (AMS) to capture and persist a distinct 'Advisory Engagement' record type, linked to the relevant project, product, or system identifier, that records which auditors participated, in what advisory capacity, and for which development phase — separate from standard audit assignment records.
requirements.rationale: Without a structured data model in the AMS, advisory involvement is either undocumented or conflated with formal audit assignments, making it impossible to systematically detect independence conflicts when scheduling post-implementation reviews. The verbatim text's distinction between advisory and post-implementation roles requires the system to support this distinction.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit Technology / CTO
requirements.frequency: Implementation: one-time configuration; ongoing record creation Per-event

requirements.requirement: Implement an automated conflict-of-interest check within the audit assignment workflow that queries the Advisory Engagement Register when an auditor is being assigned to a post-implementation review, flags any prior advisory involvement by the proposed assignee on the same product, system, or operation, and requires documented CAE override approval before assignment can proceed.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires auditors to 'carefully consider' independence impairment before proceeding. Automating the conflict check removes reliance on individual recollection and ensures the consideration occurs systematically for every assignment, directly supporting the independence protection the clause mandates.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit Technology / CISO
requirements.frequency: Triggered automatically Per-event during audit assignment; system logic reviewed Annually

requirements.requirement: Integrate the Advisory Engagement Register with the institution's project lifecycle management or change management system (e.g., JIRA, ServiceNow) so that when a major product, system, or technology operation transitions from planning/development to post-implementation, the system automatically cross-references audit advisory participants against proposed post-implementation review team members and raises an alert.
requirements.rationale: Independence risks materialise at the phase transition from development to post-implementation. By integrating audit and project lifecycle systems at this transition point, the institution creates a systematic guardrail aligned precisely with the lifecycle boundary described in the verbatim text.
requirements.frequency: Integration implemented once; automated trigger Per-event at project phase transition

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain a digital independence assessment questionnaire within the AMS, pre-populated with the auditor's known advisory engagement history for the relevant product or system, to guide and document the 'careful consideration' required by the clause before advisory role commencement and again before post-implementation review assignment.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text imposes a subjective yet critical obligation to 'carefully consider' independence impairment. Standardising this assessment into a structured, system-captured questionnaire ensures consistency, creates a defensible audit trail for BNM review, and reduces the risk of inadvertent bias in self-assessment.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit Technology / CISO
requirements.frequency: Template reviewed Annually; completed Per-event

requirements.requirement: Implement access controls and segregation within the AMS ensuring that technology auditors in an active advisory capacity on a given project cannot self-assign or modify their assignment to the corresponding post-implementation review without CAE-level approval, enforced through role-based access controls.
requirements.rationale: Without system-enforced access controls, advisory auditors could inadvertently or deliberately assign themselves to post-implementation reviews of the same initiatives, bypassing governance checks. Technical controls provide a non-bypassable enforcement layer complementing the policy and process controls required by the clause.
requirements.owner: CISO / Head of Internal Audit Technology
requirements.frequency: Controls configured at implementation; access reviews Quarterly

governance.summary: This clause requires robust governance structures that clearly define the permissibility and limits of technology audit advisory roles, establish Board Audit Committee oversight of independence risks arising from dual-role engagements, and create escalation and remediation pathways when independence impairment is identified or suspected. Governance frameworks must ensure that the audit function retains its structural independence and objectivity as a foundational assurance pillar, consistent with BNM's broader expectations for technology audit effectiveness.

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal Technology Audit Advisory Role Policy, approved by the Board Audit Committee, that defines: the permissible scope of advisory engagements (restricted to planning and development phases of new major products, systems, third-party service provider adoptions, and technology operations); the mandatory independence assessment process; conflict-of-interest thresholds that trigger automatic exclusion from post-implementation reviews; and the escalation path when independence concerns are identified.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text permits but does not mandate advisory roles, implying that the institution must set its own governance boundaries. A Board-approved policy formalises these boundaries, demonstrates regulatory compliance intent to BNM, and provides the authoritative framework within which operational and technical controls operate.
requirements.owner: Board Audit Committee Chairman / Chief Audit Executive (CAE)
requirements.frequency: Policy approved at inception; reviewed Annually and upon significant regulatory or organisational change

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal independence impairment assessment protocol, executed by the CAE (or a designated senior audit official independent of the advisory engagement), that evaluates potential material impairment to auditor independence or objectivity before any advisory engagement commences and again before any post-implementation review assignment is finalised for the same product, system, or operation.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text states that auditors are 'expected to carefully consider whether such an advisory or consulting role can materially impair their independence or objectivity.' Governance operationalises this expectation through a structured protocol, ensures the assessment is conducted by a party with appropriate seniority and independence, and produces documented evidence for regulatory scrutiny.
requirements.frequency: Per-event (at advisory commencement and at post-implementation review planning); outcomes reported to Audit Committee Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Institute a quarterly Audit Committee reporting cycle on technology audit advisory engagements, including: the number and nature of advisory engagements undertaken in the period; independence assessments conducted and their outcomes; any instances where auditors were excluded from post-implementation reviews due to prior advisory involvement; and any exceptions or escalations arising from independence concerns.
requirements.rationale: Board Audit Committee oversight of audit independence is a foundational governance requirement consistent with BNM's broader governance expectations. Regular reporting ensures the Audit Committee can detect patterns of advisory overuse, cumulative independence erosion, or structural conflicts that individual per-event assessments might not surface.
requirements.owner: Board Audit Committee Chairman / Chief Audit Executive (CAE)
requirements.frequency: Quarterly

requirements.requirement: Define and document formal escalation and remediation procedures for scenarios where a technology auditor's prior advisory involvement is assessed to materially impair their independence for a post-implementation review, including: mandatory reassignment to an independent auditor; notification to the Audit Committee; and, where impairment is discovered after a post-implementation review has commenced or concluded, a protocol for supplementary independent review.
requirements.rationale: The clause acknowledges that advisory roles 'can materially impair' independence but does not specify remediation. Governance must define what happens when impairment is identified, both prospectively and retrospectively, to prevent compromised audit findings from standing unchallenged and to demonstrate to BNM that the institution actively manages, rather than merely acknowledges, this risk.
requirements.owner: Chief Audit Executive (CAE) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Protocol established at policy inception; triggered Per-event; reviewed Annually

requirements.requirement: Include technology audit advisory role independence as a standing agenda item in the annual internal audit quality assurance and improvement programme (QAIP), with periodic self-assessments and at least triennial independent external assessments evaluating whether the institution's advisory engagement practices have, in practice, materially impaired audit independence or objectivity in post-implementation reviews.
requirements.rationale: Long-term governance effectiveness requires that the independence safeguards established under this clause are themselves subject to independent evaluation. The QAIP provides the structured mechanism for this, ensuring that advisory role practices do not gradually erode audit independence through institutional habituation or accumulated exceptions not visible in per-event reviews.
requirements.owner: Chief Audit Executive (CAE) / Board Audit Committee
requirements.frequency: Self-assessment Annually; independent external assessment at least every three years

business.summary: Financial institutions face regulatory censure, reputational damage, and operational disruption if their production data centres are not independently validated for resilience. This clause mandates periodic external scrutiny to surface latent infrastructure risks before they materialise into outages, imposing direct accountability on senior leadership to act on assessment findings. Failure to comply — particularly the absence of a board-level deliberation — signals a governance control failure to BNM examiners.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a formal vendor selection process to identify, qualify, and appoint a technically competent external service provider capable of conducting a production Data Centre Resilience Assessment (DCRA) covering all major risks and current resilience levels.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text requires the appointed provider to be 'technically competent' — a generic IT audit firm does not suffice. The institution must define objective competency criteria (e.g., Uptime Institute accreditation, ANSI/TIA-942 expertise, proven DC assessment track record) to defend vendor selection to BNM examiners and ensure assessment quality.
requirements.frequency: Prior to each triennial cycle; updated upon each material change trigger

requirements.requirement: Define and operationalise a formal trigger mechanism for initiating a DCRA outside the standard three-year cycle, specifically identifying what constitutes a 'material change in data centre infrastructure' (e.g., significant capacity expansion, power system overhaul, migration to a new facility, major cooling system replacement).
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates assessment 'when there is a material change in the data centre infrastructure, whichever is earlier' — ambiguity in defining 'material change' creates compliance risk. A documented and approved definition prevents both under-triggering (missing assessments) and over-triggering (unnecessary cost), and demonstrates proactive regulatory adherence.
requirements.frequency: Policy reviewed annually; trigger evaluated at every infrastructure change review

requirements.requirement: For production data centres managed by third-party service providers, establish a due diligence framework to evaluate whether independent third-party assurance reports (e.g., SSAE 18 SOC reports, ISO 27001 audits) are sufficient substitutes for a self-commissioned DCRA, validating alignment with the institution's risk appetite and that the scope covers similar risks required by this paragraph.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text permits reliance on third-party assurance reports only if 'consistent with the financial institution's risk appetite and tolerance' and the assurance has 'considered similar risks and meets the requirements in this paragraph.' Without a structured evaluation framework, blanket reliance on vendor-provided reports may constitute a compliance failure if scope gaps exist.
requirements.frequency: Per engagement with third-party DC providers; reassessed annually

requirements.requirement: Develop a DCRA lifecycle management programme encompassing scheduling, budget allocation, scoping, vendor engagement, and remediation tracking to ensure continuity of compliance across triennial assessment cycles without dependency on ad-hoc institutional memory.
requirements.rationale: A three-year cycle with material change triggers requires sustained programme management. Absence of formalised scheduling and budgeting leads to missed deadlines and last-minute, low-quality assessments. Programme ownership ensures compliance is embedded in annual planning cycles rather than treated as an exception.
requirements.frequency: Annually reviewed as part of technology risk management planning

requirements.requirement: Ensure that DCRA findings and remediation plans are escalated to the designated board-level committee (as specified in RMiT paragraph 8.3) for formal deliberation, and that decisions and dissenting views are captured in board committee minutes as evidence of regulatory compliance.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text is unambiguous: 'The designated board-level committee specified in paragraph 8.3 must deliberate the outcome of the assessment.' BNM examiners will inspect board minutes to verify this deliberation occurred. Incomplete or absent minutes expose the institution to findings of governance failure at the highest level.
requirements.frequency: Following completion of each DCRA

technology.summary: Technology teams must maintain a comprehensive, audit-ready state of production data centre infrastructure and ensure all resilience controls mandated under paragraphs 10.24 to 10.28 are implemented, documented, and evidence-ready for external assessors. Infrastructure change management processes must be instrumented to automatically flag material changes that trigger an early DCRA. For third-party-managed data centres, technology teams must obtain, analyse, and gap-assess assurance reports against the institution's specific DCRA requirements.

requirements.requirement: Maintain a continuously updated production data centre infrastructure inventory and configuration baseline — covering power systems, cooling, physical security, network connectivity, fire suppression, and environmental controls — to serve as the authoritative reference for DCRA scoping and evidence submission.
requirements.rationale: An external assessor's ability to 'consider all major risks and determine the current level of resilience' depends entirely on accurate infrastructure documentation. Outdated or incomplete records result in assessment gaps, finding disputes, and potential regulatory scrutiny of the assessment's validity.
requirements.frequency: Continuous; formal reconciliation quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement and evidence compliance with all technical controls specified in RMiT paragraphs 10.24 to 10.28 (covering data centre physical security, redundancy, power supply, environmental controls, and access management), and maintain a compliance traceability matrix mapping each sub-requirement to implemented controls and supporting evidence artefacts.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly states the DCRA 'shall, at a minimum, include a consideration of whether the requirements in paragraphs 10.24 to 10.28 have been adhered to.' A pre-existing internal traceability matrix accelerates external assessment, reduces assessor ambiguity, and demonstrates proactive compliance maturity to BNM.
requirements.frequency: Reviewed semi-annually; updated upon infrastructure changes

requirements.requirement: Integrate DCRA material change triggers into the Technology Change Management process such that any infrastructure change request classified as 'significant' (per the approved definition under 14.1-B2) automatically generates a DCRA initiation workflow, including risk assessment, vendor engagement, and timeline planning.
requirements.rationale: The 'material change' trigger in the verbatim text requires institutional agility — a reactive posture relying on manual identification will miss events. Embedding the trigger within change management tooling ensures no qualifying change is processed without evaluating whether a DCRA is warranted, creating an auditable compliance trail.
requirements.frequency: Evaluated at each change management review board meeting

requirements.requirement: For third-party-managed data centres, perform a structured scope-gap analysis against each vendor-provided independent assurance report (e.g., SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 certificate with Statement of Applicability) to verify coverage of risks equivalent to those in paragraphs 10.24 to 10.28, and document findings with a conclusion on whether reliance is justified.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text permits reliance on third-party reports only if 'the independent assurance has considered similar risks and meets the requirements in this paragraph.' Without a formal gap analysis, the institution cannot demonstrate to BNM that it validated report sufficiency — making blanket reliance a compliance gap regardless of report quality.
requirements.frequency: Upon receipt of each new assurance report; at minimum annually

requirements.requirement: Establish a DCRA findings remediation register to track all vulnerabilities and deficiencies identified by the external assessor, with assigned owners, remediation timelines, risk acceptance decisions (where applicable), and closure evidence, providing a single source of truth for board reporting and BNM examination.
requirements.rationale: The board-level deliberation required by the clause is substantively meaningful only if findings are systematically tracked to closure. A remediation register demonstrates that the institution treats DCRA as a continuous improvement exercise, not a point-in-time compliance tick — which is the standard BNM expects of mature institutions.
requirements.frequency: Updated monthly; reviewed at quarterly Technology Risk Committee meetings

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure the DCRA is not treated as a technical exercise but as a board-level risk management obligation with clear ownership, documented deliberation, and enforceable remediation accountability. The designated board-level committee under paragraph 8.3 must be formally constituted with DCRA deliberation embedded in its Terms of Reference. Policy frameworks must govern vendor qualification, material change classification, and reliance on third-party assurance, ensuring all decisions are defensible under BNM examination.

requirements.requirement: Embed DCRA outcome deliberation into the formal Terms of Reference (ToR) of the designated board-level committee specified in RMiT paragraph 8.3, specifying mandatory agenda items, quorum requirements, minimum deliberation standards, and documentation requirements for DCRA review sessions.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates that 'the designated board-level committee specified in paragraph 8.3 must deliberate the outcome.' 'Deliberation' implies substantive engagement, not mere notation. Embedding DCRA in the ToR ensures this is a standing obligation rather than an ad-hoc escalation, and provides BNM with clear governance traceability.
requirements.frequency: ToR reviewed annually; DCRA deliberation convened following each assessment

requirements.requirement: Develop and approve a DCRA Policy that defines: (a) mandatory qualifications for external assessors, (b) the definition of 'material change' triggering an out-of-cycle assessment, (c) minimum DCRA scope requirements aligned to paragraphs 10.24 to 10.28, and (d) conditions under which third-party assurance reports may substitute a self-commissioned DCRA.
requirements.rationale: Without a governing policy, DCRA execution relies on institutional judgment that may vary across cycles and personnel. A BNM-aligned policy creates consistent standards, protects the institution against arbitrary compliance decisions, and provides the documented framework examiners expect to see when reviewing the institution's approach to paragraph 14.1.
requirements.frequency: Approved at policy inception; reviewed every three years or upon regulatory update

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal risk appetite statement specific to data centre resilience — including explicit tolerance thresholds for infrastructure vulnerabilities, acceptable levels of third-party assurance reliance, and remediation timelines by risk severity — approved by the board and used to guide DCRA scoping, finding prioritisation, and remediation decisions.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text conditions reliance on third-party assurance reports on consistency with 'the financial institution's risk appetite and tolerance.' Without a documented, board-approved data centre risk appetite, this condition cannot be formally satisfied. The risk appetite statement also provides the governance context within which DCRA findings are evaluated and acted upon.
requirements.frequency: Reviewed annually or upon material change to DC strategy

requirements.requirement: Implement a DCRA governance calendar integrated into the institution's enterprise risk management framework, tracking triennial assessment due dates, material change triggers, vendor engagement milestones, board reporting deadlines, and remediation closure dates, with escalation protocols for schedule breaches.
requirements.rationale: Regulatory compliance with a three-year cycle across multiple data centre environments requires structured governance oversight to prevent deadline drift. A governed calendar with escalation protocols ensures accountability sits with named risk owners and that BNM examination periods are never approached with an overdue or incomplete DCRA cycle.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Head of Technology Risk
requirements.frequency: Reviewed quarterly at Technology Risk Committee; annually at Board Risk Committee

requirements.requirement: Require formal board or board-committee approval for all risk acceptance decisions arising from DCRA findings that exceed the institution's risk tolerance, with documented justification, compensating controls, and a time-bound reassessment date, ensuring accountability is retained at the appropriate governance level.
requirements.rationale: Board-level deliberation of DCRA outcomes (as mandated by the verbatim text) implies more than passive receipt of a report — it requires the board to own residual risk decisions. Formalising risk acceptance at board level ensures that unresolved findings are not silently carried forward, and that the board can demonstrate active risk stewardship to BNM.
requirements.frequency: As required following each DCRA; reviewed at subsequent annual board cycle

business.summary: This clause mandates that financial institutions obtain independent, expert validation of their network resilience posture at defined intervals, ensuring that network risks are identified and controlled in line with the institution's risk appetite. Failure to comply exposes the institution to undetected network vulnerabilities, potential operational disruptions, and regulatory sanctions from BNM. The board-level deliberation requirement ensures that network resilience is treated as a strategic business risk, not merely a technical concern.

requirements.requirement: Appoint and maintain a technically competent external service provider to conduct Network Resilience Assessments (NRA), with documented evidence of the provider's qualifications, certifications, and relevant experience in financial sector network assessments.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly requires a 'technically competent external service provider', implying that internal assessments are insufficient. Appointing an unqualified vendor exposes the institution to inaccurate risk assessments and regulatory non-compliance, potentially masking critical network vulnerabilities.
requirements.frequency: Vendor review at least every 3 years or upon contract renewal; competency re-validation upon each NRA engagement

requirements.requirement: Establish and enforce a formal NRA scheduling policy that mandates assessment completion at least once every three years and defines the institution's process for identifying and declaring 'material changes in network design' that trigger an out-of-cycle NRA.
requirements.rationale: The clause specifies 'at least once in three years or whenever there is a material change in the network design, whichever is earlier.' Without a formal definition of material change and an associated trigger mechanism, the institution risks conducting assessments on an inadequate schedule and failing to capture significant shifts in its network risk profile.
requirements.frequency: Policy review annually; material change assessment on a continuous basis

requirements.requirement: Define and document the institution's network resilience risk appetite, ensuring that controls identified and implemented following the NRA are explicitly proportionate to the approved risk appetite statement.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to 'set proportionate controls aligned with its risk appetite.' Without a documented and board-endorsed risk appetite for network resilience, the institution cannot demonstrate that its control environment is calibrated appropriately, creating both regulatory and operational exposure.
requirements.frequency: Risk appetite review annually or upon material change to business strategy or network environment

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal NRA remediation programme that translates assessment findings into time-bound, prioritised action plans with assigned ownership, tracked to closure and reported to senior management.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the assessment to 'determine the current level of resilience' and inform proportionate controls. Without a structured remediation process, identified gaps may remain unaddressed, negating the value of the assessment and perpetuating the very risks the NRA is intended to surface.
requirements.frequency: Post-assessment initiation; progress reviewed quarterly until closure

requirements.requirement: Maintain a comprehensive register of all NRA engagements, including scope, provider credentials, key findings, board deliberation records, and remediation status, available for BNM supervisory review.
requirements.rationale: As a Standard (S) marker clause, BNM expects demonstrable compliance. A centralised NRA register provides an audit trail linking each assessment cycle to its outcomes and the board's deliberation, enabling the institution to evidence compliance during regulatory examinations.
requirements.frequency: Updated continuously; reviewed prior to each BNM supervisory engagement

technology.summary: Technology teams must ensure the institution's network environment is assessment-ready at all times, with current architecture documentation, configuration baselines, and evidence of adherence to the specific network security requirements in paragraphs 10.36 to 10.43. Teams must also implement a structured process to detect and escalate material network changes that trigger an out-of-cycle NRA, and translate assessment findings into concrete technical remediation actions. The technical control environment must be demonstrably aligned with the institution's approved risk appetite.

requirements.requirement: Maintain up-to-date and accurate network architecture documentation, including topology diagrams, asset inventories, data flow maps, and configuration baselines, sufficient to support a comprehensive NRA scope covering 'all major risks'.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the assessment to 'consider all major risks and determine the current level of resilience.' An external assessor cannot meaningfully evaluate resilience without accurate, current documentation of the network environment. Outdated documentation directly undermines assessment quality and may conceal residual risks.
requirements.owner: Head of IT Infrastructure / Chief Technology Officer (CTO)
requirements.frequency: Documentation updated continuously; formal completeness review quarterly and prior to each NRA engagement

requirements.requirement: Implement and evidence ongoing adherence to all technical requirements specified in paragraphs 10.36 to 10.43 of BNM RMiT, including network segmentation, perimeter controls, monitoring, and encryption standards, and produce compliance artefacts for inclusion in the NRA scope.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text explicitly states the assessment 'shall include an assessment of the financial institution's adherence to the requirements in paragraphs 10.36 to 10.43.' Non-compliance with these specific paragraphs will be surfaced during the NRA, creating direct regulatory findings traceable to this clause.
requirements.frequency: Continuous implementation; compliance evidence compiled and reviewed at least semi-annually and prior to each NRA

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal change management process that classifies network design changes as material or non-material using defined criteria, with mandatory escalation to Risk Management and Compliance when a change is classified as material, triggering an out-of-cycle NRA.
requirements.rationale: The clause triggers NRA 'whenever there is a material change in the network design, whichever is earlier.' Without a structured change classification and escalation mechanism embedded in the IT change management process, material changes may proceed without triggering the required assessment, creating unassessed risk exposure.
requirements.frequency: Embedded in each change advisory board (CAB) cycle; process review annually

requirements.requirement: Implement technical controls identified or validated through the NRA findings that are proportionate to the institution's approved network resilience risk appetite, including redundancy, failover, intrusion detection, and network monitoring capabilities, with documented configuration evidence.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires the institution to 'set proportionate controls aligned with its risk appetite' following the assessment. Technology teams are the primary implementers of these controls. Without deliberate, risk-appetite-aligned control implementation, the NRA cycle produces findings without meaningful reduction in residual risk.
requirements.frequency: Implementation upon NRA completion; control effectiveness reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Deploy and maintain continuous network monitoring and resilience testing capabilities (including network performance metrics, availability dashboards, and periodic internal resilience testing) to provide real-time visibility into resilience posture between formal NRA cycles.
requirements.rationale: The NRA establishes a point-in-time resilience benchmark, but the network environment evolves continuously. Continuous monitoring ensures that material degradation in resilience or the emergence of new risks is detected between cycles, supporting timely escalation and triggering a review of whether an out-of-cycle NRA is warranted.
requirements.frequency: Continuous monitoring; resilience test results reviewed monthly and reported to senior management quarterly

governance.summary: This clause imposes explicit board-level accountability for network resilience, requiring the designated board committee under paragraph 8.3 to formally deliberate NRA outcomes — not merely receive them. Governance structures must ensure the NRA lifecycle, from provider appointment through to remediation closure, is governed by formal policy, documented in board records, and subject to independent oversight. The Standard (S) marker signals BNM's expectation of institutionalised, demonstrable governance rather than ad hoc compliance.

requirements.requirement: The designated board-level committee specified in paragraph 8.3 must formally deliberate the outcome of every NRA, with deliberation recorded in board committee minutes that capture the discussion, conclusions, any dissenting views, and directives issued for remediation.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text mandates that the 'designated board-level committee specified in paragraph 8.3 must deliberate the outcome of the assessment.' The use of 'deliberate' rather than 'receive' or 'note' signals an active, substantive discussion requirement. Absent formal minutes evidencing deliberation, the institution cannot demonstrate compliance with this specific governance obligation.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee Chairman / Company Secretary
requirements.frequency: Following each NRA completion; minutes finalised within 30 days of committee meeting

requirements.requirement: Adopt and maintain a board-approved Network Resilience Assessment Policy that governs the NRA lifecycle, including provider selection criteria, assessment frequency and triggers, scope requirements (covering all major risks and paragraphs 10.36 to 10.43 adherence), board reporting obligations, and remediation accountability.
requirements.rationale: A formal policy operationalises the clause's multiple obligations into an institutionalised framework, ensuring consistent execution across leadership changes and providing the documentary evidence BNM expects when examining Standard-marker requirements. Without a policy, compliance depends on individual judgment rather than systemic controls.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Compliance Officer (CCO)
requirements.frequency: Policy approved by the board and reviewed at least every 3 years or upon material regulatory change

requirements.requirement: Establish a formal due diligence and onboarding process for evaluating and approving external NRA service providers, including assessment of technical competency credentials, financial sector experience, independence from the institution, and professional indemnity insurance coverage.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires a 'technically competent external service provider.' Governance must define what constitutes technical competence and create a repeatable evaluation process to select and approve providers. Without structured due diligence, the institution risks appointing an inadequate assessor whose findings may not withstand BNM scrutiny.
requirements.owner: Chief Risk Officer (CRO) / Chief Procurement Officer
requirements.frequency: Prior to each NRA provider appointment; provider register reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Implement a board-level remediation oversight mechanism whereby the board committee receives structured progress updates on NRA-identified gaps and control implementation, with escalation triggers for overdue or high-priority remediation items.
requirements.rationale: Board deliberation of the NRA outcome implies ongoing accountability for remediation, not a one-time discussion. Without a formal follow-up mechanism, the board's deliberation obligation becomes a compliance checkbox rather than a genuine governance control, and residual risks from assessment findings may persist unaddressed.
requirements.owner: Board Risk Committee / Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
requirements.frequency: Quarterly progress updates to the board committee until all critical and high-priority findings are closed

requirements.requirement: Integrate the NRA schedule, material change triggers, and board deliberation milestones into the institution's enterprise-wide regulatory compliance calendar, with Internal Audit independently verifying that the NRA cycle and board governance obligations are executed as required.
requirements.rationale: As a Standard (S) marker clause under External Party Assurance, BNM expects the institution to demonstrate systemic, self-reinforcing compliance. Independent Internal Audit verification closes the control loop, ensuring that management self-reporting on NRA compliance is subject to objective challenge and that deviations are escalated before they become regulatory findings.
requirements.owner: Head of Internal Audit / Chief Compliance Officer (CCO)
requirements.frequency: Compliance calendar maintained continuously; Internal Audit review of NRA governance at least annually

business.summary: Financial institutions face significant regulatory, reputational, and operational risk if staff at any level are insufficiently equipped to recognise and respond to cyber threats relevant to their roles. Failure to maintain a structured, measurable, and regularly updated awareness programme directly exposes the institution to social-engineering attacks, insider-threat incidents, and BNM supervisory action. The explicit inclusion of third-party service providers extends liability management beyond the institution's own workforce.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a mandatory annual cybersecurity and technology awareness education programme covering all permanent, contract, and temporary staff across all business units and functions.
requirements.rationale: The verbatim text states the programme 'must be conducted at least annually' and applies to 'all staff'. A missing or partial rollout leaves the institution in breach of BNM's Standard requirement and creates uneven human-risk exposure across business lines.
requirements.frequency: Annual (minimum); review triggered by significant threat events or staff changes

requirements.requirement: Design and deliver role-specific cybersecurity awareness content that is tailored to the duties and access privileges of distinct staff populations (e.g., retail banking staff, treasury, IT, senior management, board).
requirements.rationale: The clause specifies education must support staff 'in undertaking their respective roles', indicating a one-size-fits-all programme is insufficient. Role-relevant training reduces phishing susceptibility, credential misuse, and procedural errors specific to each function.
requirements.frequency: Annual curriculum review; role-specific updates triggered by role changes or new threat vectors

requirements.requirement: Update programme content at least annually to reflect the current and evolving cyber threat landscape, including emerging attack techniques, regulatory updates, and incident trends relevant to the Malaysian financial sector.
requirements.rationale: The clause explicitly requires the programme to 'reflect the evolving cyber threat landscape and emerging risks'. Static content that does not address current threats (e.g., AI-generated phishing, supply-chain attacks) fails this obligation and leaves staff unprepared for modern attack vectors.
requirements.frequency: Annual mandatory refresh; ad hoc updates triggered by significant industry incidents or BNM advisories

requirements.requirement: Formally extend relevant cybersecurity awareness training obligations to third-party service providers with access to the institution's systems, data, or premises, consistent with contractual obligations under paragraph 10.48(i).
requirements.rationale: The clause states the institution 'shall also include its third party service providers in their relevant training'. Third-party personnel represent a material attack surface; failure to include them in training exposes the institution to supply-chain compromises and undermines the control environment.
requirements.frequency: Annual; at onboarding of new third-party engagements; upon material changes to third-party scope

requirements.requirement: Measure and formally report on the effectiveness of the cybersecurity awareness programme using quantitative and qualitative metrics, and remediate identified gaps within defined timeframes.
requirements.rationale: The clause mandates the institution must 'measure the effectiveness of its education and awareness programs'. Without measurable outcomes, the institution cannot demonstrate compliance to BNM examiners or justify programme investments, and cannot identify populations that remain at elevated human risk.
requirements.frequency: Post-each training cycle (minimum annual); reported to senior management and board

technology.summary: Technology teams must implement a scalable training delivery and tracking infrastructure that can reach all staff and eligible third parties, enforce completion deadlines, and generate audit-ready evidence of participation and effectiveness. The platform must support dynamic content updates to keep pace with the evolving threat landscape. Automated measurement tools such as simulated phishing campaigns and knowledge assessments are required to satisfy the effectiveness-measurement obligation.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and maintain a centralised Learning Management System (LMS) capable of assigning, delivering, tracking, and recording completion of role-based cybersecurity awareness modules for all internal staff and, where required, third-party service provider personnel.
requirements.rationale: The 'all staff' scope and annual minimum frequency create a tracking obligation that cannot be reliably met through manual processes. An LMS provides immutable completion records, automated reminders, and audit trails required to demonstrate compliance during BNM examinations.
requirements.frequency: Continuous operation; annual content refresh cycle; quarterly platform health review

requirements.requirement: Implement automated phishing simulation and social-engineering testing tools integrated with the LMS to periodically test staff susceptibility and feed results into effectiveness measurement reporting.
requirements.rationale: BNM requires the institution to 'measure the effectiveness' of programmes. Completion rates alone are insufficient; behavioural testing through simulated attacks provides empirical evidence of whether awareness has translated into changed behaviour, directly satisfying the effectiveness requirement.
requirements.frequency: Minimum quarterly simulations; results reviewed post-each cycle and incorporated into annual effectiveness report

requirements.requirement: Establish a threat-intelligence-informed content update pipeline that enables cybersecurity training materials to be revised and republished to the LMS in response to emerging threats, BNM advisories, or significant sector-wide incidents without requiring a full annual cycle.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires content to 'reflect the evolving cyber threat landscape and emerging risks', implying updates must not be limited to annual cycles. A rapid content update mechanism ensures training remains current between scheduled refresh cycles.
requirements.frequency: Triggered updates as threats emerge; formal review at minimum quarterly

requirements.requirement: Build automated compliance dashboards and reporting capabilities within the LMS that surface real-time completion rates by department, role, and staff category (including third parties), and generate exportable reports for regulatory examination purposes.
requirements.rationale: Demonstrating compliance to BNM requires documentary evidence of programme reach and participation. Automated reporting eliminates manual aggregation errors and ensures management has visibility to enforce completion prior to regulatory deadlines.
requirements.frequency: Real-time dashboard; formal reports generated monthly and at annual cycle close

requirements.requirement: Provision secure LMS access or an equivalent training delivery mechanism for third-party service providers that protects institutional data while enabling completion tracking and evidence capture consistent with internal staff requirements.
requirements.rationale: The clause requires inclusion of third-party service providers 'in their relevant training'. Third parties typically cannot access internal systems, requiring a dedicated external access pathway that maintains the same evidential standard as internal tracking without compromising network security.
requirements.frequency: Established at third-party onboarding; reviewed annually aligned with vendor risk assessments

governance.summary: Governance structures must ensure the cybersecurity awareness programme has board-level visibility, clear ownership, a documented policy framework, and formal oversight mechanisms to validate both execution and effectiveness. The institution must embed third-party training obligations into its vendor management and contract governance frameworks in alignment with paragraph 10.48(i). An annual attestation cycle must provide senior management and the board with assurance that the programme meets BNM's Standard requirements.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain a Board-approved Cybersecurity Awareness and Education Policy that mandates annual minimum training for all staff, defines role-based content requirements, sets effectiveness measurement standards, and prescribes third-party inclusion criteria consistent with paragraph 10.48(i).
requirements.rationale: BNM's Standard marker signals an obligation that must be embedded in formal institutional policy. Without a Board-approved policy, the programme lacks the authority needed to enforce completion across business units and cannot be demonstrated as systematic and institutionally mandated during supervisory reviews.
requirements.frequency: Annual policy review; ad hoc revision triggered by regulatory updates or material programme failures
